1st Lexember

< Old Borlish (a) grad liure βÄ€freely, voluntarilyβÄù, literally βÄ€with free willβÄù
< Latin grΡ¹tus βÄ€willβÄù and lΡΪber βÄ€free, unrestrictedβÄù.

< borrowed ca. 1250 from Latin d≈çnΡ¹ti≈ç, d≈çnΡ¹ti≈çnem βÄ€an act of giving, of presentingβÄù, from d≈çn≈ç βÄ€I giveβÄù.

2nd Lexember

porΟßon /po ÄΥàts…în/ [p äΥêΥàts…în] portion, share, allotment

< Old Boral porton, porcon βÄ€part, shareβÄù < Latin porti≈ç, porti≈çnem βÄ€share, portionβÄù.

porΟßonnar /Υ¨po ÄtsoΥàna Ä/ [Υ¨p…îΥêts äΥàn…ëΥê] to share, to allocate, to hand out

Verb derivation from porΟßon, ca. 1400. Displaced former partir in this sense.

porΟßonment /Υ¨po Äts…înΥàm…¦nt/ [Υ¨p…îΥêts ämΥàm…¦nt] assignment, categorisation, sorting, (mathematics) partition

Nominalisation of porΟßonnar, attested from 1650. In mathematics from 1830, originally in translation from Dutch Austeilung βÄ€distributingβÄù.

Y graΟΑleur porΟßonaurn y mansc seyon coglt pall'eΟß joun orphan.
The volunteers shared the food they had collected with the orphans here.
/i g ÄaΟΑΥàlaw Ä Υ¨p…î ¹tsoΥàno ÄnΧ© i Υàma≈΄x sij…în Υàk…îjlt pal…¦ts Υà £un …î ÄΥàfan/
[i g ¹…êΟΑΥàlaw…ê Υ¨p…îΥêts äΥànoΥên i Υàma≈΄k s än Υàk…îwt p…êΥàl…¦s Υà £ än  äΥêΥàfan]

-

Ayent dou cougl W e X, un porΟßonment dy som W + X nommau nos un mission m ant font W e bers X ΟΑa casc porΟßon toc X cos unic.

Given two sets W and X, a partition of the sum W + X is called a function m with source W and target X if each part intersects W exactly once.

3rd Lexember

absouvr /abΥàsuv ÄΧ©/ […êbΥàzuv…ê] to acquit, to redeem, to absolve

[r-conjugation, -oy variant]

< Old Boral absovre, absolvre βÄ€to absolveβÄù, a semi-learned descendant/borrowing from Latin absolv≈ç βÄ€I loosen, untieβÄù.

tort dell'ivan /Υ¨t…î Ät deliΥàvan/ [Υ¨t…îΥêd…Σl…ΣΥàva≈΄] Christmas pie*

*literally βÄ€infant's pieβÄù, in reference to the Christ child. Refers either to a specific savoury pie or to any of the pastry dishes traditionally prepared for the Christmas Eve feast (y Fest Reveglon), a celebration held in churches across Borland (Istr Boral). The practice of scoring a Catholic cross into the lid and hiding a small wooden object inside (traditionally also a cross) is a later innovation, but compare the French galette des rois. 

< Old Boral torte del ifan βÄ€child's pieβÄù (first attested 10C), from Latin torta panis βÄ€twisted breadβÄù and ΡΪnfΡ¹ns, ΡΪnfΡ¹ntem βÄ€child, infantβÄù.

Y justiΟßour absoloirn my grandmaΟΑr poll'ig delit a blasmant.

/i Υ¨ £…ΣstiΥàtsu Ä Υ¨absoΥàloj ÄnΧ© mi g ÄandΥàmaΟΑ ÄΧ© poΥ¨laj deΥàl…Σt a blaΥàsmant/

[i Υ¨ £…Σst…ΣΥàts äΥêβÄΩΥ¨ Äabz äΥàl…îj…ê≈΄ mi g Ä…êmΥàmaΟΑ…ê p äΥ¨laj d…ΣΥàl…Σt a bl…êΥàzmant]

-

[n äβÄΩΥàzaw l…îg…êΥànaΥê du Υ¨t…îΥêd…Σl…ΣΥàva≈΄ ap äΥêΥàtaΥê | f…êΥ¨l…¦n t äΥà Ä…ΣΥêβÄΩ Ä…¦j k…êx Υàawd…ê]

6th Lexember

< βÄ€without costβÄù. The first word is from Old Boral loin βÄ€away fromβÄù, from Latin longeΧ³ βÄ€from far offβÄù; the second a deverbal derivation of the verb costar βÄ€to cost, to be worthβÄù (first attested 12C), from Latin constoΧ³ βÄ€I consist of, I costβÄù. 8th Lexember

bakilau /Υ¨bakiΥàlo/ [Υ¨bak…ΣΥàlo] cod, marine fish of the family Gadidae

< Origin contested. Extant from 9C as cabeilau, bacalau (among other variants), from Middle Dutch (Mi-Willem), cf. French cabillaud βÄ€codβÄù. Entered Middle Dutch either from some derivation of baculum βÄ€stick, rodβÄù, or else from Basque kabailao βÄ€codβÄù. Formerly assumed to be a direct loan from Vask into Boral βÄ™ see also other naval/fishing terminology like hagr βÄ€mast" βÄ™ but this is now considered phonetically untenable.

J'oyeu ne HorΟΑ il pascn for bakilau.

/ £oΥàjaw ne h…î ÄΥàΟΑa …Σl ΥàpaxnΧ© f…î Ä Υ¨bakiΥàlo/
[ £ äΥàjaw n…Σ h äΥêΥàΟΑa …Σw ΥàpaxnΧ© f…îΥê Υ¨bak…ΣΥàlo]

< partial calque of Old English bΡ¹nf»≥r βÄ€bonfireβÄù, literally βÄ€bone fireβÄù, meaning a fire in which to dispose of rubbish (such as bones). Generalisation to any temporary fire built outdoors attested from 15C.

< Old Boral skurvu»ù et al., presumably from Old Norrish skyrbjΟΚgr βÄ€sour-milk tumourβÄù. Discovered in the late 15C to be caused by diet, following the famed voyage to Mendeva** of Princess Alexandra of Vascony (the first time a European royal set foot in the Novomund), after whom sandrine is indirectly named.

17th Lexember

nohas /noΥàhaz/ [n äΥàxaz] (of economics) right-wing, conservative

< 1751, from Arabic ΌÜΊ≠ΊßΊ≥ (nuαΗΞΡ¹s) with the same sense, and originally βÄ€copperβÄù. Inspired by the Copper faction in the court of Malik Hisham Al-Rashid (reigned 1601-1610) of Libya, who petitioned against the poor reforms he oversaw. The Copper, and their opposition, the Tin, took their names from the two principal colours of the Libyan banner at the time, which was quartered into two black, one white, and one orange field. These two terms βÄ™ nohas and casdir βÄ™ were borrowed into various European languages through the success of the Vascon writer David BensaiΧ¹d's treatise on statecraft The Three Cities, which was translated into over twenty languages. Compare for example French nouase, kasdir.

Stou y Duc coy l'eΟß nohas parmy sull'administration d'autortaΟΑ may accarr.
The Duke must stop the right-wing in his regime gaining more influence.
/fal i Υàd…Σk c…îj i Υ¨tsi noΥàhaz pa ÄΥàmi siladΥ¨minist ÄaΥàtsj…în Υ¨dot…î ÄΥàtaΈΗ me aΥàka Ä/
[faw i Υàd…Σk c…îj (i) Υ¨tsi n äΥàxaz p…ëΥêΥàmi sil…êdΥ¨minist Ä…êΥàtsj…în Υ¨dot…îΥêΥàtah me …êΥàk…ëΥê]

< The first word from MediΟΠval Tuscane viola, probably from Old Vascon viula, from Late Latin vitula (all with the same meaning). The second from Old Boral cemite»ùr, from Latin c≈™mΡ™tΡ™rium βÄ€burial groundβÄù, from Greek ΈΚΈΩΈΙΈΦΈΖœ³Έ°œ¹ΈΙΈΩΈΫ βÄ€sleeping chamberβÄù.

Refers to the mysterious sound of string instruments in the air, which in Borlish folklore is attributed to various spirits connected to death, and to cemeteries in particular. Usually said to be low, mournful melodies. Apparently a tradition peculiar to Borland, as no closely analogous figures are seen in stories abroad.

Featured  in the opera Ale in Fiamme βÄ€Wings AfireβÄù (a retelling of the story of Icarus which famously takes place entirely after the events of the myth), composed by Venisian Chiarina Bruno and first performed in 1824. Her first work, she is said to have heard the tale of the viol cemtir from her brother Jacomo, who had fought alongside Borlish soldiers in the Second German War.

< Phrase attested since at least 1292. Refers to the first snowfall of the year (that is, on or after the first of March), if there is one. Years with a green snowfall are generally considered unlucky, as a symptom of a long and bitter preceding winter.

soccoΟΑr /soΥàk…îΟΑ ÄΧ©/ [s äΥàk…îΟΑ…ê] to shake, to jostle, to toss

An -r conjugation verb, past participle soccos (that is, s variant). From Latin succuti≈ç βÄ€I shake, I flingβÄù with usual collapse of the -i≈ç verb class. Compare French secouer βÄ€to shakeβÄù.

##


Mondifex 2020 - 2m Janver

Ambrose¬Ι III (17 March 1435 - 29 February 1518) was king of Vascony from 1456 until his death, making him the longest ruler in Vascon history. He rose to the throne with the country at its greatest extent during his reign: from Bordew in the north to Port CalΟ® in the south, although in practice Vascon rule barely reached as far as Braga.

King Ambrose III pushed for naval development, establishing the New Navarre Enterprise in 1458. He aimed to capitalise on Vascon supremacy in the fishing trade, trading cod from Dunclothe and Valland throughout the Middlesea. And of course, it was just such a ship that landed on Mendevan¬≤ shores in 1471 and introduced Eurasia to the Novomund.

Despite losing Vascony's Middlesea coast to Burgundy and Barcelon, and despite never marrying, King Ambrose III is remembered well for presiding over a period of relative peace and prosperity. His death presaged the decline of Vascony; it lost over a fifth of its population in the Second Great Dying, and ceded Portingale (its economic heart, having benefited the most from Novomundine trade) to Andalus after the Romantic Wars of the seventeenth century.

βÄîβÄîβÄî

¬Ιin his native tongue, AmbrΟ≤ix.

¬≤that is, North American.

4m Janver - 4th January

##

5m Janver - 5th January

The Brethin of Kernow [1] (Boral: Y Brethin a Cornou) were in the late mediΟΠval period the most influential school of the Brethin religious society. They saw a substantial increase in membership through the twelfth century and counted among their members (Roman) Pope Damian VI (1199-1208) and the saint Victor of Dunchurch.

The Brethin (named for their distinctive heavy cloak; in Old Welsh, their bretin) were founded in Tremonnow [2], at that time in Devon, as an order devoted to record-keeping and the maintenance of history. Originally, they preserved in particular the history of the Christianisation of Albion [3] and Ireland, but quickly expanded their remit to secular history. They spread rapidly through the Briton states and later made inroads into France, Burgundy and even Andalus.

The Brethin of Kernow were notable for being a single organisation which admitted both men and women into their orders, although their institutions were segregated by gender. Some members were sent out of the monasteries to advise in courts across Europe, most (in)famously in the Crovatian [4] House of Domago, whose Duke so favoured his Kernish advisors that his siblings organised a coup against him.

[1] Roughly, Brittany.
[2] Monmouth (Welsh Trefynwy).
[3] Great Britain.
[4] βÄΠ Croatian.

##


8m Janver

- excerpted from βÄ€In Sickness and in Health: Medicine in pre-Arnoutszen EuropeβÄù, second edition, published by Winchester Scholastic Primers, 1961

βÄΠ the death of up to one-fifth of the population.

Of course, despite the name, not everywhere in Europe suffered so badly from the Second Great Dying as did Portingale. Those more inland areas, such as Boham and Burgundy (having lost its coastal territory to House AmigΟ≤t in 1499) endured far fewer casualties simply by virtue of being further from the main mode of transmission: naval trade. It was not by chance that the first recorded death attributable to this plague was in Damvath in Borland [1], said at the time to be the most-trafficked port city outside the Middlesea [2].

It is a common misconception, popularised further by the famous scene in Maresca's 1931 film Frutto Amaro (Bitter Fruit), that people blamed cats and dogs for the sickness sweeping the continent. This idea, first attested in connection with the Second Great Dying only several decades after the fact, perhaps originated with the tale of the monastery at Bonn, which after several deaths to an βÄ€unknown illnessβÄù in 1512 (probably luetic pox [3]) banished all animals from the monastery and (unsuccessfully) from the neighbouring village, claiming they were a Satanic influence.

In fact, the reaction to the Dying varied significantly across Europe. The countries of Albion [4], having for the most part escaped the first continental plague the century before, and furthermore still recovering from the Wars of Fealty, were perhaps the least prepared for an epidemic, and indeedβÄΠ

βÄî-
[1] DamvaΟΑ, capital city of Istr Boral, in the local language.
[2] in Europe, that is.
[3] Syphilis.
[4] Great Britain.

9m Janver

compagn /komΥàp…¦jn/ journeyman, tradesman, associate

< Old Boral compa»ùn βÄ€housemate, companionβÄù, from Late Latin compΡ¹ni≈ç βÄ€companion, messmateβÄù, literally βÄ€with-breadβÄù (a calque from Germanic gahlaibΟ¥ βÄ€comradeβÄù βÄ™ cf. loaf). Enters Boral either directly from Late Latin, or via Old French compaing, from the same source.

A journeyman is one who has completed an apprenticeship and is employed by a master tradesman. May refer more generally to any competent but undistinguished member of a trade, or even to the rank-and-file members of a given guild or society.

Y grant atelier sta un tapeΟßment de laboriostur; compagn agen a casc tabr.
The great workshop was a hive of industry, journeymen working a every table.
/i Υàg Äant Υ¨ateΥàlj…¦ Ä sta …Σn Υ¨tap…¦tsΥàm…¦nt de laΥ¨bo ÄjosΥàt…Σ Ä | k…îmΥàp…¦jn aΥà £…¦n a Υ¨kax Υàtab ÄΧ©/

βÄ€Look through the glass, into the dark! Find me a star with no name, that we might pen the first chapter of its story.βÄù

from Durgh'nen Shorzen Shouer βÄ€Through a Black MirrorβÄù by Elsebeth Sneider, 1795

___

excerpt: New Skies, Old Stories by Simon d'Estiala, published by Prindor MarsillΟ®s, 1980

βÄΠ in her twilight years she campaigned for reforms to Saxony's inheritance law, ultimately unsuccessfully, although her political society would (after her death) spearhead the suffrage movements in Saxony, Denmark, and across the Latin republics.

The Voidtale craze sparked by Sneider's seminal work persisted until the Second German War, when its optimistic and exploratory character dropped rather rapidly out of fashion. Nonetheless there are examples generally counted as Voidtale works to be found later: most notable is Narayanan's series of landscapes depicting the surfaces of other planets orbiting our Sun. Importantly, he portrays these places as barren and inhospitable, in stark contrast to the xenic [1] worlds of Sneider or her imitators. This illustrates the shift in tone in many areas of art following the war, as industry swept the world and the age of the Global Workshop dawned.

It is perhaps foolhardy to attribute the decline of Voidtale trevolds [2] solely to the gloom of post-war Europe. We might say, for example, that the idea of exploring new lands and meeting new civilisations had lost its shine, with the opening of Caddisow [3] leaving few βÄ€mysterious landsβÄù to discover, and the developing steeple-mesh [4] allowing people everywhere to communicate far more rapidly (in particular, to discover that distant nations were often much less exotic than one imagined!).

In any case, when it comes to parachthon romance [5], the middle of the nineteenth century was somewhat of a nadir. Even once Europe had recovered and the general prosperity of the Workshop decades had truly got going, theβÄΠ

___

[1] alien.

[2] novels; that is, stories written primarily for entertainment.

[3] sub-Saharan state occupying much of the territory of Botswana, Zimbabwe, and southern Mozambique.

[4] optical telegraphy, in the vein of semaphore; mostly superseded in the ensuing decades by the electric telegraph.

[5] speculative fiction, such as fantasy or science fiction.

13m Janver

jast /Υà £ast/ zinc; colloq. loose change

< 17C, from Scholastic Latin [1] iastum βÄ€zincβÄù, either from Middle Italian jasto or Vallack jastu βÄ€zincβÄù, from Persian Ί§ΊßΊ≥ΊΣ (jast) βÄ€zincβÄù, from Hindi ύΛ€ύΛΗύΞçύΛΛύΛΨ (jastaΧ³) βÄ€zincβÄù.

Discovered as an independent metal in 14C India, then traded west along Arab routes. Nonetheless, jast alloys such as brass were known in Europe from antiquity, as well as igniac jast (that is, calamine [2]), but the structure of these materials was not fully understood.

14th Janver

βÄ€Perhaps we should have sent for a Kentish witness! They might learn the subtle craft of ending the wars they start.βÄù

- origin unknown; popularly attributed to General Guillen daus Santz on the eve of the Battle of MargΟ®s, 1499

___

For example, Thieves' Script was a simple replacement code used to convey secret messages between Merch forces in the final years of the Wars of Fealty. It is said to have been adapted from similar systems used by gangs of housebreakers in Nottingham, though how such systems came to the attention of King Andrew's court is unclear. It worked by converting each letter into a sequence of three symbols, and is a very early forerunner to codes like steeplescript [1] or even modern one-zeffre encoding [2].

- Hommes en Noir; une Οâtoire d'Espionnage by Agathe Bisset, published by Οâditions de l'UniversitΟ© Ο† Paris, 2003

##

17m Janver

-excerpted from A CyclopΟΠdia of British Persons (third edition), by Wolfston Potter, published by the Yievel Brethin Primers, 1870

King Vigo the Magnificent (Vigo Magnificens, rex Brittonum)

The Kernish kingdoms at the dawn of the eighth century had only recently been unified, ruled by widowed Queen Maud of Angeus. Her son Vigo ascended to the throne upon her death in 720, shortly after the Catumagin Accord which ended hostilities and established an official border with the Kingdom of the Franks.

[Recall that the region now known as Kernow had at the time many names, including Armorica, Brittania and Dumnonia (this taken from the region in Albion whence the Britons had emigrated); the modern name dates from the eleventh century when the House of Kernow allied with the Normans to take the throne.]

King Vigo is remembered chiefly for spearheading the reconquest of Devon, which had been under the sole authority of the Kingdom of Wessex since the final defeat at Gaulford in 656 (although much of Cornwall had never entirely submitted to English rule). He had married Guinnumar, daughter to King David I of Wynneth, and had managed to secure Welsh assistance with the invasion.

After over two decades of intermittent warfare with Wessex and the victory at Petherton, Vigo was crowned King of Greater Devon (rex MagnΟΠ DumnoniΟΠ), unifying Devon and Kernow. He had at least three children: among them were Cadern the Doubter, who would become king upon Vigo's death in 761, and his daughter Iseld, who later became the first patron of the Brethin of Tremonnow, and for whom the Iseld House in Bristow is named.

/ £o peΥàΟΑaw Υ¨…¦jiΥàΟΑants deΥ¨l…Σnst ÄoΥàju Ä | pi i ΥàspalaΥàtso ne Υ¨ Äj…¦n Υàdajnt/

20m Janver

- in translation, from the collected correspondence of Absolon Mortenszen, playwright and commentator in Alburgh, Denmark

[Penned the morning of the 18th of March, 1560 NascentiΟΠ]

To the Good Sir Anscon d'Aupont,

I hope you are well recovered from the ague of which you spoke in your last missive; I need not remind you to consider leaving the city until the bulk of the trading has ended. One never knows what foreign ills merchants may bring, as my grandfather could have attested [1]βÄΠ

The town remains as dull since you departed as it was before we met, you will be pleased to hear. In point of fact, even the decent lodging-house on the strand is poorer for having banned all the interesting people in town; perhaps it speaks poorly of me that they yet allow me entrance, though I will at least avail myself of the views while I can.

My dear brother (you may remember his shoes) is celebrating the birth of his daughter in the usual way; I continue to be astonished Beatrys consented to marry him, though she has been managing to escape him and the baby often enough to visit myself and Clara. We, you will note, have not yet succumbed to the child-bearing impulses the new friar insists we all harbour within.

There is a new friar at the hostel, less talkative and (if you will believe my wife) less handsome than Daniel. More than anything he reminds me of the moralising mentor one sees in the dreadful morality plays they still show down in the city [2]; he seems the sort to dream of visiting Rome and upon arriving declare it insufficiently Christian! One supposes Lorenzo's new Machines of Helios sculpture about which you droned on at me for hours might send the good man directly into an apoplexy, which would at least be entertainingβÄΠ

The new play is a mild success [3], despite the inclement weather rather betraying the fact that Alburgh is not, to my regret, Barcelon. Clara has had marvellous fun with the new fabrics and dyes; please accept my sincerest gratitude for the billrod [4], although it is far too expensive to use regularly.

I leave with you my kindest regards,

A. Mortenszen

βÄî

[1] Records suggest Absolon's grandfather lost his wife and at least two brothers in the Second Great Dying.

[2] Presumably Copenhagen; although manuscripts of the mentioned plays have not survived, similar opinions are found in other contemporaneous letters.

[3] Mortenszen understates the matter here; his High Society was received so well that the royal family altered the route of their Grand Tour to visit Alburgh and see a performance.

[4] Literally βÄ€beetle-redβÄù, a crimson dye produced from the shell of an insect and imported from Lower Mendeva.

aΟΑief /aΥàΟΑj…¦f/ (DamvaΟΑ: [Υ¨aΟΑ…ΣΥàj…¦f]) enough, sufficient(ly many)

< from Old Boral aΟΑ iev βÄ€enough, the same amountβÄù, analysed as aΟΑ βÄ€toβÄù < Latin ad and iev, stem of ievar βÄ€to compareβÄù (which became Middle Boral yevar βÄ€to measureβÄù before falling into disuse). In fact, the Old Boral phrase is probably not a synchronic development but a direct descendant of Latin adverb adΟΠque βÄ€equally, likewiseβÄù.

Unlike many modifiers of quantity, f.ex. plusour βÄ€severalβÄù, aΟΑief follows its head noun.

I beyeu caffay aΟΑief je Lun por un centuir fair svanir.

26m Janver

from βÄ€An Abridged History of Northern AlbionβÄù by Aidan Donblane, published by Cloddale Primers, 1940 (with commentary by his daughter Anhart, 1980)

βÄΠand thus, with the aid of Harald the Blue, established the Githel Kingdom of Scotland in what had been Pictland. Records of the Picts quickly become scanty, suggesting an acceleration of the encroachment of Githel language and culture from the west, into Murray [1].

With the Northumber conquest of Eidin (modern Edinbrough) in 928, the Kingdom of Dunclothe lost the remaining vestiges of its eastern coast. However, the extended Viking in the east had left many of their eponymous Isles severely underdefended [2], and over the ensuing decades, Dunclothe under Roderick would invade many of them: first Arran in 934, and most importantly, Mann fell in 942.

While the inevitable acrimony this expansion engendered with both the Irish kingdoms and with Denmark [3] is beyond the scope of this book, we shall briefly examine its effects on Northumber, on the new Scotland, and on Wynneth to the southwest.

Relations with the Kingdom of Northumber cooled from the moment Edinbrough fell; the common enemy both states had in the Norrish (recall the conquest of Yorwick) presumably helped speed the process along. According to the relevant copies of the English Chronicle, the bride of King Radulf, who succeeded his uncle in 951, was one βÄ€Atre of CheswicβÄù; this is suspected to be the same town as Cumbrian βÄ€KeswickβÄù, although whether she was a daughter of King Roderick is unclearβÄΠ

βÄî

[1] Recent research calls into question the then-prevailing theory that Alba (and in turn Scotland) only experienced significant Norrish influence during and after the eleventh-century migrations. Placename analysis (see F. Berger, 1977) has been correlated with dig sites and suggests that the kingdom had a significant Norrish subtrate from its inception.

[2] This is a simplification: the Norrish ascendancy through the ninth century and the beginning of the tenth had at last reached its peak, and politics further east (as well as the interference of the French Normans) diminished the resources that the Sodrick kings had at their disposal.

[3] That is, the various states making up what are now Denmark and Horth, and so on.

I: The Ancients as Authority

Introduction - From Naples to Rome by way of Madine: the philosophy of the Greeks in the Single Caliphate

2m Fevruer

cordamn /k…î ÄΥàdamnΧ©/ [k…îΥêΥàdam…ên] rigging, tackle (on a sailing vessel)

< from Mediaeval Latin cordaΧ³men βÄ€(collection of) ropesβÄù, from cordoΧ³ βÄ€I tie upβÄù with the nominaliser -men. Probably an independent development, unrelated to the parallel Italian derivation cordame, with the same meaning (although later confluence is probably due to contact).

Y ci annaΟΑ seyon fait sur mar, i fruya soul parmy toΟΑ y cordamn.
While he was away at sea, he would dance around in the rigging.

3m Fevruer

rouvaΟß /ruΥàvats/ (bank) heist, robbery, stick-up

< 17C, from Middle Boral rouvar βÄ€to steal from, to burgleβÄù (now obsolete), borrowed from Germanic; probably Middle Dutch berΟ¥ven βÄ€to rob, to depriveβÄù.

Y fugnt posc y rouvaΟß valois fort ig un hom parmy connois parolar ne Theudesc.

The getaway after the heist would have gone better, had any of them spoken Dutch.

4m Fevruer

First few paragraphs from L'Οâtranger, by Albert Camus. The original French may be found here. An audio recording of the below can be found here.

L'oc jorn, mama es mort. Ben parcanΟß yer, jo saif noc. Lonc y parol campaner meyon receut dell'edifiΟß : .maΟΑr partiΟΑ. .a sevelir souscen. .humour condolendessem. L'ig signfy nien, alor. L'oc pos star yer.

L'edifiΟß a hom veil se situ a Goday, a novant stadion de DamvaΟΑ. Jo montaray sur y vectoir l'hour dou e j'hiembray l'edifiΟß ag ralley. Cossy jo farray surveil toΟΑ y nogt e soulor y repaΟΑr tardessem a souscen. J'ay dou jorn crous rovaΟΑ de my caf e stant y contingent i poΟΑe noc m'en privar. ToΟΑ mod i parisce noc allagr, donc j'ay dit meΟΑes : βÄ€A mey la no's falt.βÄù ; i no's respos. Apres j'ay saut noc deΟ·r le digr. Luy val y sorc me beudar, a loy. Lon hougr i le farra souscen doutr, candon i me verra a cordogl. Oy meΟΑes, tout es un pog com ΟΑa mama no sey mort. Posc nos arreu le seveliΟΑ, malgraΟΑ, i sera un affer prent, oc a digr robaΟΑ d'un accostur reallessem.

Jo so montaΟΑ sur y vectoir l'hour dou. Caye magn calt. J'ay repasc fait ag tarn cas Celest, par usual. Casc gent ayen tendreΟß bempart por mey, e Celest a me dit : βÄ€O n'a maΟΑr for l'un.βÄù. Cant jo parte, il aun me colluΟ·ΟΑ vars y port. Jo so hastaΟΑ com picq n'y fugnt complir, car all'hour final me fale pausar cas Emmanuel por i me prest y capel plat e manc neir. Sull'yam follau y sougl deur plusour monΟΑ.

Jo so cors fin a pie mettr ag vectoir. L'oc hast, l'oc porsief meΟΑes, fus accougl certan coll'eΟß rouΟΑ peyaΟßant, oc puΟΑ a foyail montant, no dign oc lumner rebondiscent dy rout e dy ciel, desig tout j'ay m'assoviΟΑ. A mesur dy sojorn pres enter so jo dormiΟΑ. Cant apres j'ay sveilaΟΑ, jo sta pressaΟΑ a cost d'un soldart ; luy a m'entrovaΟΑ a souriar ΟΑa j'er venuΟΑ de lontan. Jo n'ay dit for : βÄ€Vray.βÄù, desirant besogn plu aΟ·r de parolar.

L'edifiΟß es a just trey stadion dy bourg. J'ay y camin follaΟΑ a pie. J'ay bel desirar mama veir direct, y porter ay me dit ig paravant fale jo rajogn con y surveilour. Dec i no sta ancour pront, j'ay l'attis un moment. Durant ig y porter a me roulaΟΑ e soulor, jo so vas y surveilour encontrar ; l'a me receut ne sy scrittoir. L'es segr mogn de cavel gris, coll'insign martial sur tabr. L'a me sgardaΟΑ profont d'ogl mar. All'ig nos eu man pognaΟΑ, luy mell'un tenent lontemp aΟΑief por ig jo saveus noc le recovrir.

Apres, l'a y summer avant leit eΟΑ a dit : βÄ€Domn Morsaut venoy all'oc edifiΟß deur trey annaΟΑ. Sy redon enter foy de vous.βÄù

Jo creye i me blasma uncos e j'ay me mis a luy explanar, ma l'a m'interront.

βÄ€Vos deyeΟΑ noc raΟßon forgar, bon pavr. J'ay y narraΟΑ leit de vossy maΟΑr ; vos poΟΑeΟΑ noc le fornir ig seyon recas. Luy fale un hom por le curar, eΟΑ untal joun ant untal empley n'a saler y hautessem. ToΟΑ cas final, i sta lonc luy meΟΑes fort allagherrem ac.βÄù

J'ay dit : βÄ€Oc, surveilour.βÄù

L'es continuaΟΑ con : βÄ€Vos comprenΟΑ l'au eΟß bon amig, eΟß gent de sull'eld con ig i poΟΑe amanΟß communal aΟ·r, ci d'autr annaΟΑ. Vos soΟΑ joun demay e l'aus star annoyaΟΑ con vous soul.

Cossy sta l'ig. Candon i hamta cas mey, mama passa ci hour ne me gasant lon viscr. Atorn y preu semman all'edifiΟß, i plora cos frequent. Ma just mama n'au ancour s'ig habituaΟΑ. Posc plusour monΟΑ, i fos ploraΟΑ ΟΑa l'er emmenaΟΑ dell'edifiΟß. All'oc, tant, a i s'habituaΟΑ. Por ig parmy n'ay jo apen le vis a mesur dell'annaΟΑ derran. E tan dec le veir usaus my Sol enter ; lon ig contenent, y defy por y vectoir yembr, y moneΟΑ contar, eΟΑ y dou hour fallent ag sojorn passar.

Y surveilour a ancour me parolar, ma jo luy dona plu magn cur. Soulor l'a me dit : βÄ€Or, jo figur ig vos veleΟΑ desirar vossy maΟΑr veir ?βÄù

J'ay me leyaΟΑ lon alcun respos e l'a me menaΟΑ vars y port. Y ci scagl descendent, l'es lanΟßaΟΑ all'explanation.

βÄ€Nos eu le fis ne nossy picq mortuer. Por ig ci autr n'ain angoist may. Candon casc edifiΟßour fol y sougl, ci autr dienn tis par dou ben trey jorn. Certan l'ig forc novel trevagl por toΟΑ gent.βÄù

Nos eu un gardin trasaΟΑ oyon la sta magn segr veil, dignt a picq cougl. Il son tait tandic nos approismau. Portant, reΟΑr nous, lour y collocq son relanΟßaΟΑ. Il sceusn apparir com parrochet cloistaΟΑ, sou manton gros. Y surveilour es stoppaΟΑ avant y port d'un picq immoubr.

βÄ€Ac vos laisc jo, Segr Morsaut. Οêa vos fal uncos de mey, vos porreΟΑ me trovar ne my cambr. Nos suggereu vossy maΟΑr sevelir l'hour deg all'aubon. Nos creyeu vos farray surveil jusc ig cossy, com vos vouΟΑ desirar. Uncos final : vossy maΟΑr, lonc sull'amig, a pariΟΑ y desir declarar an seviel religious. J'ay l'eΟß tal en arrancaΟΑ, ma jo desira vos en addocir.βÄù

J'ay graΟß luy dar. Mama, a loy de star atheos, n'aye un cogtaΟßon soul pos a religion sy viΟΑ enter.

5m Fevruer

fondamen /Υ¨f…îndaΥàm…¦n/ basis, fundamentals, base, basics

also fondamen corpore /Υ¨k…î ÄpoΥà Äe/ chemical element(s), periodic table

< 15C, borrowed from Latin fundΡ¹men βÄ€foundation, basisβÄù, probably via French fondamen (with vowel shift by analogy with the inherited fonder βÄ€to found, to set upβÄù). Note the final stress due to borrowing; in direct descendants we instead see scamn < exΡ¹men βÄ€swarmβÄù, for example. Sense in chemistry (literally, βÄ€material basisβÄù) from early 19C, though attested in various Romance languages from the late 18C.

Fal jo t'instroy l'enter fondamen, pre tu attent uncos novel.
I must teach you the basics before you try something new.
/Υàfal  £o t…ΣnΥàst Ä…îj l…¦nΥàt…¦ Ä Υ¨f…îndaΥàm…¦n | Υàp Äe tu aΥàt…¦nt …ΣnΥàk…îz noΥàv…¦l/

Y fondamen corpore n'aug son aquifex eΟΑ ignifex.
The elements found in water are hydrogen and oxygen.
/i Υ¨f…îndaΥàm…¦n Υ¨k…î ÄpoΥà Äe Υànoj s…în Υ¨akwiΥàf…¦ks eΈΗ Υ¨ajniΥàf…¦ks/

/Υàfal  £o t…ΣnΥàst Ä…îj l…¦nΥàt…¦ Ä Υ¨f…îndaΥàm…¦n | Υàp Äe tu aΥàt…¦nt …ΣnΥàk…îz noΥàv…¦l/

Y fondamen corpore n'aug son aquifex eΟΑ ignifex.
The elements found in water are hydrogen and oxygen.
/i Υ¨f…îndaΥàm…¦n Υ¨k…î ÄpoΥà Äe Υànoj s…în Υ¨akwiΥàf…¦ks eΈΗ Υ¨ajniΥàf…¦ks/

generally-proven

oho, elements! what elements are known already? what are their names?

  Great question! :D I imagine by, say, 1950 at latest all naturally occuring elements had been discovered (in real life the last such was rhenium in 1925), but I have only fixed the Borlish names of seventeen of them so far. Here we go:

ConnuΟΑ des aubon dell'hour: / Known since antiquity:

argent - silver
aur - gold
far - iron
cuivr - copper
plom - lead
stan - tin (the above six all from Latin)
azauc - mercury (from Morrove Arabic via Portingale azouco)
brunstan - brimstone [1] (from Old English brynstΡ¹n βÄ€sulfur, lit. burn-stoneβÄù, often incorrectly linked to stan βÄ€tinβÄù.

Aer: / Airs: [2]

aquifex - aquifex [3], from Scholastic Latin aquifex βÄ€water-makerβÄù.
coronal - coronal [4], from Latin cor≈çnΡ¹lis βÄ€of the crownβÄù, referring to the corona of the Sun, where it was first detected.

ignifex - ignifex [5], from S. Latin ΡΪgnifex βÄ€fire-makerβÄù.

muria - muria [6], from Latin muria βÄ€brine, salt liquorβÄù, via muriatic acid.

xanthal - xanthal [7], from Ancient Greek ΈΨαΨΑΈΫΈΗœ¨œ² (xanthΟ≥s) βÄ€yellow, goldenβÄù, with ending by analogy with coronal.

ClaΟΑ dur: / Stiffs [8]:

adamant - adamant [9], from Latin adamans βÄ€unbreakable, hard as steelβÄù.
jast - jast [10], from S. Latin [1] iastum βÄ€zincβÄù, originally from Hindi ύΛ€ύΛΗύΞçύΛΛύΛΨ (jastaΧ³) βÄ€zincβÄù.
machovin - machovine [11], named for the Machow mine in Poland near where it was first isolated.
natron - natron [12], from A. Greek ΈΫΈ·œ³œ¹ΈΩΈΫ (nΟ≠tron) βÄ€nitreβÄù via Arabic and French, among others.

βÄî

[1] i.e., sulphur. [2] Gases. [3-7] hydrogen, helium, oxygen, chlorine, neon.

[8] Solids. [9-12] titanium, zinc, strontium, sodium

fler /fl…¦ Ä/ smell, scent, odour

< 14C, borrowed from early Middle French flair βÄ€odour, smell (especially in hunting)βÄù, a deverbal of flairier βÄ€to smell ofβÄù, from (with dissimilation) Latin frΡ¹gr≈ç βÄ€I reek ofβÄù. Any connection to Latin flagr≈ç βÄ€I burn, I blazeβÄù is disputed. Often used in the construction fler aΟ·r a βÄ€to smell ofβÄù.

Y roum enter au fler a cendr eΟΑ acier e cogr.

7m Fevruer
Y Preyer βÄ€Nossy PaΟΑrβÄù - The Lord's Prayer

In the modern day, this is one of the most widely-used translations of the Lord's Prayer in Borlish churches, coming from a popular sixteenth-century Bible translation.

Nossy PaΟΑr stant ne ciel
/Υàn…îst ÄβÄΩi ΥàpaΟΑ ÄΧ© Υàstant ne Υàtsj…¦l/
sagntfiaΟΑ sey ty nom.
/s…¦jntΥàfjaΈΗ Υàsi ti Υàn…îm/
Vin ty reyaum, advin ty graΟΑ
/Υàv…Σn ti  ÄiΥàjom | adΥàv…Σn ti Υàg ÄaΈΗ/
cossy ne tar e ne ciel.
/koΥàsi ne Υàta Ä e ne Υàtsj…¦l/
Don nos l'oc jorn nossy pan cottidian
/Υàd…în no l…îk Υà £…î Än Υàn…îst ÄβÄΩi Υàpan Υ¨kotiΥàdjan/
e pardon nos nossy deut*
/a pa ÄΥàd…în no Υàn…îst ÄβÄΩi Υàdawt/
com nos lou pardau nos deyent.
/k…îm Υàno lu pa ÄΥàdo no diΥàj…¦nt/
E dug nos noc vars tentation

ma descaΟΑen nos de mal.
/pi Υ¨d…¦skaΥàΟΑ…¦n no de Υàmal/

The doxology appears in most mainstream Borland churches:

Ke* tien* es y regn, y possenΟß eΟΑ y gloir, siecr siempr ; Amen.
/ke Υàtj…¦n …¦z i Υà Äijn | i poΥàs…¦nts …¦ΈΗ i Υàgl…îj Ä | Υàsjek ÄΧ© Υàsj…¦mp ÄΧ© βÄ• aΥàm…¦n/

*These words are archaic or obsolete in their contemporary definitions in Modern Borlish:

deut βÄ€sinβÄù < past participle of deiΧàr βÄ€to oweβÄù. Displaced by synonym peccaΟΑ, and now usually restricted to adjectival senses βÄ€due, owed, necessaryβÄù.
ke βÄ€for, since, becauseβÄù < from Latin quia with the same meaning. Already old-fashioned at the time this was written, it now survives only in certain expressions. 
tien βÄ€thine, yoursβÄù < formed after mien βÄ€mineβÄù < Latin meum, now also obsolete. The modern construction would be tell'un βÄ€your oneβÄù.

9m Fevruer

cainΟß a ney d'aost /Υàkents a Υàni daΥà…îst/ fortuitous coincidence, once-in-a-blue-moon chance

< first attested 18C, literally βÄ€(an) August snow eventβÄù, usually referring to a positive occurence. The first word analysable synchronically as cair βÄ€fall, happenβÄù plus the nominaliser -nΟß, but probably just directly from Late Latin cadentia βÄ€result, occurenceβÄù. The other words are simple descendants of Latin nivem βÄ€snowβÄù and augustus βÄ€AugustβÄù.

Nossy nuΟßal ant regnbou doblaΟΑ ny ciel sta un cainΟß a ney d'aost.
Our wedding having a double rainbow in the sky was a happy coincidence.
/Υàn…îst Ä i niΥàtsal ant  ÄijmΥàbu doΥàblaΈΗ ni Υàtsj…¦l sta …Σn Υàkents a Υàni daΥà…îst/

10m Fevruer

in translation from An Overview of Revitalist Art in Sixteenth-Century Rome, by Safourian AmigΟ≤t, published 1870 by Prindor NicΟΣs

By 1550, Rome and the surrounding Italian states had for the most part played catch-up over the last century, with respect to the study of Classical mathematics and science. Having neither the Novomundine explorations of Vascony, Kernow and Morrack to fund patronage of the new inventors, nor the very new medical advances in Denmark (that is, in the Dane Concert) to incite renewed interrogation of Classical medical texts, the various Italian states were further disadvantaged by their unchecked monarchs; and of course, nowhere more so than Rome herself, seat of a papacy that at the time still governed the spritual lives of most of Europe south of Venise.

This is not the place to discuss the causes and immediate political effects of the Great Fracture; for our purposes it suffices to note that, while on paper the Pope in Rome remained an absolute ruler (along with political dominion over several smaller Italian and German provinces), his temporal powers were in practice much diminished. In particular, the venerable prohibitions over blasphemous arts and philosophical publications within the city limits were swftly dissolved by the new Triunvirato, who ordered the obtention of great minds from around Europe; for example, this led to the establishment of the first Brethin House in all of Italy. They undertook this course of action, one assumes, less out of a sincere zeal for scientific discovery (although the personal correspondence of Lord of Houses Josforo Desmono suggests a passing interest in the subject) than a wish to introduce various new political factions into Rome in opposition to the See.

The influx of artists surrounded by scholars of antiquity led to an outpouring of artworks taking inspiration from the great legends of the Greek and Roman pantheons, dubbed in retrospect the Rivitalisto period. Apart from the many paintings and stories (usually retellings of previous myths), probably the most well-known work from this time is I Macchine di Helio (The Machines of Helios) by sculptor Lorenzo dell'Acchedia, a stylised depiction of Helios the Sun and the six known planets gyring around him. The question of exactly where Lorenzo heard about the new astronomical theory remains open: he certainly had enough opportunity in the various city foraβÄΠ

My ci fraΟΑr e mey tegnsm toΟΑ my camis ne roug fosc.
With my brothers I dyed all my shirts dark red.
/Υàmy tsi Υàf ÄaΟΑ ÄΧ© e Υàmi ΥàtijnzmΧ© Υàt…îΈΗ mi kaΥàm…Σz ne  Äuj Υàf…îx/

12m Fevruer

sevrar /seΥàv Äa Ä/ [s…ΣΥàv Ä…ëΥê] to separate, to split up, to wean (a child)

also mission sevrant /miΥàsj…în seΥàv Äant/ injective function, one-to-one map

< 11C; from Vulgar Latin sΡ™per≈ç βÄ€I divide, I distinguishβÄù, an alteration of Latin sΡ™par≈ç with the same meaning. Possibly via Old French sevrer βÄ€to separate, to severβÄù.

Sense in mathematics from 1830, in the fundamental work of mathematician Marc Ausbagn, possibly influenced by the existing term mission covrent βÄ€surjectionβÄù, a translation of the Kentish βÄ€covrend (map)βÄù.

Un mission sevrant sevr y sunt ; y ci destegnt accaΟßaΟΑ a destegnt.
An injective function separates; distinct inputs are sent to distinct outputs.
/…Σn miΥàsj…în seΥàv Äant Υàsev ÄβÄΩi Υàs…Σnt | i Υàtsi d…¦sΥàtijnt Υ¨akaΥàtsaΈΗ a d…¦sΥàtijnt/

13m Fevruer

The bookseller of Hawthorn Street was a handsome man of perhaps thirty years, and the first time Frederick met him, on a balmy summer evening in 1755, he had (by dint of some quick gossip with his sister) learnt two things about the man: first, he sold books of a higher quality and a lower price than anyone else in Sowbruck [1], or maybe even all of London, and yet always seemed as impeccably maintained as any professional in the borough (despite the reportedly small size of his household); and second, he would vanish for days at a time with little warning and still less in the way of excuses.

Fred had been privately convinced that the man was discreetly employed by the Lord Mayor's office, or by one of the better-concealed deparments of Her Majesty's administration - though bookseller seemed an odd role to foist upon a spy.

taken from the tale A Door, Once Opened by Clarissa Bellamy, published 1920 by Sagittarius Editions

Mr Talbot: Your most recent book has proven remarkably popular, especially for what many would argue is a parachthon [2] story, a genre which hasn't seen much critical success in recent years. It is also somewhat of a departure for yourself, having published primarily in the lovetale [romance] market until now. What inspired you to write this book?
Mrs Bellamy: In some ways I see it as a natural extension of the character development I had worked on before. Really, I had wanted for a while to read para-stories which focus on how people react to the fantastic around them, rather than great battles and politics and mysterious xenozones [aliens]. In the end my husband simply asked βÄ€Well then, why don't you write one yourself?βÄù. So I did.

Mr Talbot: You have mentioned that the setting required a lot of research to portray accurately. What drew you to the London of the late eighteenth century?
Mrs Bellamy: I must continually thank my sister-in-law Heleth, who is a historical scholar at the New Lyceum; her interest and counsel were both invaluable to me. Though she focuses more on the Democratic Wars themselves, I found myself more interested in the aftermath, with London a newly independent state, surrounded by uneasy allies and vulture-like enemies, waiting for a single misstep.

Mr Talbot: How would you describe the main moral or theme of your work?
Mrs Bellamy: The most apparent image to me, as I was writing the trevold [novel], was about reciprocity, the idea that any relationship between two people, two places - or indeed two times - is a mutual one. In fact, the title only came after I had finished the first draft; there's a line in one of Davis' plays that goes βÄ€A door, once opened, can be stepped through in either directionβÄù. Aside from referring directly to some of the tale's events, of course, I think that quote is emblematic of the relationship between Frederick and the bookseller.

questions from a 1921 colloquium [interview] with writer Mrs Clarissa Bellamy for the Trevold Weekly

βÄî
[1] A borough in London, in much the same area as Southwark.
[2] A genre of writing including most of what we would call fantasy and science-fiction, although the central examples here tend to involve a secondary world of some description. The first story usually attributed to the genre is Saxon writer Elsebeth Sneider's Through a Black Mirror (about which see the 11th January post).

senglottar /Υ¨s…¦n.gloΥàta Ä/ [Υ¨s…¦≈΄.gl äΥàt…ëΥê] to sob, to weep, to lament

< 12C, verbal derivation from senglot βÄ€sobβÄù (previously we have evidence of constructions such as senglot fair βÄ€to make sobsβÄù), from Vulgar Latin singluttus, a blend of Latin gluttioΧ³ βÄ€I swallowβÄù and singultus βÄ€hiccupβÄù.

Con y fin dell'ig parform l'er senglottant ingournaΟΑ.
By the end of the play he was sobbing uncontrollably.
/Υ¨k…în y Υàf…Σn deΥàlaj pa ÄΥàf…î Äm l…¦ Ä Υ¨s…¦ngloΥàtant Υà…Σngu ÄΥànaΈΗ/

##

16m Fevruer

from The Story of Borland (Y Stoir d'Istr Boral), a school history textbook published 1982 by Cordin Editions

Let us compare how the fall of Rome affected Borland and Britain (when we talk about this part of history, remember that Britain refers to all of Albion, not just the Briton states, and not all of them, either). The end of Roman life in Britain occurred quickly during the first three decades of the fifth century. By contrast, although a lot of people did leave Borland for the continent during this time, it seems like the way Borland was a little more independent from the rest of the Empire helped it. In fact, there is recent evidence that some Romans left Britain to Borland, especially from the major cities of Londinium [A19] and Camulodunum [Οê17] (now London and the Merch town of Conster).

At this time, Germanic people, particularly the Angles and Saxons, came from the mainland of Europe and settled in Borland and Britain. In Britain there was a complete shift away from Roman culture over the fifth and sixth century; the Angles established East England [D15] and Northumber [B8] in the east, while the Saxons founded Wessex and Markland in the west.

The story in Borland is similar, but less drastic. Much of the south and west coast was settled by Angle immigrants through the fifth century, where they founded Angland [J11], Southbar [L13] and several more minor kingdoms. At the same time, the informal and decentralised political structures throughout the self-described Roman towns was changing to adapt to the new condition. Several towns in the south were abandoned completely to emigration or assimilation into the Angle kingdoms, and most of the more urbanised east was unified under the rule of Prase Victor Rossetus as the dioces boralis, the Diocese of Boral.

/…Σl Υ¨figiΥà Äan aj Υàti v…¦l Υ¨l…¦jiΥàΟΑants Υ¨benoΥàtsa Ä/

19m Fevruer

text from a steeplecard [1], sent on the 3rd of March 1822 and addressed to Matilda Torricks

Dearest sister Matty -

Wishing you a belated new years [2]. Office closed until this morning with the snow and the municipal holiday. Jane says I am to be secluded for at least a fortnight hence with the baby imminent. Anxious to be presentable for the melee [3] before the 75th anniversary celebrations start next month. We hear the whole south Tamside is to be transformed.

Do visit soon. Ebbasham [4] is not so distant after all.

- Your sister Vallie

excerpt: The Communication Toolset (El Herramente de CommunicazΟ≤n) by Joan BensaΟ≠d, published 1951 by Quaterno TheorΟ©tico a Cordova

In the usual way of things, multiple nations claim the title of the first steeplemesh [5]. The very earliest incarnations of the idea arose during the Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century, but these tended to be single lines of temporary signal towers set up to span terrain that made it too costly to simply send a rider with a message. Moreover, these were set up only to transmit simple messages; for example, an hour to launch an attack.

There were two major developments required for the first true steeplemeshes. First, the means of communication. Early wartime towers usually relied on lampfire and thus were reliable only at night, or else a collection of patterned flags, which were usable at day but required a shorter distance between watchtowers [6]. The two-arm system, allowing up to nine separate states to be transmitted, was probably first tested in the Willem town of Thynen [7], where the church's weathervane was reportedly commandeered to send the first lines of the Pater Noster to the neighbouring town Bost. (Hence, probably, the name βÄ€steeplepostβÄù.)

Second, the encoding of a true alphabet, rather than a fixed sets of messages. One early Andalusian attempt (from Cordova to MΟΓlaga, usually given as the first line connecting two major cities, and essentially just formalising the signal system that had existed for the duration of their conflict with Morrack [8]) used a variant of the ancient Polybius square as discussed in Chapter 3, adapted for Arabic script. The first modern steeplescript was developed in Cracow [9] by engineer Sigmund Adamich and his son Andrei as part of the royal initiative to connect Cracow to Bitom, using a set of four symbols, traditionally referred to as βÄ€dot, dash, bar and hookβÄù.

βÄîβÄ™

[1] Very much like a telegram, although somewhat less efficiently terse.

[2] The year in this world begins in March, not January, as was the case in ours across much of Europe until the sixteenth century.

[3] A high-society term for βÄ€the tonβÄù, or βÄ€SocietyβÄù; that is, the gentry.

[4] A small town within the borders of independent London, on the site of real-life Epsom.

[5] An optical telegraphy network.

[6] Compare our semaphore system for boats.

[7] Tienen, Belgium. [8] Morroco. [9] KrakΟ≥w.

22m Fevruer

from The Story of Borland (Y Stoir d'Istr Boral), a school history textbook published 1982 by Hendric Tambour et al. for Cordin Editions

There was a steady influx of Christians into the Diocese of Boral (and later the First Kingdom of Borland) from the latter years of the fifth century, well into the seventh. Early on many of these were Roman or even Celtic Christians displaced by the Germanic migrations, and by the time of Pope Mercury II in 590N, Rome had taken a particular strategic influence in the small kingdom, which at the time was on the defensive against a stronger Southbar [1].

In the first instance, the new Kingdom was viewed as a vital Christian outpost from which Rome could send missionaries east and west to aid the conversion of Albion and the Danes. Thus the early Borlish monasteries and schools of theology were much more outward-focused than the comparatively insular Irish groups. It was to this end that the Golfhaun sanctuary was first erected (with the Latin name Asylum ΟÜstuarii [2], which contains the oldest surviving church building in Borland.

The opinions of the Papacy regarding Borland were as inconstant as the Italian lands it claimed and the men themselves. Many also saw the distant land as a convenient way to make sure inconvenient elements were far away and unable to make trouble. In this way, any given Borland monastery might house semi-heretical monks βÄ€entrusted with the conversion of the pagansβÄù, or perhaps several awkwardly-Hellenic men with a suspicious sympathy to one of the Greek heresies.

βÄî-

[1] A medieval Anglian kingdom located along the south coast of Borland.

[2] The Estuary Haven, since it is situated on the large island Nestu in the LabbaΟΑ Sound (upriver of which lies the capital DamvaΟΑ).

##

2m MarΟß

from The Latin Republics: Jewels under Tyrian Standards by Jacob Trelaune, published by the Yievle Brethin Primers, 1902

Part VI: The Republic of Provence

It would be ahistorical to claim that the republics of the Middlesea were brought about due to the unusually totalist monarchies they neighboured, or even to stake out the somewhat more popular position that those Italian monarchies grew so tyrannical as a reaction to the republics. Nonetheless, one case in which the link between republicanism and totalism is undeniable is with the Republic of Provence.

In the first half of the second millenium, Provence was first a county in the West Francian Kingdom, and following the Emergency at Dijon, the largest province (so to speak) in the Kingdom of Burgundy. The county was elevated under the house of Savoy to a duchy in the thirteenth century, and as the direct link between the capital at Geneva and all of Middlesea sea-trade, it became known as lo panier de Borgogne (the Burgundian breadbasket).

However, with the Novamundine landfall in 1471, and the accompanying Ascendancy of Vascony under Ambrose III, piracy (which had long plagued the very coast which made Provence so profitable, and which was the scourge of the venerable republics of Jeans and Venise, among others) thrived off the general prosperity. In Marsella, the raiding became so frequent that many treated it more as an occupation, and many of the wealthy fled to safer pastures inland.
 
The aid requests presented to Rudolf VIII went largely unanswered. Charitably, one might say he was distracted by the ongoing Bavarn conflict, and the religious difficulty attendant to being the northwesternmost polity yet answerable to Rome. But it is generally allowed that the young king was simply concerned only with himself (or by extension his many lovers) and with resplendent lakeside banquets.

Things came to a head in the last years of the fifteenth century. Lord Daniel Menton of the house of AmigΟ≤t had secured the aid of King Marc V of France (France having finally reached reconciliation with Normandy and avoiding the tangle that was the Albion Wars of Fealty), on the advice of the foundling-general Guillen daus Sanz. Thus prepared, he and several other noble families declared independence from Geneva on the twenty-first of June, 1497 (which to this day is a national holiday in Provence). Though this declaration would not be formally recognised until Burgundy's defeat two years later at the Battle of MargΟ®s, it was this event which cemented House AmigΟ≤t's position as primus inter pares in the new republic.

At least, that is, for the next eighteen yearsβÄΠ

βÄ€For surely the Lord has not in his infinite majesty ordained Rome, the ancient seat of the Christ-killers themselves, to be the most holy city in this world? Heaven is high above and the Holy Land is yet distant, to the clockmaker [1] as it is to the pilgrim's steps.βÄù

The years between Pope Paul I/XI's (Whether or not to include the predecessor Roman Popes, or indeed some other count including any of the antipope lines, was a controversial issue for much of the thirteenth century, so both appellations are seen. The reformist Damien I established that they should not be included, although Paul himself usually did.) first announcing the establishment of a sedes sanctissima in Augsburg and the eventual capitulation of Rome are frustratingly ill-documented, for obvious reasons. He's a handsome man, face unlined by cares, at least tonight - if you look close you can see the creases at the corners of his eyes, the slight mar of a line on either side of his lips. His smile is carefully stupid.

Ma j'ay lontemp zest aut, ig Luc Osgaut a dou character sevraΟΑ. Frequent es i y fol avant l'aucoum eΟΑ un scandal jacquottaΟΑ, ma Osgaut Corporal es parfait gournaΟΑ e profit faint demay por no star for y molin moneΟΑ prement y viΟΑ a libertin soutenent. Sey l'a astuΟßour y starchessem acatabr ja raΟΑunaΟΑ - l'a vray Agatha Rat, bempart valent ne bon mont - ben l'idiocy e masq.
But I've suspected for a long time that Lucas Osgood is two people. He's been a fool in public and a scandal often enough, but Osgood Corporal is too tightly-run, too profitable to be the money-making machine that feeds a libertine's habits. Either he has the best advisors any man ever assembled - he does have Agatha Ratte, which ought to count for a lot - or the stupidity is a mask.

Por ig me pon jo ac, or.
Which is why I'm here, really.

Alcun hom sceurn jamay Osgaut faucar - alcun hom a y lacq idiot penetraΟΑ dessou. ParcanΟß la no's nien a trovar, l'oc es fort plausibr. Me parisc attendr es prouf dy soldart por collocquour ne Vanda, ma mey jo so pavr a Arcady, eΟΑ ac ageu nos un pog different. Brutal a bas, ne mejan certan toΟΑ cas.
Nobody's ever been able to crack Osgood βÄ™ nobody's gotten under the idiot lacker. There might be nothing underneath, for all we know. I suspect it's a rite of passage for Vandham reporters, but I'm an Arcadian boy, and we do things a little differently here. Less brutally, at least in some ways.

E jo so magn feliΟß, jo crey.
And my luck is very good, I think.

14m MarΟß

excerpts in translation from the diary of NadΟ†lla fi Carol, daughter of the town reverend in Santrafew, an early trading post and latterly a significant settlement in the territory of New Provence.

1590 NascentiΟΠ

Monday 17th April

Penelope told me this afternoon that there's a nobleman at the docks' lodginghouse, some French fop who's run away from his grandmother. Honestly I'm surprised he survived the crossing; not every such man would last two months with Marco's crew, especially with their new Libyan friends. They'd never have let him hide from work belowdecks, no matter how generous the bribe he gave them for discreet passageβÄΠ

βÄΠmoreover, said grandmother is allegedly the Countess [1] d'Avosche! One cannot imagine the scandal of the heir apparent's flight (for he was, we are to understand, to inherit everything) has not ruined her influence over King David's court. No-one has any idea why this mysterious newcomer should have exchanged a chasteau on the Rhin for our humble blankpine rooms on the Becouin.

Wednesday 19th April

Along with the usual bevy of whichever goods the Concert wishes to foist upon the MastoiseΧÄt (Father believes nothing will do as much good for our diplomacy as sending more bronze and iron, or at least more men to mine and smith any we can dig up for ourselves), Marco was good enough to stockpile the Marsella weekly papers, so we have news of the old country for the first time all year. Penelope wants me to read them all to her properly tomorrow morning, but a first glance suggests at least that our heretical cousins haven't yet found themselves on the wrong end of a short ropeβÄΠ

[1] This is NadΟ†lla's mistake; Avosche was by this time a duchy.

##

23m MarΟß

from Handbook of Natural Theory, by Jason Ridley and others, published 1967 in Markland English by the Lincon Collegiate Editors

In mechanics, ≈™culux (commonly referred to simply as light) is a form of propagant lux that carries energy through the barrick extent, most commonly experienced as light or heat. In vacuo, ≈™culux moves at the normal speed, commonly written ≈™; in more common units, at a rate of 300 dichilomecon per second.

The word ≈™culux is a chimer (to the certain dismay of our more classically-minded readers!): the first half is related to such words as ≈™cumenical βÄ€of the secular worldβÄù, and here is intended to denote βÄ€universal, the entire extent ofβÄù. However, its origin is in fact the Greek ΈΩαΦΑΈΚΈΩœÖΈΦΈΒΈΫΈΙΈΚœ¨œ² βÄ€the whole worldβÄù, literally βÄ€the inhabited placesβÄù. The suffix -lux, coming directly from Latin lux βÄ€lightβÄù, is more familiar, perhaps from the new threshold mills and their unsettling martial applications. It is important to keep in mind that luxes aren't just the scary invisible rays from a blacklayer film, but include all the colours of the rainbow.

We conventionally grade ≈™culux by its period, since we interact with it in many different ways.

Light with the shortest periods are scattering, having enough energy to interact with atoms and give them a deficit of plenty; we say the atom becomes deficient. All shortform and penetrating ≈™culux is scattering, and depending on convention some further surblavic light may be included. The maximal period of light which can in theory scatter an atomic base is around 320 trihekimecon, which is still shorter than any visible light. High exposure to light of such brief period is dangerous, and fortunately the atmosphere blocks the majority of such luxes that the Sun emits (although not all, as a sunburn will attest!).

Y livrer a Rout Hawthorn sta segr formous parcanΟß trent annaΟΑ eld, eΟΑ y preu veg oyon Frederick l'encontrau, un crevoscr preuΟß ny staΟΑ de 1755, ja l'au (a mesur de raut rouΟΑar con sy sour) dou vertaΟΑ appris dy segr : y primer, ig i vende livr d'un qualtaΟΑ hautessem e noc obstroint un preΟß boncoppessem a toΟΑ hom autr ne Sowbruck, ben Londr meΟΑes, ma parisce a sgart calscon soutenuΟΑ cos immachiabr com casc vocationnaΟΑ dy muncef (degnant ig lonc y mont sy seut majon fosn picq) ; y doussem, ig i sperisce par ci jorn plusour con prevart magr e fort men por explanation.

El lirrero a Cay Hawthorn yera un hombre appuesto que teniΧ¹a trienta anΧ¹os quisaΧ¹, y quando Frederick attopoΧ¹lo a primera vez, una nochina suave de verano 1755, saviΧ¹a pueste un rumoscitino con su ermana dos hechos sorre el hombre : primer loar, vendiΧ¹a lirros de mayor calidaΧ¹ y stesso de menor precio que hace nadie a Sowbruck, o bien todo Londre, pero conduciΧ¹ase tan immacquiablemente como todos los vocazonistes del coceyo (malla su casa fuera pequina) ; segundo loar, sumiΧ¹ase diΧ¹as seguidas casi espontΟΓneamente y a fondo sin pretexto.

The bookseller of Hawthorn Street was a handsome man of perhaps thirty years, and the first time Frederick met him, on a balmy summer evening in 1755, he had (by dint of some quick gossip with his sister) learnt two things about the man: first, he sold books of a higher quality and a lower price than anyone else in Sowbruck, or maybe even all of London, and yet always seemed as impeccably maintained as any professional in the borough (despite the reportedly small size of his household); and second, he would vanish for days at a time with little warning and still less in the way of excuses.

    questions taken from the January 2003 N scitation documents for the course 1471 - 1517: War, Peace and Exploration after the Novomundine Landfall, 37st avenue in Concurrence History at the Tremonnow Open School (formerly the Brethin Mesh Institute).

Question 1 (thirty minutes)

Discuss the effect of three new foodstuffs introduced to North Africa and Europe via either (i) the Morrack involvement in Western Cappatia, or (ii) the New Navarre Enterprise.

Question 2 (ninety minutes)

βÄ€Perhaps we should have sent for a Kentish witness! They might learn the subtle craft of ending the wars they start.βÄù

- General Guillen daus Sanz on the eve of the Battle of MargΟ®s, 1499

Answer any two of the three questions related to the above quote.

(i) A colleague of yours commends daus Sanz and Lord Menton for achieving independence in just two years, noting that in Albion the conflict lasted for forty-six. In what ways might you dispute their characterisation of events?

(ii) The quote suggests that, although Burgundy was not directly involved in the Albion Wars of Fealty, knowledge of the conflict was widespread in Europe. Examine the opinion of any relevant courts (for example, that of Ambrose's Vascony) towards Kent under the Two Henries, and how these impacted Kent's standing between Albion and Gaul.

(iii) To what extent can we say that the Provincial Secession from the Kingdom of Burgundy inspired the development of the principles of Vicinity? You may wish to consider the writings of Jaume fi Gustau advocating for the Doccian language, and, if you have time, compare the Secession to the Emergency at Dijon in 1170 (although this falls somewhat outside the bounds of this course).

    from One Quiet Day by Roberta Bluemore (published in parts from 1939 until 1943 in the Pattinson Issue HM), a retelling of the plot of the 1921 sensation The Day We Let the Chaos In, an illustred trevold collaboration between the siblings Martha and Dean Armstrong. Although, as with most generally-popular works, there were many such derivative pieces published (Markland having at the time an eighteen-year withholding), Bluemore's enjoyed widespread fame, leading to a run on Pattinson's primers and a copy shortage. The full work was edited singly in 1945, and translated into Borlish in 1946 by Margret Courleon.

    taken from Middlesea Meals, published bilingually in Wessern and Kernish 1892 by Iscombe Editors. It was translated from the acclaimed 1880 mealbook Pasti Mezziterreni, written by traveller Josforo Sant'Angelo and intersperses anecdotes from his journeys with his favourite dishes.

King James Stew (Frigotta de Rei Jaume)

Of all the fish stews I sampled along the solarium coasts of Barcelon and Provence (not to mention the singular mussel-olive dish I was served on the beaches of Mayurca), none compare to the King James. Locally and affectionately called the jaumetia βÄ€little JamesβÄù, this stew is named for James VI (1691-1749), the longest-ruling monarch in Barcelon since its establishment in 1067. Although similar stews existed in the region before his reign, most simply functioned to use up whatever seafood remained from a day's catch. The King James is perhaps the first such dish made for its own sake, and indeed should for best results use hake or haddock, or a similar whitefish.

Wares (for four)

5ΈΙ sweet aumonds
eq. grain garlic, olive oil
1 large onion per cubos
1 whole pepper, sliced
some sweet drypepper, petersly and fendle (fine)
¬Ϋ pint Empordan or other white
8ΈΙ pit shadome or sauce thereof
2–Ω6ΈΙ whitefish per frangos
¬Ϋ limmon, juced

Method

First bake the aumonds until golden and grind to a coarse powder. In a broad pan heat the oil, then gently fry the aumond, garlic, onion and pepper for roughly fifteen minutes, or until the vegetables are soft.

Add the drypepper and half the herbs and stir together. After two minutes add a dram of the wine and allow it to mostly boil off. Now add the shadome sauce or chopped shadomes, pour in the rest of the wine, and cook for fifteen minutes more.

Salt the strips of fish and push them into the sauce. Cover the pan and leave for five minutes, until the fish is just cooked. Uncover, add the rest of the herbs (or whichever amount suits) and cast the juce over the dish. Serve bowled with hardbread.

Nowadays usually either meaning βÄ€distant in timeβÄù (the spatial sense mostly reserved by lontan) or else the metaphorical βÄ€unlikelyβÄù, which is first attested 18C.

    SlognaΟΑ comoscon ig nos parvendreu allagr-vescoirn-il, jo vil te colluΟ·r.

    I will stay by your side, no matter how unlikely it is that we'll get our happy ending.

##

1r May

    from the collected correspondence of Marian Siegert ~ Miller (1725βÄ™56), illegitimate daughter of Elias Villingen, second Duke of Far Suebia (the title having been created by King Frederick of Bavarn after the Blue Trans-Willem War and the dissolution of the Kingdom of Burgundy).

[received at Castle Carlsfeld in the winter of 1744]

To my dear brother Thomas,

Albert suggested that I write to you first, and I was only too happy to do so; ideally your witticisms shall be as entertaining on paper as in person, and in any case I cannot help but feel some kinship with you. We are the two fledgeling sparrows cast out from Father's nest to fend for ourselves.

I am of course exaggerating; doubtless the King can provide for any number of wards (no matter how grudgingly accepted; your evenings with Father were perhaps not as private as you hoped! Shall we just say that everyone downstairs is aware of Prince M's Indiscretion?), but neither can I complain overmuch. Albert understated the extent of his cobbler's empire here in Lepzi; it cannot compare to home (as still I think of it) but I had been prepared to face far greater hardships when I accepted his hand.

You were, as ever, entirely correct to insist on extra language tutors (and entirely too generous in letting me attend!). The compulsory Latin has revealed itself less than uselessβÄîwe run in entirely the wrong circlesβÄîbut the Dutch they have here is almost as awful as that of those Flemish performers we had in some summers past. I expect I will acclimate, but for now I am having far too much fun speaking Burgund and Boheme with Albert's company.

Does the news we get of bread troubles have any basis? We know not to trust anything the Lepzi weeklies have to say about life south of the Danaw, but the rumours about Venise and Crovatia are bad enough; do abscond back to Stuttgart at the slightest sign Bavarn is along the same trackβÄΠ

Your sister,

F. Marian Siegert

ToΟΑ pris accougl, Marc no'r certan de noc faΟ·r y tuyaut recevir, ma ancour l'oc no sta total extrane. Prefaibr untal aucoum par gent aye lou sgardaΟΑ e pesaΟΑ a lour y jorn a cortisment meΟΑes, n'y bel per admirant.

##

7m May

    censor commentary on a question from the 1985 scitation paper Consequences of Viking Statecraft, set as part of Lester Academy's series on northwestern European history.

Question 1: What factors enabled Dunclothe in the mid-tenth century to conquer the heartland territory of the Dane Sodrick (also known contemporaneously as the Kingdom of the Isles)?

Censor: On the whole a well-answered question, with the majority of respondents correctly noting the troubles further east - a typical good answer included the following:

    the several famine years during the 920s and 30s across Denmark and Horth (some incorrectly referred to the Eurasian Warming, but this began only several decades later, and in any case did not lower crop yields in the region).
    the skirmishes between Dane-ruled Borland and Sothbar, now harbouring the exilic Roman king and his family (the wife, of course, being King Roderick's cousin).
    the inevitable slowdown of the flow of wealth obtained by raiding; excellent responses compared Sodrick to Danish Jarvick, which managed to transition to a more diplomatic stance by virtue of inheriting (so to speak) a city well-positioned in its trade mesh. By 980 N Jarvick had even successfully reunited Northumbre under its own rule, although Edinbrough and her fief remained de facto independent until increasing Gallic incursion necessitated a more active defence.

Less common but by no means unwelcome was discussion of the Norman French, to whose relative prosperity many left Sodrick entirely; the extent of the depopulation is difficult to quantify but we read several good arguments along these lines. Of particular note is one student; she managed to find convincing records of triplet brothers, born in Mull around 915 N, and mentioned again in reference to a Norman raid of Marsella in 946 N.

It was somewhat disappointing that few respondents mentioned the aggressor Dunclothe in any detail - the lack of decent contemporary sources outside the Tremonnow gravatory does make discussing Roderick's court a challenge. Nonetheless, we appreciated any discussion of, for example, Oscar of Eidin's influence among the northern lords.

often and traditionally a (necessarily free-standing) item of furniture used to store crockery or utensils, and on which food might be plated before a formal dinner.

< 13C platabac(q) βÄ€sideboard, cupboard (often literally a board attached to a wall)βÄù, from late Old French plat abaque βÄ€sideboardβÄù, literally βÄ€flat boardβÄù, retaining a then-rare sense of abaque < Latin abacus βÄ€board, abacusβÄù. The mathematical sense of abaque was so predominant that one occasionally sees the reanalysis plat Ο† bac βÄ€raftboardβÄù in the contemporary literature, despite the incongruous semantics.

    Des y platabac pris i dou peutr blau e pos i l'un a sy fraΟΑr.

    He took out two mugs from the cupboard and offered one to his brother.

    /d…¦z i Υ¨pla.taΥàbak p Ä…Σz i du Υàpaw.t ÄΧ© blo e Υàp…îz i Υàl…Σn a si Υàf Äa.ΟΑ ÄΧ©/

Dalvan cos usual parmis ag ci armaΟΑ de l'oc stent passar asseyent ne hal d'agent e respir prendent un pog, sauf cas y bon Domn au fait reconnuΟΑ a accarent ig l'er Recevent, oc avenent noc moutessem a un veg par monΟΑ.
At Dalvane, those times of day were usually when you got to sit in the servants' hall and catch your breath a bit, unless her ladyship had put out word that she was In Reception to callers, which didn't happen above once a month or so.

The Gaunt melee was a different story, with a constant trickle of calling-cards and invitations turning up.

incoΟ·br /Υ¨…Σn.k…îΥàji.b ÄΧ©/ [Υ¨…Σ≈΄.k äΥàji.b…ê] inevitable, fated, imminent

< corresponding to unattested Latin adjective incohibilis βÄ€not confinableβÄù, seen in Old Boral as incoyible, then as now analysable as in- βÄ€notβÄù + coyir~coΟ·r βÄ€to prevent, to stopβÄù + -ible~-ibr βÄ€-ableβÄù. The verb comes from Latin cohibe≈ç βÄ€I confine, I hinder, I limitβÄù.

    /i Υàm…î Ät Υ¨…Σn.k…îΥàji.b ÄΧ© de.liΥàjam malΥàvats m…¦t Υàf…Σn …¦j t ÄeΥàv…îld/

##

13m May

    taken from A History of Timekeeping (C―²–Ψ―ÄΡ±―è O–±–Φ–Β―Ä–Α –ß–Α―¹―É), written by Igor Arcadich and published 1961 by Halich Quaternus Editions.

βÄΠMeneva (now TyΟΑewi, or in Wessern tradition Tythew, literally meaning βÄ€David's houseβÄù), and died on the first of March at the end of the seventh century. He was canonised by Pope Agatho (1001-1026); it is commonly told that this was a signal of Papal support for the foundering Kingdom of Wales, which had been losing people and land both to Markland and to Devon.

[Pictured left, the flag of the modern Kingdom of Wynneth (Y Deyrnas GwyneΟΑ), using the colours of Saint David]
image

The story of Saint David, while interesting, is somewhat tangential to our plot. In the early years of the twelfth century, amidst the great successes of the First Crusade, Pope Nuntius instituted a reform of the heretofore universal Julian calendar. Most relevant to us was the shift of the New Year from the Annunciation on the 25th of March to David's Day, on the first. (In fact, during the Roman period, the first month was January, and to this days certain Eastern heterodoxies celebrate the yearturning at the beginning of that month). It is thus unsurprising that the Welsh New Year celebrations are a global legend. I myself was fortunate enough to be in Deganny for the 200th anniversary of the coronation of the People's KingβÄîmy hearing has never been the same!

Of course, despite Rome's unparalleled power at this time, not all of Christendom implemented the Nuntian reforms at once. The spiritual significance of the Annunciation proved a particular sticking point; this formed one of the famed Pauline DenunciationsβÄîβÄ€the reckless diminution of our ordained measureβÄù, as he characteristically put it. Thus did the Augsburg dominions retain the Julian calendar until the Rhine Compromises much later.

The second and more involved change to the Julian calendar regarded the placement of leap years. It had been noted that the equinoces under the Julian system would drift from their astronomical ideal by one day for every 128 yearsβÄΠ

tarsc /ta Äx/ [t…ëΥêx] card, note, small paper, memo

< 11C from Old French targe βÄ€small shield, bucklerβÄù, itself from Frankish targa with the same meaning. Probably conflated with the Norrish and English cognates, which would alone have given *tarc or *targ. The shift in sense to paper dates to 16C, from clipping of fougl tarsc βÄ€buckler sheetβÄù, the term for the paper size one-half of a trevold. Specific meaning βÄ€memoβÄù from 19C.

See also:

    tarsc annonΟß βÄ€calling card, business cardβÄù

##

19m May

    excerpted from The Secret Guide to Ausbagn (English edition), sold 1967 for 1βçö30 in the Calder Museum of Health's leavingstore [1]. Published first in 1962 on behalf of the Maglen FormeuΟß Caritativia, as part of a series of tourist guidebooks for cities along both coasts of the Rustigh Strait [2].

βÄΠreign, and this at the greatest extent of the First Drengotian Empire in the west. For context, the Ausbagn of the late twelfth century is the de facto seat of the County of Sothbar; by 1190 Count Steven is known to have resided year-round in the coastal town, bequeathing the old house in HeitriΟß to his younger brother.

The location of Ausbagn on one side of the Rustigh Strait proved to be of immense strategic value, both militarily and economically. The Great Flie Flood of 1171 (which created the island Texel, about which more later) swept away much of Lower Frisland, and was devastating for all of Willemy. This was not least because the Duke, one Lovis the Tall, was in Amstel at the time, and was killed in the deluge [3]. In any case, Ausbagn was for several decades afterward the obvious choice of harbour for any trader moving from the Danish lands through the Drengot domain and onward to Spain and the riches of the Middlesea.

More relevant to history (or at least to the good reader's moneypurse!) are the Order of Saint James, in whose name was built the Peninsular Monastery which overlooks the city. Founded in the tenth century under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges, they were implicated in a popular uprising against Geoffry II and forced to flee to Borland in 1067 [4]. Today the Order (with its modern name, the Jacobin Fraternity) is in the main known for its Jaibos breweries and for owning much of the land around the city, including the site of one of the ancient Roman bathhousesβÄΠ
image

[In blue, Ausbagn and HeitriΟß; in green, Amstel and Texel; in orange, the Rustigh Strait]

βÄî

[1] in essence, a gift shop.

[2] the waters between the southern coast of Borland and what in OTL are the northern coasts of the Netherlands and western Germany.

[3] possibly; the elderly Duke may already have been dead at the time of the flood - he is known to have been suffering from consumption. The perennial theory that he was swept away on a raft and living his remaining years as a Borlish hermit can be safely ignored.

[4] believed to have the backing of local nobility - however, the emperor's supporters in Normandy were able to muster enough forces that the rebellion was quickly disavowed.

22m May

    extract from the Borlish translation of We Wake to Broken Windows, by Norman author Mark Deuthex. It was published by Maison Bateller (Ferryman House) in 1973, in the hey-day of the spycraft romance. It draws on the author's own experience working for the Red House in Roun, whose activities were revealed to the public as part of the Furore of 1961. The events of that year led to the collapse of the Drengot Collusion (in particular, Darchild's tinplate government) and eventually to the Kentish ostracon reforms, used as a model in several polities around the globe.

Lourell'ouΟßon remanent, parisc i toΟΑ cas final, es l'oc administration e cultur fugr ag saluΟßon d'ig il aun tout oblataΟΑ deull'eΟß viΟΑ.
So their remaining option, as it stands, is running from the very government and society that they have given everything but their lives to save.

L'oc es indigndaΟΑ, ma Paul Lacan es hom prest a savir ig bempart dy mont en es.
It isn't fair, but Paul Lacan, of all people, knows that most things aren't.

Par moment pes i dy ci ogl a Hector situaΟΑ ne visaΟß blanc eΟΑ incertan, agr apen.
He thinks for a while about Hector's eyes in a pale, uncertain face, almost eager.

Paul s'entrouf oyon porraun il var, e comoscon un viΟΑ enter laiscar, costroit a catr man des y fondamen βÄ™ y soul act dont Paul acaf a star fier.
Paul wonders where they will go, and how they will possibly just drop a whole life built from ground up βÄ™ the only thing Paul has ever been proud of.

Ma vray direct, Paul porra eΟΑ i vil. I vil Albion e famigl eΟΑ y Minister fugr ne Hector amant, e Hector en vil ne viΟΑ amant.
But of course, Paul can and he will. He'll run from Albion and family and the Department for love of Hector, and Hector will do it for love of life.

Primer, luy fal y moneΟΑ tan possibr portar outr.
First, he has to withdraw as much money as possible.

I possey y cla a Hector, e tandic y borser le men dessou vars y cambr tesoir de Hector, i parform pesar a haut vois commet a fin, parolant ag segr noc envoluΟΑ βÄ™
He has Hector's key, and when the teller takes him down to Hector's vault, he pretends to think aloud the whole way, talking at the uninterested man βÄ™

sy grant ojanΟß por Hector explanant, proclamant ig l'arra un picq retorsion idiosyncras ne toll'aur a Hector parsount.
explaining how much he hates Hector, and how pointlessly spending all Hector's money will be his own personal little revenge.

pog signfiant oc Hector volois noc reclamar ΟΑa Paul er vray conjuraΟßonnant novant orlosc a caΟΑen n'adamantin acatar coll'aur ; y remarcabr es y sgart luy lanΟßaΟΑ dy borser cant i part, untal pietaΟΑ a biasc.
It doesn't matter that Hector wouldn't have cared even if Paul did plan to buy ninety titanium watches with the money, the important thing is the look that the teller gives him as he leaves, a kind of sideways pity.

Just exact ig seyon vuscaΟΑ.
Exactly what he'd been hoping for.
