##

25m May

    first message sent using a Vilmanas Teleclavia, arguably the earliest household tachygraph [typewriter] capable of sending messages on the steeplemesh. It was sent on Michael's day in 1859 (less than a month after the Auroric Anomaly [1], about which much has been made in film retrospectives), and received manually by Vilmanas' friend Margreta Legaite in Tvaniste βÄ™ a machine capable of transcribing directly from the steeplemesh having not yet been developed.

A B C D E F G. HAEC TUA PENELOPE LENTO TIBI MITTIT ULISSE.
a b c d e f g. This your Penelope sends to you, long-delayed Ulisses (first line from Ovid's Heroines).

    extract from Vitoria Queiros' 1998 quaterno [textbook] Thirteen Inventions which Changed the World (Trize InvenΟßΟΒes que Refiz o Mondo), published by Pontudra Editions.

..be tempting to connect these early developments in distributed communication βÄ™ so vital to the modern concept of Vicinity βÄ™ directly to our own tachslates and meshfora [2], but it is important to keep in mind the machinery of the era. Even the self-contained tachygraph was still in its infancy, with most models only capable of setting type at at most half the speed of practised handwriting. The Armanburg Tachygraph (Tachygrafia Armangrod), which was commercially available in Poland from 1842, was so awkward to use that it was lampooned in the bit-sheets as the Bradygrafia (literally the βÄ€slow-writerβÄù, tachygraph meaning βÄ€fast-writerβÄù).

Moreover, the barrick [electric] steeplepost was still so young that it had not displaced the earlier flag-lamp system, widespread across Spain, Gaul and Italy. With some exceptions (notably the Cracow-Bitom line in southern Poland), the ground was clear for the spread of wired post across northern and eastern Europe, and indeed it is from these places that we get most of the barrick innovations of the late Workshop period (a similar phenomenon is at play in Arabian Cappatia and Lower Mendeva).

So the early wired tachygraphs were cumbersome to use and expensive toβÄΠ

βÄîβÄî
[1] Known in our timeline as the Carrington Event, or the 1859 Solar Storm, this coronal mass ejection caused stong aurorae and wrought havoc on telegraph equipment from August 29th to September 2nd. Some operators even reported being able to send messages along disconnected wires.

[2] A tachslate (Kentish /Υàtak.sl…¦jt/) is a handheld device capable of sending text messages to other such devices. A meshforum roughly corresponds to our idea of an Internet message board.

##

28m May

    from Portingale author Ezio Carvallo's 1860 trevold Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico (released in Kentish as We Arive in Unwemmed Clothes, translated 1865 by Christopher Wood), after which is named the originally-Spanish masquira genre of stories. These typically feature vigilante characters and plots driven by hidden identities, high society and complicated schemes. It has some overlap with the later spycraft genre, especially in modern works.

    The narrative focus on characters with hidden identities has led several marginal groups to embrace masquira tales, most notably the semi-public tovarick communities in the Riverine Cities. Indeed, the tovarism connection was barely obfuscated in some works (see particularly Takach's We Will Burn Together), and the popularity of the genre is widely understood to have contributed to the Household Renovation.

Ag moment oyon Matthias acavau a scap fair ne cognit dell'aucoum lougent eΟΑ y porrujon yembr dessur, y ciel innuoΟΑous au toΟΑ residu ne ros soluΟΑ e sta affauΟΑant direct vars blau Dachin ; y preu stel lusern freit sur y ci montagn slognaΟΑ norΟΑer.
By the time Matthias managed to slip away from the glittering crowd and step out onto the balcony, the cloudless sky had lost all trace of pink and was fading directly to a Dackin blue; the first stars twinkled cold above the distant mountains to the north.

Y lumner exiguous dell'Ors Majour vole bogr sy form connoscr par febiltaΟΑ cant Matthias retornau ac pre lugnt ; all'uncos sarve ig l'yemoy un convoc Escriva, un noit de lun neir.
The scant light of the Great Bear was going to be far too weak to catch his form when he returned in the hex hours; not for nothing had he obtained an Escriva invitation on the night of a new moon.

Un manton a nuot βÄ™ ben ploy meΟΑes, y heloit survale tojorn y frescur βÄ™ fos idone parfait, ma l'≈™cumen Aragonesc avant sta steΟΑig pittoresq com ja.
Cloud cover βÄ™ or even rain, the noise was always worth the chill βÄ™ would have been ideal, but the Aragonese landscape before him was as stubbornly picturesque as ever.

31r May

     continued extract from Vitoria Queiros' 1998 quaterno Thirteen Inventions which Changed the World (Trize InvenΟßΟΒes que Refiz o Mondo); see 25th May.

βÄΠperiod (a similar phenomenon is at play in Arabian Cappatia and Lower Mendeva).

So the early wired tachygraphs were cumbersome to use and expensive to get hold of βÄ™ and furthermore there was little incentive for businesses, who could simply hire a tapper [1] already fluent in the Adamich system (a professional could transmit at a rate of two hundred words per minute). Thus was the wired tachygraph adopted slowly, in fits and starts.

However, in those places where there was early adoption, the machines proved astonishingly popular. These were usually dense cities and urban areas undergoing rapid growth. In Safford [2], for example, which already had a robust postal infrastructure, the young Mayor Crawford oversaw the installation of the first redirection banks in post offices. These pioneered the system in which a given missive would be prepended with a borough code βÄ™ for example, Makerfell's was MKF βÄ™ allowing the switchers [3] to send the message through to the correct local post office, followed by a house code βÄ™ eventually standardised to four digits followed by a letter βÄ™ whence the message could be transmit to the correct machine.
image

    (above) An outgoing redirection bank, 1879. Not pictured are the spools of paper on which the house codes would be printed in Adamich notation.

    (below) Sender's copy of a letter sent via the Aiga d'Angoixa tachygraph mesh, 1882; additions in parentheses

IVS (Illa de Vinna Salvatge [4]) 86814 J

Good evening, brother! My husband is hosting a evening sorty this weekend; are you and yours otherwise occupied? If not, you are certainly welcome to attend. He wants a lakeside bonfire and connoqueria (connock dance [5]) and everything. It'll probably be the last chance we have before the new-year thaw. Yours, Marta.

[1] One employed to operate the steeplepost (that is, a telegraph operator).

[2] Second-largest city in Markland, corresponding to Salford in our timeline.

[3] People who worked at redirection banks, allowing messages to go where they were sent.

[4] literally βÄ€Wild Grape IslandβÄù, named for its plentiful fruit. We call it Οéle d'OrlΟ©ans, in Quebec.

6m Jugn

    in translation from the collected correspondence of Michael Bervisson (17th June 1799βÄ™12th January 1849), political historian and social reformist; he and his wife Hilgart were influential in the Borrhatic League, especially with regard to calls for religious tolerance. This letter to his editors is on proposed alterations to his magnum opus Roma ΟÜternal: EΟß Vascel a These (An Eternal Rome: Ships of Theseus).

(received on the morning of Tuesday 5th November, 1844 N)
Kind sirs Osgaut and Yfaur,

May this missive find you and the Vithor Historical Institute in good health and well-mint. Samuel and Clovia do better for themselves every time they return from school, although their history tutor's opinions on the collapse of the Medieval Imperia are execrable. I expect they shall both be in the melee before this blasted book is published, in any case; Hilgart is already reading the illustrated costumery in the hopes that we might all be fashionable by March (kindly do not mention hats in her company!).

Only two points must I reiterate regarding the volume. First, I absolutely cannot condense The Wall and The Hedgerow βÄ™ contrasting Hadrian's policies in Britain and in Borland is vital for understanding their different fates in the post-Classical age, for his reign marks the beginning of our island's greater autonomy. There is one correction: I have not been consistent in writing signa (for example in the name of AquΡΪs BalneΡΪs), and you may feel free to add them wherever necessary.

Second, I would like to divert as much criticism as we might possibly anticipate (in particular one aquiline fellow who shall not sully my quill) and rebut those arguments directly in the text. To this end, might we extend the chapter Twin Keys to emphasise the ways both the Drengotian Constant Empire to the west and the Malchassian Romans to the east used the Church (in the last century of its unity) to support their claims to continuity with Rome? I shall have written the requisite pages by Revillon, I assure you.

Do not hesitate to reply forthwith,
Professor Michael Bervisson

12m Jugn

    taken from A Comprehensive CyclopΟΠdia of Pre-Fracture Europe, written by Martin and Elisabeth Forman and illustrated by Goffrey Jordan; published 1937 by Winchester Scholastic Primers.

βÄΠwas thankfully less variation across Europe in these units than in, for example, the league (as we have seen).

¬ßThe Second Tetrachy

Refers to the period of roughly one hundred and fifty years for which much of Europe was under the (occasionally theoretical) rule of empire. Known also βÄ™ especially in older texts βÄ™ as the Medieval Imperia, or as the Roman Decadence.

The most limited timeframe to which this title routinely refers is from 1091, with the consolidation of Bohemia and Lower Pannonia into the German Empire under Bertholdt II, until 1170, when the Emergency at Dijon led to the establishment of an independent Kingdom of Burgundy (and soon the dissolution of the Constant Empire). More usual is to include everything from the Norman-led subjugation of Kent (and installation of the House of Kernow in Dumnonia) in 1029 to the death of Daniel IV (the Drengotian domains being much divided among his family and friends) in 1191.

The four empires constituting the Second Tetrarchy were:

    the aforementioned Constant Empire, under the Drengot dynasty and comprising for most of its duration the kingdoms of West Francia, Wessex, Markland and Dumnonia.
    the German Empire, in particular under the Houghbrocken and Andex dual imperium, and including more than a dozen states βÄ™ principally the Kingdom of Saxony, the Duchy of Willemy and the Kingdom of Bohemia.
    the Empire of the Hellenes (also called the Second Roman Empire), especially considered from its territorial maximum in 1026, controlling lands from Corsica to the shores of the Hazaran Sea.
    the Single Caliphate ruled from Cairo, primarily after the centralisation reforms of the early eleventh century (although the further territories such as the Admiralty of Cordoba or the Turkish lands were never as closely-ruled).

Even at the height of imperial control, regions within these states often had significant autonomy. In 1105, the Kingdom of Greater Sicily even went to war withβÄΠ

##

18m Jugn

    in translation from the 1998 scholastic quire Sviluppi in Philosophia dal Periodo Revitalisto (in English, Developments in Philosophy since the Revitalist Period) by historical deixist Jovanna Ariperto, researcher at the Autonome University of Saint Marinus in Lombardy. 

(excerpted from Chapter 8, titled Forging the First Link: Problems of Infinite Regression)

βÄΠpopularised by the works of Dufresne and Fayeda in the last decade of the seventeenth century, this theory asserted that all life had formed at the moment of Creation, and that each successive generation grew from miniature versions of themselves (called by Dufresne zoia, not to be confused with our modern use of the term for one-case life [1]), themselves containing yet-smaller forms for the grandchild generation, and so on. Contemporary diagrams showing a tiny human form inside a spermcase illustrate this theory along with the concurrent idea that the mother was merely the soil where the baby, planted by the father, grew.

Setting aside issues of predestination inherent in this approachβÄîthough of course this rendered the theory anathema or even heretical in the Augsburg dominia [2]βÄîthe immediate objection (see al-Siddik's 1696 work On the Children of Zeno) was that you would need to posit an infinite chain of progressively-smaller zoia to be present inside every male animal on the planet. There were two broad categories of response: first, Dufresne and many of the more theologically-minded argued that the Lord had merely provided enough zoia to last until the Apocalypse [3]. This argument could never satisfy all, however.

The response more interesting for our purposes is exemplified in Vallakis' 1702 treatise The Generic Impossibility of Self-Duplication within a Bounded Form. In the book Vallakis argues that any organism that can reproduce, or more generally anything capable of duplication, must have a sculptor guiding the division. The argument proceeds as follows:

    A self-duplicating bounded [4] structure A must contain within it all the structures necessary to create a copy of itself, which together we may call B. But then B contains everything necessary to create itself, say C. Thus we must have a descending chain of structures, each of which contains everything necessary to reconstruct the original A. But this is absurd, as A is bounded and cannot contain arbitrarily-small parts. Thus in fact A can divide only under the intervention of a guiding sculptor.

For several decades, thus, mainstream theory held only two opinions on the mechanism of life: either that all life contained an infinite chain of zoia within, or that each time new life was created it was guided in that moment by the plans of the Creator. This state of affairs would not change until the publishing in 1743 ofβÄΠ

βÄî

[1] singular zoium, from Scholastic Latin and derived from a diminutive of Greek ΈΕαΩΖΈΩΈΫ βÄ€animalβÄù. We would say βÄ€microbeβÄù or βÄ€unicellular organismβÄù.

[2] those lands under the religious authority of the Augsburg See.

[3] that is, the End Times, or the Second Coming.

[4] here intended to mean βÄ€of finite extent and containing finite informationβÄù, or something similar. A mathematical understanding of quantity and extent would not be formalised until the nineteenth century.

21r Jugn

    in continued translation from the 1998 scholastic quire Sviluppi in Philosophia dal Periodo Revitalisto [1] by historical deixist Jovanna Ariperto, researcher at the Autonome University of Saint Marinus in Lombardy. 

(excerpted from Chapter 8, titled Forging the First Link: Problems of Infinite Regression)

βÄΠplans of the Creator. This state of affairs would not change until the publishing in 1743 of Fable du Relieur Mut [1] by Rachel Desselut, daughter of explorer and infamous libertine Lord Bouvoin. The relevant portion is reproduced here.

    And yet he could not take Marcuccio as his apprentice, for how was he to instruct a pupil in the art of bookbinding? But although Ignazo could not speak, he could write. For a year and a day he wrote, describing all his methods and arts, from the pedigree of sheep for the parchment, to the age of the oak tree for the ink. In this way he taught Marcuccio in detail the production of a book exactly like the one he had himself written, save that the copy would be utterly blank. Upon the last page, Ignazio wrote his final instruction: Et omnia hic scripta scribas illicβÄîAnd all that is written here, you shall write therein. And thus Marcuccio received the book, and studied from it. And after many months did he return to Ignazio, twin books in his arms.

Desselut's work lays out the separation of Form and of Method, and how they allow for the unsculpted duplication of a bounded being. However, her work would go unrecognised for nearly two decades (due predominantly to the disruption of the Democratic Wars), during which time she would be forced to flee first to Norman Kent [2] and later to Provence, via Portingale. It was in these years that she first began corresponding with mathematician Raymond al Mustafa, who was at the time resident in Barcelon under the Duke of Aragon's patronage. Their letters offer a tantalising glimpse into early methodics [3], although both were more interested in the implications for life. Desselut inferred the existence of a vital language (in her own writing she called it the Divine Word) present in the cases [4] of the body. Despite later rumours, she probably did not believe this language was literally Hebrew, although she made several analogies in her publications.

In 1770 the first real self-describing book was written by an anonymous author and donated to the University of Hulm. The university has duplicated the book once every four years in as exact a way as possible, and the original, alongside the most recent copy, may be viewed in their Department of Heredity building. In a peculiar twist, since the early twentieth century the ink has had to be sourced from a different tree than is specified in the book, as theβÄΠ

βÄî

[1] Developments in Philosophy since the Revitalist Period; Parable of the Mute Bookbinder

[2] That is, Normandy and Flanders.

[3] Computing, or programming, although at this point it is not even at the level of, say, our Ada Lovelace and Charles Babbage.

[4] Cells: this vital language is of course DNA (known eventually in this world as heredian acid or, less commonly, as nucalic acid).

Y nouΟß casel, nommaΟΑ tan y nux, er primer mis a regest annaΟΑ 1713, posc bre y descovart toΟΑ recostroint all'heir Arnoutzen de viΟΑ unicasel.

27m Jugn

    recipe taken from the 1763 mealbook A Complete Lithing of the Towns and Cities of Markland, for the Edification of the Good Food Amateur, written by independent traveller Lord Carrow, Baron of Ebbasham [Epsom]. Although popular across Albion, Ireland and Borland (the Borlish translation being infamously inaccurate), it never sold in large numbers on the continent or further afield. His later volumes, An Account of the Vittles of the Various German Peoples and On the Sundry Uses of the Shadome Fruit [tomato], were more enduring, being republished several times even after his death.

The Lonxter [Lancaster] Yatherpot

Lonxter is a castle town on the banks of the Lone, being the northernmost city and river along the western coastline of Markland. Its signature dish is the yatherpot, a filling mutton or lamb meal to be prepared for a large family with its distinctive sliced tartoffer covering. The name is simply Merch for βÄ€together potβÄù, as it is a dish one can cook in a single large pot or bowl.

Wares (for six)
5ΈΙ butter or lard
3–Ω6ΈΙ lamb or mutton per frangos [strips]
4 sheep's codnere [kidney], trimmed
2 large onions, 6 carrots, sliced
3ΈΙ rough flour
some salt, molasses, vinegar, spices per gustibus
1¬Ϋ pint lamb or chick broth
3 leaves dried lorrer [bay leaf]
3–Ω6ΈΙ tartoffer, peeled and sliced

Method

    Melt a little of the butter in a large shallow dish and sear the lamb in batches, remove to a plate, then repeat with 3 trimmed and sliced lamb kidneys, the onions and the carrots (these last two remaining).
    Dust with flour, leave for two minutes, then add the seasoning and broth, and allow to boil. Pour in the lamb, kidneys and lorrer and remove from the fire.
    Arrange the tartoffer by slices over the meat and then glaze with hot butter. Place on a lid and cook for 1+¬Ϋ hours on a low fire.
    Once the tartoffer are well-browned, remove from the fire and serve immediately.

9m Juglot

    scitation questions (in translation) from the  University Faculty of Domain History, from the course Religion in the Second Millenium: Belief, Community and Division, given Summer 2007 N following a reconfiguration of their course offering.

Question 1 (60 minutes)
Write a short speech aimed at the interested layman advocating the three religious developments of the last millenium in Europe which you hold to have been most influential. You should introduce the events with appropriate context, discuss their similarities and differences, and justify their importance. For example, you might choose one of

    the German Secession; the Great Fracture; the Deviance Movement

    the First Crusade; the Muslim migrations northward into the Russias and Tartary; the Prodigal Faiths from Mendeva and Cappatia

Question 2 (120 minutes)
This question focuses in particular on the emergence of Deviance Theology (the Anti-Paradise Movement) in the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries.

(i) What evidence is there that <Deviance> originates as a mistranslation from Wessern, and meant at first <defiance, rebellion>? Consider both the semantic plausibility andβÄîcruciallyβÄîthe significance of <defiance> to the Promethean clubs across Albion; the term of course is better evidenced in their Danawan counterparts.

(ii) In both Albion and the Danaw Kingdom at this time there were significant religious minorities and the Church (of whichever stripe) was strongly associated with local authority. Was it these commonalities that made them fertile ground for Anti-Paradise thought?

(iii) To what extent was the resurgence of the Modest Arrangement in Spain and Gaul in reaction to these new religious ideas? Compare the fervence of Modest thinkers like the Marquis de Caillat or proto-Blue Marian Diana Theodora Queiroz to the philosophers of earlier Modest-like movements.

(iv) In your opinion, who is, or are, the most likely author(s) of the incendiary 1799 publication Et Nous Amerons les Fux (And We Will Love the Fire), a seminal work in Deviance Theology? Back up your theory with modern and contemporary sources, and contrast the likelihood of at least two other candidates.

camin cadaraΟΑ /kaΥàm…Σn Υ¨ka.daΥà ÄaΈΗ/ [k…êΥàm…Σn Υ¨ka.d…êΥà Äah] primrose path, easy life, life of hedonism, the road to hell

that is, a deceptively easy or appealing decision of series of decisions, which however lead one astray or to damnationβÄîespecially choices offered by a malevolent actor

< 17C, coined by writer and philosopher CaΟΑoval Thoitom in his 1641 allegory Through the Heghlant Forest, or the Ruin of Little Lady Alissa, literally meaning βÄ€distilled pathwayβÄù. MalgraΟΑ, piratary spas graΟß all'Abanc Novomondin ne 1471 eΟΑ all'AscendanΟß vascon coherent. A Marsegl, la stan gazou frequent aΟΑief por ig plusour ageurn com ΟΑa y citaΟΑ fos occupaΟΑ ; donc y risc fuyeurn heilant vars scurtaΟΑ certanessem.
However, piracy thrived with the Novomundine landfall in 1471 and the accompanying Ascendancy of Vascony. In Marsella, the raiding became so frequent that many behaved as though the city were occupied, the wealthy fleeing to safer pastures inland.

Y ci rekest por aΟ·ΟΑanΟß stant fait a Rudolf VIII remaneurn plurem lon respos. Por cartaΟΑ, l'er parcanΟß divertiΟΑ pall'ig conflict bavarian continu, ma cos usual nos dirreu y rey joun er just curant for a luy (ben a cost de luy, a sell'amour nombrous) eΟΑ a festeul spendent sur lac.
The aid requests presented to Rudolf VIII went largely unanswered. Things came to a head in the last years of the fifteenth century. Thus prepared, he and several other noble families declared independence from Geneva on the twenty-first of June, 1499.

15m Juglot

    from a review in the Fiellas Dimenja (Sunday Leaves/Sheets), a Marsellan weekly newspaper incorporated in 1871. It focuses primarily on recreation and entertainment, unlike the older weeklies dating back to the sixteenth century, which were limited more to βÄ€high-browβÄù topics.

14th October, 1950

βÄΠwelcomed back from the New Cambrick facilities; Captains Kerry and Montpiu landed safely at Fort daus Sanz early Friday morning.

REVIEW: Zachariah

A departure from Kiawaman's usual fare, this blacklair [horror] tale takes us back to the 1820s amid the disintegration of the Long Peace and the beginning of Reaction War. From the gaslit streets of independent Boxa [~Novi Sad, Serbia] to the recovering shell of Stamboul [Istanbul/Constantinople], this adaptation of Petcov's classic trevold [novel] Majat vCherveno (The Red Man) tries to recreate the dread and mystery of watching our four heroes slowly discover the true nature of the eponymous Zachariah.

Unfortunately, the beautiful landscape shots and the addition of a rich Tavancine tourist (whose plotline is mostly borrowed from the book's Elenor storyline) are the high points of an otherwise messy film. In an attempt to incorporate some of the more traditional aspects of vampire folklore, Kiawaman grants Zachariah mystic powers and has the heroes defeat him by means of a sanctified wooden stake (not, as in the book and in popular wisdom, by burning). These new powers, in the hands of a better writer, could have set up a dynamic between Zachariah and Vania as artists of the same craftβÄîinstead, we are given the tired trope of a woman torn between the affections of two men. It doesn't help that the audience barely believes there is any choice to make; Vania has no chemistry with either lead.

The symbolism of fire and butterflies that permeated the book is also entirely absent from this adaptation. Apparently afraid to present Zachariah as light or good in any way, we get a cold manor house and bat-infested (!) roofs. Never mind the rich history of the vampire as a figure clinging to life in all its forms, in the blood of the living, in the dancing flames and in the metamorphosis of the butterfly. The film relies heavily on gory fights (with what is clearly wine as a bad stand-in for blood, in a disappointing reversal) and a pounding orchestra who leave the few quiet scenes no room to breathe.

This will be no classic: come only for the views (of beautiful forests, and a bevy of handsome actors, I concede) and two hours of mindless tragedy. - Jacint Brunat

21r Juglot

    taken from A Politic Mapbook of Middle-Age Europe (4th edition), by various authors, published 2005 by Winchester Scholastic

Albion and Ireland, 1028 NascentiΟΠ

This year is the last in pre-Drengotian Albion. 1029 will bring the Kernovan Conjuring and Norman Accession, and the succeeding decades will see what might have been the new steady state of these islands disintegrate.

In the south, Kent under Rodulf is stable and much expanded from its position in the 9th and early 10th centuries, having subdued Wessex (minus Anglia, already lost to Markland in the Treaty of Oxford) but suffered renewed Dumnonian pushes from the west. Canterbury's most powerful vassals lie to the north in Essex across the Thames, ably run by Alfred the Tall. The elderly patriarch Rodulf of Kent lingers on his deathbed after eight decades of political man≈™uvring.

To the west we have the ever-fractious kingdoms of Ireland, lately unified as but four (with the breaking of the Ultagh in the north-east): Tyrconnal, Connaght, Lenster and Munster. The tales of Tyrconnal and Lenster are dominated by wars at once with the Norrish to the east and the other Irish to the west, while the houses of Connaght and Munster vie for the position of High King of Ireland.

The north holds the confederate Kingdoms of the Danes, newly untethered from overseas rule. The once-mighty kingdom of Northumbria is rendered but one province of the whole, yet the city of Yorwick remains the richest city on Albion (thanks to economic ties in all directions). Still more northerly lie the two-minded Kingdom of Scotland and the last remains of Horther rule in Albion persist in Orkney and Shetland.

Between all these we find those bitter rivals, Markland and Wales. Cadwallan, erstwhile king of Wynneth, finds himself the unexpected ruler of all WalesβÄîa fortuitous position of strength against equally formidable Markland under Oswin the Becoming.

A loy i tene pos, appenent sur y pladisc, cla portant ne man scert e vin Empordan (bon cos ajoutant) ne man distr.
Instead she remained standing at the bar, keys in her left hand and a gratifyingly-good Empordan wine in her right.

1r Aost

suraΟΑoir /Υ¨si ÄaΥàΟΑ…îj Ä/ [Υ¨si. Ä…êΥàΟΑ…î.j…ê] to exaggerate, to make too big, to outsize, to overstate

##

2m Aost

    taken from the 2008 fourth edition of condensed study materials for New Vithor course Histoir Concorrent, s16 : Mort, Vascel eΟΑ y Motion Celest (in English, Concurrence History of the 16th Century: Death, Ships and the Movement of the Heavens).

βÄΠit is usual to consider the feudal system entirely defunct by 1550 N, at least outside of insular nations like Burgundy or Awry. There were a variety of reasons for this collapse, some more widely accepted than others.

Often cited is a labour shortage of serfs due to the Second Great Dying, allowing those peasants who remained to demand more favourable conditions from their lords. This is evidenced by the correlation [Iscombe 1967] observed between population loss and liberalisation (as well as how quickly the nascent middle class grew). For example, Borland under Joseph III lost over four hundred thousand people, one-seventh of its population; serfdom (already on its last legs) was legally abolished in 1564 N, but by that point it had not de facto existed on the island for a generation.

One counterexample to the above trend is Vascony, which, thanks to Novomundine profits in furs and other goods, actually saw in increase in population from 1500 to 1550 (excluding Portingale) due to immigration. The end of the Vascon Ascendancy with the War of the Two Queens would end feudalism in Vascony by the end of the century, however.

(It is worth mentioning that this was not the main factor in ending feudalism in the east, where the Islamic conquests of Kiew and Russian Cazany upended the nobility more directly.)

One, perhaps unexpected, effect of the decline of the feudal system was the strengthening of laws such as sumptuary codes. These were intended [Rone, 1988] to reinforce the social distinctions between serf, freedman and nobleβÄîalthough states had had similar laws almost since Classical times, enforcement of these rules became more stringent and draconian. There is even one account of an Emma Chasteron being executed in Dovres for wearing βÄ€silk in saphires purpel and petal embroderieβÄù to churchβÄΠ

But the application of copper's counter-zoic [anti-microbial] manner with the furthest-reaching consequences can only be the stauron retainer [intra-uterine device]. 15m Aost

    from the collected study notes for the course 1471βÄ™1517: War, Peace and Exploration after the Novomundine Landfall, 37th avenue in Concurrence History at the Tremonnow Open School.

Of course, Vascony was not the only nearby state attempting to reach Asia by sea. Ambrose's nemesis Munir al-Hamdawi of Morrack had in 1463 established a rival marine enterprise in the New World Company (Ί¥Ί±ΌÉΊ© ΊßΌ³ΊΙΊßΌ³ΌÖ ΊßΌ³Ί§Ί·ΌäΊ·), and just five years later the ship Orion (ΊßΌ³Ί§ΌéΊ®ΌéΊßΊ±) voyaged from Anfa [Casablanca] to Aden. They made port in Vascon Sant Adrian [Bioko] and encountered Chinese merchants in Mombasa, whose reports to the Southern Emperor would change the course of history forever (see 1524-1600: The Collision and the Fracture).

After the Landfall, the Company's efforts were swiftly redirected to the Novomund [the New World] (as were the naval efforts, however paltry, of most of Europe at this time). However, a notable exception was the relatively-new Kingdom of Britain, where Howel's ships had already set up resupply ports along the African coast. Having profited from the ongoing Wars of Fealty which raged across the rest of Albion [Great Britain], the king was convinced more opportunity lay in connecting the European market directly to Asia. Doubtless he also wanted to displace the Borlish supremacy in the northern trade (although this ambition would not be achieved in his lifetime).

In any case, the tale of the twin voyages of the Cynthia and the Ph≈™bos (of Morrack and Britain respectively) remains to this day one of the greatest stories of exploration and discovery the world has ever known, measuring up to Homer's Odyssey and Quoming's Peoples of the Sea. Directed by Umanick and Chinese traders, mac Kellot's Ph≈™bos sailed to Gurjat, Madray and eventually made port at Borune in 1516. This was at the time almost the south-eastern extremity of the known Vetomund.

Meanwhile, the Cynthia under al-Casmi (himself a Persian immigrant to Morrack) had no less interesting a journeyβÄΠ

20m Aost

       from the collected study notes for the course 1471βÄ™1517: War, Peace and Exploration after the Novomundine Landfall, 37th avenue in Concurrence History at the Tremonnow Open School.

βÄΠthe south-eastern extremity of the known Vetomund.

Meanwhile, the Cynthia under al-Casmi (himself a Persian immigrant to Morrack) had no less interesting a journey. First making port at the various Arcabil and Meridian outposts, already a significant source of wealth, the ship continued along the Cappatian coast*. Here in al-Casmi's logbooks we read of the Dowahin straits and island (from the Arabic for βÄ€smokeβÄù, after the many smoke-towers the captain writes of seeing the inhabitants of the island build, and which dissuaded him from making landfall thereon).

(*Remember that the continent would not be so named for several more decades yet.)

But the bulk of the Cynthia's journey would come next, as the ship entered the southern Horizic Ocean. At first they sailed north along the jagged coastline, possibly as far as Maboutian lands, although al-Casmis records are unclear. In any case they eventually sailed west into the blue. It is not entirely clear which Horizic Isles the Cynthia encountered on this part of the voyage. What is known is that on midsummer of 1515 they alighted on Jazirat Asnam (the encounter being infamous for its acrimony), and that on 11 Shaban 921 the crew began to perform the salat forward, not aft, as the astronomer aboardβÄîone Taisir as-SadikβÄîpredicted they had passed the longitude of the antipode of Mecca.*

(*Further recall that truly accurate longitude measurement would not be achieved until the invention of the timehold in the mid-seventeenth century.)

The next spring the Cynthia finally arrived in a place almost recognisable as Asia, though the inhabitants were as dark-skinned as anyone in the Arab world. This archipelago was hence named Asia Cahal (or in modern parlance Cahalla), and in the records of al-Casmi's time on Bijai (the largest island thereof) there is an account of an old folktale; one which will explain why the Cynthia, on leaving Bijai, went not west, but southβÄΠ

The adverb parmig derives from parmy βÄ€among, alongsideβÄù, literally βÄ€by the middleβÄù, from Latin per medium.

< late 20C, clipping of ardois βÄ€slate, slabβÄù, also βÄ€screen, displayβÄù, referring to touchscreens originally in the brandname TachArdois (evoking Greek œ³αΨΑœ΅αΩ†Χ¹œ² βÄΔ takhΟΚs βÄ€swift, quickβÄù) and the generic term ardois tactil βÄ€tactile slateβÄù. 23m Aost

    story excerpt from Callot Rhig's 1834 collection of short stories Aur Nercow (Fool's Gold), exploring the disconnect between what we say we want, and what will truly make us happy.

Diabr sta i feliΟß a se trovar ac, ΟΑa meΟΑes oy passau i jorn sou perigl d'applatiscenΟß par col livr.
He was damned lucky to be here, even if he now lived in danger of being crushed by a book landslide.

Arthur fo vas ag var ogtoΟß eld, e fo reuluΟΑ posc nouf annaΟΑ descovrent i sta l'ivan extrane d'un famigl lon gravitaΟΑ mendrem.
Arthur had gone to war at eighteen, and come back nine years later to find himself the extraneous child of a family of little import.

Sur mar ben ne Madray er i vis com cim forgaΟΑ, un ven dont slanΟßa experiΟß vocationner cordamn fruyant e tout souvant.
At sea or in Madray he'd been a forged expert, a wellspring of professional expertise who knew the ropes and had seen it all.

Cas luy a Carvantel, i fo denuΟΑ ancour joun segr, hom a pog addoΟßment eΟΑ a alcun histoir empley.
Back in Carvantel he'd become a young man again, one with little training and no experience.

Pre y var un atelier carpenter au le benoΟßaΟΑ un apprendent, ma l'ig parisce remotessem a jouneΟß.
He'd been apprenticed to a carpenter's shop before the war, but that felt like a lifetime ago.

For sarve i ac a hom occir, stant dissuas.
All he was good at now was killing people, which was discouraged.

24m Aost

romannesc /Υ¨ Äo.maΥàn…¦x/ [Υ¨ Äo.m…êΥàn…¦Οß] civilised, popular, folk, romantic, quaint

< 10C in rumanesc etc. βÄ€of the Roman peoplesβÄù, which is to say of Borland, Italy, Iberia and France, from roman βÄ€RomanβÄù with adjectival suffix -esc < Germanic -iskas. Soon (by late 11C) used to denote popular or folk stories, music and so on, as opposed to Classical or ecclesiastical works, or any works in Latin. Already by 17C it means βÄ€quaint, old-fashioned, charmingβÄù, but more positive senses returned in the late 18C with the Romantic Revival of the Long Peace.

    L'eΟß cont mi-mythos a rey e volour son nommaΟΑ stoir romannesc.

    These semi-mythical stories of kings and thieves are called romantic tales.

26m Aost

    questions taken from the January 2003 N scitation documents for the course 1471 - 1517: War, Peace and Exploration after the Novomundine Landfall, 37th avenue in Concurrence History at the Tremonnow Open School (formerly the Brethin Mesh Institute). [continued from 13m Avril]

Question 3 (sixty minutes)

(i) Answer the following short questions on the Vascon Ascendancy in under 100 words each.

    (Έ±) Who is the most probable out of the many people who are claimed to have been lovers of King Ambrose III?
    (Έ≤) What caused the infamous sinking of the Estellas daus Sanz, which would have been the second lone ship to make the Atlantic crossing?
    (Έ≥) Why did the War for the Barcelon Succession last for only 4 days?
    (Έ¥) Where did Vascony trade for the oranges and other citrus fruits required by the Sandrine Design?
    (ΈΒ) When was the strict iamb 5' first used in Vascon poetry?

(ii) To what extent did the fact that the northern parts of Mendeva were less immediately profitable than the East lead to the end of the Vascon Ascendancy?

Question 4 (forty minutes)

EITHER

(i) Summarise the arguments for and against the veracity of al-Casmi's account of the tale of the Great Southern Island, including recent hereditarian findings and their implications for patterns of migration, and contemporary Tecamaw accounts of the decline of star navigation.

OR

(ii) Discuss the societal effects of the reintroduction of long-distance boating and sail on the Tecamaw nation and the Bijai, with evidence taken from the various written records of the Borunesk Great Meeting of 1517.

    She was infamous during her later life for her great influence in the court of David of France, with rumours that she was in fact his mother persisting for the rest of his reign. 1r Septembr

    short extracts taken from Y Stoir d'Istr Boral (The Story of Borland), second edition, a school history textbook published 1993 by Cordin Editions, one of the foremost guilds producing educational books for national schools.

βÄΠwe will perhaps never know if the conquering Romans called our land ΡΪnsula boreΡ¹lis βÄ€Island in the NorthβÄù before they arrived in force at the daybreak of the first millenium, or if the name is a post hoc creation after the native Kelt name, enis borag βÄ€Island of the DawnβÄù (so called for its easterly position with respect to Albion and Ireland)βÄΠ

βÄΠthere remains little outside contemporary Roman accounts of the Bora Ale, the Borland Kelts, bar geographical features such as the river DuΟΑam (from du dammo βÄ€at the riverβÄù) and, possibly, personal names such as Ivocq and HaΟΑr (though for most of these we also have alternate explanations)βÄΠ

βÄΠin Ptolemy's description of the Pritannoi we can understand he referred to the entirety of the Insular Kelts as a whole; this grouping was later divided, first by the English Strait at some point before Caesar's first invasion of Albion, and later by the Irish Sea, although of course this neat partition is confused by the Scots and by the Welsh communities along the RustighβÄΠ

βÄΠthe latter years of the eighth century, as the Viking Age dawned. The last purely English war of the time was the Merch subjugation of East Anglia, simultaneously establishing the Great Border between the mouths of the Severn and the Thames (see the Oxonian 796 N chronicle), and inciting a renewed influx into the petty kingdoms of Angland and SouthbarβÄΠ

4m Septembr

    from the 1914 short lovetale Chargez-moy Garir (Command that I be Healed), written by longtime author and Millstone veteran (serving in the Second Medic Force of Valois) Honorine Maignard. She was the daughter of Grant-Jeu Lombard diplomat Audace Saviano, who was instrumental in the establishment of the Italian Concert in 1891.

I no's dur a continuar soll'apres dy mort de sy mariΟΑ.
It isn't difficult to go on in the wake of her husband's death.

Sometimes Claire feels like it should be.

Ig l'au y cainΟß tan toccaΟΑ, ig l'au parmis tan direct all'aΟΑombracer Mr Becond le dirisc n'ouvr sug, y mort aus le fragnt.

L'aus l'incendiaΟΑ toΟΑ a tar.
It should have burned her down to the ground.

Ma a loyβÄîa loy i le refay.
But insteadβÄîinstead it remakes her.

Y celebr Claire Robiquet pren martel por aΟ·ΟΑar con y recostroinΟß, Auscapel stant cicatriciΟΑ ja ancour pall'abat dy Grant Centuir, acr a farm laiscaΟΑ crous, blazaΟΑ ;
The illustrious Claire Robiquet steps up to help with the reconstruction,  scarred all over again by the Grand Army's fight, acres of farmland left a burned-out husk;

e Claire se pon avant un grauf e resaif y signfianΟß d'un objectif aΟ·r.
and Claire stands at a grave and rediscovers what it means to have purpose.

< 11C in later Old Boral, participle derivation from aΟΑelir βÄ€to grant landβÄù (> modern βÄ€to improveβÄù), first referring to the stewards in the royal court responsible for granting land to lower nobles, such as barons. 7m Septembr

    start of a letter sent in 1828 via steeplepost from Pentrose (a port town on the south coast of Borland) to Axton (a rapidly-growing industry town which at the time was located in French Guillenne [1], in the duchy of Avosche, known to Paris as Grand-Aix).

    The Rustigh Strait postal crossing from Ausbagn to Texel was not yet connected with air-steeples. In fact, the eventual connection only lasted a generation or so before a barrick line [electric telegraph cable] was laid downβÄîone of the first such lines, beaten by Dovre-Caleis, which were successfully joined in 1852.

To the inestimable NiklΟΓs de Fecht,

Or should I be saying Nicolas Defechet, perhaps? You will at the very least do me the service of not moving to Paris, city of greed and carrion [2], even if you must learn some Proper French for the sake of your wife. (I have always preferred the Roun standard on aesthetic grounds, although I cannot entirely forget politics even here!) As Hortense is certainly reading, I shall pass on Clara's well-wishes and reiterate that you are welcome to make use of the Vauhan lencorve [line of credit]; just ask for Marduth at the Tyche Bank.

Pether's lot are also destined, it seems to leave fair, perfidious Albion. Despite my continuing assurances of welcome here they are determined to sail the great Mendevan river to whatever future they can find in Hasiny [3]. I must admit the prospect of being thousands of miles from any Dutchman has its appeal. (Forgive me! The last decade has made provincials of us allβÄîmayhap even Provincials! [4])

The library has finally provided me with DΟΦmmeyer's treatise on collection and delivery; I will not appease you by pretending to have read it, but be assured I will find the hours for it this very weekβÄΠ

βÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄîβÄ™
[1] Corresponding approximately to Alsace, Lorraine and the Netherlands.

[2] The Kingdom of France was seen to have profited unjustly from the Second German War, gaining significant territory while (according to many) not investing much manpower or material resources.

[3] A Mendevan [North American] state now legally separate from Britain [a state comprising Wales, Devon, Kernow and parts of northern Ireland and Albion], corresponding roughly to East Texas, Louisiana, Arkansas, Oklahoma, and western Mississippi.

[4] This pun on βÄ€inhabitants of ProvenceβÄù and βÄ€rural, sheltered peopleβÄù is not uncommon in letters at this time, but not well understood in retrospect.

< attested from 15C in Kigman's poetry, an idiom literally meaning βÄ€to take the hammerβÄù. The noun is from later Latin martellus, diminutive of a variant of marculus βÄ€hammerβÄù. The verb is a direct descendant of Latin prehend≈ç βÄ€I take, grasp, seizeβÄù.

    from academic textbook In Context: The Vascon Ascendancy by Katrin Hindman, contributing to the In Context series, published by Nottinger University Primers through the 1980s and 90s.

Y Reyaum Vascon a Navarr eΟΑ Aquitain asseyeu ag commet dy regn d'Ambrosc III un eΟΑegl moneter vital.
The Vascon Kingdom of Navarre and Aquitaine at the start of Ambrose [AmbrΟ≤ix] III's reign inhabitated a key economic position.

Y paΟ·s sta l'onc entr y ci gran magl trocant republican ny Miterran (particuler posc Port CalΟ® redugnt a mesur dy fenΟß 1449)

eΟΑ y colanΟß marcer accelerandessem des Albion goineΟΑ e des, aust ancour, y citaΟΑ colluΟ·sc Theudesc e bautesc.
and the accelerating flow of goods from Welsh Albion and, further east, the Dutch and Graven guild cities.

Cant Andalus abattoy insorgenΟß politicβÄîdou cris a succession e l'abansanc 1457 ne Lisbon, com eΟß primerβÄî

dont ci sodal prepondraurn ja parmy l'aristocracy e posseyeurn forΟß politic remarcabr ny cort a Ambrosc.
members of which still predominated in the aristocracy and held significant political power in Ambrose's court.

Pall'oc faΟßon sta y stabliscment dy Novel Enterpris Navarran un manouvr treblaΟΑ :
In this way the founding of the New Navarre Enterprise was a three-fold man≈™uvre:

primer, oc reitfiau y position de Vascony com forniscour e porΟßonnour suprem de marΟß trocaΟΑ par Europ enter ;
first, it solidified Vascony's position as supreme supplier and distributor of trade goods throughout Europe;

dovem, oc dau a sy vassal y possendessem un rout vars autortaΟΑ e risceΟß y thron noc menaΟßant ;
second, it gave his most powerful vassals an avenue to power and riches that did not threaten his position,

treim (ig degnant i poΟΑe bogr savir), oc mis Vascony ne plaΟß por y descovart y signfiandessem ny siecr cinqueΟß acavarβÄΠ
third (though he could not know this), it set up Vascony to make the most important discovery of the fifteenth centuryβÄΠ

< first attested 10C as ede»ùl βÄ€small room, defensive coverβÄù, showing that the martial meaning was a very early semantic extension. Descends directly (though presumably from a northern form, given the incomplete lenition of stressed /d/) from Latin ΟΠdicula βÄ€small temple, chapel, house, or roomβÄù, diminutive of ΟΠdis βÄ€temple, roomβÄù. No religious sense is confirmed in the Boral; compare Lombard edicola βÄ€kiosk, stand < shrine, alcoveβÄù.

    Jo slanΟßau all'eΟΑegl, attenent a respirar calmessem.

    I rushed to the alcove, trying to breathe more quietly.

    / £o slanΥàtso a.leΥàΟΑijl | aΥàt…¦.n…¦nt a Υ¨r…¦s.piΥàrar kalΥàm…¦.s…¦m/

##

13m Septembr

    remainder of a letter sent in 1828 via steeplepost from Pentrose to , from the mathematician Darren Brodus (of the Vithor New University but at the time in convalescence at his family home) to his brother-in-law NiklΟΓs der Fecht, himself a professor at the  Benet School.

(Pentrose in red,  in blue)
image

βÄΠlibrary has finally provided me with DΟΦmmeyer's treatise on collection and delivery; I will not appease you by pretending to have read it, but be assured I will find the hours for it this very week! Dear Johan insists it is all atmospherical and futile nonsense, but then Johan trusts no mathematics since Craddock's book on distance room. Honestly I'm surprised he consents to use even the mechanical methods, but I suppose he isn't quite bone-headed enough to make his workshop designing more difficult for himself.

Have you any plans to go south this summer? I hear they shall reopen the arena in Verona to performancesβÄîthey say Bruno's Wings Afire will be the first shown there since the war! I had the pleasure of knowing her brother Jacom before she was ever known to the wider world (apparently they are astonishingly similar in appearance, even for twins); we fought together at Hulm in 21. That was an astonishingly-mismanaged offensive and in all honestly prolonged the war for months, but I mustn't start this rant, or I will never end.

Jacom had nothing but praise for his sister and their parents, in any case (although the desire for a warm bed and the call of peacetime Furlain no doubt inflenced his recollection), so I cannot do anything but recommend the opera to you. If in the end you hate the singing you will at least have the pleasure of restored Roman architecture!

Your friend,
Darren Brodus

##

19m September

    excerpted from 1997 academic textbook Del Christianizamento dell'Isole Norroveste (On the Christianisation of the Northwestern Isles), written by historian of mediΟΠval religion Dijaca Rofotto, working as a researcher and archivist for the Librario Soprano (Sovereign Library) of the Autonome University of Saint Marinus.

βÄΠsurvived, however, in those few remaining monasteries spared from the Danish raids (note in particular the Chronicle of Carlile, which survives only in copy at the Tremonnow Brethin House, having presumably been ferried across the Irish Sea before the monastery at Carlile burnt down in around 1013 N).

But to understand the final years of Wulfstan the Younger of Northumber, we must look two centuries earlier, to the very first Viking raids on the shores of Albion and Ireland. The tales of similar raids on Borland townsβÄîand on monasteries in particularβÄîwould surely have been common knowledge by the time they spread westward, sacking Halgrive in the summer of 764.

Nonetheless, the English kingdoms were prepared for these raids (and eventually, invasions) to vastly different degrees. Compare the fates of Merch Lincon to Northumbrian Yorwick: the former never fell, and in fact became the most heavily-fortified town in Markland for the several decades it served as the de facto border of Danish rule. In part this was the inevitable result of tensions along the Welsh border; even during the relatively-long period of peacetime both Theodric the Awefast and Owain of Brochwel maintained ever-more levy forces to police the long Severn Line.

By contrast, Yorwick almost gave itself over to Dane rule. Though convincing records are either fragmentary or clearly biased (even Brethin writing in this period cannot be entirely trusted), it is generally believed that the alderman's court threw him out pre-emptively as a sop, hoping to offer enough treasure freely to the invaders that they could avoid a total destruction of the city. Yorwick's key economic position within Dane-ruled Albion in the next century certainly suggest these efforts were not unsuccessfulβÄΠ

25m Septembr

    excerpted from David Huhn's economic history textbook Stadstahten: Sichrer Haven (City-States: Safe Harbor), a treatise on the role of independent trade cities in the development of mercantilism. The book was published in 1923 with funding from the Lunburg Schazhaus.

βÄΠclose of the thirteenth century as a convenient cutoff. The fiction of a surviving German Empire was laid to rest with the corpse of the first Kingdom of Saxony, with the domain of the Hoghbrocken dynasty reduced to their core lands upstream in Lepzi and Magdeburg. The newly-reduced Margrave Baldur of Saxony, being only 4 years old at the time, was understandably outman≈™uvred by the nascent Stadbund.

But the guild-states known variously as the Stadbund, the Samslutning or the North Collision (we see in Latin documents usually collisio theodisc) did not, of course, appear fully-formed at the dawn of the fourteenth century. We can trace the roots of intercivic contracts restricting tariffs, tolls and other fees back to the early twelfth century. Indeed, even at the height of the MediΟΠval Imperia, traditionally portrayed as a world (or a least a Europe) of central authority, mayors of the towns in the low Elbe and its tributaries were collaborating independently of their respective lieges.

We must look at early Bardwick, which along with Hamburg and Lunburg comprised the founding coreβÄîand later the trading heartβÄîof the Stadbund. Its particular interest stems from its dual position as court of the Hoghbrocken kings and as a highly mercantile trade city. King Ruprecht II in particular will serve as a usefulβÄΠ

7m Octobr

    excerpted from Borlish book Lugnt Ros Sur Ont Lugnt (Rosy Dawn on Glittering Waves), a popular account of the development of seafarers since the 1471 Landfall. Written by amateur historian Descard Ausogn in 1929, it was criticised for its uncritical view of pirate culture, and in particular his depiction of their role in the Romantic Wars.

βÄΠexample of early international cooperation, the Bordew-Audaz canal was finally completed in 1679, following over a decade of delays due ultimately to Vascony's losses to Andalus in the south.

These great infrastrusture projects characterise the Romantic period, although these decades would not gain that appellation for another century. The name is first used after the Democratic Wars, when the latter half of the seventeenth century was seen, nonsensically, as the last golden age of refinement and civilisation. It is hard not to contrast these insular, squabbling European states of the 1660s with, say, the great geoscopic voyages of mac Kellot and al-Casmi of the previous century, or the renewed Novomundine interest of the next.

Nonetheless, it is in this time that we can see the familiar modern map begin to emerge across Europe. The shape of Vascony begins to stabilise at last, and to the east the German marksmen and great mayors contemplate, perhaps, the idea of cooperation. Even the fractious religious politics further south threaten to give way to the more secular animosities of popular unrest and land grabs.

In particular, Naples finally finds itself unshackled, neither from defunct Greek imperial rule, nor from the heathens of Sicily, nor from the Papal embrace of Rome. With this context King Ladislau's obsessive focus on military strength, and in particular the Marina Ottima, seems entirely reasonableβÄΠ

And thus Marcuccio received the book, and studied from it. And after many months did he return to Ignazio, twin books in his arms.

PART Έ™: Answer the following questions in a single word. (maximum 5 minutes)

1. In which county, ruled by Count JerrΟ≤n, did the 1170 "Emergency" take place, utterly destroying the keep and leading to the secession of Burgundy from (and eventually the dissolution of) the Constant Empire?

tegnt visacer /Υàtint Υ¨vi.zaΥàts…¦r/ [t…Σ…±Υ¨vi.z…êΥàts…¦:] complexion, facial appearance

< 18C, literally "facial tint", first used in the early ethnographies translated into Boral. The noun tegnt "tint, shade, colour, dye" is from the Latin tinctus "dipped in, treated, coloured, tinged", past participle of ting≈ç "I wet, moisten, dip, immerse" (whence Boral tegndr). The adjective is a standard derivation from visaΟß "face, visage", from Old Boral visaz»ù "face, regard, look". This is as if from Latin *visΡ¹ticum, though it is likely a later derivation, from vis < visus "look". Justification for the retention of short <i> is unclear but forms with <e> are entirely unattested.

Lour y tegnt visacer suggeren jorn larc sou sol blanc.

Their complexions were indicative of many days in the sun.

/lur i Υàtint Υ¨vi.zaΥàts…¦r Υ¨s…Σg. £eΥàr…¦n  £…îrn lark su Υàs…îl blank/

    excerpted from a 1934 copy of the Fiellas Dimenja, a Marsellan entertainment newspaper incorporated in 1871 by the Guiraud Colloquium. ...replaced with new blood as VrΟ©queu Margouille qualifies for the Kentish team going into the fourth LGT. Despite rumours that lineball is looking westward to Hasiny next year, the bilateral committee has refused to comment.

FILM IN REVIEW
- Hœân a KyvodoΟΑ (The Ascendant), in odyssey from the 7th of July

I shall be honestβÄîthe timing of this release so soon after the solstice perhaps let me better indulge the obviously-propagandist elements of Dormaget's latest historical courtly drama. The Vascon Ascendancy is no new ground for the screen, with Agochino's more personal Sou Ambroxo (I am Ambrose) defining the popular perception of Vascony's longest-reigning king. Nevertheless, I find The Ascendant to be a charming retelling of those turbulent years after the 1471 Landfall, not least in its choice to focus on the more minor personages of Ambrose's court.

As an example (though her Vask is terrible in the few scenes they stray from Cambrick), Lyneth Madox plays Jata, the seamstress-wife of an Enterprise sailor. It is through her eyes that we can understand the whole of the city of Bilvaw as it is changed by the Crown's attentions. Throughout, Madox gives Jata a steel resolve and implacable practicality sadly lacking in today's usual dramatic protagonists.

Domaget uses the narrow view to illuminate the broad in every aspect of his filmmaking, and we really see the benefits of such an approach here. If the scenes were confined to the palaces and castles of Huzatre and Cartaw, it would be hard to connect the Welsh tangles in Albion (one does rather wish the film had spent less time on the Wars of Fealty, but such things are inevitable when Britain sets a film in the fifteenth century) to the second marriage of Alexandra of Vascony and her famed voyage. Instead we can feel the sense of parental obligation warring with wanderlust in both threads of this filmic tapestry.

The Ascendant will doubtless be a staple of the summer screen, and it well deserves the honour. For the best experience I recommend you spend the afternoon drinking Empordan and singing as many songs of glory as you can think of.
βÄ™Jacint Brunat

βÄΠwould not be formally recognised until Burgundy's defeat two years later at the Battle of MargΟ®s, it was this event which cemented House AmigΟ≤t's position as primus inter pares in the new republic. 

At least, that is, for the next eighteen years of manoeuvring against a hungry Barcelon and their piratical allies who de facto ruled Marsella (it is generally understood that Lord AmigΟ≤t [Daniel Menton] established Marsella as the capital of Provence in large part to closely monitor Jondumal's fleets, about which see Part III: Old Jeans). By the end of his rule in 1517, Lord AmigΟ≤t had solidified the borders with Burgundy and Barcelon, with Narbona left with double obligations to Menton and to Jaume III.

It is also supposedβÄîAmigΟ≤t did not do us the service of keeping a chronicleβÄîthat his rule lasted eighteen years to allow Menton to ensure his chosen successor enjoyed near-unanimous support of the Concert, the Provincial legislative house modelled after the Venisians'. His first choice Raimond Aurellex, who had married his youngest sister and whose brother Lohis had died leading the charge at the Battle of Satillew, died in a hunting accident in 1511 (and despite the popular understanding, this particular incident was likely not an assassination).

In the end, the Concert elected Menton's cousin Antoin AmigΟ≤t to lead the Concert under the close attention and martial guidance of daus Sanz, through whom Daniel Menton doubtless exercised his influence long after his nominal stepping down. But military advice would be of only limited use to Antoin, as in 1519 a new threat arrived in Europe not seen since the days of the German Secession. The Second Great Dying struck naval powers early and mercilessly, and Provence was to be no exceptionβÄΠ

autun /oΥàt…Σn/ autune, (in general) sparkling wine

also written vin autun /vi.noΥàt…Σn/ autune wine

< from early 18C Autun, from phrase vin d'Autun βÄ€wine from AutunβÄù, a region on the French-Burgund border where the first widely-sold aphlox wine (commonly, fizzing wine) was produced by Vindrons Sairzaus, thanks to improvements in sealant technology and glass-making. (The VS would go on to pioneer high-pressure components in machinal production through the Workshop decades.) The word has been used for fizzing wine in general since the 1810s.

28m Octobr

    translation into Borlish and English of a recipe for Frigotta de Rei Jaume (King James Stew), taken from mealbook Pasti Mezziterreni (Middlesea Meals), written in 1880 by traveller Josforo Sant'Angelo.

Preu cos : l'amand tostir fin a aurfiaΟΑ e lou pisar ne pouvr gros. An frigsoir laΟΑ l'ougl raccaldar, e soulor l'amand, agl, ugnlac eΟΑ arjon frigr par cinqueΟß minut atorn, ben fin ag leum sein mol.
First bake the almonds until golden and grind to a coarse powder. In a broad pan heat the oil, then gently fry the almond, garlic, onion and pepper for roughly fifteen minutes, or until the vegetables are soft.

Scon cos : y gran pevr e demy a vurt mettr e toglar con. Posc dou minut un dragm ag vin mettr e parmettr de boglir pres apart. Or y rougl scadom ben scadom pisaΟΑ mettr, y remanent ag vin scencar dessur, e tout cocinar cinqueΟß minut may.
Add the drypepper and half the herbs and stir together. After two minutes add a dram of the wine and allow it to mostly boil off. Now add the shadome sauce or chopped shadomes, pour in the rest of the wine, and cook for fifteen minutes more.

TarΟß cos : y zossa a pescot saleyar e lou appoyar ny rougl. Y frigsoir covauclir e laiscar cinq minut, fin ag pescot sein just cogt. Y repasc descovrir, appendr con y remanent ag vurt (ben y sunt calscon desiraΟΑ) eΟΑ y saf jait dessur. Le dar ne bol con pan dur.
Salt the strips of fish and push them into the sauce. Cover the pan and leave for five minutes, until the fish is just cooked. Uncover, add the rest of the herbs (or whichever amount suits) and cast the juice over the dish. Serve bowled with hardbread.

atras /aΥàtraz/ […êΥàt Äaz] across (as an adverb), over, from one side to the other

< first seen as one word in 12C atras βÄ€acrossβÄù, making it one of the last positional adverbs to enter common useβÄîearlier a tras is attested with the same meaning. ##

31r Octobr

    in translation from L'Inclusion Deviante di Conti Polari (The Deviance Incorporation of Folk Tales), 1970 sociological work by Dr Tanasio Capretti, theologian and humanist at the University of Florence. It was published simultaneously with dozens of other related books from academies across the world, in order to coincide with the hundred and twentieth anniversary of the Laic Declarations and the concommittant realignment in the mid-nineteenth century that heralded the Good Game period.

Chapter 14: La Caccia Salvaggia (The Wild Hunt)

Just as the Danes attributed the thunder to the wrath of Thor, so too we can imagine whence came the tales of a great army of hunting party of ghosts, riding and shouting across the night sky. The howling winds, after all, sound not so differently from the howls of hunting dogs. From isolated hamlets north of the Humber to snow-blanketed Alpine villages, the motherly advice given to all children when they hear the Great Wild Hunt (or the Herlathing, or the WΟΦtendes Heer) passing overheard is the same: stay inside, look away, do your best to pretend they aren't there.

Most scholars today accept that the various folk traditions of a ghostly hunt stem from a single ancient source, at least in Europe (the similar Lanhyatian stories are of course unrelated). Many of these tales come specifically from the Danes' Oskarrej via cultural transmission in the Viking AgeβÄîfor example, in Borland the story of the cauΟß ragnt shows clear signs of Danish origin (not least that it is usually led by Balegr, a common kenning for Odin), but the tales often incorporate the graveyard fiddlers, who are idiosyncratic to the island.

Like so many of these tales, what had been amoral or even virtuous characters in the early mediΟΠval period (see Chapter VI, and the works of Braun & Kindler) were transformed by Christianisation into devils and demonic figures. It should therefore be of no surprise that the early Deviance Movement seized the imagery of the Wild Hunt for themselves. CrΟΓyenschot's celebrated Ad Pentaculum spends much of the runtime detailing Jochem's induction into the Yacht, including oneβÄΠ

< from 10C in Old Boral vriΟΑel βÄ€curtains; bandages, wrapped clothβÄù, a nominal diminutive derivation from vriΟΑar βÄ€to wrap, plait, bind (of cloth)βÄù; the verb became obsolete by the Middle Boral period, displaced by fasΟßar (modern fassar). This verb came from Old English wrΡΪΟΨan βÄ€to twist, wrap, bindβÄù, from Classical German wrΡΪΟΨan βÄ€to weave, twistβÄù. This also gives French ridew βÄ€curtainsβÄù, probably via High German ridil βÄ€ribbonβÄù (note that Norman French retains cortine, from Late Latin c≈çrtΡΪna βÄ€curtainβÄù).

    Un hom au y vriΟΑel jognt por alcun lumner yemois y cambr.

    Someone had closed the curtains so no light would enter the bedroom.

folker /f…îlΥàk…¦r/ [f äwΥàk…¦Υê] fern, bracken, thick brambles; countryside covered in these plants, especially when it is thus impassable; (in mathematics) fractal, self-similar figure

< first attested 11C in various forms as Old Boral folcair, falca»ùr, felcair,βÄΠ βÄ€fern, brackenβÄù. This comes from a reconstructed Vulgar Latin *filicΡ¹ria (βÄ€thicket of fernsβÄù), although there were probably variants with inital vowel as <a> or <u>. 3m Novembr

    in translation to Borlish and English from the 1998 scholastic quire Sviluppi in Philosophia dal Periodo Revitalisto (Developments in Philosophy since the Revitalist Period) by historical deixist Jovanna Ariperto, researcher at the Autonome University of Saint Marinus. 

RenduΟΑ connuΟΑessem par y ci ouvr a Dufresne e Fayeda y dezein final de siecr settoΟß, l'oc apponment affirmau toΟΑ viΟΑ aurn se formaΟΑ ag moment de Creation.
Popularised by the works of Dufresne and Fayeda in the last decade of the seventeenth century, this theory asserted that all life had formed at the moment of Creation.

I parmaneu n'affirmant casc veunt prosequent crescoirn des gendr minuscr de lou (nommaΟΑ zoia par Dufresne, valent noc oc broΟΑiar con y benoΟß actual dy parol viΟΑ unicasel descrint), lou meΟΑes form minusclessem stant y generation grand-figl, et cetera.
It further stated that each successive generation grew from miniature versions of themselves (which Dufresne called zoia, not to be confused with our modern use of the term for one-case life), themselves containing yet-smaller forms for the grandchild generation, and so on.

Figur temporane un form human piquessem afont ne casel sperm mostrant illustrn l'oc apponment l'un eΟΑ y creinΟß y dou ig un maΟΑr no sta for hum y babe (semnaΟΑ dy paΟΑr) la aΟΑoint.
Contemporary diagrams showing a tiny human form inside a spermcase illustrate both this theory and the impression that the mother was merely the soil where the baby, planted by the father, grew.

Ci attriboint, problem de predestination innaΟΑ coll'oc parceuΟßonβÄîpu certan y ci rendoirn l'apponment abhorrent ben heres ny domain AugsbourgerβÄî
Setting aside issues of predestination inherent in this approachβÄîthough of course this rendered the theory anathema or even heretical in the Augsburg dominiaβÄî

y reclam pront (veyaΟΑ d'al-Siddik l'ouvr 1696 Ag Figl a Zeno) sta y fait de recarnt un caΟΑen infiniΟΑ de zoia jamay picquessem postular, voluΟΑ hamtar ne casc animal mascr sur y Tar.
the immediate objection (see al-Siddik's 1696 work On the Children of Zeno) was that you would need to posit an infinite chain of progressively-smaller zoia to be present inside every male animal on the planet.

##

6m Novembr

    taken from A Mapbook Compendium of Europe, from the Division of Rome until the German Secession (450βÄ™1250 N), published by the Tommarth Open School of History in 1997. It is used as part of the supplementary reference materials provided for students sitting history scitations in many Merch universities.

The Drengotian Empire, named for its ruling family the Drengot (formerly the ruling ducal family of Normandy) had by 1120 N maintained stable borders for several decades. The empire owes this impression of peace in part to the decentralised structure of the realm: Tolose even held a standing army for its perennial skirmishes with neighbouring Barcelon.

But of course none were more independent in the so-called Constant Empire (a name used only in retrospect in an attempt to lend the realm gravitas, referencing Rome's District of Constantius) than Kernow and Devon, who a century earlier had been vital to the Norman conquest of Kent. Indeed, in 1121 N King Vigo of Greater Devon would attack Wales without the support of Emperor Roger II.

Roger II spent most of his reign in Paris, with his younger brother Matthew ruling in his name in London. By 1120 N, much of the empire's treasury has been spent on defending the long eastern border with Willemy. In particular, the construction of a new Great Castle at VΟ©sonΟßon had been completed the year before, the last point in a defensive line spanning from Flanders to the Alps.

7m Novembr

broccat /broΥàkat/ [b Ä äΥàkat] brocade, thick silk fabric, usually embossed with embroidered patterns and perhaps gold thread; by extension, gaudy or over-formal clothing

< 14C in Middle Boral broccat "brocade", probably directly from Italian broccato "embossed cloth" via naval trade, but possibly via Burgund or another French variety (see also cascat "waterfall"). The Italian is from brocco "small nail", ultimately of Gaulish Kelt origin. Pejorative sense from the early 19C, developing in the Straightforward Fashion popular across northern Europe at that time.

    Y vriΟΑel stan ny broccat y ci annaΟΑ a sy paΟΑr ramenant.

8m Novembr

zoia /zoΥàja/ [z äjΥàja] microbe, microorganism; any of the one-celled forms of life

< late 17C in translation of medical and biological texts, from Scholatic Latin zoia "microbe", originaly a neuter plural but quickly used as a singular. The Latin formed from Greek *zoion (ΈΕαΩΖαΩêΈΩΈΫ /zo.i.on/), literally "small animal". For a period in the late 19C some attempted to restrict its use to microbes without kernel [nucleus] but this never caught on. Whether to include bdella [viruses] as zoia was more contentious, and is still inconsistent across modern languages.

    MemoraΟΑ vos y zoia cholera produgnt ?

    Do you remember which bacteria cause cholera?

    /Υ¨me.moΥàraΈΗ vo i zoΥàja Υ¨ko.leΥàra proΥàdajnt/

NEWS AFIELD: SCANDAL! Hasinic [2] PRINCE ADMITS 30βÖä MILLION OF DEBT

Prince Pether Tamaha, lesser-known son of the illustrious Hasinic monarchy, has admitted to the press that he owes thirty million poise (over twenty million trone) in debt to speculation companies and to national banks across the continent from Mashick to Ambrosia [3]. Furthermore, his grandfather the King has declined to bail him out, in an unprecedented move.

Longtime speculation has held that the young prince (who hosted a lavish thirtieth birthday celebration in the Davarn Heul [4] this February, with over five thousand attendees) has been regularly running through his funds and relying on his family's coffers. "For King Maldwen to now stem the flow of gold to his grandchild indicates one of two things," writes Matties Gramunt, Provincial ambassador to Gulf Mendeva [5]. "Either personal relations between the prince and his family have deteriorated to a serious nazir, or else the Tamaha monarchy is itself in dire financial straits." The steward convoker to the family has so far refused to comment on the issue.

The prince himself has not been seen in public since his participation in the Taishan [6] centenary celebration at the border city of...

[4] The Sun Hotel, a world-famous resort town. [5] North America's southern coast. [6] from Taisha, a Gulf Mendevan state (cognate with Texas).

18m Novembr

    scitation questions taken from the 1986 paper for the course Scoclat e Coscou : Cappatia par y KintanΟß Mondial (Chocolate and Rubber: Cappatia during the Twining Century), taught by the Faculty of Domain History at the New Vithor University in Borland.

PART B: The BrΟ≥derrat Wayports in Cappatia

1. Read the following excerpt from Haddani's notoriously-innaccurate Empire of the Sun and answer the subsequent questions.

βÄ€It was the pride of Emperor Matthias II that brought the Germans across the Atlantic in search of new lands and peoples in the mid-sixteenth century. News of the joint Morrack and British voyages had inflamed all Europe with an irrepressible wanderlust, and while the Kazmid and Vascon enterprises had firmly established themselves as sole merchants in eastern Mendeva and Horizic Cappatia, few had yet mapped the Atlantic coastline of Cappatia. Thus there was an attainable goal for the Emperor to assign to the great fleet at his disposal: discover all that these lands had to offer, from Berpittang wood to crimson Mackow feathers.βÄù

a) With reference to the Neuwillem BrΟ≥derratcronike (1561-97), evaluate Haddani's framing of the factors that led to the German exploration of Atlantic Cappatia in the latter half of the sixteenth century.

b) To what extent was Emperor Matthias II (1542-68) able to influence the mercantile decisions of the Stadbund at all? Compare the stance of the Drachman merchant dynasty towards the various monarchic European powers with which they dealt, and the underlying economic conditions leading to these dynamics.

c) How separately can we consider the activities of the BrΟ≥derrat in establishing Neuwillem, Parnow and other wayports from the similar activities of Morrack in Mashick and Tavancy; what collaboration and cross-pollination of methods and technologies, if any, occurred between the Vetomundine companies?

19m Novembr

accapellar /aΥ¨ka.peΥàlar/ […ê.ka.p…ΣΥàl…ëΥê] to don a hat, to put a hat on

< attested, at least in the past participial form accapellaΟΑ βÄ€wearing a hatβÄù (the only form in productive use, though the finite verb occurs sporadically), since the fourteenth century. It is modelled after other verbs in a-X-ar, such as arrimbandar βÄ€to beribbonβÄù, abancar βÄ€to make landfallβÄù and akirtar βÄ€to igniteβÄù. Specifically, it is derived from capel βÄ€hatβÄù, from Vulgar Latin cappellus with the same meaning, itself a diminutive from Latin cappa βÄ€cape, cloakβÄù.

    Lou dou accapellaurn e sorteurn accougl.

    They both put on their hats and left together.

    /lu du aΥ¨ka.peΥàlo.rnΧ© e s…îrΥàtaw.rnΧ© aΥàkujl/

20m Novembr

pareiveΟΑr /Υ¨pa.riΥàv…¦.ΟΑrΧ©/ [Υ¨pa. Ä…ΣΥàv…¦.ΟΑ…ê] windowsill, ledge, skirting

< attested since 18C as parey-veΟΑr βÄ€windowsillβÄù, modelled after French pareil-verre with the same meaning (Boral otherwise having very few such verb-noun compounds). Literally meaning βÄ€levels the glassβÄù, the verb comes from parey βÄ€same, equalβÄù, a dialect form from Vulgar Latin pariclus, from Latin pΡ¹r βÄ€even, equalβÄù. The noun veΟΑr is directly from Latin vitrum βÄ€glassβÄù. The hyphen was dropped in the 1870 reform a dreΟßment which standardised Boral spelling.

21r Novembr

    excerpted in Boral translation from Saxon mathematician Johans DΟΦmmeyer's 1827 publication Sammel und Sendungen (Sets and Functions), a seminal work that attempted to resolve some of the disagreements in the mathematical community regarding the proper definition of limits, the continuum, and related concepts.

Nos approismau y matier com Ferenc (GeometriΟΠ Inquadrabiles, 1819) par casc concreuΟßon des axiom daΟΑ admettent.
We take the approach of Ferenk (GeometriΟΠ Inquadrabiles, 1819) in allowing any concrescence of given axioms.

Por y continuum cos particuler caurreu nos uncos starchessem : dou concreuΟßon calscon (y cinq soumission enonΟßaΟΑ ne capparol 7 suffignt) son de natur jammel.
For the continuum in particular we will obtain something stronger: any two concrescences (satisfying the five propositions listed in Chapter 7) are, essentially, identical.

Un bustr senglessem vil y method illustrar. Nos eu idea naΟΑeu a mission s des font W vars bers X ; l'oc attriboy a casc sodal ne W l'un ne X.
A simpler example will demonstrate the principle. We have an intuitive idea of a function s from source W to target X, assigning to each element of W one from X.

Por l'oc mission costroir com cougl manifest, nos poum calscon de dou noΟßon ja noscon vis benoΟßar.
To construct this function explicitly as a set, we may make use of either of two notions we have already seen.

Primer, nos definisc ig mission son porΟßonment dy som W+X la afont casc porΟßon inclun un sodal exact des X.
First, we define functions to be partitions of the disjoint union WβäîX in which each part contains precisely one element of X.

Cossy cogleu nos con un x ne X y cougl (voit, possibr!) de w ne W, suffignt ig wβÜΠx.
Thereby we collect with an x in X the (possibly empty!) collection of w in W for which wβÜΠx.

24m Novembr

    in translation from 1970 historical review Conjugation, Operation e Diabolica in Musica : Y CommouΟΑ Deviant Primal (The Early Deviance Movement), written by Borlish popular historian Catrin VeΟßount, published to coincide with the hundred and twentieth anniversary of the Laic Declarations.

βÄΠnow arrive on our own shores, for we cannot ignore MonΟßating any longer.

The sixth Baron MonΟßating was born to Lord Josua and Lady Evangelin in the winter of 1770 at their estate in Launembrig. Lord Josua, inevitably, was himself a controversial figure in Damvath society for several reasons: having been born in the Cappatian staddomain, having married a noblewoman of Warnine descent (far too recently for Vetomundine sensibilities), and finally having inherited the Barony of Haubrath from his second cousin following a decade-long legal battle (one of the longest legal disputes in Borland history). His decision thereupon to dissolve the senior title and retain only MonΟßating was the final nail in the coffin for any hope of respectability.

Nonetheless they remained in Borland (complaining endlessly about the climate, if accounts in their acquaintances' letters are to be believed!) and bore six children, of whom our hero Simon was the eldest. Raised in the shadow of scandal, of legal and political battles, Simon MonΟßating learnt early how thinly-justified the power structures of the world could be. The Launembrig estate hosted regular gatherings of theorists and philosophers; the earliest writings we have in Simon's hand are from a tutor's worksheet carried by accident to Denmark in the papers of Jan Prestr in 1779 (author of Discriptione Bestiarum Suboles Ferentis βÄ€On the Classification of the Child-Bearing BeastsβÄù).

However interesting his childhoodβÄîby eighteen Simon could play the clavier and the torriot, and speak Latin, Dutch, and Vascon (and even Warnine, according to his defamers)βÄîwe remember the sixth Lord MonΟßating today for his later escapadesβÄΠ
