##

30.5: Masquira Romance

Masquira romance is a genre of stories which typically feature criminal (or crime-fighting) characters, as well as plots driven by hidden identities, high society and complicated schemes. It overlaps with the slightly-later spycraft genre, especially in modern works which draw on both literary traditions.

The name 'masquira' derives from Ezio Carvallo's 1860 trevold Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico "In Either a Mask or a Suit", which is widely considered to have pioneered the genre.

The kevler romance is a subgenre of masquira focused on glamorous thefts and schemes, particularly popular in nineteenth-century Borland.

History
Although many of the tropes of the genre have been use in literature since time immemorial, we see the birth of a distinct genre of masquira romance in the late Workshop period, through the prolific work of Portingale author Ezio Carvallo. His dozen-strong series of trevolds, for example, almost single-handedly created the concept of a kevler story (although we can see a parallel trend in the older Cathayan tapsue "magpie" tales).

The popularity of masquira supplanted to some extent the dwindling voidtale fashion in Europe, as well as various herdtale traditions across the Novomund.

In the 1940s and 50s there was a resurgence in popularity for masquira romance, christened the 'Spring Tide'; this period saw many film adaptations the genre's classics and thus cemented its place in modern popular culture.

Social Detaxion
The narrative focus in masquira romance on characters with hidden identities has led several marginal groups to embrace masquira tales over the decades. Perhaps most notably, the genre was very popular early on with the semi-public tovarick communities in the Riverine Cities. These communities spread the influence of masquira through Europe and the Novomund, particularly along with the broader Deviance movement.

Indeed, the tovarism connection was barely obfuscated in some works (see particularly Takach's We Will Burn Together), and the popularity of the genre is widely understood to have contributed to the Household Renovation.

The popularity of the subgenre of kevler romance in Borland during the Good Game period has attracted much commentary. In the context of a scandal-ridden royal family and a generalised class-levelling instinct pervading the politics of contemporary Borland, it is perhaps unsurprising that "stories of heroes entering high society as outsiders in connit in order to make away with the hoarded treasures of the intolerably wealthy" struck a particularly harmonious chord.

Notable works
A Question of Identity (IdentaΟΑ Partenent), 1981 tale by Borlish author Carl Havatr
Apollo on Holiday (Apollon CogmaΟΑer), 2009 story written by Endric Kirennaga
In Either a Mask or a Suit (Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico), 1860 trevold by Ezio Carvallo which named and pioneered the genre
Men of Jet and Diamond (Dynnon o VuhuΟΑ a Diamont), 1890 tale by Cadœâgan Torriœâr of Hasiny later adapted into a wildly-successful film in New Provence
One Evening Will Have to Be Enough (Un Seir Deura Soffire), 1913 trevold by Rone author JosephΟ®ne Foilliers notable for being the first book on record to sell more than a million copies in its first year
Outrun the Dawn (Die Demrung Ο€verholn), 1946 work written by Bronswick native Elke Hannawald remembered for subverting the trope of the masked hero by revealing the protagonist's identity in the book's second chapter
Waging World Peace (PaΟß Mondial Accommettent), 1968 giftale written by Neuvort author Dion Becolla
We Will Burn Together, written by Takach Domoko and notable for its openly tovarick characters

31.1: 2-D Geometry

    excerpt in translation from the correspondence of Dewock Barclythe (1452-1539 N),Friar of Tremonnow; these lines are from a missive sent to his acquaintance Lluprando da Treviso at the University of Saint Marinus.

Quemant es locutionnar ig cas y plan d'Euclid, dou form mozaΟΑ calscon son tojorn parallel, oc a digr il hamtn un plan parey.
It is good to remark that in the context of EuclidβÄôs plane, all mozate forms are parallel, which is to say that they are coplanar.

L'oc parceuΟßon a nossy JarleΟΑ menaΟΑ all'apponment ig form mozaΟΑ posn un ongit por asmuth posseΟ·r innaΟΑ, cos equal com se poΟΑ un tramou fornir con l'un asmuth des dou ouΟßon.
This observation has led our Jarleth to conjecture that mozate forms might possess themselves a sort of direction, in the same way that a line segment may be endowed with one of two directions.

L'ongit por asmuth y tesquallessem posseΟ·br par un form com circol ben scaΟΑr es lon dout l'ongit aut par un orlosc soler.
The most natural sense of direction a form like a circle or square might possess is undoubtedly the sense of a sundial.

Calscon exact vel signfiar ig un cercol ay voltisc soler ben guyer, ne diagram defagl noβÄôs l'oc voltisc depegntar; y vellisqment normal de saget benoΟßar suffig ac fort just com cas form lign.
Whatever precisely it should mean for a circle to be sunward or awkward, in diagram it is not difficult to depict; the usual expedient of arrowheads suffices as well here as it does for line forms.

##

31.5: The Deviance movement

The Deviance movement, also known as Anti-Paradise theology, is a religious, artistic and political ideology characterised by non-conformity as well as exploration of the avant-garde in all areas of society.

The term βÄ€devianceβÄù has been applied to the movement almost since its inception, with both positive and negative affect. Some scholars have theorised that the term reflects the Wessern dialect pronunciation of standard βÄ€defianceβÄù, although this is not widely accepted.
History

The Deviance movement arose in the late eighteenth century during the Long Peace, in an environment of rapid scientific progress. In Europe, much of the development of Anti-Paradise theology took place within the Promethean clubs of Albion and Danaw. These regions were both home to significant religious minorities and the Church (of whichever stripe) was strongly associated with local authority.

An important early work to the Deviance movement was the anonymously-published βÄ‰And We Will Love the FireβÄô (originally Et Nous Amerons les Fux), which began circulating in 1799 to immediate moral outrage. Though almost a dozen plausible candidates for its authorship have been seriously proposed, no consensus has ever been reached on the issue.

The early Deviance movement coincided with the height of the voidtale craze launched by Elsebeth SneiderβÄôs 1795 trevold 'Through a Black MirrorβÄô. Many of the ideas conjured in voidtale works were incorporated into Anti-Paradise theology, and SneiderβÄôs works in particular were embraced by the movement. However, Sneider herself despised Anti-Paradism; in her own words:

    βÄ€It is not only their strange ideas and lonely, nihilist universe, dear ThifΟΛne, but that so many of their number have approached me as if I were their most natural ally!βÄù

The early public reaction to the Deviance movement varied significantly. It has been blamed for the resurgence of the Modest Arrangement in Spain and Gaul.

In 1850 a tide of secularisation began with the passing of the Laic Declarations in Belgrade, which have largely been credited to the strong influence of Anti-Paradise theology in the Riverine Cities after the collapse of Danaw.

In the later nineteenth century the Deviance movement was connected to the spread of the genre of masquira romance, possibly via their shared connections to tovarick communities.
Features

Many composers of the early nineteenth century were affiliated with the Deviance movement, and considered their music to be in the Deviant spirit. The music of Deviance composers emphasised emotion and the manipulation of the audience, and this helped make music the least controversial part of Anti-Paradism in its first few decades.

The Deviance movement was interested in folk traditions and stories; one example is the European tale of the Wild Hunt, which was portrayed in the younger CrayenschotβÄôs opera Ad Pentaculum. They were particularly interested in those figures which had been cast as demons or evil spirits by the Church.

Supporters of the early Deviance movement are often portrayed as wearing lewd or otherwise-inappropriate clothing; it is difficult to ascertain the extent to which this reflected a real practice, as opposed to it being an invention by their detractors.
Notable supporters

    Dr Mickel CrΟΓyenschot (fl. c19), physician in French Willemy and early public tovarick
    Lord Simon, sixth Baron MonΟßating (b. 1770), infamous libertine of the Damvath melee
    Poshack Nerwaman (fl. c19), famed lady explorer originally of Mashick, sister to the composer Mollam Bushta
    Ton CrΟΓyenschot (fl. c19), composer remembered for the opera Ad Pentaculum, marked entirely in five

32.3: oblimanΟß "passion"

- desire, craving, longing, strong attraction towards something or the condition of wanting something deeply;
- passion, enthusiasm, zeal, fervour, a powerful emotion or excitement in the pursuit of some end goal;

Etymology: nominal derviation from oblimar "to desire, crave", from synonymous Medieval Latin oblΡΪm≈ç (or directly from a Latin noun oblΡΪmantia, though there is no record of this derivation in Latin until the fifteenth century). This verb is formed from the intensive prefix ob- and the verb lΡΪm≈ç "I am eager for, I lust after".

In turn, lΡΪm≈ç and the related noun lΡΪmus reflect a relatinisation of Old Boral lim "lust" (now archaic outside the expression lim aΟ·r "be eager"), which must be a descendant of synonymous Latin libΡΪd≈ç (accusative libΡΪdinem; compare the outcome of Latin -t≈Ϊdinem as -tum in e.g. soltum "solitude", presumably via an intermediary -t≈Ϊmine).

##

32.5: Monarchs of Borland

summary of the lives of two rulers of Borland in the first millennium.

Victor Rossetus 'the Red-haired' (fl. c6) was the first Prase of the Boral Diocese. He was the first unitary ruler of an independent polity comprising a significant fraction of the island of Borland. He unified the urbanised east of the land while the west and south were settled by Angles to form Angland and Southbar, respectively, and it was under him that so-called 'Roman Borland' first began to succesfully repel the new settlers.

---

Jothey, King of Borland (fl. 898), known in Borlish as JoΟΑeg / £oΥàΟΑij/, was the last ruler of the First Kingdom of Borland before it fell to the Danes in 898. The kingdom was conquered when Jothey was only a young man, shortly after his marriage to Brenna of Barrow, cousin of King Roderick of Dunclothe.

However, the life of King Jothey (as recorded in Brethin chronicles) continued as a monarch-in-exile for several more decades. He fled with his wife to Sothbar, where they had several children. They remained at court there for over twenty years, until the skirmishes between Sothbar and Dane Borland finally ended with Sothbar's capitulation and the unification of the island for the first time since being part of the Roman Empire.

Fearful of imprisonment or even execution so as to fully extirpate the former royal line and thus nullify the possibility of future rival claimants, Jothey fled once more with his wife and children to Kent.

##

33.1: Film Review

excerpt in translation from a review of Zachariah, a horror film about a vampire released in 1950.

Un sgaranΟß deull'ouvr usual de Kiawaman, l'oc fabr zortlasc nos ramen vars y dezein 1820, parmy y scadriscenΟß dy PaΟß Lonc eΟΑ y commet de Rix Reagent.
A departure from Kiawaman's usual fare, this blacklair tale takes us back to the 1820s, amid the disintegration of the Long Peace and the beginning of Reaction War. 

Des y rout accis sou aer ny Boxa autonom vars l'ossatur parsanant de Stamboul, l'oc adaptation dy trevold canonic de Petcov 'Majat Cherveniat' (Y Segr Roug) atten a form redar ag gur e l'enigma ne nossy catr heros sgardar y natur vray dy Zacharia eponym descovrir cos lent.
From the gaslit streets of independent Boxa to the recovering shell of Stamboul, this adaptation of Petcov's classic trevold 'Majat Cherveniat' (The Red Man) tries to recreate the dread and mystery of watching our four heroes slowly discover the true nature of the eponymous Zachariah.

InfeliΟß es ig y ci bel horam d'≈™cumen e l'affixion d'un cogmaΟΑer risc tavancin (dont y cainΟß secuΟΑ es majortaΟΑer emprontaΟΑ des y ci d'Elenor ny livr) sein y zantr soltumesc n'un film scompiglous ceter.
Unfortunately, the beautiful landscape shots and the addition of a rich Tavancine tourist (whose plotline is mostly borrowed from the book's Elenor storyline) are the high points of an otherwise messy film.

##

33.5: The Wars of Fealty

The Wars of Fealty were a series of conflicts between the polities of Albion in the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries which lasted almost fifty years.

History
The traditional account asserts that the Wars of Fealty broke out due to a dispute over the location of the Redstone Chair, a low-backed wooden throne inlaid with a sandstone block said to have been the stone over which the tenth-century Elved Marriage (which ended hostilities between Dane Northumber and Markland for almost a century and on which many subsequent monarchs had been crowned) was solemnised.

As political alliances enmeshed the polities of Albion, over some years the entire island was dragged into the conflict. It threatened to spread further still: France spent nearly two decades with growing animosity towards Kent under the Two Henries to the north, but the two polities reached reconciliation in the early 1490s.

The relatively-new Kingdom of Britain (formed of Wales and its outlying tallaths, along with Kernow and Sodrick) started from a position of strength, and managed to extract many concessions in the early years of the wars. This culminated in the 1483 Battle of Rexam, which expanded the borders of Wales in the north and killed the last Duke of Chestre; after this, Britain was able to avoid the harsher conflicts of the wars' latter years.

In the final years of the Wars of Fealty, a code known as Thieves' Script was employed by the forces of Andrew III of Markland, apparently adapted from similar systems used by gangs of housebreakers in Nottingham. It is remembered today as a very early precursor of codes like steeplescript or modern one-zeffre encoding.

Aftermath
The poorer economic conditions of the Albic polities in the wake of the Wars of Fealty and the ongoing population displacements/migrations throughout the island left them woefully vulnerable to the ravages of the Second Great Dying. Almost twice as many deaths per capita are recorded in Markland and English Kent compared with Norman Kent or Willemy across the British Sea.

The advantageous position of the Kingdom of Britain, by contrast, contributed to the successses of King Howel of Britain's expansion of naval trade (typified by the ship Ph≈™bos circumnavigating Africa and reaching Borune in 1516).

To the east, Borland (never directly involved in the Wars of Fealty) had received an influx of immigration from Albion, as people were displaced in the many border conflicts and attempted conquests. In particular, this period saw substantial Devoner and Kernish immigration to the cities of southern Borland.

34.1: Wearing a Mask or a Suit

extract in translation from Ezio Carvallo's 1860 trevold Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico "Wearing a Mask or a Suit".

Ny dal dessou splendisce y floy l'arrimbandant con azauc, tandic repars dereΟΑr stan y collin parmy crevoscr raΟΑunant.

pog lumner lougen dagaΟΑy, ma il sarven soul a y vogdeΟß dell'≈™cumen remartellar.

Ne sgardant de cur may, l'oc surcou un segr pariscent y camin follar nacleu deur y floy. Matthias forrovau; luy souvenen noc y sigt d'alcun najel hormejaΟΑ ag quarf, eΟΑ y floy trasir ceter vel lontan par najar.

The man must be a local, come to gawk at the foreigners.

34.2: sbay "gawp"

The somewhat pejorative sense in Modern Boral is likely due to interference from the circumfix s- -ar (as in sboccar "to smirk"). The Latin is a derivative of synonymous bad≈ç, which is of unclear (possibly onomatopoeic) origin.

##

34.5: The Drengot Collusion

The Drengot Collusion was a multinational alliance-federacy in north-western Europe. Named for the older Drengot Empires (themselves named for the Norman noble family who first united much of Gaul and Albion), it grew from various treaties in the mid-nineteenth century into the principal power in the region until its dramatic collapse in 1961.

History
The Drengot Collusion arose among the French polities of the Global Workshop period. Shortly thereafter the New Urban Mesh (a collection of somewhat-autonomous cities including independent London, as well as Axbane on Borland) was subsumedβÄîor took control of, depending on one's personal biasesβÄîthe nascent Collusion.

The entirety of Borland entered the Collusion in 1894 as part of a series of events there that also led to the abdication of the Queen Natalia II and the concomitant dissolution of the Borlish monarchy.

Alongside the cities of London and Paris, Kent was the political centre of the Drengot Collusion, with many governmental buildings located in Rone.

Tensions between the Collusion and their southern neighbours (to wit, Vascony and Provence) grew particularly tense in the two decades following the Millstone War. For this reason it is notable that the Fiellas Dimenja newspaper (headquartered in Marsella) enjoyed considerable readership across the Collusion even in the 1930s.

Although Portingale was never part of the Collusion, it occupied a peripheral position in its orbit. This led to diplomatic tensions with Vascony, located directly between the Collusion and Portingale. For a time, Drengot astraphor supplied (via undersea powerline) much of the baric needs of Portingale, thus relying on Vascon goodwill not to interfere. This situation changed in the 1950s, with Portingale funding threshold mills to supply its own astraphor.

The Furore of 1961
At the time of the Furore, the Collusion was being administered by a tinplate government under Romney Darchild.

One of the scandals of the Furore was the revelation of the clandestine activities undertaken by the Red House (the popular name of the Collusion's Ministry for Information) in Rone. These activities, enumerated in the Darchild memorandum, acted as a powderkeg, first bringing down the current administration and shortly thereafter the entire Collusion.

Aftermath
One effect of the collapse of the Drengot Collusion was the implementation of the Kentish ostracon reforms, which would go on to be used as a model in several polities across the globe.

##

35.1: Going to Brasil

extract in translation from popular history text Going to Brasil [Newfoundland], an account of the first few Vascon voyages to Mendeva [North America] after the Novomundine Landfall of 1471. The book was written in 1971 by Ambrosian author CristΟ≤f Unquenjica as part of the quincentenary commemorations in that polity.

Ig degnant nos posseu lon fregn l'origin vecinar por y nom Mendeva (sur un cart surveunt des y veyaΟß coll'Abanc meΟΑes es scaut terrae mendevalensis, lonc y parol vask mendeval "vest"),
While we can confidently pinpoint the first usage of the name Mendeva (a surviving map from the Landfall voyage itself is marked terrae mendevalensis, after the Vask mendeval "west"),

y benoΟß dy nom Brasil poll'austessem parmy ci gran istr ambrosian possey un histoir magn mattougleressem.
the use of the name Brasil for the easternmost of the major Ambrosian isles has much murkier origins.

Certan cos anneyant es y nom adaptau y mythos irΟΑonnesc d'Oil Breasail, un heilant a vest d'IrΟΑon appos star covart d'un vel a nievr jamay for l'un jorn par casc set annaΟΑ.
It is essentially certain the name was inspired by the Irish myth of Oil Breasail, a landmass west of Ireland that was said to be shrouded in mist except for one day every seven years.

Istr stikettaΟΑ con Brasil ben parol comportant son vis dagaΟΑy sur cart dy rimmot atlantic ny MitaΟΑ-Asc aval, cos usual magn picquessem eΟΑ austessem ag loy real.
Islands labelled Brasil or similar wordforms appear sporadically on late-medieval maps of the Atlantic coast, usually much smaller and further east than the real place.

35.3: lon fregn "undoubtedly"

lon fregn /l…în frijn/ [l ä…±Υàf Äin]

Etymology: literally "without risk", phrase first seen in the sixteenth century in the first sense, extending to the second in the first few decades of its use. As a phatic expression it is briefly fashionable in the Good Game period, and returned to common use in the late twentieth century.

##

35.5: Verona & War

Verona is a city in the north-east of the Italian peninsula.

History
During the tenth century and into the time of the Second Tetrarchy, Verona and its province were contested between the Kingdom of the Lombards (and latterly, Lombardy) and the German Empire. The Verona branch of the Adelchian dynasty ruled Lombardy during the eleventh century.

In the late twelfth and early thirteenth centuries, Verona was part of (and the most prosperous city in) the duchy of Furlain.

Verona was badly affected by the Second German War of the early nineteenth century. The arena in Verona, a classical amphitheatre, was reopened for performances shortly after the end of the war.

People
Chiarina Bruno (fl. 1824), librettist and composer who wrote the opera Wings Afire
Jacob III (1087-1130), king of Lombardy, who was the last of the Verona branch of the Adelchians to grow up in Verona

---

The Second German War (1815βÄ™23) was a conflict centred on German lands but involving almost all the polities of Europe. It took place in the early nineteenth century and marks the conventional end of the Long Peace. This war is considered part of the global phenomenon of Reaction War, which includes politically unrelated but contemporaneous conflicts around the world.

Although Vascony at first managed not to become embroiled in the conflict, once its interests in Mendeva and the East were threatened, then-king Hernand IV had no choice but to declare against the Northern Concert and in favour of the Convoy.

Borland participated in the war, with Borlish soldiers present alongside Bavarn and Furlaine fighters at the disastrously mismanaged Battle of Hulm in 1821.

The Northern Concert were a major power involved in the war, and they were allied with Borland, not uncontroversiallyβÄîthe alliance sparked many mozard protests in cities across the country.

The Kingdom of France was seen to have profited unjustly from the Second German War, gaining significant territory while (according to many) not investing much manpower or material resources.

In the 1820s and 30s, immigration to the Novomund (and especially to New Provence) spiked from those countries which had been most devastated by the war.

36.1: Heist Fiction

excerpt from the Borland vicine mesh distributed library pages on masquira romance.

Nell'eΟß ouvr a CarvalloβÄîl'oc preu livr no stant for y primer parmy un rig de doΟßanβÄîern introdoit plusour dy qualtaΟΑ natural por un racont masquiral, e sell'ouvr lou accomblaurn primer accougl com untaΟΑ cohernt.
It was Carvallo's worksβÄîthis first book being only the first in a dozen-strong seriesβÄîwhich introduced many of the essential properties of a masquira tale, and first presented them together as a coherent whole.

Com bustr, Jalico forgau pres solter y noΟßon dy romanΟß kevlar ant heros deyent un sonc preΟßous accarr a mesur d'un vellisqment convoluΟΑ straccanΟß e delusion partenent;
For example, Jalico almost singlehandedly creates the conceit of the kevler in which our protagonist must obtain a valuable item by means of a complicated scheme involving trickery and deception; 

nos reconnoscm y parol meΟΑes nel y jout sarvir durant certan hour, dec un inceuΟßon borallesc aΟ·r.

36.2: goΟΑou "tunnel"

Extended use in the sense of "chimney, flue" is widespread through nearby languages. The word has also been borrowed into Merch and has undergone folk etymology, becoming gothrow /Υàgu.ΟΑ…Ι…ô/ as though a univerbation of "go through".

36.5: Wars and Rumours of Wars

a compilation of my notes on several major events in the history of the world around Borland.

The First German War (βÄ™1770) was a conflict in eighteenth-century Europe which cannot neatly be lumped in with the somewhat-earlier Democratic Wars. Its conclusion is usually taken as the start of the Long Peace.

Saxony was ascendant immediately following the First German War, controlling more territory than it had in any point in its history. This stood in in good stead for early industrialisation, especially in the capital of Lepzi.

-----

The Democratic Wars were a collection of international and civil wars which took place in the mid-eighteenth century.

The earliest incarnations of steeplepost technology are seen during the Democratic Wars; usually these consisted of lines of temporary signal towers which could only send simple coded messages, such as an hour to launch an assault. One more advanced and permanent system was used to connect Cordova to Malaga in Andalus during their particular conflict with Morrack.

Albion saw many changes in the aftermath of the Democratic Wars. Long contested between Markland and Kent, the city of London found itself newly independent for one final, lasting time.

The end of the Democratic Wars and the concomitant massive migrations of people is suspected to have contributed to the global spread of pennackit dystentery, which begins in earnest in 1753.a compilation of my notes on several major events in the history of the world around Borland.

The First German War (βÄ™1770) was a conflict in eighteenth-century Europe which cannot neatly be lumped in with the somewhat-earlier Democratic Wars. Its conclusion is usually taken as the start of the Long Peace.

Saxony was ascendant immediately following the First German War, controlling more territory than it had in any point in its history. This stood in in good stead for early industrialisation, especially in the capital of Lepzi.

37.1: The Letters in the Study

excerpt in translation from 1899 housebound enigma 'The Letters in the Study', written by Iscombe author Cavra Herquet as an early and successful example of the genre.

Y heloit dy sorty ern affauΟΑaΟΑ an zoum remot candon Guilmet follau y galleir vogt ny manoir apar.
The noise of the party was a distant hum as Guilmet wandered through the empty corridors of the house.

I sovemblau con y deit lonc l'adornment plu nell'eutaΟΑ, y motif convoluΟΑ tragΟßant par ogl.
She ran her fingers along the unfashionable wallpaper, tracing the intricate patterns with her eyes.

L'aye tojorn l'oc majon amaΟΑ; l'oc er luy pariΟΑ com un cas-sey-apart a mesur dy ci meis stival stendiscent entr period scholer, ci yonoscon sell'aldr quΟ·scen y tombr sodal por poΟΑir escapat fair tras Mendeva.
She had always loved this house; it had been like a second home to her during those long summer months between terms when her parents had shut the family home to go gallivanting across Mendeva.

I tornau par un cogn e lor yeme paus avant un portrait larc sy granpaΟΑr depegntant.
She turned a corner and came to a stop in front of a large portrait of her grandfather. 

Y vesson reit noc obstroint, y segr ny pegntur luy apparisc magn; il ern meΟΑes forniΟΑ d'ogl parey.
Besides the stiff dress, the man in the painting looked much like her; they even had the same eyes.

37.2: seucq "footpath"

seucq /sawk/
- footpath, trail, a way set intentionally for people to walk along;
- trail, tracks, footsteps, those marks left by an animal or vehicle after it has passed by and which can be followed;
- legacy, aftereffects, impact, the consequences of a person's life and the ways in which they have changed the world;
- (in particular) descendants, progeny, one's children and their children etc. as opposed to one's forebears

Etymology: from Old Boral seucque, siuke "track, path", from Borland Latin silqua, sΡ™lqua "track, trail (of an animal)". Thought to reflect a Borland Kelt word *seilquar, from earlier *sleiquro (in which case cognate with Welsh ≈²œâybr "path").  Extended in sense to metaphorical "things left behind one" at least by the fifteeneth century but probably earlier.

37.3: svattour "scout"

svattour /svaΥàtur/ [zv…êΥàt äΥê]
- scout, a person or group of people sent out to perform reconnaissance or otherwise bring in information (especially in a military context);
- investigator, private detective, an agent hired by a person for the purpose of evidence gathering;
- harbinger, failsafe, early warning, a person or thing which gives advance notice (especially of danger or other unwanted occurrences)

also svattar /svaΥàtar/ [zv…êΥàt…ëΥê] to scout, investigate, act as an early warning

Etymology: agent noun and verb from Middle Boral svat, svet "reconnaisance, scouting" (now obsolete), from synonymous early Saxon sweat /zv…¦Υêt/. The word was borrowed in the thirteenth century, during the various conflicts subsequent to the German Secession. Extension from military contexts is attested in the eighteenth century, with its use by the svattour pavar private detectives of Damvath.

Nos nolau hur dar for an svattour noscon confiabr.
/n…îz noΥàlo h…Σr dar f…îr an svaΥàtur noΥàx…în k…înΥàfja.brΧ©/
[n…îz n äΥàlo h…ΣΥê Υàd…ëΥê f…îΥêβÄΩ Äan zv…êΥàt äΥê n äΥàx…în k ä…±Υàfja.b…ê]
1p not.fut-imp.1p hire give only to=indef PI 1p.ins trust-pot
We were only going to hire a PI we could trust.

37.4: fouceur "handsome"

fouceur /fuΥàtsawr/ [f äΥàtsa.w…ê]
- handsome, pretty, having a pleasing appearance and especially (of a person) being physically attractive;
- charming, charismatic, exuding personal charm and inducing people around to like one;
- (archaic or affected) wily, sneaky, full of clever tricks making one difficult to keep up with or predict

Etymology: from Middle Boral, originally in the last sense as an attributive use of the (now-obsolete) noun fouceur "rascal, trickster". This reflects Old Boral fo(r)cevr "rogue, blackguard", a direct descendant of Latin furcifer "scoundrel, (derogatory) slave", literally "yoke-bearer". The shift in sense from "deceptive > wily > charming > handsome" is somewhat extreme, yet every stage (occurring over approximately two centuries from 1650 to 1850) is well-attested in the historical record.

Cain Celest e Marca un per fouceur.
/ken tseΥàl…¦st e marΥàka …Σn p…¦r fuΥàtsawr/
[k…¦n ts…ΣΥàl…¦st e m…êΥêΥàka m Υàp…¦Υê f äΥàtsa.w…ê]
fall-3p Celest and Marca indef pair pretty
Celeste and Marca make a handsome couple.

##

38.1: Scandal Abroad

excerpt in translation from an article in the 14th October 1950 edition of the Fiellas Dimenja (Sunday Sheets), a Marsellan weekly newspaper focusing primarily on recreation and entertainment. 

NOVEL ALLORS: SCANDAL! PRINΟ΅ HASINIC ADMET 3βÖä MIL MURDON DEUT
NEWS AFIELD: SCANDAL! Hasinic PRINCE ADMITS 30βÖä MILLION OF DEBT

PrinΟß PeΟΑer Tamaha, heir men connuΟΑ des y dynasty hasinic famous, a admis por y nonΟßamen trey mil murΟΑon de pois deΟ·r (oc dou mil myriad de tron surcontant) com sculd a colluy speculatour e tan a tesoir paΟ·sal tras y tarmagn des Mascic vars Ambrosia.
Prince Pether Tamaha, lesser-known son of the illustrious Hasinic monarchy, has admitted to the press that he owes thirty million poise (over twenty million trone) in debt to speculation companies and to national banks across the continent from Mashick to Ambrosia. 

Par surcresc, sy granpaΟΑr y Rey a refusaΟΑ le sauvar de decoxion, stant jainΟß a daΟΑ lon preluΟ·scment.
Furthermore, his grandfather the King has declined to bail him out, in an unprecedented move.

Postulation jaΟßol es convencuΟΑ ig y joun prinΟß (oc ayent un jubilation opulent de nascenΟß trentem receut ny Davarn Heul fevruer nuverrem, ant may de cinq mil d'assistour) no's lon hiat sy redon despardent eΟΑ y scurtaΟΑ dell'askouΟΑ parent confiant.
Longtime speculation has held that the young prince (who hosted a lavish thirtieth birthday celebration in the Davarn Heul this February, with over five thousand attendees) has been regularly running through his funds and relying on his family's coffers.

38.2: teir "to number"

teir /tir/ [t…ΣΥê]
- number, be, to consist of some number of people or things in a group;
- come to, cost, be worth some amount of money;
- be (at a distance of), be located, to exist at some specified distance from or height above a reference point;
- (obsolete except in certain idiomatic expressions) be had by, to be in the possession of somebody

Etymology: from Old Boral teir "to span, to cover (an amount of land), to stretch (a distance)", from Latin teg≈ç "I cover up, I clothe". Extended to many contexts in which the object of a copular phrase is a quantity or amount of substance.

Y centuir de vossy fraΟΑr tein dou lau dy castel.
/i ts…¦nΥàtajr de v…îstri ΥàfraΟΑrΧ© tin du lo di kaΥàst…¦l/
[i ts…ΣnΥàtaj…ê de Υ¨v…îst Ä…Σ Υàf Äa.ΟΑ…ê tin du lo di k…êΥàst…¦w]
def army of 2p.gen def brother be.at-3p two league from-def castle
Your brother's armies are 4 miles from the castle.

38.3: quic βÄ€activeβÄù

quic /kw…Σk/
- active, working, on, having the power of acting (especially, of moving or of functioning as a person or machine);
- in progress, ongoing, presently being undertaken;
- conscious, awake, up, (of a person) not asleep and especially being out of bed

Etymology: from Old Boral quic βÄ€alive, awakeβÄù, a borrowing from Old English cwic βÄ€living, live; intelligent, keenβÄù. Formerly also used of plants for βÄ€thriving, growing wellβÄù which likely represents an intermediate meaning between its original sense and the more common modern usage.

    Hau, ty diac noβÄôs quic!
    /ho | ti djak n…îz kw…Σk/
    hey | 2s.gen mic neg=be.3s active
    Hey, your micβÄôs off!

38.4: hiat βÄ€pauseβÄù

hiat /hjat/ [Οßat, jat]*
- pause, lull, a period of reduced activity;
- gap, break, an interval in space or time which interrupts an otherwise-continuous object or process;
- (theatre, film) intermission, interval, the break between two parts of a drama;
- (music notation) rest, a symbol indicating that oneβÄôs instrument is not to be played

Etymology: sixteenth-century borrowing reflecting Norman hiat, Lombard hiato or another similar cognate, originally in the musical sense. These are taken directly from Latin hiΡ¹tus βÄ€opening, gap, aperture, cleftβÄù, a noun derivation from hi≈ç βÄ€I yawn, gape, am openβÄù.

    Nos benoΟßaum y hiat por douΟßur acatar.
    /n…îz Υ¨be.noΥàtsom i hjat p…îr duΥàts…Σr Υ¨a.kaΥàtar/
    [n…îz Υ¨be.n äΥàtsom i Οßat p…îΥê d äΥàts…ΣΥêβÄΩΥ¨ Ä…êkΥàt…ëΥê]
    1p use-pst-1p def gap for dessert buy-inf
    We used the interval to get sweets.

*both pronounciations (and even the misspelling yat) are widespread in the modern period, though the latter is in some quarters proscribed.

##

38.5: Steeplepost

Steeplepost refers to any of a group of similar technologies (flag-lamp, barrick, longform, gyre, etc.) used for transmitting messages over long distances.

Development
The very earliest incarnations of the steeplemesh arose during the Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century, but these tended to be single lines of temporary signal towers set up to span terrain that made it too costly to simply send a rider with a message. Moreover, these were set up only to transmit simple messages; for example, an hour to launch an attack.

These early wartime towers usually relied on lampfire and thus were reliable only at night, or else a collection of patterned flags, which were usable at day but required a shorter distance between watchtowers. The two-arm system, allowing up to nine separate states to be transmitted, was first tested in Thynen (a town in Willemy), where the church's weathervane was reportedly commandeered to send the first lines of the Pater Noster to the neighbouring town Bost. (Hence, probably, the name βÄ€steeplepostβÄù.)

By 1820 Ambrosia had unveiled its Steeplepost Tapestry in Aiga d'Angoixa, which promised to allow the (very slow) transmission of images along the lines by communicating the brightness of each pip in an enormous grid, in the manner of a cross-stitch. It was on the Tapestry that the infamous Lies of 1824 were first displayed.

The very first cross-sea steeplepost lines were established around the same time, with air-steeples in the Rustigh Strait connecting Borland to Willemy during the 1830s. This link would last only until 1854, however, when it was replaced with a barrick line.

The middle of the nineteenth century saw the flag-lamp system gradually superseded by barrick lines generally. Uptake was uneven owing to the twin constraints of geography and vested interests; for example, Mendevan powers tended to embrace the new technology more enthusiastically than their similarly-situated Vetomundine counterparts.

The new lines made partially-automated transmission much easier to develop. A prototype wired tachygraph, the Vilmanas Teleclavia, first succesfully sent a message via the steeplemesh in 1859.

Encoding
Steeplescripts, or methods by which a message is translated into a transmissible form, have many forerunners in secret codes used for spycraft and warfare. For example, Markland under King Andrew III during the Wars of Fealty employed "Thieves' Script" to send secret messages, converting each letter or common word into a series of three symbols.

One early line in Andalus (from Cordova to MΟΓlaga, usually given as the first line connecting two major cities, and essentially just formalising the signal system that had existed for the duration of their conflict with Morrack) used a variant of the ancient Polybius square adapted for Arabic script.

The first modern steeplescript was developed in Cracow by engineer Sigmund Adamich and his son Andrei as part of the royal initiative to connect Cracow to Bitom, using a set of four symbols, traditionally referred to as "dot, dash, bar and hook".

Cultural Detaxion
The spread of the steeplemesh reduced overland communication times extremely rapidly (by the end of the nineteenth century some places boasted thrice-daily post delivery, not to mentioned the nascent wired tachygraphs), and undersea barrick links would further connect the world.

Steeplemesh may have contributed to the decline of the voidtale fashion, demystifying formerly-unknowable distant lands and weakening the cultural narrative of the pioneering documentarian explorer.

39.1: The Pinprick Eden

excerpt from The Pinprick Eden, a work of parachthon romance released in 1911 Zangathy by Mica Lus, the first trevold from that Mendevan polity to be published in translation across the Vetomund.

Forn pog may a deg meis sceut des l'annihilaΟßon dy mont, e caye je sy nascenΟß.
It had been just over ten months since the end of the world, and it was her birthday. 

Kimmela au l'un je nascenΟß jubilaΟΑ abord dy kenonaut deja, durant y gyr por mantien.
Kimmela had celebrated one birthday on board the voidsailer already, during the maintenance gyre. 

L'ig au un jorn teut con allagrtaΟΑ e spien, e lor un sorty souvr no'r pariΟΑ tesqual a bas a respirar.
That had been a day of happiness and hope, and throwing a party had seemed as natural as breathing. 

L'oc annaΟΑ i poΟΑe alcun jubilaΟßon vray dijarr lon jant.
This year she couldn't stomach any real celebration. 

Kimmela gasau pall'oscbou veΟΑr vars y vogt semnaΟΑ de stel atras.
Kimmela stared out of the viewport at the star-strewn void.

L'oc sta bel, de faΟßon freit e mortal, ant stel noc lougent yon parmig splendisce y Sol ja briglandessem for com picq.
It was beautiful, in a cold and deadly way, with untwinkling stars among which already the Sun shone only slightly brighter. 

Se poΟΑe y Tar foscaΟΑ de fum fos visibr pall'appragl ny kenonaut, ma i douta.
The smoke-blackened Earth might yet be visible with the voidsailer's equipment, though she doubted it.

39.2: sovragl "belt"

sovragl /soΥàvr…¦jl/ [s äΥàv Ä…¦j ä]
- belt, girdle, a band worn around the waist to hold clothing (usually pants) to one's body;
- sash, cummerbund, a decorative band of cloth worn across the body;

Etymology: direct descendant from Latin subligΡ¹culum "loincloth, kilt" exhibiting l-r dissimilation (attested from as early as c8 in Old Boral sovreia»ùle "waistband, overskirt") likely reinforced by the etymologically-unrelated prefix sovr- "over, above". Synchronically analysable as sovrar "pass, go over" + -agl, an unproductive nominal suffix producing words for tools or other instruments.

Y sovragl seyon me daΟΑ er adornaΟΑ par fylau.
/i soΥàvr…¦jl siΥàj…în me daΈΗ …¦r Υ¨a.d…îrΥànaΈΗ par fiΥàlo/
[i s äΥàv Ä…¦j ä Υ¨sj…îm.m…ΣΥàdah …ΣΥ¨ Äa.d äΥêΥànah p…ëΥê f…ΣΥàlo]
def belt 3s.ins 1s.obl give-p.pst cop.imp decorate-p.pst by crow
The belt she gave me was decorated with crows.

39.3: laur mettr coll'un man "take matters into one's own hands"

laur mettr coll'un man /lor Υàm…¦trΧ© k…îΥàl…Σn man/ [loΥê Υàm…¦.t…ê k äΥàl…ΣmΥ¨man]
- take matters into one's own hands, to deal with a problem alone especially if due to others responsible having failed to tackle it;
- shoulder responsibility, to accept that one is responsible for something bad or difficult;
- (more generally) get to work, to start doing a (usually lengthy or otherwise-challenging) job;
- (jocular or self-effacing) masturbate, to stimulate oneself with the hands

Etymology: Idiom in use since the Middle Boral period, literally "to put work with the one hand". It is an extension of preexisting laur mettr "to tackle an issue, to take a job", from now-rare laur "work" < Latin labor (or a deverbal from laurar "to work") and mettr "to put" < Latin mitt≈ç "I send". In the last sense, first attested in writing in 1883 (possibly; more certain usages by the early c20), via a lewd reinterpretation of the literal sense of the idiom.

Οêa vos nouΟΑ l'occeir jo met laur coll'un man!
/ΟΑa v…îz nuΈΗ l…îkΥàsir  £o m…¦t lor koΥàl…Σn man/
/ΟΑa v äzΥànuh l äkΥàs…ΣΥê  ùo m…¦t loΥê k äΥàl…ΣmΥ¨man/
if 2p won't-2p 3s.acc=kill.inf 1s put work with.def=one hand
If you won't kill him then I'll do it myself!

39.4: enΟßar voug "be completely wrong"

enΟßar voug /…¦nΥàtsar vuj/ […ΣnΥàts…ëΥê vuj]
- be completely wrong, to miscalculate or misjudge entirely so that one's answer or action does not resemble the correct one in any respect;
- do something incorrectly, to perform an action (especially to operate a machine or to attempt an artistic endeavour) with absolutely nothing approaching expertise or skill;
- be utterly useless at, to have no talent whatsoever for a particular skill

Etymology: Idiom attested from the sixteenth century, literally meaning "to start incorrect". The verb enΟßar "to begin, start" is a direct descendant of Latin initi≈ç "I begin, originate", while the adjective voug "wrong, mistaken, incorrect" is a medieval borrowing from Old English w≈çh "wrong, crooked, bent" (giving modern Merch ough /uΥêf/ "bad").

Il enΟßan y dou voug cas toriot luΟ·r.
/…Σl …¦nΥàtsan i du vuj kaz Υ¨toriΥàj…ît laΥàj…Σr/
[Υ¨i.l…ΣnΥàtsan i du vuj kaz Υ¨t äΥà Äj…ît lΟΠΥà ù…ΣΥê]
3p begin-ipf.3p def two home.of torriot play.inf
They're both useless at playing torriot.

##

39.5: Andalus

Andalus is a polity in the south of the Spanish peninsula. The languages of Morrove Arabic and Mostara Spanish are both widely spoken in the region.

History
Ninth-century Andalus saw the flowering of theoretic advancements; in particular, glassmaking techniques accelerated rapidly due to a cut-off of supply from the east, leading to the prodigious export of so-called "jackdaw glass".

The Brethin religious order spread into the Christian-majority areas of Andalus from the north during the eleventh and twelfth centuries. Around this time Andalus lost significant territory in the north and east to Christian polities, such as the newly-established Kingdom of Barcelon.

In the fifteenth century, while its neighbour Vascony was ascendant, Andalus was beset by struggles. Controlled from across the straits by Morrack, its issues included two successive succesion crises as well as the 1457 Lisbon Massacre.

Vascony was forced to cede Portingale, its economic heart, to Andalus after a series of military successes in the Romantic Wars of the mid-seventeenth century.

Andalus was embroiled in conflict with Morrack (as its neighbour attempted a reconquest) for a prolonged period in the eighteenth century; some but not all of the involved skirmishes are traditionally grouped with the concurrent Democratic Wars unfolding globally. A signalling line from Cordova to MΟΓlaga set-up at this time would develop into the world's first steeplepost connection between two major cities.

People
Abdul Raman IV (fl. 1188), ruler of the Admiralty of Cordova under the Single Caliphate
Joan BensaΟ≠d (fl. 1951), author of the 1951 scholastic quire 'The Communication Toolset' (El Herramente de CommunicazΟ≥n)
Jorala (fl. c18-19), landscape artist born in Cazris whose later work was influenced by the voidtale craze
Judeta Barracin (fl. 1919), author who helped pioneer the genre of social comedy with her work 'Flaxen Hearts'
Tal of Murcia (fl. 1211), medieval scholar who in 1211 wrote 'On Unknown Areas Scribed by Arcs'

##

40.1: Vikings in Northern France

excerpt in translation from reference quire On the Dane Supremacy in Kernow [Brittany] and the Creation of Normandy, written by deixist historian Professor Jockin Abiscander.

Y premendessem sta l'aΟΑoinΟß de commovenΟß viking lonc y rimmot.
Most pressing was the inexorable rise in Viking activity along the coast. 

Gazou ag banc dy Sein e l'Orn ern inceut ci meis paravant, ma dagormay il spanoirn ag vest; y monastir a Landvennick meΟΑes se cof incendiaΟΑ a cainΟß l'an 888 N. 
Raids along the Seine and the Orne had begun in earnest some decades prior, but now spread west; even the monastery at Landvennick was burned to the ground in 888 N. 

Necun no'rn y commovenΟß dy dannesc vecinaΟΑ com piglment; ag dou cost dy Canal caf batman benoΟßaurn l'instabiltaΟΑ politic (oc a digr y sfondranΟß Wessaxon ag norΟΑ eΟΑ y ci querel lombard ag souΟΑ) por eΟΑegl concarr e fragossau instanΟßar.
Nor were the Danes' activities confined to looting; on both sides of the Channel war leaders took advantage of political instability (the collapse of Wessex in the north and the Lombard disputes in the south) to conquer territory and settle. 

Scey evidenΟß ig gent dannesc reseyen doutr meΟΑes y flou Vir, oc stant part lonc y tarm fransc e domnon com l'er traitaΟΑ nell'Accord Catumagin dy siecr ogt,
There is evidence of such Danish settlement even past the Vire river, part of the Franco-Dumnonian border as agreed in the eighth-century Catumagin Accords, 

e portan posc y Fransc concedoirn solemn l'oc feu ny siecr deg e cossy corporalisaurn Normandy, y Vir remaneu y tarm.
although when this fiefdom was officially ceded by the Franks in the tenth century to form Normandy, the Vire remained the border.

40.2: batman "vigilante"

batman /batΥàman/
- vigilante, one who acts outside the law to right a perceived wrong (often to avenge a murder or similar act of violence);
- mercenary, hitman, one who enacts violence (especially assassinations) for hire;
- warfarer, warrior, combatant, one engaged in warfare as a member of a band of soldiers

Etymology: seventeenth-century clipping of earlier abatman "combatant, mercenary", which descends from Old Boral abat manc/Οß "mercenary (band), hired soldier", literally "war-seller". The first element abat "war" is a deverbal noun from abatr "fight, make war" (modern abattr), while the second manc/Οß "seller, stall, booth" is of unclear origin.

The status of the final consonant in manc/Οß (since lost) is disputed. If palatalised /mants/, the word is thought to reflect Insular Latin manentia "shop, household, inn", nominal derivative of mane≈ç "I stay, remain, spend the night" (compare fenΟß "split, break" from Latin findentia < find≈ç "I cleave, split"). The alternative theory that it descends from Latin merx "goods, wares" (modern marΟß) is semantically tempting but phonetically implausible.

If instead the word is unpalatalised /mank/, it is generally agreed that it reflects Latin mang≈ç "dealer, seller (of slaves)", either directly from the LatinβÄîalthough one would expect to see also *mangon < mang≈çnemβÄîor via the Old English descendant mangian "to trade, traffic in". In either case, the final consonant does not appear anywhere after the eleventh century, perhaps due to influence from the English suffix -man "person, man".

Se poΟΑe noc l'eΟß rumour d'un batman corroular.
/se poΥàΟΑe n…îk l…¦ts riΥàmur d…Σn batΥàman Υ¨ko.ruΥàlar/
[se p äΥàΟΑ3 n…î l…¦s  Ä…ΣΥàm äΥê d…Σm b…êtΥàman Υ¨ko. Ä äΥàl…ëΥê]
rfl can-ipf neg def-p.px rumour of-indef vigilante verify-inf
The rumours of a vigilante were unsubstantiable.

40.3: oscbou "porthole"

oscbou /oxΥàbu/
- porthole, gunport, a window set into the hull of a ship for cannons to fire through (or in modern use simply for the view);
- hatch, an (often internal) opening in a wall for people to pass things such as prepared food through

Etymology: Middle Boral borrowing from contemporary Vascon ox (de) buy /…î É de bwi/ "gunport", literally "ox's eye". The word ox "eye" descends from Latin oculus and is cognate with Boral ogl /…îjl/, while the word buy "ox" was adapted to the Boral bou "ox", both from Latin b≈çs, bovem "cow".

    Pront jo vis pall'oscbou my sour cocinant.
    /pr…înt  £o v…Σz Υ¨pa.l…îxΥàbu mi sur Υ¨ko.tsiΥànant/
    [p Ä…în  ù ä Υàv…Σz Υ¨pa.l äxΥàbu mi Υàs äΥê Υ¨ko.ts…ΣΥànant]
    immediate 1s see.pst by-def=porthole 1s.gen sister cook-p.prs
    I immediately saw my sister cooking through the hatch.

40.4: nell'eutaΟΑ "trendy"

nell'eutaΟΑ /Υ¨n…¦.lawΥàtaΈΗ/ [Υ¨n…¦.l…êwΥàtah]
- fashionable, trendy, Ο† la mode, in, up to date with the latest fashions and allowing one to display having good taste, a refined aesthetic sense or simply ample wealth

Etymology: adpositional phrase literally meaning "in the current fashion", attested since the late eighteenth century originally among the aristocratic melle of Borland's major cities. The noun eutaΟΑ "mode, fashion, vibe, zeitgeist, style" is a nominal derivation from the verb eir "to go" (compare the past participle eut "gone"), which descends directly from Latin e≈ç "I go".

Y cambr ayen pieΟΑry nuaΟß nell'eutaΟΑ.
/i Υàkam.brΧ© …¦Υàj…¦n pj…¦ΟΑΥàri njats Υ¨n…¦.lawΥàtaΈΗ/
[i Υàkam.b ÄβÄΩ…ΣΥà ù…¦m pj…ΣΥà Äi njas Υ¨n…¦.l…êwΥàtah]
def bedroom have-ipf-3p stonework nude in-def-mode
The bedrooms had fashionable exposed stonework.

##

40.5: Albion

Albion is the largest of the Northwestern Isles, situated directly north of Gaul. To its west and east lie Ireland and Borland, respectively.

History
Germanic migration from the continent into Albion took place through the fifth and sixth centuries, leading to the establishment of Markland, Kent, Wessex, Anglia, Northumber and several smaller and short-lasting kingdoms.

The westward encroachment of Saxons drove a emigration of Kelts from (what would become) Devon to Kernow, and for the period between the Battle of Gaulford (656) and Vigo the Magnificent's reconquest the entire peninsula was under the sole authority of Wessex.

The original calling of the Brethin religious order, founded in the eighth century, was to chronicle the Christianisation of Albion and Ireland.

Vikings raids began in Albion with the destruction of Halgrive in 824, although news of Dane incursions would have travelled west from Borland for some years by that point. While Northumber would fall completely to Dane rule, neighbouring Markland withstood (due perhaps to a fortunate lull in its perennial conflict with the Welsh kingdoms) even as much of its eastern territory was settled by the Danes.

The Guithle kingdom of Scotland was established in the ninth century with the help of Harald the Blue of Sodrick, displacing or merging with the existing Pict culture and language.

Sodrick lost much of its territory to the kingdom of Dunclothe in the tenth century, as it had been weakened by extended vikings to the east and the short-term decline in the Dane Supremacy.

By the turn of the second millennium, the south of the island is dominated by an expanded Kent (having recently subdued the remains of Wessex) below Markland and a now-unified Kingdom of Wales. To the north lie the confederate kingdoms of the Danes and then a Scotland even now influenced by Norrish and English peoples.

Much of southern Albion was conquered in the eleventh century by Normandy (with the aid of Devon in the form of the Kernovan Conjuring), consolidating the island bar Wales as far north as Edinbrough into what would become the First Drengot Empire.

The kingdom of Wales began to expand in the thirteenth century, conquering Devon after a long campaign. Tensions with Markland remained high (as they had done for almost a millennium), with the reignition of the Mallor Wars and the sack of Car Vantel/Rexam in 1301.

The late fifteenth century saw much of Albion (and some of its neighbours) embroiled in the Wars of Fealty, which lasted for forty-six years. While other polities in Europe sought to expand their trade meshes (either westward following the Novomundine Landfall or exploiting new routes to Asia), the majority of the wealth and armies of Albion was dedicated to war.

The polities of Albion were still recoving from the Wars of Fealty in 1519 and thus were extremely vulnerable to the Second Great Dying.

Albion saw many changes in the aftermath of the Democratic Wars of the eighteenth century. Long contested between Markland and Kent, the city of London found itself newly independent for one final, lasting time.

The Long Peace around the turn of the nineteenth century saw the Deviance movement take root across Albion, initially via the mesh of Promethean clubs that had arisen over the preceding decades. The movement flourished perhaps due to Albion's many religious minorities and the strong association of the Church with local authority.

Polities
Britain
Drengot Collusion
Dunclothe
Kingdom of Greater Devon
Kent
Markland
Northumber
Scotland
Sodrick
Wales
Wessex

References
An Abridged History of Northern Albion by Aidan Donblane, published by Cloddale Primers, 1940 (with commentary by his daughter Anhart, 1980)
Daughters of the Sea: The Northwestern Isles by Challow Huddresfell, published by Safford University Primers
The Alphabetical Albion by Lucas Osborne et al., published by Winchester Scholastic Primers, 1920
Post-Drengotian Albion: the Long Thirteenth Century by Professor Nuraddin Venzer, published by the New Lincoln School, 1973
Albic Medicine in the Workshop Decades by Dr Harold Westcamp, published by the Conster Health Edifice, 1992

41.1: A Baptism of Fire

excerpt in translation from A Baptism of Fire: Christianity and the Graven, written by Agata Naziunas and published in 1988 by LeidΟ©iai GivatΟ©s.

Ant captanΟß particuler scey y ducaΟΑ carniol, oc cos remarcabr y citaΟΑ abondant de Veron gournant, tan eΟΑ eiΟΑel uvrautr austessem.
Of particular interest is the duchy of Crain, which importantly controlled the prosperous city of Verona, as well as fertile lands further east.

Ig i declarau sy promotion dy Papacy Roman er plausibr nastiΟΑessem des faibiltaΟΑ a des farvour religious vray, ma toΟΑ cas final l'aye un eΟΑegl parfait por contriboir ag Batesm dy NorΟΑ.
Their declaration in support of the Roman Papacy was likely more pragmatic than out of true religious fervour, but in any case they were perfectly positioned to contribute to the Baptism of the North.

Degnant ig l'Ordr de Sagnt Hemma all'Aug Calm (y Convey Australier) fosn y Batesm condugnt con soutien moneΟΑer de Taxon a Hongary par dezein plusour, sou contingent dy Secession German denoy y Batesm un accommission prement.
Though the Order of Saint Hemma of Still Water (the Convoy Australier) had been leading the Baptism with monetary backing from Taxon of Hungary for several decades, it is in the context of the German Secession that the Baptism became an urgent project.

Con Hermene II stant un novel pap totalistessem, sceye hougr ig Augsbourg sceus heresy respandr rapidessem a vel Rom y NorΟΑ parvenir.
Along with a new, more totalist Pope in Hermeneus II, it was feared that Augsburg might spread heresy faster than Rome could reach the North.

41.2: batesm "baptism"

batesm /baΥàt…¦.zmΧ©/ [b…êΥàt…¦.z…êm]
- baptism, christening, the Christian rite in which a person (usually a child) is received into the church and given a name;
- (more generally) naming, the act of giving a person or thing a name;
- induction, hazing, any ceremony whose purpose is to introduce new members to some association, especially if the process if unpleasant or even dangerous

Etymology: via Old Boral batesme "baptism, christening" and synonymous Late Latin baptisma from Ancient Greek Έ≤Έ§œÄœ³ΈΙœÉΈΦΈ± (bΟΓptisma, "dipping in water, baptism"). Used outside of a Christian context from as early as the late thirteenth century; in the last sense it arises in the student culture of Vithor in the eighteenth century.

Scerraun doΟß batesm ac fait l'oc jorn.
/x…¦Υàron d…îts baΥàt…¦.zmΧ© ak fet l…îk  £…îrn/
[Οß…ΣΥà Äon d…îs b…êΥàt…¦.z…êm ak Υàfet l äΥà ù…îΥên]
there.be-fut.3p twelve baptism here do-p.pst def-s.px day
Twelve baptisms will be performed here today.

41.3: orn "order; rowan"

orn /…îrn/ […îΥên]
1.
- order, ordering, arrangement, programme, a particular sequence or way of setting out a collect of people, objects or events;
- order, group, troop, band, a number of people in a formal arrangement towards the furthering of some agenda (in particular, playing music or purveying violence or similar aid);
- command, captaincy, power, the position of having authority to require people to do something;
- recipe, program, instructions, algorithm, a list of steps itemising the proper method for achieving some task

Etymology: either from a native Old Boral orne, ordne, "ordering, arrangement" or borrowed from contemporaneous Old French orne, ordre, both reflecting (as a direct descendant or an early semi-learned loan) Latin ≈çrd≈ç, ≈çrdinem "arrangement, rank, group, troop, rite". Extended senses almost definitely adapted directly from the Latin reflex in the Middle Boral period (cf. short-lived later borrowing ordin).

Nos luΟ·sceu primer nell'orn.
/n…îz Υ¨la.jiΥàxaw priΥàm…¦r n…¦Υàl…îrn/
[n…îz Υ¨la. ù…ΣΥàxaw p Ä…ΣΥàm…¦Υê n…ΣΥàl…îΥên]
1p play-1p first in-def=order
We're first on in the programme.

2.
- mountain ash, rowan, any of several trees in the genus Sorbus, found across Europe and Mendeva;
- (proscribed) ash, tree of the genus Fraxinus (prescriptively, frasn);
- javelin, lance, a light spear thrown with the hand and used as a weapon

Etymology: Direct descendant of Latin ornus "mountain ash, rowan" via the synonymous orne in Old Boral. In the last meaning, a conscious revival of an Old Boral usage which had vanished for centuries.

L'ivan asseye sur y ranscel dell'orn.
/liΥàvan aΥàsje s…Σr i ranΥàx…¦l d…¦Υàl…îrn/
[l…ΣΥàvan …êΥàsje s…ΣΥêβÄΩ Äi  Ä…ënΥàΟß…¦w d…ΣΥàl…îΥên]
def=child sit-ipf on def branch of-def=ash
The child was sat on the rowan's branch.

##

41.4: soΟßavau "aside"

soΟßavau /Υ¨so.tsaΥàvo/ [Υ¨so.ts…êΥàvo]
- aside, sidenote, comment, remark, an incidental statement made quietly or in such a way that most people cannot hear it;
- (theatre, film) nondiegetic dialogue, dialogue said by characters or a narrator directly to the audience, so that the (other) characters cannot hear it;
- afterthought, a post-hoc idea or comment that only occurs after the time when it might have been a useful contribution

Etymology: eighteenth-century borrowing from Vascon soza-vau "aside, commentary, lit. under-voice", originally in the context of theatrical performance. The preposition soza "under, beneath" descends from Latin adverb subta, with influence from the form soz < subtus; the noun vau "voice" is an alteration of earlier vou, from the synonymous Latin v≈çcem.

Y scuir dau un soΟßavau losc ne parmanent a pie.
/i xajr do …Σn Υ¨so.tsaΥàvo l…îx ne Υ¨par.maΥàn…¦nt a pje/
[i Υàxa.j…ê do …Σn Υ¨so.ts…êΥàvo l…îx ne Υ¨p…ëΥê.m…êΥàn…¦nt a pje]
def boy give-pst indef aside rude in keep-p.prs at foot
The boy muttered a rude remark and kept walking.

##

41.5: Gurjat

Gurjat is a polity on the western coast of India.

History
The medieval period saw a flourishing of mathematics across India, and Gurjat was no exception. For instance, the now-familiar angle addition identities for vatre and arrhumb [sine and cosine] were first noted by the mathematicians of the city of UjΟ©n.

Gurjat was one of the stopping points for the British ship the Ph≈™bos under Captain mac Kellot during its famed voyage in the early sixteenth century. The ship spent several months at port there in 1512, after having circumnavigated Africa and before continuing on to Madray [Madurai] and thence to the Great Borunesk Meeting of 1516.

Gurjat enjoyed a significant trade empire in the seventeenth century, spanning from African ports like Ungotia [Zanzibar] and even Vascon-controlled Sant Adrian [Bioko] in the west to Cathay (and eventually New Leudong) in the east. Gurjat traders made up a good fraction of those merchants expelled from Moscat in 1652, several months before the fall of the Ibadine Imamate to Hadramot forces.

People
- Manesh Camdar (fl. 1813), life theorist whose research on fecundation in marine sponges provided the first proof that it comprises the union of sperm and egg cases

##

42.1: Flight of the Bluejay

excerpt in translation from Flight of the Bluejay (in Borlish Vol dy Gay Azur), a trevold written by Carl KajoΟßman and originally released serially from 1931 to 1935.

MichΟΠl flaΟΑrau l'immoubr yert vars y routel dessou, casc broit minuscr oyent com menaΟß.
Michael clambered down the building to the alley below, conscious of every tiny noise.

I save lon alcot vole Sara hour passar pre smarcr; sell'attent surcayen tor veint minut teir, e posc y fin parvenent den sy deit ern gelaΟΑ e l'aye se convencuΟΑ ig y costojan ayent y rout follaΟΑ trottant sta a pognt de retornar an moment calscon por l'accusar d'emboscment a flannar.
He had no idea how long it might take Sara to emerge; in the event it was about twenty minutes, by the end of which his fingers were icy and he was convinced that the policeman who had strolled along the street would be back at any moment to accuse him of lurking with intent.

Ma cos final i sta yent, accappelaΟΑ e fassaΟΑ de rob, con y valigs.
But at last she was there, in hat and coat, with the suitcase.

I l'oncau par pas plusour fin a MichΟΠl smargoy deull'ombr e le prenoy de sy man.
She lugged it a few paces until he slid out of the shadows and took it from her hand.

42.2: lon-lembic "teetotaller"

lon-lembic /Υ¨l…în.l…¦mΥàb…Σk/
- teetotaller, non-drinker, one who abstains from drinking any alcoholic beverages;
- alcohol-free drink, a beverage which has been altered so as not to contain any alcohol

Etymology: first attested 1902 as a univerbation of phrase lon lembic "without alcohol", probably adapted from slightly earlier French and Dutch compounds. The word lon "without" reflects Latin adverb longΡ™ "far away". The noun lembic "ethanol; (more broadly) alcoholic drink" descends from Scholastic Latin lembΡΪcum "alembic, still; (later) distilled spirits", which is borrowed via Arabic from Greek αΦ³ΈΦΈ≤αΩëΈΨ (ΟΓmbΡΪx) "cup with a spout; alembic".

Cascif eΟΑ vos un lon-lembic convoccaΟΑ ac?
/kaΥàx…Σf …¦ΈΗ v…îz …Σn Υ¨l…în.l…¦mΥàb…Σk Υ¨k…în.v…îΥàkaΈΗ ak/
[k…êΥàΟß…Σf …¦ v…îz …Σn Υ¨l…în.l…ΣmΥàb…Σk Υ¨k…î…±.v äΥàkah ak]
Why have.2p 2p indef teetotaller invite-p.pst here

42.3: murΟΑon "ten thousand"

murΟΑon /m…ΣrΥàΟΑ…în/
- myriad, the number ten thousand;
- countless, a very great number

Etymology: from Old Boral murΟΑ, mirΟΑ "countless, very many; ten thousand" with the addition of then-productive augmentative suffix -on (from Latin nominal derivational suffix -≈ç, -≈çnem). In turn this word is a semi-learned form via Late Latin m»≥rias, m»≥riadem from Ancient Greek ΈΦαΩΓœ¹αΩêαΨΑΧ¹œ² (m≈ΪriΟΓs) "ten thousand, countless".

The forms with <u> (reflecting [ è]) instead of expected <i> is unlikely to be any remnant of Hellenising Latin <y> /y/ (which was never common as far afield as Borland). More plausible is a sporadic labialising effect from the preceding /m/, also seen in words like mug < Latin mΡΪca "crumb".

In the Old Boral period it is most often used indeterminately for any large quantity; its more specific sense returns to common use (initially in merchant accounts) in the thirteenth century.

Y gent ne Safford Outr tein 440 murΟΑon.
/i  £…¦nt ne saΥàf…îrd utrΧ© tin katrΧ© kaΥàr…¦nt mirΥàΟΑ…în/
[i  ù…¦n ne s…êΥàf…îΥêd Υàu.t…ê tin Υ¨kat.k…êΥ¨ Ä…¦m.m…ΣΥêΥàΟΑ…în]
There are 4.4 million people in Utter Safford.

42.4: nervous "confident"

nervous /n…¦rΥàvuz/
- confident, assured, optimistic, having confidence in one's ability to accomplish something;
- bold, fearless, daring, showing a willingness to take risks or acting without reservation;
- vigorous, robust, energetic, being healthy and strong and characterised by lively activity;
- (dated or humorous, of meat) sinewy, stringy containing tough fibres and thus being difficult to eat

Etymology: from Middle Boral nervous "sinewy; vigorous", borrowed from synonymous Latin nerv≈çsus along with noun nerf < Latin nervus "nerve, sinew; vigour". The extension in meaning from "vigorous" to "bold" and then to "confident" had taken place by the start of the nineteenth century.

Son il nervous d'issaur ny cors?
/s…în …Σl n…¦rΥàvuz diΥàsor ni k…îrz/
[s äΥàni n…¦ΥêΥàvuz d…ΣΥàsoΥê ni Υàk…îΥêz]
be.3p 3p confident of-great.success in-def contest
Are they sure they'll win the contest?

##

42.5: Two Pens

collected notes on a couple of the many famous people from across the history of this alternate world.

Ezio Carvallo (fl. 1860) was a Portingale author most remembered for his contributions to the development of the genre of masquira romance.

Works
Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico (In Either a Mask or a Suit), a 1860 work which would be the first in a twelve-strong series of tales following the vigilante thief Matthias o Grallon.
The book was released in Kentish in 1865 as 'We Arive in Unwemmed [Immaculate] Clothes', translated by Christopher Wood; Anscon Polgat translated the book into Boral under the title 'Cuscon ReΟΑr Cognit' (Whoever [We Are] Behind [Our] Disguises)

--

Catrin of Kernow (d. 1233) was an influential figure in Tetrarchic Borland.

Family
Catrin of Kernow was the elder sister of King Alan VI of Greater Devon, who orchestrated the Realignment of Devon in 1225. Their uncle was Damian the Wise, the first (Roman) Pope to be elected from among the Brethin religious order.

Legacy
In 1292 the chronicle 'Y ViΟΑ a Catrin de Cornou' (The Life of Catrin of Kernow) was written, detailing her early life, marriage into Borlish noblility and eventual assassination in 1233. The chronicle is particularly important for being the first longform work written in Borlish (as opposed to Latin) about a lay-person.

43.1: Mysarnos

excerpt in translation from 1943 history book The Mysarnos Problem: the Enigma of an Inconvenient Codex, written by classicist Theodora RiphΟΠna.

Ma neΟΑan carrug mettr avant naumagl, retorneu ag citaΟΑ yon a nossy racont sy commet.
But lest we get ahead of ourselves, let us return to the city where our saga begins.

Y citaΟΑ a Mysarnos es l'anΟßan cafloy ny PrincipaltaΟΑ de Mystra, un stat mordether stant vassal sou Rom des y siecr onΟß fin a sy dissolution com issent parmy ci dy Rix Romannesc dy siecr settoΟß.
The city of Mysarnos is the former capital of the Principality of Mystra, a Roman mordether vassal state from the eleventh century until its dissolution as a result of the Romantic Wars in the seventeenth.

L'oc nom es tan denuΟΑ cos janual l'ig benoΟßaΟΑ por y bourg grec vecin ja sceint (stant tardessem corporalisaΟΑ cos solemn com un muncef de Mysarnos) connuΟΑ nell'asc antic com Pylos.
The name has retrospectively also been applied to the nearby preexisting Greek town (which was later officially incorporated as a municipality of Mysarnos) known in antiquity as Pylos.

A sy sommet ny siecr seΟß sta Mysarnos un mojol por y commouΟΑ revitalist, ant un pouvlanΟß teint pres trey murΟΑon.
At its height in the sixteenth century Mysarnos was a hub for the Revitalist movement, with a population of nearly thirty thousand.

- hindsight, retrospection, an understanding of the significance and nature of events after they have occurred;
- perspective, unblinkeredness, the ability to see a situation from a sufficiently uninvolved position that one can understand it without distraction or bias

Etymology: Middle Boral borrowing alongside adjective janual "retrospective, backward-looking" from Scholastic Latin iΡ¹nuΡ¹litΡ¹s "hindsight", both derivatives of iΡ¹nua "doorway, entrance" or IΡ¹nus "Janus, two-headed god of transitional spaces".

Me valois noc y fabr assistr con my maΟΑr.

43.3: mordeth "expedition"

mordeth /m…îrΥàd…¦ΈΗ/
- expedition, an often-military undertaking involving travel either into enemy territory or some analogous target;
- raid, foray, incursion, a short-term and violent move into a region to ransack it for spoils or to inflict damage;
- jaunt, adventure, a short excursion for diversion or pleasure especially involving some (possibly planned) excitement;
- (historical) crusade, conquest, any of the military expeditions undertaken in the medieval Middlesea under varying degrees of Papal authority

Etymology: early Middle Boral borrowing from contemporary Welsh mordeith "sea voyage, raid, conquest by sea", corresponding to a compound of modern moœâr "sea" and teith "journey, trip". Originally used in particular for the various wars of conquest in the Middlesea conducted at a significant distance during the eleventh to thirteenth centuries. While some of these had ostensible religious authority motivating them, many (especially further from the Masreck [Levant]) were more transparently opportunistic.

Il feirn mordeth amont por y poiΟΑ dy Nil trovar.
/…Σl firn m…îrΥàd…¦ΈΗ aΥàm…înt p…îr i p…îjΈΗ di n…Σl troΥàvar/
[…Σw f…ΣΥên m äΥêΥàd…¦ΈΗ …êΥàm…înt p…îΥêβÄΩ Äi p…îjΈΗ di Υàn…Σw t Ä äΥàv…ëΥê]
3p do-pst.3p foray upstream for def well of-def Nile find-inf
They went on an expedition to find the source of the Nile.

43.4: toxen "alarm"

toxen /t…îkΥàs…¦n/ [t ägΥàz…¦n]
- alarm, klaxon, a sound intended to give notice of approaching danger or an upcoming deadline;
- (more genereally) warning, alert, signal, any means of informing someone of a new (especially a problematic) development

Etymology: early Middle Boral borrowing from Old French tocsin, tocseing etc. "alarm, ringing of a bell" (originally in a military context), itself from Old Vascon toca-segn "alarm", literally "strikes (a) bell". These reflect respectively Vulgar Latin toccΡ¹re "to touch" and Latin signum "signal, mark".

Da mey toxen pront la Yam Olau veir.
/da mi t…îkΥàs…¦n pr…înt la jam oΥàlo vir/
[da mi t ägΥàz…¦n p Ä änΥàla Υ¨ ùam  äΥàlo v…ΣΥê]
give 1s.dsj alarm immediately there uncle Olau see-inf
Alert me as soon as you see Uncle Ollie.

##

43.5: Barcelon

Barcelon is a Middlesea polity in the east of the Spanish peninsula.

History
In its early existence from the ninth to the thirteenth century, the county of Barcelon (soon to be the duchy and indeed from 1067 the kingdom) was ruled by the House of Barcelon. It had perennial skirmishes with Tolose to the north, so that even when Tolose became a vassal state to the First Drengot Empire in the eleventh century, it maintained a standing army for protection against Barcelon.

By the late twelfth century, the Kingdom of Barcelon controlled significantly more territory at the expense of Andalus, including the population centres of Saragossa, Valencia and (briefly) Toledo.

Barcelon conquered the region of Tolose from Ascendant Vascony in the first decade of the sixteenth century, thereby (along with territorial gains by Burgundy) depriving it entirely of its Middlesea coastline. In the same period, the War for the Barcelon Succession took place, although notably it only lasted for four days.

The early sixteenth century also saw Barcelon rattle sabres with the newly independent Provence, involving piratical raids on Provincial cities (and especially Marsella) supported financially by Barcelon. Part of the resolution involved the region of Narbona having double obligations to King James III of Barcelon and to Lord AmigΟ≤t in Provence.

The longest-reigning monarch of the Kingdom of Barcelon is James VI (Jaume VI), who ruled into the eighteenth century for 58 years and after whom is named the famous jaumetia, a whitefish stew.

People
Monarchs
Martha II (Marta II, crowned 1186)
James III (Jaume III, fl. 1517)
James V (Jaume V, fl. 1670), who spent much of his childhood in a Cordin wardship in Borland
James VI (Jaume VI, reigned 1691βÄ™1749), longest-reigning monarch in the history of the Kingdom of Barcelon
Other
Raymond al Mustafa (fl. 1750), mathematician who worked with Rachel Desslut on the nascent field of methodics while he was under the patronage of the Duke of Aragon

##

44.1: Borrowings from Borlish

excerpt in translation from Daughters of the Sea: The Northwestern Isles, written in 1956 by Merch author Challow Huddresfell.

Nossy vecin all'aust n'aun jamay y pinnacr mont respanent acavar dy gent BreΟΑon cas asteller e foyer.
Our neighbours to the east have never matched the globe-spanning workshop and household vanguard of the British.

Nentamen, ΟΑa meΟΑes par proximtaΟΑ soul, y lengaΟß anglesc son cos parmanent vanagl borallesc adaptant de jaΟßol com y MitaΟΑ-Asc.
Nonetheless, if only by virtue of proximity, the English languages have been adapting Borlish terms even from the medieval period.

Com bustr, degnant ig nos ligau y ci siecr tost nell'Istr posc-roman con migration des Albion vars Istr Boral (cos particuler, par issent dy reduction markesc d'Anglia Auster ny siecr nouf),
For example, while we associate the early centuries of the post-Roman Isles with migration from Albion into Borland (in particular, after the Merch subjugation of East Anglia in the ninth century),

eΟß parol com danny "un treublant, applattiscent", bather "baΟΑr con fil" e parren "sojornour, un alandaΟΑ, corrovaΟΑ" entraurn VeΟΑr Anglesc des VeΟΑr Boral ny siecr deg soul (lonc lour y preu yenΟß ne chronic de monastir).
words like danny "a nuisance, an irritating person", bather "stringed musical intrument" and parren "traveller, newcomer, novice" enter Old English from Old Borlish in the tenth century alone (according to their first attestations in monastic chronicles).

44.2: jou follant "on the fence"

jou follant / £u f…îΥàlant/ [ ùu f äΥàlan(t)]
- on the fence, undecided, weighing pros and cons, not having chosen between one of many (especially two) options;
- between a rock and a hard place, facing a dilemma, being in a difficult situation with no obviously good options

Etymology: idiom first attested in the seventeenth century, literally meaning "walking on a balance beam", with the image of an acrobat balancing on a seesaw. The noun jou "yoke, carrying pole, balance beam, seesaw" descends directly from Latin iugum with the same meaning, while the verb follar "to walk along, to tread" reflects Old Boral follar "to stamp, crush, tread on", from a Latin noun via Late Latin full≈ç "I trample, I full (cloth)".

L'er jou follant cas l'empley all'ambit acceptar.
/l…¦r  £u f…îΥàlant kaz l…¦mΥàpli Υ¨a.lamΥàb…Σt Υ¨ak.s…¦pΥàtar/
[l…¦Υê  ùu f äΥàla≈΄ kaz l…ΣmΥàpli Υ¨a.l…êmΥàb…Σt Υ¨ak.s…ΣpΥàt…ëΥê]
3s=cop.ipf yoke tread-pt.p wrt def=job at.def=bureau accept-inf
He was on the fence about taking the job at the bureau.

44.3: carrug mettr avant naumagl "get ahead of oneself"

carrug mettr avant naumagl /kaΥàraj Υàm…¦trΧ© aΥàvant noΥàm…¦jl/
- get ahead of oneself, count one's eggs before they hatch, jump the gun, to start on some activity without being adequately prepared;
- put the cart before the horse, make a mountain out of a molehill, to mix up one's priorities and treat minor things as being disproportionately important (or vice versa)

Etymology: idiom used since the medieval period in various languages but first seen in its modern form in Boral in the sixteenth century. The phrase literally means "to put the cart in front of the oxen", in which the noun carrug "cart" descends via Old Boral carru»ù from synonymous Late Latin carr≈Ϊca (from Gaulish).

Meanwhile, naumagl "ox, castrated bull employed as a draught animal" enters the language in the early Middle Boral period from Norman un naumail /nawΥàma é/ "an ox/a steer", rebracketed from earlier un aumail, itself a masculine variant of aumaille "cattle, livestock". This comes from earlier almaille, armaille, from Latin neuter plural animΡ¹lia "animals" reinterpreted as a feminine singular.

Mettau noc carrug avant naumagl; ja n'eu nos y muncef meΟΑes saut.
/m…¦Υàto n…îk kaΥàraj aΥàvant noΥàm…¦jl |  £a naw n…îz i m…ΣnΥàts…¦f meΥàΟΑ…¦z sot/
[m…ΣΥàto n…îk k…êΥà Äaj …êΥàvan n äΥàm…¦j ä |  ùa naw n…îz i m…ΣnΥàts…¦f m…ΣΥàΟΑ…¦z sot]
put-1p.sbj neg cart in.front ox | yet neg-have.1p 1p def area even leave.p.pst
Let's not get ahead of ourselves; we haven't even left the borough yet.
