44.4: hurstar "equip"

hurstar /h…ΣrΥàstar/ [h…ΣΥêΥàst…ëΥê]
- equip, be supplied with, to obtain something necessary (especially weaponry or similar tools) in order to carry out a specific action or task;
- don, put on, to clothe oneself with some article of clothing or accessory;
- be taught, self-teach, train oneself in, to learn a particular skill in preparation for some specific situation

Etymology: descendant of Old Boral hurstar "decorate, adorn" with a shift in argument structure (the patient changing from the thing receiving ornament to the items used to adorn) in evidence from the fourteenth century onward. This itself is a borrowing from synonymous Old English hyrstan; compare Merch hirstend /Υàh…ΣΥês…ôn/ "decorative, extra, superfluous".

Dou xiphar hurstaurn glay e me lou svingaurn.
/du ksiΥàfar h…ΣrΥàstornΧ© gle e me lu sv…ΣnΥàgornΧ©/
[du ks…ΣΥàf…ëΥê h…ΣΥêΥàstoΥê≈΄ gle e me lu zv…Σ≈΄ΥàgoΥên/
two merc equip-pst.3p sword and 1s.obl 3p.acc wield-pst.3p
Two mercenaries picked up swords and brandished them at me.

##

44.5: Cordin and Brenna

collected notes on a major population centre on Borland, as well as the last queen of the First Kingdom of Borland.

Cordin is a city in Borland. It is known for its prolific press Cordin Editions, which from the late twenthieth century on has provided textbooks to schools nationwide. Its name is usually considered to be unrelated to the Borlish word cordin "string".

History
In the late 1660s the young James V of Barcelon was a ward in Cordin.

By the Long Peace period Cordin had become one of the largest cities on the island, with a thriving high society to rival that of the capital in Damvath.

People
Professor Marc Jeichey (fl. 2019), lecturer in concurrence history at the University of Cordin who spent some years working in peripatetico at the Dijon New School
Theign Roufler (fl. 2016), scholar and author of book 'From Tablets to Slates: The Story of Education' (Des Delt vars Dois : Y Stoir d'Education)

---

Brenna of Barrow (fl. 898) was the cousin of King Roderick of Dunclothe. She married the young King Jothey of Borland shortly before the First Kingdom of Borland fell to the Danes in 898. Subsequently she fled with her husband to Sothbar, where she bore several children.

Two decades later Sothbar surrendered to Dane Borland and the family were forced to flee once more, this time to Kent. Much of our knowledge of the remainder of her life is preserved through the Brethin copies of her correspondence with her aging uncle in Dunclothe.

##

45.1: Early Methodics

excerpt in translation from 1999 scholastic quire The Grimmer Hall: Developments in Early Methodics (originally published in Cambrick as ≈¹ys y GrymrageΟΑ: Dasplygad nhy Methodig Kysevin), written by New Leudong historian and methodist Cai Sonquo during his tenure at the University of Mehannin (Brisbane).

ObligaΟΑ no's un sautel lonc demay cas figuration por y despley ondatim benoΟßar por regest exactessem augrimar dy gyr de corp cisjovial.
It does not take too great a conceptual leap to use the waveform display to compute more accurate records of the gyres of cisjovial bodies.

Vray, pre y siecr novoΟß tost ja encontrau nos prefaction magn tranq de conjonction planeter Ixion envount ny scaunΟß dell'astronom ambrojan Fabian MostΟ†f,
Indeed, already by the early 1800s we see very precise predictions of planetary conjunctions involving Ixion [Ceres] in the writings of Ambrosian astronomer Fabian MostΟ†f,

granfigl dy mathematurg Raymond al Mustafa oc meΟΑes ayent aΟ·ΟΑaΟΑ a y fondamen de methodic defassar deur cinquant annaΟΑ.
grandson of the mathematician Raymond al Mustafa who himself had helped develop the foundations of methodics a half-century earlier.

Nell'oc asc er MostΟ†f laurant all'observatoir de Paratzon ne Brasil auster e noc obstroint y sar campaner parvasiscent se cous progression,
At this time MostΟ†f was working at the observatory at Paratzon in eastern Brasil [Newfoundland] and despite advancements in the spreading steeplemesh,

collaboration tras l'ocean atlantic remanen lent, descovart plusour sceint fait cos istraΟΑ ny Novomont e Vetomont y dou.
collaboration across the Atlantic remained slow, with several discoveries made separately in the Novomund and Vetomund alike.

##

45.2: sortir ag vousc "to tryst"

sortir ag vousc /s…îrΥàt…Σr …¦j vux/
- to tryst, rendezvous, to meet somebody in private to be intimate with them;
- to hook up, to have a casual sexual experience with someone

Etymology: idiom attested from the late eighteenth century but probably older, literally meaning "to go out to the strawberries". The verb sortir "to go out" is a descendant of Latin sortior "to draw by lots, to distribute" with an unclearly-motivated but very early semantic change (compare cognate forms across Gaul and northern Italy).

The noun vousc "strawberry" reflects Old Boral ba»ù vousc (cf. modern Boral bag /b…¦j/ "berry"), presumably a calque of Old EnglishβÄôs synonymous strΟ©awber»ùe "strew-berry", so called for its plant growing low down, the fruits appearing to have been strewn across the ground. The putative verb ?voscar/vojar "to strew, scatter" is otherwise unattested, howeverβÄîit is tempting to postulate a borrowing from Borland Kelt.

Nos venau de retornar posc sortir ag vousc.
/n…îz veΥàno de Υ¨re.t…îrΥànar p…îx s…îrΥàt…Σr …¦j vux/
[n…îz v…ΣΥàno de Υ¨ Äe.t äΥêΥàn…ëΥê p…îx s äΥêΥàt…ΣΥêβÄΩ Ä…¦j Υàvux]
1p come-ipf.1p from return-inf after go.out-inf to.def strawberry
We had just got back from a tryst together.

45.3: ollem "elm"

ollem /…îΥàl…¦m/ [ äΥàl…¦m]
- elm, (especially) wych elm, tree of the genus Ulmus with asymmetric leaves and small petalless flowers;
- (slang, sometimes derogatory) person from Pentrose, a port town on the south coast of Borland, especially in the context of regional rivalry or applied to someone of British descent

Etymology: via Old Boral olem "elm", apparently reflecting an unattested Insular Latin ulΡ™mus as a variant of ulmus "elm" influenced by Kelt *lΡ™m "elm". We also see forms such as olmair in the Old Boral period with the addition of suffix -air to denote a type of tree.

Use of the word as a demonym for Pentrose is in evidence from the nineteenth century (cf. the banner and nickname of the local lineball team) but likely predates it, given the historical prevalence of elm trees in the area.

Bosc d'ollem sarf a bog fair.
/b…îx d…îΥàl…¦m sarf a b…îj fer/
[b…îx d äΥàl…¦m s…ëΥêf …ê Υàb…îj feΥê]
wood of-elm serve to bow make-inf
Elm wood is used to make bows.

45.4: birgasq "apricot"

birgasq /b…ΣrΥàgask/ [b…ΣΥêΥàgask]
- apricot, pale orange-coloured fruit of a tree in the Prunus genus which spread throughout the Roman Empire by way of Armenia;
- a pale yellow-orange colour like that of the fruit;
- something or someone considered particularly admirable or coveted;
- a particularly contented or satisfied emotional state;

Etymology: from the Middle Boral period, a borrowing from northern Portingale o birgasque "the apricot", variant of earlier obirgoque apparently influenced by synonym damasco, literally "from Damascus". This in turn comes from Arabic ΊßΌ³Ό£Ί®ΌéΊ±Ό£Ό²ΌèΌàΌ² (al-barq≈Ϊq) "apricots, (later) plums", probably via the form albercoc in Barcelon.

Its figurative extension is in evidence from the eighteenth century, originally used to describe particularly attractive women; compare similar uses of various words for fruits in other languages.

Tu a birgasq oc aubonβÄîte cay cal fallout?
/ti a b…ΣΥêΥàgask …îk oΥàb…în | te ke kal faΥàlut/
[tsja b…ΣΥêΥàgask Υ¨…î.g äΥàb…în | t…Σ Υàke kaw f…êΥàlut]
2s have apricot s.px morning | 2s.obl fall what venture
You look happy this morningβÄîwhat're you up to?

##

45.5: Willemy

Willemy is a polity in northern Europe, situated east of Norman Kent and France.

History
The Duchy of Willemy was a minor polity within the German Empire during the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries. Forming much of the empire's border with the First Drengot Empire to the west, defence against the marksmen of Willemy led Drengot Emperor Roger II to spend greatly on castle fortifications from Flanders to the Alps.

The Great Flie Flood of 1171 swept away much of Lower Frisia in Willemy, creating several new islands in the Rustigh Strait (such as Texel) and killing then-ruler Lovis the Tall, who was in Amstel at the time. The devastation in the region is thought to have allowed Borlish Axbane to flourish as a trading post in the following century.

In the Revitalist Period and for some time afterwards, Willemy was split between neighbouring powers and portions of its territory changed hands regularly. The duchy of Avosche was unusually stable and held by France for over a century.

Trading operations from Willem and its cities in the Stadbund (the BrΟ≥derrat) contributed to staddomains in Atlantic Cappatia, most notably Neuwillem, whose BrΟ≥derratcronike (comprising a record of its activities from 1561 to 1597) is an important first-hand account of conditions in the region at the time.

Willem was the focal point of the seventeenth-century Blue Trans-Willem War, usually numbered among the Romantic Wars. This war led incidentally to the dissolution of the Kingdom of Burgundy and the creation of the Duchy of Far Suebia in Bavaria.

The two-arm system for sending steeplepost was first deployed in Willem in the early nineteenth century, to send the first lines of the Pater Noster from the church in the town of Thynen to neighbouring Bost. This event is likely what led to the name 'steeplepost' being applied to the technology.

People
Rulers
Duke Lovis the Tall (d. 1171), who perished in the Great Flie Flood
AdaillΟ© Nassow, Duchess d'Avosche (1524-1608), infamous during her life for her influence at the court of David of France
Other
Carlyn van Stede (fl. 1965), author of parachthon giftale 'While Gold Yet Runs in My Veins' (Zolang Gout dΟ≥r myn Ο¹dren Noh VlΟ≥ye)
Dr Mickel CrΟΓyenschot (fl. c19), noted early supporter of the Deviance movement and public tovarick even as France embraced the Modest Arrangement.
NiklΟΓs der Fecht (fl. 1828), a mathematician in the city of 
Tewis Camigner (fl. c16), theorist who noted the high sugar content of beet syrup, though betraver sugar would not be produced commercially for another two centuries

46.1: Wearing a Mask or a Suit (cont.)

continued excerpt from Portingale author Ezio Carvallo's 1860 trevold Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico, pioneer of the masquira genre of stories.

Y porrujon cercondaurn y preu taug enter ny palaΟß, e donc au Matthias prudenΟß fait por y cogn reconnoscr ant asmuth vars y rugment dallottaΟΑ.
The balcony ran around the entire first storey of the palace, and Matthias had been careful to note which corner faced the rugged uplands.

L'aer sta fresc e clar, eΟΑ y porrujon sta pres vogt d'alcun hom.
The air was fresh and clear, and the balcony was almost deserted.

Caye freit demay por y plurem dell'host cas Escriva vinn sou y ciel ovart eΟΑ admirn la y sigt, eΟΑ y ci autr hom pog sur y porrujon stan haft demay ne collocq por y corp angost ne rob fosc parcevir dimeyant lonc y mur.
It was too cold for most of the Escrivas' guests to stand out in the open air and admire the view, and the few other people on the balcony were too busy chatting to notice the slight body in dark robes edging along the wall.

Lonc exact l'aΟΑendr sceye un lumner blau e febr, ignau aΟΑief a visibr apen, appariscent ny ciel norΟΑer just dessur dy loffocq.
Precisely on schedule a faint blue light, so soft that it was barely visible, appeared in the sky to the north just above the horizon.

46.2: bro "dude"

bro /bro/ [b Äo]
- (vocative) dude, bro, mate, term of address for a (usually male) friend used generally in an admiring or appreciative context;
- macho, jocky, masculine (person), a person typified by the impression of physical strength and other male-associated characteristics

Etymology: often conflated with synonymous Willem broe or Merch brow /bru/ (both clippings of words for "brother") and likely influenced in usage by these, but thought to be otherwise unrelated. In the Middle Boral period we primarily see the spelling brau "hero, worthy man", suggesting a connection to braf "brave, heroic" from Italian via French.

Early and uncertain attestions of a word ?prau "poss. fighter, warrior" constitute an alternative etymology; this word may reflect the Latin adjective pravus "perverse, vicious", although the semantic connection is tenuous.

Nos aum issaur magn je ados, bro!
/n…îz om …ΣΥàsor m…¦jn  £e aΥàd…îz | bro/
[n…îz om …ΣΥàsoΥê m…¦jn  ù…êΥàd…îz | b Äo]
1p have-pt.1p success many day.of behind | bro
We won so much yesterday, bro!

46.3: descorΟßar "peer"

descorΟßar /Υ¨de.x…îrΥàtsar/ [Υ¨de.x äΥêΥàts…ëΥê]
- peer at, eye, to look at deliberately as if with difficulty or apprehension;
- inspect, scrutinise, to examine something with great care or detail;
- (rare, literally) peel, shell, to remove the bark or peel from the surface of a tree or plant

Etymology: originally in the last sense, either a native formation in the Middle Boral period from noun scorΟß "bark, peel" and prefix de- indicating reversal or movement away, or else a calque of Latin dΡ™cortic≈ç "I remove the bark or peel" formed in the same way.

Metaphorical use is seen almost immediately and by the eighteenth century the verb has almost entirely been supplanted in its literal sense by periphrastic construction scorΟß delir de "to remove bark/peel from".

Il ern l'imascry descorΟßant ag cogn jagerrem.
/…Σl …¦rn Υ¨li.maΥàxri Υ¨de.x…îrΥàtsant …¦j k…îjn  £aΥàg…¦.r…¦m/
[…Σl…¦Υên Υ¨li.mΟΠΥàx Äi Υ¨de.x äΥêΥàtsant …¦j k…îjn  ù…êΥàg…¦. Ä…êm]
3p cop.ipf-3p def=sculpture eye-p.prs at.def corner far-comp
They were peering at the sculpture in the far corner.

46.4: gevou "watch out!"

gevou /geΥàvu/ [g…ΣΥàvu]
- (exclamation) watch out, beware, be careful, exhortation to remain safe by acting in a cautious manner;
- forewarning, heads-up, alert of upcoming danger given ahead of time;
- caveat, proviso, stumbling block, an exception or other awkward condition that is worth knowing about and may need working around

Etymology: first in exclamatory usage, borrowed in the seventeenth century from French phrase a guΟ© vous "look out!" (later written together as guΟ©-vous and used as an ordinary noun), literally "to (the) lookout, you!". The noun guΟ© "watchman, vigil, lookout" descends from Old French, borrowed from the Frankish descendant of Classical German vactu≈ç "watch, vigil".

The word's phonetic resemblance to the English word "careful" is coincidental.

DeΟΑ lour gevou cant y rafayat revin.
/d…¦ΈΗ lur geΥàvu kant i Υ¨ra.feΥàjat reΥàv…Σn/
[Υàd…¦.l äΥê g…ΣΥàvu kanβÄΩti Υ¨ Äa.f…ΣΥàjat  Ä…ΣΥàv…Σn]
give-imp.p 3p.obl watch.out while def tailor come.back.sbj
Give them a heads-up if the tailor comes back.

##

46.5: Channels

The Rustigh Strait (Borlish: Naur Rustig /nor r…ΣΥàstaj/, Frisian Nao Rustig /n…ëw Υàr è.st…Σg/, literally "calm strait") is the channel of water separating the southern coast of Borland from the northern coasts of Flanders and Willemy.

The strait's position often gave it great strategic importance, in terms of both military and trade. For example, coastal cities on the strait like Axbane and Amstel [Amsterdam] were natural stopping points for ships trading between Dane lands to the east and the Welsh and Vascon trade meshes to the west.

During and following the Albic Wars of Fealty, British communities of immigrants spread along the cities on both sides of the Rustig, such as Pentrose.

An air-steeple connection was set up in the 1830s across the Rustig from NausΟßod on Borland to Laicouche in French Guillenne (now LagcΟ≥ch), where the strait was less than seven leagues across. In 1854 this was superseded by the creation of an undersea barrick cable.

--

Methodics is the field of study pertaining to the discovery, development and improvement of methods. In this scholastic context a method is any series of instructions that may be unambiguously and repeatably followed to produce some result.

History
For millennia methods were developed sporadically without much awareness of general methodical principles. For example, an early method in the historical record is Euclid's procedure for finding the coarsest scale common to two whole numbers. Later we find methods involving tables of pre-computed values, such as Nadim's Method for multiplication by means of the Trapolitan identity for arrhumb [cosine].

Methods with more immediate practical use were continually developed; by the sixteenth century the threepoint method [triangulation] for calculating distances was vital in creating accurate maps for the explorations of the Intertwining Century.

One the first major results in abstract methodics came from the study of life, with Rachel Desselut's 1743 work Parable of the Mute Bookbinder, which argued that in order for living matter to duplicate, it must contain within itself an explicit method describing its construction. Although heredian acid [DNA] would not be discovered for another century, her work accurately predicted the necessary separation of Form and Method that would become the bedrock of the study of heredity.

An incresingly-important category of method from the eighteenth-century onward has been procedures for encrypting text, either to conceal it or else simply to convert it into a suitable form for a given steeplepost.

References
Drawing in the Sand: the History of Calculation, a collaborative reference book originally published 1954 in Napolitan as Scheme ne Rene: a Storia d'Amiradanza by the Agenza Scholaria Fodgesa

##

47.1: King James Stew

excerpt in translation from 1880 mealbook Pasti Mezziterreni, written by traveller Josforo Sant'Angelo.

De toΟΑ y ragost a pescot meyon provaΟΑ lonc y rimmot solary ne Barcelon e ProvenΟß (noc obstroint y desc unic a pallort eΟΑ olif me sarviΟΑ sur y ci play ne Mayorca), alcun den poΟΑ acavar ag Rey Jagm.
Of all the fish stews I sampled along the solarium coasts of Barcelon and Provence (not to mention the singular mussel-olive dish I was served on the beaches of Mayurca), none compare to the King James.

SurnommaΟΑ cos vecin eΟΑ ant tendreΟß y jaumetia "picq Jagm", l'oc ragost pren sy nom de Jagm VI (1691-1749), y monarch de regn y stendiscendessem ne Barcelon des stablir a 1067.
Locally and affectionately called the jaumetia βÄ€little JamesβÄù, this stew is named for James VI (1691-1749), the longest-ruling monarch in Barcelon since its establishment in 1067.

Degnant ig sceusn ragost comportant ny region pre sy regn, y majortaΟΑ sarven for toΟΑ y mansc consumir oc remanent des y vanc d'un jorn.
Although similar stews existed in the region before his reign, most simply functioned to use up whatever seafood remained from a dayβÄôs catch.

Y Rey Jagm es parcanΟß y preu desc untal cogt por gra de sey, e certan val por issent l'ottem luΟß ben hus benoΟßar, ben untal pescot blanc.
The King James is perhaps the first such dish made for its own sake, and indeed should for best results use hake or haddock, or a similar whitefish.

47.2: un tarΟß dagr "neither fish nor fowl"

un tarΟß dagr /…Σn tarts d…¦jrΧ©/ […Σn Υàt…ëΥês Υàd…¦j…ê]
- neither fish nor fowl, something or someone not easily categorised, not fitting into the established roles of a given situation;
- grey area, ambiguity, a situation intermediate between more familiar states that is not clear or where the rules are not known
- maverick, renegade, iconoclast, law unto oneself, a person refusing to be bound by societal conventions

Etymology: clipped form of earlier proverb no plom ben aur ja tarΟß dagr "neither lead nor gold but [a] secret third [thing]". This has itself been frozen in an archaic dialect due to its appearance in the evergreen plays of KinollesauβÄîin Modern Boral we might instead see necun plom ben aur, aglon un treyem scos.

The word tarΟß "third" descends directly from synonymous Latin tertius and now exists in complementary distribution with later derivation treyem < trΡ™s "three". The disused adjective dagr "secret, hidden, unknown" comes via Old Boral da»ùl, da»ùr from Old English deagol "secret, obscure, dark".

L'eΟß aldr aun ja un tarΟß dagr me rent.
/l…¦ts aldrΧ© on  £a …Σn tarts d…¦jrΧ© me r…¦nt/
[l…ΣΥàtsaw.d ÄβÄΩon  ùa …Σn Υàt…ëΥês Υàd…¦j…ê me Υà Ä…¦nt]
def-p.px parent have-3p yet idf third secret 1s.obl make-p.pst
With these parents I've been neither fish nor fowl.

47.3: haft "busy"

haft /haft/ [haft]
- busy, occupied, engaged in activity to a sufficient extent that one would have difficulty doing something else at the same time;
- in use, occupied, active, currently being used in a way that prevents others from using the same thing;
- full, booked out, having been reserved for use entirely so that there is no space for anyone new

Etymology: via Old Boral haft "seizure, capture, imprisonment", apparently a deverbal of an unattested *haftar which would reflect Old English hΟΠftan "to seize, capture, imprison". Its use in apposition as an adjective is seen from the fourteenth century, originally in the sense "captive, unfree" and soon after in its modern senses.

Quisteu raut neΟΑan y davarn sey haft.
/kwiΥàstaw rot neΥàΟΑan i daΥàvarn si haft/
[kw…ΣΥàstaw  Äot n…ΣΥàΟΑan i d…êΥàv…ëΥên si Υàhaft]
call-1p.sbj quick lest def hotel be.sbj busy
Let's call now before the hotel's fully booked.

47.4: pallort "mussel"

pallort /paΥàl…îrt/ [p…êΥàl…îΥêt]
- mussel, any of several groups of bivalve shellfish with long asymmetric shells;
- halfwit, dullard, someone perceived to be lacking in intelligence

Etymology: from Old Boral palorde "clam, mussel", from synonymous Vulgar Latin *palorda, itself an alteration of Classical pel≈çris, pel≈çridem "large clam or mussel". The word has been used as an insult since at latest the sixteenth century.

Jo comprene noc sceye pallort ny farrago.
/ £o Υ¨k…îm.preΥàne n…îk xiΥàje paΥàl…îrt ni Υ¨fa.raΥàgo/
[ ùo Υ¨k…îm.p Ä…ΣΥàne n…îk Οßje p…êΥàl…îΥêt ni Υ¨fa. Ä…êΥàgo]
1s understand-ipf neg there.be-ipf mussel in-def hash
I didn't realise the hash would have mussels in.

##

47.5: The Modest Arrangement

The Modest Arrangement was a social movement in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries characterised by more stringent enforcement of morality codes, especially with respect to familial powers.

The Arrangement saw a resurgence in the regions of Gaul and Spain in the early nineteenth century after something of an ebb during the Long Peace. This has been attributed to the rising popularity of the Deviance movement inspiring a backlash among its opposers; certainly Modest ideology depicts itself as less secure and more embattled in its later incarnations.

One of the most salient aspects of the Modest Arrangement from the modern perspective is its propensity to censorship, particularly of works deemed contrary to decency. Famously, author AntΟ≤in Herrel (latterly Tony Smith) was forced to flee Vascony to Kent after attempting to publish his blasphemous work 'Earthly Scene' (Vista Terrena).

The Second German War of the 1820s saw the grip of the Modest Arrangement weaken in many places, if only incidentallyβÄîas faith in existing power structures waned so too did people's trust in those powers' enforcement of the law. The days of Modest Vascony were numbered almost as soon as its interests in Mendeva and the East were threatened and the VallabrΟ§ administration under King Hernand declared an alliance with the Convoy.

Although the Modest Arrangement would not survive the 1850 Laic Declarations in Belgrade in any official capacity, polities like Vascony, (New) Provence and Morrack would retain some hallmarks of the Arrangement well into the twentieth century.
Proponents

    Ruvien Baume, Marquis de Caillat (fl. 1810), noted for his fervence in opposition to Deviance Theology
    Diana Theodora Queiroz, considered to have laid the groundwork for the later Blue Marian movement

##

48.1: Study Materials

excerpt in translation from the condensed study materials for the New Vithor course Concurrence History of the 16th Century: Death, Ships and the Movement of the Heavens.

Un bustr l'oc eutaΟΑ dessur contradignt es Vascony; l'oc a graΟß a profit Novomondin des pelur eΟΑ autr enascment un aΟΑoinΟß cas pouvlanΟß souvaΟΑ de 1500 vars 1550 (Portingale noc ig inclus) com issent d'immigration.
One counterexample to the above trend is Vascony which, thanks to Novomundine profits in furs and other goods, actually saw in increase in population from 1500 to 1550 (excluding Portingale) due to immigration.

Y fin dell'AscenenΟß Vascon con y Var dy Dou Quen vole denou mettr ag model feudal ne Vascony pre fos y siecr fis, portant.
The end of the Vascon Ascendancy with the War of the Two Queens would end feudalism in Vascony by the end of the century, however.

(Val svoccar ig l'oc no sta kynous y dicerrem vars y model feudal finir nell'aust, yon y ci concarnΟß islamesc de Kieu e de Cazany russesc souvartoy y nobiltaΟΑ cos colombandessem.)
(It is worth mentioning that this was not the main factor in ending feudalism in the east, where the Islamic conquests of Kiew and Russian Cazany upended the nobility more directly.)

L'un effeit parcanΟß imprefaibr dy diminution dy model feudal sta y reitfianΟß de ley com cod suntuer.
One, perhaps unexpected, effect of the decline of the feudal system was the strengthening of laws such as sumptuary codes.

48.2: flottisc "fleet"

flottisc /fl…îΥàt…Σx/ [fl äΥàt…ΣΟß]
- fleet, flotilla, convoy, a group of ships or occasionally of other vehicles;
- (by extension) navy, the collective naval force of a country;
- backup, reinforcements, relief force, additional troops or equipment sent to support a military action (also in metaphorical use)

Etymology: originally in the first sense only in Old Boral flotisc, deverbal noun from verb flotir "to float, to be still in the water", itself a borrowing likely from Old English flotian "to float".

Jo no poΟΑ lou affaΟßar pre vienn y flottisc.
/ £o no p…îΈΗ lu Υ¨a.faΥàtsar pre Υàvj…¦nnΧ© i fl…îΥàt…Σx/
[ ùo n ä Υàp…îh lu Υ¨a.f…êΥàts…ëΥê p Äe Υàvj…¦nΥê i fl äΥàt…ΣΟß]
1s neg can 3p.acc face-inf before come-3p def fleet
I can't confront them until reinforcements arrive.

48.3: pernix "nimble"

pernix /p…¦rΥàn…Σks/ [p…ΣΥêΥàn…Σks]
- nimble, dextrous, deft, agile, skillful with one's hands and able to make very precise movements;
- swift, fleet, rapid and graceful in motion;
- subtle, crafty, insidious, manipulative, being able to direct someone else's decisions without their knowledge

Etymology: learned borrowing in the Middle Boral period from Latin pernix "nimble, agile, swift", thought to be connected with perna "ham, haunch, gammon" (cf. inherited Boral parn "bacon"), although the expected Latin form would be *pernΡ¹x.

Its latter sense develops in the eighteenth century under the malign influence of unrelated perniΟßous "harmful, pernicious, deleterious", from synonymous Latin pernici≈çsus, a derivative of pernec≈ç "I kill, slay, annihilate".

L'eye doutr y verroil ant deit pernix.
/liΥàje ΥàdutrΧ© i v…¦Υàr…îil ant dit p…¦rΥàn…Σks/
[lje Υàdut. ÄβÄΩi v…ΣΥà Ä…îj älβÄΩan dit p…ΣΥêΥàn…Σks]
3s-go-ipf past def lock with finger deft
She picked the lock with nimble fingers.

48.4: 1st Lexember

coΟΑelostaΟΑ "caution"

coΟΑelostaΟΑ /koΥ¨ΟΑe.l…îΥàstaΈΗ/ [k äΥ¨ΟΑ…¦.l äΥàstah]
- caution, wariness, vigilance, watchfulness, the state of begin alert to potential dangers or mistakes;
- anxiety, alarm, pessimism, an unpleasant condition of mental unease or concern about the uncertain future

Etymology: nominal derivative of coΟΑelous "cautious, wary; attentive, aware" first attested in the fifteenth century. This adjective is itself a derivative of noun coΟΑel "caution" (now obsolete, but compare French choile "lie, deceit" and various learned forms across Romance), from synonymous cautΡ™la in Latin. Extension to more negative emotional states is seen from the eighteenth century.

Sy coΟΑelostaΟΑ er enΟßant a nos tardir.
/si koΥ¨ΟΑe.loΥàstaΈΗ …¦r …¦nΥàtsant a n…îz tarΥàd…Σr/
[si k äΥ¨ΟΑ…¦.l äΥàstaΟΑβÄΩ…¦Υê ÄβÄΩ…ΣnΥàtsant a n…îz t…êΥêΥàd…ΣΥê]
3s.gen caution cop.ipf start-p.prs at 1p slow-inf
His caution was starting to slow us down.

##

48.5: 2nd Lexember

sarzin "buckwheat"

sarzin /sarΥàz…Σn/ [s…êΥêΥàz…Σn]
- (obsolete) Muslim, Saracen, a person who is a follower of Islam;
- (archaic or dialect) non-Christian, pagan, practioner of a religion other than Christianity and especially one performing practices associated with pre-Christian peoples or peoples not yet exposed to Christianity;
- (historical) cairn, a conical heap of stones placed by the pre-Roman Kelt people around Borland or a modern such heap resembling these;
- (historical, by extension) milestone, a pillar or pile of stones placed at the side of a road to mark distance;
- buckwheat, an originally Asian plant whose fruit is widely used in cooking as a pseudocereal

Etymology: in its only surviving modern sense, a clipping of the earlier bla sarzin "Saracen wheat" which is first attested in the fifteenth century, in reference to its eastern origin (the plant having been brought to Europe via the Turkish Riyalid Sultanate or possibly via Russian Cazany).

Likewise, in the senses "cairn, milestone" it is a clipping of pieΟΑr sarzin "Saracen stones", from the perception that these piles of stones were pre-Christian monuments to pagan gods.

The word itself is a borrowing from Old French sarrazin "Muslim, Saracen" reflecting Late Latin SaracΡ™nus and Ancient Greek ΈΘαΨΑœ¹αΨΑΈΚΈΖΈΫœ¨œ² (SarakΡ™nΟ≥s). These originally referred to an Arabic or para-Arabic people of North Arabia.

Y cociner enΟßa voug cas hojol ne sarzin.
/i Υ¨ko.tsiΥàn…¦r …¦nΥàtsa vuj kaz hoΥà £…îl ne sarΥàz…Σn/
[i Υ¨k…î.ts…ΣΥàn…¦Υê ÄβÄΩ…ΣnΥàtsa Υàvuj kaz h äΥà ù…îw ne s…êΥêΥàz…Σn]
def cook start-ipf wrong wrt pancake in buckwheat
The cook was terrible at buckwheat pancakes.

---

excerpt from The Delicious Story of Dough and Oil, a work of popular history detailing the history of many related foods across the globe. Published (originally in Awrish as TΟ©sta Olaivan VlΟΓxerte "A World of Dough in Oil") in 1963 by first-time writer and famed chef Shomoji Taxon, who in his later years would go on to present the first cooking programme shown simultaneously on both sides of the Atlantic.

βÄΠsee its eastern origins by its names in the languages of EuropeβÄîwhether in Horther mΟΗurick ("Moorish wheat") or Bavarn creshke (originally from a Russian word meaning "Greek"). Nonetheless, sarzin [buckwheat] flour has since its introduction become a key component of diets across the continent.

We turn first to a dish which originated in Vallack communities and spread west upriver as the region was united politically under the Kingdom of the Danaw [Danube] during the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. Even after the kingdom's collapse and the rise of the Riverine Cities in the nineteenth century, farama dry-pottage has remained an important tradition from Pojon [Bratislava] to Gollamo [Ruse].

Farama seems originally to have been associated with wedding anniversaries, as an inexpensive (at least, before the recipe was augmented with unseasonal berries or spices like dallasine [cinnamon]) of celebrating each passing year. Over time the traditions around farama have shifted; in many places the sarzin dish is now served on birthdays.

Along with the area's distinctive hodgel-cakes [in this case, blini], farama enjoyed an explosion in popularity worldwide (usually divorced from its vicine traditions) after their prominent inclusion in the filmβÄΠ

48.6: 3rd Lexember

gallaiΟΑ "galaxy"

gallaiΟΑ /gaΥàleΈΗ/ [g…êΥàleh]
- galaxy, collection of stars all orbiting a common centre;
- seemingly-uncountable collection of people of things conceptualised as comprising a vast variety;
- (dated) the Milky Way, the galaxy in which the Sun and Earth are found and which is most readily visible in the night sky

Etymology: from late Old Boral galaix, galais "the Milky Way", learned borrowing from synonymous Latin galaxiΡ¹s and Ancient Greek (ΈΚœçΈΚΈΜΈΩœ²) Έ≥αΨΑΈΜαΨΑΈΨαΩêΧ¹αΨ±œ², literally "milky circle". Altered in the early Middle Boral period under the influence of laiΟΑ "milk", from Latin lac, lactem "milk" (or possibly from a Borland Kelt form; compare Cambrick ≈²aeth "milk").

Used as a proper noun until the discovery of other galaxies in the Good Game period of the late nineteenth century. Metaphorical use for other vast collections of things is seen since the seventeenth century, especially in poetry.

PostulaΟΑ es l'oc roc vien d'un autr gallaiΟΑ.
/Υ¨p…î.stiΥàlaΈΗ …¦z l…îk Υàr…îk vj…¦n d…Σn ΥàotrΧ© gaΥàleΈΗ/

There's speculation that this rock comes from another galaxy.

orin "homeland"

- homeland, hometown, origins, the place where one was born or which one considers to be home;

excerpt in translation from a 1828 steeplepost letter sent from Pentrose to  by mathematician Darren Brodus.

Vos vouΟΑ cas mendrem outr bon me fair par noc desmanneΟßar a Paris, citaΟΑ de vareΟß e carogn, ΟΑa meΟΑes tu derra pieΟß Fransc Ustol parsir por gra de ty fem.
You will at the very least do me the service of not moving to Paris, city of greed and carrion, even if you must learn some Proper French for the sake of your wife. 

Dec Hortense oc lerra cos certan, jo vil ci bon vouΟΑ de Clara communeyar e rescaur ig vos porreΟΑ pront se sarvir dy lencorf cas Vauhan; for caraΟΑ Marduth ag Tesoir Tyche.
As Hortense is certainly reading, I shall pass on Clara's well-wishes and reiterate that you are welcome to make use of the Vauhan lencorve; just ask for Marduth at the Tyche Bank.

Y scourm de Pether son tan voluΟΑ, parisc i, a partir de bel Albion traiΟßonous.
Pether's lot are also destined, it seems, to leave fair, perfidious Albion.

Ig degnant jo lour day ascuranΟß continu de xeny oy il son resoluΟΑ sur najar lonc y gran flou mendevan vars un ventur calscon seyon trovabr a Hasiny.
Despite my continuing assurances of welcome here they are determined to sail the great Mendevan river to whatever future they can find in Hasiny.

Jo dey admettr ig y sigt de murΟΑon de lau teir entr mey e casc hom Theudesc es formous naΟΑusc.
I must admit the prospect of being thousands of miles from any Dutchman has its appeal.

49.2: 6th Lexember

laundar "slip"

laundar /lonΥàdar/ [l änΥàd…ëΥê]
- slip, skid, slide, glide, to move along a slippery surface without traction;
- trip, stumble, stagger, to walk clumsily so as to (almost) fall over;
- err, misspeak, misstep, to be mistaken about something;
- pass, elapse, (of time) to be spent, especially without one achieving anything of import

Etymology: from Old Boral laundar "slip, err", either from an unattested synonymous Latin verb *lΡ¹bund≈ç or a contemporary verbal derivation of laund "slippery, unsteady" (the modern launt "slip, misstep, faux pas" is likely a later deverbal from the Middle Boral period). In either case the word originates with Latin lΡ¹bundus "slippery", participial form of deponent verb lΡ¹bor "I slip, sink, vanish, err".

49.3: 7th Lexember

garaf "grip"

garaf /gaΥàraf/ [g…êΥà Äaf]
- grip, friction, purchase, the state of being able to hold onto something without slipping;
- handhold, foothold, handle, part of a surface which allows one to hold on so as not to be moved;
- help, assistance, encouragement, physical or emotional aid from somebody that enables one not to fail;
- mastery, understanding, competence, the state of having learnt everything about something so that one can act with confidence in that domain

Etymology: from Middle Boral garaf "grip, handle", a borrowing from Portingale garafa "handle", of indeterminate origin. Either an alteration of earlier grafio, garfio "hook", from Latin graphium "stylus, pen", or else borrowed from Andalus Arabic Ί§ΌéΊßΊ±Ό£Ό¹ΌéΊßβÄé (gΟΓrfΟΓ) "handful, claw".

49.5: 9th Lexember

epu "express"

epu /eΥàpi/ […ΣΥàpi]
- (of a shop or similar) express, mini, providing a more limited but more focused service than usual;
- cheap, good value, having a gratifyingly low price for the level of quality offered;
- bitesize, mini, having been stripped down in complexity and/or size for easier immediate consumption;

Etymology: modern borrowing from French Ο©pu "express, direct, unhindered, available, at hand", first seen in Boral in the names of imported shopfronts such as Panier, whose Panier Οâpu establishments were much smaller but offered better deals than the main line.

Cognate with Boral speut "free, unfettered, clear", the word Ο©pu reflects Old French espeΟΦ, past participle of moribund verb espeΟ·r "to free, untangle, prepare". from Latin expedi≈ç "I remove the shackles of, I carry out, I am profitable".

49.7: 11th Lexember

stoccadar "lunge forward"

stoccadar /Υ¨st…î.kaΥàdar/ [Υ¨sto.k…êΥàd…ëΥê]
- thrust, lunge, push, to make an advance with force and especially while wielding a melee weapon;
- advance, assault, to move forward as part of a military operation;
- lobby, campaign, to push for a particular policy proposal

Etymology: verbal derivative from stoccad "a lunge, thrust" either in Middle Boral or in the synonymous French estoccade, estoccader before their adoption into Boral. This noun, which is a borrowing from Dutch stooken "to poke, stab", is reflected in Boral stoc "truncheon, club".

jannestar "sweep"

jannestar /Υ¨ £a.n…¦Υàstar/ [Υ¨ ùa.n…ΣΥàst…ëΥê]
- sweep, brush, to clean (a surface) using a broom or similar implement;
- (archaic or humorous) involve, embroil, drag in, to cause to be overly involved in;
- brush off, disregard, dismiss, to pay no attention to;

- misery, woe, distress, wretchedness, a state of great unhappiness characterised by physical or mental affliction;

50.4: 15th Lexember

aposc "forth"

aposc /aΥàp…îx/ […êΥàp…îx]
- forth, forward, ahead, in the direction of increasing time or degree or out from a particular place;
- henceforth, going forward, in future, from now on, at all times after the present;

Etymology: adverbial derivation from prepostion posc "after, since" < Late  Latin posque < post quem "after which". This word was originally  restricted to taking complements "after I get back" rather than nominal arguments "after the wedding", for which one would use now-obsolete pos < Latin post "after, behind"; the former had displaced the latter by  the fifteenth century.

- camp, campsite, an outdoor place acting as a temporary accommodation either in tents or entirely outdoors;
- improvisation, kludge, stopgap, patch, a temporary set-up formed by convenience or due to time constraints that one intends to replace with something more permanent

Etymology: from Old Boral bav»ùl (probably /baw é/) "tent, campsite", cognate with Cambrick pabell "tent" in coming via a Kelt form (the Borland reflex exhibiting characteristic initial voicing) from Latin pΡ¹pili≈ç "butterfly, (later) tent". In the latter sense the word is seen from the sixteenth century, originally with reference to a room's hasty arrangement of appliances/furniture or similar processes that resemble the ad-hoc set-up of a campsite.

opening of an 1813 letter sent by Dorothe Shock, proprietress of Miszen [1] emporium De Marnoshring, to her mother Johanne in her home village of Siorow. This missive is taken from the collected correspondence kept in trust at the Marnosh Gravatory [archives], the enterprise having grown dramatically from a single shop selling trinkets to a quasipolitic guild [multinational corporation] controlling much of the market for children's toys and various material hobbies. 

My dearest mother,

First I must reassure you that [her brother] young Crazi has arrived in Miszen safe and in good spirits. He has not taken to commerce as enthusiastically as you had hoped (or at least not as well as he has taken to nights out with soldierly friends and woodheaded mornings!) but his allegedly-charming features seem to have contributed to a string of advantageous deals with the usual suppliers.

You shall I expect be unsurprised to hear that the Potchware [2] woodcarvings refuse to be bought at even half the cost of importing them (never mind Hamburg's stanglehold on the Elbe); eventually I managed to convince a particuarly-credulous customer from Crovatia that they were the only attested artworks of a remote people of Chrysia, which limited our losses to an acceptable amount.

The mayor continues to promise better roads out west to LepziβÄîit is nonsensical that one cannot make the journey in a day and must suffer an uncomfortable night in the only Ossatz lodginghouse with tolerable chambermaids. I cannot see that such projects will find funds for themselves so long as the garrison demands its gold, and so long as the Boheme menace rattles its sabre across the southernβÄΠ

---

[1] An urban regional centre in Saxony on the site of our Meissen.
[2] A staddomain in Cappatia [South America] known at the time for its sugar and cocoa exports, and not (yet) renowned as a centre of artistic expression.

50.6: 17th Lexember

alcheΟΑr "once more"

alcheΟΑr /alΥàke.ΟΑrΧ©/ […êΥàg…¦.ΟΑ…ê]
- once more, anew, afresh, occurring again after a period of absence or otherwise with a definite break from the past;
- (obsolete) twice, occuring two times

Etymology: Middle Boral extension of now-obsolete eΟΑr "again, twice, repeatedly" after various function words like alcant "once", alcun "none" and alcot "how much". The element alc- descends from Latin determiner aliquΡΪ "some, any", while the adverb eΟΑr is either a direct reflection of Latin iterum "again, a second time" or a deverbal of eΟΑrar "to repeat", from related Latin iter≈ç "I repeat, I do again".

excerpt in translation from the diaries of NadΟ†lla fi Carol, who grew up in sixteenth-century Santrafew in the early days of New Provence.

Y fastig provisour ne bosc pin es parcevibr auvorn, e des l'argument entr PaΟΑr e Dembro l'es menaΟΑ vars digr y motif senglessem vel ig nos descenau certan pieΟß dy pieΟΑry deur y cost surveunt e cossy forniscau y naf con fastig for ne bosc.

L'oc idea aye apen pagrmen encostaΟΑ pre toll'aucoum ediculat ern tijon svingant, e soulor Zocala venoy aval de sy ci acraΟß ne moscon por y preu veg dell'oc annaΟΑ.

excerpt from the introduction of Modern Developments in Benjamine Detaxion [Aromatic Synthesis], a 1942 quire written by alchemist Ferris Bewly through the Conster Health Edifice and intended to provide a reference on the recent synthesis of various new substances and medicines around the world.

βÄΠJawan frankincense.

The first major section of this work will cover those hebetant substances [painkillers] which have come out of workshops globally over the last two decades. The sterling example is undoubtedly cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine (CD) [1], sold under various names and perhaps most familiar to Markland as the coveted 'Tommy pills', is a product of collaboration between the Branwen Sanatory in New Leudong and the University of Rimack in Tavancy [2] to find hebetants with faster action and fewer adversions.

Comprising (as the longform name implies) branches of cozebdone oil and maschal [3] in opposed position, the substance was first synthesised in 1932 and has already surpassed salix-derivative pills [4] in annual sales everywhere from Sangathy to Adighe [5]. This is due to the former's greater efficacy in relieving pain, which finds it more widely used than the previous bevy of remedies which may merely help to reduce symptoms of fever.

In sufficiently high concentrations CD has also been demonstrated to function in unguento, although the inefficiency and commodity of thisβÄΠ

---

[1] Here the longform unambiguous name for ibuprofen.
[2] Polities in southeastern Chrysia [Australia] and northwestern Cappatia [South America].
[2] Or as we would call them, isobutane (2-methylpropane) and propionic (propanoic) acid.
[4] For example, aspirin.
[5] Polities in far-inland Mendeva [North America] and the Caucasus.

By the time we do have evidence of manoscard melody in contemporary popular music, we see use of the scale across western Europe as far north as Mondwell [Anglesey] and as far south as Bagliemmo [Palermo], so it is difficult to determine its true homeland. 

Cay appondr ig y kyriel spanoy des Luberon tras Borgogn e soulor lontanessem par y siecr treΟß un postulation ant evidenΟß tan com toΟΑ autr, oc a digr alcun entr no's cos particuler suajoir.
The presumption that it radiated from Luberon through Burgundy and outward through the thirteenth century is as well-evidenced as any alternative hypothesis, which is to say not particularly convincingly. 

Certain scholer aun soumis ig l'oc asmuth d'influyenΟß fos vray ados, eΟΑ ig y kyriel se cous introdoit ne Manosq par un joun monc ayent le saut des y hammel a sy figleΟß.
Some scholars have argued that the direction of influence was in fact the reverse, and the scale was brought to Manosque by a young monk who had it from his childhood village.

excerpt from Lux ΟÜterna: The First Century of Threshold Force, written in 2002 (at the time in Dutch with the title Onslackbar VlΟΓy: DrampelstΟΗweth tΟΩdes har RΟ©st Οâw "Unquenchable Fire: Threshold Force in its First Century") by Cawen ba Dirxen at the VaronΟΗw Higher Edifice. This documentarian book was commissioned to commemorate the centenary of the discovery of the atomic nux [nucleus] in the city's NΟΩstad Atellier (then privately owned and run by substantial theorist Lohic Courvoisier de NΟΩstad).

βÄΠhas since 1996 produced more killdark [uranium]βÄîpredominantly processed immediately into tarmarette [yellowcake] formβÄîthan the combined output of all Chrysia [Australia].

Hints at the discrete nature of the composition of atoms (a phrase which itself would not have sat well with either side of the Atomicity Rift; atomicists were yet wedded to the intrinsic Leucippic model, while their opposition regarded the entire scheme as a merely useful fiction) had been encroaching into the understanding of matter for decades. Since the first corporal enumerations [periodic tables] in the 1840s it had been realised that naΟ·vely ordering corporals by weight led to inelegant groupings when alchemical properties were taken into account (most notably the strong motivation to place janthine [iodine] among its detaxional brethren like muria [chlorine]), and no theoretical basis for these transpositions would be proposed for decades.

At the same time, the apparently-unrelated phenomenon where the lightest corporals had all been measured to have weights almost exact multiples of that of aquifex [hydrogen], while this was not true at all of heavier ones (even such prosaic substances as copper!), demanded explanation. In 1881βÄîa dozen years before the invention of a device that could even test her theoryβÄîKievan theorist Nike Sarantylack proposed to resolve both questions at once. Her "mulberry" model (so-called by her detractors) supposed each atom to comprise a number of each of two differentβÄΠ

52.6: 31st Lexember

ubictaΟΑ "ubiquity"

ubictaΟΑ /Υ¨i.b…ΣkΥàtaΈΗ/ [Υ¨i.b…ΣkΥàtah]
- ubiquity, omnipresence, the quality of being everywhere;
- totalism, universalism, ideology characterised by the application of principles uniformly in all contexts;
- Ubiquity, a particular ideology which rose to prominence in the late nineteenth century and defined one pole of global power through the twentieth

Etymology: late medieval borrowing from non-classical Latin ubΡΪquitΡ¹s "ubiquity", nominal derivation of ubΡΪque "everywhere". Applied in philosophical contexts throughout the Latinate Vetomund from the sixteenth century, and used in particular for Ubiquity (originally as an insult) in 1848, in the translation of lady explorer Poshack Nerwaman's dismissive critique of political developments in Mashick [*Mexico].

UbictaΟΑ ablegau mell'yam deur Ommad.
/Υ¨i.b…ΣkΥàtaΈΗ Υ¨a.bleΥàgo m…¦Υàljam Υàdawr …îΥàmad/
[Υ¨i.b…ΣkΥàtah Υ¨a.bl…ΣΥàgo m…ΣΥàljam Υàda.w…ê ÄβÄΩ äΥàmad]
ubiquity expel-pst 3s.gen-uncle out.of Ommad
Ubiquity drove my uncle from Ommad.

53.1: Shady Dealings

translated excerpt from a 1813 letter from Dorothe Shock in Miszen [Meissen] to her mother Johanne.

I n'a y viΟΑ commercer adoptaΟΑ con tan farvour com tu aye le vuscaΟΑ (ben toΟΑ cas con un farvour men all'ig yon l'a casc noit jolleyant con amig soldartaΟß eΟΑ aubon a test boscer adoptaΟΑ!)
He has not taken to commerce as enthusiastically as you had hoped (or at least not as well as he has taken to nights out with soldierly friends and woodheaded mornings!) 

ma sy tegnt visacer fouceur cos rumoraΟΑ pariscn aΟ·r impet daΟΑ an congeries a venΟßon fort san con y ci forniscour usual.
but his allegedly-charming features seem to have contributed to a string of advantageous deals with the usual suppliers.

Tu nil ΟΑa j'ay bon scoc recevir n'oyent ig l'eΟß ensignment boscer poscvaran son neyant a se cavir acataΟΑ teint mitaΟΑ dy taus meΟΑes innajaΟΑ la (no dign y dominanΟß de Hambourg sur troc lonc Elf);
You shall I expect be unsurprised to hear that the Potchware woodcarvings refuse to be bought at even half the cost of importing them (never mind Hamburg's stanglehold on the Elbe);

toΟΑ cas final j'acavau un conement de Crovatia stant enfoldesc particuler ig l'eΟß fosn y soul ouvr d'art connuΟΑ d'un pouvlanΟß remot ne Chrysia, oc ayent nossy despart limitaΟΑ an quantaΟΑ supportabr.
eventually I managed to convince a particuarly-credulous customer from Crovatia that they were the only attested artworks of a remote people of Chrysia, which limited our losses to an acceptable amount.

53.2: quesq "damson"

quesq /kw…¦sk/ [kw…¦sk]
- damson, a subspecies of plum tree with sweeter and slightly ovoid fruits;
- damson, the fruit of this tree;
- deep purple, plum, aubergine, the colour of the damson fruit;
- cream of the crop, pick of the litter, the choice or most desirable part of something

Etymology: from Middle Boral (prun) quesq "damson (plum)", borrowed from Dutch dialectal queske, queshken "damson". This is a variant of synonymous standard Saxon tweshge and Bavarn zweschen, both borrowed from Vulgar Latin davascena "damascene plum" (cf. Classical damascΡ™num).

In metaphorical use a clipping of phrase quesq a tort "damson in the pie", attested from the seventeenth century.

Jo cogleu y quesq y maΟΑuressem dy ranscel.
/ £o k…îjΥàlaw i kw…¦sk i Υ¨ma.ΟΑiΥàr…¦.s…¦m di ranΥàx…¦l/
[ ùo k äjΥàlaw i kw…¦sk i Υ¨ma.ΟΑ…ΣΥà Ä…¦.s…êm di  Ä…ê…≤ΥàΟß…¦w]
1s gather-pst def damson def ripe-comp of-def branch
I picked the ripest damson on the branch.

53.3: mandrin "cyanide"

mandrin /manΥàdr…Σn/ [m…ênΥàd Ä…Σn]
- cyanide, a compound containing a -CβâΓN group or the CNβ¹Μ anion;
- (specifically) hydrogen cyanide, a colourless and very poisonous liquid;

(not to be confused with sandrin "vitamin C, ascorbic acid".)

Etymology: adjectival derivation from mandr "almond" (from the presence of cyanide in almonds) first used in expressions like mandrin a lix "potassium cyanide" in the eighteenth century.

Tag enverimmaΟΑ con mandrin occeyeu ty quanga.
/t…¦j …¦nΥ¨ve.r…ΣΥàmaΈΗ k…în manΥàdr…Σn Υ¨ok.siΥàjaw ti kwanΥàga/

tea poison-p.pst with cyanide kill-pst 2s.gen secretary
Your secretary was poisoned with cyanide-laced tea.

53.4: aversion "opposition"

aversion /Υ¨a.v…¦rΥàzj…în/ [Υ¨a.v…ΣΥêΥàzj…în]
- opposition, resistance, pushback, dissent, the state of being in disagreement with someone's actions;
- discontent, disgruntlement, dissatisfaction, resentment, feelings of unhappiness with the status quo;
- resistance, rebellion, uprising, revolt, organisation struggling for liberation against an authority seen as illegitimate (especially when underground or otherwise cloak-and-dagger)

Etymology: from Middle Boral aversion "opposition, resistance", seemingly the confluence of the two Latin roots Ρ¹vert≈ç "I turn away, repulse" and non-classical advert≈ç "I oppose, fight against" (a backformation from adversΡ¹rius "opponent"). In the last sense used especially for Collusion-supported movements through the twentieth century in regions dominated by Ubiquitous administration.

Banc redirigent machinal receveurn magn aversion.
/bank reΥ¨di.riΥà £…¦nt Υ¨ma.kiΥànal Υ¨re.tseΥàvaw.rnΧ© m…¦jn Υ¨a.v…¦rΥàzj…în/
[ba≈΄k  Ä…ΣΥ¨di. Ä…ΣΥà ù…¦n Υ¨ma.k…ΣΥànaw Υ¨ Äe.ts…ΣΥàva.w…ên m…¦jn Υ¨a.v…ΣΥêΥàzj…în]
bank redirect-p.prs mechanical receive-pst.3p much opposition
Automated redirection banks were met with significant opposition.

##

53.5: Brasil

Brasil is an island in the North Atlantic off the eastern coast of Mendeva. In the Novomund it was both the first location to be temporarily settled by people from the Vetomund and also the inception of sustained contact following the Landfall of 1471.

Unlike the name Mendeva, it is not known when the name Brasil was first used for this land. What is understood is that it was inspired by the Irish myth of Oil Breasail, a landmass west of Ireland that was said to be shrouded in mist except for one day every seven years. Islands labelled Brasil or similar wordforms appear sporadically on late-medieval maps of the Atlantic coast, usually much smaller and further east than the real place.

The Princess Alexandra of Vascony (sister of Ambrose the Ascendant) was the first Vetomundine royal to set foot in the Novomund, landing in Brasil in 1477.

By the mid-20th century Brasil had been fully incorporated as a region of Ambrosia, retaining only a few vestiges of local political independence. In 1950 the Brasil town of Paratzon formed one end of a new Atlantic link between Ambrosia and Morrack.

Places
Paratzon, a coastal town in eastern Brasil which is home to an observatory
People
Fabian MostΟ†f (fl. c19), astronomer at the Paratzon observatory and grandson of mathematician Raymond al Mustafa
CristΟ≤f Unquenjica (fl. 1971), author of popular history text Going to Brasil, an account of the first few Vascon voyages to Mendeva

##

54.1: Halloumi Hash

translated recipe from 1880 mealbook Pasti Mezziterreni (Middlesea Meals), written by world-traveller Josforo Sant'Angelo.

Primer dragm ougl raccaldar n'un frigsoir laΟΑ e tatover frigsar sur foy preuΟß un cart hour, mout toglaΟΑ (fin a y mornΟß se crostn eΟΑ y carn se molfin).
First heat some oil in a broad pan and fry the tattover over gentle fire for a quarter-hour, often stirred (until the pieces crispen and the flesh becomes soft).

Bulb de ceul eΟΑ y pevr jarras affigr e ci cocinar longhessem naΟΑusc.
Add in the stalk-onion bulb and the jarraspepper and cook a little longer.

Mistur souseΟ·r, ougl ancour raccalder ny frigsoir, eΟΑ y kes frigsar dessur jusc aure.
Subside the mix and heat more oil in the pan, on which fry the cheese until golden.

Tatover e kes condensar eΟΑ envouvr cos total tandic aromar con sal e pievr sec ne calscon quantaΟΑ casc quemant.
Bring together the tattover and cheese and fully involve while including salt and drypepper in whichever amount suits.

Farrago porΟßonnar ne bol covaucliΟΑ e ci retenir jos y foy, soulor toΟΑ ou coroccar sur y frigsoir savoraΟΑ e ci frigsar volonter.
Divide the farrago by covered bowls and hold near the fire, then crack the eggs into the seasoned pan and fry as desired.

Cos final casc ou mettr dessur dy desc eΟΑ y ligt dy ceul fair scadrir atras.
Finally place the eggs atop each dish and scatter with the rest of the stalk-onion.

54.2: souseΟ·r "set aside"

souseΟ·r /Υ¨su.ziΥàj…Σr/ [Υ¨su.z…ΣΥàj…ΣΥê]
- set aside, put away, to place something out of the way so that it can be used later;
- ground, run aground, to steer into too-shallow waters and get stuck there;
- settle, resolve, to deal conclusively with a problem;
- outclass, wipe the floor with, to surpass in a contest decisively

Etymology: from Middle Boral, a borrowing from Latin subsΡΪd≈ç "I crouch, subside" modelled on other verbs in -seΟ·r like asseΟ·r "dwell" and posseΟ·r "own". First attested in the latter two senses in the seventeenth century, in early use seen particularly in the context of diplomatic disputes.

L'agent dy Majon Roug souseyeu l'ig scard commoΟΑer.
/laΥà £…¦nt di maΥà £…în ruj Υ¨su.ziΥàjaw laj xard Υ¨k…î.moΥàΟΑ…¦r/

def-agent of-def house red settle-pst def-s.dt shard rebel
The Red House agent dealt with that resistance group.

54.3: hoissar "whistle"

hoissar /h…îjΥàsar/ [h äjΥàs…ëΥê]
- whistle, to make a sound by forcing air through one's mouth;
- blow, to make music with a wind instrument or noise with a whistling device;
- disobey, flout, to express contempt for rules or laws by word or action;
- skive, skip, shirk, to renouce one's responsibilities especially with regards to school or work

Etymology: from Middle Boral hoisΟßar "whistle, flout", probably of onomatopoeic origin. Otherwise, perhaps a borrowing from Old English hwistlian "to whistle". Its subsequent semantic development is unclear but mirrored in e.g. Dutch fluiten "to play the flute, to scorn, to scoff".

Hour alcot es tu y toriot hoissant?

time how.much be 2s def torriot blow-p.prs
How long have you been playing the torriot?

54.4: trencaΟß "sharp"

trencaΟß /tr…¦nΥàkats/ [t Ä…Σ≈΄Υàkas]
- sharp, having an edge which can cut very easily;
- freezing, (of weather) bitterly cold;
- wry, sarcastic, ironic, humorous in a particularly self-aware or deprecating way

Etymology: adjectival derivation from trencar "cut, slice", of uncertain origin. Perhaps from Vulgar Latin *trinic≈ç "I cut in three"; otherwise likely an alteration of Latin trunc≈ç "I maim, dismember" under Kelt influence.

Caye trencaΟß yon jo venoy primer n'Ausbagn.
/k…¦Υàje tr…¦nΥàkats j…în  £o veΥàn…îj priΥàm…¦r nozΥàb…¦jn/

fall-ipf sharp where 1s come-pst first in-Axbane
It was freezing when I first came to Axbane.

##

54.5: Medieval Mathematics

Nadim of Trapoles (fl. c12) was a mathematician at the Observatory in Trapoles, which at the time was a major Middlesea port for the Single Caliphate during the Second Tetrarchy. He is remembered for formulating the Trapolitan identity

[image]

which allows one to multiply two quantities using only addition, subtraction and enumerations of arrhumb [cosine tables].

---

Jambor of Hungary (fl. c12) was an early king of Hungary following the state's succession from the principality the century before. He oversaw significant expansion of Awrish influence, entering into personal union with eastern principalities such as Halich and Vallack, and becoming an ally of the Malchassian Romans to the south.

The great Library of Sobjan was built during his reign, an enduring gravatory of contemporary works. These included mathematical texts from the Single Caliphate which were at that time being translated into Latin in great quantities.

---

Saint Marinus (Italian: San Marino) is a historical city in the Italian state of Lombardy. It is recognised for the renowned Autonome University, which having been founded in the eleventh century is one of the oldest still-running educational establishments in the world, and especially for its Sovereign Library on the slopes of Titan.

Scholars of the Autonome University were among the first to translate many mathematical texts from Arabic into Latin; for instance, they compiled a liber numerorum in 1216 N which (surviving in a copy at the Library of Sobjan) contains the earliest-known enumeration of arrumb values in Europe.
People

    Jovanna Ariperto (fl. 1998), deixist researcher at the Autonome University of Saint Marinus
    Dijaca Rofotto (fl. 1997), historian of medieval religion for the Sovereign Library
    Lluprando da Treviso (fl. 1491), engineer at the Sovereign Library remembered for his work on the augrim of mozate forms

##

55.1: Marian Siegert

excerpt in translation from the collected correspondence of Marian Siegert ~ Miller (1725βÄ™56).

Albert me suggereu por te scaur primer, e lon nien for hugt ay jo l'oc fait; ne bon mont voun ty bel nau caressem m'amusar n'encost com ne voΟß, ma toΟΑ cas final jo no poΟΑ kiglar pieΟß appariscenΟß sentir de vous.
Albert suggested that I write to you first, and I was only too happy to do so; ideally your witticisms shall be as entertaining on paper as in person, and in any case I cannot help but feel some kinship with you. 

Nos som y ci dou tog pluottant jait deur y niaΟß a PaΟΑr por deΟ·r gilaΟßar soul.
We are the two fledgling sparrows cast out from Father's nest to fend for ourselves.

Jo so certan y vertaΟΑ suraΟΑoint; lon fregn pos y Rey un sunt calscon de puvon fornir con vitagl
I am of course exaggerating; doubtless the King can provide for any number of wards 

(ΟΑa cos indignant alcot sceint acceptaΟΑ; ty ci crevoscr con PaΟΑr no stan parcanΟß tan retenuΟΑ com tu speraus!
(no matter how grudgingly accepted; your evenings with Father were perhaps not as private as you hoped! 

Veleu nos sengr digr casc gent dessou connoscn l'ig InsoutiltaΟΑ dy PrinΟß M ou?), ma ΟΑa meΟΑes jo no poΟΑ larc jamir.
Shall we just say that everyone downstairs is aware of Prince M's Indiscretion?), but neither can I complain overmuch.

55.3: pluottar "fledge"

pluottar /Υ¨pli.…îΥàtar/ [Υ¨pli.j äΥàt…ëΥê]
- fledge, (of a young bird) to grow feathers and thus be able to fly from the nest;
- metamorphose, (of an insect) to mature from a larval stage into a mature adult insect;
- mature, grow up, come of age, to age from a child to an adult (or metaphorically to do so in terms of emotional maturity);
- to gain experience in a given field, to attain mastery of some skill

Etymology: originally only in the first sense, a verbal derivative from Old Boral pluot, pluvot "down feathers, (later) beard stubble". This is a diminutive (possibly via a Kelt form) of plum "feather", from the synonymous pl≈Ϊma in Latin.

We have enough supplies for a month.

##

55.5: Portingale

Portingale is a polity in the western part of the Spanish peninsula, bordering Vascony and Andalus, both of which have in the past conquered parts of its territory.

History
Portingale had been established as an independent Christian kingdom in the eleventh century, having previously been a subordinate county in Leon.

Much of northern Portingale was conquered by Vascony during the Vascon Ascendancy under Ambrose III, as far south as Braga and (nominally) CalΟ®s. The wealth that came to Vascony in the decades after the Novomundine Landfall of 1471 N accrued in particular to the coastal towns of conquered Portingale and to Leon.

The rump Portingale in the south (which had retained some meagre autonomy under Vascon stewardship) fell to Morrack in 1457 N as part of the expansionist campaigns of King Munir al-Hamdawi.

With its population concentrated along the coast, Portingale was ravaged early and mercilessly by the Second Great Dying in the years following the plague's arrival in Europe in 1519 N, with one fifth of the population dying within the decade.

After a series of military defeats during the Romantic Wars of the mid-seventeenth century, Vascony was forced to cede its Portingale territory (its economic heartland) to Andalus.

Portingale at first attempted to remain neutral during the Democratic Wars of the eighteenth century, and as such received a great number of war refugees in this period. However, the All-Saints' earthquake of 1755 N severely weakened Portingale's position; as such its warring neighbours invaded Portingale soon after.

The Drengot Collusion eventually grew to count Portingale within its orbit during the first decades of the twentieth century (and perhaps held some responsibility for the Portingale Anarchy of the 1920s and 30s), although the polity was never a full member of the alliance-federacy. This led to diplomatic tensions with Vascony, located directly between the Collusion and Portingale.

For a time, Drengot astraphor supplied (via undersea powerline) much of the baric needs of Portingale, thus relying on Vascon goodwill not to interfere. In response to this precarity, in the 1950s and 60s Portingale became a pioneer of threshold force, building enough mills that by 1970 it was exporting astraphor as far afield as Lombardy.

Places
Braga, a town renowned for a continent-leading lineball team and their 82,000-seat stadium
Corunne, Portingale's northernmost city and home to the Roxa Comba, a weekly paper affiliated loosely with the Peujon Rous media guild
People
Eustace II (fl. c12, crowned 1151), longest-ruling monarch of Portingale
Ezio Carvallo (fl. 1860), author credited with the invention of the genre of masquira romance
Marcio Dourao (fl. 1930), author of lovetale _Galatheo_ which was banned in both Vascony and Morrack
Victor Fidalgo (fl. 1950), collocker for the Roxa Comba
Maya Tavares (fl. 1950), political theorist for the de Sarumptice Diplomatic Edifice

##

56.1: The Romantic Period

excerpt in translation from Lugnt Ros Sur Ont Lugnt (Rosy Dawn on Glittering Waves), written by amateur historian Descard Ausogn in 1929.

L'eΟß gran accommission corrogationnal descrein y liesc romannesc, con tan l'eΟß dezein nolen l'ig stikettanΟß accarr pre un siecr may.
These great infrastrusture projects characterise the Romantic period, although these decades would not gain that appellation for another century.

L'oc nom es primer benoΟßaΟΑ posc y Rix Democratic, candon y mitaΟΑ nuverrem ny siecr settoΟß er stimaΟΑ, lon raΟßonnabiltaΟΑ, stant un final d'asc aure cas reloΟΑeΟß e civilisation.
The name is first used after the Democratic Wars, when the latter half of the seventeenth century was seen, nonsensically, as the last golden age of refinement and civilisation.

Defagl es comparaΟßon noc fair entrell'eΟß stat insoler e querellant n'Europ ny dezein 1660 e, com bustr, y ci gran veyaΟß geoscopic de mac Kellot eΟΑ al-Casmi ny siecr derran, ben l'envou Novomondin repleniΟΑ ny sevent.
It is hard not to contrast these insular, squabbling European states of the 1660s with, say, the great geoscopic voyages of mac Kellot and al-Cazmi of the previous century, or the renewed Novomundine interest of the next.

Nentamen, nell'oc asc es ig poum nos y cart modern e suetessem vistar enΟßant a smargr tras Europ.
Nonetheless, it is in this time that we can see the familiar modern map begin to emerge across Europe.

56.2: corrogar "build infrastructure"

corrogar /Υ¨ko.roΥàgar/ [ko. Ä äΥàg…ëΥê]
- build infrastructure, develop, to undertake public development by providing somewhere with (say) transport links and utilities like water and electricity

Etymology: Backformation from noun corrogation "infrastructure, public works", from synonymous corrogΡ¹ti≈ç in Scholastic Latin. This comes from Medieval Latin corrogΡ¹ta "corvΟ©e, collective labour provided by demand", itself probably formed by ellipsis of a Late Latin phrase opera corrogΡ¹ta "invited work", from corrog≈ç "I bring together by request, I invite".

Nos corrogau alcheΟΑr l'oc treborth.
/n…îz Υ¨ko.roΥàgo alΥàk…¦.ΟΑrΧ© l…îk treΥàb…îrΈΗ/

56.3: reloΟΑeΟß "finery"

reloΟΑeΟß /Υ¨re.loΥàΟΑ…¦ts/ [Υ¨ Äe.l äΥàΟΑ…¦s]
- splendour, grandeur, magnificence of appearance;
- refinement, elegance, gentility, sophistication, an esteemed manner associated with those of status;
- finery, regalia, bling, decoration or ornamentation especially of clothing

Etymology: noun derivative of reloΟΑ "fine, fancy, refined", which descends uncomplicatedly via Old Boral from Vulgar Latin relautus "elegant, splendid, fashionable". This is a variant of synonymous lautus, which is an adjectival derivative of lav≈ç "I wash, clean".

56.4: liesc "period"

liesc /lj…¦x/ [lj…¦Οß]
- term, period, era, duration, an amount of time considered as a demarcated unit;
- terminal, end, limit, endpoint, the latest or furthermost point of something;
- deadline, cutoff, the last moment in time when something is possible;

##

56.5: Vascony

Vascony (Vascon: Vasconna), or more fully the Vascon Kingdom of Navarre and Aquitain (Vascon: Lo Reiame VascΟ≤n a Navarr'i Aquitanna, Vask: Navarron t'Aquitainon Vasca Herria) is a polity of western Europe occupying parts of southern Gaul and northern Spain.

History
Vascony was formed from the political union of Aquitain and Navarre, later expanding to vassalise Leon in the twelfth century.

The fifteenth century saw the Vascon Ascendancy, which began by most accounts in the reign of Ambrose II and reached a peak with his grandson Ambrose III. At the start of this period Vascony was at its territorial maximum: it had conquered northern Portingale and subjugated the rest as a nominally-independent client, as well as much of southern Burgundy (which is to say, enough of Provence to share a border with the Italian states of Jeans and Lombardy).

In 1458 Ambrose III established by royal decree the New Navarre Enterprise, whose primary aim was to solidify Vascon supremacy in the trade of North Atlantic fish through the Middlesea. British dominion over the Atlantic had long frustrated Vask fishing families, members of which still predominated in the aristocracy. It was ships of this guild that in 1471 sighted Brasil and brought news of the Novomund to Europe.

The decades following the Novomundine Landfall brought new prosperity to Vascony, and in particular to Portingale. At the same time, the polity suffered militarily: the Kingdom of Burgundy retook Provence in 1494 (sparking the 1497βÄ™99 War of Provincial Independence), and a decade later the remainder of Vascony's Middlesea coast (primarily the region of Tolose) was taken by Barcelon.

Being a maritime power, Vascony had explored parts of the African coast (as far south as Sant Adrian [Bioko]) during the fifteenth century. However, for much the same reason Vascony lost over a fifth of its population to the Second Great Dying in the years after the plague's arrival in Europe in 1519. Despite this, thanks to Novomundine profits in furs and other goods, the polity actually saw in increase in population from 1500 to 1550 (excluding Portingale) due to immigration.

The Vascon Ascendancy is generally considered to have ended with the War of the Two Queens near the end of the sixteenth century, which also saw the formal end of feudalism within the polity.

The parts of Mendeva first explored by (and whose trade was monopolised by) VasconyβÄîto wit, the island of Brasil and the lands westward thereofβÄîwas named Ambrosia in honour of Ambrose III.

During the Romantic Wars of the mid-seventeenth century, Vascony fought primarily with Morrack to the south. The polity was forced to cede Portingale, its economic heart, to Andalus (then controlled by Morrack) after a series of military defeats.

In an example of early international cooperation, a canal system connecting Bordew to Audaz in Barcelon was completed in 1679.

Vascony was at the vanguard of the Modest Arrangement, a social movement in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries characterised by more stringent enforcement of morality codes, especially with respect to familial powers. Famously, author AntΟ≤in Herrel (latterly Tony Smith) was forced to flee Vascony to Kent after attempting to publish his blasphemous work 'Earthly Scene' (Vista Terrena).

At first, Vascony managed not to become embroiled in the Second German War of the 1820s. Once its interests in Mendeva and the East were threatened, however, then-king Hernand IV had no choice but to declare against the Northern Concert and in favour of the Convoy.

Into the twentieth century, Vascony maintained strong ties with its neighbour Provence by virtue of a shared enmity towards the Drengot Collusion to their north.

People
Monarchs
Ranolf VI, (crowned 1170), during whose reign Leon became a vassal of the kingdom
Ambrose III "The Ascendant" (17 March 1435βÄ™28 February 1518), who presided over the Vascon Ascendancy
Hernand IV (fl. 1820), who reigned during the height of the Modest Arrangement and the Second German War
Other nobles
Princess Alexandra of Vascony (b. ca. 1430), first European royal to set foot in the Nvomund
Prince Mark of Tolose (d. 1453), who predeceased his son Ambrose III
Princess Natalia of Vascony (b. ca. 1440), younger sister of Ambrose III
Prince Victor of Astorga (b. ca. 1440), younger brother of Ambrose III
Writers
David BensaiΧ¹d (fl. 1751), author of the influential political treatise "The Three Cities"
AntΟ≤in Herrel (fl. 1820), who was forced to flee to Kent after his work was banned in Vascony
GonΟßala JaubΟ®t (fl. 1902), who later in life moved to Valencia
GalarΟßa fi MolhomΟ© (fl. 2014), lovetale writer turned popular historian
GarcΟ≠a Ramires (fl. 1920), who translated Bellamy's "A Door, Once Opened" into Leon

57.1: Bad Soap

    excerpt in translation from the 18th of July 1971 of Borlish magazine Niacer, which focuses on domestic topics.

Set recoglt de savon e d'ougl landant son oy eliminaΟΑ deur y misol a varous posc tesmogn de conement ant orteyanΟß gra se caut par benoΟß den.
Seven soap and washing-oil products have been removed from shop shelves after customers have reported severe rashes after use.

L'eΟß recoglt (troveΟΑ enonΟß total n'affis) son abrangent a bar venΟßaΟΑ dy colluy connuΟΑ Pax e Candeller.
The products (for a full list see insert) include bars from the well-known cleaningware companies Pax and Chandler.

NarraΟΑ svattour suggern y blasmabr sey un lot impur a copros lauric de natron, un matier fameyant addaΟΑ cos respanent n'ougl e bar a savon.
Early reports suggest that the culprit may be an impure batch of natre's laurick cuprose, a foaming substance widely added to soap oils and bars.

Gimmolt vars l'origin dy contamination furtivir n'aun solution yent, ma y defait de bustr comportant referiΟΑ des y ligt dy marΟß d'enascment de Libya (dont Istr Boral innasc y majortaΟΑ dell'eΟß ivorc alchemic por detaxion) appen vars ig y spin vel y molin poliscent a Haldanvion hamtar.
Efforts to trace the source of the contamination are as yet inconclusive, though the lack of any similar cases reported in the rest of Libya (from where Borland imports the majority of these alchemical products for detaxion)'s export market suggests that the issue may lie with the refining mills at Haldanvion.

57.3: got ag mar fondr "tilt at windmills"

got ag mar fondr /g…ît …¦j mar Υàf…în.drΧ©/ [g…ît …¦j Υàm…ëΥê Υàf…în.d…ê]
- tilt at windmills, go on a wild-goose chase, to persist in attempting something with no chance of success;
- waste time, faff around, to use up some period in doing nothing productive;
- (euphemistic or jocular) masturbate, jerk off, to pleasure oneself

Etymology: idiom attested in various forms since the sixteenth century, and in the modern form since the eighteenth, literally "to spill a drop of the sea". Likely at first a reference to events in a burger play of the period. Extension to the last sense is attested only from 1968 but may be much older.

[ ùo f…în g…ît …¦j m…ëΥê ke du miz]

I've got nothing done for two months.

##

57.5: Damvath

Damvath is the capital city of Borland, located on the River Dam some leagues inland from the coast.

Toponymy
The name Damvath derives from the Norrish name DamvaΟΑ "ford on the Dam". The river's name is pre-Roman and is likely cognate with the Tames in Albion and the Tanga in Portingale.

History
Damvath was established first as the capital of Dane-ruled Borland after the exile of King Jothey to Sothbar in the last years of the ninth century.

In the late fifteenth century, Damvath became the most-trafficked port city in Europe outside the Middlesea. This was no doubt helped by the city's stability in comparison to the cities of contemporary Albion, embroiled in the bloody Wars of Fealty.

14th Sep: Arrived at Brest. Storm inland delayed delivery of beef so departed several hours behind schedule.
18th Sep: Did not make port at Bournemouth. Cannonfire and conflagration even in the harbour. Will resupply instead at Honeflower.
βÄî logbook 1492 for the myton βÄ€San TomΟ©βÄù, en route from CalΟ®s to Damvath.

The first Borlish victim of the Second Great Dying was in the docks of Damvath in 1519.

During the Long Peace of the late eighteenth century, the location of the Borlish parliament was fixed following the completion of the Jacobin Estates on the (then) outskirts of Damvath. In these years the city saw increased immigration from nearby polities such as Denmark and Willemy, looking for greater economic prosperity, as well as the establishment of an early detective force with the so-called 'tuppence scouts'.

In the modern era Damvath is renowned for extensive Christide celebrations.

58.1: The First Drengot Empire

excerpt in translation from reference quire A Mapbook Compendium of Europe, from the Division of Rome until the German Secession (450βÄ™1250 N), published by the Tommarth Open School of History in 1997.

Ma lon digr alcun nell'Empir Constant noncupaΟΑ ayen tan autonomy com Cornou e Dorogn, eΟß ayent ny siecr amont y hagr vital contriboit por y concarnΟß norman de Cantuer.
But of course none were more independent in the so-called Constant Empire than Kernow and Devon, who a century earlier had been vital to the Norman conquest of Kent.

Cos vray, ne 1121 y Rey Vigo de Magn Dorogn vole Cambry gazovar lon approu ben soutien dell'Emperour Roger II.
Indeed, in 1121 King Vigo of Greater Devon would attack Wales without the support of Emperor Roger II.

Roger II passau y bempart de sy regn a Paris, ant ig sy fraΟΑr postir Matheu regna por gra de luy a Londr.
Roger II spent most of his reign in Paris, with his younger brother Matthew ruling in his name in London.

Je 1120, largeΟß des y tesoir dell'empir er ja spent a fin oc y tarm lonc auster contr Willemy defendr.
By 1120, much of the empire's treasury had been spent on defending the long eastern border with Willemy.

Cos particuler, y costroinΟß d'un novel Gran Castel a VesonΟßon fo compliΟΑ l'an amont, stant y pognt final n'un rig costojal y lign entr Flandr eΟΑ y Montagn Alpin afferjarant.
In particular, the construction of a new Great Castle at VesonΟßon had been completed the year before, the last point in a defensive line spanning from Flanders to the Alps.
