58.3: dehir "split"

dehir /deΥàh…Σr/ [d…ΣΥàh…ΣΥê]
- split, split open, dehisce, separate, to break apart (especially along a seam) and fall into two halves;
- gape, yawn, to open wide;
- burst, rupture, to come apart (often violently) due to internal pressure;
- fall apart, collapse, topple, to lose structural integrity and fall to pieces;
- (figuratively) fall apart, unravel, break down, go to pieces, to suddenly lose all hope for success (especially of plans)

Etymology: Middle Boral borrowing from Latin dehΡΪsc≈ç "I gape, yawn, split open", used primarily in botanical contexts as of blossoms or fruits. Ending in -ir modelled after other change-of-state verbs like finir, jo finisc "to finish, I finish". In figurative use it may have been facilitated by the pre-existing metaphor of the flower as a work or achievement.

Nos se ponau voud a sy vellisqment dehiscn.
/n…îz se poΥàno vud a si Υ¨v…¦.l…ΣskΥàm…¦nt deΥàh…ΣxnΧ©/
[n…îse p äΥàno vud a si Υ¨ve.l…ΣskΥàm…¦n d…ΣΥàh…Σ.Οß…ên]
1p rfl put-1p.imp mad at 3s.gen plot split-3p
We looked forward to his plans falling apart.

##

58.4: zi "yeah"

zi /zi/ [zi]
- yeah, sure, yep, mhm, informal form of oc or ja "yes" used to denote agreement or assent;
- innit, y'know, colloquial form of ou used as a sentence-final particle in polar questions

[used only in informal speech and only recently in writing]

Etymology: Contraction of standard es i […ΣΥàzi] "is it", part of a series of similar contractions of the copula with pronouns, like sco "am I", sti "are you (sg)" which despite their verb-subject inversion are also nonstandardly used in declarative clauses (sti'n connart "You're a dick").

Its use in polar questions seems to date back to the sixteenth century (though usually written out as es i until the twentieth), while using it to mean "yes" is much more recent and was initially restricted to correcting negative questions. In this it resembles French si "yes, in response to a negative question", and indeed on the basis of phonetic similarity direct French influence has often been supposed.

Zi, sco'n bal n'oc ort.
/zi | x…în bal n…îk …îrt/
[zi | x…în baw n äΥàg…îΥêt]
yeah | i'm=indef ball in-s.px rise
Yeah, things're looking up.

##

58.5: Fiellas Dimenja

Fiellas Dimenja (English: Sunday Sheets) is a weekly newspaper almost continuously published from Marsella since its incorporation in 1871. The paper's notional remit comprises entertainment and recreational news, although in practise it has regularly covered weightier subjects.

History
Fiellas Dimenja was established in 1871 by Corsican businessman Toscobardo Matutte in concert with the Guiraud Colloquium.

Fiellas was an early adopter of machinal broadcast direct to homes via their tachygraphs, first offering digests in this form in the 1920s.

The paper developed a significant international readership through the twentieth centuryβÄîby 1934 it was republished synchronously in Vascony and New Provence, and was even read in Collusion territory, despite the rising tensions of the post-Millstone Europe.

By 1950 it held the record for the longest uninterrupted weekly mass-market publication, without interruption by the 1937 Veldsvindung nor the Millstone War (although it briefly moved its printers to ManΟ≤sca during the worst of the bombings).

Fiellas' renown grew further after its significant role in the Furore of 1961, which (not undeservedly) earned the paper a reputation for investigative journalism alongside its ostensible purpose of delivering entertainment and recreational news.

Correspondents
Jacint Brunat (fl. 1950), reviewer of books and films
For the best experience I recommend you spend the afternoon drinking Empordan and singing as many songs of glory as you can think of. [The Ascendant (1934)]

This will be no classic: come only for the views (of beautiful forests, and a bevy of handsome actors, I concede) and two hours of mindless tragedy. [Zachariah (1950)]

59.1: Waning Feudalism

extract in translation from condensed study materials for the course Histoir Concorrent, s16: Mort, Vascel eΟΑ y Motion Celest, taught at the Vithor New School.

Interim, valibr es prudenΟß fair neΟΑan l'eΟΑegl travodaΟΑer starchiscent dy paΟ·sanry cas lour y segnour feudal mescomprendr com ΟΑa un planfianΟß abrangent dy ci doujug grader nell'Europ dy siecr seΟß.
However, one should be careful not to construe the strengthening negotiating position of the peasantry with respect to their landlords as a broad levelling of the hierarchies of sixteenth-century Europe. 

Ne loy plusour apportaurn autortaΟΑ central (cos usual monarch, ma all'idea remainn y republic latinβÄîparticuler ProvenΟßβÄîtan eΟΑ y ci patriarchat auster) veloΟß n'assaut y possenΟß degredient ag nobiltaΟΑ vecin por poΟΑir lourell'un meΟΑes condensar.
In many places central authorities (usually monarchs, although bear in mind the Latin republicsβÄîin particular ProvenceβÄîas well as the eastern patriarchates) took swift advantage of the local nobility's declining power in order to consolidate their own. 

Com bustr, argutaΟΑ es ig ne Rom l'oc proces menau colombant vars y Gran FragnΟß e donc vars l'accession dy Triunvirato (may la den ny Capparol 13: Art e Theory Revitalist).
For example, it has been argued that this process in Rome led directly to the Great Fracture and the accession of the Triunvirato (about which more in Section 13: Revitalist Art and Theory).

59.3: doujug grader "hierarchy"

- hierarchy, power structure, pecking order, ladder, an organisation of people or sometimes objects or concepts by rank

The noun doujug "cobweb; network, system" was originally a northern dialect term from the phrase doun jug /dund £…ôj/ "July's down feathers", while the adjective grader is a regular derivation from grad "rank, (noble) title, status", from Latin gradus "step, rank".

59.5: 19th-century Espionage and the Invention of Software

    collected notes on two entirely unrelated works of literature.

Men of Jet and Diamond (originally published in Welsh under the title Dynnon o VuhuΟΑ a Diamont) is an 1890 trevold written by Hasinic Welshman Cadogan Torrior, shortly after he graduated from the University of Nadacow [capital of Hasiny, located not far from the coast on the banks of the *Mississippi] as a scholar of diplomacy.

Published by Blackburn Editions, the work is a rather late member of the first wave of masquira romance. The book was modestly popular in eastern Mendeva [North America], but became much more widely known after being adapted by a New Provence guild into the wildly-succesful 1951 film 'Jet White' (New Provincial Blanc Sabaje).

---

The Parable of the Mute Bookbinder (originally published in Burgund as Fable dew Reillour Mut) is a philosophical treatise written in 1743 by noblewoman Rachel Desselut in response to Vallakis on the problem of the bounded self-duplication of living things (in brief: how can a finite being contain within itself the ability to create a full copy of itself?).

Desselut's work lays out the separation of Form and of Method, and how they allow for the unsculpted duplication of a bounded being. She argues that in order for living matter to duplicate, the Form must contain within itself an explicit Method describing in some readable language its construction. Thus she inferred the existence of a vital language (in her own writing she called it the Divine Word; we now know it as heredian acid [DNA]) present in the cases of the body.

60.1: Malice Aforethought

excerpt in translation from The Varied Benefits of Malice Aforethought (published originally in Sharrovian Borlish as MaleΟß PrefiguraΟΑ e Toll'AdvantaΟß Den), a 2001 trevold by first-time author FrougΟΑr OnΟßala.

Caye un crevoscr affreidant je Avril joun eΟΑ y ci residu final de staΟΑ ern gliΟΑant apart con un bris fresc de harvest.
It was a chilly evening in early April, the last remnants of summer finally drifting away on a crisp autumn breeze.

Cos exact y gendr a crevoscr volois Laum faΟ·r passar cas luy ne sy randy quemant tacquaveyan nell'autranΟß compliscent affignt an scaunΟß deut a edition je l'aubon ados,
Exactly the type of evening Laum would have preferred to spend in his cosy Tacquave apartment putting the finishing touches on an article due for edition the next morning,

ben parcanΟß jolleint an tarn beΟßon scencant con sy colleg por un je nascenΟß ben munganΟß jubilarβÄîben l'explanation doujugher calscon por lembic consumir.
or perhaps out at a bar getting drinks with his coworkers to celebrate a birthday or anniversaryβÄîor whichever flimsy excuse for alcohol consumption.

Aglon, l'er aziant n'un manoir a Kevig scaunt dy deshormejanΟß poll'indrec cariΟΑ d'un dynast quidam.
Instead, he was in a mansion in Kevig covering the launch of some dynast's pet charity.

L'oc gendr a narraΟΑ nel Laum cos usual scaur den, ma Jale Fylaver aye frith a coup se caut e pres luy kestar por y martel prendr.
Not the sort of story Laum usually covered, but Jale Fylaver had come down with flu and practically begged him to take the reins.

60.2: los "flagstone"

los /l…îz/ [l…îz]
- flag, flagstone, slab, paving stone, a flat rectangular piece of rock or stone used for paving or roofing;
- stepping stone, a stone that can be stepped on in crossing an obstacle like a stream or marsh;
- building block, component, a single part used to build a larger construction or system;
- (stair) landing, the flat area at the top of a flight of stairs;
- platform, dais, podium, a raised area on which to stand;
- pavement, sidewalk, the (often paved) areas at the side of a road where people can walk;
- (figuratively) foundation, basis, building block, stepping stone, a means of advancing from one's current position

Etymology: originally in the first sense and descending from Old Boral los "flag, slab", reflected by synonymous lausa in Medieval Latin (and later Vascon llosa "slate", among others). This is thought to be from a Kelt form *lausΡ¹ related to known lΡΪvus, whence for example Irish lie "stone, pillar".

60.3: reu "stream"

reu /raw/ [ Äaw]
- stream, brook, creek, beck, a small river;

- module, class, course, a taught series of lessons or lectures covering a particular topic in the context of a syllabus with many concurrent such courses;
- channel, source, feed, a particular source of media (whether radio, TV, internet, etc)

Etymology: from Old Boral riu "stream, small river", from the synonymous rΡΪvus in Latin. First attested in the scholastic sense from eighteenth-century student slang at the Vithor New School, and in the last sense first applied to tachygraph casting in the twentieth.

60.4: nis "effort"

nis /n…Σz/ [n…Σz]
- effort, exertion, the work involved in doing something;
- Ο©lan, vigour, ardour, zeal, spirit, energy, active strength of body or mind in its capacity for expending effort towards some goal;
- strain, force, pressure, power exerted to physically push against something;
- responsibility, fault, obligation, duty, something for which one is responsibility;
- care, attention, investment, concern, interest, the state of being focused on something

Etymology: from Old Boral nis, ne»ùs, ni»ùs et al. "effort, vigour", which descends uncomplicatedly from Latin nΡΪxus, nΡΪsus "strain, forward momentum". This is the participle of deponent verb nΡΪtor "I lean on, advance, strive, contend", which does not survive in any descendant.

##

60.5: Santrafew

Santrafew (Merch /s…ônΥàt…Ιa.f…ôw/; Provincial Sant Rafeu /sanΥàtra.vaw/) is the capital of New Provence. Located on the Mendevan coast on the banks of the Becouin River, it is one of the oldest European settlements on the continent's mainland.

History

Santrafew was founded in 1564 as a trading post for Provincial merchant guilds. It was named for the archangel Raphael in his guise as a patron of travellers. In its first century, pocket communities of people appeared in Santrafew, as the fleets of Provence employed mariners from across the Middlesea.

Early imports into Santrafew included horse stocks and metal tools, traded in exchange for furs and new foodstuffs.

In 1590 Derek Nassow, the heir apparent to the Duchy d'Avosche in Willemy, fled overseas to Santrafew for unclear reasons. This led to an incendiary scandal that rocked courts across Western Europe and especially that of King David of France.

The 1621 Becouin Treaty outlined the legal relationship of Santrafew to the Mendevan peoples of the region for some generations. Following the British Sack of Santrafew, in which the MastoisΟ®t in particular were alleged to have collaborated, the treaty was broken by Mayor Alexandre fi Marco's embargo and ten-league exile of the MastoisΟ®t in particular (and other Mendevan groups in practice).

Immigration into Santrafew spiked dramatically in the 1820s, in the aftermath of the Second German War. The newcomers arrived in greatest numbers from Barcelon and Lombardy, spurred on by a combination of wartime displacement and presumably some familiarity with Provincial language.

Santrafew had means sufficiently-fast commnication across the Atlantic Ocean by the 1930s that papers such as Fiellas Dimenja could be published simultaneously there and in Vascony.

People

Alexandre fi Marco (fl. late c17), mayor responsible for the exile of Mendevan peoples from the vicinity of Santrafew
Sauveir d'Orz (fl. 1620βÄ™26), town mayor remembered only for his impotence
Carol Zancoud (d. 1630), reverend who accrued paramount (but unofficial) political power over the town
NadΟ†lla fi Carol (fl. 1590), daughter of Carol Zancoud whose extensive diary has survived to the modern day and is a vital window into early life in the settlement

References

The Making of Mendeva, a notoriously-inaccurate account written by In mac Harlach in 1888
Physician, Heal Thyself: The First Century of Santrafew (originally in Borlish as Medic, Cur Tey : Y Preu Siecr a Santrafeu), focusing mostly on diary extracts from various settlers

Portan ΟΑa veleu nos l'annaΟΑ aval de Wolfstan II de NorΟΑumbr comprendr, nos deyeu sgart mettr a dou siecr amont, ag bon preu gazou viking sur y rimmot d'Albion e d'IrΟΑon.
But to understand the final years of Wolfstan II of Northumber, we must look two centuries earlier, to the very first Viking raids on the shores of Albion and Ireland.

Y racont d'eΟß gazou comportant sur bourg n'Istr BoralβÄîe sur monastir cos particulerβÄîstan quippe denuΟΑ notoir ubic prell'hour yon l'eΟß spanoirn vars y vest e Halgrive fo piglaΟΑ ne staΟΑ de 824.

Nentamen, y reyaum anglesc aun s'apprestaΟΑ a l'eΟß gazou (e cos final eΟß irrouΟßon) souvar par gra inequal jagerrrem.

CompareΟΑ y lot de Lincon marchesc con ig de Yorvic norΟΑumbran: y primer cou jamay, e certan denoy y bourg starchiΟΑ cos impilaΟΑessem ne Marclont tras y dezein plusour candon l'aye veraΟßter benoΟß com y tarm de regn dannesc.

61.4: avant sy rau "shamelessly"

avant sy rau /aΥàvant si ro/ […êΥàvan si Υà Äo]
- shamelessly, brazenly, flagrantly, with no regard for someone's comfort or propriety;
- in full view, openly, in public, exposed, easily visible to someone (especially of something you would expect to be concealed)

Etymology: from early Middle Boral au sy rau […ôu si r…ôu] "brazenly, in a way that provokes ire", literally "in front of the disdain (of someone)". The preposition au "in front of", from Latin apud "by, near, unto", had obsolesced and been supplanted by synonymous avant (from ab ante "from before") by the nineteenth century.

The noun rau "disdain, scorn, (esp. societal) disapproval" is also obsolete outside this idiom, and is furthermore of unclear etymology. If of Latin origin all of reus "guilty", rabidus "furious" and an unattested *r≈çdus "slander" (from r≈çd≈ç "I slander") have been suggested; if German then perhaps some cognate of Merch rough "unkind, rude". Attempts to connect the word to Welsh rhew "frost, disdain" are semantically tempting but otherwise unsubstantiable.

Synchronically the idiom is usually misinterpreted as containing the unrelated word rau "uncooked vegetables, salad" (from Kentish raw "raw"), as though avant my rau meant "in front of my salad".

    L'er mell'yam sleghant avant my rau!
    /l…¦r m…¦Υàljam sliΥàjant aΥàvant mi ro/
    [l…¦Υê m…ΣΥàljam zl…ΣΥàjant …êΥàvan mi Υà Äo]
    3s-cop.ipf 1s.gen=uncle slander-p.prs in.front.of 1s.gen βÄ†disdain
    He was shamelessly slandering my uncle!

##

61.5: Parachthony

Parachthon art refers to works depicting a world different from the real world, encompassing everything from merely divergent histories to entirely invented settings. The word parachthon was coined in the mid-nineteenth century and derives from the Ancient Greek prefix œÄΈ±œ¹Έ±- 'beside, contrary to'" (cognate with Latin prae- and English fore-) attached to the root œ΅ΈΗœéΈΫ 'ground, soil, earth, world' (cognate with Latin humus 'soil'.)

History

A seminal work of modern parachthon literature is Elsebeth Sneider's 1795 trevold 'Through a Black Mirror' (originally written in Saxon Dutch as Durgh'nen Shorzen Shouer), arguably the first true voidtale work. Its publication sparked the so-called 'voidtale craze' of grand voyages through the void visiting colourful, bustling xenic worldsβÄîin this respect this tradition recalls the popular imaginings of the Earth-bound geoscopic voyages of the Intertwining Century nearly three hundred years earlier.

The voidtale craze persisted until the 1820s, at which point its optimistic and exploratory character dropped rather rapidly out of fashion faced with the horrors of the Second German War. By then the fashion had spread as far afield as Madray and China, with the renowned landscapist Narayanan commissioned to paint vistas of the seven other planets then known.

The mid-nineteenth century was somewhat of a nazir for parachthon art. Romance moved on to masquira and herdtale's more mundane settings, while historical revivals populated the world's stages. With the maturation of genres in the twentieth century we begin to see purposeful melding of parachthon elements in stories of other sorts, like lovetales and ΟΠnigmata; this and the incorporation of related movements like Futurism brought parachthony back into popular art for good.

The progress of technology was often presaged by parachthon works, and so many items of vocabulary for modern contrivances have their origin there. As examples, the 'stafemesh' (now usually just the 'mesh') is lifted from the pages of Ashford Pasquier's 1968 trevold Starsail, and the use of 'slate' for a flat surface displaying information can be traced all the way back to Hadasse Docquerl's 1872 work 'Dealsome Onomatory of the New London' (originally published in Borlish as Un Onomatoir Partial dy Novel Londr).

References

New Skies, Old Stories by Simon d'Estiala, published by Prindor MarsillΟ®s, 1980

62.1: Lonxter Yatherpot

recipe taken in translation from the 1763 mealbook A Complete Lithing of the Towns and Cities of Markland, for the Edification of the Good Food Amateur, written by independent traveller Lord Carrow, Baron of Ebbasham.

Un pog dy bouΟΑr fondr n'un gran paΟΑel scaudr e l'agnel crostar par lot, emmenar an ardois, soulor oc refair a trey rennot d'agnel taglaΟΑ e trencaΟΑ, all'ugnlac eΟΑ ag morn (tandic l'eΟß dou derran retenir).
Melt a little of the butter in a large shallow dish and sear the lamb in batches, remove to a plate, then repeat with three trimmed and sliced lamb kidneys, the onions and the carrots (these last two remaining).

Ci yettar con farin dessur, laiscar dou minut, soulor y savoranΟß e boglon affigr tout parmettant de boglir. L'agnel, rennot e fougl a laurer lor y paΟΑel emmenar deur y foy.
Dust with flour, leave for two minutes, then add the seasoning and broth, and allow to boil. Pour in the lamb, kidneys and lorrer and remove from the fire.

Y tatover despleyar ne trenc sur y carn e soulor eΟß voncirar con bouΟΑr calt. EΟß covauclir e cogr un hour e demy sur foy preuΟß.
Arrange the tartover by slices over the meat and then glaze with hot butter. Place on a lid and cook for an hour and half on a low fire.

Candon son y tatover rent fort broun, tout emmenar deur y foy eΟΑ acqueldar pront.
Once the tartoffer are well-browned, remove from the fire and serve immediately.

62.5: The Mysarnos Codex

The Mysarnos Codex is an illustrated manuscript written entirely in an unfamiliar script which eluded decipherment for centuries.

Context
The Codex was composed in the sixteenth century and first stored by somebody (presumably a monk) at the Library of Saint George the Martyr, one of the largest buildings in the sprawling church complex located north of the city of Mysarnos.

Mysarnos was at the time the capital of Mystra, a mordether vassal of the Roman Empire. Located at the western end of the Athenian Way, Mysarnos flourished as a hub for the Revitalist movement during the sixteenth century, receiving half of all Greece's imports and swelling to a population of nearly thirty thousand.

Authorship
The author of the Mysarnos Codex has never been conclusively identified, although several candidates have been nominated. Despite extant and complete membership records for the area's monasteries, no mention is made of the production of the Codex in contemporary chronicles.

Content
The Codex is thought to have originally comprised over two hundred vellum illustrated folios (several are lost but gaps can be deduced from various tallies). The illustrations include both mundane scenes such as poppy fields as well as fantastical vistas like armoured men on dragonback.

The text consists of a set of approximately 180 different glyphs (there is disagreement on whether some pairs of glyphs are mere variants or indeed separate), arranged into words and paragraphs.

Decipherment
For centuries scholars presumed the Mysarnos glyphs to have been entirely invented by their author. The Good Game period of the late nineteenth century saw this narrative collapse, with artefacts first from old Pylos (by then a neighbourhood of Mysarnos) and then from Crete surfacing bearing the same glyphs.

It became clear that the author of the Mysarnos Codex had had access to a store of writing in this script, and perhaps had even known how to read it (many words within the Codex matched the truly ancient examples). The revelation that the Mysarnos glyphs were being used to write a form of Greek only came in 1939, with the unearthing of the lost First Folio at the venerable Library of Sobjan. This text began with a fictionalised biography of the author written both in contemporary Greek and in the codex's script, which allowed for direct comparison and thence, with work, a full decipherment.

References
The Mysarnos Problem: the Enigma of an Inconvenient Codex, a popular narrative history written by classicist Theodora RiphΟΠna in 1943

##

63.5: Devon

Devon (Welsh Devneint, locally Dewna; Borlish Dorogn) refers geographically to the southwestern peninsula of the land of Albion, and politically to one of the most longstanding tallaths of Britain.

History

Devon is named for the Dumn≈çniΡΪ (the Latin name reflecting Classical Kelt DubnonΡΪ or DubnantΡΪ, meaning "(people) of the deep valley"), a Kelt people who inhabited the region in Roman times.

Devon was conquered piecemeal by the Saxons, with the defeat at the |Battle of Gaulford in 656 putting the area under the sole authority of the Kingdom of Wessex (although the westernmost region is thought never to have fully submitted to its rule).

In the eighth century, King Vigo the Magnificent of the Britons spearheaded a reconquest of the peninsula with the support of David I of Wynneth, obtained via his marriage to King David's daughter Guinnumar. Over two decades of intermittent warfare were ended with the victory at Petherton, and Vigo was crowned the first king of Greater Devon (rex MagnΟΠ DumnoniΟΠ), uniting Devon with the region later known as Kernow in Gaul.

The Brethin religious order was founded in eighth-century Tremonnow, at that time in Devon (now in Glousing).

Devon remained in political union with Kernow through the Kernovan Conjuring of 1028 and the concomitant formation of the First Drengot Empire, which claimed control over much of Albion and most of Gaul. Greater Devon became an ambitious and independent-minded vassal of the Empire, with the House of Kernow always closely aligned with the imperial Drengot family.

The collapse of the First Drengot Empire caused political upheaval in Greater Devon. The Realignment of 1225 saw Devon become de facto independent from Kernow, now ruled by a cadet branch of the royal family.

However, Devon's independence would not last long; Ivan of Cartaw conquered Devon as part of the various Welsh territorial gains of the thirteenth century. Soon thereafter David IV of Wales declared the Tallath of Devon (and awarded rule thereof to Ivan's son Marduth).

People

Monarchs of Greater Devon

Vigo the Magnificent (Vigo RagΟ≥rwyh, d. 761), who led the reconquest of Devon from Kernow
Cadern the Doubter (crowned 761), son of Vigo the Magnificent
Alan III (crowned 883), who lost territory in Kernow to the Franks
Vigo IV (fl. 940), last monarch to be crowned in the original cathedral of Nauned
Vigo VII (fl. 1120), who attempted without success to conquer the southeastern principalities of Wales
Alan VI (fl. 1225), who orchestrated the Realignment of Devon

Nobility

Golleck/Gorvan of Kernow (fl. c9), granted dominion over Devon by his uncle Alan III
Iseld of Devon (fl. 750), daughter of Vigo the Magnificent and first patron of the Brethin

Others

Damian the Wise (fl. c12), the first Pope to be elected from among the Brethin religious order

##

64.1: Electromagnetic Radiation

excerpt in translation from 1967 work Handbook of Natural Theory, written by Jason Ridley and others.

Ustol es ig nos dau grad a ≈™culux par sy mesur, dec nos lour eu envou con de tan faΟßon divers.
We customarily grade ≈™culux by their span, since we interact with them in many different ways.

Lumner ant y mesur cortessem es scadriscent, ant nis suffignt por atom toccar e cossy lou fornir de defait a plentaΟΑ; dighau l'atom dien defaint.
Light with the shortest spans is scattering, having enough energy to affect atoms and give them a deficit of plenty; we say the atom becomes deficient.

ToΟΑ ≈™culux breyessem e penetrant y dou son scadriscent, e lonc ustol se poΟΑ certan lumner surblavesc jagerrem admettr.
All shortform and penetrating ≈™culux are scattering, and depending on convention some further surblavic light may be included.

Y mesur y longessem a lumner poΟΑent in experientia un fondamen atomic scadrir es tor 400 magr polΟß, ja  no stant lonc com lumner visibr calscon.
The longest span of light which can in theory scatter an atomic base is around 400 scant-inches, which is not yet as long as any visible light.

Scey hasart ne gran illumination des lumner a mesur tan cort, e feliΟß es aer avogl y majortaΟΑ a tal lux nastiΟΑ par y Sol (pu noc l'entertaΟΑ, com vil bruslanΟß tesmognar!). 
High exposure to light of such brief span is dangerous, and fortunately air blocks the majority of such luxes that the Sun emits (although not all, as a sunburn will attest!).

##

64.5: First Drengot Empire

The First Drengot Empire, or occasionally the Constant Empire, was the westernmost of the four empires comprising the Second Tetrarchy (also the Medieval Imperia). At its greatest extent it claimed control over much of Albion and most of Gaul. Its major vassals were the kingdoms of Northumber, Markland, Kent, Greater Devon, France and Burgundy, along with several lower-ranking demesnes.

The Empire is named for its ruling family House Drengot, formerly rulers of Normandy alone. The lesser-used name Constant Empire has been employed sporadically by historians or propagandists attempting to claim the prestige of the Classical-era District of Constantius, which occupied roughly the same geographical extent.

The structure of the Empire was quite decentralised; see for example the standing army kept by vassal Tolose for its perennial skirmishes with neighbouring Barcelon. The emperor usually held court in Paris (moved during the eleventh century from Roun)

History

The beginnings of the First Drengot Empire lie with the Kernovan Conjuring, through which the House of Drengot in Normandy invaded and conquered Kent with the support of Greater Devon's House of Kernow. As recompense for their support, the Drengots helped install the House of Kernow on the Devoner throne. This invasion was triggered by the death of the elderly patriarch Rodulf of Kent, who had ruled since childhood for nearly eight decades.

The Empire was most stable in the decades around the turning of the twelfth century, having reached its greatest territorial extent. However, by 1120 much of the treasury had been spent on defending the long eastern border with Willemy. In particular, the construction of a new Great Castle at VΟ©sonΟßon had been completed the year before, the last point in a defensive line spanning from Flanders to the Alps.

In 1170 the Kingdom of Burgundy ceased to be a vassal of the First Drengot Empire, due to the political turmoil of the Emergency at Dijon.

The First Drengot Empire formally ceased to exist in 1191 upon the death of Daniel IV; his demesne was divided between his immediate family and his allies.

Emperors

Nathaniel II (fl. 1067), who quashed a popular uprising in Flanders
Roger II (fl. 1121), under whose aegis Devon attempted to invade Wales without success
Daniel IV (d. 1191), last ruler of a united Drengot Empire

65.1: The Thousandfold Fateling

    extract in translation from 1949 trevold Den Tusynfold Spoatling (later published in Borlish under the title L'Ivan a Doum MurΟΑon "The Child with Ten Thousand Destinies"), written by established children's author Viola Toft.

Ny reyaum catrem, y gran castel sorgent com un casner deur y citaΟΑ ag mar jonc sta vert.

Y pieΟΑr dy cost heilander stan covart de mos pille, tandic y bris saler cola sur yarΟß boscer yeΟΑrot portant.
The stones of the land side were covered in thick moss, while the salt breeze streamed over trellises of ivy.

Y fenestr meΟΑes lughen con veΟΑr zajadau ag colourβÄîneΟΑan y clartaΟΑβÄîde smaraud.
Even the windows glittered with jackdaw glass the colourβÄîif not the clarityβÄîof emeralds.

A starn dy mur citaΟΑer naja cos lent y gamblas de sy paΟΑr lonc y canal argent sceint just con y rout leyaΟΑ, trencant a biasc cos artificial par y mersc paneller.

Vray, jusc oy ern il passaΟß faint de tal tardiscenΟß ig ΟΑa y costoscment ern porsief lour dant lor il voloisn ja certan star attegnt eΟΑ oncaΟΑ alcheΟΑr vars Tisholm.
In fact, they had been making such slow progress that if the authorities were pursuing them they would surely already have been caught and dragged back to Tisholm.

##

65.5: Second Roman Empire

The Second Roman Empire, seldom or historically referred to as the Empire of the Hellenes, the Empire in Byzantium, Malchassian Rome (specifically denoting the centuries of rule by the Malchassian dynasty) or the Eastern Roman Empire (which extends somewhat further back in time), was the political and cultural continuation of the (Classical) Roman Empire following the reconquest of the city of Rome from the Eastern Goths by Emperor Justinian I. The Empire persisted in some form for over a millennium, with its final collapse occurring in the seventeenth century.

History
Many Christian heresies had their origins in the Second Roman Empire, especially in the first millennium. These include all the so-called 'Grecian heresies', as well as Marmeronism. When individuals in the Empire who espoused such viewpoints acquired significant influence, the Empire had a propensity for exiling them (contrast the habit of bodily mutilation practised on political, as opposed to religious, dissidents) to far-flung monastic communities.

During the Second Tetrarchy, the Empire reached a territorial maximum (with the capture of Derban in 1026), controlling lands from Corsica in the west to the Hazaran Sea in the east. However, by this time the outlying regions enjoyed significant autonomy, especially where the independent power of the Roman Papacy loomed larger than the might of Constantinople.

Following the fall of the northern Lombards in Italy, the ruling family fled to the Empire and managed to obtain appreciable clout there; some members were appointed to administer the Empire's western themes, and the written accounts of others are history's strongest sources for certain events during the turmoil of the German Secession in the thirteenth century.

Having fallen into somewhat of a state of disrepair, the fourteenth century saw extensive reconstruction of the Hagia Sophia along with expansion of the complex, headed by Burgund architect NicolΟ† s'Avignon. (It is for this reason that s'Avignon is sometimes jocularly referred to as the "architect of the Empire's demise", given the tragic events that would take place within the Hagia Sophia three hundred years later.)

##

66.1: The "Metric" System

from Handbook of Natural Theory, by Jason Ridley and others, published 1967 by the Lincun Collegiate Editors.

Cas y mesur 'degm' noncupaΟΑ er tandem l'axiom de se sarvent d'un los soul par casc quantaΟΑ compromis, e retenuΟΑ ern pie e lau y dou ne benoΟß com mesur a tagl sengr.
The so-called 'dime' measures eventually compromised the principle of having a single base for each quantity, and retained both the foot and the league as primary measures of length.

Y pie er normalisaΟΑ cos quidam naΟΑusc stant y mojol entr dou mesur un picq pog inequal: y "pie romannesc" eΟΑ y "paum accenser" cathayan.
The foot was standardised somewhat arbitrarily as the midpoint of the very slightly discrepant "Roman foot" and the Cathayan "surveyor's span" [βâà3dm].

Y lau, stant jusc oy irruniscablessem meΟΑes tras Europ ag pie, ern fis a plaΟß d'un murΟΑon a pie;
The league, which had been even less consistent across Europe than the foot, was fixed at ten thousand feet [βâà3km];

y mesur cas ustol y "migl" vole cavir inclus parmy y mesur degm com cinq mil pie, ben un demy a lau.
the traditional measure of the "mile" would be folded into the dime measures as five thousand feet, or half a league.

L'oc contingent es autraΟΑ alcant soul dagormay, candon y pie (e cossy scient y lau) se cou un redefinition ne 1946 por ig y veloΟßtaΟΑ normal ≈™ tey cos exact deg gran lau par second.
This state of affairs has changed only once since, with the redefinition of the foot (and therefore the league) in 1946 setting the normal speed [the speed of light] ≈™ to precisely ten great leagues per second.

67.3: stovir "be necessary"

stovir /stoΥàv…Σr/ [st äΥàv…ΣΥê]
- (archaic or elegant) be necessary, be needed, be obligatory, to be logically or legally required;
- lack, be missing, to (potentially) suffer from not having;
- be wanted, be desirable, to be desired by someone

Etymology: from Old Boral istover "be necessary", a verbal derivation of istof "obligaton, necessity, need". This is a univerbation of Latin phrase est opus "there is need". The shift to less urgent wants is relatively recent, occuring over the course of the nineteenth century. Stovir still connotes stronger desire than the alternative construction desirar "to want" (colloquially 'sirar), which sees more use for polite requests.

/me stu f…îrt i lax laΥàtsar kant …Σz Υ¨no.diΥàse/

67.4: jougl "knock-out blow"

- knock-out blow, haymaker, sockdolager, a punch that renders one's opponent unconscious, insensible, or otherwise unable to continue to fight;
- rout, trouncing, decisive victory, an instance of winning a contest by a wide margin;
- clincher, determiner, the act or moment that is decisive in settling something

also jougl fair / £ujl fer/ knock out, KO, render unconcious; jougl cavir / £ujl kaΥàv…Σr/ be knocked out, fall unconscious

Etymology: from Middle Boral jougl "death blow, killing strike", deverbal from joglar "to slay, to decisively kill, esp. to slit the throat of". This descends via synonymous Old Boral io»ùlar from Latin iugul≈ç, verb derivative from iugulum "collarbone, throat; fig. a murder". Less-fatal use appears first in the context of prize-fighting.

##

67.5: Romantic Wars

The Romantic Wars were a loosely-circumscribed collection of wars, uprisings and other conflicts across Europe which took place through the middle of the seventeenth century. As a whole they are sometimes considered the last truly localised conflicts, since by the next century wars of similar scales would begin to involve the Novomund, and would feature alliances between the powers of Europe, Africa and Asia alike. The years 1640 to 1670 cover the 'central' conflicts of the Romantic Wars, although some more peripheral fights may spill earlier or later.

The Romantic period (to wit, the decades during with the Romantic Wars took place) were characterised by great corrogation projects. For example, the Bordew-Audaz canal was completed in 1679, connecting the Atlantic and the Middlesea without having to pass through Morrack-controlled seas.

Perennial adversaries Vascony and Morrack continued their protracted fight through the seventeenth century. Having been on a marked decline for a century, Vascony was forced to cede its economic heart in Portingale to Andalus, which at that time was controlled by Morrack.

Several polities and vassal states did not survive the Romantic Wars; for example, the Principality of Mystra in Greece, which had existed as a vassal of the Second Roman Empire since the eleventh century, was formally dissolved in the aftermath of the Romantic Wars.

Willemy was the focal point of the Blue Trans-Willem War (not to be confused with the White Trans-Willem War), which indirectly led to the dissolution of the Kingdom of Burgundy and the creation of the Duchy of Far Suebia in Bavarn.

Borland's involvement in the Romantic Wars was restricted to a failed invasion (culminating in the Hawkirch Surrender of 1632), along with unstable arrangements with neighbouring polities regarding military aid in their conquests.

Much has been made of the involvement of pirates (to a greater or lesser extend endorsed by political powers) in the Romantic Wars, although recent historiography tends to downplay how much impact these factions really had.

The Roman conflict in Armenia of the 1640s is contemporaneous with the Romantic Wars, but its inclusion within them is a matter of scholarly dispute. Opponents point out that this fight was merely the latest in a series of essentially-identical conflicts between the Second Roman Empire and Arab (and later Turkish) peoples over control of the Hazaran Sea.

##

68.1: Early Medieval Borland

excerpt in translation from The Story of Borland, published (originally in Boral as Y Stoir d'Istr Boral) for use in classrooms in 1982 by Cordin Editions.

Cay confluyenΟß dy ci pouvr germannesc ny souΟΑ e nell'ovest con y pouvr romannesc nell'aust e ny norΟΑ se produgnt for lent, interim, tras y plusour siecr sevent.
The mixing of the Germanic peoples of the south and west with the Romans of the east and north would proceed only slowly, however, for the next couple of centuries.

VassalaΟß ja galaΟΑ vexa y heilant, e conflict afont menan vars concert entr Boral e Sothbar ben entr Boral eΟΑ Anglont com issent dy contingent politic.
The question of fealty vexed the land, with conflicts leading to temporary alliances between Boral and Sothbar or Boral and Angland depending on the political climate.

Tandem, ny siecr novem joun y rey roman Stiglan dau sell'heir Marc ne mariΟΑaΟß ag dottr soul a Alfey d'Anglont, cossy lorry reyaum unfiant e Sothbar sforΟßant a vassalaΟß eunovir posc y Battagl a Fendal n'atorn 810 N.
Eventually, in the early ninth century Roman king Stiglan married his son Marc to the only daughter of Alfegh of Angland, unifying their kingdoms and forcing Southbar to pledge fealty after the circa 810 N Battle of Fendal.

Ma a veg meΟΑes yon y contingent treblaΟΑ n'Istr Boral er toΟΑ cas final sign mostrant vars stabiltaΟΑ, aΟΑoint er y frequenΟß eΟΑ y forΟß de gazou dannesc sur y bourg rimmer dell'istr.
But at the same time as the tripartite situation of Borland was finally showing signs of stabilisation, Danish raids on the island's coast towns were increasing in frequency and strength.

68.2: ne ΟΑougl "rushed"

ne ΟΑougl /ne ΟΑujl/ [n…ΣΥàΟΑ ä.j ä~n…ΣΥàΟΑw…Σw]
- rushed, hasty, slapdash, done in an overly quick way due to a lack of time;

68.3: teissir "weave"

teissir /tiΥàs…Σr/ [t…ΣΥàs…ΣΥê]
- knit, weave, spin, to form something by passing lengths or strands of material over or through themselves;
- plait, wreathe, braid, interweave, to weave together the strands or locks of;
- devise, plot, scheme, concoct, to come up with a plan or similar undertaking;
- put together, compose, connect, join up, to bring together disparate elements into some sort of network or chain;
- deduce, infer, to come to a conclusion;
- tell, narrate, to describe events in the form of a story

Etymology: originally in the first senses, from Old Boral teissir, tistre, te»ùsre (among many other forms) "to weave, work with thread". These all derive from either Latin texere "to weave" or a Late Latin texΡ™re exhibiting a change of conjugation. Use in the narrative sense is attested as far back as the Classical period; much of the metaphorical usage is likely extended from this first idiom.

68.4: jug "continually"

jug / £aj/ [ ùaj]
- continually, ever, still, remaining in a given state or continuing on a given course;
- immediately, instantly, now, starting at this exact moment;

Etymology: from Old Boral iui /d £yj/ "always, forever, without stopping", which descends directly from Late Latin i≈Ϊge "constantly, all the time". Replaced in the "always" sense by tojorn < toΟΑ jorn "all day" by the Middle Boral period. The expected modern spelling juy was over time altered to jug under the influence of the Latin form. In modern spoken usage the word is confined mostly to imperative constructions.

Ag port penoy sign scaunt 'AbseΟ·r Jug'.

[…¦j p…îΥêt p…ΣΥàn…îj sajn xont …êbΥàzj…ΣΥê  ùaj]

##

68.5: The German Secesssion

The German Secession was a schism in Christendom which took place in the first half of the thirteenth century. Often considered on of the most influential religious developments of the second millennium, it led to the permanent formalisation of the Ausburg See as independent from Rome with its separate papal line beginning with Paul I/XI.

The Secession generally refers to the period from Paul I/XI's Denunciations and declaration of the sedes sanctissima in Augsburg in 1225 to the capitulation of Rome in 1251, although religious strife due to the schism would wrack the polities of Germany and surrounding regions for centuries to come.

The 1210 declaration of a renewed crusade for Jerusalem by Pope Just, and the military catastrophes that occurred in the course of this push, were a major source of discontentment in Rome. Further, Europe was at the time in the throes of the First Great Dying, a plague that would be surpassed in severity only by the Second Great Dying of the early sixteenth century. (These events are related, in that the plague is thought to have arrived in Europe carried by returning crusaders).

The decades after the Pauline Denuncations saw each individual duchy (and in some cases each county!) declare either for Rome or for Augsburg, in an unstable patchwork of allegiances that would lead to the collapse of the German Empire in spectacular fashion.

The ongoing Baptism of the North (the project to fold the northeasternmost part of Europe into Christendom) was inflamed by the Secession. Along with a new, more totalist Roman Pope in Hermeneus II, it was feared that Augsburg might spread heresy faster than Rome could reach the North.

The events of the Secession have been a perennial subject of dramatisation, most famously (and perhaps most innacurately) in 1961 historical epic 'With the North Star to Guide Us'.

References
'A Mapbook Compendium of Europe, from the Division of Rome until the German Secession (450 - 1250 N)', published by the Tommarth Open School of History in 1997
'A Baptism of Fire: Christianity and the Graven' by Agata Naziunas, published 1988 by LeidΟ©iai GivatΟ©s
'Comparing the German Secession to the Great Fracture; how the Final Antipope emerged from a popular reaction to the Failed Crusade, and how to understand his successors' reactions to Rome in the sixteenth century', scholastic writing in the 1901 publication of theological journal 'Proclamatio Divior'

69.1: Gingerbread

abridged extract in translation from reference work Folk Customs and Holidays of the North: a Compendium, published in 1876 by Safford University Primers.

Magn comoscon Istr BoralβÄîtan e largeΟß allorsβÄîvel se contentar n'auteΟß declarar sull'idea de pan vastel aromaΟΑ douΟß, y method sengr (ja pos oc no star lon y form a jonnovar faΟ·ΟΑ par Istr Boral) vien tostessem meΟΑes all'innascment laΟΑ de zucar n'Europ.
Much as BorlandβÄîand many other placesβÄîmight like to claim ownership of the concept of sugar-spiced cakebread, the basic recipe (though perhaps not Borland's preferred buttefly shape) predates even the mass import of sugar into Europe.

GingembraΟΑ apparisc ne siecr doΟß tandic abatment continu ag Masrecq augta gingembr eΟΑ autr marΟß vars l'ovest, eΟß mout zucarraΟΑ par miel.
Inchbread [1] arrives in the twelfth century as ongoing warfare in the Masreck [2] brought inchroot [3] and other goods west, often sweetened with honey.

PostulaΟΑ cos usual es ig jonnovar son y symbol primer de NaΟΑal sur Istr Boral com un parsonfianΟß de metamorphos e de viΟΑ alcheΟΑr tandic y mont mort dell'yarn reflorisc vars y prinvir.
It is usually supposed that butterflies are the primary symbol of Christide on Borland as a representation of metamorphosis and new life as the dead world of winter grows again into spring.

Degnant ig l'oc intermain un fabr starc, eΟΑ ig a mesur dy siecr a y symbol dy jonnovar se caut adoptaΟΑ par commouΟΑ divers por l'oc bers meΟΑes complir, y tesmogn historic no's idone antal racont sengr.
While this makes for a good story, and while the symbol of the butterfly has over the centuries been adopted by various movements for this very purpose, the historical record is more ambiguous.

69.2: contentar "appease"

contentar /Υ¨k…în.t…¦nΥàtar/ [Υ¨k…în.t…ΣnΥàt…ëΥê]

69.5: Avosche

Avosche (Paris French /aΥàv…îΥê É/, Willem Dutch /Υàa.vusx/) is a political subregion of Willemy centred on the city of Axton (Grand-Aix /g ÄaΧÉΥàd…¦iks/, Aughen /Υà…î.…ΘnΧ©/), with a strong regional identity. At various points a county, a duchy and a march, it has seen both continual territorial instability (situated between French and Dutch powers) and major urban development into the modern period.

Etymology
The region Avosche is named for its first ruling house, which in turn takes its title from its founder Cuonrat Aihuosc (seen in Latin as Corradus Aevoscus). The name is of obscure origin; the first element presumably reflects Classical German ehu- "horse", while the second may be from suffix -iscas "-ish" (though the vowel is problematic).

Compare the English surname Davush (Merch /daΥê É/, Kentish /ΥàdaΥê.v…Σ É/), which derives from an illegitimate branch of House Avosche.

History
The County of Avosche emerges shortly after the split of the Kingdom of the Franks and the concomitant creation of Willemy. The founding house of Avosche was soon after superseded by marriage by the neighbouring House Nassow (which originated in Francony).

The county was elevated to a duchy in the early sixteenth century, with Roger d'Avosche becoming the first duke. At this time the region was ruled by France. Roger's daughter, AdaillΟ© Nassow, succeeded him and went on to rule for many decades until her death in 1608.

During her reign AdaillΟ© accrued significant influence over her liege David of France. This was punctured by the scandal of her grandson and heir apparent DΟ©ricque fleeing to New Provence in 1589.

After a century of stability as a vassal of France, the seventeenth century was particularly eventful for Avosche; it changed hands as the result of various wars and peacetime treaties on five separate occasions. Especially bloody was the Blue Trans-Willem War, which also dissolved the Kingdom of Burgundy.

References
LΟΓt os Danse: De Hartoin βÄ‰n Avosche (Let us Dance: The Duchess d'Avosche), biography published in 1919

70.1: Review: The Ascendant

excerpt in translation from a review of the 1934 film The Ascendant appearing in Marsellan newspaper Fiellas Dimenja.

Domaget se sarf dy sgart angost por y laΟΑ illuminar par casc taug ny forganΟß a sy film, e vray veim nos l'eΟß advantaΟß n'un tal eΟΑeglment ac dessur.
Domaget uses the narrow view to illuminate the broad in every aspect of his filmmaking, and we really see the benefits of such an approach here.

Οêa y belem ern grivlaΟΑ a se produgr ny palaΟß e castel a Huzatr e Cartau, defagl all'auditoir vel y comport vistar entr y glonscel cambresc n'Albion ag scon nuΟßal d'Alexandra a Vascony e sy veyaΟß famous.

Aglon poum nos y sensation a sculd parental sentir all'abat contr gam sgarant lonc y dou cainΟß secuΟΑ nell'oc caΟΑen filmer.

L'Ascenent vil lon fregn denir siemprtaΟΑ sull'icon stival, e l'oc merit fort l'ig sign a ΟΑoranΟß.

For the best experience I recommend you spend the afternoon drinking Empordan and singing as many songs of glory as you can think of.

70.5: Monks and Metropoleis

The Jacobin Fraternity (Borlish FraterntaΟΑ Jagovin), historically also known as the Order of Saint James, is a religious organisation based in the city of Axbane on the south coast of Borland.

The Fraternity were founded in tenth-century Flanders under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges. In the eleventh century they were implicated in a local popular uprising against Drengot Emperor Geoffry II and forced to flee over the sea to Borland. There they built the Peninsular Monastery which still overlooks Axbane.

Funds from the Fraternity contributed to the Jacobin Estates on the outskirts of Damvath, which on their completion in the late eighteenth century became the fixed location of Borland's parliament.

Today the organisation is known mostly for the guild they own, Jaibos Breweries, as well as for owning significant lands in the city (including the site of one of the ancient Roman bathhouses there).

---

The Riverine Cities were an alliance-federacy of small independent states along the Danaw River and its tributaries linked by treaty and trade in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries. Each state consisted primarily of a single urban area and the surrounding rural hinterland, though the enforcement of territorial borders was often rather nominal.

The political structure of the Riverine Cities grew out of the ashes of the Kingdom of the Danaw, which had collapsed in spectacular fashion after the Second German War of the 1820s.

The Cities were a hotbed for underground or subversive organisations, including the Deviance movement, and were host to several semi-public tovarick communities. This environment was likely fostered by the presence of significant religious minorities and the strong ties between the Church and the local urban authorities.

Members of the Riverine Cities included Belgrade, Boxa, Gollamo and Pojon.

##

71.1: Atomic Theory

excerpt in translation from Lux ΟÜterna: The First Century of Threshold Force, written in 2002 by Cawen ba Dirxen at the VaronΟΗw Higher Edifice.

Des posc y preu enombranΟß corporal d'an 1840 er venuΟΑ all'idea oc orn dar a corporal cos naΟΑeu lonc pondranΟß mena vars coglenΟß fatuous ΟΑa volir nis prestar a lorry natur alchemic
Since the first periodic tables in the 1840s it had been realised that naively ordering elements by atomic weight led to inelegant groupings when chemical properties were taken into account

(y celeberrem ig stove de raΟßon starc janthin inquillir parmy sy confraΟΑr detaxional com muria),
(most notably the strong motivation to place iodine with similarly-reacting substances like chlorine),

eΟΑ alcun motivation theoretic por eΟß tal per de corporal trocar nell'orn nole soumission cavir pre dezein.
and no scientific basis for swapping such pairs of elements in the order would be proposed for decades.

A veg temporane, l'occorrenΟß pariscent lon conjogntur yonoscon ig lonc mesuranΟß y corporal y legeressem teyen tout pondranΟß atomic monfiaΟΑ pres exact com l'ig a aquifex,
At the same time, the apparently-unrelated phenomenon wherein the lightest elements had all been measured to have atomic weights almost exact multiples of that of hydrogen,

cant oc se mantene bogr cas y ci lordessem (corporal tal cojan meΟΑes com es cuivr!), flagita expostion cavir.
while this was not true at all of heavier ones (even such prosaic substances as copper!), demanded explanation.

71.5: Threshold force

Threshold force [nuclear fission power] is the force derived from splitting the nuxes [nuclei] of atoms. The name 'threshold' refers to the so-called 'threshold heft [mass]' of an unstable substance like killdark [uranium], above which one has a self-sustaining detaxion [1].

History
The story of threshold force begins in 1902, with the discovery of the atomic nuxβÄîthat is, the discovery that almost all of the heft of an atom is located near its centre. This took place at the NΟΩstad Atellier (then privately owned and run by substantial theorist [2] Lohic Courvoisier de NΟΩstad) in the Cappatian city of WarnΟΗw [3].

The first threshold mills capable of supplying force for general use were in operation by the 1960s. These, and the unsettling martial applications of threshold force which had already been demonstrated on a small scale, brought the concept of harmful shortform luxes [gamma rays] into the popular consciousness. Many a blacklayer [horror movie] of the period and thereafter features unstoppable invisible rays inflicting horrors on the characters.

The corporal used in the vast majority of threshold mills, killdark, was first isolated in the early nineteenth century in the Irish shire of Killdara (whence, via Welsh kildΟΓrc 'from Killdara', the substance's name).

Portingale was a pioneer of threshold force, funding research into its effectivity in the 1950s in a bid to reduce their dependence on astraphor [electricity] exported by the Drengot Collusion. The polity's efforts were repaid in full; Portingale became a net exporter of astraphor in the 1970s.

References

Lux ΟÜterna: The First Century of Threshold Force, written in 2002 by Cawen ba Dirxen

---
[1] a generic term describing any process involving the change of one thing into another; synthesis, reaction.
[2] a chemist focused on the properties and structures of atoms and single molecules.
[3] from a *Dutch rendering of *GuaranΟ≠ + ΟΗw 'island', located where VitΟ≥ria (ES), Brazil is.

72.2: ombisc "resignation"

ombisc /…îmΥàb…Σx/ [ ämΥàb…ΣΟß]
- resignation, stepping down, retirement, the act of leaving a job or other duty

βÜ£ ombisc souvr /…îmΥàb…Σx Υàsu.vrΧ©/ [ ämΥàb…ΣΟß Υàsu.v…ê]

The verb phrase translates literally as "to release resignation, to let go of resignation". Compare var souvr "declare war" and ley souvr "uphold the law".

##

72.5: Markland

Markland is a polity of Albion, situated east of Wales, north of London and Kent, and south of Northumber and Sodrick.

Markland 2000.png
(The approximate extent of Markland proper in 2000 N.)

History
Markland originates with Saxon settlements in sub-Roman Britannia, which in the west formed (what would go on to be named) mearce land "borderland". Compare the related demonym Mierce "people of the border", whence the Latin name Mercia and native Merchman "man from Markland".

The petty kingdom of Anglia was annexed by Markland with the Treaty of Oxford in the late eighth century, at the dawn of the Viking Age. This established the persevering border between the mouths of the Severn and the Tames, and incited renewed influx of people into the petty kingdoms on Borland.

Markland weathered the Viking Age much more successfully than neighbouring Northumber, though much of its eastern coast was settled by Danes; the city of Lincon served as the de facto border of Danish rule for several decades. In part this was the inevitable result of tensions along the Welsh border; even during peacetime Theodric the Awefast maintained substantial levy forces.

The tenth-century Elved Marriage (which took place near the modern city of Leeds) ended hostilities between Markland and Dane forces in the east for almost a century. It is said to have been solemnised over a sandstone block later inlaid into the Redstone Chair, a low-backed wooden throne on which many subsequent monarchs were crowned.

Around the turn of the second millennium, Markland was conquering territory from the northern reaches of Wales (incidentally leading to Pope Agatho's canonisation of the Welsh St David). The perennial conflicts between Markland and Wales would come to be collectively referred to as the Mallor Wars.

The First Drengot Empire of the eleventh and twelfth centuries counted Markland as a major vassal, conquered from the south following the subjugation of Kent by Normandy and Greater Devon in 1029.

The Mallor Wars were reignited after a quiescent period by the martial-minded King Andrew II, with the Sack of Rexam in 1301 during which the original St Brigid's Abbey burned to the ground.

Markland was embroiled in the Wars of Fealty in the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries. Tensions over the location and possession of the Redstone Chair boiled over into a conflict that would last forty-one years. Markland conceded several battles to the young Kingdom of Britain in the early years of the wars, culminating in the 1483 Battle of Rexam which expanded the borders of Wales in the north and killed the last Duke of Chestre.

In the final years of the Wars of Fealty, a code known as Thieves' Script was employed by the forces of King Andrew III, apparently adapted from similar systems used by gangs of housebreakers in Nottingham. It is remembered today as a very early precursor of codes like steeplescript or modern one-zeffre encoding.

The poor economic conditions and population displacement in Markland following the Wars of Featly led it to suffer almost twice as many deaths per capita from the Second Great Dying compared to polities like Willemy.

Long contested between Markland and Kent, the city of London found itself independent for one final, lasting time following the Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century.

The spellings of various towns and cities in Markland were rationalised during the Long Peace period of the late eighteenth and early nineteenth centuries (for example, Conster for former Colncester /Υàk…în.st…ô/).

The 2010 ≈£cumen Count, a regular census, recorded that Utter Safford was the secondmost populous city in Markland proper with 4.4 million inhabitants, after the capital of Tommarth.

People
Monarchs
Theodric the Awefast (fl. 800), repeller of several attempted vikings into Merch territory
Oswin the Becoming (fl. 1029), last ruler of an independent Markland before the First Drengot Empire
Andrew II (fl. 1301), who reignited the Mallor Wars with the Sack of Rexam
Andrew III (fl. 1500), ruler of Markland during the latter years of the Wars of Fealty
Authors
Ferris Bewly (fl. 1942), alchemist responsible for the quire 'Modern Developments in Benjamine Detaxion'
Laurent Burnows (fl. 2015), famous for children's series 'Back to Front' and later for the more mature series 'An Inconvenient Armageddon'
Anna and Ralph Highwater (fl. 1930), cowriters of the series of books 'Games for Sorties and Excourses'
Challow Huddresfell (fl. 1956), historian who wrote 'Daughters of the Sea: The Northwestern Isles'
Jason Ridley (fl. 1967), who with others wrote the quire 'A Handbook of Natural Theory'
Dr Harold Westcamp (fl. 1997), who wrote the series of textbooks 'Albic Medicine in the Workshop Decades'
Others
Sarzan Dawkinholm (fl. c19), long-serving and much-lambasted Outland Minister

excerpt in translation from a review of 1934 film The Ascendant by Jacint Brunat, which appeared in Marsellan newspaper Fiellas Dimenja.

Jo nil for vertaΟΑβÄîopportun es l'oc edition tan brey posc y solstiΟß neΟΑan jo pos noc parcanΟß y ci sodal haubrjoranΟßasterrem ovart concedr ny fabr cortal e historic nuverrem de Dormaget.

##

73.5: Ambrosia

Ambrosia (Vascon/Vask Ambroxa) is a polity of north-eastern Mendeva, comprising several islands and much of the mainland up to the Five Seas. It borders New Provence to the south.

History

The story of Ambrosia begins with the Novomundine Landfall of 1471, when Vetomundine travellers encountered Mendeva for the first time since sporadic visits by the Norrish. A Vask fishing expedition (part of the New Navarre Enterprise) landed on the island of Brasil, which would go on to host the first European settlements in Mendeva.

The parts of Mendeva first explored by (and whose trade was monopolised by) VasconyβÄîto wit, the island of Brasil and the lands westward thereofβÄîwas named Ambrosia in honour of Ambrose III of Vascony.

Ambrosia achieved political and economic independence from Vascony in the early nineteenth century as part of the continent-wide phenomenon of the Mendevan Unmooring.

The first long-distance transmission of an image took place in Ambrosia in 1820, employing the so-called Steeplepost Tapestry (consisting of a wall of tiles painted black and white on each side). This device would soon go on to be the subject of the infamous Lies of 1824.

By the 1880s the most urbanised parts of Ambrosia were connected by wired tachygraph, allowing messages to be sent from home to home.

In 1950 the Japetos submarine Atlantic link conveyed its first messages. It was laid down between Gadire in Morrack and eastern Brasil.

Places

Aiga d'Angoixa, a major city on the Oxagre River and location of the original Steeplepost Tapestry

Illa de Vinna Salvatge, a built-up island in the Oxagre

Paratzon, a town in eastern Brasil

People

Fabian MostΟ†f (fl. c19), astronomer who worked at the observatory at Paratzon

CristΟ≤f Unquenjica (fl. 1971), author of popular history text Going to Brasil

##

74.5: The Pistol Mesh

The pistol mesh (Borlish sar pistol), also referred to simply as the mesh, is the global system of long-distance communication which employs a single normalised method for transmitting messages from one endpoint augrimmer to another.

Etymology
The phrase "pistol mesh" (in the Roun French form mail pistre) was first used by writer Ashford Pasquier in his 1968 parachthon work Starsail, describing technology that could transmit written messages, sounds and moving images between people in different star systems.

The phrase literally means "network for messages": the word pistol reflects post-Classical Latin pistola "epistle, written decree", aphΟΠretic form of synonymous epistola; the word mesh descends from Old English masc "net".

History
The pistol mesh grew out of several earlier technologies, all the way back to the first flag-lamp steeplepost lines in the eighteenth century allowing short texts to be sent between signal towers.

A century later sees the emergence of tachygraphs, devices allowing one to type text directly onto the barrick steeplepost from the comfort of one's home. However, it was only with the rise of general augrimmers in the twentieth century that we start to recognise the modern pistol mesh.

By the late twentieth century, the pistol mesh was sufficiently mature that whole enterprises could be conducted using it as a medium. For example, at this time the Tremonnow Open School (formerly the Brethin Mesh Institute) offered history courses across Albion entirely via mesh. Another impactiful development was the blossoming of 'distributed bookhouses', repositories for free-to-access information (much of it lifted from books out of withholding) to which anyone could contribute.

##

75.1: Astronomy Questions

excerpt in translation from scitation questions for the Safford Technic Scole's Spring 1989 course Along Natural Theory V: Astronomy from Lorenzo to th'Alluning.

Posc y closiscment dy Var Theudesc Primer d'an 1770, Saxony er mi ascenent e brouca auteΟß sur eiΟΑel may all'ig seyon aut a veg calscon ne sy histoir. CossireΟΑ y scaunΟß de Sneider sull'urbanfianΟß tost cohernt de Lepzi. DiΟßr alcot er sy scaunΟß influΟ·ΟΑ pall'oc e par y nuΟßal d'an 1772 entr luy eΟΑ Otto Sneider, scholer ag Sterncundhaus?

Consider Sneider's writing about the attendant early urbanisation of Lepzi. To what extent did this and her 1772 marriage to Sterncundhaus scholar Otto Sneider influence her writing?

"L'eutaΟΑ Trevogt macquau un generation de domn e segr astr mirant e donc luy dabr es nis toΟΑ cas final vars y descovart vogder lonc mi plaΟß dy siecr novoΟß; y vecinanΟß dy planet Janus par bint InΟΓm, eΟΑ y costregnΟß dy veloΟßtaΟΑ normal, com bustr." 75.2: lessy "whitened"

lessy /l…¦Υàsi/ [l…ΣΥàsi]
- whitened, bleached, having been acted upon by a bleaching agent;
- faded, washed out, worn out, played out, having lost an original colour or more generally having lost vitality or energy;
- (of hair, chocolate etc) white, considered as a deviation from a former or more basal colour;
- bleach blond(e), having been treated so as to significantly lighten the colour (of hair)

Etymology: originally in the first sense from Old Boral leissi "bleached, whitened; (n.) lye soap". This descends from Vulgar Latin lixΡΪvia "lye", via lixΡΪvus "made of lye" from lixa "lye, alkaline water".

Tu desir un beller epu a scoclet lessy ou?
/ti deΥàz…Σr …Σn b…¦Υàl…¦r eΥàpi a xoΥàkl…¦t l…¦Υàsi u/

75.4: astr mirar "stargaze"

astr mirar /ΥàastrΧ© miΥàrar/ [Υàas.t…ê m…ΣΥà Ä…ëΥê]
- stargaze, to look up at the stars at night;
- to perform astronomy, to act as an astronomer;
- daydream, woolgather, to have a spontaneous series of thoughts disconnected from reality

Etymology: phrase meaning "to look at stars", employing the poetic term astr "celestial body, star", borrowed from synonymous astrum in Latin. This sense of mirar "be amazed by, marvel at" to mean "look at" is also somewhat formal or poetic. In metaphorical use the phrase is seen as early as the fifteenth century.

##

75.5: Burgundy

Burgundy (Burgund French Borgogne /b…îgΥàg…î…≤…¦/) is a polity in Gaul, situated south of France, west of Bavarn, north of Provence and east of Vascony. It has for most of its history been ruled from the city of Geneva.

History

During the Second Tetrarchy period of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, Burgundy was a major vassal of the First Drengot Empire. Following the Emergency at Dijon in 1170, which presaged the collapse of said empire, Burgundy became a de facto autonomous kingdom.

In the early fifteenth century with the Vascon Ascendancy, Burgundy lost its Middlesea coast to Vascony. Burgundy managed to wrest the territory of Provence back from Ascendant Vascony in 1494. This would however be a very temporary state of affairs: the region seceded with the 1497βÄ™99 War of Provincial Independence, ending in Burgundy's defeat at the Battle of MargΟ®s.

No longer having any coastal territory, Burgundy weathered the Second Great Dying, which began in 1519, comparatively well; the principal vector of plague transmission was via naval trade. This relative stability may be related to the kingdom's (unusually late for the region) formal retention of the feudal system until the dawn of the seventeenth century.

The title of King of Burgundy was dissolved after severe losses to neighbouring Bavarn in the Blue Trans-Willem War of the late seventeenth century. The former capital Geneva became a subordinate city in the Duchy of Far Suebia.

A polity named Burgundy was formed once more (now without a monarch) in the nineteenth century, as part of the diplomatic process leading to the formation of the Drengot Collusion.

76.1: Origins of Manoscard

excerpt in translation from 1976 music history quire Shallmews and Fithels: the influence of post-Tetrarchic Hymnal Practice on Popular Music.

Nentamen, y character dell'eΟß caum vienn dy poiΟΑ a nou fanglanΟß preluΟ·scendessem yon no se poΟΑ for apponment addoit a them.

Cal teissir, com bustr, des y benoΟß communal dy kyriel manoscart ne bempart all'eΟß ouvr?

##

76.2: caum "piece"

caum /kom/ [kom]
- (obsolete) prayer, psalm, canticle, a religious text when set to music;
- hymn, any song of praise or worship;
- (more broadly) song, piece of music, an individual work of musical expression with or without lyrics;
- poem, artwork, piece, any artistic creation and especially one which is written;
- article, story, report, piece of nonfiction writing as might be published in the press;
- (mesh) post, any original work (prototypically writing) published to the mesh

Etymology: disputed. Semantically it should derive from Latin carmen "poem, incantation, prayer", but the phonological development is troublesome. Vocalisatio of /r > u/ is otherwise only attested in the presence of following /rΧ©/, as in Maucr "Wednesday" < *Marcre < MercuriΡΪ; this has led some scholars to postulate an Insular Latin variant *carmus, carmera, but this is unattested.

Reanalysis of the plural carmina as a feminine singular is, however, not out of the question. One might then suppose that dissimilation produced *carmira, but again this is unattested.

The word has seen significant semantic bleaching over time; the sense "poem" is first seen in the fifteen century, the sense "article" in the eighteenth, and the sense "(mesh)post" in the late twentieth.

Jo componoy l'oc caum por cinq baΟΑr.
/ £o Υ¨k…îm.poΥàn…îj l…îk kom p…îr ts…Σnk Υàba.ΟΑrΧ©/
[ ù ä Υ¨k…îm.p äΥàn…îj l…î kom p…îΥê ts…Σ≈΄k Υàba.ΟΑ…ê]
1s compose-pst def-s.px piece for five instrument
I arranged this piece for five musicians.

orΟΑenjanΟß /Υ¨…îr.ΟΑ…¦nΥà £ants/ [Υ¨…îΥê.ΟΑ…ΣnΥà ùans]
- invention, innovation, some new mechanism or technique that one has designed;
- contrivance, contraption, device, some means of carrying out a given task;

This descends from the particularly-variable Old Boral orΟΑenΟßar, orthanscar, aΟΑincquar "to invent, make up", which appears to be the confused chimera of Old English noun orΟΨanc "creativity, skill, invention" and related verb Ρ¹ΟΨenΡ΄an "to conceive of, think up".

##

76.5: The Workshop Period

The Workshop period, or the Global Workshop, refers to the middle decades of the nineteenth century. It is characterised by the rapid expansion and development of industry across the world. Usual historiography places the Workshop period between the Long Peace and the Good Game.

The Workshop period brought prosperity to parts of Europe which had been devastated by the Second German War of the 1820s, such as the Riverine Cities.

The impact of the Workshop period can be seen in artistic trends, with the shift away from voidtales and towards (on the one hand) realistic portrayals of the industrial working class and (on the other) optimistic speculation on future material advancements.

The guild VS (Vindrons Sairzaus), which sold the first widely-sold aphlox wine, would in this period expand its scope to other high-pressure and sealing components in manufacture, securing a very profitable niche.

The late Workshop period saw many barrick breakthroughs, concentrated in eastern Europe and Lower Mendeva.

The Drengot Collusion arose among the French polities of this period. The contemporaneous New Urban Mesh (including London and Axbane, among others) was subsumed byβÄîor took control ofβÄîthe nascent Collusion.

Many items of vocabulary were innovated in the Workshop period, including recoil "manufacture", underwrit "logo", counterzoic "anti-microbial" and retainer "IUD".

References
Albic Medicine in the Workshop Decades, a series of volumes by Dr Harold Westcamp published by the Conster Health Edifice, funded by the Markland Ministry for Health.

Toll'emendanΟß por y mas son compliΟΑ, jusc oy, ΟΑa vray meΟΑes a i y ci dou rig d'ardois derran allignaΟΑ cos scaΟΑr.

77.2: egreir "disembark"

egreir /eΥàgrir/ […ΣΥàg Ä…ΣΥê]
- disembark, get off, go ashore, to leave a vehicle;
- step out, to exit a place on foot;
- sally forth, to set out in the manner of a soldier onto a battlefield;
- pass away, expire, perish, (euphemistically) to die

Etymology: Middle Boral borrowing from Latin Ρ™gredior "I step out, disembark, overstep, leave", a compound of prefix ex- "out" and root gradior "I walk, march". It was modelled on the sole native descendant of a gradior compound, engreir "to begin; to sleep with" (since lost, although the participle engres "way in, starting point; sexual intercourse" remains). In euphemistic use the verb is first attested in the eighteenth century.

77.3: lavaΟß "wasteful"

77.4: orruΟΑar "seem"

orruΟΑar /Υ¨…î.riΥàΟΑar/ [Υ¨…î. Ä…ΣΥàΟΑ…ëΥê]
- amount to, be, to be the same as or equivalent to;
- seem, to give the impression of or to be thought of in a particular way;
- (impersonal, with dative experiencer) think, deem, reckon, to have an impression of something

Etymology: from Old Boral oruΟΑar, ouruΟΑar "to take up space, take the form of; to amount to", from Late Latin obr≈Ϊt≈ç "I overwhelm, conceal, cover, take up space". This is a regularisation (with unexplained lengthening of the root vowel) of synonymous obru≈ç in Classical Latin, based on the past participle. In impersonal use it appears to be syntactically modelled on Germanic constructions like English methinks "it seems to me".

It seemed an awful waste of time.

##

77.5: Continents and Conjugality

The Novomund, also called the New World, refers to the lands to the west of the Atlantic Ocean. Inhabited for over a myriad years, the history of these lands comes together with that of the Vetomund following the Novomundine Landfall of 1471.

The Novomund is composed of the two great landmasses of Mendeva and Cappatia [North and South America], along with over a dozen major islands (such as Brasil [Newfoundland] and Cocain [Cuba]) and innumerable lesser ones.

Many crops now cultivated worldwide were first farmed in the Novomund, including shadome fruit, tartover, shockle and mikecorn [tomatoes, potatoes, chocolate and maize]. Products such as coshow [rubber] and kistmaw [tobacco] also derive from plants native to the Novomund.

---

The Household Renovation of the late nineteenth century was an attitudinal shift towards non-traditional familial structures, the institution of marriage, and the social role of children.

It was prefigured by the deemphasis of religion in government in those countries party to the 1850 Laic Declarations of Belgrade, as well as contraceptive medical innovations of the time like coshow bed-sleeves [latex condoms] and stauron retainers [IUDs].

The popularity of the masquira [~heist/vigilante] literary genre, with its hidden identities and lampooning of upper-class mores, is usually understood to have come hand in hand with the Household Renovation (not to mention the enduring popularity of masquira in tovarick [queer] circles).

Early embracers of the Household Renovation included Britain, the Riverine Cities and New Leudong [a polity in south-eastern Australia]; the ethos would spread (especially via Britain's cultural cachet in Gulf Mendeva) elsewhere over the subsequent decades.

##

78.1: Decline of Feudaliam

excerpt in translation from the condensed study materials for the New Vithor course Concurrence History of the 16th Century: Death, Ships and the Movement of the Heavens.

L'ustol es y model feudal stimar defonct cos enter posc 1550 N, ben toΟΑ cas deur polity insoler com Borgogn eΟΑ Avry.
It is usual to consider the feudal system entirely defunct by 1550 N, at least outside of insular polities like Burgundy and Awry.

Soumis son un diverstaΟΑ a mejan por l'oc dissolution expondr; creudessem orruΟΑ certain parmy a ci autr.
A variety of reasons for this collapse have been suggested; some are more widely accepted than others.

Mout soumont is un tramou de labour sarver deut ag Scon Gran MorrenΟß, abiltant ig ci paΟ·san ayent survecuΟΑ flagitn contingent dijarblessem de lorry segnour.

For example, Borland under Joseph III lost over forty myriad people, one-seventh of its population;
