##

78.5: The Good Game period

The Good Game period refers to the final decades of the nineteenth century, especially when construed as a time of rising global powers formed of alliances and meshes of trade and technology binding the world together. It conventionally follows the Global Workshop period of spreading industry and ends with the outbreak of the Millstone War.

The same Laic Declarations of Belgrade in 1850 which prefigured the social and moral shifts of the Household Renovation also set the stage for the political realignment of the Good Game decades, which new alliances between states that could not have countenanced such ties even a generation earlier.

This period sees the establishment of several alliance-federacies that would go on to define the geopolitics of the twentieth century, such as the Drengot Collusion in north-western Europe (whose expansion concluded in 1894 with the accession of Borland).

The perception of the Good Game period as a time of glamorous espionage and political machinations behind closed doors is not entirely deservedβÄîcompare the popular perception (via books and film) of the 1887 Desarden affair involving a London ambassador and the king of Nackon Day with the tawdrier reality. Nevertheless, the period has cemented itself as a popular setting for spycraft and masquira stories.

The kevler romance arose as a distinct subgenre of masquira in this period, featuring elaborate plans and schemes usually aiming to steal some priceless item.

The 1880s saw a brief fad for 'Morrack fashion' in countries across Europe and the Novomund, in areas such as dress, architecture and food. There was, for example, a trend of incorporating exposed stonework draped in tapestry and curtains in contemporary buildings.

Scientific and technological advances of the Good Game period include discovering other galaxies, determining the normal speed, the first household mesh-linked tachygraphs, and stauron retainers.

##

79.1: The Welsh Viking

excerpt in translation from scitation questions in the January 2014 Tremonnow Open School concurrence history course 844βÄ™900: Early Dane Kingdom-Building across the Northwestern Isles.

(i) Deut ag ci havan stapr e ci instanΟßment dannesc entr Dublin e Yorvic, ben ceter, aye y principaltaΟΑ ne GuineΟΑ meyon suffignt por primaltaΟΑ sur Poves condensar? Souvin ne soumoin y porΟßonment de Mondovel stant concordaΟΑ entr GuineΟΑ e Sodric, oc stant tesmognaΟΑ cos parey ny chronic d'Oltogn e de Carleul.
(i) Was it because of the Dane wayports and settlements between Dublin and Yorwick that the principality of Wynneth had the resources to consolidate primacy over Powes? Include reference to the partition of Mondwell [Anglesey] agreed between Wynneth and Sodrick, as mentioned in both the Ulstre and Carlile chronicles.

(ii) Dell'asc vecuΟΑ d'Owain ap BlethenβÄîluy rent legender com Ovan de Jolliad, l'amig stival eΟΑ enmig yemal por y Οßaucr arthuricβÄîcal poum nos savir de mout alcot segr cambresc de nascenΟß ern droughant de gazou viking tandic y fragossau dannesc denen kintaΟΑessem con lorry vecintaΟΑ?
(ii) What can the life of Owain ap BlethenβÄîrendered into legend as Owen of Jolliad, sometime-friend sometime-foe of the ArthuriansβÄîtell us about the extent to which Welsh-born men participated in viking raids as the Dane settlements became more enmeshed with their vicinities?

(iii) DescreyaΟΑ ant detagl toll'actour connuΟΑ abord dy ReconcarnΟß a Castr d'an 887 N, tan con y contingent politic yon cascun parmy se troven eΟß ayent menaΟΑ vars y faction idiosyncras y battagl parlocant.
(iii) Describe in detail the known actors party to the Reconquest of Chestre in 887 N, along with the political context each found themselves in which led to the idiosyncratic alliances on either side of the battle.

##

79.2: jagottaΟΑ "jagged"

jagottaΟΑ /Υ¨ £a.g…îΥàtaΈΗ/ [Υ¨ ùa.g äΥàtah]
- jagged, notched, serrated, rough, being unevenly cut or having a similar texture to something so cut;
- craggy, rugged, (of landscape) characterised by unpredictable changes in elevation;
- abrasive, harsh, disagreeable, being overly agressive in one's manner and causing irritation

Etymology: past participle of jagottar "to notch, serrate", from noun jagot "notch, snag, sharp projection", of uncertain origin. Likely a diminutive of a form *jac, jag, which must be related somehow to the synonymous jag in MerchβÄîthe direction of borrowing is however unclear since the Merch is only attested from the fifteenth century. Attempts have been made to connect the words to Borland English Οßagge "shrub" but the semantics are doubtful.

OrruΟΑ l'occijon commettr con pujoncr jagottaΟΑ.
/…îΥàr…ΣΈΗ Υ¨l…îk.siΥà £…în k…îΥàm…¦.trΧ© k…în piΥà £…în.krΧ© Υ¨ £a.g…îΥàtaΈΗ/
[ äΥà Ä…Σh Υ¨l…îk.s…ΣΥà ù…în k äΥàm…¦.t…ê k…îm p…ΣΥà ù…î≈΄.k…ê Υ¨ ùa.g äΥàtah]
seem def=murder commit-inf with dagger jagged
It seems the murderer used a serrated dagger.

79.3: emendanΟß "fix"

emendanΟß /Υ¨e.m…¦nΥàdants/ [Υ¨e.m…ΣnΥàdans]
- fix, repair, something done to mend a broken object or situation;
- resolution, solution, answer, something that solves a problem

Etymology: noun derived from emendar "to fix, repair, resolve, sort out", which descends (via Old Boral amendar, later re-classicised) from Latin Ρ™mend≈ç "I improve, remedy, cure".

L'emendanΟß ottem plagra alcun hom.

def=fix best please-fut.3s no person
The best solution will please nobody.

79.4: cay seg a veΟΑerrem "elephant in the room"

cay seg a veΟΑerrem /ke sij a veΥàΟΑ…¦.r…¦m/ [ke sij a v…ΣΥàΟΑ…¦. Ä…êm]
- (int.)  tactful acknowledgement of a lie or secret everyone knows about but nobody will call out;
- (n., usually hyphenated) elephant in the room, such a lie or secret

Etymology: idiom first attested in this form in the late eighteenth century but having many variants beforehand; literally "it takes six (months) for the eldest (child)". This refers to a couple's first child being born only six months after the wedding, a situation where it is clear to everyone what happened but to mention it explicitly would be impolite.

Quippe alcun hom nil y budr de cay-seg-a-veΟΑerrem svoccar.
/kw…ΣΥàpe alΥàk…Σn …îm n…Σl i Υàbi.drΧ© de keΥ¨si.ja.veΥàΟΑ…¦.r…¦m sv…îΥàkar/
[kw…ΣΥàpe Υ¨a.g…ΣΥàn…îm n…Σl i Υàbi.d…ê de k…ΣΥ¨si.j…ê.v…ΣΥàΟΑ…¦. Ä…êm zv äΥàk…ëΥê]
surely no person won't def harbinger of elephant.in.the.room bring.up-inf
Surely nobody will mention the looming elephant in the room.

##

79.5: Voidtale

Voidtale (Borlish trevogt /treΥàv…îjt/, portmanteau of trevold "novel" and vogt "outer space") is a subgenre of parachthon romance which focuses on voyages through outer space visiting alien worlds.

The genre was pioneered by Saxon author Elsebeth Sneider with her 1795 work Through a Black Mirror, which sparked a three-decade long fashion for the subject known as the "voidtale craze". This first period is characterised by a strongly pre-empirical understanding of conditions on other worlds: no particular attention is paid to the details of breathable air or bearable gravity, and each bustling planet is peopled by very human societies. Indeed, more than anything else these stories resemble the "geoscopic voyage" tales of the previous century, which depict sailors exploring unknown corners of the Earth and encountering the peoples there.

The voidtale craze is usually said to end with the Second German War in the 1820s, although later works like Narayanan's landscapes (which portray barren and inhospitable surfaces of the other planets orbiting the Sun) are generally counted as part of the same tradition.

It has been claimed that the voidtale craze inculcated an enthusiasm for astronomy in its generation, and contributed to the cosmic discoveries of the mid-nineteenth centuryβÄîincluding the discoveries of the planet Janus and the speed of light.

References

New Skies, Old Stories, 1980 work written by Simon d'Estiala and published by Prindor MarsillΟ®s

##

80.1: Uneventful Alburg

excerpt in translation from the collected correspondence of Absolon Mortenszen, playwright and commentator in Alburg, Denmark.

J'ay spien vos seyaΟΑ fort parsanaΟΑ des y mephit dont scavoist vos ne vossy pistol derran;
I hope you are well recovered from the ague of which you spoke in your last missive;

Me stou bogr vos soumonir a cossirar y citaΟΑ salir fin a fis sera y prepondranΟß a commarΟß.
I need not remind you to consider leaving the city until the bulk of the trading has ended.

InconnuΟΑ tojorn es cal contamen foran posn marcant galar, com sceus my granpaΟΑr tesmognar.
One never knows what foreign ills merchants may bring, as my grandfather could have attested.

Y bourg reman cojan equal des vossy sgaranΟß com sceye pre nossy acconnosc, vos plaΟß d'oΟ·r.
The town remains as dull since you departed as it was before we met, you will be pleased to hear.

Ja galaΟΑ, y casatabr dijarbr sull'ig rif meΟΑes surcay issugtessem posc toΟΑ gent de nis nell'oc bourg ablegar;
In point of fact, even the decent lodging-house on the strand is poorer for having banned all the interesting people in town;

parcanΟß ig il me parmet jug d'entrar es mal me desconent, malgraΟΑ jo vil toΟΑ mod me sarvir dy ci sigt la tandic possibr.
perhaps it speaks poorly of me that they yet allow me entrance, though I will at least avail myself of the views while I can.

80.2: bojar "move"

bojar /boΥà £ar/ [b äΥà ù…ëΥê]
- move, shift, bump, budge, to cause to be displaced from a position;
- dislodge, free, extricate, to remove from a position in which something was firmly set
- reconsider, to change one's mind

Etymology: from Old Boral bojar "move, shift", originally in both intransitive (it moved) and transitive (I moved it) use but by the Middle Boral period only in the latter. This is a borrowing from synonymous Old French bougier, from Vulgar Latin bullic≈ç "I boil, bubble up", a frequentative from bulli≈ç (whence Borlish boglir "to boil").

My granmaΟΑr noloy se bojar cas scadom pascr.
/mi granΥàma.ΟΑrΧ© noΥàl…îj se boΥà £ar kaz xaΥàd…îm Υàpa.xrΧ©/

1s.gen gran won't.pst budge-inf home.of tomato eat-inf
My gran wouldn't reconsider eating tomatoes.

80.3: exeunt fair "go away"

exeunt fair /…¦kΥàsawnt fer/ […ΣgΥàzawnt f…¦Υê]
- go away, leave, depart, exit, to go out from a place or situation;
- bow out, back out, withdraw, walk away, to resign or disavow something especially in a deft manner

Il aun ig rekestaΟΑ jo faΟß exeunt lon massant.

80.4: rif "riverbank"

rif /r…Σf/ [ Ä…Σf]
- bank, riverbank, the area along the sides of a river or stream;
- shore, seashore, strand, a stretch of coastline (especially when stony as opposed to sandy);
- beachfront, seaside, promenade, terrace, esplanade, a walkway between land and sea often lined with shops

Etymology: uncomplicated descendant via Old Boral rif from Latin rΡΪpa "riverbank, seashore". By the Middle Boral period we see a clear semantic separation between sandy play "beach, bay" and stony rif, although some place names like Volandry (from dialect volan "gull" and rif "beach") fail to fit this dichotomy.

Nos erau flannant lonc y rif, ig carnt yon beller acatar.
/n…îz eΥàro flaΥànant l…înk i r…Σf | aj Υàka.rnΧ©t j…în b…¦Υàl…¦r Υ¨a.kaΥàtar/
[Υ¨no.z…ΣΥà Äo fl…êΥànan l…î≈΄k i  Ä…Σf | aj Υàka. Ä…ênt j…în b…ΣΥàl…¦ΥêβÄΩ Ä…êkΥàt…ëΥê]
1p ipf-1p stroll-p.prs along def bank | s.dt seek-p.prs where sweet buy-inf
We were walking down the strand, looking to buy sweets.

##

80.5: Tavancy

Tavancy (Merch /t…ôΥàvan.s…ôi/, Run'allo [Quechua] Tawanden /taΥàwan.d…¦n/), formerly the Tavancine Empire, is a political in western Cappatia [South America] occupying the coast between the Isthmus [of Panama] to the north and Maboutian [~Chilean] lands to the south.
Toponymy

The name Tavancy (Latin Tavantia) is a back-formation from the adjective Tavancine (Latin tavantΡΪnus). This comes from the earliest Latin name imperium tavantΡΪnum first attested in the sixteenth century, which was borrowed via Morrove [Morrocan] Arabic as a somewhat-mistaken partial calque from the Run'allo tawantin-suyu "the quartet of provinces".
History

The first Vetomundine people to document the Tavancine Empire were Morrack [Morroco] traders with the New World Company (Ί¥Ί±ΌÉΊ© ΊßΌ³ΊΙΊßΌ³ΌÖ ΊßΌ³Ί§Ί·ΌäΊ·), who had already begun dealing with Arcabil [Caribbean] peoples and with Mashick [the Aztec Empire]. Through the sixteenth century several wayports along the west Cappatian coastline were established by said traders, some of which would grow into substantial staddomain [~export colony] settlements.

The death of Tavancine Emperor Cariwasari in 1660 is suspected to have been an assassination carried out on Morrack orders, purportedly to allow for more profitable trade in silverβÄîMorrack was at the time running low on funds to support their ongoing wars in Vascony and Libya.

In the early 1930s, a collaboration between alchemists at the Branwen Sanatory in New Leudong and the University of Rimack in Tavancy led to the discovery of cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine [ibuprofen] (in pill form familiarly called "CD" or, in Markland, "Tommy pills") as an effective hebetant [painkiller] without significant adversions [side effects].

81.2: ajoutaΟßar "be fun"

ajoutaΟßar /Υ¨a. £u.taΥàtsar/ [Υ¨a. ù ä.t…êΥàts…ëΥê]

    be fun, entertain, divert, engage, to be interesting in an enjoyable way;

Etymology: eighteenth-century verbal derivation from ajoutaΟß "gratification, enjoyment, satisfaction", itself derived from ajoutar "gratify, help out, please" with the somewhat-pejorative or diminishing suffix -aΟß (from Latin -Ρ¹ceus "shaped like"). This root verb descends via Old Boral aioftar from post-Classical Latin adiuvit≈ç "I help out, assist, am useful", from earlier synonymous adiuv≈ç with a frequentative derivation.

81.4: vis "in fact"

vis /v…Σz/ [v…Σz]

    evidently, of course, manifestly, as you know, used to introduce information which one feels is sufficiently obvious that it should have come up by now;
    in fact, actually, in reality, indeed, used to introduce a statement which corrects some part of a previous understanding;
    it transpires, it turns out, apparently, used to introduce surprising new information

Etymology: extracted in the late Middle Boral period from expressions like vis esβÄΠ "it is seen thatβÄΠ", as the past participle of veir "to see" < Latin vide≈ç "I see". Speculation that it is instead a retention of Old Boral ivis "sure, certain", a borrowing from synonymous Old English gewiss, is unsupported by any intermediate attestations (though cf modern Merch awice /…ôΥàw…ôis/ "indeed, yes").

The word is often found in the collocation Or visβÄΠ "Well, actuallyβÄΠ", especially when it is used at the start of sentences.

    Vis no som nos jamay eiΟΑ a DuncloΟΑ.
    /v…Σz no s…îm n…îz  £aΥàme iΈΗ a d…ΣnΥàkl…îΈΗ/
    [v…Σz n äΥàs…înn äz  ù…êΥàme iΈΗ a d…Σ≈΄Υàkl…îΈΗ]
    in.fact neg be.1p 1p never go-p.pst to Dunclothe
    We've actually never been to Dunclothe.

##

81.5: Madray

Madray is a polity in the southeast of India governed from the city of the same name [Madurai].

The British ship Ph≈™bos in the early sixteenth century stopped at the port of Romeswarm [Rameswaram] in Madray on its geoscopic voyage around Africa and on to Borune [Borneo] (where it would meet the Morrack [Moroccan] ship Cynthia travelling in the other direction).

The death of King Rahulamathavan in 1735 is thought to have been due to an early outbreak of pennackit dysentery [cholera] (although global outbreaks of the disease would not begin in earnest until 1753).

As part of the general Democratic Wars period in the mid-eighteenth century, many states quarrelled for control over parts of India, including Madray. Both relatively nearby powers like China and distant trade interests like Britain became embroiled.

The eighteenth century also saw many technological developments out of this region. For example, the use of pedal holes in musical instruments was pioneered by the peria nadaswarm (the apex of arms races to widen pitch ranges and increase volume) which would influence instruments like the torriot into their modern forms.

The voidtale craze begun by Elsebeth Sneider in 1795 reached as far as Madray; the renowned landscapist Narayanan is especially remembered for his series of works depicting the planets from their surfaces.

82.1: Dane-ruled York

excerpt in translation from 1997 academic textbook Del Christianizamento dell'Isole Norroveste (On the Christianisation of the Northwestern Isles), written by historian of Middle-Age religion Dijaca Rofotto.

NaΟΑusc sta l'oc un issent incoΟ·br de commouΟΑ lonc y tarm cambresc;
In part this [York falling and Lincoln standing] was the inevitable result of tensions along the Welsh border;

durant meΟΑes l'epoc paΟßous stendiscent a comparaΟßon, Theodoric l'Auvast eΟΑ Owain de Brochwel y dou aurn jug centuir corrovaΟΑ magn surfaint por y Lign Severn defendr.
even during the relatively-long period of peacetime both Theodric the Awefast and Owain of Brochwel maintained ever-more levy forces to police the long Severn Line.

Contrig, Yorvic eu sauf a se condonar sou regn dannesc.
By contrast, Yorwick almost gave itself over to Dane rule.

Degnant ig regest suajoir sein ben scarder ben tendenΟßous cos clar (scaunΟß brethin meΟΑes dell'oc epoc no's enter confiabr),
Though convincing records are either fragmentary or clearly biased (even Brethin writing in this period cannot be entirely trusted),

creut cos general es ig y cort dy scavin l'ablegau preluΟ·scent com Οßamoc,
it is generally believed that the alderman's court threw him out pre-emptively as a sop,

ne spien de vanc aur aΟΑief oblatar volonter ag gazovour por poΟΑir un devastation total dy citaΟΑ evitar.
hoping to offer enough treasure freely to the invaders that they could avoid a total destruction of the city.

82.2: vogt dy foscur "dead of night"

vogt dy foscur /v…îjt di foΥàx…Σr/ [v…îjt d…Σ f äΥàx…ΣΥê]
- pitch blackness, inky blackness, a particularly deep darkness;
- dead of night, small hours, witching hour, the part of the night conceived of as the darkest and farthest from day

Etymology: idiom first attested in the poetry of 15C writer Drominir, literally "emptiness of darkness". The noun vogt descends from Vulgar Latin *vocitus "empty", a variant of the synonymous vacuus in Classical Latin. The noun foscur is a noun derivative of adjective fosc "dark" which descends from Latin fuscus "dimly lit, black".

Y lamp se quΟ·sceu, y coloir augtant ne vogt dy foscur.
/i lamp se Υ¨kwaj.iΥàxaw | i koΥàl…îjr ojΥàtant ne v…îjt di foΥàx…Σr/
[i lamp s…Σ Υ¨kwa.j…ΣΥàxaw | i k äΥàl…îj…êβÄΩ ¹ äjΥàtan n…Σ v…îjt d…Σ f äΥàx…ΣΥê]
def light rfl turn.off-pst | def hall send-p.pst in void of-def darkness
The light went out, plunging the hall into darkness.

82.3: cas sar "online"

cas sar /kaz sar/ [k…êsΥàs…ëΥê]
- online, available over or accessed via the Internet;
- cyber-, pertaining to the Internet or to activities conducted on the subject of or via the Internet

Etymology: phrase first attested in the 1980s, from the preposition cas "pertaining to, on the subject of, at the home of, with respect to, when it comes to" (via Old Boral cas from Latin casa "house") and the noun sar "fishnet, mesh, network" (from the synonmous Vask). The noun sar in this usage ("the Internet") is shortened from the phrase sar pistol "lit. the message mesh", itself translating Roun French mail pistre /ma £ piΥêt…ô/ in the same sense. This was first used in 1968 work of fiction Starsail, by Ashford Pasquier.

Nos som gran sviΟΑour a conemir cas sar.
/n…îz s…îm gran sviΥàΟΑur a Υ¨ko.neΥàm…Σr kaz sar/
[n äΥàs…îm g Äan zv…ΣΥàΟΑ äΥêβÄΩ Äa Υ¨ko.niΥàm…ΣΥê k…êsΥàs…ëΥê]
1p be.1p big fan at shop-inf wrt mesh
We're big fans of online shopping.

82.4: scenar "stage"

scenar /xeΥànar/ [Οß…ΣΥàn…ëΥê]
- stage, put on, produce, perform, to put together a theatrical production as an acting troupe;
- orchestrate, undertake, arrange, direct, carry out, to plan and go through with some scheme or undertaking with many moving parts

Etymology: Middle Boral verbal derivation from scen "(theatre) stage; platform, soapbox" (formerly sometimes re-classicised as scΟΠn), borrowed from Latin scΟΠna "stage, scenery" from Ancient Greek œÉΈΚΈΖΈΫΈ° "tent, stage". In metaphorical use seen from the eighteenth century.

Y tissour aun un exeunt scenaΟΑ.
/i t…ΣΥàsur on …Σn …¦kΥàsawnt xeΥànaΈΗ/
[i t…ΣΥàs äΥêβÄΩ Äon …Σn …ΣgΥàzawn Οß…ΣΥànah]
def weaver have.3p indf exit stage-p.pst
The weavers have staged a walkout.

##

82.5: London

London (Borlish /Υàl…în.drΧ©/) is a city and polity on the island of Albion. The city is downstream on the River Tames, and the polity comprises much of the Tames' watershed. It is bordered to the north by Markland, the south by Kent, and the west by Britain.

History
London was established by the Romans shortly after their invasion around 50 N, named from a Kelt placename referring to the local floodplain.

After time as a secondary city in Albion, London became the imperial capital of the region once more during the Second Tetrarchy as part of the First Drengot Empire.

The First Burning of London took place in 1290; at least 400 people were killed in the ensuing week-long disaster. This was one of several conflagrations in the first half of the second millennium, many due to the city being perennially conquered from the north or south.

The Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century saw London finally gain lasting independence from its neighbours in 1748 under Queen Margaret, who could trace her ancestry to the royal families of both Markland and Kent. The office of Lord Mayor remained politically powerful enough to rival that of the new monarch, however.

London is home to the New Lyceum, a preeminent research university established in the late eighteenth century.

Seventy-fifth anniversary celebrations in 1823 saw significant infrastructural investments in the region, with a great deal of building along the south bank of the Tames.

London's population grew rapidly during the Global Workshop period of the mid-nineteenth century. It became a lead member of the New Urban Mesh of several major cities in the region, and spearheaded the Mesh's takeover of (or submission into) the larger Drengot Collusion.

The independent polity of London has been long associated with spycraft, with events like the 1887 Desarden Affair (involving the London ambassador to Nackon Day) and the many Londoners in the Roun Red House (the intelligence centre for the Drengot Collusion) implicated in the Furore of 1961 cementing this image in the popular imagination.

Places
Sowbruck, a borough south of the Tames
Ebbasham, a minor spa town
People
Monarchs
Matthew Drengot (fl. 1120), younger brother of then Drengot Emperor Roger II; Matthew ruled the northern half of the Empire in his brother's name
Margaret (fl. 1748), first ruler of an independent London
Other
Clarissa Bellamy (fl. 1920), author of many lovetales and the parachthon work A Door, Once Opened
Heleth Bellamy (fl. 1920), researcher at the New Lyceum specialising in the Democratic Wars
Gunna Despath (fl. 1920), translator who was born Gonnilt DispaΟΑ in Borland

1. Ne cal comtaΟΑ, regnaΟΑ sou Comt Jerron, se prodoy l'UrgenΟß noncupaΟΑ d'an 1170, y donjon devastant absoluΟΑ e menant vars y secession de Borgogn (e tandem y dissolution) dell'Empir Constant? [Dijon]

83.3: eir sauf a "all but"

eir sauf a /ir sof a/ […ΣΥê sof a]
- come close to, all but, almost, to go nearly so far as to do something

Etymology: idiom literally "to go except to", first attested in the fifteenth century. The verb eir "to go" descends directly from Latin e≈ç "I go", while the preposition sauf "except" is slightly problematic. The regular descendant of Latin salvus "free (of)" should be sau; the final -f may be due to influence from Old French.

J'eu sauf a lou sveilar n'ortellent atras.

##

83.5: Loveless Lepzi

Lepzi is the capital city of Saxony, located on the Bleak Alstre.

History
Lepzi first acted as political centre for the First Kingdom of Saxony, very briefly just prior to its collapse at the close of the thirteenth century. The Houghbrocken dynasty ruled from there for some years before being removed to Magdeburg when the house was reduced to the four-year-old Marksman Baldur.

With the conclusion of the First German War in 1770, Saxony was ascendant and controlled more territory than it had at any point in its history. Lepzi was subject to attendant early urbanisation and trebled in population in just fifty years. It was one of the vanguards of the Global Workshop period, exploiting vast amounts of locally-mined coal power.

The Blue Marian Society, which agitated for inheritance and suffrage law reforms, was founded in the nineteenth century in Lepzi.

People
Albert Siegert (fl. 1744), owner of a leatherworking and cobbling industry based in Lepzi
Elsebeth Sneider (m. 1772, d. ~1830), author and originator of the voidtale craze with her 1795 work Through a Black Mirror
Otto Sneider (m. 1772), scholar at the Leipzi Sterncundhaus

---

Rachel Desselut (fl. 1743) was a French philosopher. She is best remembered for her 1743 work The Parable of the Mute Bookbinder, which discusses problems of infinite regression and of identity.

She was born to Maurice Desselut, eighth Lord Bouvoin, who was a writer and explorer. Fittingly he was also an infamous libertine (the house name Desselut deriving from an Old French word meaning dissolute, philandering).

She was the first to lay out in our modern terms the philosophical distinction between Form and Method, which allows an entity to self-replicate (constructing its Form from the Method's instructions, then transcribing the Method blindly). These concepts would go on to be vital in the study of methodics and to general augrimmer practice.

Desselut inferred from her work the existence of a vital language, now known to be composed of heredian acid.

Degnant ig l'oc declar nole cavir reconnuΟΑ cos solemn jusc Borgogn fos vencuΟΑ dou annaΟΑ apres posc y Battagl a MargΟ®s, caye l'oc occorrenΟß ayent l'eΟΑegl de LinaΟß AmigΟ≤t reitfiaΟΑ com preu entr par por y nou republic.

84.4: jagat "setting"

jagat / £aΥàgat/ [ ù…êΥàgat]

- continuity, world, universe, a setting shared by a collection of fictional works;

Etymology: nineteenth century borrowing via Vascon from Gurjat ύΣ€ύΣ½ύΣΛ <jagat> "world, universe", used as a term of art for "setting" in literary criticism. This is a learned reflex of synonymous Sanscrit ύΛ€ύΛ½ύΛΛύΞç <jagat>, from a root cognate with English "come". Its poetic usage recalls the original sense but is not used in ordinary speech or writing.

##

84.5: Lower Mendeva

Lower Mendeva (Borlish Mendeva Bassem /Υ¨m…¦n.deΥàva Υàba.s…¦m/) is a geographical region comprising the southern section of the continent of Mendeva, located west of the Mendevan Gulf. The term sometimes also includes the islands of the Arcabil, which are located within said Gulf.

Contact between Lower Mendeva and the Vetomund began in the late fifteenth century with merchant ships from the Morrack New World Company (established by Malik Munir al-Hamdawi in 1463). In turn, traders therefrom encountered the peoples of the Arcabil, the Mashick Empire in Lower Mendeva, and further south the Tavancine Empire in Cappatia.

The region is predominated by the polity of Mashick, which stretches from coast to coast and so far north as to border Mivock in the west and Taisha in the east.

Many barrick innovations of the late Workshop period came out of Lower Mendeva, likely due to practical concerns around the rapid spread of wired post (there being little previous adoption of the flag-lamp steeplemesh in the region to displace).

By the late twentieth century, this region had become major exporters of foods such as mause and amback.

Many trade goods originated in Lower Mendeva, including billrod dye, shockle, shadome and tashban.

##

85.1: The Jacobin Fraternity

excerpt in translation from Y GiΟΑ Scos por Ausbagn (The Secret Guide to Axbane), sold for 1βçö in the Calder Museum of Health's leavingstore.

BenoΟßabr por seucq historic (ben toΟΑ cas por y cromn dy bon leyour!) son l'Orn a Sagnt Jagm, ant nom dont costroit er y Monastir Peninsoler nossy citaΟΑ surplombant.
More relevant to history (or at least to the good reader's moneypurse!) are the Order of Saint James, in whose name was built the Peninsular Monastery which overlooks the city.

FondaΟΑ ny siecr deghem sou y patronisment de Domn Amegl a Brug, l'orn ern envoluΟΑ par un smout pouvlancer contr Geoffry II eΟΑ ern forΟßaΟΑ a enfugr vars Istr Boral d'an 1067.
Founded in the tenth century under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges, they were implicated in a popular uprising against Geoffry II and forced to flee to Borland in 1067.

Oy es l'Orn (sy nom modern dignant, y FraterntaΟΑ Jagovin) cos majortaΟΑer reconnuΟΑ por sell'yeustmandry Jaibos e por bempart dell'eiΟΑel atorn dy citaΟΑ posseyent, ig inclus y loy d'un parmy y ci bagn roman antic yon Ausbagn recief sy nom.
Today the Order (with its modern name, the Jacobin Fraternity) is in the main known for its Jaibos breweries and for owning much of the land around the city, including the site of one of the ancient Roman bathhouses for which Axbane is named.

85.2: surplombar "overhang"

surplombar /Υ¨s…Σr.pl…îmΥàbar/ [Υ¨s…ΣΥê.pl ämΥàb…ëΥê]
- overhang, hang over, impend, to be suspended above;
- overlook, to be located higher up than (as of a building on a hill);
- threaten, imperil, to place in a dangerous position;
- impinge upon, affect, interfere with, clash with, to have a (usually negative) impact on;
- to immediately precede in time

Etymology: Middle Boral coinage from sur- "over" (< Latin super-) and plomb "lead, weight, sound", conjuring the image of a weight suspended above something. In the third and fourth senses, from the inherent danger of the suspended object falling on one (attested from the sixteenth century); in the last sense originally in guild jargon and perhaps then jocular (attested from the early twentieth century).

85.3: seucq historic "posterity"

turn.out def-s.dt husband of 3p.gen daughter work indf brewer

##

85.5: Laic Declarations

The Laic Declarations were a series of diplomatic documents produced by an international committee of delegations from several dozen polities in Belgrade in 1850. These documents outlined procedures for uncoupling established religions from administrative state functions, and the legal status of people with minority faiths.

At this time, Belgrade was one of the Riverine Cities, an alliance-federacy of small independent states along the Danaw River and its tributaries. These cities had risen from the ashes of the Kingdom of the Danaw, which had collapsed in spectacular fashion after the Second German War of the 1820s. As such, Belgrade was an early hotbed of new religious and legal thinking, with a renowned Promethean club associated with the Deviance movement and a semi-public tovarick community.

Notable non-signatories of the Declarations included New Provence (at the time probably the most Vetomund-oriented polity in Mendeva) and Persia.

In some respects the Declarations were prefigured by the waning of the censorious and moralistic Modest Arrangement. They laid the groundwork for the Household Renovation of the late nineteenth century, which comprised further shifts in social attitudes around family and marriage. The fact of such major international collaboration in writing the Declarations also prefigured the dawn of the subsequent decades' Good Game period, characterised by alliances and espionage.

Laic adoption would go on to spread globally; for example, in Gulf Mendeva the persistent social prestige of Britain is thought by some to have expedited the contemporary social changes of the region.

The hundred-and-twentieth anniversary of the Laic Declarations was celebrated in academia with the Long Future project, including the synchronised publication of dozens of popular works chronicling the Declarations' origins, course and aftermath.

References

Conjugation, Operation e Diabolica in Musica: The Early Deviance Movement, 1970 work by Borlish popular historian Catrin VeΟßount

The Deviance Incorporation of Folk Tales, 1970 sociological work by Dr Tanasio Capretti

##

86.1: Letter to the Editor

excerpt in translation from the collected correspondence of MichΟΠl Bervisson (17th June 1799βÄ™12th January 1849), political historian and social reformist. This exceprt is from a letter to his editors concerning proposed alterations to his magnum opus Roma ΟÜternal: EΟß Vascel a These (An Eternal Rome: Ships of Theseus).

Dovem, m'ajoutabr vel improbation redirigir tan com sera parcanΟß nos prevenibr (cos particuler l'un bougr aquilin nolent my plum teurfiar) eΟΑ y ci argument refutar cos direct ny scrit.
Second, I would like to divert as much criticism as we might possibly anticipate (in particular one aquiline fellow who shall not sully my quill) and rebut those arguments directly in the text.

Nell'oc spien, posseu nos y capparol Caf Jammel prolongar por caglou irrayar a comoscon l'Empir Constant drengoΟßan all'ovest eΟΑ y Roman malchassian all'aust y dou issaroirn y Cligs (tras y siecr derran yon uniΟΑ) por lorry pretension vars continuitaΟΑ con Rom basfiar?
To this end, might we extend the chapter Twin Keys to emphasise the ways both the Drengotian Constant Empire to the west and the Malchassian Romans to the east used the Church (in the last century of its unity) to support their claims to continuity with Rome?

Jo vil aΟ·r toΟΑ y fougl recas pre Reveglon, jo vos aiΟß.
I shall have written the requisite pages by Revillon, I assure you.

VacilleΟΑ bogr pre respondr jug; Scholer MichΟΠl Bervisson.
Do not hesitate to reply forthwith; Professor MichΟΠl Bervisson.

86.2: improbar "disapprove of"

improbar /Υ¨…Σm.proΥàbar/ [Υ¨…Σm.p Ä äΥàb…ëΥê]
- disapprove of, condemn, blame, to think badly of someone for their actions;
- criticise, critique, find fault with, censure, to enumerate things one disapproves of;
- scold, rebuke, reprove, tell off, to speak harshly to due to disapproval of someone's behaviour

Etymology: backformation from improbation "criticism, critique, condemnation, censure, blame, disapproval" (originally in legal use for "official reprimand for immorality"), from synonymous improbΡ¹ti≈ç in Latin.

Y maΟΑr improbau sell'heir posc l'ig fo reuluΟΑ.
/i ΥàmaΟΑrΧ© Υ¨…Σm.proΥàbo s…¦Υàlir p…îx laj fo rawΥàl…ΣΈΗ/
[i ΥàmaΟΑβÄΩΥ¨ Ä…Σm.p Ä äΥàbo s…ΣΥàli.j…ê p…îx laj fo  Ä…êwΥàl…Σh]
def mother scold-pst 3s.gen=son after def-s.dt be.pst come.back-p.pst
The mother scolded her son when he got back.

##

86.3: hapr "groceries"

hapr /Υàha.prΧ©/ [Υàha.p…ê]
- groceries, produce, commodities, shop-bought foodstuffs and sometimes other household supplies;
- (loosely) shopping, recently-bought goods that are relatively inexpensive and characteristic of daily or weekly purchases;
- (strictly) fruit and veg, goods which may be bought at a greengrocer's

Etymology: direct descendant of Old Borlish happle "fruit, collectively; yield of a fruit harvest" (since displaced by native frugt < Latin fr≈Ϊctus), borrowed from Old English ΟΠppel "apple, fruit; sphere". The appearance of the initial h- is unexplained, but may be a hypercorrection; at this point /h/ was absent in native Borlish vocabulary and was being reintroduced via Germanic loanwords. Its modern sense is influenced by the compound haprman "greengrocer; (later) wholesaler".

J'eye sauf a aΟ·r y hapr yent.
/ £iΥàje sof a …¦Υàj…Σr i Υàha.prΧ© Υàj…¦nt/
[ ù…ΣΥàje sof a …ΣΥà ù…ΣΥêβÄΩ Äi Υàha.p…ê Υà ù…¦nt]
1s=go-ipf except to have-inf def groceries get.p.pst
I'd nearly got all the groceries.

##

86.4: bougr "fellow"

bougr /Υàbu.grΧ©/ [Υàbu.g…ê]
- fellow, chap, cove, bloke, guy, a man and especially an ordinary man of the street;
- (archaic) scoundrel, rogue, cad, blackguard, someone (usually a man) without honour or virtue

Etymology: only recently (early twentieth century) ameliorated from an insult, via Middle Boral bougr "villain, criminal, enemy; pagan, apostate, non-believer" from Old Boral bougre "heretic, evildoer". This reflects Medieval Latin Bulgarus "from Bulgaria", from Old Slavonic, and was probably introduced via contemporary French legal codes.

Un bougr insuet vien de laurar cas y haprman.
/…Σn Υàbu.grΧ© …ΣnΥàsj…¦t vjen de loΥàrar kaz i Υ¨ha.prΧ©Υàman/
[…Σm ΥàbugβÄΩ Ä…ΣnΥàsj…¦t v ù…¦n de l äΥà Ä…ëΥê kaz i Υ¨ha.p…êΥàman]
indf guy novice come of work-inf wrt def grocer
A new guy's just started at the grocer's.

##

86.5: Bavarn

Bavarn (BΟ≥arn /ΥàpoΥê.…ëΥên/) is a centrally-located German polity of Europe. Among others, it borders Bohemia, Burgundy, Furlaine, Saxony, and Willemy.

History

During the Second Tetrarchy, the Kingdom of Bavarn was one of the two main players in the German Empire; the Empire was for some time ruled jointly by the Houghbrocken and Andex dynasties of Saxony and Bavarn respectively.

Thirteenth-century Bavarn was the epicentre of the Pauline Denunciations and the German Secession, leading to the establishment of the Augsburg See. The concomitant strife saw each vassal in Bavarn declare either for Rome or for Augsburg, in a powderkeg patchwork of allegiances that would collapse the Empire entirely. Bavarn itself would never regain as much Adriatic coast as it had enjoyed just prior to these events.

In the fifteenth century, Bavarn was embroiled in an extended series of conflicts with Burgundy. By this time it had also become a technological centre, with workshops developing entirely new glassmaking techniques.

Following the Blue Trans-Willem War of the late seventeenth century, Bavarn gained significant territory from the Kingdom of Burgundy, which subsequently dissolved. As result, then-king Frederick created the Duchy of Far Suebia, ruled from Burgundy's former capital Geneva.

In the eighteenth century, famine and a shortage of grain led to popular unrest that threatened to destabilise the state; the examples of Venise and Crovatia to the south demonstrated how badly things could go for the current regime.

The Second German War embroiled Bavarn, allied with (among others) Borland and the Northern Concert; a particularly significant event during this war was the disastrously mismanaged Battle of Hulm in 1821.

People

Monarchs

Conrad II (fl. 1120), who ruled from Regensburg

Adalmar (fl. c12), who was succeeded by Empress Katharina of Germany, the last ruler of a united Germany for over five hundred years

Frederick (fl. c17), who created the Duchy of Far Suebia

Nobility

Elias Villingen, second Duke of Far Suebia

Others

Wilhelm Bruez (fl. 1225), mayor of Augsburg

Paul von Burchardt (fl. 1225), writer of the Pauline Denunciations and instigator of the German Secession

Marian Siegert ~ Miller (1725-1756), illegitimate daughter of Elias Villingen

##

87.1: Deviance Exam

scitation questions in translation from the 2007 course Religion in the Second Millenium: Belief, Community and Division, set by the  University Faculty of Domain History.

(ii) Nell'istr Albion eΟΑ y reyaum Danau y dou temporane sceyen mendranΟß moulous cas religion eΟΑ y Cligs (e lorry gendr calscon) stan ligaΟΑ cos starc accougl con autortaΟΑ vecin. Desig l'eΟß qualtaΟΑ commun ben ceter denoirn il amaΟßgat ΟΑescant por cogtaΟßon Contr-Paradis?
(ii) In both Albion and the Danaw kingdom at this time there were significant religious minorities and the Church (of whichever stripe) was strongly associated with local authority. Was it these commonalities that made them fertile ground for Anti-Paradise thought?

(iii) DiΟßr alcot sta y resmargenΟß dell'ApprestanΟß Modest ne Spagn e Gaul com effeit dell'eΟß novel idea religious? CompareΟΑ y farvenΟß de cuidour modest ig inclus y Marchis de Caillat ben Diana Theodora Queiroz y pro-Marian Azur contr y philosoph de commouΟΑ preluΟ·scent cosvour a Modest.
(iii) To what extent was the resurgence of the Modest Arrangement in Spain and Gaul in reaction to these new religious ideas? Compare the fervence of Modest thinkers like the Marquis de Caillat or proto-Blue Marian Diana Theodora Queiroz to the philosophers of earlier Modest-like movements.

(iv) Lonc vossell'opinion, cu scavoy (ben scavoirn) cos incaindessem l'edition incendier d'an 1799 Et Nous Amerons les Fux (E Nos Amareu ci Foy), un ouvr tarromour cas Theology a DesveyanΟß? TesmogneΟΑ vossell'apponment con poiΟΑ modern e temporane, e hasmeΟΑ dou ben may autr ouΟßon cas plausibiltaΟΑ.
(iv) In your opinion, who is, or are, the most likely author(s) of the incendiary 1799 publication Et Nous Amerons les Fux (And We Will Love the Fire), a seminal work in Deviance Theology? Back up your theory with modern and contemporary sources, and contrast the likelihood of at least two other candidates.

87.2: teurfiar "sully"

teurfiar /tawrΥàfjar/ [Υ¨ta.w…êΥàfj…ëΥê]
- tar, blacken, to coat with tar or otherwise cause to become black;
- soil, stain, dirty, sully, to make something unclean or less pure (physically or morally);
- defame, tarnish, smear, besmirch, slander, to damage the reputation of

Etymology: Middle Boral derivation from noun teur "tar" with the suffix -fiar (< Latin -fic≈ç) which forms verbs meaning "to make/render _". The noun teur is an Old Boral borrowing from Old English teoru "tar, resin, earwax", which is related to "tree". Almost immediately we see use of the verb, particularly in contemporary legal cases.

87.3: amaΟßgat ΟΑescant "breeding ground"

The noun amaΟßgat "nursery, childcare" is borrowed via Mostara amazgato "flowerbed; nursery" from Morrove Arabic ΊßΌéΌ³Ό£ΌÖΌéΊ≥Ό£Ό²ΌéΌâβÄé <al-mΟΓsqa> "seedbed". ##

87.5: Merch

Merch is an English language of the German family of languages which evolved on the island of Albion. It is closely related to other English languages like Kentish and Wessern, and is the primary language of the polity of Markland.

Contemporary Merch descends form the Early Merch spoken in Markland, which itself descends from Anglian varieties of Old English.

This article principally describes the acrolect of Merch spoken in and around the capital city of Tommarth.

Phonology
Consonants
The consonant phonemes found in Merch are:
/m n/
/p t t É k/
/b d d £ g/
/f ΈΗ s  É h/
/v ΟΑ z/
/w l …Ψ j/
and they are subject to the following orthographic conventions:

b d f h k l m n p t v w z represent their IPA symbols.
j r y represent /d £ …Ψ j/ respectively.
c usually represents /s/ before i e y and /k/ elsewhere.
awice /…ôΥàw…ôis/ "indeed" < OE ΡΓewiss "certain"
coholl /k äΥàh…îl/ "sulfide" < Ar ΌÉΌèΊ≠Ό£Ό³ "kohl"
g usually represents /g/ but may represent /d £/ before i e y.
eagre /ΥàeΥê.g…ô/ "acid" < L Ρ¹cer
nargill /ΥànaΥê.d £…Σl/ "coconut" < Ar ΌÜΌéΊ±Ό£Ί§ΌêΌäΌ³ "coconut palm"
girl /geΥêl/ "young person"
q appears almost always in the diagraph qu to represent /kw/.
quoit /kw…ôit/ "tyre, torus"
s represents /z/ between vowels and word-finally (unless doubled), and /s/ otherwise.
Brasil /b…Ψ…ôΥàz…Σl/ "Newfoundland" < Ir Breasail
betimes /b…ΣΥàt…ôimz/ "earlier"
surblavick /soΥêΥàbl…¦Υê.v…Σk/ "ultraviolet" < L blavus "blue"
x represents /gz/ between vowels, and /ks/ otherwise.
- oxy /Υà…îg.ziΥê/ "stubborn"
- ≈™culux /Υà…¦.kj…ôΥ¨l äks/ "EM radiation" < Gr ΈΩαΦΕΈΚΈΩœ² "house", L lux "light"
There are several digraphs of letters with -h: ch gh ph sh th wh.
ch usually represents /t É/, but sometimes also /k/.
chalk /t Éalk/ "chalk"
yacht /jakt/ "cult" < Du jaght "hunting party"
gh usually represents either / É/ (following i) or /f/ (following u).
wight /w…Σ Ét/ "person" < OE wiht "thing, creature"
ough /uΥêf/ "bad" < OE w≈çh "wrong, crooked"
NB I'ght /Υà…ôift/ "I must", contracted from I ought
ph represents /f/.
aphlox /Υàa.fl…îks/ "carbon dioxide" < Gr Έ§- "without" and œÜΈΜœ¨ΈΨ "flame"
sh represents / É/.
shaken / É…¦Υêk…ô/ "to shake"
th usually represents either of /ΈΗ ΟΑ/, and sometimes /t/.
thrift /ΈΗ…Ψ…Σft/ "energy" < ON ΟΨrift "prosperity"
yeathre /ΥàjeΥêΟΑ…ô/ "together" < OE geador
wh represents /f/.
- white /f…ôit/ "white"
There are several situations in which written consonants may be silent.
In certain endings: participle -end /…ôn/, infinitive -en /…ô/, superlative -est /…ôs/.
discurrend /d…ΣΥàsk ä.…Ψ…ôn/ "different" < L discurrΡ™ns "roaming"
chammen /Υàt Éa.m…ô/ "to chew" (poss. onomatopoetic)
tharvest /ΥàΈΗaΥê.v…ôs/ "least sociable" < OE ΟΨeorf "unleavened"
Coda r lengthens the preceding vowel and word-final -re is pronounced /…ô/.
interpel /…ΣnΥàt…¦Υê.p…ôl/ "interact with" < L interpell≈ç "I disturb"
sundre /Υàs än.d…ô/ "many" < OE sundor "separately"
Post-tonic -st- immediately before a closed syllable is pronounced /s/.
hirstend /ΥàheΥê.s…ôn/ "extra" < OE hyrstan "to ornament"
pistol /Υàp…Σ.s…ôl/ "message" < L epistola
Likewise, post-tonic -v- is lost in some common words.
morovre /m äΥà…ΨoΥê(…ô)/ "as well"
evre /…¦Υê(…ô)/ "always"
Vowels
The vowels of (this variety of) Merch comprise the short vowels:
/a …¦ …Σ …î  ä …ô/
the long vowels:
/aΥê …¦Υê eΥê iΥê …îΥê oΥê  âΥê/
and two diphthongs:
/…ôi …ôu/
which are subject to the following orthographic conventions:

In stressed syllables, "short" a e i~y o u represent /a …¦ …Σ …î  ä/ (noting that schwa cannot be stressed).
samn /samn/ "set, collection" < OE samnian "gather"
whelp /f…¦lp/ "baby animal"
quick /kw…Σk/ "awake"
thon /ΟΑ…în/ "one, someone", contracted from the one
bulk /b älk/ "cargo"
In unstressed syllables, a e i~y o u instead represent /…ô …ô~…Σ …Σ  ä  ä/βÄîexcept word-finally, where /…Σ  ä/ become /iΥê …ô/.
defectend /d…ΣΥàf…¦k.t…ôn/ "positively charged"
behemoth /b…ΣΥàhiΥê.m äΈΗ/ "hippopotamus"
costumery /k äΥàstj…ôu.m…ô.…ΨiΥê/ "fashion plate"
"Long" a e i~y o u represent /…¦Υê iΥê …ôi uΥê j…ôu/.
- besake /b…ΣΥàz…¦Υêk/ "because"
- mete /miΥêt/ "standard, gauge" < OE metan "measure"
- ty /t…ôi/ "to join"
- poke /puΥêk/ "container" < OFr poque "bag"
- huge /hj…ôud £/ "huge"
There are plenty of digraphs representing vowels.
Coda -r: ar er ir or ur represent /aΥê …¦Υê eΥê oΥê oΥê/.
davarn /d…ôΥàvaΥên/ "grand hotel, resort" < W tafarn "inn"
-a: ea oa represent /eΥê oΥê/.
roaden /Υà…ΨoΥê.d…ô/ "to travel"
-e: ee ie ue represent /iΥê …ôi j…ôu/.
conspue /k änΥàspj…ôu/ "to deride" < L c≈çnspu≈ç "I spit upon"
-i/y: ai ei oi and ay ey oy represent /…ôi …ôi~iΥê …ôi/.
fain /f…ôin/ "willing(ly)"
-o: oo represents /uΥê/.
soon /suΥên/ "as soon as"
-u/w: au eu ou and aw ew ow represent /…ôu …ôu …ôu~uΥê/.
blew /bl…ôu/ "blue"
coshow /Υàk…î. ÉuΥê/ "rubber" < Quechua kawchu
Examples
A jalicked knave bought a new quistre.
/…ô Υàd £a.l…Σ.k…ôd kn…¦Υêv b…îft …ô n…ôu Υàkw…Σ.st…ô/
A guy in a tuxedo bought a new phone.

Aquifex is the temendest corporal.
/Υàa.kw…ΣΥ¨f…¦ks …Σz ΟΑe ΥàtiΥê.m…ôn.d…ôs ΥàkoΥê.p ä.…Ψ…ôl/
Hydrogen is the most abundant element.

Do thy breches of and put thysen abed!
/duΥê ΟΑ…ôi Υàb…ΨiΥê.t É…ôz …îf …ôn p ät ΟΑ…ôiΥàs…¦n …ôΥàb…¦d/
Take off your clothes and get in the bed!

By way o'dreend al therin ough, he asked "As swich?"
/b…ôi w…ôi …ôΥàd…ΨiΥê.…ônβÄΩdal ΟΑ…ôΥà…Ψ…Σn uΥêf | hiΥê Υàa.sk…ôd | az sw…Σt É/
While doing everything wrong, he asked "Like that?"

Psolick tersures may breken wines brewing of.
/Υàs…î.l…Σk Υàt…¦Υê.zj…ôz m…ôi Υàb…ΨiΥêk…ô w…ôinz Υàb…Ψ…ôw…Σ≈΄g …îf/
Sulphites can stop wine fermentation.

88.2: huyaΟß "organised crime"

huyaΟß /haΥàjats/ [h…êΥà ùas]
- organised crime, mob activity, groups carrying out illegal acts seen as a collective, or the illegal acts themselves;
- corruption, unethical administrative or executive practices in government or business

Etymology: nineteenth-century coinage originating in Pentrose slang, a derivative of local dialect term huy "gang, mob, syndicate" with the pejorative suffix -aΟß < Latin -Ρ¹ceus "resembling". The noun huy goes back to the fifteenth century, and is a borrowing from Kernish chui "crowd, rabble" (cf. Cambrick why "movement").

Y madarc yeme lucr de huyaΟß.
/i maΥàdark jeΥàme ΥàlikrΧ© de haΥàjats/

88.3: vrugheΟß "moorland"

vrugheΟß /vrajΥàets/ [v Ä…êΥà ù…¦s]
- heath, heathland, moor, moorland, a tract of level uncultivated land with sandy soil and scrubby vegetation;
- waste, wasteland, badlands, a place with no remaining resources or metaphorically any uninteresting place

Etymology: descendant of Old Boral vru»ùeΟß "heath, moor", which is a nominal derivation of vru»ù "heather" (modern vrug) with the suffix -eΟß < Latin -itia. The word vru»ù is borrowed from Borland Kelt vr≈Ϊg "heather", cognate with synonymous frΟΓgh in Irish.

Nos miraum y horam d'un vrugheΟß laΟΑ.
/n…îz miΥàrom i hoΥàram d…Σn vraΥàj…¦ts laΈΗ/
[noz m…ΣΥà Äom i h äΥà Äam d…Σ…± v Ä…êΥà ù…¦s laΈΗ]
1p look.at-pst.1p def panorama of=indf moor broad

88.4: desveyar "deviate"

desveyar /Υ¨d…¦s.viΥàjar/ [Υ¨d…¦.zv…ΣΥà ù…ëΥê]
- swerve, veer, yaw, to go out of a straight line of motion;
- deflect, deviate, detour, turn aside, to change course (or metaphorically, plans);
- dodge, sidestep, to move out of the way of something

Etymology: from Old Boral desveyar "stray, deviate, go off course", inherited from Late Latin disvi≈ç "I leave the path", a variant of synonymous devi≈ç in Classical Latin. Alternatively, a later formation from Old Boral vey "path, course" < Latin via "road" (compare modern veyaΟß "trip, journey").

Nos stou desveyar por y tragres eluΟ·r.
/n…îz stu Υ¨d…¦s.viΥàjar p…îr i traΥàgr…¦z Υ¨e.lajΥà…Σr/
[n…îz stu Υ¨d…¦.zv…ΣΥà ù…ëΥê p…îΥêβÄΩ Äi t Ä…êΥàg Ä…¦z Υ¨e.l…êΥà ù…ΣΥê]
1p must veer-inf for def parade elude-inf
We'll detour to avoid the parade.

##

88.5: Convoy Australier

The Convoy Australier (Merch /Υàk…în.v…ôi …ôΥàst…Ψ…¦Υê.liΥê…ô/), officially the Order of Saint Hemma of Still Water, is a Christian organisation aligned with Rome which emerged in the Second Tetrarchy period. It has undergone several major transformations of purpose since its inception.

History
The Convoy began in the eleventh century in the Duchy of Crain, a member of the German Empire subordinate to Bavarn. In the first decades of its existence the Convoy was in essence a (quite literally) glorified band of mercenaries.

The Convoy was heavily involved in the first crusades and related ventures, from the Principality of Mystra in Greece to the then-unsuccessful forays in Jerusalem. It was appointed to administrate the new states of Antioch and Port Simon soon after their conquests in the twelfth century.

Pope Just revoked the Convoy's rulership of Antioch and Port Simon in 1208 as the culmination of a escalating inter-faction struggle. However, it is generally agreed that this act saved the Convoy a great amount of trouble in the long run (given the imminent disaster of the Failed Crusade). The Convoy was subsequently assigned to the courts of Svonimir of Crovatia and Taxon of Hungary; it was thus ideally placed to lead the Baptism of the North, even as the troubles of the German Secession loomed.

The German Secession increased the urgency of the Baptism of the North; Rome feared that Augsburg might spread its new heresy faster than Rome could reach the North. As a consequence, the Convoy grew substantially in numbers and wealth during the thirteenth century, and outlying chapters were founded as far afield as Borland and (eventually) Lithow [Lithuania].

##

89.1: Four Miles to Freedom

excerpt in translation from 2007 work Four Miles to Freedom (originally released in Borlish as Catr Lau a LeurtaΟΑ), written by Pentrose-based author Matheu Smodarac. Set in the Pentrose of the early twentieth century, it was praised for its depiction of the city recovering from the ravages of the Millstone War.

Lampionment baric n'aye presenΟß deja caut lonc y picq routel yon trovaΟΑ ern y cambr pijon d'Ivocq, clignaΟΑ com annex denover entr un livry a tom rediveu eΟΑ y tarn svelt Y Dou Ollem faΟßadant vars Rout VeΟΑr Foris.
Electric streetlight had not yet reached the little alleyway on which Ivock's boarding-house stood, squeezed like an afterthought between a second-hand bookshop and the narrow Two Elms pub which faced Old Foris Street.

I quaucau sortent sull'yeul ovart, y stanc passant dessou dell'ig lumner aurfiaΟΑ de lamp y cercondment bagnant vars un caldour monochrom.
He hurried out onto the thoroughfare, walking through the pools of that golden lamplight which bathed the surroundings in warm monochrome.

Y peyan ja vat de ploy ern lousc jaint tandic l'eye tras y rout e desveyau theatrfiour arreloΟΑeΟßaΟΑ exeunt faint des y Paviglon e colant vars y quarf souterran y proismessem.
The still-wet cobbles glinted as he crossed the road and dodged theatregoers in their best clothes exiting the Pavilion and streaming to the nearest dusker station.

Con tan cous moglastr l'octobr stan preuΟß contr y saΟßon, eΟΑ Ivocq remorde l'ouΟßon de sy dorangot gras pres toΟΑ pront. 
Despite the damp it was unseasonably warm for October, and Ivock regretted opting for his heavy overcoat almost at once.

89.2: yeul ovart "thoroughfare"

yeul ovart /jawl oΥàvart/
- thoroughfare, highway, main street, a major road especially conceived as connecting two towns or district
- (computing) a high-speed network connection

Etymology: collocation first seen in the sixteenth century, literally meaning "open street". The adjective ovart "open, public" descends from Vulgar Latin opertus, alteration of Classical apertus "open". The noun yeul "street, wide road" reflects Old Borlish ieol "unpaved road" (as opposed to vei "paved road"), which given Welsh heol "route, passage" is presumably of Borland Kelt origin. In the latter sense attested from 1977.

Y davarn faΟßada y jogntur de dou yeul ovart.
/i daΥàvarn Υ¨fa.tsaΥàda i  £…îinΥàt…Σr de du jaul oΥàvart/
[i d…êΥàv…ëΥên Υ¨fa.ts…êΥàda i  ù äinΥàt…ΣΥê de du  ùawl  äΥàv…ëΥêt]
def hotel face-ipf def junction of two street open
The hotel was at the intersection of two main roads.

89.3: annex denover "afterthought"

annex denover /aΥàn…¦ks Υ¨de.noΥàv…¦r/ […êΥàn…¦ks Υ¨de.n äΥàv…¦Υê]
- afterthought, an action or reflection done after the fact;
- something done at the last-minute or in an improvised manner, like a correction, addition or improvement

Etymology: idiom attested from the nineteenth century, literally "final-stage addition". The noun annex "supplement, appendix, addition, boost, enhancement, refinement" is a deverbal from verb annexar "to supplement, append, boost, refine", which comes via Scholastic Latin annex≈ç from Classical annexus "attachment".

The adjective denover is a contemporary derivation from denou "conclusion, ending, denouement; the final stage of any process", itself also a deverbal from denovar "untie, unravel; end". This is an Old Borlish derivation from nou "know, tangle", from the synonymous n≈çdus in Latin.

L'oc sorty enter sta tor annex denover.
/l…îk s…îrΥàti …¦nΥàt…¦r sta t…îr aΥàn…¦ks Υ¨de.noΥàv…¦r/
[l…î s äΥêΥàti …ΣnΥàt…¦Υê sta t…îΥêβÄΩ Ä…êΥàn…¦ks Υ¨de.n äΥàv…¦Υê]
def-s.px party whole be.ipf like addition ending
This whole party was sort of last-minute.

89.4: sveldr "whittle"

sveldr /Υàsv…¦l.drΧ©/ [Υàzv…¦l.d…ê]
- pluck, to remove the feathers from a bird;
- whittle, carve, to cut or shave a piece of wood with a knife;
- reduce, thin, cut away at, to gradually make something smaller or less numerous;
- winnow, to separate out the best members or items of a group and remove the rest;
- (colloquial) rob, swindle, con, cheat, to deprive of property by deceit or force

Etymology: from Old Borlish isveldre "to pluck, remove parts of", a pleonastic derivative of a presumed *veldre descending directly from Latin vell≈ç "I pluck, depilate, demolish". Extension to woodworking is attested by the thirteenth century, in abstract use it is seen from the fifteenth, and the colloquial sense first arose in the late eighteenth before a revival (or re-coining) in the twentieth.

Nos stou y ΟΑanc sveldr prell'eicon zar.
/n…îz stu i ΟΑank Υàsv…¦l.drΧ© Υ¨pr…¦.liΥàk…în zar/
/n äΥàstu i ΟΑa≈΄k Υàzv…¦l.d…ê Υ¨p Ä…¦.l…ΣΥàk…în z…ëΥê/
1p be.needed def mob whittle-inf before-def=sport serious
We need to thin out the crowd before the real competition.

##

89.5: Second Tetrarchy

The Second Tetrarchy, in older works also called the Medieval Imperia, refers to the period of roughly two centuries at the dawn of second-millennium Europe when a good deal of the land was (at least nominally) under the jurisdiction of four empires: the First Drengot Empire, the German Empire, the Second Roman Empire, and the Single Caliphate.

The name "Second Tetrarchy" was first used in the eighteenth century by the writings of Vascon historian David BensaΟ≠d, in reference to the Roman Tetrarchy of the early fourth century, although the two political regimes had little else in common.

The First Drengot Empire began with the Kernovan Conjuring of 1028, when Normandy conquered Kent with the support of Greater Devon. Over the next years House Drengot went on to consolidate lands from the Middlesea to Edinbrough.

This unity endured until the Emergency at Dijon in 1170, when the Kingdom of Burgundy ceased to be a Drengot vassal; the empire was formally dissolved in 1191 and divided among the heirs and allies of Daniel IV.

The German Empire descended from the eastern half of the expansive Kingdom of the Franks after its scission in the ninth century, combining the Eastern Franks with Willemy. In its first centuries it expanded east and south; the kingdoms of Saxony and Bavarn became the empire's preeminent vassals, with their Andex and Houghbrocken dynasties ruling in turn.

By the end of the twelfth century, the northern cities were developing into the mercantile Stadbund and away from imperial authority. The slow erosion of the German Empire accelerated rapidly during the German Secession of the thirteenth century, and by the end of that century the fiction of a surviving German Empire was laid to rest with the corpse of the first Kingdom of Saxony.

The Second Roman Empire had existed since the (short-lived) reconquest of Rome from the Eastern Goths by Justinian I in 536; it would persist in some form for over a millennium. The eleventh and twelfth centuries did see the empire reach a territorial maximum, controlling territory from Corsica in the west to Derban on the Hazaran Sea in the east.

It was only in the late seventeenth century that the Second Roman Empire ceased to exist in any meaningful sense (despite several periods of only nominal continuity), due to a succession crisis and a series of dramatic civil wars.

The Single Caliphate stretched from Spain to Persia, and was the product of several centralisation reforms from Cairo consolidating authority over the various successor states to the Abbasid Caliphate (although the further territories such as Cordova or the Turkish lands were never as closely-ruled).

90.1: London in the "Good Game" period

question and marking advice in translation from the Dijon New School 2019 Concurrence History scitation paper "Family, Tradition and Language: the Changing Culture of the Good Game Decades"

Descorrir sull'ort dy Magl Urban Novel e sur lorry sousunΟßon ny Collujon, ant caglou irrayant all'aΟΑoinΟß de Londr lonc y traiscnant dell'epoc d'Atelier Mondial.
Discuss the rise of the New Urban Mesh and their subsumption into the Collusion, with emphasis on the growth of London following the Global Workshop period. 

Cos tranquessem, comparaΟßon fair dy racont de lorry benoΟß de spionnaΟß all'Aust por gournment dirigir lonc asmuth san cas commarΟß con y realtaΟΑ plausibr.

Valour dar ag postion d'emphas sur y droug d'orΟΑenjanΟß marin e cos particuler sur y cavibiltaΟΑ de carbon por y commarΟß Urban basfiar.
Give credit for emphasising the role of maritime technology and especially access to coal in supporting the Urban trade.

Respos carr ci mostrant ig y stuΟΑant es eiΟΑ doutr a for y lasc d'an 1968 "Sr Desarden, PrinΟß NuΟßal" sgardar; ig l'es aglon suet ag mesur dy seucq de James Desarden nell'Adminstration Muncever cos equal pre sell'ascreuΟßon a Nacon Day e posc y scandal d'an 1887 y dou.

90.2: zar "serious"

zar /zar/ [z…ëΥê]
- serious, committed, for keeps, risky, undertaken with something staked on the outcome;
- purposeful, deliberate, (of an act) not done lightly but instead with full regard of the possible consequences;
- meaningful, high-stakes, dramatic, tense, (of a narrative situation) there being a lot on the line that might be lost or not achieved;
- competitive, heated, rivalrous, antagonistic, (of a game) characterised by a strong desire to win on both sides or even of outright hostility;
- cutthroat, dog-eat-dog, with a ruthless focus on personal gain

also cair zar "to matter, be important, have consequences"

Etymology: from sixteenth-century zar "risky, high-stakes", transferred from the name of a gambling dice game. This comes via Napolitan zara from Arabic Ί≤ΌéΌ΅Ό£Ί± (zahr) "dice". Doublet of hasart "peril, danger", which comes via French. Used exclusively of games and competitions until the nineteenth century, when the idiom with cair is first attested.

"Cay zar!" dis jo cant corrau nos.
/ke zar | d…Σz  £o kant k…îΥàro n…îz/
[ke z…ëΥê | d…ΣΥà ùo kan k äΥà Äo n…îz]
fall serious | say.pst 1s when run-ipf-1p 1p
"It's not a game!" I said as we ran.

dorangot /Υ¨do.ranΥàg…ît/ [Υ¨do. Ä…ê≈΄Υàg…ît]

Etymology: from the eighteenth century, borrowed from Paris French dourangote "riding coat, overcoat". This is adapted from the now-obsolete Kentish term Durham-coat, named for the city in Northumber.

90.4: star luΟ·scent a "have to do with"

star luΟ·scent a /star Υ¨laj.iΥàx…¦nt a/ [st…ëΥê Υ¨la.j…ΣΥàΟß…¦nβÄΩta]
- have to do with, be related to, be relevant to, to have any bearing on (some person, topic, etc)

Etymology: idiom first attested in the fifteenth century, literally "to be playing at" and likely derived via the sense "to be participating in (some activity)". The participle luΟ·scent "playing" comes from luΟ·r "to play", which descends from a late Insular Latin l≈ΪdΡΪre, l≈ΪdΡ™scere "to play". These are both conjugational regularisations of l≈Ϊdere in Classical Latin. See also the following idiom involving this construction:

Com es oc luΟ·scent a ney ne Nivon? "What's that got to do with anything?"

...ney ne Nivon /ni ne niΥàv…în/ [ni ne n…ΣΥàv…în]
- general interjection in response to a perceived non sequitur, cf. English "What's that got to do with the price of fish?"

Etymology: literally "What does this have to do with snow in Japan?", first used in the nineteenth century and of obscure motivation; perhaps just in reference to the unlikelihood of snow in Japan affecting anything in Borland. The noun ney "snow" reflects Latin nix, nivem "snow", while the placename Nivon "Japan" is a sixteenth-century borrowing eventually from φ½Ξφ€§, contemporaneously pronounced /niΥà…Ηon/.

My commission hander no's bogr luΟ·scent all'occijon.
/mi Υ¨k…î.miΥàsj…în hanΥàd…¦r n…îz b…îjrΧ© Υ¨laj.iΥàx…¦nt aΥ¨l…îk.siΥà £…în/
[mi Υ¨k…î.m…ΣΥàsj…în h…ênΥàd…¦Υê n…îz b…îj…ê Υ¨la.j…ΣΥàΟß…¦nβÄΩt…êΥ¨l…îk.s…ΣΥà ù…în]
1s.gen usage sex-adj neg-be at.all play-p.pst at-def=murder
My sex life has nothing to do with the murder.

##

90.5: Sandrine

Sandrine (Merch /Υàsan.d…Ψ…ôin/) is a soluble substance found in citrus and other fruits and vegetables. It is one of the substances essential to mammal life; a severe defect of sandrine is what causes scurvy.

(Do not confuse this substance with mandrine, a deadly poison.)

The curative effects of citrus fruits for scurvy were suspected as early as the fourteenth century. By the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries, with the much longer geoscopic voyages following the Novomundine Landfall of 1471, efforts to prevent scurvy became more active. For example, King Ambrose III of Vascony (the 'Ascendant') instituted the Sandrine Design in 1485 by which ships would be required to stock dried fruits and syrups. This scheme, named for his sister Alexandra and the first European royal to set foot in the Novomund, is where the name sandrine originates.

However, such schemes did not have lasting effects. This was partly due to expense but much more significantly, due to the lack of understanding of the mechanism by which fruit prevented scurvy. Thus, fruit would be preserved in ways which removed their sandrine content (for example, boiling or use of copper vessels) and sailors would suffer scurvy anyway, leading guilds to abandon stocking fruits altogether.

Truly theoretic understanding would not come until the seventeenth century, when it was discovered by chance that the guy-coney can also develop scurvy (humans and guys are rare among animals in not creating sandrine in our bodies from sugar). This allowed for much more rigorous and time-efficient testing on guys, to determine what preparations of fruits and vegetables were effective at preventing scurvy. By the 1700s it had become common practice to stock lemon juice diluted with strong alcohol on long sea voyages.

91.1: Name the Wars

extract in translation from 2006 Borlish trevold EΟß Abat EnonΟßar (Name the Wars in English), which blends elements of the masquira and housebound enigma genres

Sur Segr Hendric Cortis au staΟΑ y cur forniΟΑ por y figl orphan de sy fraΟΑr postiressem candon n'aye BaΟΑot for dou meis eld.

tras annaΟΑ lorry parrinment vars region divers n'Africa e Chrysia abrejant por poΟΑir remanir cas BaΟΑot tras casc staΟΑ candon i repaΟΑre de schol.
for years curtailing their trips to far-flung regions across Africa and Chrysia so that they could be there each summer when he returned from school.

91.4: droug "contribution"

droug /druj/ [d Äuj]
- contribution, participation, the act of helping out in some undertaking;

Nos creim y droug terraun dessur de murΟΑon pavar.

##

91.5: Millstone War

The Millstone War (1897βÄ™1908) refers to the period of global military conflict often considered as the dawn of modern warfare. Though not all of the wars involved spanned the entire eleven years, they are all characterised by a fundamental asymmetry: defensive tactics had outstripped offensive technology, and so front lines worldwide ground to barely-moving trench fortifications.

The Millstone War marks the end of the Good Game period; its aftermath saw a comprehensive realignment of the previous system of alliances.

The last years of the Millstone War saw the first military use of aircraft, as well as air-bombing of cities including Marsella in Provence.

Political tensions rose globally in the decades after the Millstone War, as the inadequacy of the post-war settlements became apparent. In particular, relations between Drengot Collusion and their southern neighbours of Vascony and Provence quickly soured.

---

Honorine Maignard (fl. 1890βÄ™1940) was an author in France active for over fifty years in the early twentieth century. She was the granddaughter of the Lombard diplomat Audace Saviano, who during the Good Game period was instrumental in the formation of the Italian Concert.

During the Millstone War she served in the Second Medic Force of Valois for more than six years.

---

Vincent Margouille (fl. 1960βÄ™1990) was a Norman author and professional lineball player. In 1965 he led the Kent team to its only victory in the Lineball Global Tourney to date. He is the son of VrΟ©queu Margouille, who also played for Kent in the 1935 LGT.

From 1981 on, he authored his best-known series (beginning with Upstairs, Downstairs), which follows the people living in a manorhouse during the Good Game period. Later instalments portray their experiences during the Millstone War.

##

92.1: BarruΟ®c

excerpt in translation from 1997 work BarruΟ®c by Ambrosian author Roger Cusallas.

Y castel es gran. L'es tojorn tars dec Yam Mott parsou ig gent veln le targr, e candon Halinne lour beut bonjorn il faun cortiseΟß e sourey eΟΑ exeunt.
The castle is big. It's always clean because Uncle Mott pays people to clean it, and when Halinne says hello to them they bow and smile and move on.

Parveg ΟΑa i se ren invisibr i vil lou sevir; i n'a alcun pretension vars spionnaΟß ben lorry secret jamay descondr, ma scey uncos consolant ny viscr de collocq entr hom crescuΟΑ.
Sometimes when she's invisible she follows them; she doesn't mean to spy and would never tell their secrets, but there's something soothing about the murmur of adult conversation.

Ny castel luy scey un quil pille yon i poΟΑ ΟΑalleyar dessur com un stelmarin lon meΟΑes un ortegl porrujonnant doutr y tarm.
In the castle she has a soft bed on which she can spread out like a starfish without a toe sticking over the edge.

Tras sy fenestr poΟΑ i y havan eΟΑ y stivadour ag ci quarf veir, dessou alcot aΟΑief por i pos apen y visaΟß la reconnoscr.
From her window she can see the harbour and the people on the docks, so far down she can hardly distinguish the faces.

Cant Halinne sta ivan i poΟΑe gringlar tras sy fenestr e lor cair par pog pie vars arp.
When Halinne was little she could tumble out the window and fall a couple of feet onto grass.

Yam Mott am moneΟΑ, lon digr, ma i nel jamay oc faΟ·r a plaΟß de Halinne. 
Uncle Mott likes money, of course, but he'd never choose it over Halinne.

A sgart calscon i nil jamay deΟ·r l'ig trianΟß commettr, dec Halinne nil ceu nien taussar.
In any case he'll never have to make the choice, because Halinne will hardly cost anything.

92.2: scagl a natur "the way of things"

scagl a natur /x…¦il a naΥàt…Σr/ [Οß…¦il a n…êΥàt…ΣΥê]
- convention, the way of things, elements of society or the wider world considered innate and unchangeable;
- hierarchy, pecking order, a social economic or political system in which people or things are ranked and given corresponding status

Etymology: literally "nature's staircase", derived in the Middle Boral period from the Scholastic Latin scΡ¹la nΡ¹t≈Ϊrae "the Great Chain of Being" (literally, the ladder of nature). This is the theological concept of the hierarchy of all matter and life, thought by medieval Christianity to have been decreed by God, and adapted from similar ideas in Platonic and Aristotelian philosophy.

Often in the phrase step ny scagl a natur "part of the natural order of things, one's place in a hierarchy", where step "step, rung, foothold" is a borrowing from Old English.

Oc voscon accommis va contr y scagl a natur.
/…îk voΥàx…în Υ¨a.k…îΥàm…Σz va Υàk…în.trΧ© i x…¦il a naΥàt…Σr/
[…î v äΥàx…în Υ¨a.k äΥàm…Σz va Υàk…în.t ÄβÄΩi Οß…¦il a n…êΥàt…ΣΥê]
s.px 2p.ins undertake-p.pst go against df ladder at nature
What you've done is against the natural order of things.

92.3: ascreuΟßon "assignment"

ascreuΟßon /Υ¨a.xrawΥàts…în/ [Υ¨a.x Ä…êwΥàts…în]
- assignment, allocation, the bestowal of a role upon someone or something;
- attribution, imputation, credit, the act of assigning someone or something as a cause;
- categorisation, classification, ranking, the process of arranging things into group determined by certain characteristics

Etymology: seventeenth-century noun form derived from ascreir "ascribe, impute, attribute, assign" by analogy with nouns like parceuΟßon "perception" from parcevir "to perceive". The verb reflects Latin ascrΡΪb≈ç "I appoint, enrol, insert, add in writing".

AjoutaΟß alcot a tu dell'ascreuΟßon n'Adighe?
/Υ¨a. £uΥàtats alΥàk…ît a ti d…¦Υ¨la.xrawΥàts…în Υ¨na.daΥàje/
[Υ¨a. ù äΥàtats …êΥàg…ît a ti d…ΣΥ¨la.x Ä…êwΥàts…în Υ¨na.d…êΥà ùe]
enjoyment how.much have 2s of-df=assigment in=Adigh
How are you enjoying your assignment to Adigh?

##

92.4: rediveu "second-hand"

rediveu /Υ¨re.diΥàvaw/ [Υ¨ Äe.d…ΣΥàvaw]
- second-hand, used, (of goods) previously owned by someone else;
- clichΟ©d, hackneyed, unoriginal, repeated often enough to become tiresome;
- trite, cheesy, tropey, (of writing) unreflectively and unskillfully reproducing common narrative elements

Etymology: borrowing from Latin redivΡΪvus "resurrected, second-hand" modelled on vΡΪvus > Middle Borlish veu "living" (now obsolete). Metaphorical extension is seen from the late eighteenth century.

Jo stim y lasc rediveu cos outr.
/ £o st…Σm i lax Υ¨re.diΥàvaw k…îz ΥàutrΧ©/
[ ùo st…Σm i lax Υ¨ Äe.d…ΣΥàvaw k äΥàzu.t…ê]
1s deem df film clichΟ©d adv complete
I find the film totally unoriginal.

##

92.5: Marsella

Marsella (Provincial /masΥàse é…î/; Borlish Marsegl /ma ÄΥàsijl/) is a city on the Middlesea [Mediterranean] and the capital of Provence.

History

A raid by itinerant Norman mercenaries sacked the city of Marsella in 946 N, nominally on behalf of the Malchassian Romans. As it happens, records of this raid contain the first unambiguous mention of identical triplets (Danes born on the isle of Mull around 915) in European history.

In the Second Tetrarchy period of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, Marsella was a major population centre in Burgundy within the First Drengot Empire.

During the Vascon Ascendancy, the region of Provence (including Marsella) was conquered by Vascony. Burgundy's reconquest in 1494 caused so much instability that the region rose up in revolt, sparking the 1497βÄ™99 War of Provincial Independence.

Newly-independent Provence led by Daniel Menton, Lord AmigΟ≤t, established Marsella as its capital. It is generally understood that he wanted to monitor the piratical allies of Barcelon who had ruled the city de facto for some time.

By the turn of the seventeenth century there were multiple weekly newspapers operating from Marsella. None of these remained in print until the modern day; the earliest extant publicationsβÄîsuch as the entertainment and recreation newspaper Fiellas DimenjaβÄîwere incorporated in the nineteenth century.

During the Millstone War in the early twentieth century, Marsella suffered heavy air-bombing as part of the world's first such use of aircraft in wartime.

Today Marsella is famed for its midsummer celebrations which also commemorate the anniversary of its independence from Burgundy.

##

93.1: Leipzig Letter

excerpt in translation from the collected correspondence of Marian Siegert ~ Miller (1725βÄ™56), illegitimate daughter of Elias Villingen, second Duke of Far Suebia.

Tu aye, com tojorn, bon cos enter ne flagitar aΟΑelent lengacer lavaΟß aΟ·r (e cartaΟΑ demay cos enter ne my presenΟß parmettr!).
You were, as ever, entirely correct to insist on extra language tutors (and entirely too generous in letting me attend!).

Y Latin obligaΟΑ a se manifestaΟΑ com vacquer lon kiveuβÄînos se trovau ny mal ΟßaucrβÄîpy y Theudesc ac seyon parolaΟΑ se dien ojous equal com ig dy ci actour flamesc noscon aut posc man a staΟΑ.
The compulsory Latin has revealed itself less than uselessβÄîwe run in the wrong circlesβÄîbut the Dutch they have here is almost as awful as that of those Flemish performers we had some summers ago.

Jo prefay volir habituar, ma interim jo so gavent auvorn demay ne borgogn e bohemian parolant coll'eΟß soiΟß d'Albert.
I expect I will acclimate, but for now I am having far too much fun speaking Burgund and Bohemian with Albert's company.

No scey alcun los ag nonΟßamen noscon caut d'eromn cas pan?
Does the news we get of bread troubles have any basis?

Nos saveu nien confiar declaraΟΑ par y hebdomader lepcian sur viΟΑ ag souΟΑ dy Danau, ma y rumour Venis e Crovatia partenent dan hougr aΟΑief; veleΟΑ enfugr reunt vars Stoccart ΟΑa un sign mendrem bodant ig Bavarn erra vars asmuth parey.
We know not to trust anything the Lepzi weeklies have to say about life south of the Danaw, but the rumours about Venise and Crovatia are bad enough; do abscond back to Stuttgart at the slightest sign Bavarn is along the same track.

93.2: porrujonnar "protrude"

porrujonnar /Υ¨p…î.ri. £…îΥànar/ [Υ¨p…î. Ä…Σ. ù äΥàn…ëΥê]
- protrude, stick out, jut, overhang to extend from a surface or boundary;
- impend, be imminent, to threaten to happen very soon;
- stick one's neck out, go out on a limb, to take a risk putting one in a vulnerable position

Etymology: Middle Borlish verbal derivation of porrujon "balcony, mezzanine" (at the time meaning "overhang, bulge, thrust"). This is from Old Borlish verb p(r)odrure "thrust forward, bulge out", from Latin verb pr≈çtr≈Ϊd≈ç "I push forwards, I protrude". In metaphorical use, seen from the seventeenth century.

Carra je NaΟΑal oc porrujonnant.
/kaΥàra  £e naΥàΟΑal …îk Υ¨p…î.ri. £…îΥànant/
[k…êΥà Äa  ùe n…êΥàΟΑal …î Υ¨p…î. Ä…Σ. ù äΥànant]
fall-fut day.of Christmas s.px protrude-p.prs
Christmas is just round the corner.

93.3: Eden soluΟΑ "a lost Eden"

Eden soluΟΑ /eΥàd…¦n soΥàl…ΣΈΗ/ […ΣΥàd…¦n s äΥàl…Σh]
- lost Eden, Arcadia, Shangri-la, utopia, an imagined paradise of complete bliss and peace conceived of as being located in a distant past

Etymology: literally "an Eden that has been let go", an idiom first attested in Kigman's fifteenth-century poetry. The adjective soluΟΑ is the past participle of souvr "solve, let go, release, launch", which descends directly from the mostly synonymous solv≈ç in Latin.

pog cossirn lorry liesc scholer la un Eden soluΟΑ.
/p…îj k…îΥàs…ΣrnΧ© l…îΥàri lj…¦x skoΥàl…¦r la …Σn eΥàd…¦n soΥàl…ΣΈΗ/

few consider-3p 3p.gen term school-adj there indf Eden let.go-p.pst
Few remember their time at that school fondly.

93.4: ΟΑalleyar "spread-eagle"

ΟΑalleyar /Υ¨ΟΑa.liΥàjar/ [Υ¨ΟΑa.lj…ëΥê]
- spread-eagle, stretch, to lie down with the arms and legs outstretched;
- luxuriate, bask, to enjoy some luxury in the manner of (for example) a sunbather

Etymology: from Middle Borlish ΟΑalleyar "spread-eagle, to stretch taut", extended from its used in tannery to describe leather stretched out on tenterhooks. This reflects Old Borlish ΟΑal»ùar "to be attached to, to be fastened to", directly from synonymous adallig≈ç in Latin.

Dou cat ern ΟΑalleyant sou sol blanc.
/du kat …¦rnΧ© Υ¨ΟΑa.liΥàjant su s…îl blank/
[du kat …¦Υên Υ¨ΟΑaΥàljan su s…îl bla≈΄k]

Two cats were basking in the sunlight.
