93.5: Lexember 2023 - 1: ceu "basically"

ceu /tsaw/ [tsaw]
- basically, essentially, pretty much, in effect, for all intents and purposes, not literally but sufficiently close that any difference is irrelevant;
- like, kinda, sorta, a filler word used to hedge a statement;
- (in negative sentences) hardly, barely, only just, to a minimal extent

Etymology: from Middle Borlish, original a legal term of art used to distinguish an act from a legally distinct act with the same consequences or punishment. In this use it was borrowed from Latin ceu "just like, just as if, as (if) it were". By the eighteenth century it had moved into general use, along with the innovated negative sense (originally restricted to the phrase ceu nien "barely anything, almost nothing").

Remanen ceu alcun toscivr ag platabac.
/Υ¨re.maΥàn…¦n tsaw alΥàk…Σn toΥàx…Σ.vrΧ© …¦j Υ¨pla.taΥàbak/
[Υ¨ ¹e.m…êΥàn…¦n tsaw …êΥàg…Σn t äΥàΟß…ΣvβÄΩ Ä…¦j Υ¨pla.t…êΥàbak]
remain-ipf-3p barely none sandwich at.def sideboard
There were hardly any sandwiches left on the buffet table.

---

Sodrick (Merch /Υàs…î.dr…Σk/, Welsh HeleΟΑ /heΥàl…¦ΟΑ/) is a region of the Northwestern Isles comprising many small islands north of Wales and west of Scotland.

Toponymy
The name Sodrick was originally a demonym for the people of Soder (Old English Sodor), which descends from the Old Norrish suΟΑreyjar "southern islands".

History
Sodrick was settled during the Norrish ascendancy of the eighth and ninth centuries, at the same time as Danes arrived on the shores of Albion and Ireland. Harald the Blue of Sodrick contributed to the establishment of the Kingdom of Scotland in the ninth century. Even the Welsh island of Mondwell was half controlled by Sodrick for some time after a partition agreed between Sodrick and Wynneth that was recorded in both the Ulstre and Carlile chronicles.

During the tenth century, the kingdom of Dunclothe under Roderick invaded and conquered many of the isles of Sodrick from the Danes, including Arran in 934 and (most significantly, as a power centre) the isle of Man in 942. The decline of Dane power in Sodrick has been connected to the decreased efficiency of obtaining wealth by raiding over time.

As Dunclothe took control of Sodrick, many fled or were expelled to places like Dane-ruled York and Ireland, or to relatively prosperous Normandy. Among their number is recorded the first unambiguous mention of identical triplets (Danes born on the isle of Mull around 915) in European history.

By the fifteenth century, Sodrick was subordinate to Wales, and it was incorporated as an outlying tallath of the Kingdom of Britain when it was formed.

- be in freefall, crash, (of figures) to decrease rapidly;

Etymology: Middle Borlish borrowing from French gringueler "to tumble, roll down", itself from Dutch cring "circle, ring".

My trelamn a blad gringlau tout a coup.
/mi treΥàlamnΧ© a blad gr…ΣnΥàglo tut a kup/
[mi t Ä…ΣΥàlam.nβÄΩa blad g Ä…Σ≈΄Υàglo tut a kup]
1s.gen triangle at card tumble-pst all at strike
My house of cards collapsed all at once.

93.7: reunt "back"

reunt /rawnt/ [ Äawnt]
- back, to or in an earlier state or place;
- at odds, contrariwise, in a manner which contradicts something;
- once more, again, over, another time (especially when performing an action twice returns one literally or metaphorically to one's starting point)

Etymology: present participle of reuvr "come back, return, turn over, flip, do again, repeat". This descends via Old Borlish reouvre from Latin revolv≈ç "I unroll, repeat", from volv≈ç "I roll, tumble".

Y jonglour fey l'ig bal yemois reunt.
/i  £…înΥàglur fi laj bal jeΥàm…îiz rawnt/
[i  ù ä≈΄Υàgl äΥê fi laj bal  ù…ΣΥàm…îiz  Äawnt]

94.1: grindr "score"

grindr /Υàgr…Σn.drΧ©/ [Υàg Ä…Σn.d…ê]
- judge, rate, rank, evaluate, to estimate the worth of;
- score, grade, mark, to indicate the correctness of and give a score to (an exam, etc.);
- deliberate, decide between, mull over, weigh, to consider pros and cons in the course of making a decision;
- (slang) date, be dating, to go out and perhaps sleep with multiple people in turn often as a precursor to commencing a steady relationship

Etymology: from Old Borlish grindre, greindr "sift, separate, allocate, rank", which reflects a Vulgar Latin form *grΡ™ni≈ç, grΡ™nere "I sift, sieve, distinguish between". Despite the formal differences, by the semantics we can deduce that this is an altered form of Classical Latin cern≈ç "I sift (etc.)" modelled on the perfect stem (cf. crΡ™vΡΪ "I sifted", crΡ™tus "sifted, separated"). The initial voicing is sporadic, but shared with related noun grivr < cribrum "sieve".

AlcheΟΑr my paΟΑr enΟßau a grindr n'Avril.
/alΥàk…¦.ΟΑrΧ© mi Υàpa.ΟΑrΧ© …¦nΥàtso a Υàgr…Σn.drΧ© naΥàvr…Σl/
[…êΥàg…¦.ΟΑ…ê mi ΥàpaΟΑβÄΩ Ä…ΣnΥàtso a Υàg Ä…Σn.d…ê n…êΥàv Ä…Σl]
anew 1s.gn father start-pst at date-inf in=April
My dad's been dating again since April.

excerpt in translation from the 1990 edition of collected sample scitation papers for the University of Lester's Faculty of Domain History.

Y benoΟß de lengaΟß cas racontaΟßon poΟΑ stroy nos descondr sur y discrepanΟß entr region n'Albion ayent y sfondranΟß dy Preu Empir DrengoΟßan ammonir. ArguteΟΑ:
The use of language in storytelling can tell us much about the differences between regions of Albion which presaged the collapse of the First Drengot Empire. Discuss:

(i) y droug dy recougl dy siecr catorΟß "Y ci Cont ag Bosc n'Enfield" poll'expostion ranscellant cas cont a pouvr dy Duc SaΟΑour e sy Band MasquaΟΑ normalisar, 
(i) the role of fourteenth-century compendium The Tales of Enfield Wood in codifying the divergent folklore interpretations of the Farmer Duke and his Masked Band,

(ii) y dehiscenΟß aΟΑoint entr y lengaΟß markesc e cantesc com es tesmognaΟΑ par y parol inquittaΟΑ nell'oc recougl, e
(ii) the growing separation between the Merch and Kentish tongues as evidenced by the vocabulary attested in this compendium, and

(iii) diΟßr alcot se poΟΑ motivation dar all'identfianΟß dy scrittour all'oc ouvr con Jovan d'Yare (y reconnuΟΑessem d'aΟ·r l'accont historic Y Gran MorenΟß scaut).
(iii) the extent to which the traditional identification of this work's author with John of Yare (most known for having written historical account The Great Dying) can be justified.

94.2: moglastr "moist"

moglastr /m…îjΥàla.strΧ©/ [m äjΥàla.st…ê]
- damp, moist, somewhat wet (usually to an undesired extent);
- (of the weather) damp, wet, miserable, as of the period between showers or shortly after rainfall;
- weepy, tearful, lachrymose, inclined to crying and sadness

Etymology: derivative of moglar "to wet, dampen, moisten" with the pejorative suffix -astr < Latin -aster "resembling, quasi-". This verb descends from Vulgar Latin *molli≈ç "I soak, drench", related to mollis "soft, tender".

Nossy can am noc eir tras port candon cay moglastr.

94.3: ranscellar "diverge"

ranscellar /Υ¨ran.x…¦Υàlar/ [Υ¨ Äan.Οß…ΣΥàl…ëΥê]

94.4: tars "clean"

tars /tarz/ [t…ëΥêz]
- clean, wiped, scrubbed, having been cleansed by some means;
- blank, unmarked, devoid of markings such as writing;
- (of a facial expression) blank, emotionless, neutral, not revealing anything of one's true thoughts;
- sheer, utter, downright, absolute, unmitgated, offering no exception or qualification

Jo sceu noc grap aΟ·r all'outraΟß tars dell'ig bougr.

94.5: ouΟß "silence"

- silence, peace, quiet, tranquility, the absence of sound;

Cordin Editions (Borlish Edition Cordin /Υ¨e.diΥàtsj…în k…îrΥàd…Σn/) is a publishing house founded in the mid-twentieth century and based in the city of Cordin on Borland.

The company, which is affiliated with the University of Cordin, is recognised for producing educational books for national schools. It also published syllabi covering schooling throughout childhood which were taken up as a standard by most schools in the polity.
Publications

    The Boral Province in the century after Hadrian (Y ProvinΟß Boral par y siecr posc Hadrian, 1952), written by late classical historian Robiaut Scarvon
    The Story of Borland (Y Stoir d'Istr Boral, first edition 1982, second edition 1993), a school history textbook
    Shepherds of Hambrick: the Small History of Dane Borland (Acr a SauΟΑ Vellig, 2008), written by Sconet YdreΟß about Borland in the tenth century
    From Tablets to Touchscreens: The Story of Education (Des Delt vars Dois: Y Stoir d'Education, 2016), written by Theign Roufler in the style of an academic textbook but intended to be read by a large lay audience

---

Southbar (Borlish Sothbar /s…îΈΗΥàbar/ [s ähΥàb…ëΥê]) is a region of Borland in the southwest of the island. Its largest population centre is the city of Axbane.
History

The petty kingdom of Southbar was founded by Anglian immigrants in the fifth century, along with Angland and several more minor kingdoms. It quickly established itself in opposition to Angland and to the rump sub-Roman Borlish state. At the turn of the ninth century it saw an influx of immigration from East Anglia after it was subjugated by Markland.

After an alliance between the First Kingdom of Borland and Angland and the Battle of Fendal (810), Southbar was forced to swear fealty as a subordinate kingdom.

Southbar held out for some time against the Danes after the fall of the First Kingdom of Borland, and even harboured the exiled King Jothey and his wife Brenna of Barrow. Nonetheless, by the end of the tenth century Sothbar had been incorporated into Dane Borland.

In the twelfth century Southbar had become a county. Its de facto seat was Axbane; by 1190 Count Steven is known to have resided year-round in the coastal town, bequeathing the old house in HeitriΟß to his younger brother.

94.6: cigl "boyfriend, girlfriend"

cigl /tsajl/ [tsajl]
- boyfriend, girlfriend, significant other, partner, one member of an unmarried romantic and/or sexual relationship;
- darling, dear, honey, baby, a generic term of endearment for one's lover

Etymology: from Old Borlish cigle /tsi…Θl…ô ~ tsi é…ô/ "trinket, tchotchke, proverbially worthless object", from synonymous *cΡΪculus in Vulgar Latin. This is a diminutive from Latin cΡΪccum "trinket", from an Ancient Greek form ΈΚΈ·ΈΚΈΚΈΩœ² which is probably imitative. Semantic drift to the modern sense (via the term of address) is evidenced from the eighteenth century.

My sour nil pausar ne parolar de sy cigl.
/mi sur n…Σl poΥàzar ne Υ¨pa.roΥàlar de si tsajl/
[mi s äΥê n…Σl p äΥàz…ëΥê ne Υ¨pa. Ä äΥàl…ëΥê de si tsajl]

My sister keeps talking about her boyfriend.

94.7: jusser "downstairs"

95.1: frend "stranger"

frend /fr…¦nd/ [f Ä…¦nd]
- stranger, neither a friend nor an acquaintance;
- outlander, foreigner, someone from a different place or (especially) a different polity;

---

Celtic Borland

Y pouvlanΟß celtesc d'Istr Boral (y Bora Ale noncupaΟΑ temporane) no's fort mis a regest. Y pog bustr tesmogn de lorry lengaΟß sousonn an dehiscenΟß for recent des y lengaΟß britton; creut es ig il alandau sull'istr des Albion, e noc des y tarmagn cos direct.
The Celt population of Borland (self-described as the Bora Ale) is poorly documented. The few attestations of their language suggest only a recent divergence from the Briton tongue; they are thought to have arrived on the island from Albion, not directly from the continent.

L'oc cultur er conjognt ag sar marcer d'Europ par mejan dell'enascment de stan e de far. Jarry innajaΟΑ dell'oc epoc es yent trovaΟΑ tras toll'istr des loy allors remot com Egypt e Pers.
This culture was connected to the European trade mesh via the export of tin and iron. Imported pottery of this period has been found across the island from as far afield as Egypt and Persia.

Y Celt stan compos ne form de bempart tribu; cascun afferjarau picq region eΟΑ er regnaΟΑ des fortalleΟß colliner. PlaΟß religious diΟßr plusour temporane son desseveliΟΑ; y celeberrem es y labyrinth a Ο΅adrosc, oc ayent largeΟß d'apponment lon fondamen cas sell'origin e bers.
The Celts were organised into many tribes, each covering small regions and ruled from hill-forts. Several important religious sites of this time have been unearthed; most famously the Ο΅adrosc labyrinth, which has sparked much unfounded theorising as to its origin and purpose.

##

95.2: spaiΟΑoir "drawer"

spaiΟΑoir /speΥàΟΑ…îir/ [sp…ΣΥàΟΑ…î.j…ê]
- drawer, compartment, an open-topped box that one can slide in and out of the cabinet that contains it;
- (figuratively) category, pigeonhole, box, one of several groups of things or people categorised by some means

Etymology: sixteenth-century derivative of verb spaiΟΑar "slide, scrape" with the locative suffix -oir, which comes from -t≈çrium in Latin. The verb is an Old Borlish derivative of paiΟΑ "floor, smooth surface", which directly reflects Latin pavΡΪtum "beaten, trodden upon" from pavi≈ç "I beat, ram, tread down". In figurative use the word is seen from the late eighteenth century.

Scey un spaiΟΑoir scos ne sy tablaur.
/xi …Σn speΥàΟΑ…îir x…îz ne si taΥàblor/
[Οßi …Σn sp…ΣΥàΟΑ…î.j…ê x…îz ne si t…êΥàbloΥê]
there.is indf drawer hide.p.pst in 3s.gn desk
Her desk has a secret drawer.

95.4: key "button"

---

Roman Papacy

The Papacy in Rome is the spiritual seat of much of Christendom.

Despite intermittent conflicts with the Greek patriarchs and several abortive antipope lineages, the Roman Papacy remained the ultimate authority over all Christendom until the German Secession of the early thirteen century. This created a lasting competing seat in the Augsburg See which did not recognise the Roman Papacy's legitimacy.

The Great Fracture of the mid-sixteenth century severely diminished the temporal powers of the Papacy. While on paper the Pope in Rome remained an absolute ruler (along with political dominion over several smaller Italian and German provinces), in practice the new Triunvirato had political dominion over the city of Rome.

People

Mercury II (fl. 590), who took a particular interest in Borland as a stronghold of Christianity in the north
Agatho (1001βÄ™1026), who canonised David, patron saint of Wales
Nuntius (fl. ca. 1100), who instituted a reform of the Julian calendar and moved the New Year to the 1st of March
Damian V (1110-1121), born Humbert of Ravenna in 1047
Damian VI (1199βÄ™1208), called 'the Wise', and first member of the Brethin in the role
Just (1208βÄ™), who called for a renewed Crusade for Jerusalem in 1210 (known to history as the Failed Crusade)
Hermeneus II (fl. ca. 1250), more totalist than his predecessors in reaction to the German Secession and instigator of the Baptism of the North

- crank, a bent piece of an axle with which one can impart rotation;
- (customary, poetic) cubit, a unit of distance nowadays equal to two feet

Etymology: via Old Borlish coud, coft "elbow, bend" from Latin cubitum "elbow, cubit". This is related to verb cub≈ç "I lie down, I recline".

The Romantic Wars

scitation questions in translation from the Dijon New School's domain history course Strategy in the Romantic Wars.

i) Y Conflict Hellen n'Armeny a mesur dy dezein 1640 val sodaltaΟΑ parmy ci Rix Romannesc ou? SoumettaΟΑ argument sell'inclujon basfiant e hasmant, cur particuler dant ag kerellanΟß religious cohernt ag Retorsion Rialid.
i) Should the Hellenic Conflict in Armenia of the 1640s be classified as a Romantic War? Give arguments for and against its inclusion, with particular attention to the religious strife concomitant with the Rialid Revenge.

ii) Ant soumoin all'ouvr Var lon Mur par Vijairana, arguteΟΑ y ci apponment y faΟ·blessem endeutant motivation dar all'ableganΟß de toΟΑ y marcant gourjat e seldivan des Moscat d'an 1652 N (1062 H), meis plusour pre y concarnΟß dell'Imamat Ibadinean par soldatry hadramoΟßan.
ii) With reference to Vijairania's War Without Walls, discuss the most popular theories attempting to explain the expulsion of all Gurjat and Seldivan traders from Moscat in 1652 N (1062 H), several months before the fall of the Ibadine Imamate to Hadramot forces.

iii) DiΟßr alcot poΟΑe Morrac profit tenir des y mort dell'Emperour Cariwasari por enajanΟß d'argent vars y Vetomont monfiar e cossy bors yembr por lorry rix ne cors con Vascony e Libya?
iii) To what extent was Morrack able to exploit the death of Emperor Cariwasari to increase silver exports to the Vetomund and finance their ongoing wars with Vascony and Libya?

96.4: quallour "passenger"

- passenger, one who rides or travels in a vehicle but who is not the driver or a crewmember;

96.5: osceyan "birthday"

osceyan /Υ¨o.xiΥàjan/ [ äΥàΟßjan]
- birthday, the anniversary of the day on which someone was born;
- birthday party, a celebration of somebody's birthday;
- (more generally) anniversary, a celebration of something lasting a number of years

Etymology: from Middle Borlish oscey-an "birthday celebration", literally "marks the year". The verb osceyar "to mark, commemorate" (now often specifically "to celebrate a birthday" by backformation) is a derivative of now-obsolete osc "notch, mark, tally". This noun is of obscure origin and presumably unrelated to osc "victim" < Latin hostia, although osceyar has on occasion been deliberately misinterpreted as meaning "victimise, prank, haze" for comic effect.

For my birthday we went to the theatre.

---

Revillon

Revillon (Borlish Reveglon /Υ¨re.vijΥàl…în/) is the Borlish term for the eve (and often the entire day) before Christide. It refers also to the specific cultural practice on the island of the Winter Feast (y Fest Reveglon), a celebration held on Revillon in churches across the land to which everyone in attendance brings food.

The Borlish name Reveglon means "vigil", and is a Middle Boral borrowing from the synonymous French reveillon, itself originally from Latin evigil≈ç "I wake up". Earlier names include jolveisre (modern jolleistr "excursion, jaunt"), taken from Old Norrish jΟ≥lveizla "Yule feast", and fleΟΑoul (of unknown ultimate etymology), both of which referred specifically to the celebrations.

Evidence of similar winter solstice festivities involving communally-prepared feasts on Borland predates even the Roman invasion, making Revillon one of the longest continuously-practised folk traditions in Europe (bearing in mind that the practice has evolved considerably with time). Third-century Roman historian Ammian Marcellinus provides us with an early written mention of the Winter Feast, which appears (under its original name Flid≈çlus, which gave the aforementioned fleΟΑoul) in one of his asides on the Northwestern Isles. Post-imperial sources in the first millennium almost exclusively criticise the tradition as pagan and unholy; however, by the twelfth century the practice is fully appropriated by the Church.

Queen Natalia II of Borland abdicated on Revillon of 1894 as part of the political upheaval surrounding Borland's entry into the Drengot Collusion.

Food
Foods traditionally associated with the Revillon Feast include:

the tort dell'ivan /Υàt…î Ät Υ¨de.liΥàvan/ ("child's pie", in reference to the Christ child), a savoury dish often of lamb. The practice of scoring a cross into the lid and hiding a small wooden object inside (traditionally also a cross) is a later innovation, but compare the similar galette des rois seen on the continent. This name for the dish is attested as early as the tenth century in Old Boral torte del ifan /t…î…Ψ.t…ô d…¦l iΥàfan/, although the modern recipe including lamb and potato is not seen in writing until the eighteenth century.
jonnovar zucarraΟΑ /Υ¨ £…î.noΥàva Ä Υ¨zi.kaΥà ÄaΈΗ/ "candied butterflies", a sugar-spiced cakebread particular to the island which ever since their earliest iterations have been generously spiced (usually with dalassine bark and inchroot). At this time of year they are formed into butterfly shapes, popularly thought to represent the metamorphosis of a dead winter into the new life of spring.

DNA in the 18th century

    excerpt in translation from the 1998 scholastic quire Sviluppi in Philosophia dal Periodo Revitalisto by historical deixist Jovanna Ariperto.

Desselut ratiocinau ig exist un lengaΟß vital (parmy sy scaunΟß a luy i le nommau y Parol Divin) present ny ci casel dy corp.
Desselut inferred the existence of a vital language (in her own writing she called it the Divine Word) present in the cases of the body.

Rumour tardessem noc obstroint, plausibr no's ig i stimaus l'oc lengaΟß hebreu a verb, con tan l'exponois comport plusour ne sell'edition.
Despite later rumours, she probably did not believe this language was literally Hebrew, although she made several analogies in her publications.

D'an 1770 y preu livr se descreint sceint er scaut par un scrittour lon nom e lor condonaΟΑ vars l'UniverstaΟΑ ne Houlm.
In 1770 the first real self-describing book was written by an anonymous author and donated to the University of Hulm.

L'universtaΟΑ a y livr facsimilaΟΑ alcant sur y quadrennial cos exact largecer com possibr, e l'original, a cost y scavr y nuverrem, es sgardabr nell'edifiΟß la dell'Andron a Heredtatur.
The university has duplicated the book once every four years in as exact a way as possible, and the original, alongside the most recent copy, may be viewed in their Department of Heredity building.

---

Britain

Britain (Cambrick Prydein) is a polity of Albion centred on Wales but extending through Devon and Dunclothe and out to Kernow and parts of Ireland.

History
The Kingdom of Britain was founded in the fifteenth century, formalising the suzerainty of Wales over the tallaths of Devon and Dunclothe. The state profited greatly from the Wars of Fealty of the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries which raged across the rest of Albion.

Britain's economic success at this time drove ventures in Africa. Resupply ports along the western coast eventually allowed for the flourishing of direct sea trade between Europe and Asia.

The seventeenth century saw the dawn of British exploration of the Novomund, founding staddomains along the southern Gulf. These would coalesce into several polities known collectively as British Mendeva over the next centuries, the largest of which is Hasiny. Britain here found itself in conflict with Provence's settlements on the east coast; for example, the Sack of Santrafew was undertaken by an alliance of British and Mastoiset forces.

After the Chrysian Landfall, emigration from Britain (along with more formal state-building enterprise from Cathay) helped form the modern polity of New Leudong.

In the latter years of the nineteenth century, British Mendeva overtook Britain in population and industry. Nonetheless, Britain retained some cultural prestige over the Novomundine polities. In part this was sustained via migration of many members of the 'new rich' across the Atlantic, and the resultant intertwining of the respective upper-class societies through marriage. It is debated whether this prestige accelerated the export of Household Renovation social mores from Britain to Mendeva.

References
A CyclopΟΠdia of British Persons (third edition), written by Wolfston Potter and published by the Yievle Brethin Primers in 1870

97.7: outrcaum "coda"

outrcaum /Υ¨utrΧ©.Υàkom/ [Υ¨u.t…êΥàkom]
- coda, finale, a passage of music that brings a movement or piece to a conclusion;
- climax, culmination, the peak of a narrative's action;
- zenith, acme, apogee, pinnacle, peak, the highest point or state of anything

The compound has been calqued into other languages, including Merch outsong /Υà…ôut.s…î≈΄g/ "coda". In extended use the word is attested from the middle of the nineteenth century.

98.1: Masquira

excerpt in translation on the subject of the masquira fiction genre.

RomanΟß a masquira es un spaiΟΑoir de narraΟΑ appopulaΟΑ cos central con parsonaΟß outrley (ben delit hasmant), tan com tapeΟßment viglaΟΑ de character masquaΟΑ, y bon melle e vellisqment convoluΟΑ.
Masquira romance is a genre of stories which typically feature criminal (or crime-fighting) characters, as well as plots driven by hidden identities, high society and complicated schemes.

I surpon y spaiΟΑoir de spionnaΟß pog tardessem, cos particuler n'ouvr modern poiΟΑant de casc linaΟß romanial.
It overlaps with the slightly-later spycraft genre, especially in modern works which draw on both literary traditions.

Y nom 'masquira' descen des y trevold d'an 1860 par Ezio Carvallo Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico "RobaΟΑ de Masq ben Jalicq", stant laΟΑ cossiraΟΑ com ayent y spaiΟΑoir tarront.
The name 'masquira' derives from Ezio Carvallo's 1860 trevold Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico "In Either a Mask or a Suit", which is widely considered to have pioneered the genre.

RomanΟß a kevlar es un souspaiΟΑoir de masquira caglou irrayant a volry opulent, celebr maxime n'Istr Boral dy siecr novoΟß.
Kevler romance is a subgenre of masquira focused on glamorous thefts, particularly popular in nineteenth-century Borland.

Degnant ig bempart y jout dy spaiΟΑoir son benoΟßaΟΑ ne literatur des aubon dell'hour, nos veim y nascenΟß d'un spaiΟΑoir de romanΟß a masquira nell'epoc d'Atelier nuverrem.
Although many of the tropes of the genre have been used in literature since time immemorial, we see the birth of a distinct genre of masquira romance in the late Workshop period.

##

98.5: Tachygraph

A tachygraph (Merch /Υ¨ta.k…ΣΥàg…Ψaf/, also informally a tachy or tacky /Υàta.k…ôi/) is a device with a keyboard for typing text onto paper, or more recently directly onto a slate.

Etymology

The word "tachygraph" originates in the mid-nineteenth century as a brand name. It is derived from Ancient Greek œ³αΨΑœ΅αΩ†Χ¹œ² βÄΔ (takhΟΚs) "quick, fast" and Έ≥œ¹αΨΑΧ¹œÜœâ βÄΔ (grΟΓph≈ç) "draw, write".

History

Early tachygraphs in the 1830s and 40s were extremely cumbersome, with most models only capable of setting type at a fraction of the speed of practised handwriting. The Armanburg Tachygraph (Tachygrafia Armangrod), which was commercially available in Poland from 1842, was so awkward to use that it was lampooned in the bit-sheets as the Bradygrafia (literally the "slow-writer").

Since very early on, attempts have been made to connect tachygraphs to the steeplemesh, so that one could type a message onto paper and simultaneously transmit a copy of the message to someone else (ideally, automating a tachygraph to type the message out also on the receiving end).

The first tachygraph specifically able to send messages on the steeplemesh was the Vilmanas Teleclavia, designed in Lithow by Tomas Vilmanas in 1859. Its first message (the first line from Ovid's Heroines, reproduced below) was sent on Michaelmas of that year to his friend Margreta Legaite in Tvaniste.

¬Ζ A B C D E F G ¬Ζ HAEC TUA PENELOPE LENTO TIBI MITTIT ULISSE ¬Ζ
a b c d e f g. This your Penelope sends to you, long-delayed Ulysses.

Historical films have made much of the fact that this message was sent less than a month after the 1859 Auroric Anomaly, but there is no evidence Vilmanas considered its effects (though he must have been aware of the stories of messages along unpowered steeplepost wires).

In the late nineteenth century, the Faxim brand of tachygraph was sold across Borland.

99.1: Laurz Arnoutszen

excerpt in translation on the subject of Laurz Arnoutszen.

Laurz Arnoutszen (fl. 1540) sta un sanat dannesc dont y droughanΟß cas saniscment pestrisceu y mesur de histoir medical n'Europ, oc diΟßr alcot por ig se pos cas medicin parolar d'un Europ pre Arnoutszen e l'un posc Arnoutszen.
Laurz Arnoutszen (fl. 1540) was a Danish physician whose work in sanitation shaped the course of European medical history, to such an extent that in the context of medicine one can speak of pre-Arnoutszen and post-Arnoutszen Europe.

Interim, histoir sevrar cossy poΟΑ y vertaΟΑ suraΟΑoir par y fanglanΟß eΟΑ issaur d'autr hom pre sy droughanΟß sepondr.
However, this periodisation can go too far in neglecting others' advancements and successes prior to his work.

An moment quidam posc d'an 1535 i collaborau a sy cosvour Elisa Arnoutszen.
At some point after 1535 he collaborated with his cousin Elisa Arnoutszen.

Sell'heir hamtau sona de folcr cas histoir medical, auteΟß ayent cos parΟßal sur y descovart de veunt unicasel.
His son was also pivotal in the history of medicine, being partially responsible for the discovery of single-celled organisms.

Sy gran homily cas lodrigneth medical eΟΑ autr issarnΟß la contenoy y benoΟß de lembic cadaraΟΑ com diffrucΟßon liquid rannuΟΑar.
His great treatise on medical handiwork and other practice included recommending the employment of distilled lembick as liquid disinfectant.

- toilet, loo, a fixture used for urination and defecation;

Theory X: Miscellanea (answers on sheet 256)

~1. Which of the following children of Zeus does not give his name to any of the four greater jovial moons?
  (i) Minos, king of Crete
 (ii) ΟÜacos, grandfather of Achilles
(iii) Perseus, who slew the gorgon Medusa
 (iv) Zethos, one of the founding twins of Thebes

~2. A span [wavelength] of five hundred scant-inches [127 nm] marks the legal limit of what sort of ≈™culux [electromagnetic radiation]?

~3. How many mora does an atom of the corporal [chemical element] 'killdark' [uranium] have?
(i) 90
(ii) 92
(iii) 94
(iv) 96

~4. What contemporary astronomical theory is instantiated by the sculpture I Macchine di Helio, carved by Lorenzo dell'Acchedia?

~5. The Branwen Sanatory, the medical research facility where the painkiller zebdomaschal [ibuprofen] was first synthesised, is located in which polity?
(i) Britain
(ii) Hasiny
(iii) Tavancy
(iv) New Leudong

~6. Elsebeth Sneider's 1795 work Through a Black Mirror sparked what literary craze with a theoretical bent?

~7. Which celestial object did astronomer Coscatl bint InΟΓm discover in the mid-nineteenth century?
(i) Minerva [Uranus]
(ii) Ixion [Ceres]
(iii) Janus [Neptune]
(iv) the cisjovial ring [asteroid belt]

~8. Inspired by Rachel Desselut's work in early methodics [programming], the world's oldest self-describing book is kept in which university?

~9. Gurjat theorist Manesh Camdar was the first person to observe the moment of fecundation [fertilisation]; which animal was he studying?
(i) Sea hedgehogs
(ii) Sea sponges
(iii) Damsel flies
(iv) Charon bugs

~10. Which nineteenth-century Merch theorist wrote magnum opus Astraphor and Light, fully characterising plenty [charge] in a single equation?

100.1: Threshold Force

Y stoir de forΟß a sougl a sell'inceuΟßon d'an 1902, posc y nux atomic descovrirβÄîoc a digr, descovrir ig pres toΟΑ y moul d'un atom es se situant vars y centr den.
The story of threshold force [nuclear power] begins in 1902, with the discovery of the atomic nuxβÄîthat is, the discovery that almost all of the heft of an atom is located near its centre.

L'oc occorreu all'Atelier a NΟΩstad (lor posseΟ·ΟΑ e gournaΟΑ nell'un man par theorist soustanΟßal Lohic Courvoiseir de NΟΩstad) ny citaΟΑ cappaΟßan a VaronΟΗw.
This took place at the NΟΩstad Atellier (then privately owned and run by substantial theorist Lohic Courvoisier de NΟΩstad) in the Cappatian city of VaronΟΗw.

Y corporal benoΟßaΟΑ ny majortaΟΑ imman de molin a sougl, kildarc, er preu cadaraΟΑ ag siecr novoΟß mi ny scir irΟΑonnesc de Killdara (dont vien y nom dy soustanΟß, tras y parol cambresc kildΟΓrc 'de Killdara').
The corporal [element] used in the vast majority of threshold mills [plants], killdark [uranium], was first isolated in the mid-nineteenth century in the Irish shire of Killdara (whence, via Welsh kildΟΓrc 'from Killdara', the substance's name).

Portingal sta un tarromour cas forΟß a sougl, interrogment sur sy laboriostaΟΑ ag dezein 1950 parsount neΟΑan mantenir ig il basn par astraphor enajaΟΑ des y Collujon Drengot.
Portingale was a pioneer of threshold force, funding research into its effectivity in the 1950s in a bid to reduce their dependence on astraphor [electricity] exported by the Drengot Collusion.

100.2: aujon "magazine"

aujon /oΥà £…în/ [ äΥà ù…în]
- magazine, periodical, a non-academic publication comprising folded sheets attached together;
- assemblage, parade, display, gathering, a collection of people of things to be looked at;

##

100.5: Voyage of the Ph≈™bos

The Ph≈™bos was a flagship of the Kingdom of Britain in the early sixteenth century. Having profited greatly at the expense of the other polities of Albion [Great Britain] during the Wars of Fealty, Britain under King Howel sought to extend its trade mesh past its posts down the west African coast, and trade with Asia directly by sea.

To this end, a coterie of ships led by the Ph≈™bos (and itself captained by Martan mac Kellot) was tasked with sailing as far east as possible. After rounding Africa and making port in such places as Ungotia [Zanzibar], they were directed by Umanick [Omani] and then Chinese traders. Via Gurjat and Madray [1], they eventually arrived in Borune, at that time the southeasternmost land known in the Vetomund.

The Ph≈™bos was thus present at the Great Borunesk Meeting of 1517. The ship's meeting with the Morrack ship the Cynthia in the port city of Sinquan is the definitive moment in the course of the Meeting, and it appears in several book and film adaptations. It is popularly assumed that the crews could not understand one another; in fact at least a dozen crewmembers of the Ph≈™bos spoke some Arabic, as the British and Morrack trade meshes had overlapped for centuries.

Unlike the Cynthia, the Ph≈™bos would not attempt to circumnavigate the globe; tales of the magnitude of the oceans ahead dissuaded mac Kellot from such a course.

---

[1] polities of India whose names are cognate with IRL Gujarat and Madurai.

##

101.1: Drengot Collusion

excerpt in translation on the subject of the Drengot Collusion.

Y Collujon DrengoΟßan sta un alleyanΟß-federaΟß de paΟ·s monfeyaΟΑ n'Europ norΟΑovester.
The Drengot Collusion was a multinational alliance-federacy in northwestern Europe.

NommaΟΑ lonc y ci Empir DrengoΟßan anΟßan (lou stant nommaΟΑ lonc y linaΟß nobr normant ayent bempart de Gaul e d'Albion unfeyaΟΑ),
Named for the older Drengot Empires (themselves named for the Norman noble family who first united much of Gaul and Albion),

l'oc crescoy des concort divers ny siecr novoΟß mi vars y possenΟß principal ny region denir fin a sy sfondranΟß dramatic d'an 1961.
it grew from various treaties in the mid-nineteenth century into the principal power in the region until its dramatic collapse in 1961.

Y Collujon DrengoΟßan smargoy parmy y polity francesc nell'epoc dell'Asteller Mondial.
The Drengot Collusion arose among the French polities of the Global Workshop period.

pog asc apres er y Nou Sar Urban (un congeries de citaΟΑ autonom naΟΑusc oc Londr sovran abrangent, tan com Ausbagn n'Istr Boral)
Shortly thereafter the New Urban Mesh (a collection of somewhat-autonomous cities including independent London, as well as Axbane on Borland)

caut sousuntβÄîben denuΟΑ autour, lonc y nosthubr idiosyncras seyon autβÄîdy Collujon smargent.
was subsumed byβÄîor took control of, depending on one's personal biasesβÄîthe nascent Collusion.

101.2: raussiΟΑ "backwards"

101.3: scroΟßabr "agonising"

scroΟßabr /xroΥàtsa.brΧ©/ [x Ä äΥàtsa.b…ê]
- agonising, painful, excruciating, torturous, causing extreme pain;
- harrowing, terrifying, nerve-racking, causing extreme fear or emotional distress;
- (sometimes proscribed) bothersome, exasperating, vexing, mithering, causing annoyance

Etymology: derivative of scroΟßar "to agonise, torment, vex", which descends directly via Old Borlish from synonymous excruci≈ç "I torture" (related to cruci≈ç "I crucify"). The last sense is disliked by some but is attested from the early nineteenth century.

Torn all'An ne Deganny interman un cainΟß scroΟßabr.
/t…îΥên aΥàlan ne Υ¨de.gaΥàni Υ¨…Σn.t…¦rΥàman …Σn kents xroΥàtsa.brΧ©/
[t…îΥên …êΥàlan ne Υ¨de.g…êΥàni Υ¨in.t…ΣΥêΥàman …Σ≈΄ kens x Ä äΥàtsa.b…ê]
turn at-df=year in Deganny constitute indf event agonising
New Year's in Deganny is a harrowing experience.

101.4: appragl phasm "television set"

appragl phasm /aΥàpr…¦jl ΥàfazmΧ©/ […êΥàp Ä…¦jl Υàfa.z…êm]
- television set, an electronic device that receives and displays TV signals

Etymology: twentieth-century collocation, from appragl "device, machine, tool" and phasm "(the technology of) television, image transmission". The first component descends from Medieval Latin apparΡ¹culum "tools, equipment", a derivation of appar≈ç "I prepare, equip". The second is a learned borrowing directly from Ancient Greek œÜΈ§œÉΈΦΈ± βÄΔ (phΟΓsma) "apparition, phantom, image".

Nossell'appragl phasm n'a ceu alcun grastum.
/n…îΥ¨s…¦.laΥàpr…¦jl Υàfa.zmΧ© na tsaw alΥàk…Σn graΥàst…Σm/
[n äΥ¨s…¦.l…êΥàp Ä…¦jl Υàfa.z…êm na Υ¨tsa.w…êΥàg…Σn g Ä…êΥàst…Σm]
1p.gen=device TV ng=have basically no thickness
Our television set is almost completely flat.

##

101.5: New Urban Mesh
 
The New Urban Mesh was an alliance of autonomous cities in Northwest Europe in the nineteenth century. Among its members were London, Conster, Axbane, Pentrose, Amstel, Anvers and Gant.

History
The Mesh emerged contemporaneously with similar political structures elsewhere, such as the Riverine Cities along the Danaw. This rise of such federacies of city-states has been attributed to the rapid population growth and expansion of cities in the run-up to the Global Workshop period. Several of the Mesh's cities were simultaneously also part of pre-existing polities (for example, Axbane and Pentrose were never legally independent from Borland).

The cities of the Mesh are popularly associated with wheeling-and-dealing policy, as well as aggressive and global use of espionage. However, the extent to which the Mesh was (for example) able to steer politics in the East to their trade benefit is likely overestimated.

In the late nineteenth century (ca. 1890), London led the Mesh to unite politically with the Drengot Collusion, at the time a nascent alliance of the French polities of Norman Kent, Paris and Burgundy. Whether it is more correct to say that the Mesh was subsumed into the Collusion, or that the Mesh took control of the Collusion, has long been a topic of heated debate.

References
Mr Desarden, Prince Consort (1968), a film based on the 1887 scandal around James Desarden, London's ambassador to Nackon Day. The affair is widely thought to have been politically motivated and a product of direct meddling from the Mesh.

##

102.1: The Jacobin Fraternity

Y FraterntaΟΑ Jagovin, cos historic tan connuΟΑ sou nom dell'Orn a Sagnt Jagm, es un communtaΟΑ religious y citaΟΑ Ausbagn hamtant ag rimot souΟΑer n'Istr Boral.
The Jacobin Fraternity, historically also known as the Order of Saint James, is a religious organisation based in the city of Axbane on the south coast of Borland.

Y FraterntaΟΑ er instauraΟΑ ne Flandr ag siecr deg sou y patronnement d'Amegl de Brug. Ag siecr onΟß l'oc er envoluΟΑ com conjuraΟßonnour n'un smout pouvlancer vecin contrell'Emperour DrengoΟßan Joffrey II eΟΑ obligaΟΑ a enfugr tras mar vars Istr Boral. La costroyeurn il y Monastir Peninsoler ancour Ausbagn surplombant.
The Fraternity was founded in tenth-century Flanders under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges. In the eleventh century it was implicated in a local popular uprising against Drengot Emperor Geoffry II and forced to flee over the sea to Borland. There they built the Peninsular Monastery which still overlooks Axbane.

MoneΟΑ des y FraterntaΟΑ droughau ag PalaΟß Jagovin ny ci bandlau atorn de DamvaΟΑ, eΟß posc lorry compliscenΟß ag siecr ogtoΟß nuverrem denent y posson fis por y parolment borallesc.
Funds from the Fraternity contributed to the Jacobin Estates on the outskirts of Damvath, which on their completion in the late eighteenth century became the fixed location of Borland's parliament.

Sceint oy l'orn es reconnuΟΑ cos majortaΟΑer por y colluy seyon aut, Yeustman Jaibos, tan com eiΟΑel spanent ny citaΟΑ posseyent (ig inclus y plaΟß dell'un parmy y bagn roman antic la).
Today the organisation is known mostly for the guild they own, Jaibos Breweries, as well as for owning significant lands in the city (including the site of one of the ancient Roman bathhouses there).

102.2: requΟ·Οßon "weekend"

- weekend, the (usually two-day) break in the working week often including a holy day of rest;
- (religion) day of rest, Sabbath day, the period set aside for rest and worship

Etymology: originally restricted to religious use, borrowed from Latin requiΡ™ti≈ç "rest, repose" and modelled on verb requΟ·r "to rest, be comforted". This descends from related verb requiΡ™sc≈ç "I rest, am supported" in Latin. In the modern era the three weekends Fri-Sat, Sat-Sun and Sun-Mon are recognised by law in Borland (in line with the rest of the former Drengot Collusion and many other polities).

102.4: bacaget "cupboard"

bacaget /Υ¨ba.kaΥà £…¦t/ [Υ¨ba.k…êΥà ù…¦t]
- cupboard, cabinet, a piece of furniture with shelves for storing cookware or food;
- closet, wardrobe, a similar piece of furniture for storing clothes

Etymology: Middle Borlish, from contemporary French metathetic variants bacaget, cabbagΟ®s "cupboard; lean-to, cabin, hut". The first part of this word is related to cabbas "basket; storeroom" < Latin capΡ¹x "spacious", while the second may reflect ajouster "to add" (in dialect form agestir) or jeter "to throw".

##

102.5: British Mendeva

British Mendeva refers to the polities of Mendeva which have undergone significant settlement from or otherwise been significantly influenced by the Kingdom of Britain. The region is mostly contained within the larger region of Gulf Mendeva. The polity of the region with the most population and global influence is Hasiny.

History

In the seventeenth century, exploratory voyages by ships of the Kingdom of Britain founded staddomains along the Mendevan Gulf, which would grow via settlement, treaty and conquest into the polities of British Mendeva.

The 'herdtale' tradition of storytelling which originated in nineteenth-century Mendeva was brought to wider recognition via several popular works published jointly in British Mendeva and Britain. Conversely, the masquira genre overtook the region later in that century following its popularity in the Vetomund.

British Mendeva overtook Britain in population and industry in the latter years of the nineteenth century. Nonetheless, for some time Britain retained a position of cultural cachet; it has been suggested this contributed to British Mendeva's rapid adoption of the ethics of the Household Renovation (as compared to much more ambivalent New Provence and Mashick, for instance) during this time.

Unlike earlier centuries, the nineteenth century saw significant transmigration of the Ο©lites between British Mendeva and Britain, as well as much more intermarriage. In part this can be attributed to the newness of an autochthonous Ο©lite west of the Atlantic, but mostly this is simply due to faster ships and greatly-reduced travel times.

##

103.1: Graveyard Fiddlers

Y viol cemtir intermann un jout cyclant ny cont a pouvr borallesc.
The graveyard fiddlers are a recurring motif in the folklore of Borland. 

PostulaΟΑ es ig il sein gast a mort sont ovittaΟΑ con y broit enigmatic e lontan de baΟΑr viol oΟ·ΟΑ cos particular jos cemtir eΟΑ all'hour prell'ort sol.
They are said to be death spirits responsible for the mysterious sound of string instruments in the air heard especially near cemeteries and in the hour before dawn.

Lorry melody son cos usual descrit com mogn e dolorous.
Their melodies are usually described as low-pitched and mournful.

OrruΟΑ y viol cemtir sein un costum peculier n'Istr Boral, dec ustol pouvlancer local no son abrangent d'alcun figur parallel cos ovart.
The fiddlers appear to be a tradition peculiar to Borland, as neighbouring folk traditions do not include any obviously analogous figures.

Il son ottem reconnuΟΑ allors lonc ig il forn svoccaΟΑ ny melodram Al Igne (Ale in Fiamme) par Chiarina Bruno.
They are most known abroad from their inclusion in opera Wings Afire (Ale in Fiamme) by Chiarina Bruno.

Disc l'oyeus y cont dy viol cemtir des sy fraΟΑr, oc ayent abattuΟΑ a cost de soldart borallesc a mesur dy Var Theudesc Dovem.
She is said to have heard the tale of the graveyard fiddlers from her brother, who fought alongside Borlish soldiers in the Second German War.

103.2: hau "hi"

hau /ho/ [ho]
- hi, hey, hello, an informal greeting;
- hey, oi, a way to get somebody's attention

Etymology: in the first sense, a continuation of Middle Borlish hauΟΑ! "hello, good day", literally "health". The word hauΟΑ "health", now archaic, was borrowed in the Old Borlish period from synonymous h«ΘlΟΨ in contemporary English. The form and informality of the greeting have been influenced by the second sense, which is probably of onomatopeic origin.

"Hau a toΟΑ gent," dis y giΟΑour quaucant.
/ho a t…îΈΗ  £…¦nt | d…Σz i giΥàΟΑur kwoΥàkant/

"Hi, everyone," said the hurried guide.

103.3: yela "ciao"

yela /jeΥàla/ [j…ΣΥàla]

bye, ciao, an informal formula used by a speaker who is leaving; (archaic) goodbye, farewell, used also in formal contexts;
(sometimes proscribed) hello, hi, used also as an informal greeting

Etymology: via Middle Borlish yelaΟΑ "farewell, goodbye" from Old Borlish vos ielaΟΑ "may you fare well, be strong, abide, endure, persevere". This is a conjugated form of ieleir "abide, persist, persevere" (the would-be reflex yelir is obsolete by the fourteenth century), which is thought to reflect Latin haere≈ç "I cling, adhere, remain fixed". As a greeting the word is attested from the early twentieth century.

I dis "Yela!" pre bouΟΑ souvr.
/i d…Σz jeΥàla pre buΈΗ Υàsu.vrΧ©/
[i d…Σz j…ΣΥàla p Äe buh Υàsu.b…ê]
3s say.pst bye before call let.go-inf
He said "Bye!" then hung up.

- towel, a cloth used for drying something wet;

103.5: Making History

shortform scitation questions lifted from the 2021 edition of the Tremonnow Open School's domain history course 'Making History', which covers the process by which events and people are compiled into historical narratives.

(questions given approximately in order of complexity)

1. Which 14th-century historian first referred to the First Drengot Empire of three centuries earlier as the 'Constant Empire' in an attempt to link it to the District of Constantius (the unrelated part of the fourth-century Diocletian Tetrarchy ruling over Roman Brittania and Gaul)?

2. Name three international conflicts (not including the First German War) which took place during the 'Long Peace' period, here defined as lasting from 1770 until 1815.

3. Who wrote 1872 work 'An Incomplete Dictionary of the New London'? As the crowning example from the Futurism period of parachthon romance, it contains the first attestation of 'slate' for a screen displaying information, along with what other lasting coinages?

4. During which decades of the twentieth century did the Portingale Anarchy take place? Which legal developments of the prior years were abruptly reversed as a result of the breakdown of state control?

5. Compare the stability of the Christian states in the Holy Land before and after the Failed Crusade in the early thirteenth century. Which organisation had their authority over Antioch and Port Simon revoked by Pope Just in 1208, thus saving them from said debacle?

6. The Deviance movement has been credited with contributing to the decline of the Modest Arrangement through the first half of the nineteenth century, particularly in Gaul and Spain. To what extent is this narrative supported by the evidence?

104.1: Jambor of Awry

Jambor d'Avry sta un rey primal n'Avry pog asc apres dy succession dy polity des y principaltaΟΑ ny siecr derran.
Jambor of Awry [~Hungary] was an early king of Awry following the polity's succession from the principality of the previous century.

I le gournau a mesur de spanjon d'influyenΟß Majoric, entrant n'union monarchic con y ci principaltaΟΑ auster ne HaliΟß e Vallac, e denent un alley ag Roman malchassian ny souΟΑ.
He oversaw significant expansion of Majoric influence, entering into personal union with the eastern principalities of Halich and Vallack [~Galicia, Wallachia], and becoming an ally of the Malchassian Romans [~Byzantines] to the south.

Y gran Livrer de Sobjan er costroit durant sy regn, stant un gravatoir parmanent d'ouvr temporane.
The great Library of Sobjan was built during his reign, an enduring archive of contemporary works.

L'eΟß incluyen scrit mathematic des y CaliphaΟΑ Sengr, eΟß auvorn ne proces de traducΟßon a latin.
These included mathematical texts from the Single Caliphate, being translated into Latin in great quantities.

Ci siecr apres, un dy descovan dy Livrer intermaneu cla por y Codex Mysarnos notoir descifrar.
Centuries later, one of the Library's manuscripts was instrumental in decoding the famed Mysarnos Codex.

104.2: cougt droccaΟΑ "CD"

cougt droccaΟΑ /kujt droΥàkaΈΗ/ [kujt d Ä äΥàkah]
- CD, compact disc, an disc-shaped form of data storage read by laser

Etymology: literally "notched disc", attested from the late twentieth century as a calque of Cambrick coetan dolkiada "disc with indentations" (partly chosen so as to preserve the abbreviation CD). The noun cougt "disc, discus; annulus, quoit" is a Middle Borlish borrowing from French couite "discus, quoit, flat stone", of unknown further origin. The adjective is related to droc "notch, indentation", from Germanic.

Jo no's un cougt droccaΟΑ benoΟßant posc un dezein.
/ £o n…îz …Σn kujt dr…îΥàkaΈΗ Υ¨be.noΥàtsant p…îx …Σn deΥàzin/
[ ùo n…îz …Σ≈΄ kujt d Ä äΥàkah Υ¨be.n äΥàtsan p…îx …Σn d…ΣΥàzin]
1s ng-be indf disc notch-p.pst use-p.pst after one decade
I haven't used a compact disc in ten years.

veronic /Υ¨ve.roΥàn…Σk/ [Υ¨ve. Ä äΥàn…Σk]
- plastic, a synthetic solid hydrocarbon-based polymer;
- (historical, in particular) celluloid, any of a variety of thermoplastics created from nitrocellulose and camphor used as photographic film;

- plastic, made of plastic;
- spineless, passive, obliging, willing to comply or change oneself without objection

Etymology: 19C, former trademark also written as lacq Veronic "Veronic shellac". The word was borrowed from Medieval Latin veronΡΪx "resin, varnish", perhaps from Byzantine Greek Έ£ΈΒœ¹ΈΒΈΫαΩëΧ¹ΈΚΈΖ (BerenΟ≠kΡ™), a city of Cyrenaica. In metaphorical use the word is seen from as early as the 1920s.

104.4: hopr "tool"

hopr /Υàh…î.prΧ©/ [h…î.p…ê]
- tool, implement, instrument, device, a self-contained piece of equipment for performing a task;
- gear, equipment, gadgetry, a collection of tools;
- (nautical) tackle, the collection of ropes on a ship for lifting and pulling things;
- toy, plaything, something (often for a child) to play with;
- laughing stock, butt, someone who is the object of ridicule;
- (slang) gun, switchblade, any personal handheld weapon especially when illegally obtained;
- (slang) dick, cock, someone's (most commonly one's own) penis

Etymology: from Middle Borlish hopr "tool, instrument; equipment, gear", from synonymous hople in Old Borlish. This is a borrowing via Old French from Medieval Latin hopla "gear, equipment; tackle", from Greek αΫÖœÄΈΜαΨΑ βÄΔ (hΟ≥pla) "tackle; weaponry". Used for children's toys from the sixteenth century, for laughable people since the eighteenth, and in the last senses from the twentieth.

104.5: An Authorial Assortment

brief references for some writers previously mentioned in my worldbuilding notes.

Olexi Harchenko (fl. c19) was a Zahid Russian [1] author from the city of Boxa [~Novi Sad, Serbia]. He wrote the widely-translated mystery work –†–Α–Ζ–≥–Α–¥―²–Ψ (DΟ©nouement), whose title was borrowed into Italian as rasgatto "resolution, unravelling of a mystery", and from there into many European languages.

Thoffina'h Girmont (fl. 2005) is a British author who wrote Arthurian romance Y Οêreic o Redr…·th (The Dragon of Redrouth). The book is loosely based on the old tales codified in eleventh-century England (and reworked in post-Imperia Wales), but owes much more to the ahistorical reimaginings of the Long Peace [~1800] period.

Heudar FiΟΑaut (fl. c14) was a Borlish writer who is an important source of vernacular Middle Borlish texts. His most famous work is CatreΟΑejnt Lagrem (Twenty-four Laments); his works together contain many first attestations of Borlish words (for example, graΟßant "merciful").

Tanasio Capretti (fl. 1970) was a theologian and humanist at the University of Florence. He wrote sociological work L'Inclusion Deviante di Conti Polari (The Deviance Incorporation of Folk Tales) to commemorate the 120th anniversary of the 1850 Laic Declarations of Belgrade, a landmark accord for secular governance in many polities.

Yollamaniza Elcabir (fl. 1943) was a Mashick [~Mexican] author and one-time spy. He wrote the spycraft novel La te Hozuho Sineton (No Slumber Will Seize Him) in 1943 partly based on his own experiences, in the aftermath of the Lovac Wars and the bombing of his home city of Moshtar [~San Francisco]. His writings were revealing enough that governments across Gulf Mendeva [Mexico Gulf states] issued warrants for his arrest.

---

[1] A Slavic ethnic group present in the northern Balkans and descended from westward-migrating immigrant communities.

##

105.1: Parachthony

excerpt in translation on the topic of parachthony (speculative fiction/sci-fi and fantasy).

Y siecr novoΟß a plaΟß meg intermane un nazir naΟΑusc cas art parachthon.
The mid-nineteenth century was somewhat of a nadir for parachthon art.

RomanΟß desmanneΟßa vars y jagat cojanessem de masquira e cont de kiveu, cant resorgenΟß historic hamtan y scen mondial.
Romance moved on to masquira and herdtale's more mundane settings, while historical revivals populated the world's stages.

Cosogr y pluottanΟß de spaiΟΑoir ag siecr veint enΟßau nos a l'incorporaΟßon volonter veir de mornΟß parachthon afont de stoir allors, com cont d'amour eΟΑ enigma;
With the maturation of genres in the twentieth century we begin to see purposeful melding of parachthon elements in stories of other sorts, like lovetales and ΟΠnigmata;

l'oc tan eΟΑ y sousunΟßon de barstant comportant com Futurism ramenaurn parachthony alcheΟΑr jug nell'eutaΟΑ.
this and the incorporation of related movements like Futurism brought parachthony back into popular art for good.   

Y defas de machinaΟßon er mout ammont par ouvr parachthon, e donc sonc plusour nell'onomatoir orΟΑenjanΟß modern nommant aun lorrell'orn ec destr.
The progress of technology was often presaged by parachthon works, and so many items of vocabulary for modern contrivances have their origin there.

Com bustr, y 'sar pistol' vien colombant des y fougl dy trevold d'an 1968 'Kenopleus', par Ashford Pasquier.
As an example, the 'pistol mesh' is lifted from the pages of Ashford Pasquier's 1968 novel 'Starsail'.

105.2: haval "doll"

haval /haΥàval/ [h…êΥàval]
- doll, puppet, effigy, dummy, a (usually small or crude) three-dimensional representation of a person;
- mannequin, a life-size model of a human body for displaying or fitting clothes;
- model, mockup, miniature, a simplified or scale representation of something

adj.
- model, toy, miniature, simplified or scaled down;
- mock, imitation, fake, ersatz, not genuine

Etymology: from Middle Borlish haval "doll, puppet" originally in southern dialects around Pentrose, a borrowing from contemporary Cambrick haval "likeness, image; doll, effigy". In adjectival use potentially attested from as early as the sixteenth century.

Jan's acavaΟΑ a casc batman haval allaΟßar.

105.3: ΟΑonc "crooked"

ΟΑonc /ΟΑ…înk/ [ΟΑ…î≈΄k]
- crooked, bent, having one or more bends or angles;
- askew, tilted, twisted, off-centre, turned or positioned to one side;
- wry, ironic, sardonic, drily humorous or bitter;
- unlucky, unfortunate, beset by misfortune

Etymology: via Old Borlish from Latin aduncus "hooked, bent, curved", from uncus "hook" (whence Borlish onc "hook, clasp, link, trigger"). Metaphorical extension is seen from the fifteenth century.

Ant heu ΟΑonc l'ovroy y valigs.

##

105.5: Astraphor

Astraphor (also called barric force) is the set of phenomena associated with the presence and movement of plentiful substances, or the thrift that one can harness from such movement.

Etymology

The word 'astraphor' was coined in the seventeenth century to describe the theoretical source of the sparks certain substances (amber, fur, etc.) would create in darkness after being rubbed. It derives from Ancient Greek roots αΦÄœÉœ³œ¹αΨΑœÄΈ° (astrapαΗ½) 'lightning' and -œÜœ¨œ¹ΈΩœ² (-phΟ≥ros) "bearing"; although the expected form would be 'astrapophor', even in the earliest texts we see only the shorter word (possibly due to transcription error).

The modifying form 'barric' derives from parallel work, and originates in Arabic Ί®ΌéΊ±Ό£Ό²β¹© (barq) "lightning".

History

The deep theoretical connection between astraphor and light was explored thoroughly in the mid-nineteenth century; the unimaginatively titled magnum opus Astraphor and Light by Merch theorist Wexter Hathwall managed to fully characterise the mechanics of astraphor in a single equation.

At this time, the earlier flag-lamp steeplemesh was in the process of being displaced by barric meshes. Places without a pre-existing extensive flag-lamp system were in many cases faster to adopt the barric mesh; for example, by 1870 the world's longest wired post line on land spanned the length of Lower Mendeva.

The first undersea barric lines were laid in the 1850s in Kent (Dover to Cales), and from Texel in Willemy to Axbane in Borland, each distances of less than twenty leagues. By the following century hundreds of such lines crossed the world's oceans.

In the 1950s, the Mortar coalition in Portingale funded research into using threshold force to produce astraphor so as to reduce dependence on imports from the Drengot Collusion; by the 1960s the polity was a world pioneer in constructing threshold mills, becoming a net exporter of astraphor in 1971.

sentences in translation on the subject of Lower Mendeva [Central America].

Ny region es y polity a Mascic predominant, oc spanent entr y dou rimot e vars y norΟΑ aΟΑief por poΟΑir Mivoc toccar nell'ovest e Taisca toccar nell'aust.
The region is predominated by the polity of Mashick, which stretches from coast to coast and so far north as to border Mivock in the west and Taisha in the east.

OrΟΑenjanΟß barric plusour dell'epoc d'Atelier jagerrem smargoy des Mendeva Bassem, deut plausibr a reclam addoΟßmental cas y respanment rapid d'accaΟß filer (no sceint pog adopΟßon paravant dy sar a gonfan e lucern por deslocar ny region).
Many barric innovations of the late Workshop period came out of Lower Mendeva, likely due to practical concerns around the rapid spread of wired post (there being little previous adoption of the flag-lamp steeplemesh in the region to displace).

Pre y siecr veintem jagerrem, l'oc region fo denuΟΑ enajour diΟßr de mansc com maus eΟΑ ambac.
By the late twentieth century, this region had become major exporters of foods such as mause and amback.

MarΟß larc aun origin ne Mendeva Bassem, ec gles bilrod, scoclet, scadom e tascban abrangent.
Many trade goods originated in Lower Mendeva, including billrod dye, shockle, shadome and tashban.

##

106.5: Axbane

Axbane (Borlish Ausbagn /ozΥàb…¦jn/) is a major city on the southwest coast of Borland. It is located at the narrowest part of the Rustigh Strait, less than twenty leagues from Willemy on mainland Europe.
Toponymy

Both the English and Borlish names descend from (the dative case of) the original Latin name for the city AquΡΪs BalneΡΪs "to the bathing waters". Compare the city of Bath in Albion and of  (Grand-Aix /g ÄaΧÉΥàd…¦is/, Aughen /Υà…î.xnΧ©/) [Aachen] in Willemy.
History

As a Roman city, Axbane was home to an extensive bathhouse complex, for which the city is named.

With the German migrations of the early medieval period, Axbane was demoted to a minor port in the Anglian kingdom of Sothbar, which comprised the southwestern portion of Borland.

The Peninsular Monastery which overlooks the city was erected in the name of the Order of Saint James (the Jacobin Fraternity), which had fled to Axbane in 1067 after a popular uprising against Drengot Emperor Geoffrey II in Flanders. In the modern day, the Order remains a powerful presence, owning much land in the city and a successful series of breweries.

The Axbane of the late twelfth century is the de facto seat of the County of Sothbar; by 1190 the earl Steven (Steign) is known to have resided year-round in the coastal town, bequeathing the old house in HeitriΟß to his younger brother. At this time, the location of Axbane on one side of the Rustigh Strait was of immense strategic value. The Great Flie Flood of 1171 swept away much of Lower Frisland, and Axbane was for several decades afterward the obvious choice of harbour for any trader moving from the Danish lands through the Drengot domain and onward to Spain.

Axbane eventually became one of the star cities of the Stadbund, an association of mercantile cities in the region. The throughput of the Axbane port is thought to have accounted for over a third of European trade outside the Middlesea by 1400 N. The late fourteenth century also saw this Borlish city develop a particularly close relationship with Bremen.

The 1850s saw the Axbane-Texel undersea barric line, one of the world's first such steeplepost connections across the sea. At this time, Axbane was part of the New Urban Mesh along with independent cities like London.

References

    The Secret Guide to Axbane (Y GiΟΑ Scos por Ausbagn), published first in 1962 on behalf of the Maglen FormeuΟß Caritativia, as part of a series of tourist guidebooks for cities along both coasts of the Rustigh Strait.

In the first decades of its existence the Convoy Australier was in essence a (quite literally) glorified band of mercenaries.

L'oc aye se pleis auvorn dell'eΟß mordeth eΟΑ y confraΟΑr primer, des y PrinΟßipaltaΟΑ de Mystra ny GreΟß vars y fallout imparyent a Jerusalem.
It was heavily involved in the first crusades and related ventures, from the Principality of Mystra in Greece to the unsuccessful forays in Jerusalem. 

L'oc fo cargaΟΑ con y mission d'y stat novel n'Antioch eΟΑ y Havan Simon administrar tost posc lorry concarnΟß ag siecr doΟß.

However, it is generally agreed that this act saved the Convoy a great amount of trouble in the long run (given the imminent disaster of the Failed Crusade).

##

107.5: Grimmers

A grimmer (Merch /Υàg…Ψ…Σ.m…ô/, Borlish grimmour /g Ä…ΣΥàmu Ä/), formerly and formally an augrimmer, is a device that can be given methods to carry out sequences of arithmetic or logical operations. These methods enable grimmers to perform a wide range of tasks. Grimmers are the substrate of the pistol mesh, which links together billions of such devices.

Calculating devices from the earliest abaci prefigure the grimmer, but its true potential (the ability to perform essentially arbitrary computation, given the correct instructions) arose from theoretic discussions on the possibility of self-replication. In the eighteenth-century, Rachel Desselut argued that in order for living matter to duplicate, its "Form" must contain a readable "Method" describing its construction. In this way she predicted the form-method separation essential to modern grimmer operation.

Etymology

The word grimmer is a clipping of augrimmer, literally "one who calculates, one who performs mathematical computation"; the word referred to a profession until the mid-twentieth century. This derives from noun augrim "computation, calculation", which originally meant "calculation using Arabic numerals" in particular, and which was a loan via French augrisme from Medieval Latin algorismus. This reflects the name of Persian mathematician al-Khwarismi, whose works introduced the positional number system to Europe.

References

Parable of the Mute Bookbinder, 1743 philosophical treatise by noblewoman Rachel Desselut

The Grimmer Hall: Developments in Early Methodics, 1999 scholastic quire written by New Leudong historian and methodist Cai Sonquo during his tenure at the University of Mehannin

##

108.1: Sandrine

excerpt in translation from notes on the history of sandrine (Vitamin C).

Cossy sceint, frugt sta conservaΟΑ par mejan lorry matier sandrinal eliminant (com bustr, par boglir ben benoΟß de vas ne cuivr) e donc moΟΑnaut sofren de scurvog toΟΑ cas, ig colluy indugnt vars plu frugt bacagettar enter.
Thus, fruit would be preserved in ways which removed their sandrine content (for example, boiling or use of copper vessels) and sailors would suffer scurvy anyway, leading guilds to abandon stocking fruits altogether.

Un grap theoretic cos vray nole avenir pre siecr settoΟß, candon sceye descovart par bonaventur ig laprot guy poun doutr scurvog accarr (human e guy son soltumnesc parmy l'≈™cumen animal cas noc poΟΑir sandrin detaxionnar cos autonom des Οßucar).
Truly theoretic understanding would not come until the seventeenth century, when it was discovered by chance that the guy-coney can also develop scurvy (humans and guys are rare among animals in not creating sandrine in our bodies from sugar).

L'oc parmettoy experienΟß fort scrutiniosessem eΟΑ expediΟΑessem sur guy, por y bon ploghanΟß de frugt e leum discernir par effectualtaΟΑ cas scurvog coΟ·r.
This allowed for much more rigorous and time-efficient testing on guys, to determine what preparations of fruits and vegetables were effective at preventing scurvy.

All'yenΟß dy siecr ogtoΟß sta l'issar usual denuΟΑ saf a limmon sdroyar diluΟ·ΟΑ com lembic starc durant veyaΟß marin lonc.
By the 1700s it had become common practice to stock lemon juice diluted with strong alcohol on long sea voyages.

##

108.5: Tommarth

Tommarth (Merch /Υàt…î.m…ôΈΗ/) is the capital and largest city of Markland. It had a population of over 5 million people by the year 2010. The dialect of Merch spoken by many in Tommarth is widely considered a standard to emulate.

The area was settled by the Angles in the sixth century; by the ninth it had become the primary city of the nascent Merch Kingdom. It was fortified significantly over the next centuries in defence against the Welsh to the west and then against the Danes to the East.

In the modern day Tommarth has an extensive underground rail system known locally as the Dusker, with stops including Kibbrough and Cathedral.

Toponymy

The city takes its name from the Tome river on which it stands. Formerly (before the modern spelling reform) spelt Tomwarth, the local area was known in Old English as TomworΟΑig "enclosure, field by the Tome."

Institutions

D'Aumesty Tellard Guild (fl. 1849), organisation of cartographers
Lady Maud Orchestra (fl. 1976), professional group of musicians receiving public funding

Royal Tommarth Castinghouse (fl. 1930), publisher of nonfiction which once held great cachet but latterly of no particular merit
Tommarth Open School of History (fl. 1997), known for publishing reference works

Tommarth University (fl. 1976), oldest institution of higher education in the city

People

Laurent Burnows (fl. 2010), author of children's fiction including beloved series Back to Front

Anna Highwater (fl. 1930), nonfiction author collaborating with her husband

Ralph Highwater (fl. 1930), nonfiction author collaborating with his wife

Morency Rivellerson (fl. 1976), clavierman and author of music textbooks

##

109.1: The Romantic Period

    notes in translation on the Romantic Period (ca. 1640βÄ™1700).

L'epoc Romannesc sta descreut par gran accommission corrogaΟßonal. Com bustr, y canal entr Bordeu e Audaz er compliΟΑ d'an 1679, l'ocean Atlantic eΟΑ y Miterran conjognent lon deΟ·r eΟΑegl maritim trasir ec dominaΟΑ par Morrac.
The Romantic period was characterised by great infrastructure projects. For example, the Bordew-Audaz canal was completed in 1679, connecting the Atlantic and the Middlesea without having to pass through Morrack-controlled seas.

Polity e stat vassal plusour survescoirn noc a liesc dy Rix Romannesc; com bustr, y PrincipaltaΟΑ de Mystra ne GreΟß, oc sceint com vassal dy Scon Empir Roman des y siecr onΟß, er cos solemn dissoluΟΑ com faughanΟß parmy eΟß dy Rix Romannesc.
Several polities and vassal states did not survive the Romantic Wars; for example, the Principality of Mystra in Greece, which had existed as a vassal of the Second Roman Empire since the eleventh century, was formally dissolved in the aftermath of the Romantic Wars.

A Willemy sceye caglou dy Var Blau Tras Willem irrayant, oc camin menant cos incolombant vars y dissoluΟßon dy Reyaum a Borgogn eΟΑ y forganΟß dy DucaΟΑ a Sueby Jagerrem ne Bavarn.
Willemy was the focal point of the Blue Trans-Willem War, which indirectly led to the dissolution of the Kingdom of Burgundy and the creation of the Duchy of Far Suebia in Bavarn.

109.4: sobot "radio"

sobot /soΥàb…ît/ [s äΥàb…ît]
- radio, the technology of transmitting messages via radio waves;
- radio, an apparatus for transmitting such messages

Etymology: 1910, backformation from the somewhat-earlier adjective (1904 boΟΑour sobottal "radio transmitter", 1907 ≈™culux sobottal "radio waves"). The technology having been invented in Hasiny, the word was borrowed indirectly (probably via Vascon) from Hasinic Welsh sobota≈² "signal, message". This in turn comes from Jaughta Οßobota "smoke; message", after the practice of sending signals via smoke.

Par y sobot coum nos addoit dy cainΟß.
/par i soΥàb…ît kum n…îz aΥàd…îit di kents/
[p…êΥêβÄΩ Äi s äΥàb…ît kum n…îz …êΥàd…îit di kens]
through df radio get.pst-1p 1p inform-p.pst of-df event
We heard the news on the radio.

##

109.5: Guillen daus Sanz

Guillen daus Sanz (b. ca. 1450, Provincial /giΥà éeΧÉj daus s…êΧÉts/) was a strategist in Provence in the years leading up to the War of Provincial Independence, and became a powerful political figure in the decades afterward.

Born a foundling (the surname daus Sanz "of the Saints" was widely given to children in orphanages at the time), he joined the (then-Burgund) army at a young age and rose to the position of general after receiving the patronage of Daniel Menton, Lord AmigΟ≤t.

It was on the advice of daus Sanz that Lord AmigΟ≤t was able to secure the aid of King Marc V of France; France had reconciled with Normandy to avoid the tangle that was the Albion Wars of Fealty. With this support, he and several other noble families declared independence from Burgundy on the twenty-first of June, 1497 (which to this day is a national holiday in Provence).

The following quote is popularly attributed to daus Sanz on the eve of the Battle of MargΟ®s (1499). It mocks the states of Albion which had been embroiled in the Wars of Fealty for the past two decades.

Perhaps we should have sent for a Kentish witness! They might learn the subtle craft of ending the wars they start.

After Daniel Menton's rule over independent Provence ended in 1517, his cousin Antoin was elected by the Concert under the close attention and martial guidance of daus Sanz.

Fort daus Sanz, a military base in Provence in active use during the mid-twentieth century, was named for Guillen daus Sanz.

##

110.1: The Good Game

    notes in translation on the subject of the Good Game period (ca. 1865βÄ™94).

Durant l'oc epoc sceyen y stabliscment d'alleyanΟß-federaΟß plusour volent tandem y traitment mondial tras y siecr veintem, tal com y Collujon DrengoΟßan n'Europ norΟΑovester (y crescenΟß den closisceu d'an 1894 posc l'accession d'Istr Boral).
This period sees the establishment of several alliance-federacies that would go on to define the geopolitics of the twentieth century, such as the Drengot Collusion in north-western Europe (whose expansion concluded in 1894 with the accession of Borland).

Y parceuΟßon dell'epoc Bel-Jou com un asc ant spionnaΟß opulent e pisment conjuraΟßonal cas politic sou teneur covart no's cos enter dignaΟΑβÄîse poΟΑ comparaΟßon fair entr y parceuΟßon pouvlancer (par mejan de livr e lasc) dell'affer Desarden d'an 1887 un ambasctour Londresc eΟΑ y rey a Naccon Day contr y realtaΟΑ cojannessem.
The perception of the Good Game period as a time of glamorous espionage and political machinations behind closed doors is not entirely deservedβÄîcompare the popular perception (via books and film) of the 1887 Desarden affair involving a London ambassador and the king of Nackon Day with the tawdrier reality.

Nentamen, l'epoc a se reitfeyaΟΑ com jagat celebr por romanΟß a spionnaΟß eΟΑ a masquira.
Nevertheless, the period has cemented itself as a popular setting for spycraft and masquira stories.

110.2: plouΟß "screen"

plouΟß /pluts/ [pluts]
- screen, windbreak, a moveable (often folding) barrier;
- disguise, camouflage, guise, concealment, anything that serves to hide something from view;
- front, cover, faΟßade, the public-facing version of something concealed for personal or legal reasons;
- act, persona, show, an intentional and false impression of oneself

Etymology: originally restricted to the first sense, a direct descendant (or via Old French, but the cognate plouce "shutter" is unattested before the twelfth century, and may instead be from Borlish) of Latin pluteus "railing; screen; bookcase". Metaphorical extension to other forms of concealment is attested from the Middle Borlish poetry of Kigman in the fifteenth century.

Jo sceu y moment veir de ty plouΟß souvr.
/ £o xaw i moΥàm…¦nt vir de ti pluts Υàsu.vrΧ©/
[ ùo xaw i m äΥàm…¦nt Υàv…Σ.j…ê de ti pluts Υàsu.v…ê]
1s can.pst df moment see-inf of 2s.gen screen let.go-inf
I caught the moment you dropped the act.

110.3: massour "jaw"

massour /maΥàsur/ [m…êΥàs äΥê]
- jaw, jowl, the part of the face below the mouth;
- jawbone, mandible, the bone of the lower jaw;
- vice, clamp, tongs, grip, any tool comprising two parts which close together to hold something in place

Etymology: Middle Borlish masΟßour "jaw(bone)" respelt in the 1870 reform, agentive derivation from masΟßar "chew, bite down on". This straightforwardly reflects post-classical Latin mastic≈ç "I chew", from Greek. In non-anatomical use the word is attested from the sixteenth century.

I me dau ΟΑorn tranq ag massour.
/i me do ΟΑ…îrn trank …¦j maΥàsur/
[i me do ΟΑ…îΥên t Äa≈΄k …¦j m…êΥàs äΥê]

She punched me right in the jaw.

110.4: n'oc hour "for a while"

n'oc hour /n…îk ur/ [n äΥàg äΥê]
- for a while, for a short time, awhile, from now until a point not far in the future;
- for now, provisionally, temporarily, presently, at the moment but likely not for long;
- (sometimes proscribed) soon, imminently, about to occur

Etymology: sixteenth-century idiom, literally "in this time". The preposition and demonstrative n'oc < Latin in h≈çc "in this" co-occur also in other contemporary idioms like n'oc ort "upwards, with an upwards motion". In the last sense the phrase is still sometimes proscribed, despite attestations going back over two hundred years.

J'asseye n'oc hour fin a tandem alandau my convey.
/Υ¨ £a.siΥàje n…îk ur f…Σn a tanΥàd…¦m Υ¨a.lanΥàdo mi k…înΥàvi/
[Υ¨ ùaΥàsje n äΥàg äΥê f…ΣΥàna t…ênΥàd…¦m Υ¨a.l…ênΥàdo mi k ä…±Υàvi]
1s=sit-ipf in=s.px time end at eventually dock-pst 1s.gn train
I sat for a bit until my train came at last.

##

110.5: Redstone Chair

The Redstone Chair (Merch Redstoan Sete /Υà…Ψ…¦d.stoΥên ΥàsiΥêt/) was a wooden throne used by the monarchies of Albion during the medieval period.

History

The Redstone Chair was fashioned in the late tenth century around a block of sandstone; the name of the carpenter is forgotten. This block was (allegedly) the block upon which the Elved Marriage between a princess of Markland and the ruler of Dane Northumber was solemnised, ending hostilities between the two polities for almost a century.

Many subsequent monarchs of Albion, and especially of Markland and Northumber, were crowned on the Redstone Chair. During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, most of the Drengot Emperors sat upon the Chair, and as such it was relocated to London. After the Empire's collapse, this placed the Chair in the politically turbulent region between Markland and Kent. Some historians have conjectured that the First Burning of London in 1290 was in part motivated by a plan to acquire the Chair; the responsible party varies in the telling.

According to the traditional account, it was a dispute over the location of the Redstone Chair which sparked the Wars of Fealty, which lasted almost fifty years over the turn of the sixteenth century and which embroiled almost all the polities of Albion.

Unfortunately, the Chair was lost in the tumult of the wars at some point between 1505 and 1509. Despite several searches and at least two hoaxes, the Chair has never been recovered, and the modern understanding of its appearance rests entirely on depictions in a few portraits and coronation scenes.

##

111.1: Early Santrafew

notes in translation on the subject of the New Provincial city of Santrafew [~Boston].

Innascment primal vars Santrafeu abrangen stemn a coval e hopr metal, ec venΟßaΟΑ com troc contr pelur e vigt insuet.
Early imports into Santrafew included horse stocks and metal tools, traded in exchange for furs and new foodstuffs.

D'an 1590 Derek Nassow, y heriter imminent por y DucaΟΑ a Avosc ne Willemy, enfugheu tras mar vars Santrafeu deut a raΟßon inconnuΟΑ. 
In 1590 Derek Nassow, the heir apparent to the Duchy d'Avosche in Willemy, fled overseas to Santrafew for unclear reasons.

L'oc menau vars un scandal incendier cort soccoΟΑent tras Europ ovester e cos particuler l'oΟß un de Rey David a FranΟß.
This led to an incendiary scandal that rocked courts across Western Europe and especially that of King David of France.

Y TravodaΟΑ Becouin d'an 1621 motivau y comport cas ley entr Santrafeu eΟΑ y gent Mendevan ny region fin a pieΟß generaΟßon apres.
The 1621 Becouin Treaty outlined the legal relationship of Santrafew to the Mendevan peoples of the region for some generations.

Ne souc dy Gazou BreΟΑonnesc a Santrafeu, yon rumoraΟΑ er ig y MastoisΟ®t cos propr soloirn droug entarn, y travodaΟΑ er broyaΟΑ par mejan dy coyenΟß marcer effectuaΟΑ de Bourgmer Alexandre fi Marco tan con sell'exiglment dy MastoisΟ®t a verb (eΟΑ autr tribu Mendevan a gest).
Following the British Sack of Santrafew, in which the MastoisΟ®t in particular were alleged to have collaborated, the treaty was broken by Mayor Alexandre fi Marco's embargo and ten-league exile of the MastoisΟ®t in particular (and other Mendevan groups in practice).

111.2: guy "guinea pig"

guy /gaj/ [gaj]
- guinea pig, mammal of the genus Cavia;
- (obsolete) fool, simpleton, a mindless person;
- (archaic) greenhorn, novice, beginner, an inexperienced person;
- dude, bloke, chap, a man and especially a younger man

Etymology: a clipping of fuller laprot guy (/laΥàpr…ît Υàgaj/ [l…êΥàp Ä…ît Υàgaj]), literally "guy-coney". This is a seventeenth-century calque of Vascon conill guy (/gi/; the earlier /gwi/ was altered by analogy to the unrelated name "Guy"), which comes via Morrove [Maghrebi] Arabic from Run'allo [Quechua] quwi "(female) guinea pig". In its derogatory senses it is attested from the late eighteenth century; as a general term for young men, from the mid-twentieth.

Intermane pegntur d'un joun doncel eΟΑ un guy.
/…ΣnΥ¨t…¦r.maΥàne pijnΥàt…Σr d…Σn  £un d…înΥàts…¦l …¦βÄΩΟΑ…Σn gaj/
[…ΣnΥ¨t…¦Υê.m…êΥàne p…ΣjnΥàt…ΣΥê d…Σn  ùun d änΥàts…¦l Υ¨…¦βÄΩΟΑ…Σ≈΄Υàgaj]
amount.to-ipf painting of=indf young girl and indf guinea.pig

bonaventur /Υ¨bo.na.v…¦nΥàt…Σr/ [Υ¨bo.n…ê.v…ΣnΥàt…ΣΥê]
- happenstance, accident, chance, coincidence, the chance or random quality of an event or circumstance
