111.4: a gest "de facto"

a gest /a  £…¦st/ […êΥà ù…¦st]
- de facto, in practice, in fact, regardless of official or legal status;
- actually, in fact, used to introduce a contradictory remark

Etymology: a Middle Borlish borrowing from legal Latin ad gestum "de facto", literally "to the deed", along with parallel formation a verb < ad verbum "de jure", literally "to the word". In general contexts it is seen from the sixteenth century.

A gest, aucel son progenies jug dez dragon.
/a  £…¦st | oΥàts…¦l s…în Υ¨pro. £eΥànj…¦z  £aj d…¦z draΥàg…în/
[…êΥà ù…¦st |  äΥàts…¦l s…în Υ¨p Äo. ù…ΣΥànj…¦z  ùaj d…¦z d Ä…êΥàg…în]
at deed | bird be.3p descendant still out.of dinosaurs
In fact, birds are descended from dinosaurs.

##

111.5: Battle of Rexam (1483)

The Battle of Rexam (Merch Feight at Rexam /f…¦ Ét …ôt Υà…Ψ…¦ks…ôm/, Cambric Brwydr Car Vante≈² /Υàb…ΨuΥê.d…ô kaΥê v…ônΥàt…¦…§/) was a major combat between Markland [~Mercia] and the Kingdom of Britain [~Wales+] which took place in 1483 during the Wars of Fealty.

Rexam (formerly in the Merch language spelt Wrexham) had been a contested town on the border of Wales and Markland almost from the moment the latter was founded. In 1301, in an act which reignited the Mallor Wars, King Andrew II sacked the town.

The relatively-new Kingdom of Britain (formed of Wales and its outlying tallaths [provinces], along with Kernow [Brittany] and Sodrick [Man and the Hebrides]) started from a position of strength, and managed to extract many concessions from Markland in the early years of the wars. This culminated in a decisive victory at the Battle of Rexam, where the last Duke of Chester was killed.

After this battle, Britain was able to take lasting control of the area around Rexam (though the attack on the city of Chester shortly thereafter was rebuffed). The polity then managed to recuse itself from the continued conflicts of the Wars of Fealty; the rest of the polities of Albion would remain embroiled for another thirty years.

The battle and its aftermath were portrayed in 1931 work "Children of the Battle of Rexam" (released originally in Cambric as Pa Οêywdon y Plant Car Vante≈² "What the Children of Car Vantel Said"), a fictionalised account of the three orphaned children of the Duke of Chester written by schoolteacher Jon Paldreth.

##

112.1: In the Ballroom

excerpt in translation from Ezio Carvallo's 1860 work Tejan de Masquira ou Jalico (In a Mask or a Suit).

Ny salon caye zoum dy murmuranΟß nievrous d'un murΟΑon a collocq, parfis atras con bau sporadic d'hom ricottant.
The salon hummed with the indistinct murmur of myriad conversations, punctuated by occasional barks of laughter.

Matthias eye sarpentin entr haΟßot de jolleyour, y hal parcorrent a gas doutr plumaΟß remeyaΟßant e caliΟß ne crystal.
Matthias weaved between knots of revellers, his eyes scanning the room over bobbing plumes and crystal flutes.

Bojay minΟß vacillan ne candellabr adornaΟΑ, y tapeΟßment eΟΑ y paiΟΑ trave bagnant con splendiscment quemant.
Tapers flickered in ornate candelabras, casting a warm glow over the wall-hangings and the parquet floors.

Sur un los ny cogn jagerrem, un party a raspour luyen ne coutrant un xivol nervous cant per trasen y hal ne voltiscent, lorry cottegl e mantel faint souvolar capaΟß.
On a dais at the far end, a coterie of string players sawed through a sprightly xivola as couples whirled around the room, their skirts and capes sweeping widely.

Matthias cave parcevet de visaΟß seyon connuΟΑ; y nas aquilin all'ambasctour Cantesc, y sourey poliΟΑ a Domn Gassola, un bevey diamantin relugnt seyon vis nuverrem sur y col ag Duches a Astorga.
Matthias caught glimpses of familiar faces; the Kentish ambassador's aquiline nose, Lady Gassola's polished smile, a sparkling diamond parure he'd last seen adorning the Duchess of Astorga.

112.2: imminent "prospective"

imminent /Υ¨…Σ.miΥàn…¦nt/ [Υ¨…Σ.m…ΣΥàn…¦nt]
- projecting, overhanging, suspended above;
- prospective, anticipated, likely or expected to happen;
- newly elected, (of a candidate) voted in but having not yet taken office;
- apparent, (of an heir) having a claim to a title that cannot be superseded by the birth of anyone else

Etymology: Middle Boral borrowing from Latin imminΡ™ns, present participle of immine≈ç "I overhang; I am intent upon, I am near at hand". The verb imminir was briefly current but had obsolesced by the end of the seventeenth century. Its original use in Borlish was in the phrase heriter imminent "heir apparent", as opposed to heriter provisoir "heir presumptive".

My paΟΑr es collocquant con y gournour imminent.
/mi Υàpa.ΟΑrΧ© …¦z Υ¨k…î.l…îΥàkwant k…în i gurΥànur Υ¨…Σ.miΥàn…¦nt/
[mi ΥàpaβÄΩ Ä…Σz Υ¨k…î.l äΥàkwant k…în i g äΥêΥàn äΥêβÄΩΥ¨ Ä…Σ.m…ΣΥàn…¦nt]

My father is interviewing the governor-elect.

112.3: sou teneur covart "in secret"

sou teneur covart /su teΥànawr koΥàvart/ [su t…ΣΥàna.w…ê k äΥàv…êΥêt]
- in the dark, under the cover of darkness, without any light;
- in secret, secretly, out of view, sub rosa, concealed from any eavesdroppers or spies

Etymology: idiom attested from the eighteenth century, literally "under covered darkness"; probably formed by analogy to preexisting sou ciel ovart "in plain view", literally "under open sky". The noun teneur is an uncomplicated descendant from Latin tenebrae "darkness"; the adjective covart is the past participle of covrir "to cover", from Latin cooperi≈ç "I cover, overwhelm, clothe".

Il son se rajognent sou teneur covart pieΟß semman.
/…Σl s…în se raΥà £…îj.n…¦nt su teΥànawr koΥàvart pj…¦ts s…¦Υàman/
[…ΣΥàs…în se  Ä…êΥà ù…îj.n…ênt su t…ΣΥàna.w…ê k äΥàv…êΥêt pj…¦s s…ΣΥàman]
3p be.3p rfl meet-p.prs under darkness cover.p.pst some week
They have been meeting in secret for a few weeks.

112.4: abrangir "include"

abrangir /Υ¨a.branΥà £…Σr/ [Υ¨a.b Ä…ênΥà ù…ΣΥê]
- include, comprise, comprehend, to consider a part of some set of things;
- span, cover, contain, to extend sufficiently so as to include

Etymology: via Scholastic Latin abrange≈ç "I include" from Portingale abranger "include", itself of obscure origin. Possibly related to Old French rengier "to line up, form rows", from Frankish (either hring "ring" or ranc "upright"); otherwise perhaps an unconventional descendant of Latin verg≈ç "I bend, am situated".

Y pascman abrange zaquesc e desc assovent y dou.

The eatery had both snacks and full meals.

112.5: North American History Quiz

from the 1930 book Prove your Knowledge!, a part of the series Games for Sorties and Excourses. This was a collection of casual party games compiled and published by Merch couple Anna and Ralph Highwater through the Royal Tommarth Castinghouse.

History XI: The Novomund (answers on sheet 236)

1. Which emperor of Tavancy [~the Inca], whose 61-year reign from 1599 until 1660 is the longest of any monarch in Cappatian history, is suspected to have been assasinated by Morrack [Morrocan] agents in order to be able to increase the volume and decrease the price of silver exports?

2. For whom were the Sturgovan [Appalachian] Mountains named?

3. With which Mendevan people did the nascent polity of New Provence principally trade during the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries?

4. In what year was the Steeplepost Tapestry in the Ambrosian city of Aiga d'Angoixa [~Quebec City] erected, the first device capable of displaying an image communicated via the steeplemesh?

5. What astronomical event inspired the naming of the major Mashick city of Moshtar [~San Francisco] in 1780?

6. What property of matter was discovered by the Cappatian city of VaronΟΗw [~VitΟ≥ria, ES]'s NΟΩstad Atellier in 1902?

7. Which was the first of the staddomains [colonial tradeposts] along the eastern Cappatian coast to be established, with an official chronicle dating to 1561?

8. A predecessor to the Lineball Global Tourney, what was the name of the international lineball competition first held in AwasΟΚconda [~ParanΟΓ] in 1909 between twenty teams?

9. Which was the only Novomundine polity to have a delegation present at the signing of the Laic Declarations in Belgrade?

10. What was the name of the first Chinese ship to alight in the Novomund?

113.1: Introduction to a Recipe

excerpt in translation from acclaimed 1880 mealbook Pasti Mezziterreni (Middlesea Meals), written by world-traveller Josforo Sant'Angelo.

Farrago a Pot Cypriot
Cypriot Pan-Farrago

PreΟΑauc vos seyaΟΑ feliΟß aΟΑief a un orin ny rimot Masrecq broucar
Unless you have the good fortune to hail from the Masreck coast 

(degnant par boc y staddenzan CappaΟßan ain un kes cosvour, la fait quippe des laiΟΑ a vacq),
(although I hear the Cappatian staddenzen have a similar cheese, there made of course with cow's milk),

vos stoura se contentar an kes Cypriot quidam conservaΟΑ sou salmoir oc, ja pos ragostant cos immachiabr nell'oc mistur pouvler,
you will need to content yourself to brine-stored Cypriot cheese which, while perfectly appetising in this traditional mΟ©lange,

noc incaint vars y repasc deliΟßous attegndr meyon pris a cost y mar posc un jorn passaΟΑ ny ruin a Knossos.
is unlikely to match the exquisite meal I took by the sea after a day spent among the ruins of Knossos.

Se pos reclamar ig jo no fos cos forΟßaΟΑ for souvolant ne souc de grantaΟΑ, emmenaΟΑ lonc y mesur par y gra d'asc reΟΑr mey;
One might object that I must simply have been swept up in grandeur, carried along by the weight of aeons behind me;

de my sgart jo vil aglon y cociner reconnoscr.
for my part I am inclined to credit the chef.

113.2: raspar "scrape"

raspar /raΥàspar/ [ Ä…êΥàsp…êΥê]
- scrape, scratch, graze, grate, rasp, to draw a sharp or rough object along a surface while exerting pressure;
- play, bow, to create sound on a string instrument by means of drawing a bow across a string

Etymology: twelfth-century borrowing from Old French rasper "scratch, grate", itself from synonymous hrasp≈çn in Frankish. The extension to string playing, as well as the noun raspour "string player", is seen from the late sixteenth century.

J'yemoy sciΟΑ aΟ·r kes raspaΟΑ demay sad.

113.3: parcorrir "scan"

- scan, examine, go over, skim, to look at sequentially or from one end to the other;

113.4: souvolar "sweep"

souvolar /Υ¨su.voΥàlar/ [Υ¨su.v äΥàl…êΥê]
- sweep, be swept, to be caused to rise up into the air from the ground (as of the clothing of a person spinning, or of dust);
- glide, to move through the air gracefully and unpropelled;
- go along with something, play along, to cooperate in continuing something by passively allowing it;
- (in a performative context) yes-and, commit to the bit, take things in stride, go with the flow, stay in character

Etymology: borrowing from Latin subvol≈ç "I fly upwards from below", adapted to existing sou- "under" and volar "fly". In metaphorical use the word is attested from the eighteenth century; in a theatrical context it is seen from 1888, originally in use by a specific school of acting in Axbane.

Y rimband ag fruyour souvolaurn apar.
/i r…ΣmΥàband …¦j fraΥàjur Υ¨su.voΥàlornΧ© aΥàpar/
[i  Ä…ΣmΥàband …¦j f Ä…ê ù äΥê Υ¨su.v äΥàloΥên …êΥàp…êΥê]
df ribbon at.df dancer sweep-pst-3p through
The dancer's ribbons swept through the air.

113.5: Delicious Etymology

excerpts in translation from the Rotcham Veriloquy, the premier etymological dictionary of Merch. It has been published in several editions since the late nineteenth century via the press of the university of Rotcham [Rochdale].

- mikecorn n. /Υàm…ôik.k…îΥên/ [maize] a cereal grass of Lower Mendevan [Central American] origin. 1530 compound of mike alt. mayk, makee and corn < Morrove [Moroccan Arabic] ΌÖΌäΌÉ ¬Ζ maik (1502) < a language of the Arcabil, poss. Veonic [Wayuu] maiki "mikecorn".
- shadome n. /Υà Éa.duΥêm/ [tomato] a plant of the nightshade kind having edible fruits; the fruit of this plant. 1570 < Vascon xadoma (1534) < Morrove Ί¥ΊΖΌÖ ¬Ζ shed'oma (1510) < Normal Nawat [Classical Nahuatl] shitomatl, denoting a kind of fruit used in stews and sauces. This is thought to be a derivative from the verb tomΡ¹wi "to swell".
- shockle n. /Υà É…î.k…ôl/ [chocolate] a drink or later a foodstuff prepared from the quattle [cacao] bean. 1623 backwards from shockled adj. /Υà É…î.kl…ôd/ "containing shockle" < Vascon xoc(o)lat (1596) < unknown root, poss. Nawat chicol "wooden stick" or Mayattan chocol "hot".
- tartover n. /taΥêΥàtuΥê.…ô/ [potato] a plant of the nightshade kind with starchy underground tubers. 1583 alt. tartove < Vascon tartova (moresca) "(Moorish) truffle" (1555) < Tuscan tartuvo alt. tartuva "truffle" < Latin terrae t≈Ϊber "truffle, lit. tuber of the earth". See also familiar and hypocoristic forms like tover /tuΥê.…ô/, tattow /t…ôΥàtuΥê/ and tatter /Υàta.t…ô/.
- tashban n. /Υàta É.ban/ [vanilla] a plant of the orchid kind bearing podlike fruit whose seeds yield a extract used for flavouring and perfuming. 1608 < Vascon taxbΟ†n (1567) or French tacheban < Morrove ΊΣΊ¥Ί®ΊßΌÜ tashbΡ¹n (1518) < poss. Nawat tashpΡ¹n, or directly < Thebevan [Totonac] ta≈²pΟΓn, denoting a specific variety of the tashban orchid.

114.1: A Transit of Venus

excerpt in translation from scitation questions lifted from Safford Technic Schole's Spring 89 course Along Natural Theory V: Astronomy from Lorenzo to the Alluning.

Respos dar an soutas calscon dell'ec entrou sevent a mesur de tre hour.
Answer any number of the following questions in the next three hours.

2. Soumoin jaint vars BensaΟ≠d ne L'Appragl de CommuneyaΟßon, argument fair sur y gimmolt mondial d'an 1677 por y trasson de Maucr parcevir com bustr primal de collaboraΟßon theoretic tars tarm.

114.2: xivol "waltz"

xivol /ksiΥàv…îl/ [ks…ΣΥàv…îl]
- waltz, xivola, a style of dance originating in Vascony during the Long Peace (1770βÄ™1815) characterised by triple time and a rapid tempo;
- metaphorically, the state of many people simultaneously carrying out independent tasks and moving around each other without getting in each other's way

Etymology: via French xivole from Vascon xΟ§vola /Υà Éi.vo.la/ "waltz". This itself is a borrowing from the earlier and slightly different Italian dance scivola "waltz, word stressed on the third-from-last syllable", from verb scivolare "glide, slip". This is plausibly a descendant of Latin exsΡΪbil≈ç "I hiss forth".

Although the original xivola dance had a rather restrained tempo, the average performance speed of xivola music has greatly increased over time. Xivola is usually conceived of as a crowd dance, in which an entire roomful of people participates.

Sy xivol aye tug aglon de skisteΟß.
/si ksiΥàv…îl …¦Υàje taj …¦jΥàl…în de sk…ΣΥàst…¦ts/
[si ks…ΣΥàv…îl iΥà ùe taj …ΣjΥàl…în de sk…ΣΥàst…¦s]
3s.gn waltz have-ipf passion instead of elegance
He waltzed with more passion than grace.

114.3: trave "parquetry"

trave /traΥàve/ [t Ä…êΥàve]
- parquetry, the technique of using wooden tiles to create geometric patterns especially as used in flooring;
- jigsaw, tangram, a puzzle in which the aim is to reconstruct a picture or shape that has been cut into small pieces

Etymology: from a specialised use of Middle French travΟ© "crossed with wooden beams", via noun traf, tref "beam, mast" from Latin trabs "timber, beam, rafter". The puzzle sense is attested from the late nineteenth century.

Il son y trave costroint toΟΑ y jorn.

They've been doing the jigsaw all day.

114.4: soumoin jair "reference"

soumoin jair /suΥàm…îin  £er/ [s äΥàm…îin  ùeΥê]
- reference, cite, to quote the words of another with attribution;
- credit, shout out, acknowledge, to publicly mention someone especially in order to thank them

Etymology: idiom meaning "to throw reference" attested from the seventeenth century. The noun soumoin is a deverbal of soumonir "prompt, remind", which descends from Latin summone≈ç "I privately advise, give a hint". The verb jair comes from Latin iaci≈ç "I throw, fling".

Nos veleu soumoin jair a nossy conemour.

1p will.sj-1p reference throw-inf to 1p.gn regular

##

114.5: On the Bookshelf

In Sickness and in Health: Medicine in pre-Arnoutszen Europe is a quire chronicling the development of medical practice in Europe from antiquity until the time of physician Laurz Arnoutszen in the sixteenth century (the adoption of whose techniques constituted a sea-change in the effectiveness of medicine). It was written by Andrew Knowles in the mid-twentieth century. It was published in Kent by Winchester Scholastic Primers in multiple editions; the second edition was released in 1961 with a foreword by Dr Laura Norris, a lecturer in Heredity at Canterburgh University.

New Skies, Old Stories (originally published in Provincial French as NovΟ® Cial, Anzan Conte) is a work of popular history detailing the origins of the various threads of parachthon fiction. It was written by Simon d'Estiala and published by Prindor MarsillΟ®s in 1980. Its thesis is that many of the tropes often considered to have been invented by particular parachthon authors are in fact simply examples of existing tropes used in new settings.

Men in Black (originally published in Parisian French as Hommes en Noir: une Οâtoire d'Espionnage) is a layperson's account of the history of spycraft across the world. It was written by Agathe Bisset and published by Paris University Editions in 2003.

The Alphabetical Albion is an encyclopaedia of the political history of Albion. It was written by a consortium of authors including Lucas Osborne and published by Winchester Scholastic Primers in 1920. The book organises itself into twenty-six sections, one for each letter of the English alphabet in order, with varying levels of contortion required to justify the conceit. Headings include "F: The Wars of Fealty", "Q: Unanswered Questions" and "X: The Necessity of Xenia".

##

115.1: The Numeric Week

excerpt in translation from the 14th October 1950 edition of the Roxa Comba, in their regular column called The Numeric Week. The Roxa Comba is a weekly paper based in the Groin [A CoruΟ±a] and affiliated loosely with the Russet Dove media guild.

Y Semman cas Nombr - SambaΟΑ 14 Octobr 1950
The Numeric Week - Saturday 14th October 1950

1500: Y lonctum par lau de Japetos III, y novel conjogntur Atlantic oy depos des Morrac vars Ambrosia eΟΑ ayent sy preu boΟΑanΟß augtaΟΑ je Maucr derran. VellisquaΟΑ cos original pall'eΟß administraΟßon a catr man d'an 1943, l'accommission dene demoraΟΑ veg monfeyaΟΑ deut ag confluyenΟß de hey cas bors e, lon digr, y sounΟß dy var. Y snar conjogn y citaΟΑ de Gadir ag floy Soux e Paratzon ne Brasil auster.
1500: The length in leagues of Japetos III, the new Atlantic link laid down between Morrack and Ambrosia which conveyed its first messages this last Wednesday. Originally conceived by the two administrations in concert in 1943, the endeavour was delayed several times due both to budgetary concerns and, of course, the outbreak of war. The cable connects the cities of Gadire on the river Soux [Agadir, Morocca] and Paratzon in eastern Brasil [St John's, Newfoundland].

3: L'enombr de vouΟΑ dessur dell'opposson ny cambr Yansief yon y soumission dy clou Zachet por interrogment sull'effectualtaΟΑ de molin a forΟß sougl se cof yer eligt, ne souc d'un veΟΑrvent imprefait cas y jure suajonnant dy Minister a Οêar. Partejan aun (ΟΑa lorry nosthubr sey visant vars y Collujon ben contrig) caglou irrayaΟΑ cos divers sull'eΟß advantaΟß cohernt a men basant a astraphor DrengoΟßan e sull'occasion de l'Andron a Alchemy ne Lisbon extollir com mojol ny doujug mondial.
3: The number of votes by which the Mortar coalition's proposal to fund effectivity research into threshold force mills [nuclear power plants] passed in the Yansieve chamber yesterday, after a surprising volte-face from the Ministry for Farming advisory board. Proponents have (depending on their sympathies or lack thereof regarding the Collusion) pointed variously to the benefits of relying less on Drengot astraphor [electricity] and of elevating Lisbon's Faculty of Alchemy to a hub on the world stage.

115.2: trasson "transit"

- transit, crossing, an instance of something moving across or through something else;
- transition, change, the process of change from one state to another;
- link, bridge, connection, anything placed between two distinct sections

115.3: exedr "waiting room"

exedr /…¦kΥàs…¦.drΧ©/ […ΣgΥàz…¦.d…ê]
- antechamber, anteroom, forecourt, vestibule, entrance hall, a room between the outside and the main interior of a building;
- (historical) exedra, a small room for discussion lined with benches;
- waiting room, lounge, a space inside a public for people to sit and wait (especially a particularly well-appointed or luxurious such space);

115.4: quarvol "airport"

quarvol /kwarΥàv…îl/ [kw…êΥêΥàv…îl]
- airport, an area designed for landing and takeoff of commercial passenger aircraft

Etymology: early twentieth century, portmanteau of quarvet "(bus, taxi, ferry) station, a stopping place for transportation" and vol "flight". The noun quarvet is a diminutive of quarf "docks, harbour", borrowed in the Old Borlish period from Old English hwearf "embankment, wharf". The noun vol is a deverbal from volar "fly", from Latin vol≈ç "I fly".

Cay l'oc convey visaΟΑ ag quarvol?

fall df=s.px train bound to.df airport
Is this the train to the airport?

##

115.5: Turbulent Times

collected notes on various events and periods which took place at the time of the Long Peace (ca. 1770βÄ™1815).

The Decadence of Belgrade refers to the Kingdom of the Danaw [Danube] in its final decades before the Second German War (1815βÄ™23) and the subsequent dissolution of the state. The Decadence also represents the territorial maximum of the kingdom, having wrested Bulgary [Bulgaria] from the suzerainty of the Second Roman Empire [~the Byzantines] after the latter's final collapse in the seventeenth century, and more recently having consolidated control over lands at the northwestern border with Bavarn [Bavaria].

The Mendevan Unmooring (Borlish DeshormejanΟß Mendevan /deΥ¨z…îr.meΥà £ants Υ¨m…¦n.deΥàvan/) is the collective name for the various wars and diplomatic developments at the turn of the nineteenth century which led to the weakening and sometimes severing of formal ties of polities in Mendeva [North America] from those in the Vetomund [Old World]. The more explosive conflicts such as the War for Ambrosia [~east and north of the Great Lakes] and the Hasinic Revolution [~lower part of the Mississipi watershed] (against Vascony and Britain, respectively) often overshadow quieter events like the Departure of Mashick [~Mexico].

The Romantic Revival (Borlish ResorgenΟß Romannesc /reΥàz…îr.g…¦nts Υ¨ro.maΥàn…¦x/) was an artistic and political movement which arose towards the end of the eighteenth century. It was characterised by optimistic portrayals of heroism, and especially a belief in a 'brighter past' in the seventeenth century that could be recaptured. It is due to this movement's depiction that a loose congeries of wars in the mid-seventeenth century have become known as the Romantic Wars.

116.1: Fun-loving Firefighter

excerpt in translation from MescainΟß ben Delit? "Accident or Crime?", one of a series of episodic stories about first responders in Damvath, published since the 1990s by a collective of Borlish authors.

Zaf er denuΟΑ quΟ·scour dec apar l'aye l'occajon a foyaΟßar eΟΑ a gent aΟ·ΟΑar veg temporane.
Zaf had become a firefighter because it let him show off and help people at the same time.

L'oc no sta bogr autraΟΑ; sull'oc, toΟΑ cas, aye i jamay y vertaΟΑ dis.
That hadn't changed; that, at least, he had been truthful about.

I cof ancour l'ig ΟΑougl meΟΑes des y toxen oΟ·r, des y vagn viglar con sy balis briglant e sy vagiscour hurlant, des port caugtrant deur lorry souglβÄîsouvanΟß y gahaΟΑasterrem de gent feirn sy cour battr. 
He still got the same rush from hearing the alarm, from driving the engine with its brilliant lights and screaming noisemakers, from kicking in doorsβÄîpeople's worst days made him feel alive.

L'ec sceusn solciΟΑ inquillir n'un hom incaindessem vars cuidanΟß de sey.
Someone more prone to self-reflection might have felt qualms about that.

A sgart calscon, y squadron encontraurn noc veram mort tal mout.
In any case, the crew didn't actually see deaths that often.

Nevertheless, it was inevitable that sometimes the stretcher-bearers were occupied halfway across the city, or that a routine call turned deadly.

116.2: ΟΑar "farming"

ΟΑar /ΟΑar/ [ΟΑ…êΥê]
- farming, farmwork, agriculture, tilth, the business and activity of cultivating land and raising livestock;
- (in certain idiomatic expressions, more generally) work, toil, labour, drudge, any particularly strenuous activity;
- (colloquially) sex, intercourse, the act of having sex with somebody

Etymology: Middle Borlish deverbal from ΟΑarar "plough, till; farm; labour", originally restricted to crop production and not animal husbandry. This descends uncomplicatedly from Latin adar≈ç "I plough, till". In the last sense it is attested since the early twentieth century but is thought to be much older; cf. Tuscan arare "to plough; to have sex with".

Whether or not the namers of the Minister a Οêar "Ministry for Agriculture" were aware of the slang usage, many caricaturists have since seized on the pun.

L'a un sgart mallovicher de ΟΑar.
/la …Σn sgart Υ¨ma.lo.v…ΣΥàk…¦r de ΟΑar/

3s=have indf view overly.optimistic of farming
He's very naΟ·ve about farmwork.

116.3: veΟΑrvent "vane"

veΟΑrvent /Υ¨v…¦.ΟΑrΧ©Υàv…¦nt/ [Υ¨v…¦.ΟΑ…êΥàv…¦nt]
- vane, weathervane, weathercock, a  mechanical device rotating around one axis and attached to an elevated object such as a roof for showing the direction of the wind;
- anemometer, windmeter, a more sophisticated device for measuring and recording the speed and direction of the wind;
- (metaphorically) volte-face, about-face, U-turn, flip-flop, a sudden reversal of opinion or strategy

Etymology: folk-etymological alteration of Old Borlish veΟΑrevit "vane" under the influence of vent "wind" < synonymous ventus in Latin. The Old Borlish is a borrowing from Old Norrish veΟΑrviti "vane", literally "weather knower". Synchronically interpretable as "wind-glass"; though they are not normally made of glass, the word has been analysed as referring to glassware used for measurement. In metaphorical usage the word is seen from the eighteenth century.

Y torvegl indugs ec mesuranΟß suprem de veΟΑrvent tras istr.
/i t…îrΥàvijl …ΣnΥàdajz …¦k Υ¨me.ziΥàrants siΥàpr…¦m de Υ¨v…¦.ΟΑrΧ©Υàv…¦nt traz Υà…Σ.strΧ©/
[i t äΥêΥàv…Σjl …ΣnΥàdajz …¦ Υ¨me.z…ΣΥà Äans s…ΣΥàp Ä…¦m de Υ¨v…¦.ΟΑ…êΥàv…¦nt t Äaz Υà…Σ.st…ê]
df storm induce.pst p.px output supreme of vane across island
The storm led to record windspeed measurements nationwide.

116.4: clou "segment"

clou /klu/ [klu]
- clove, segment, one of the parts (as of a fruit or bulb) into which any body naturally separates or is divided;
- sector, piece, slice, part of a circle bounded by two radii and an arc they subtend (also of any circular object);
- faction, party, bloc, a political organisation of voters and/or politicians who share common goals

Etymology: from Old Borlish clou /klu(v)/ "clove (of garlic)", borrowed from synonymous clufu in Old English. The meaning was extended to other portions of things in the Middle Borlish period; in the phrase clou politic "political party" it is attested from the seventeenth century.

Il aun cascun dou clou a vastel ja paut.
/…Σl on kaΥàx…Σn du klu a vaΥàst…¦l  £a pot/

3p have.3p each two piece at cake already eat.p.pst
They've eaten two pieces of cake each.

##

116.5: Kernovan Conjuring

The Kernovan Conjuring (Borlish ConjuraΟßon Cornovan /Υ¨k…î…≤. £…ΣΥàrants Υ¨k…îr.noΥàvan/) was a collaboration between the Kingdom of Greater Devon and Roger Drengot, Duke of Normandy to press the Duke's claim to the throne of Kent. This plot is considered the proximate cause for the creation of the First Drengot Empire across Gaul [France] and southern Albion [Great Britain].

The Conjuring was carried out in 1029, following the death of King Rodulf of Kent. Kent under the long-reigning Rodulf had prospered, with recognised supremacy over the rump Wessex (albeit not uncontested by Devon) and over Essex to the north. After eight decades of political manoeuvring, Rodulf lingered on his deathbed and the succession was deeply uncertain.

Although no references to a written treaty have survived, it is presumed that the House of Kernow aided Normandy in their bid for the Kentish throne in exchange for support in becoming the new ruling dynasty of Greater Devon. This bargain was upheld, but it only took a couple of decades for Greater Devon to become a vassal of the First Drengot Empire.

In what was either impressive long-term strategy or merely opportunism, the victorious Roger of Normandy would go on to conquer Kent's neighbours, including Essex and Markland, and then venture south to annex much of the rest of Gaul. He was crowned Emperor Roger I in the midst of this territorial expansion in 1038.

The recently-unified Wales under Cadwallan (erstwhile King of Wynneth) managed to remain mostly free of Drengot control. This was likely achieved by steering a course between Greater Devon and the Drengot, neither of whom wanted to other to have dominion over Wales.

## 

117.1: New Urban Mesh

notes in translation on the subject of the New Urban Mesh.

Y Nou Sar Urban intermane un alleyanΟß de citaΟΑ autonom naΟΑusc n'Europ norΟΑovester durant y siecr novoΟß.
The New Urban Mesh was an alliance of somewhat autonomous cities in Northwest Europe in the nineteenth century.

Parmy sy sodal stan Londr, Conster, Ausbagn, Pentros, Amstel, Anvers e Gant.
Among its members were London, Conster [Colchester], Axbane, Pentrose, Amstel [Amsterdam], Anvers [Antwerp] and Gant [Ghent].

Y Sar smargoy cos contemporane e parallel a apprestanΟß politic comportant allors, tal com y CitaΟΑ Floyan lonc y Danau.
The Mesh emerged contemporaneously with similar political structures elsewhere, such as the Riverine Cities along the Danaw [Danube].

Plusour parmy y citaΟΑ ag Sar stan cos simoltane part de polity ja sceint (com bustr, Ausbagn e Pentros no stan jamay sovran a verb deur Istr Boral).
Several of the Mesh's cities were simultaneously part of pre-existing polities (for example, Axbane and Pentrose were never legally independent from Borland).

L'oc ort de tal federaΟß de stat citaΟΑer se caf ascreut all'aΟΑoinΟß pouvlancer eΟΑ y crescenΟß de citaΟΑ rapid tras l'approism dell'epoc all'Atelier Mondial.
This rise of such federacies of city-states has been attributed to the rapid population growth and expansion of cities in the run-up to the Global Workshop period.

Y Sar cos particuler sta ag rostr de machinaΟßon cas vascel costroinΟß, eΟΑ i poΟΑe un ronΟß remarcabr cavir dy recoglenΟß de carbon mondial.
The Mesh in particular was at the forefront of shipbuilding technology, and had access to an appreciable fraction of the world's coal production.

##

117.2: mallovicher "naΟ·ve"

mallovicher /Υ¨ma.lo.viΥàk…¦r/ [Υ¨ma.l ä.v…ΣΥàk…¦Υê]
- naΟ·ve, rose-tinted, ingenuous, overly optimistic or blind to obvious pitfalls and downsides;
- chipper, bubbly, pert, peppy, cheerful in an annoying way

Etymology: 19C, adjectival derivation from mallovic "someone who is naΟ·ve and cheerful to a ridiculous or irritating extent". This is a borrowing from synonymous Merch mallowick or directly from the character Mall-Lowick /Υàmal Υàlu.…Σk/ and her eponymous novel, in which she is a young girl subjected to various travails but never losing her hopeful demeanour. Her name is a compound of Mall, a hypocoristic form of Mary, and Lowick, which comes via Old French LoΟ·c from Frankish HloΟΑwig (cf. Vascon Luixa and Napolitan Ludgia [and IRL Louise]). The name was applied almost immediately in a mocking way to a person seen as similar to Mall-Lowick.

J'osc sy cigl novel; i's mallovicher sad demay.
/ £ox si tsajl noΥàv…¦l | …Σz Υ¨ma.lo.v…ΣΥàk…¦r sad deΥàme/
[ ùox si tsajl n äΥàv…¦l | …Σz Υ¨ma.l ä.v…ΣΥàk…¦Υê sad d…ΣΥàme]
1s=hate 3s.gn SO new | 3s=be pert gravely too
I hate her new girlfriend; she's far too chipper.

##

117.3: grabattour "paramedic"

grabattour /Υ¨gra.baΥàtur/ [Υ¨g Äa.b…êΥàt äΥê]
- paramedic, EMT, a person trained to medically stabilise injured people on-site before they can be transported to a hospital;
- (historical, esp. Millstone War) stretcher-bearer, a soldier or auxiliary serviceman who carries the wounded away from the battlefield on stretchers

Etymology: 20C, agentive derivation from grabat "stretcher, gurney; emergency medical transport". This is a borrowing via another Romance language from Latin grabΡ¹tus "cot, pallet, camp bed", from Greek. Transfer from the military context to peacetime healthcare is attested from the 1950s.

J'addoceu my sour d'ig j'er grabattour parsiscent.
/Υ¨ £a.doΥàtsaw mi sur daj  £…¦r Υ¨gra.baΥàtur Υ¨par.ziΥàx…¦nt/
[Υ¨ ùa.d äΥàtsaw mi s äΥê daj  ù…¦Υê Υ¨g Äa.b…êΥàt äΥê Υ¨p…êΥê.z…ΣΥàΟß…¦nt]
1s=inform-pst 1s.gn sister of-comp 1s=cop.ipf EMT study-p.pst
I told my sister I was training to be a paramedic.

117.4: extollir "elevate"

extollir /Υ¨…¦k.st…îΥàl…Σr/ [Υ¨…¦k.st äΥàl…ΣΥê]
- elevate, raise, uplift, heighten, hoist, to cause to be at a higher position;
- promote, advance, to give a higher position in an organisation;
- upgrade, enhance, to provide with better features

Etymology: 16C, borrowing from Latin extoll≈ç "I elevate, erect, praise". Extension to promotion in a company occurred in the nineteenth century; to technology in the twentieth.

Jo's cuidant cos nuvr a my dois extollir.
/ £…îz kajΥàdant k…îz Υàn…Σ.vrΧ© a mi d…îjz Υ¨…¦k.st…îΥàl…Σr/
[ ùoz k…êjΥàda≈΄ k äΥàzn…ΣvβÄΩ Äa mi d…îjz Υ¨…¦k.st äΥàl…ΣΥê]
1s=be think-p.prs adv recent at 1s.gn smartphone upgrade-inf
I've been meaning to upgrade my phone.
I've been meaning to upgrade my phone.

##

117.5: Sound Changes from Vulgar Latin to Borlish

The dialects of Borlish are the only surviving descendants of the Insular variety of Vulgar Latin, which comprised Borlish Latin and British Latin. This variety was not overly divergent from the contemporary (ca. 3rd to 5th century) Latin of the continent, with the following exceptions:

    The loss of intervocalic  /w/, as evidenced in inscriptions by e.g. clam "key" (continental clavem) and denio "I reach" (continental devenio).
    The loss of wordfinal  /s/, probably preceded by a period of debuccalisation where final  was pronounced /h/. The dating of this sound change is controversial; cf. fifth-century *a femna* "the women (acc.)" (Classical hΡ¹s fΡ™minΡ¹s "these women").

The second of these local changes was grammatically significant, as it weakened the distinctions between forms in noun declensions. Indeed, already by the Old Borlish period all number and case distinctions in nouns have been lost.

    Posttonic vowel loss
    V > βàÖ / "VC_CV
    Final syllable simplification
    m > n / _# if stressed
    m s > βàÖ / _# otherwise

e.g. amictate /Υ¨a.mikΥàta.te/ "friendship" < amΡΪcitΡ¹tem

    Palatalisation part 1
    [t k]j [d g]j > t É d £
    k g > t É d £ / _[i e]
    k > j / _C except [r l w j]
    Fortition
    w > Έ≤ /#_
    j > d £ / #_, V_V

e.g. iazo /Υàd £at.t Éo/ "I lie down" < iace≈ç

    Consonant lenition part 1
    p t k b d g s > b d g Έ≤ ΟΑ …Θ z / V_[V m l r n j w]
    Palatalisation part 2
    nj lj > …≤  é
    Cj > C ≤ > jC except w or word initially
    Final vowels reduce to schwa (unless stressed)
    V > …ô / _# unless stressed

e.g. sabe /Υàsa.b…ô/ "he knows" < sapit; saibe /Υàsaj.b…ô/ "I know" < sapi≈ç

    Weak consonants lost
    [Έ≤ ΟΑ …Θ] > βàÖ / V_V
    Mid-vowels break in stressed open syllables
    …¦ e …î o > je e o ow / stressed open
    …¦ …î > e o / elsewhere
    Lenition part 2
    p t k b d g > b d g Έ≤ ΟΑ …Θ / V_V
    C: > C
    Loss of much final schwa
    …ô > βàÖ / except after consonant clusters

e.g. liei /ljed £/ "halt" < Insular laedicum "collision"

    Affricate shift
    t É d £ > ts  £
    sk palatalises (fills gap with  £), Έ≤ shift
    sk Έ≤ >  É v
    Vowel u is fronted
    u > y
    Mid-vowel rhotic shift
    e > a / _rC
    e > i / _r#
    Full loss of schwa
    …ô > βàÖ
    Epenthesis before r
    βàÖ > d / [n …≤]_r
    βàÖ > b / m_r

e.g. cendre /Υàtsen.drΧ©/ "ash" < cinerem; puΟΑir /pyΥàΟΑir/ "rot" < p≈ΪtΡ™re

    Vowels become lax in closed syllables
    i e o y > …Σ …¦ …î  è / closed syllables
    Final consonants devoice
    b d g v z ΟΑ  £ > p t k f s ΈΗ  É / _#
    l-vocalisation and y-diphthong shift
    [l y] > w / _[C #]
    Front vowels merge in weak position
    […¦ …Σ  è …¦j] > …Σ / #_sC
    […¦ …Σ  è] > …¦ / _w[C #]
    Diphthong shift part 1
    ai …¦i …Σi > e i …ôj
    aw …¦w …îw > o …ôw u
    Palatals and …Θ are lost (regenerating some diphthongs)
    …≤  é > n l / #_
    …≤  é …Θ > jn jl j

e.g. aut /ot/ "had" < awt < ayt < ayd < aud < aΥàΈ≤u.d…ô < aΥàbu.t…ô < aΥàbu.to < Vulgar *hab≈Ϊtus;
ismargre /…ΣsΥàmar.grΧ©/ "arise" < …¦jsΥàmar.gr…ô < ejsΥàmer.gr…ô < …¦ksΥàm…¦r.gre < Vulgar *exmergere

This is the stage, around the turn of the thirteenth century, usually referred to as late Old Borlish:

    βÄΠsovravnt il deft nostre sa»ùntaΟΑesem eΟΑ atavalesem n i»ù atrevre golfhavn se»ù hamar dont y verb divin ismetre ac povre paian. peΟΑev soul ez font istovent por vn nov cli»ùs d istroienz istablirβÄΠ
    /soΥàvront …Σl d…¦wt ΥànostrΧ© Υ¨sajn.taΥàΟΑ…¦s…¦m …¦ΟΑ aΥ¨ta.vaΥàl…¦s…¦m n…ôj aΥàtr…ôwrΧ© g…îlfΥàhon sej haΥàmar d…înt i v…¦rb diΥàv…Σn …ΣsΥàme.trΧ© ak ΥàpovrΧ© paΥà £an βÄ• peΥàΟΑ…ôw sul ets f…înt …Σs.toΥàv…¦nt p…îr yn nu cl…ôjs d…ΣsΥàtr…îj…ônts is.taΥàbl…Σr/
    βÄΠuphold our most sacred and ancient duty to let Gulfhaven be the centre from which we will send the Word of God to pagan lands. We ask only for the necessary funds for a new teachinghouseβÄΠ

Moving into the Middle Borlish period:

    Rhotacisation of syllabic l
    l > r / C_# except [j w]_#

This gives Borlish its signature pairs like capabr "competent"; capablessem "more competent".

    AphΟΠresis of initial isC
    …Σ > βàÖ / #_sC

Like IRL Italian, this means that many words gain an initial vowel only to lose it a millennium later: Latin stannum "tin" > Old Borlish istan "tin" > Modern Borlish stan "tin". However, this process also deletes initial vowels that were there from the start: Latin historia "story" > Old Borlish istoir "story" > Modern Borlish stoir "story".

    Break-up of sr clusters and hiatus
    βàÖ > t / s_r
    βàÖ > v / […î o u]_V
    uv > ov

The second of these rules is particularly interesting, since it regenerates some <v> which had been lost in the time of Insular Latin. For example, Latin novellus "new" had Insular form noellus "new" > Old Borlish noel "new" > Modern Borlish novel "new"

    Front rounded vowels are lost
    y  è > i …Σ

This provides Borlish with another of its signature features, namely <u> /i …Σ/: tu /ti/ "you (sg)"; prun /pr…Σn/ "plum". The loss of front rounded vowels led to some homophonous pairs like lun lin /l…Σn/ "moon; flax". In many cases this led to one of the pair falling out of use (in this case the latter was replaced with the competing form linsc).

    Voicing of final s
    s > z / _#

The precise timing of this sound change is hard to determine, since it does not interact with any nearby sound changes. Likely this originated as a sandhi effect before a following vowel-initial word, e.g. tu es un /ti …¦βÄΩz…Σn/ "you are a...", before being extended to all contexts.

    Diphthong shift part 2
    aj ej ij > …¦j i aj
    …ôj …ôw > aj aw

Borlish's answer to the IRL Great Vowel Shift, these changes are somewhat more modest (and take place a little later, between 1600 and 1800). In combination with the loss of front rounded vowels, however, these changes determine much of Borlish's least intuitive orthographic behaviour. For example, we have muy /maj/ "crumb", where <uy> underwent the changes /yj > ij  > aj/.

The previous shift reflects the outcomes in the Damvath Standard dialect of Borlish; these recent changes differ greatly in applicability across the island. We clos with an 1800s sound shift:

    Late consonant backing
     É r > x  Ä

The latter of these appears to be part of a general areal phenomenon of backing rhotics. A single word reflecting both these changes is scuir /xaj Ä/ "boy".

##

118.1: London

notes in translation on the subject of the city of London.

Posc un liesc com citaΟΑ seconder n'Albion, Londr redenoy alcheΟΑr y cafloy imperial ny region durant y Scon Tetrarchy ne sona d'un part dy Preu Empir DrengoΟßan.
After time as a secondary city in Albion, London became the imperial capital of the region once more during the Second Tetrarchy as part of the First Drengot Empire.

Y Preu ΟêurnΟß a Londr fis loy d'an 1290; ag mendrem 400 hom ern occis deut ag catastroph d'un semman cohernt.
The First Burning of London took place in 1290; at least 400 people were killed in the ensuing week-long disaster.

L'oc no sta for un parmy conflagraΟßon plusour ny preu demy dy scon millenner, bempart dec y citaΟΑ se cou cos saΟßonner concas des y norΟΑ ben y souΟΑ.
This was one of several conflagrations in the first half of the second millennium, many due to the city being perennially conquered from the north or south.

Posc l'eΟß Rix Democratic a plaΟß meg ny siecr ogtoΟß yemoy Londr cos final un autonomy durabr deur sy paΟ·s vecin d'an 1749 sou Quen Margaret, poΟΑent sy caΟΑen ancestral ligar ag linaΟß reyal de Marclont e Cant y dou. L'offiΟß de Segr Bourgmer remane possent cos politic aΟΑief por hast pareΟ·r coll'oΟß dy nou monarch, interim. 
The Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century saw London finally gain lasting independence from its neighbours in 1748 under Queen Margaret, who could trace her ancestry to the royal families of both Markland and Kent. The office of Lord Mayor remained politically powerful enough to rival that of the new monarch, however.

##

118.2: rostr "prow"

rostr /Υàr…î.strΧ©/ [Υà Ä…î.st…ê]
- prow, bow, the front part of a ship or boat;
- vanguard, the leading members of a convoy of travellers or the leading part of a vehicle (especially of a train);
- (in extended use) forefront, leader, the front part of anything and especially in metaphor to denote the earliest adopters of a technology or custom;
- nib, the point of a pen or similar tool;
- tip, any pointed extremity

Etymology: an uncomplicated descendant from Latin r≈çstrum "bill, beak, snout, muzzle; prow". The latter meanings are thought to derive from the anatomical senses of the Latin word. Extension from boats to other vehicles and then into metaphorical usage occured during the Middle Borlish period.

Y rostr a my creΟΑon es coroccaΟΑ.
/i Υàr…î.strΧ© a mi kreΥàΟΑ…în …¦z Υ¨ko.r…îΥàkaΈΗ/
[i Υà Ä…î.st ÄβÄΩa mi k Ä…ΣΥàΟΑ…în …Σz Υ¨ko. Ä äΥàkah]
df nib at 1s.gn pencil be crack-p.pst
The tip of my pencil has snapped. 

##

118.3: balis "beacon"

balis /baΥàl…Σz/ [b…êΥàl…Σz]
- beacon, signal fire, a warning light to communicate some message (especially in a military context);
- lighthouse, a tower supporting a light to guide sailors;
- (metaphorically) anything designed to be extremely conspicuous or mark the way;
- aerial, antenna, a device for transmitting electronic signals into the form of radio waves

Etymology: a borrowing via Middle French balise "beacon; buoy" from contemporary Portingale baliza "buoy". This appears to be from an unattested Late Latin pΡ¹lΡΪtia, related to pΡ¹lus "stake, post". In the sense "lighthouse" it is clipped from the phrase tour balis "beacon tower"; in the last sense it is attested from the early twentieth century.

Nos stoun balis sobottal luicessem.
/n…îz stun baΥàl…Σz Υ¨so.b…îΥàtal lajΥàts…¦.s…¦m/
[n…îz stun b…êΥàl…Σz Υ¨so.b äΥàtal l…êjΥàts…¦.s…êm]
1p need-3p beacon radio bright-comp
We need stronger radio transmitters.

##

118.4: sounΟß "release"

sounΟß /sunts/ [suns]
- release, the act of letting something or someone go or of causing something or something to become unstuck or untangled;
- launch, the movement of a vessel from land or moorage into the water;
- outbreak, outburst, the sudden appearance of something such as illness or war;
- (vulgar) orgasm, climax, the peak of sexual pleasure

Etymology: nominal derivative from verb souvr "solve, let go, release, launch", from solv≈ç "I loosen, untie"; or, directly from a Latin noun solventia "a loosening, an untying". In the last sense attested from as early as the eighteenth century in erotic literature; the term continues to be dispreferred in formal or technical contexts.

Y sounΟß de var interromoy eΟß accommission corrogaΟßonal.
/i sunts daΥàbat …ΣnΥ¨t…¦.roΥàm…îj …¦ts Υ¨a.k…î.m…ΣΥàsj…în Υ¨k…î.roΥ¨ga.tsoΥànal/
[i suns d…êΥàbat …ΣnΥ¨t…¦. Ä äΥàm…îj …¦βÄΩΥ¨tsa.k ä.m…ΣΥàsj…în Υ¨k…î. Ä äΥ¨ga.ts äΥànal]
df release of=war interrupt-pst p.dt project infrastructural
War breaking out interrupted those infrastructure projects.

##

118.5: Hasiny

Hasiny is the largest and most populous of the polities of British Mendeva. This refers to the region of Mendeva which have undergone significant settlement from or otherwise been significantly influenced by the Kingdom of Britain.

Hasiny is centred on the lower parts of the watershed of the Bahassassin River, but its lands extend sufficiently far northeast so as to share a short border with New Provence along part of the Sturgovan range. It borders the polity of Taisha to the west, and its capital city is Nadacow, which lies on the Bahassassin.

The name Hasiny derives from the confederation of the same name, which extended across lands which are now part of Hasiny and Taisha.

The languages of administration in Hasiny are Jaughta (JahtΟΓ, the Mendevan language spoken by a majority of the populace) and the local variety of Welsh (which differs in important ways from the dialects of British or Chrysian Welsh).

Hasiny was the homeland of the herdtale genre of fiction, which sought to depict a rural idyll with a focus on animal husbandry.

The resort town of Y Davarn Heul (The Sun Hotel) was constructed in the nineteenth century for Hasiny's ruling Tamaha dynasty, gaining such fame that the word "davarn" has entered many languages with the meaning "grand hotel, resort".

The first radio was built in Hasiny in 1903. The fourth Lineball Global Tourney (LGT) in 1935 took place in Hasiny, the first time it was held in Mendeva.

People
Bayac Cosœâteg (fl. c19), diarist and political agitator
Kjartan Rhysvor (fl. 1889), author of spycraft work While Embers Cool (Pan Oera'r Glo)
King Maldwen Tamaha (fl. 1950), long-reigning monarch
PeΟΑr Tamaha (fl. 1950), bon-vivant prince notorious for perennial debt troubles
Cadœâgan Torriœâr (fl. 1890), author of masquira work Men of Jet and Diamond (Dynnon o VuhuΟΑ a Diamont)

##

119.1: Libya

collected notes in translation on the polity of Libya.

Durant y Scon Tetrarchy loy fignt ny scon millenner primal, Libya (y nom temporane Ifricia dignant) sta un admiraltaΟΑ majour ny CaliphaΟΑ Sengr, regnaΟΑ de Tunis.
During the Second Tetrarchy of the early second millennium, Libya (under the name Ifricia) was an important admiralty of the Single Caliphate, ruled from Tunis.

Tandic prepondranΟß imperial n'Europ e n'Afric norΟΑer inceveu sy dissoluΟßon, tan cossy y CaliphaΟΑ Sengr; un admiraltaΟΑ autonom ne Libya er depos d'an 1237 (a veg ceu temporane coll'admiraltaΟΑ Morrou nell'ovest).
As imperial hegemony in Europe and northern Africa began to fall apart, so did the Single Caliphate; an independent admiralty in Libya was declared in 1237 (at roughly the same time as the Morrove admiralty to the west).

Noc obstroint ig y flottisc Libyan n'er ja commarΟßant cos direct ag Novomont ny siecr seΟß, moΟΑnaut des y region stan frequent armaΟΑ par y flottisc marcander de Morrac e (cos particuler) de ProvenΟß.
Although Libya's navy was not yet trading directly with the Novomund in the sixteenth century, seamen from the region were commonly employed in the merchant fleets of Morrack and (especially) of Provence.

Ny siecr settoΟß primal, y gonfan de Libya abrange dou quarrel neir, un blanc eΟΑ un oransc.
In the early seventeenth century, the banner of Libya comprised two black fields, one white and one orange.

119.2: sleut "politician"

sleut /slawt/ [zlawt]
- politician, representative, someone engaged in politics as an elected or appointed official;
- smooth talker, sycophant, silver tongue, someone skilled in charming people or especially in keeping the peace

Etymology: substantive use of the past participle of verb sleir "to elect, to vote for", itself via Old Borlish isler "elect" from Vulgar Latin exlig≈ç (Classical Ρ™lig≈ç "I pick, choose, elect"). The term was used almost immediately as a somewhat derogatory term for anyone perceived to act in the manner of a politician.

Noc imprefait es ig tell'heir denoy sleut.
/n…îk Υ¨…Σm.preΥàfet …¦z aj t…¦Υàlir deΥàn…îj slawt/
[n äΥ¨g…Σm.p Ä…ΣΥàfet …Σz aj t…ΣΥàli.j…ê d…ΣΥàn…îj zlawt]
ng surprising be comp 2s.gn=son become-pst politician
I'm not surprised your son's a politician.

##

119.3: a squid "on the take"

a squid /a skw…Σd/ [a skw…Σd]
- on the take, corrupt, bought, bribed, accepting money from illicit sources in exchange for their influence;
- subverted, in the pocket (of), secretly under someone else's control

Etymology: 19C, analysable as a phrase meaning "at handshake", using the slang term squid "handshake; informal agreement, under-the-table deal". However, it is unclear whether the noun squid is a backformation, as the phrase may have been borrowed wholesale from contemporary Welsh ΟΓ sguyd "with a (hand)shake", a derivative of verb squid "shake, toss, tremble".

J'aye spien ig l'oΟß madarc no fos a squid.
/ £…¦Υàje spj…¦n aj l…îts maΥàdark no f…îz a skw…Σd/
[ ù…ΣΥàje spj…¦n aj l…îs m…êΥàd…êΥêk no f…îz a skw…Σd]
1s=have-imp hope cmp df=s.dt cop ng be.sbj.imp at handshake
I hoped that that cop wasn't on the take.

##

119.4: rubic "sporty"

rubic /riΥàb…Σk/ [ Ä…ΣΥàb…Σk]
- pertaining to sports;
- sporty, athletic, inclined to physical activity;
- competitive, cutthroat, eager to win against others

Etymology: a 19C backformation (based on analogy to the adjectival -ic ending) from rubicon "sports, athletic activity". This itself is an 18C folk-etymological form (evoking the idiom y Rubicon trasir "to pass the point of no return; to start a fight"), possibly via a French or English language, from Willem Dutch robicong "competition, sport, athletics". This is a compound of rob "fight, contest, brawl" and iken "to be compared, compete".

Le scey tojorn quondant con un Οßod rubic.
/le xi toΥà £…îrn kw…înΥàdant k…în …Σn ts…îd riΥàb…Σk/
[le Οßi t äΥà ù…îΥên kw änΥàda≈΄ k…în …Σn ts…îd  Ä…ΣΥàb…Σk]
3s.ac there.is always lurk-p.prs with indf squad sporty
She's always hanging out with sporty friends.

119.5: Seventeenth Century Studies

shortform questions lifted from a 2024 scitation paper for the course Details of the Seventeeth Century (Y Siecr SettoΟß a Sgart Jusser), an avenue of concurrence history offered by the Jacobin University of Axbane.

1. Which groups were expelled from Moscat in the Ibadine Imamate in 1652, and which local power would go on to seize the city some months later?

2. Which two rivers of Gaul were connected by a canal for the first time in 1679, in a bid to connect the Atlantic to the Middlesea without having to pass through Morrack-controlled seas?

3. Where was the treaty of 1621 which outlined the legal relationship of Santrafew to the local Mendevan peoples signed?

4. For how long did Emperor Carivasary of Tavancy rule until his suspected assassination in 1660?

5. Which future monarch of Barcelon spent several years of his childhood in Cordin as a ward of the Borlish royal family during the 1660s and 1670s?

6. Which power attempted to invade Borland in the seventeenth century, leading to the Hawkirch Surrender of 1632?

7. The terms "copperplate" and "tinplate" in political theory originate in David BensaΟ≠d's treatise The Three Cities; he coins these terms in his description of the court of which seventeenth-century monarch of Libya?

8. What astronomical event of 1677 inspired theorists from across the world to undertake long journeys, including the Danish astronomers Gudmund and Rebecca Scrander's storied voyage to the Seldivan court?

9. What did the invention of the timehold in the mid-seventeenth century allow people to measure accurately for the first time?

10. The trade mesh of which Indian polity stretched from Sant Adrian to Cathay by the end of the seventeenth century?

##

120.1: Rearguard

excerpt in translation from the historical trevold Rearguard, set during the eleventh-century mordether [crusade] conquests in the Masreck [Levant].
 
Dec orruΟΑ i carra noc un respos contr sy convoc a pan rompr accougl, e dec i sera attent reunt ag garnΟßon toΟΑ cas, Omar quauc vars y donjon.
Since it seems his invitation to eat together will not be accepted, and since he is expected back at the garrison anyway, Omar hurries to the tower.

Ac son y soldart nuvr ΟΑorant, sur lorrell'assignment aglon de ny masquet cosogr y paΟ·san, deut ag besogn ig il noun se situar lontan demay dy gran port condon y toxen incoΟ·br sorc por avartr ig y preu rostr Roman son detectaΟΑ.
The soldiers have been praying here, at their posts rather than in the mosque with the people, because they cannot be caught too far from the gates when the inevitable alarm goes up that the first Roman vanguards have been spotted.

Lonc y commant dy gournour, band aun toΟΑ y poiΟΑ enverimmaΟΑ ben coyenΟßaΟΑ, eΟß arbr tos, eΟΑ y feu tars de tout calscon benoΟßabr com vitagl e vegr.
By order of the governor, men have poisoned or blocked all the wells, cut down trees, and scourged the land of anything that could be used for provisions and fodder.

Y barbarin son dolent sou y caldour bruslant, ma il son avanΟßant nentamen.
The barbarians are suffering in the scorching heat, but they advance nonetheless.

##

120.2: ne fousc "broke"

ne fousc /ne fux/ [n…ΣΥàfux]
- broke, penniless, destitute, having no money;
- bankrupt, ruined, in a position of not having enough money to pay one's debts

Etymology: nineteenth-century idiom literally meaning "in (a) pit". The noun fousc "pit, hole in the ground; trap, snare, pitfall" descends with meaning pracatically unchanged via Old Borlish fouz /fut É/ from fovea in Latin. The coventionalised intensifier for this idiom is arΟΑ "steep", which has been analogised to other expressions for poverty; cf. povr arΟΑ "very poor".

Jo luy dau un cromn neΟΑan i surcous ne fousc arΟΑ.
/ £o laj do …Σn krun neΥàΟΑan i s…ΣrΥàkuz ne fux arΈΗ/
[Υ¨ ùo.l…êjΥàdo …Σ≈΄ k Äun n…ΣΥàΟΑan i s…ΣΥêΥàkuz ne fux …êΥêΈΗ]
1s 3s.obl give-pst indf purse lest 3s end.up-imp.sbj in pit steep
I gave him some cash so he wouldn't be flat broke.

120.3: inoΟ·ΟΑ "silent"

inoΟ·ΟΑ /Υ¨…Σ.n…îjΥà…ΣΈΗ/ [Υ¨…Σ.n äΥàj…Σh]

##

120.5: Borland before Borlish

excerpt from Borland before Borlish (Istr Boral pre Borallesc), a history textbook published in 1983 though the New Vithor University.

βÄΠdiscussion of the Kelt peoples who lived on Borland before the Roman conquests of the Flavian period is limited by our lack of written sources. The few inscriptions we do have are mostly translations of placenames and the names of tribes in Roman sources, appearing since the onset of diplomacy and trade with Rome since Julius Caesar's expeditions to Britannia in the first century aN.

One important exception is the name the Borland Kelts appear to have used collectively to distinguish themselves from the Britons: the Bora Ale. There has been speculation for centuries that the name Borrhaloi (which the Greek explorer Pytheas gives alongside Bretannike, and which became Latin BorΡ¹lΡ™s) is of Kelt origin, and not merely a derivative of Greek Έ£ΈΩœ¹œ¹αΨΕœ² "the North Wind, the North". The 1932 unearthing at the Livaucr site of a burial marker reading IANTUMOR ABONDUU ALE "Jantumarus of the Abonduvii" proved that ales (pl. alΡ™, cognate with Welsh ail "second", Latin alius "other") was used locally to mean "people, tribe".

Hence, Bora Ale is reconstructed as meaning "people of the East", with bora(g) "East, sunrise" corresponding to Welsh bore "morning" and Irish mΟΓrah "tomorrow". It may even have originated as a exonym applied by the Britons, which would explain the hypothesised sense development of a word which originally meant "others".

Of course, the Borland Kelts did not always conceive of themselves as an indivisible group, and over a dozen tribal names are attestedβÄΠ

121.1: Second German War

notes in translation on the subject of the Second German War.

Y Var Theudesc Dovem (1815βÄ™23) sta un rix y condensaΟΑessem n'eiΟΑel Theudesc ma pres toΟΑ y polity n'Europ pleitent. 
The Second German War (1815βÄ™23) was a conflict centred on German lands but involving almost all the polities of Europe.

L'interman y finisc lonc ustol dy PaΟß Lonc.
It marks the conventional end of the Long Peace.

L'oc var es cossiraΟΑ com un part dell'occorrenΟß mondial dy Rix a ReacΟßon, ig abrangent de conflict desjognt cas traitment ma contemporane tras y mont.
This war is considered part of the global phenomenon of Reaction War, which includes politically unrelated but contemporaneous conflicts around the world.

Degnant ig Vascony acavaus cos iniΟßal a noc denir jannestaΟΑ ny conflict, alcant ig sell'envou ne Mendeva e nell'Aust aurn se caut periglaΟΑ, rey de lor Hernand IV n'aye l'ouΟßon sauf var souvr contr y Concert NorΟΑer e sociant con y Convey.
Although Vascony at first managed not to become embroiled in the conflict, once its interests in Mendeva and the East were threatened, then-king Hernand IV had no choice but to declare against the Northern Concert and in favour of the Convoy.

Istr Boral se pleitoy ny var, cossy sceint soldart Borallesc present a cost batman Bavarn e Furlan ag catastroph cas mal gournment dy Battagl a Houlm d'an 1821. 
Borland participated in the war, with Borlish soldiers present alongside Bavarn and Furlain fighters at the disastrously mismanaged Battle of Hulm in 1821.

121.2: baΟΑaspat "nonplussed"

- indignant, outraged, startled, astonished, in a state of heightened emotion and reflexive disapproval

Etymology: first mentioned in the eighteenth century as a new piece of fashionable slang, probably of dialectal origin. Thought to be a compound; the first part is a form of baΟΑar, local variant of sbayar "gawp, goggle" descending from Medieval Latin (ex)bat≈ç, exbad≈ç "I gape, yawn". The second part spat is presumably a variant of spait, past participle of spagir "frightened, scared" and descending from Vulgar Latin expave≈ç "I am terrified of".

- thicket, copse, grove, stand, a dense but small collection of trees or shrubs;

##

121.5: Pluralisation

Final /-s/ is lost really early on in the development of Borlish; it's one of the very first sound changes that distinguishes it from the mainland Romance languages, occuring by around 500 AD. This, along with the loss of the genitive case (replaced with the ablative) makes the noun declension of typical nouns look like this:
Image

Notice that masculine nouns typically have only two forms, and feminine nouns only three. Once final vowels also get neutralised to schwa (and eventually lost entirely), the noun paradigm for regular nouns has completely collapsed.

A few nouns briefly retain an irregular paradigm of the following shape, with a separate nominative singular:
Image

However, by the late Old Borlish period these have all been levelled to (usually) the oblique form; sour "sister" is the only counterexample where instead the nominative singular survives.

Several strategies for marking noun number were innovated during the Old Borlish period, mostly involving demonstratives like ille, iste and ipse. However, the forms that survive to the present derive from the demonstrative hic "this". Specifically, we have:

    oc "singular" < hoc, neuter singular
    ec "plural" < haec, neuter plural

In the late Old Borlish period we see the redevelopment of a proximate-distal contrast, with new distal demonstratives formed using the adverb i "there" (since lost; cf. Modern Borlish la < illΡ¹c):

    oΟß < oc i "that" < hoc ibi
    eΟß < ec i "those" < haec ibi

Coda: Some Modern Borlish Names

There are a few Celtic names which survive in modern Borlish, for various reasons.

A very few names entered Borland Latin from the local Celtic during the Roman Empire. As a possible example, the male name Ivocq is thought to reflect Borland Celtic *iwok- "like a yew tree".

More Celtic names enter Borlish at various stages since the medieval period. One of the earliest is the female name Jonnovar, cognate with Guinevere and Jennifer, and which is also used as a common noun meaning "butterfly".

122.1: Breakfast Time

excerpt in translation from the beginning of the short story Breakfast Time by Derin Edala.

Mell'heir es cogntiΟΑ de liesc recorrent veg ancour.
My sonβÄôs stuck in a time loop again.

I crey jo saif noc, lon digr.
He thinks I donβÄôt know, of course.

I n'a jamay me descont ig ly cay l'oc (ben ig i poΟΑ l'oc fair, parcanΟß; jo no's scur de calun).
HeβÄôs never told me that this happens to him (or that he can do this, possibly; IβÄôm not sure which it is).

Plausibr es ig jo's un mal maΟΑr, ΟΑa jo n'ay me provaΟΑ a l'ig confeyanΟß dignar.
Maybe IβÄôm a bad mother, if I havenβÄôt proven myself worthy of that trust.

Ma no scein for tan veg yon se poΟΑ y heir sgardar sy jorn passant a monnocquant ant impatienΟß infruniscent, reagent de tout teyon dis cos rapid demay just com picq eΟΑ alcun goppiet mostrant ag cainΟß y goppietandessem meΟΑes, e soulor descenent souscen desmecchiattaΟΑ ma rajounaΟΑ eΟΑ ant un exuberanΟß novel a cost un incainΟß vars emboscment souvar dy cos y previsiblessem meΟΑes, fin a tu discern y ratiocinaΟßon evident.
But there are only so many times that you can watch your son trudge through a day with bored impatience, anticipating everything you say just a little too quickly and showing no surprise to even the most surprising event, and then come downstairs the next day disoriented but rejuvenated and with a new zest for life and a tendency to get blindsided by even the most predictable things, before you make the obvious connection.

##

122.5: Vascon Ascendancy

    collected notes on the period of Western European history known as the Vascony Ascendancy.

The Vascon Ascendancy (Vascon: L'AscendenΟßa Vasconna; Vask: Igualdea Vascoina) refers to the polity of Vascony from the early fifteenth century until the late sixteenth. At this time it enjoyed a territorial maximum due to several successful military conquests, as well as a general increase in prosperity.
Image

(Vascony, 1456 N)

The beginning of the Ascendancy is usually placed at the start of the reign of Ambrose II, who was crowned in 1410. His reign was marked by a succession of wars, both west into northern Portingale (taking all of Galice and even down to Braga), and east into Burgundy (taking the wealthy coastal regions of Tolose and Provence).

Ambrose III "the Ascendant" (for whom the period was retrospectively named) succeeded his grandfather in 1456. He established the New Navarre Enterprise two years later, an expanded merchant fleet with the dual aims of monopolising the trade of North Atlantic fish through the Middlesea and of creating trade ports down the western coast of Africa. This led to greatly increased fishing activity, and it was Vask ships travelling north past Valland [Greenland] that encountered Brasil [Newfoundland] in the 1471 Novomundine Landfall.

Trade with the Novomund, especially once the far more favourable southern route was discovered, enriched Vascony and especially the port towns in Portingale and Leon. Cities flourished, and there was also an outpouring of new artistic techniques from painting to poetry.

The Ascendancy is by most accounts over by the War of the Two Queens in the second half of the sixteenth century. By this time the land of both Portingale and Provence had been lost and the polity was reduced to its heartland. Furthermore, the Second Great Dying killed almost one in seven people in Vascony (although immigration was high enough to offset the loss of population by 1550).
Art

    I am Ambrose (Sou Ambroxo), a 1923 film in Portingale by director Agochino, which is a character study of King Ambrose the Ascendant through his long life.
    The Ascendant (Hwn a GuodoΟΑ), a 1934 film in Welsh by director Dormaget, which chronicles in particle the years immediately after the 1471 Novomundine Landfall.lm in Welsh by director Dormaget, which chronicles in particle the years immediately after the 1471 Novomundine Landfall.

##

123.5: Dart Geometry

excerpted from Drawing in the Sand: the History of Calculation, a collaborative reference book originally published 1954 in Napolitan as Scheme ne Rene: a Storia d'Amiradanza by the Agenza Scholaria Fodgesa.

βÄΠtempting to draw a straight line from Euclid's Elements until the introduction of new instantiations of geometry in Ferenc's pioneering 1819 work GeometriΟΠ Inquadrabiles, seeing any developments in the meantime as merely the proving of ever more particular theorems. However, this fails to acknowledge the truly conceptual advancements in the field, which would go on to influence diverse parts of mathematics and theory.

In this section, we chart the development of the "dart method" as an extension of Euclidean geometry. In all the theorems of The Elements, no distinction is made between a line AB and its reverse BA. It is only in the late fifteenth century that scholars like Barclythe and da Treviso begin to assign directions to lines (it is unclear whether the parallel developments at the Hicma Academy in Baghdad are due to direct communication or simply an independent invention).

It was this seemingly trivial addition that enabled geometers to do two important things. First, it brought ablate [negative] quantities into the realm of respectability in European mathematics; although the idea of "less-than-no things" was absurd (nonetheless, ablate numbers were already being used in the account-books of Venise and other trade cities to denote debts), no-one was perturbed by the concept of returning to one's starting point by trdavelling from A to B, and then from B to A. Second, it allowed for stronger ties between geometry and the newly-imported algebra; adding together two directed lines was conceptually as easy as laying two arrows point-to-vane.

In this way, one could translate a geometric statement like "the lines a, b and c form a triangle" into the algebraic equation "a + b = c". Indeed, over the proceeding decades mathematicians worked to render all the proofs of the Elements into this form. Extending the theory to mozate [two-dimensional] shapes tookβÄΠ

124.1: Historical Linguistics Exam

excerpt in translation from sample scitation papers for the University of Lester's Faculty of Domain History, published in 1990.

Entrou 5 (trent minut) Argument fair ou ne promoΟßon ben n'opposson cas dou calscon parmy l'ec thes.
Question 5 (thirty minutes) Argue either in favour of or in opposition to any two of the following theses.

(i) Y parol Anglesc 'silver' a un origin incertanβÄîparol referent all'oΟß matier son accart des un lengaΟß vars un autr tras millenner par mejan de sar commarcer laΟΑ. L'origin y plausiblessem dell'oc parol (v. sudrabs ne Lithovan, 'asrvae ne Hausan, zirar ne Vasq) com parol trocher es π£ÄΪπ£¹çπ£¨ù (surpum) n'Acadian ataval.
(i) The word 'silver' is of uncertain originβÄîwords referring to the substance have been borrowed between languages for millennia via extensive trade networks. The most likely origin of the word (cf. Lithowan sudrabs, Hausan 'asrvae, Vask zirar) as a trade-word is in ancient Acadian π£ÄΪπ£¹çπ£¨ù (surpum).

(ii) Y benoΟß de run Sinnesc lonc son, un issar por nom certan e por autr parol d'origin foran des antictaΟΑ e tesmognaΟΑ ne polity veΟßinant (v. lettranΟß maniocan primal) des y sieΟßr settem N, caf reconnoscenΟß ne Cathay sou y regn Kitannesc deut a gimmolt volonter por y polity sevrar deur sell'eΟß veΟßin souΟΑer.
(ii) Usage of Sinic runes soundwise, a practice in certain names and other words of foreign origin since antiquity and seen in neighbouring polities (cf. early maniocan [man'y≈çgana] script) from the seventh century N, gains currency in Cathay during Kitan rule due to purposeful efforts to sunder the polity from their southern neighbours.

124.2: entr hugt e gavisc "torn"

entr hugt e gavisc /Υà…¦ntrΧ© hajt e gaΥàv…Σx/ [Υà…¦n.t…ê hajt e g…êΥàv…ΣΟß]
- torn, unable to decide between two (good) options;
- living it up, going from strength to strength, experiencing a continual abundance of good fortune

Etymology: idiom from the fifteenth century (Kigman), literally "between happiness and joy". The noun hugt "happiness" descends from Old Borlish hu»ùt, a borrowing from Old English hyht "hope"; the noun gavisc is a deverbal from gavir "enjoy, feel happy", which descends directly from Latin gaude≈ç "I rejoice, make merry". Used primarily with negative valence (portraying the necessary choice as an imposition) until the nineteenth century, at which the latter sense begin to be attested.

L'indrec de my sour es se trovant entr hugt e gavisc.
/l…ΣnΥàdr…¦k de mi sur …¦z se troΥàvant Υà…¦ntrΧ© hajt e gaΥàv…Σx/
[l…ΣnΥàd Ä…¦k de mi s äΥêβÄΩ Ä…Σz se t Ä äΥàvant …¦n.t…ê hajt e g…êΥàv…ΣΟß]
df=charity of 1s.gn sister be rf find-p.prs between happiness and joy
My sister's charity is going from strength to strength.

124.3: fis "done"

fis /f…Σz/ [f…Σz]
- set, stuck, immovable, placed somewhere on a more-or-less permanent basis;
- done, complete, finished, having nothing else of an activity or project to do;
- spent, exhausted, empty, having been entirely used up;
- despairing, hopeless, resigned to the inevitability of failure or defeat;
- screwed, finished, in a hopeless situation

Etymology: past participle of figr "set down, place down, lay" used also as the past participle of figr a "finish, finish off, complete, stop" (where perhaps fis la would be more regular). In the sense of having run out of something, the word is seen from the sixteenth century; in the last senses it is seen from the seventeenth.

Nossy foyail es fis e scein lau pre yonoscon.
/n…îΥàsi f…îΥàjel …¦z f…Σz e xin lo pre Υ¨jo.noΥàx…în/
[n äΥàsi f äΥà ùel …Σz f…Σz e Οßin lo p Äe Υ¨ ùo.n äΥàx…în]
1p-gn fuel be done and there.be-3p league before wherever
We're out of petrol in the middle of nowhere.

124.4: dignant "appropriate"

dignant /dajΥànant/ [d…êjΥànant]
- becoming, suitable, appropriate, fit, done, conforming to standards of behaviour or aesthetics;
- named, called, known as, AKA, alias, having been given an appellation

Etymology: present participle of dignar "merit, deserve, be worthy of", itself a verbal derivation from Old Borlish di»ùn "worth, merit". This reflects Latin adjective dignus "worthy, appropriate, meet". The latter senses are seen from the late Middle Borlish period.

Tal inquit no son dignant de mell'yam.
/tal …ΣnΥàkw…Σt no s…în dajΥànant de m…¦Υàljam/
[tal …Σ≈΄Υàkw…Σt no s…în d…êjΥànan de m…ΣΥàljam]
so quote ng be.3p fit of 1s.gn=uncle
Such words are unworthy of my uncle.

##

124.5: Who's Who (1700s Edition)

    collected notes on various figures active during the eighteenth century.

Raymond al Mustafa (fl. 1750) was a mathematician and philosopher. He was resident for some time in Barcelon under the Duke of Aragon's patronage. During this time he corresponded with fellow theorist Rachel Desselut, and their letters outline some of the concepts that would go on to define the field of methodics.

He spent his latter years travelling, eventually settling in Brasil [Newfoundland]. His grandson Fabian MostΟ†f became an eminent astronomer at the Paratzon Observatory, using mathematical techniques developed in part by Raymond (like the waveform display [Fourier transform] to compute celestial orbits).

-

Marian Siegert ~ Miller (1725βÄ™56) was the illegitimate daughter of Elias Villingen, second Duke of Far Suebia [~a region west of Swabia]. She grew up at the ducal seat in Castle Carlsfeld as a recognised bastard, and was close with the Duke's younger legitimate children. She married Albert Siegert, a merchant in leather and a magnate of the shoemaking industry, at the age of eighteen, and moved with him to Lepzi [Leipzig].

From her regular correpondence with her half-brother Thomas she is known to have learnt to speak Burgund as a child and Bohamish [Czech] as an adult. With Albert she had several children, the eldest of whom would become the pioneering author Elsebeth Sneider.

-

Lord Mathurin Carrow, Baron of Ebbasham [Epsom] (fl. 1763) was a traveller who published accounts of his journeys. He is remembered in particular for his mealbooks which compile recipes from the places he visited.

An early work of his, A Complete Assembly of the Towns and Cities of Markland for the Edification of the Good Food Amateur, was popular across the Northwestern Isles [Britain, Ireland and Borland] (the Borlish translation being infamously inaccurate) but failed to sell further afield. Later volumes like An Account of the Vittles of the Various German Peoples and On the Sundry Uses of the Shadome Fruit [tomato] were far more enduring, being republished several times even after his death.

125.1: Early Modern Morrack

excerpt in translation from notes on the polity of Morrack [Morocco].

Y Colluy dy Nou Mont nommaΟΑ cos auspecer sta corporalleyaΟΑ par Malic Mounir al-Hamdawi d'an 1643, e pres pront la nonΟßamen dell'Abanc Novomondin fos yent a cort ern vascel dy Colluy accaΟßaΟΑ vars l'ovest.
The prophetically-named New World Company was established by Malik Munir al-Hamdawi in 1463, and almost as soon as news of the Novomundine Landfall had reached court were ships of the Company sent westward.

Cay l'oc flottisc ayent communeyanΟß fait con y gent dell'Arcabil, tan com y dou empir Mascic e Tavancin.
It was this fleet which made contact with the peoples of the Arcabil, as well as the Mashick and Tavancine empires.

Vascel Morrac stan y primer a y mont circonnavigar, stant parvenuΟΑ ag Gran Rajognt Borunesc d'an 1519 via Cappatia.
Morrack ships were the first to circumnavigate the world, having reached the Great Borunesk Meeting of 1519 via Cappatia.

Souspeut es ig y mort dell'Emperour Tavancin Carivasary d'an 1660 fo veraΟßter un assassinat accommis lonc commant Morrac, cos cuidaΟΑ por y cΟΠros ovrir d'un commarΟß cas argent profitandessem des Tavancy.
The death of Tavancine Emperor Cariwasary in 1660 is suspected to have been an assassination carried out on Morrack orders, purportedly to allow for more profitable trade in silver from Tavancy.

125.2: laur d'asn "drudgery"

laur d'asn /lor Υàda.znΧ©/ [loΥê Υàda.z…ên]
- drudgery, drudge work, toil, grunt work, menial and tedious labour;
- tedium, ennui, boredom, a general sense of listlessness and lacking of engaging activity

Etymology: idiom meaning "donkey's work", attested from the eighteenth century. The noun laur "work, job" is a deverbal from laurar "to work"; the noun asn "donkey; dunce" descends directly from synonymous asinus in Latin.

J'azia ne spien ig un hom interromois my laur d'asn.
/ £aΥàzja ne spj…¦n aj …Σn …îm …ΣnΥ¨t…¦.roΥàm…îjz mi lor Υàda.snΧ©/
[ ù…êΥàzja ne spj…¦n aj …Σn …îm …ΣnΥ¨t…¦. Ä äm…îjz mi loΥê Υàda.s…ên]
1s-stand-ipf in hope cmp indf person interrupt-ipf.sbj 1s.gn toil of=donkey
I stood, hoping someone would interrupt the tedium.

125.3: ΟΑorn de hanc "sideswipe"

ΟΑorn de hanc /ΟΑ…îrn de hank/ [ΟΑ…îΥên de ha≈΄k]
- swipe, sideswipe, hook, a punch or other strike from the side;
- bolt from the blue, a completely unexpected event (especially one for which you are utterly unprepared)

Etymology: phrase meaning "punch from (the) flank", used figuratively to describe an unexpected event since the late eighteenth century. The noun ΟΑorn "punch" is a deverbal from now-obsolete aΟΑornar "to punch", itself directly from synonymous addurn≈ç in Insular Latin (of ultimate Kelt origin). The noun hanc "hip, hipbone; flank, loin, side" descends from Vulgar Latin hanca "hip", from a German source related to ankle in English.

Y victoir dell'ig sleut sta un ΟΑorn de hanc.
/i v…ΣkΥàt…îjr d…¦Υàlaj slawt sta …Σn ΟΑ…îrn de hank/
[i v…ΣkΥàt…î.j…ê d…ΣΥàlaj zlawt sta …Σn ΟΑ…îΥên de ha≈΄k]
df victory of-df=s.dt politician be.ipf indf punch of flank
That candidate winning came out of nowhere.

125.4: govondry "canteen"

govondry /Υ¨go.v…înΥàdri/ [Υ¨go.v änΥàd Äi]
- canteen, cafeteria, a small cafe inside a larger establishment (e.g. military base, school, prison or workplace);
- tuck shop, lunch counter, snack bar, a counter selling small items of food

Etymology: Middle Borlish govonry "inn, eatery", place derivation from now-obsolete govon "innkeeper, barkeeper". This appears to descend directly from Latin caup≈ç "tradesman, innkeeper". It specialisation to small eateries and counters within organisations takes place first in a military context during the eighteenth century.

J'acatau dou quignou de nossy govondry.
/Υ¨ £a.kaΥàto du kwajΥànu de n…îΥàsi Υ¨go.v…înΥàdri/
[Υ¨ ùa.k…êΥàto du kwajΥànu de n äΥàsi Υ¨go.v änΥàd Äi]
1s=buy-pst two pastry of 1p.gn canteen
I bought two pastries from our canteen.

##

125.5: Denmark

Denmark is a peninsular polity located between the North and Graven Seas.

History
Denmark was part of the homeland of the Danes during the Dane Supremacy in the medieval period, from which viking raids and colonisation projects left. The rulers of Borland and much of Dane England during this time were dynasties originating in Denmark.

During the sixteenth century Denmark was part of the Dane Concert, a monarchic union of northern states. The royal family undertook a grand tour of their realm in the years around 1560, visiting places like Alburgh. It was this period which saw Revitalism reach Denmark, with renewed interrogation of Classical texts.

Instability in Denmark belying the Long Peace of the late eighteenth century led to an uptick in emigration, both to nearby polities like Borland and also the distant staddomains of Cappatia.

The expansion of suffrage in Denmark took place through the latter half of the nineteenth century, parallel to similar movements in Saxony and the Latin republics. During this time Denmark was drawn into the orbit of the Drengot Collusion, although it was never fully incorporated.

People
Elisa Arnoutszen (fl. 1540), nurse in the household of Duke Roger d'Avosche and later a physician in her own right
Laurz Arnoutszen (fl. 1540), famed physician whose treatise on medical handiwork revolutionised European practice
Absolon Mortenszen (fl. 1560), playwright and commentator who lived in Alburgh and is remembered for his play High Society
Gudmund & Rebecca Scrander (fl. 1677), astronomers who voyaged to Seldiva to observe a transit of Venus
