126.1: Secret Notes

excerpt in translation from the 1930 work Notas a Hurt "Secret Notes", an example of the spycraft genre written in Vascony by author Micolau Zorriqueta (whose similar series of works about a Huzatre detective is more widely remembered).

Martin scoutau y baΟΑrment durant hour monfeyaΟΑ e fo mesteyaΟΑ n'yembr sciΟΑ posc i lanΟßau ogl a sell'orlosc a caΟΑen ig for veint minut n'ern laundaΟΑ.
Martin listened to the ensemble for several hours and was disappointed to discover when he glanced at his watch that only twenty minutes had passed.

Cos ouveg i recuidau ig Jacq n'aye besogn aut de sy presenΟß la bogr; l'ec fardig a torriot dont Martin aye sy parcorrenΟß oΟ·ΟΑ ig crevoscr ayen son nervous e meΟΑes foyaΟßant.
Sullenly he reflected that James hadn't needed him in attendance at all; the bassoon solos Martin had heard him rehearsing last night sounded confident and even virtuosic.

Manifest tan sta ig lorry caf maldigt au l'orn enter recompos.
It was also apparent that the accursed leader had rearranged the programme.

Y cΟΠros de Jacq caint zaressem vole avenir ag liesc outr dy crevoscr, e donc Martin poΟΑe noc meΟΑes sortir ortellent ag tarnot dy loy ny hiat.
James's biggest moment would come right at the end of the evening, so Martin couldn't even sneak out to the venue's bar at the interval.

Intermane un ovartur des l'ig melodram dont l'au se caut emmenaΟΑ an scenanΟß lascer e soulor invectivaΟΑ avars durant dou hour.
It was an overture from that opera he'd been dragged to a film staging of and subsequently ranted at about for two hours.

Sceyen hombeul ant cinq pie entarn, i creyeβÄîpu veram i n'aye alcun nis prestaΟΑ la.
There were monsters with five feet in it, he thoughtβÄîalthough he really hadn't been paying attention.

126.5: Chemical Etymology

excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary of Alchemy, a reference quire compiled by Rotcham University Primers.

- moron n. /Υàm…îΥê.…Ψ…în/ [proton] a deficient [positively charged] particle as found in atomic nuxes [nuclei]. 1888 < Kiew [Kyiv] Russian –Φ–Ψ―Ä–Α ¬Ζ mora (1882) < Ancient Greek ΈΦœ¨œ¹ΈΩΈΫ ¬Ζ mΟ≥ron "mulberry, blackberry", in reference to Nike Sarantylack's "mulberry model" of the atom as containing a number of each of two different sorts of particle.

- lucifer adj., n. /Υàl…ôu.s…Σ.f…ô/ [radioactive (substance)] of a substance, spontaneously producing subatomic particles or ionising light; such a substance. 1894 < Vascon lucifer < Latin l≈Ϊcifer "light-bringing", from l≈Ϊx "light" and -fer "carrying". Connected only incidentally with proper noun Lucifer "Satan".

- killdark n. /Υàk…Σl.daΥêk/ [uranium] the corporal [element] whose atoms have 92 mora, a lucifer [radioactive] grey metal. 1810 < Welsh kildΟΓrc "killdark" (1805) < the Irish shire of Killdara, where the corporal was first isolated. Plausibly respelt after "dark"; the Wessern English form darick /Υàda.…Ψ…ôk/ "dark" may also have played a role.

- lix n. /l…Σks/ [potassium] the corporal whose atoms have 19 mora, a soft grey metal acted on by air and water and thus never found in tesquo [in nature] as a metal. 1797 < Latin lixa "lye", which is the caustic of lix [potassium hydroxide].

- janthine n. /Υàd £an.ΈΗ…ôin/ [iodine] the corporal whose atoms have 53 mora, found as a grey solid, a purple-black liquid and a purple gas. 1791 < Scholastic Latin janthΡΪnus < Ancient Greek αΦΑΈ§ΈΫΈΗαΩêΈΫΈΩœ² "violet-coloured".

127.1: Medieval Empire

excerpt in translation on the Second Roman Empire [~the Byzantines] in the high medieval period.

Ne souc dy ruin dy Lombardesc norΟΑer n'Italy, y linaΟß regnant enfugheurn vars l'Empir e sceurn autortaΟΑ remarcabr allaΟßar la.
Following the fall of the northern Lombards in Italy, the ruling family fled to the Empire and managed to obtain appreciable clout there. 

PieΟß sodal se courn lorry ascreuΟßon com agathin por y them ovester nell'Empir, eΟΑ y tesmogn scaut par eΟß autr intermann l'inquit historic y confiablessem cas occorrenΟß particuler durant y scompigl dy Secession German ny siecr treΟß.
Some members were appointed to administer the Empire's western themes, and the written accounts of others are history's strongest sources for certain events during the turmoil of the German Secession in the thirteenth century.

Stant dissoluΟΑ n'un form vabian closnautr naΟΑusc, a mesur dy siecr catorΟß venoirn recostroinΟß effus dy cathedral Sant Sophia tan com spanjon dy quaΟΑeglad, dirigiΟΑ pall'architect Borgond NicolΟ† s'Avignon.
Having fallen into somewhat of a state of disrepair, the fourteenth century saw extensive reconstruction of Saint Sophia's cathedral along with expansion of the complex, headed by Burgund architect NicolΟ† s'Avignon.

Deut all'oc es ig s'Avignon dign parveg e cos joculant y dobanΟß de "l'architect dy ruin dell'Empir", la sceint y cainΟß tragic volent se produgr afont dy Sant Sophia aval de tre cent annaΟΑ.
It is for this reason that s'Avignon is sometimes jocularly referred to as the "architect of the Empire's demise", given the tragic events that would take place within Saint Sophia's three hundred years later.

127.2: y cΟΠros ovrir de "make possible"

y cΟΠros ovrir de /i keΥàr…îz …îΥàvr…Σr de/ [i k…ΣΥà Ä…îz  äΥàv Ä…ΣΥê de]
- enable, make possible, allow for, permit, to create conditions such that (something) is more feasible or more likely to occur

Etymology: idiom from the nineteenth century, literally "to open the opportune moment of". The verb ovrir descends (under the influence of covrir "cover") via Old Borlish ovrir, avrir from Latin aperi≈ç "I reveal, open". The noun cΟΠros "window of opportunity, opportune moment" is a modern borrowing from Ancient Greek ΈΚΈ±ΈΙœ¹œ¨œ² "period of time, season; opportunity".

Sell'osceyan vil y cΟΠros nos ovrir de lembic gostar.
/s…¦Υ¨l…î.xiΥàjan v…Σl i keΥàr…îz n…îz …îΥàvr…Σr de l…¦mΥàb…Σk g…îΥàstar/
[s…ΣΥ¨l…îΥàΟßjan v…Σl i k…ΣΥà Ä…îz n…îz  äΥàv Ä…ΣΥê de l…ΣmΥàb…Σk g äΥàst…êΥê]
3s.gn=birthday will df opportunity 1p open-inf of alcohol taste-inf
Her birthday will give us the chance to try alcohol.

127.3: broncar "flinch"

broncar /br…înΥàkar/ [b Ä ä≈΄Υàk…êΥê]
- flinch, wince, recoil, to reflexively move away from something perceived as unpleasant or a threat;
- stumble, trip, slip, lurch, to make a sudden unsteady movement;
- balk, back out, chicken out, get cold feet, to refuse to act due to (espeecially sudden) fear or uncertainty

Etymology: from Old Borlish broncar "stumble, misstep", cognate with synonymous bronchier in Old French but of unknown further origin. Perhaps connected to Latin broccus "buck-toothed" or truncus "docked, cut off", but neither is particularly semantically tempting. Unhelpful also are the equally unsourceable examples of Lombard bronco "thorny branch" and Barcelon bronc "knot in wood".

Jo vole pogntar apar ma jo broncau.
/ £o voΥàle p…îjnΥàtar aΥàpar pi  £o br…înΥàko/
[ ùo v äΥàle p äjnΥàt…êΥêβÄΩ Ä…êΥàp…êΥê pi  ùo b Ä ä≈΄Υàko]
1s will-ipf poke-inf through but 1s balk-pst
I meant to flirt but I chickened out.

127.4: snuvr fair "sniff"

snuvr fair /Υàsn…ΣvrΧ© fer/ [Υ¨zn…Σ.v…ê feΥê]
- sniff, sniffle, snivel, snort, to make an audible inhalation through the nose (especially when the nose is blocked);
- scoff, turn up one's nose, sneer, to react with contempt or scorn

Etymology: verb phrase from snuvr "sniff, snort", itself a derivation from the verb snuvlar, which had become obsolete by the seventeenth century. This descends from Old Borlish isnuvlar, a borrowing from Old English snyflan "to have a runny nose". In the latter sense the phrase is attested from the eighteenth century.

No stou bogr snuvr fair dell'oΟß cΟΠros.
/no stu b…îjrΧ© sn…ΣvrΧ© fer d…¦Υàl…îts keΥàr…îz/
[no stu Υàb…î.j…ê Υàzn…Σ.v…ê feΥê d…ΣΥàl…îs k…ΣΥà Ä…îz]
neg be.necessary at.all sniff do.inf of-df=s.dt opportunity
The opportunity's nothing to sniff at.

127.5: Deadly Disasters

notes on the subject of various (alt) historical disasters.

The Great Flie Flood was a natural disaster in 1171 Willemy which submerged or swept through large parts of Frisland. Inflowing waters of the Rustig Strait created the island of Texel, and the freshwater Lake Almere (which fed into the Strait along the Flie river) became brackish and eventually fully saline as a maritime gulf.

The then-duke Lovis the Tall, who had been staying in the environs of Amstel for some months, is the most famous (and most historically influential) casualty of the Flood, but estimates have suggested a death toll approaching ten thousand.

The destruction is thought to have spurred the development of Axbane on Borland as an alternative, safer harbour for traders passing through the Rustigh Strait.

---

The First Burning of London was a conflagration in 1290 which lasted for a full week and killed approximately four hundred people. This was the earliest of several such burnings, at least two of which are more accurately described as sackings or conquerings. Unlike (say) the Fourth Burning, it is purely conjectural that this particular fire was deliberately set as part of a plot to steal the Redstone Chair (presumably orchestrated by some Markland or Kentish noble).

---

The Failed Crusade was an undertaking which aimed to recreate the March of Jerusalem. The city and its surrounds had been captured during the First Crusade and the first Marksman of Jerusalem, Savino the Just, had acceded in 1119, but it had fallen less than seventy years later.

This crusade was called in 1210 by Pope Just (a direct descendant of Lord Savino), and it was utterly unsuccessful. The campaign went so badly that the Convoy Australier having their control of Port Simon and Antioch revoked in 1208 is generally regarded as a boon for the Convoy.

The various military catastrophes stoked discontent in Rome and elsewhere, along with the outbreak of the First Great Dying (a plague thought to have been brought to Europe by returning crusaders).

##

128.1: St Margaret Meeting

excerpt in translation from the 1920 work A Door, Once Opened by Clarissa Bellamy.

L'aer bisan de harvest feya mornΟß ag gien d'Evangelin cant i quaucau par Rout San Margaret, con sy mantel raffleyant dereΟΑr.
The crisp autumn air nipped at Evangeline's cheeks as she hurried down St Margaret's Lane, her cloak billowing behind her.

Cayen l'yeul a Londr un aucoum vivaΟß des y commovenΟß de marcant e carrot, eΟΑ y fler de castagn stant attost ΟΑolen des y vagn d'un venΟßour proism.
The streets of London were alive with the bustle of merchants and carriages, and the scent of roasting chestnuts wafted from a nearby vendor's cart.

I clignau y picq pachet fassaΟΑ ne papir broun contr sy peitrin, ogl lanΟßant la por garantir ig l'oc remaneus fast.
She clutched the small parcel wrapped in brown paper to her chest, glancing at it to ensure it remained secure.

Cant l'approisma y torn vars Rout Hawthorn oc menant vars y livrer de Cavendish, un figur smargoy des vogt dy foscur.
As she approached the turn onto Hawthorn Street that led to Cavendish's bookshop, a figure emerged from the shadows.

Y domn sta cort naΟΑusc eΟΑ i porta un curtel de bilrod plen, con sy visaΟß vellaΟΑ par un capel nell'eutaΟΑ e laΟΑ d'orlaΟß.
The woman was rather short and wore a dress of rich billrod, her face obscured by a fashionable wide-brimmed hat.

128.2: fardig "solo"

fardig /farΥàdaj/ [f…êΥêΥàdaj]

    solo, music played by a single performer;
    chance to shine, big moment, moment in the spotlight, an oppoortunity to be noticed to do demonstrate one's skill;
    moment of truth, critical point, a time when one's skills or resolve are finally tested (especially after a period of complacency or uncertainty)

Etymology: seventeenth century, originally in the musical sense. Borrowed from a continental language, likely Vascon fardig(a) or Portingale fardigo/a "solo, played alone". These come from a derivative of the Arabic root Ό¹ΌéΊ±Ό£Ί· ¬Ζ fard "alone, single, solitary". Extension to non-musical contexts is seen from the late nineteenth century.

    Jo save l'oc caum contene un fardig d'aloud.
    / £o saΥàve l…îk kom Υ¨k…în.teΥàne …Σn farΥàdaj daΥàlud/
    [ ùo s…êΥàve l…î kom Υ¨k…în.t…ΣΥàne …Σ…± f…êΥêΥàdaj d…êΥàlud]
    1s know-ipf df=s.px piece contain-ipf indf solo of=lute
    I knew this piece contained a lute solo.

128.3: lau pre yonoscon "in the middle of nowhere"

lau pre yonoscon /lo pre Υ¨jo.n…îΥàx…în/ [lo p Äe Υ¨ ùo.n äΥàx…în]

    in the middle of nowhere, isolated, a long way from anywhere of interest like settlements or amenities;
    stuck, lost, making no progress on a problem

Etymology: early nineteenth century idiom literally meaning "leagues before wherever". The noun lau "league" comes via Old Borlish from Insular Latin lauga "league" (cf. leuga, legua), originally a Gaulish unit. The pronoun yonoscon "wherever, anywhere" is an extension of yon "where" < Latin ubicumque "wherever" by analogy to words like calscon "whichever" < Latin quΡ¹liscumque "of whatever quality". In metaphorical use from the early twentieth century.

    Nos feim un baugl lau pre yonoscon.
    /n…îz fim …Σn bojl lo pre Υ¨jo.n…îΥàx…în/
    [n…îz fim …Σm bojl lo p Äe Υ¨ ùo.n äΥàx…în]
    1p do.pst-1p indf camp league before wherever
    We camped in the middle of nowhere.

128.4: increiltaΟΑ "disbelief"

increiltaΟΑ /Υ¨…Σn.krilΥàtaΈΗ/ [Υ¨…Σ≈΄.k Ä…ΣlΥàtah]
- doubt, disbelief, incredulity, the inability to believe something;
- doubt, suspicion, mistrust, the inability to trust someone

Etymology: Middle Borlish noun derivation of increil "disbelieving, mistrustful", orphaned negative of (by then obsolete) creil "gullible, trusting". This descends from Latin crΡ™dulus, whence also creu "an oath of frustration or (ironically) appreciation, lately particularly in sexual contexts", which is a 20C borrowing from Portingale crΟ©u via the meaning "idiot, git, jerk".

Y taflot nos souvolau un nievr d'increiltaΟΑ atorn.
/i taΥàfl…ît n…îz Υ¨su.voΥàlo …Σn nj…¦vrΧ© Υ¨d…Σn.krilΥàtaΈΗ aΥàt…îrn/
[i t…êΥàfl…ît n…îz Υ¨su.v äΥàlo …Σn Υànj…¦.v…ê Υ¨d…Σ≈΄.k Ä…ΣlΥàtaβÄΩΟΑ…êΥàt…îΥên]
df game 1p sweep-pst indf fog of=doubt around
The game sowed seeds of mistrust among us.

128.5: Mathematical Etymology

excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary o'Mathematics, a reference quire compiled by Rotcham University Primers.

zeffer /Υàz…¦.f…ô/ [zero] the number of things in an empty collection, the quantity one fewer than one. Before 1350 < Scholastic Latin zefer, zefrum (alt. zephirus by false connection to Zephyr) < Arabic ΊΒΌêΌ¹Ό£Ί± βÄΔ αΙΘifr "empty, void" < in sense from Sanscrit ύΛΕύΞ²ύΛ®ύΞçύΛ· βÄΔ ≈¦≈ΪnyΟΓ "empty, void".

ablate /Υàa.bl…ôt/ [negative] fewer than zeffer, as of a debtor's money or a distance backward. 1481 < Lombard ablad < Scholastic Latin ablΡ¹tus "taken away, withdrawn, removed", first used in financial accounts and taken up by mathematicians some decades later.

dart /ΥàdaΥêt/ [~vector] a segment of a line, considered with a choice of which end is the head and which the vane. Around 1470 < Early Merch dart "throwing spear, dart" < Middle French dart, dard < Medieval Latin dardus < Frankish *dar≈çth < Classical German *dar≈çthus "light spear, javelin, dart". Extended to its mathematical sense by the pictorial analogy of drawing arrowheads on line segments.

augrim /Υà…ôu.g…Ψ…Σm/ [~arithmetic] the mathematics of calculation with numbers; computation more generally. Around 1220 < Drengot augrime, algrisme etc < Old French algorisme < Scholastic Latin algorismus "the use of Arabic numbers" < Arabic ΊßΌ³Ί°ΌéΌàΌéΊßΊ±ΌêΊ≤Ό£ΌÖΌêΌäΌë βÄΔ al-αΗΒawΡ¹rizmiyy, a name of the Persian polymath whose mathematical works introduced much of mathematics to Europe.

arrhumb /Υàa.…Ψ…ôm/ [cosine] the sending of an angle to the quotient of the height of a rod rotated by that angle from the vertical to the full length of the rod. Early Merch arrum, arrom (the modern spelling is influenced by unrelated rhumb "compass bearing") < Arabic ΊßΌ³Ί±ΌÖΊ≠ βÄΔ ar-rumαΗΞ "spear, shaft". This appears to be a metaphorical extension related to the use ofΌàΊΣΊ± βÄΔ watar < Sanscrit ύΛ€ύΞçύΛ·ύΛΨ βÄΔ jyΡ¹Χ¹ "bowstring" for vater.

129.1: The Furore of 1961

notes in translation on the collapse of the Drengot Collusion.

All'hour dy Tourf, y Collujon sta gournaΟΑ par un administraΟßon stanne sou Romney Darchild.
At the time of the Furore, the Collusion was being administered by a tinplate government under Romney Darchild.

Sceye parmy toΟΑ y scandal dy Tourf y descujon dy traitment furtif accommis par y Majon Roug noncupaΟΑ (y nom commun por y Minister a Moustr dy Collujon) situaΟΑ a Roun.
One of the scandals of the Furore was the revelation of the clandestine activities undertaken by the Red House (the popular name of the Collusion's Ministry for Information) in Roun.

L'ec gest, enombraΟΑ ny Brevier de Darchild, surcourn un kirt ag madou, cos primer l'administraΟßon actual dessoclant eΟΑ apres cos surrun y Collujon enter.
These activities, enumerated in the Darchild memorandum, lit the fuse, first bringing down the current administration and shortly thereafter the entire Collusion.

Un rebondisc dy sfondranΟß dy Collujon DrengoΟßan sta l'effectuanΟß dy reform Cantuesc cas ostracon, ec volen a lorry mesur sarvir com model ny polity plusour tras y mont.
One effect of the collapse of the Drengot Collusion was the implementation of the Kentish ostracon reforms, which would go on to be used as a model in several polities across the globe.

129.2: Οßaveyar "cushion"

Οßaveyar /Υ¨tsa.viΥàjar/ [Υ¨tsavΥàj…êΥê]
- cushion, pad, to furnish with cushions or similar soft things;
- cushion, soften, to absorb or deaden the impact or weight of something;
- sugarcoat, sanitise, prettify, to make (something) more palatable or otherwise easier to accept

Etymology: seventeenth-century backformation from noun Οßaveyal "cushion, pillow", which descends via Old Borlish Οßa(r)ve»ùal "pillow" from synonymous cervΡΪcal in Latin (the loss of /r/ is irregular but compare Οßavel "brain" < cerebellum). In the last sense the verb is especially seen in the phrase y los Οßaveyar "to sweeten the pill", literally "to cushion the slab".

Dig mey; tu va noc a y los me Οßaveyar.
/daj mi | ti va n…îk a i l…îz me Υ¨tsa.viΥàjar/
[daj mi | ti va n…îβÄΩga i l…îz me Υ¨tsavΥàj…êΥê]
say 1s.dsj | ti go neg to df slab 1s.obl cushion-inf
Tell me; you don't have to sugarcoat it.

129.3: supellex "paraphernalia"

supellex /Υ¨si.p…¦Υàl…¦ks/ [Υ¨si.p…ΣΥàl…¦ks]
- paraphernia, miscellanea, stuff, assorted items and especially the things that accrue over time in the course of some pastime or occupation;
- apparatus, kit, equipment, tools of the trade, the set of items one needs to do some activity;
- habits, wont, things that one does as a matter of course (often in a negative sense);

Etymology: 17C borrowing from Latin supellΡ™x "furniture, utensils, kit", likely related to leg≈ç "I collect, gather". Extension to behaviours is seen from the late nineteenth century.

Jo trouvau nien benoΟßabr entr y supellex dy collocquour.
/ £o truΥàvo rj…¦n Υ¨be.n…îΥàtsabrΧ© Υà…¦ntrΧ© i Υ¨si.p…¦Υàl…¦ks di Υ¨k…î.l…îΥàkwur/

1s find-pst nothing useful among df paraphernalia of-df journalist

129.4: kirt ag madou "catalyst"

kirt ag madou /k…Σrt …¦j maΥàdu/ [k…ΣΥêt …¦j m…êΥàdu]
- catalyst, trigger, spark, inciting incident, a thing or event that sets something (especially something of outsized impact) into motion;

Etymology: idiom attested from the 18C, literally "spark to the kindling". The noun kirt "spark" descends from synonymous kirte in Old Borlish, and is probably onomatopoeic in origin. The noun madou "tinder, kindling" is a borrowing from Middle French (a)madou "lure, bait; kindling, touchwood", a deverbal of amadouer "coax, lure; kindle". This verb is thought to descend via Old Norrish mata "lure, feed" from Classical German *mat≈çnam "feed".

Cay rix un kirt ag madou por defas machinaΟßonal.
/ke r…Σks …Σn k…Σrt …¦j maΥàdu p…îr deΥàfaz Υ¨ma.kiΥàna.tsoΥànal/
[ke  Ä…Σks …Σ≈΄ k…ΣΥêt …¦j m…êΥàdu p…îΥê d…ΣΥàfaz Υ¨ma.k…ΣΥàna.ts äΥànal]
fall war indf spark to.df tinder for progress technological
War is a catalyst of technological development.

##

129.5: Arcabil

The Arcabil are a collection of islands in the Atlantic, east of Mashick. They comprise hundreds of landmasses, of which Cocain is the largest.

Toponymy
The name "Arcabil" has traditionally been considered to be an uncomplicated use of Arabic ΊΘΌéΊ±Ό£Ί°ΌéΊ®ΌêΌäΌ³ βÄΔ ('archabΡΪl) "archipelago", borrowed into Scholastic Latin as Arcabilia (and treated as a plural on the basis of its -bilia ending). However, there is little evidence of such a word existing in Arabic in the fifteenth century.

The possibility has been raised of a metathetic and dissimilatory variant of unattested ΊßΌ³ΌÉΊ±ΌäΊ® βÄΔ (al-carΡΪb) "the Carib", using a local name for a group of peoples of the Arcabil, but the phonetics are tenuous.

History
The Arcabil were first encountered by Vetomundine powers in the late fifteenth century, during the first stage of the New World Company's voyages west under the aegis of Malik Munir al-Hamdawi. By the early sixteenth century Morrack had established several semi-permanent outposts there, which were important ports for the circumnavigation of the Cynthia under Fredun al-Casmi.

The orange liqueur known as "fleuse" is a genericisation of the Fleus d'Arcabil mixer first manufactured in the early nineteenth century on a Borlish-speaking island of the Arcabil.


People
Judeta Barracin (fl. 1919), author of social comedy born in Andalus who spent her formative years in the Arcabil
LoΟΑaΟß Berxon (fl. 1910), who grew up in the Arcabil and who was an important member of the Outland Arcabil Association after her emigration to Borland

130.1: The Heedless Shoal

extract in translation from 1952 work Hus Tars (literally "Sheer Haddock", published in translation under the title "The Heedless Shoal"), written by Haubrath author Marc Lambount. It has become a staple of Borlish lessons for those learning the language in the schools of Markland, despite the title's homophony with Merch his tarse "his penis".

Y port dy cahut closisceu ne Οßottant e Jacq plombettau sur y hamac criscant, las de sy post quiΟΑeyant. 
The cabin door slammed shut and Jake flopped onto the creaky bunk, exhausted from his gruelling shift.

Y puΟΑ ojabr dell'effum fΟΠx de ronΟß insinua toΟΑ sy cambr sarraΟΑ.
The horrible stench of diesel exhaust permeated his cramped quarters.

De truc l'accaΟßau sy cofrsac ragottiscent ny gaheΟß, dont y govon roglfeyaΟΑ sonaurn de lorry vagiscment famigler.
He shoved his weathered backpack into the locker, whose rusty hinges made their familiar screech.

A starn, l'eΟß ont inexorabr contonen y tors dy vascel e menan jug sy cogtaΟßon a lorrell'istranΟß maritim.
Outside, the relentless waves battered the ship's hull, a constant reminder of their isolation at sea.

No sceye magn solaΟß deur y caldour soumargent cant tossottau l'appragl d'aeraΟßon antic.
There was little relief from the oppressive heat as the ancient ventilation system wheezed.

Durant ig l'allonga, sy front moglaΟΑ de suour, Jacq recuida ant un benaulaΟßon tart cos inutr all'eΟß meis gelaΟΑ seyon passaΟΑ sur un javigour ny Mar NorΟΑer.
As he lay there, sweat beading on his brow, Jake thought back with too-late appreciation to the icy months he'd spent on a North Sea fishing trawler.

130.2: carrot "carriage"

carrot /kaΥàr…ît/ [k…êΥà Ä…ît]
- carriage, wagon, a (usually horse-drawn) vehicle designed for transporting people;
- car, carriage, trolley, a single detachable part of a train;
- module, component, a self-contained (often interchangeable) part of a system which interfaces predictable with other parts

Etymology: diminutive of Old Borlish car "wagon", which descends from Latin carrus "cart, wagon; load" (the suffix may have been added to distinguish the word from car "dear, darling", although the root survives as car "car, automobile" nonetheless). Use for trains is seen with their invention in the nineteenth century; metaphorical extension from that sense is seen in the twentieth.

I laundau cant i descene des y carrot.
/i lonΥàdo kant i Υ¨d…¦.xeΥàne d…¦z i kaΥàr…ît/
[i l änΥàdo kant i Υ¨d…¦.Οß…ΣΥàne d…¦z i k…êΥà Ä…ît]
3s trip-pst as 3s go.down out.of df carriage
He tripped as he got out of the carriage.

130.3: bisan "crisp"

bisan /biΥàzan/ [b…ΣΥàzan]
- crisp, fresh, (of the air) dry and cold;
- bracing, refreshing, invigorating, giving someone energy or alertness;
- candid, hard-hitting, blunt, frank, truthful without consideration for finer feelings

Etymology: originally in the first sense, a Middle Borlish derivation from now-obsolete bis "cold air; specifically, a north wind". This is borrowed via French bise "north wind", of uncertain origin. Possible connections include Tuscan bigio "grey, cloudy" or Classical German *bib≈ç "I shiver".

Me stove pieΟß parol bisan dar a my fraΟΑr.
/me stoΥàve pj…¦ts paΥàr…îl biΥàzan dar a mi fraΟΑrΧ©/
[me st äΥàve pj…¦s p…êΥà Ä…îl b…ΣΥàzan d…êΥêβÄΩ Äa mi Υàf Äa.ΟΑ…ê]
1s.obl must-ipf some word crisp give-inf to 1s.gn brother
I had to tell my brother some hard truths.

130.4: effus "extensive"

effus /…¦Υàf…Σz/ […ΣΥàf…Σz]
- extensive, wide-ranging, covering a large area;
- far-reaching, comprehensive, big, major, significant, affecting many things

Etymology: 16th-century borrowing from Latin eff≈Ϊsus "poured out, discharged", past participle of effund≈ç "pour out, discharge" (itself borrowed at roughly the same time as effondr "spread out, disperse, scatter"). As a vaguer word simply meaning "significant" it is seen from the late 18th century.

J'yeme noc sciΟΑ ig l'aye un majon de livr tal effus.
/ £jeΥàme n…îk x…ΣΈΗ aj l…¦Υàje …Σn maΥà £on de Υàl…ΣvrΧ© tal …¦Υàf…Σz/
[ ù…ΣΥàme n…î ΥàΟß…ΣβÄΩΟΑaj Υàlj3 …Σm m…êΥà ù…în de Υàl…Σ.v…ê tal …ΣΥàf…Σz]
1s=get-ipf ng known cmp 3s=have-ipf indf house of book so extensive
I didn't realise her library was so extensive.

##

130.5: Angland

Angland (Borlish Anglont /anΥàgl…înt/ […ê≈΄Υàgl…înt]) is a region in Borland in the west of the island. Its largest population centre is the city of Vithor.

History

The petty kingdom of Angland was founded by Angle immigrants in the fifth century, along with Southbar and several more minor settlements. It quickly established itself in opposition to Southbar and to the rump sub-Roman Borlish state in the east. At the turn of the ninth century it saw an influx of immigration from East Anglia after that kingdom was subjugated by Markland.

After a series of temporary alliances between Angland and Borland, the marriage of Roman king Stiglan's son Marc to the only daughter of Alfegh of Angland unified the kingdoms for good. This was shortly followed by the Battle of Fendal (810) which forced Southbar to swear fealty as a subordinate kingdom. Not long thereafter was Angland conquered by the Danes along with the rest of the island.

The Borland varieties of Old English survived longest in Angland, with records suggesting that an English language descending from the original settlers was spoken in rural parts of the region until as late as the seventeenth century.

Places

Ο΅adrosc, an area of moorland in the northwest which features a labyrinth built in Kelt times
Gambrig, a village and nearby monastery noteworthy for its medieval chronicle in a variety of Old English
Hawkirch, site of the Hawkirch Surrender of 1632
Vithor, regional capital on the west coast known for the New Vithor University, a major research institution

131.1: Catrin of Kernow

collected notes in translation on the subject of Catrin of Kernow.

Catrin de Cornou (m. 1233) sta un parson influyent n'Istr Boral durant l'Empir MitaΟΑ-Asc.
Catrin of Kernow (d. 1233) was an influential figure in Borland during the Medieval Imperia.

Catrin de Cornou sta y sour antir de Rey Alan VI de Magn Dorogn, oΟß y Rallignment de Dorogn accommettent d'an 1225.
Catrin of Kernow was the elder sister of King Alan VI of Greater Devon, who orchestrated the Realignment of Devon in 1225.

Lorrell'yam sta Damian y Saif, y preu Pap (ne Rom) stant sleut des entrell'orn religious y Brethin.
Their uncle was Damian the Wise, the first (Roman) Pope to be elected from among the Brethin religious order.

D'an 1292 sceye y chronic Y ViΟΑ a Catrin de Cornou scaut, y stoir detaglant de sy viΟΑ joun, sy mariΟΑaΟß con un nobr Borallesc e sell'assassinat d'an 1233. 
In 1292 the chronicle The Life of Catrin of Kernow was written, detailing her early life, marriage to a Borlish nobleman and eventual assassination in 1233.

Y chronic a un gravitaΟΑ particuler dec l'interman y preu ouvr prolongaΟΑ scaut ne Borallesc (all'opposson de Latin) a them d'un laic.
The chronicle is particularly important for being the first longform work written in Borlish (as opposed to Latin) about a lay-person.

131.2: liesc bouclant "time loop"

liesc bouclant /lj…¦x buΥàklant/ [lj…¦Οß b äΥàklant]
- time loop, (in fiction) a period experienced by one or more people repeatedly;
- (by extension) routine, monotonous or repetitive activity such as errands that are done many times with little variation

Etymology: idiom from the late 19th century, literally "looping duration". The noun liesc "term, period, duration" is a Old Borlish deverbal from lejar "stop moving, crash" (the word originally meaning "endpoint"), from Insular Latin laedic≈ç "I hit, collide". The verb bouclar "loop, curl, go in a circle" derives from Old Borlish boucle "loop, bracelet" (modern boucr), via Old French from Latin buccula "chinstrap".

The phrase was first used in the translation of 1882 work Cludor ncar Vantel "A Postman in Wrexham", which features such a time loop.

Com veleΟΑ vos farar ΟΑa vos cay un liesc bouclant?
/k…îm veΥàl…¦ΈΗ v…îz faΥàrar ΟΑa v…îz ke …Σn lj…¦x buΥàklant/
[k…îm v…ΣΥàl…¦ v…îz f…êΥà Ä…êΥê ΟΑa v…îz ke …Σn lj…¦Οß b äΥàklant]
how will.sbj-2p 2p fare-inf if 2p fall indf duration loop-ptcp.p
What would you do if you were in a time loop?

131.3: foyaΟßant "virtuosic"

foyaΟßant /Υ¨f…îj…êΥàtsant/ [Υ¨f…îjΥàtsant]
- virtuosic, gifted, masterful, demonstrating immense technical skill;
- flamboyant, resplendent, dazzling, theatrical, bold or audacious in the way one does something;
- showy, showing off, peacocking, ostentatious, being prideful in demonstrating one's abilities

Etymology: present participle of foyaΟßar "glare, shine brightly, dazzle; show off, flaunt". This is itself a derivation from foyaΟß "glare, intense light; gaudiness, extravagance", an Old Borlish extension of foy "fire" < focus "hearth" with the pejorative suffix -aΟß < -Ρ¹ceus "shaped like". The noun foyaΟß "mess, jumble" < fodi≈ç "I dig up" is unrelated but may have had semantic influence.

The word appears in both approving and disapproving contexts from as early as it is attested.

Cay tell'yoc veram un praxitel foyaΟßant.
/ke t…¦lΥàj…îk veΥàram …Σn Υ¨prak.siΥàt…¦l Υ¨f…î.j…êΥàtsant/
[ke t…ΣΥàlj…îk v…ΣΥà Äam …Σm Υ¨p Äak.s…ΣΥàt…¦l Υ¨f…îjΥàtsant]
fall 2s-gn=husband truly indf sculptor virtuosic
Your husband is truly a masterful sculptor.

131.4: recuidar "reflect"

recuidar /Υ¨re.kajΥàdar/ [Υ¨ Äe.k…êjΥàd…êΥê]
- reflect, consider, ponder, muse, introspect, think to oneself, to actively think about something or about what one knows;
- deduce, realise, infer, construe, to reach a conclusion by reasoning

Etymology: Middle Borlish coinage derived from cuidar "think, suppose, intend". This descends directly from Latin c≈çgit≈ç "I think, reflect, have in mind". The extension in Borlish to deduction is attested from the 18th century.

Jo recuida jamay noiteyar n'un davarn.

1s reflect-ipf never spend.night-inf in=indf resort

##

131.5: Jeans

Jeans (Merch /d £eΥênz/; Borlish Jen / £…¦n/; Jean Zena /Υàze.na/) is an Italian polity and city of the same name. It comprises the northwest Middlesea coastline of Italy bordering Provence as well as the island of Corse. It has a long history as one of the so-called 'Latin Republics'.

Toponymy

The Merch name Jeans was borrowed from Old French Jannes /Υàd £a.n…ôs/, deriving originally from Genua in Latin. This is probably simply the classical local word for "knee" (cf. Classical Latin genu), in reference to the shape of the coastline it lies on.

History

Jeans is the oldest of the Latin Republics, having come into existence in the tenth century. Along with Venise, it serves as a counterpoint to the notion that the Second Tetrarchy period in the eleventh and twelfth centuries was solely comprised of great centralised empires. The leader of Jeans, known as the console, was elected from among the merchant nobility.

In 1207 N Jeans invaded the Tyrrhene Isles of Corse and Sardine, wresting them from Roman control. Only the northernmost of the two islands would remain in the Jean orbit more permanently.

Piracy plagued the Middlesea coast for the same reason that maritime trade was so profitable for Jeans. It reached a peak at the turn of the sixteenth century as newfound wealth from the Novomund flooded the region.

People

Franco Cicala (r. 1119-22/4), first console from the Cicale family

References

The Latin Republics: Jewels under Tyrian Standards, a 1902 historical reference quire by Jacob Trelaune (cf. in particular 'Part III: Old Jeans')

big /baj/ [baj]
- (historical) chariot, a two-wheeled horse-drawn vehicle as used in Bronze and Iron Age warfare;

132.3: run "symbol"

run /r…Σn/ [ Ä…Σn]
- character, letter, the visual representation of a letter of a writing system;

132.4: sce "wheelhouse"

sce /xe/ [Οße]
- (historical) cathedral, the principal church of an (arch)diocese containing the episcopal throne;
- (historical, specifically) papal see, Holy See, the jurisdiction of a pope;
- venue, arena, stadium, theatre, the building in which an event happens;
- place, venue, setting, the place where anything happens;
- field, domain, the study of something in particular;
- (in the expression ne sce de) wheelhouse, bailiwick, domain, a person's area of authority or expertise

Etymology: originally in the first senses, descending via Old Borlish sce, sie "see, (arch)diocese" from Latin sedes "seat; residence; (Late Latin) episcopal see". Broadening to more general locations occurred in tandem with the import of cathedral to replace most of the religious senses. The idiom ne sce de "in the wheelhouse of" is attested from the 19th century.

J'admet ig no scey l'augrim a nacleu ne sce de mey.

1s=admit cmp ng there.is df=computation at slope in venue of 1s.dsj
I admit differential calculus isn't in my wheelhouse.

##

132.5: Dime measures

The dime measures (less commonly known as the 'tithe measures' or 'tenth measures') are a modern system of units based on factors of ten. The name derives from the French dΟ°me "one-tenth", from synonymous decimus in Latin.

The dime measures were codified during the nineteenth century for ease of communication, as theoretical development coalesced into a global endeavour. They have been updated several times since, as it has become feasible to use more convenient yardsticks.

Standard modifiers may be added to each unit to increase or decrease their size by factors of 10. In most common use are:

    great (10β¹¥)
    tithe (10β¹Μ¬Ι)
    petty (10β¹Μβ¹¥)
    scant (10β¹Μβ¹Η)

Length

There are three bases for dime lengths: the inch, the foot (10 inches) and the league (10β¹¥ feet). At first, the length of the foot was fixed somewhat arbitrarily as the midpoint of the very slightly different Roman foot and the Cathayan "surveyor's span". In 1946, the foot was redefined to make the normal speed [speed of light] (denoted ≈™) equal to precisely ten great leagues per second.

Several other traditional measures of length were given dime values. For instance, in dime measures the mile is equal to five thousand feet or half a league.
Heft [mass]

There are four bases for dime hefts: the scruple, the ounce (100 scruples), the tabre (100 ounces) and the ton (100 tabres). The third of these units takes its approximate value from the Persian mann and its name from the city of Tabris [Tabriz]. The ounce was originally defined to be the mass of a cube of water of side length one inch; this was later refined to specify that the water be at its triple point of pressure and temperature.
Derived units

Many units are defined in terms of these base units of length and heft, as well as the second. For example, the shove measures the might [force] required to accelerate a heft of one tabre to a speed of one foot per second.

Y peyan dessou de mell'azig stan sdruΟßollabr ag ploy dy crevoscr, relougen com argent deborsellaΟΑ.

The lampposts flickered weakly and cast dancing shadows on soot-dark walls.

L'aer pene cos lort, parvasiΟΑ con y fler a hum moglastr e con y fum des murΟΑon a goΟΑou.
The air was heavy, thick with the scent of damp earth and the smoke of a thousand chimneys.

Un carrot passau broyent tandic sy rouΟΑ clacquaurn sur y pieΟΑr jagottaΟΑ eΟΑ aug moΟΑer souvolau den all'orlaΟß de my jacquot.

Jo fey un soΟßavau de maldicΟßon ma pausau noc a lauparβÄîstove alcun hour despardr.
I cursed under my breath but didnβÄôt break strideβÄîthere was no time to waste.

Jo poΟΑe ja l'eΟß murmuranΟß lontan de voΟß oΟ·r com j'aye spien, eΟß interront par y sounΟß sporadic de ricottanΟß heloidous.
I could already hear the distant murmur of voices I was hoping for, punctuated by the occasional burst of raucous laughter.

133.2: quiΟΑeyar "exhaust"

- exhaust, tire, frazzle, (as of work) to be physically gruelling;
- tax, challenge, to be difficult to accomplish

Etymology: the descendant of Old Borlish quiΟΑe»ùar "sicken, nauseate; unsettle, rattle, unnerve", derived from dialectal quiΟΑ "belly, stomach, intestines". This is a borrowing from Old English cwiΟΨ "womb; stomach". The shift in meaning from disgust or emotional disturbance towards hard work is complete by the eighteenth century (although related forms in rural dialects retain some earlier senses).

133.4: monnocquar "trudge"

- trudge, lumber, plod, traipse, to walk heavily or with some difficulty;
- struggle, persevere, to work on a task while finding it difficult

Etymology: first attested in the 18th century; further origin obscure. Attempts to connect the word "monk"βÄîperhaps moigne or monhe from regional French and Portingale dialects respectivelyβÄîstumble on phonetic grounds (and the proposed semantic connection of a monk's plodding gait is tenuous).

More tempting might be Arabic ΌÖΌéΌÜΌ£Ό΅ΌèΌàΌÉ βÄΔ (manh≈Ϊk) "exhasuted, spent", although no account of the word's journey to Borlish has been corrobated. Claims of a Novomundine origin also lack convincing evidence. Perhaps simply an onomatopoeic or fanciful coinage.

133.5: Chemical Etymology (cont.)

excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary of Alchemy, a reference quire compiled by Rotcham University Primers [1].

athimar /Υàa.ΈΗ…ΣΥ¨maΥê/ [antimony] the corporal [element] whose atoms have 51 mora, a reflecting grey half-metal known historically from its coholl [sulphide]. Around 1400 < Scholastic Latin athimΡ¹ris "coholl of athimar" < synonymous Arabic ΊΞΌêΊΪΌ£ΌÖΌêΊ· βÄΔ 'ithmid, possibly from Copt. Applied to the corporal itself (as opposed to the ore) since the eighteenth century.

coholl /k äΥàh…îl/ [sulphide] any substance formed of brimstone [sulphur] and a metalβÄîfor example, fool's gold is a coholl of iron. 1610 < Vascon cohol "coholl of athimar [stibnite]" < Arabic ΌÉΌèΊ≠Ό£Ό³ βÄΔ kuαΗΞl "eye-black", after the use of this substance as make-up. The term was generalised to all such metal compounds by Zaravcar in the late eighteenth century.

machovine /Υàma.k äΥàv…ôin/ [strontium] the corporal whose atoms have 38 mora, a yellow-grey metal. 1789 < Scholastic Latin machovΡΪnus "machovine" < Machov, the town in Poland where the corporal was first isolated from the nearby mine's ore. The ash [carbonate] of machovine has seen use as a catalyst for the production of sugar from the braxoch beet.

maschale /m…ôΥàsk…¦Υêl/ [propionic acid] the acid oil [carboxylic acid] with a spine [carbon chain] of length three, a malodorous clear liquid. 1850 < Scholastic Latin maschΡ¹le < Ancient Greek ΈΦαΨΑœÉœ΅αΨΑΧ¹ΈΜΈΖ "armpit", after the substance's presence in human sweat as produced by skin zoia [microorganisms].

[1] a publishing house based in the city of Rotcham [Rochdale, Greater Manchester] and affiliated with the local university.

134.1: Lachesism

excerpt in translation from the video 'Lachesism', written by John Koenig.

Tras un murΟΑon d'annaΟΑ, nos eu y ciel sgardaΟΑ e se cleunfeyaΟΑ des hougr.

134.2: govon "hinge"

govon /goΥàv…în/ [g äΥàv…în]
- hinge, a jointed device that allow the swinging of a door;
- pivot, axle, any thing on which something turns;
- centre of rotation, the point which remains fixed by a turning motion;
- pole, (in astronomy) either of the two points on the Earth's surface that intersect its axis of rotation;
- turning point, critical moment, an instant or decision with significant consequences;
- obsession, passion, fixation, the object of someone's obsessive interest

Etymology: originally in the first sense, the descendant of Old Borlish govon "hinge, tenon, pin". This reflects Vulgar Latin g≈çf≈ç "nail, peg, dowel", a variant of synonymous gomphus in Classical Latin. The extension to a generic point of rotation (and from there to all other senses) had occurred by the Revitalist period.

##

134.5: A Motley Miscellany

The Bahassassin River [1] is the longest river on the continent of Mendeva [North America], with a total length of just over two thousand leagues. The river passes through multiple polities, including Zangathy and Hasiny, and its eastern tributaries descend from the Sturgovan Mountains [the Appalachians]. The largest city on the banks of the Bahassassin is Nadacow, the capital city of Hasiny. The name of the river comes from the Carovacian [Caddo] name bΟΓhassΟΓssin "mother of rivers".

[1] comprising the Missouri River and the portion of the Mississippi River downstream of St Louis.

---

Mark of Tolose (d. 1453) was the son of King Ambrose II of Vascony, and the father of King Ambrose III of Vascony. Although he was crown prince, he died three years before his father and thus never reigned. He married Margaret of Iscombe [Exeter] shortly before 1435. Along with Ambrose, they had three other children: their first child Alexandra, and two younger children Victor of Astorga and Natalia.

---

Wings Afire (Ale in Fiamme) is an opera written by Venetian Chiarina Bruno and first performed in the Verona Arena in 1824. It was the first work staged in the Verona Arena since it was shuttered at the outbreak of the Second German War.

The opera is a retelling of the legend of Icarus which takes place entirely after the events of the myth, with references to the dead Icarus in the dialogue. It follows the matron of a clothiery who meets a grieving Daedalus; she helps him to rediscover the joy of invention. The opera's music features the low-pitched mournful melodies of the graveyard fiddlers from Borlish folklore; Bruno is said to have heard of them from her brother Jacomo.

##

135.1: A Hotel in Nadacow

excerpt in translation from the early chapters of masquira trevold Men of Jet and Diamond, published (originally in Welsh as Dynnon o VuhuΟΑ a Diamont) by Hasinic Welshman Cadogan Torrior in 1890, shortly after he graduated from the University of Nadacow as a scholar of diplomacy.

Tandic y ΟΑorof dy crevoscr descenn y scagl vars y pascman, Zoe se met afaΟß eΟΑ expatey pall'exedr gran dy davarn.
As the evening crowds make their way to the restaurant, Zoe starts to meander through the hotel's grand atrium.

Y placcou fay survol dessur n'un emblemat de veΟΑr entaccaΟΑ jout jaint sur y paiΟΑ ne mamour poliΟΑ.
The ceiling soars above, a mosaic of stained glass casting patterns on the polished marble floor.

Zoe parcour un porrujon adornaΟΑ oc y gardin cloistaΟΑ dessou surplombant, yon scey un stanc scaudr reflecΟßon tenent de lumner lougent de constellaΟßon faus inquilliΟΑ ny fastig corvaΟΑ.
Zoe crosses an ornate mezzanine overlooking the indoor gardens below, where a shallow pond reflects the twinkling lights of false constellations embedded in the domed roof.

Posc y crasc d'un port defonct, Zoe egrey ortellent ny rout kieΟΑ deur y davarn.
With the creak of a disused door, Zoe slips into the quiet streets outside the hotel.

L'aer es fresc, y fler portant d'un bris des y Bahassassin e d'uncos douΟß des y cociner jos camin dont i memour y stal passant oc jorn paravant.
The air is cool, scented with a breeze from the Bahassassin and something sweet from the streetside cooks whose stalls she remembers passing earlier that day.

135.2: fΟΠx "pollutant"

fΟΠx /f…¦ks/ [f…¦ks]
- slag, dross, waste products from some chemical process (including mining or combustion);
- waste, detritus, any discarded or unwanted material;
- pollutant, a harmful substance which contaminates the environment;
- (figuratively) dregs, scum, the worst and lowest part of something (especially of people)

Etymology: modern borrowing from Scholastic Latin faex "unwanted product of a chemical reaction", a specialised use of Classical faex "sediment, brine, rouge, dregs". Extension from scientific usage is attested from the eighteenth century, and its use as an insult is seen soon afterwards.

L'oc detaxion es aphlox psolic effumant com fΟΠx ou?
/l…îk Υ¨de.takΥàsj…în …¦z aΥàfl…îks psoΥàl…Σk Υ¨…¦.fiΥàmant k…îm f…¦ks u/
[l…îg Υ¨de.t…êkΥàsj…în …Σz …êΥàfl…îks ps äΥàl…Σk Υ¨…¦.f…ΣΥàmant k…î…± f…¦ks u]
df-s.px reaction be.s CO2 deoxygenated outgas-p.prs as waste q
Is this reaction producing waste carbon monoxide?

135.3: runam "secretly"

runam /riΥànam/ [ Ä…ΣΥànam]
- secretly, in secret, covertly, on the QT, not letting anyone else know about something;
- privately, in private, sub rosa, behind closed doors, in a setting conducive to secrecy

Etymology: from Middle Borlish runam "by magic, using sorcery; in secret, hidden from view" (the semantic shift mirroring that of "occult"). This is a derivative of contemporary run "symbol, spell, talisman" using the now-unproductive adverbial suffix -am, descending from Latin anima "soul" (compare kireΟΑam "wholeheartedly", coram "in person").

Nos erau runam alleyant con lou commet a fin.
/n…îz eΥàro riΥànam Υ¨a.liΥàjant k…în lu k…îΥàm…¦t a f…Σn/
[Υ¨n…î.z…ΣΥà Äo  Ä…ΣΥànam Υ¨alΥàjant k…în lu k äΥàm…¦t a f…Σn]
1p cop.ipf-1p secretly ally-p.prs with 3p.dsj start to end
We were secretly on their side the whole time.

135.4: brevier "memo"

brevier /breΥàvj…¦r/ [b Ä…ΣΥàvj…¦Υê]
- memo, memorandum, note, reminder, a short bit of writing to prevent one forgetting something;
- summary, overview, prΟ©cis, synopsis, a summarised description of something;
- brief, report, a description of events

Etymology: Middle Borlish borrowing from Latin breviΡ¹rium "short piece of writing", from brevis "short". Originally restricted to legal contexts, the word had entered common usage by the sixteenth century.

No scerraun alcun imprefait ny brevier je posc.
/no x…¦Υàron al.k…Σn Υ¨…Σm.preΥàfet ni breΥàvj…¦r  £e p…îx/
[no Οß…ΣΥà Äon Υ¨a.g…Σn Υ¨…Σm.p Ä…ΣΥàfet ni b Ä…ΣΥàvj…¦Υê  ùe p…îx]
ng there.be-fut.3p no unexpected in-df brief day.of after
There won't be any surprises in tomorrow's report.

##

135.5: Kernow

Kernow (Merch /Υàk…¦Υê.n…ô/, Borlish Cornou /k…î ÄΥànu/) refers to the north-western peninsula of Gaul. Known in antiquity as Armorica, Brittania or Dumnonia, it was resettled in the early medieval period by Kelt peoples from Albion.

History

The Kernovan kingdoms at the dawn of the eighth century had only recently been unified, ruled by widowed Queen Maud of Angeus. Her son Vigo ascended to the throne upon her death in 720, shortly after the Catumagin Accord which ended hostilities and established an official border with the Kingdom of the Franks. Vigo spearheaded the reconquest of Devon from the kingdom of Wessex, uniting the two peninsulas as the Kingdom of Greater Devon.

The polity suffered somewhat during the ninth and tenth centuries, with the encroachment of the Franks in the east and with extensive Viking raids along the coasts (the monastery at Landvennick was even burned to the ground in 888 N).

The region derives its modern name from the House of Kernow, which in the eleventh century took power by means of an alliance with Normandy. This Kernovan Conjuring led to the establishment of the First Drengot Empire, of which Kernow was a minor vassal.

The Brethin of Kernow enjoyed significant religious influence during the late medieval period.

Kernow became part of the Kingdom of Britain in the fifteenth century as it incorporated territories further afield from its heartland in Wales.

People

Catrin of Kernow, sister of the Devoner king and influential figure in thirteenth-century Borland

Pope Damian VI (the Wise), reigned 1199-1208 N

Saint Victor of Dunkirk, d. 1245 N

##

136.1: Historiography Questions

excerpt in translation from shortform scitation questions in the 2021 edition of the Tremonnow Open School's domain history course 'Making History', which covers the process by which events and people are compiled into historical narratives.

-1. Cal historicour dy siecr catorΟß fornisceu primer y Preu Empir DrengoΟßan sceint tre siecr paravant con nom de 'Empir Constant', oc moliscent a le ligar con y Pag de ConstanΟß (y part frender dy Tetrarchy Diocletian dy siecr catr, Bretagn Roman e Gal administrant)?
-1. Which 14th-century historian first referred to the First Drengot Empire of three centuries earlier as the 'Constant Empire' in an attempt to link it to the Page of Constance (the unrelated part of the fourth-century Diocletian Tetrarchy ruling over Roman Brittania and Gaul)?

-2. NommeΟΑ tre conflict entr polity (y Var Theudesc Primer abseΟ·ΟΑ) ec loy fignt a mesur dell'epoc dy 'PaΟß Lonc", ac definiΟΑ com y liesc d'an 1770 fin a 1815.
-2. Name three international conflicts (not including the First German War) which took place during the 'Long Peace' period, here defined as lasting from 1770 until 1815.

-3. Cu scaveu l'ouvr d'an 1872 'Un Onomatoir ParΟßal dy Novel Londr'? Com y bustr y reconnuΟΑessem des l'epoc Futurist de romanΟß parachthon, i contien y preu inquit de 'ardois' por un icon moustr despleyant indicar, tan com cal autr fanglanΟß lengacer surveunt?
-3. Who wrote 1872 work 'An Incomplete Dictionary of the New London'? As the crowning example from the Futurism period of parachthon romance, it contains the first attestation of 'slate' for a screen displaying information, along with what other lasting coinages?

- huddle, crowd, to stand close together;
- snuggle, cuddle, to be cosy or intimate with someone;

136.3: jacas "chatter"

jacas / £aΥàkaz/ [ ù…êΥàkaz]
- chatter, jabber, prattle, babble, inconsequential or childish talk;
- gossip, dirt, hearsay, scuttlebutt, idle talk about someone else's private affairs

Etymology: deverbal from jacassar "chatter; gossip", a borrowing from synonymous jacasser in French. This verb comes from jacasse "magpie; busybody", a derivative of the name Jacques. Possibly reinforced by the Merch jackass "male donkey".

136.4: infruniscent "bored"

infruniscent /…ΣnΥ¨fri.niΥàx…¦nt/ […Σ…±Υ¨f Äi.n…ΣΥàΟß…¦nt]
- bored, disinterested, unenthusiastic, not feeling entertained or enjoyment;
- impatient, irritable, restive, wanting to move on from what one is currently doing

Etymology: Middle Borlish, negated participle of the verb frunir "to have fun, enjoy oneself". This is a borrowing from Latin fr≈ΪnΡΪscor "I enjoy", which was used non-Classically as an intransitive verb. In the latter senses it is attested from the eighteenth century.

Y stuΟΑant infruniscent assisten y leiΟßoir.

df student bored attend-ipf-3p df lecture
The bored students were at the lecture.

##

136.5: The Novomund (briefly)

This term Novomund for the great landmasses of the western hemisphere is attested from the early sixteenth century, reflecting a univerbation of latin novus mundus "new world". 
Mendeva [North America]

The name Mendeva was formed backwards from Latin MendevalΡ™nsis "from Mendeva", attested already on the maps made during the 1471 Landfall (the demonym was later rederived as MendevΡ¹nus "Mendevan" from the placename). This comes from Vask [Basque] mendeval "west", itself likely a borrowing from Vascon vent de val "valley wind".

    Ambrosia [~Quebec, the Maritimes, New England] is a Vascony-settled polity named for King Ambrose III of Vascony (r. 1456βÄ™1518).
    Zangathy [~the Dakotas, Minnesota, Iowa, Wisconsin, Winnipeg, Ontario] is a landlocked polity occupying the upriver Bahassassin [Mississippi] directly west of the Five Seas [Great Lakes]. Its name reflects Daccotan [Dakota] za≈΄athi "flint region".
    New Provence [~New England, New York, and south as far as South Carolina] is a coastal polity extending westward to the Sturgovan Mountains [Appalachians] which grew from a series of trading post established by Provence merchants.
    Hasiny [~Louisiana, Texas, Mississippi, Arkansas, Oklahoma] is the largest polity in so-called British Mendeva, a few states along the Gulf settled by the Kingdom of Britain [~Wales, etc]. It takes its name from the Carovacian [Caddo] name HasΟ≠ne "our people" for one of the previous nations the modern polity is descended from.
    Taisha [~Texas, Coahuila] is a minor polity of Gulf Mendeva at the crossroads of British and Morrack [Moroccan] influences. Its name comes via Arabic Tayisha' from Carovacian [Caddo] taΟ≠sha' "welcome".
    Mivock [~northern California] is a minor coastal polity on the Lovaqua [Pacific] known for its congenial climate. Of exceedingly sundry influence, its name reflects the name miwoc of the local Mendeva peoples and languages.
    Mashick [~Mexico, southern California, New Mexico, Arizona] is the largest polity in Lower Mendeva, the descendant of the Mashick [Aztec] Empire with heavy Morrack influence. Its name derives via Arabic from the Nawat [Nahuatl] meshi'ca' "Mashick people".

The Arcabil [~the Caribbean] are a collection of islands in the Atlantic, east of Mashick. They have a complicated history of influences from various Vetomundine powers. The name probably comes via the Arabic cognate of "archipelago".

    Cocain [~Cuba] is the largest of the islands of the Arcabil. Its name comes from the European folk tradition of a "land of plenty" (compare pays de Cocaigne, paΟ≠s de Cucanna, paese della Cuccagna).

Cappatia [South America]

European languages take the name Cappatia of this continent from Latin CappΡ¹tia, which comes via Arabic from Run'allo [Quechua] cappacha "this world".

    Tavancy [~Colombia, Ecuador, Peru, Boliva, northern Chile] is major polity on the western coast, descended from the Tavancine [Inca] Empire. Its name comes from the Latin imperium tavantΡΪnum, which was borrowed via Arabic as a partial calque from the Run'allo tawantin-suyu "the quartet of provinces".
    Staddomains, rather small polities, dot the eastern coast. These grew out of individual trading posts and have many different influences. Examples abound from Dutch influence in Neuwillem, Parnaven and WaronΟΗu to Borlish influence in MonΟßating.
    Dowahine [~southern Patagonia, Tierra del Fuego] is a minor polity at the southern tip of Cappatia. Its name comes from the Arabic word for "smoke", after the smoke-towers sighted there by Captain al-Casmi in 1513.

137.1: The Henchmen of Zenda

excerpt in translation from the [real-life] book The Henchmen of Zenda by KJ Charles, a 2018 pastiche of the 1894 romp The Prisoner of Zenda.

L'yarn parmane com un madarc obstroint tras semman perpetu e monoton, e soulor i fey exeunt pre un cocoro.
Winter lingered like an unwanted policeman for endless monotonous weeks, and then departed overnight.

Nos se sveilau an jorn affaΟßaΟΑ de lag verdeyant parsemnaΟΑ d'ec borjon de flour, cant orruΟΑe l'eiΟΑel enter fos sy quidel yettant a fragnent a vivaΟßtaΟΑ a coup.
We woke one day to green meadows dotted with the beginnings of flowers, as the entire country seemed to throw off its blankets and burst into life at once.

Aucel enΟßaurn a cantar, agnel apparisceurn des zefr, y paΟß dy Castel a Zenda fo smuΟ·ΟΑ, eΟΑ y reu de my viΟΑ denoy trent veg convoluΟΑessem.
Birds sang, lambs appeared from nowhere, the peace of the Castle of Zenda was shattered, and my existence became thirty times as complicated.

Dell'eΟß dou vertaΟΑ derran, com de tan cainΟß, jo no poΟΑ for blasm jair a Rupert de Hentzau.
For the latter two facts, as for so many things, I can only blame Rupert of Hentzau.

Bon Deu, comoscon Hentzau illuminar sur fougl?
My God, how to convey Hentzau on the page?

No se poΟΑ l'acavar cos honest neΟΑan contrvenir all'Act cas EdiΟßon Obscen.
One could not do it honestly without contravening the Obscene Publications Act.

137.2: reunΟß dy blad "roll of the dice"

reunΟß dy blad /raunts di blad/ [ Äauns di blad]
- roll of the dice, coin flip, shot in the dark, gamble, a risky or uncertain attempt at doing something;
- chance, opportunity, a time when one can try something (especially when one didn't expect to be able to do so)

Etymology: idiom attested from the eighteenth century, literally "flip of the card" and taken from gambling jargon. The word reunΟß "flip, inversion, return" is a noun from reuvr "come back, turn over", which descends directly from Latin revolv≈ç "I roll over, I repeat". The word blad "playing card" is a borrowing from Saxon blad "leaf, page; playing card", cognate with blade in English.

M'orruΟΑ ig l'oc vellisqment sey un reunΟß dy blad.
/m…îΥàr…ΣΟΑ aj l…îk Υ¨v…¦.l…ΣskΥàm…¦nt si …Σn raunts di blad/
[m äΥà Ä…ΣβÄΩΟΑaj l…îg Υ¨v…¦.l…ΣskΥàm…¦nt si …Σn  Äauns di blad]
1s.obl=seem cmp df=s.px ploy be.sbj indf flip of-df card
I think that this gambit is a real shot in the dark.

137.3: tourf "furore"

tourf /turf/ [t äΥêf]
- furore, uproar, outcry, tumult, a loud or angry commotion;
- protest, riot, demonstration, gathering of people in public to demand something

Etymology: early modern borrowing from Welsh twrf "disturbance; crowd, mob", itself originally a borrowing from Latin turba "disorder; throng". The word gained currency during the Democratic Wars of the mid-eighteenth century, form which it obtained its political undertones.

Jo fo sveilaΟΑ je aubon posc par y tourf.
/ £o fo sviΥàlaΈΗ  £e oΥàb…în p…îx par i turf/
[ ùo fo zv…ΣΥàlah  ùe  äΥàb…în p…îx paβÄΩ Äi t äΥêf]
1s be.pst wake-p.pst day.of morning after by df furore
I was woken in the morning by the riot.

137.4: borjonnar "impend"

borjonnar /Υ¨b…îr. £…îΥànar/ [Υ¨b…îΥê. ù äΥàn…êΥê]
- impend, forthcome, loom, be imminent, show signs of, to be about to start;
- bring forward, prepone, expedite, accelerate, to make something happen sooner

Etymology: Middle Borlish verb from borjon "bud; beginning, first sign", cognate with Old French burjon "bud, sprout". This is either from Frankish burio "offshoot, descendant", or from Latin burri≈ç "down, fluff" (or both). Proscribed in the latter senses by stickler, although attestations are widespread from the 19th century.

Skig aular ag quarf ΟΑa cay ploy borjonnant.
/skai oΥàlar …¦j kwarf ΟΑa ke pl…îi Υ¨b…îr. £…îΥànant/
[skai  äΥàl…êΥêβÄΩ Ä…¦k kw…êΥêf ΟΑa ke pl…îi Υ¨b…îΥê. ù äΥànant]
refrain walk-inf to.df station if fall rain impend-p.prs
Don't walk to the station if it looks like rain.

##

137.5: The Dangers of DHMO

An illustration of the language Merch (the most widely-spoken language in the polity of Markland) through close analysis of the following example sentence.

Al as drink the leigh of aquifex ar sicker to dy.
/al …ôz d…Ψ…Σ≈΄k ΟΑ…ô leΥê É …ôβÄΩΥ¨va.kw…ΣΥàf…¦ks aΥê Υàs…Σk…ô t…ô d…ôi/

Translated to IRL English with Boralverse jargon:

Everyone who drinks aquifex's lye will surely die.

Something which seems minor but which actually affects the aesthetic of Merch greatly is the lack of the "three-letter rule". In IRL English, almost all function words (nouns, adjectives, verbs) are at least three letters long. This rule is why we have such pairs as "inn - in" and "bee - be", as well as the redundant double letters in words like "ebb" and "egg". By contrast, Merch never did anything analogous, as can be seen here in the words al, ar and dy (cf "all", "are" and "die").

Merch also has somewhat less French influence than IRL English (while Kentish has somewhat *more*), due to the incomplete replacement of the English nobility there during the period of the Norman-ruled First Drengot Empire. Evidence of this in the above sentence is in the word sicker "certain" < Old English sicor, which lost out to French-derived "sure" in IRL English.

Grammatical differences on display here are: (1) the use of "as" in relative clauses, e.g. "the man as I love", "the man as loves me"; (2) more colloquial use of "be to VERB" with (non-volitional) future meaning, e.g. "the boats ar to sink!".

Finally, the scientific jargon in this alternate history is of course entirely different. The word leigh < OE lΡ™ag is cognate with IRL "lye; potassium/sodium hydroxide", and has been generalised to refer to any hydroxide substance. The word aquifex is a Scholastic Latin coinage (meaning "water-maker") for "hydrogen".

Thus we could informally convey the Merch sentence in IRL English as:
> Drinking hydrogen hydroxide has a 100% mortality rate.

Which of course it doesβÄîeventually.

##

138.1: Avosche

notes in translation on the region of Avosche (Paris French /aΥàv…îΥê É/, Willem Dutch /Υàa.vusx/) in Willemy [Lotharingia].

Y region d'Avosc dey nom a sy linaΟß regnant primer, oΟß sey meΟΑes sy grad prenent de sy fondour Cuonrat Aihuosc (inquittaΟΑ ne Latin com Corradus Aevoscus).
The region Avosche is named for its first ruling house, which in turn takes its title from its founder Cuonrat Aihuosc (seen in Latin as Corradus Aevoscus).

Y nom a un origin obscur; se poΟΑ appondr ig y sodal primer se dey ag German Classic *ehu-* "coval", cant y dovem se dey parcanΟß ag fin *-iscas* "-esc" (pu sy voyal apport vandy).
The name is of obscure origin; the first element presumably reflects Classical German *ehu-* "horse", while the second may be from suffix *-iscas* "-ish" (though the vowel is problematic).

ConfereΟΑ y surnom Anglesc Davush, oc stant congeniΟΑ des un mancel nothe de LinaΟß Avosc.
Compare the English surname Davush, which derives from an illegitimate branch of House Avosche.

Y ComtaΟΑ d'Avosc smarc pog asc apres dy dehiscenΟß dy Reyaum dy Frankesc e dy creaΟßon cohernt de Willemy.
The County of Avosche emerges shortly after the split of the Kingdom of the Franks and the concomitant creation of Willemy.

Y linaΟß fondant d'Avosc fo surrun apres par mejan de mariΟΑaΟß eut doutr par y LinaΟß Nassou collindant (oc sell'orin ayent ne Francony).
The founding house of Avosche was soon after superseded by marriage by the neighbouring House Nassow (which originated in Francony).

soupleyar /Υ¨su.pliΥàjar/ [Υ¨su.pl…ΣΥà ù…êΥê]

Etymology: via Old Borlish sopleir "fill up, furnish, supply, provide" (later conflated with sople»ùar "require, take, consider necessary") from synonymous supple≈ç in Latin. Used for gifts originally in the context of granting land and titles to people.

Nos som plait de l'ambit agathiner vos soupleyar.

[n…îz s…îm plet de l…êmΥàb…Σt Υ¨a.g…ê.ΈΗ…ΣΥàn…¦Υê v…îz Υ¨su.pl…îΥà ù…êΥê]

138.3: pre un cocoro "unexpectedly"

pre un cocoro /pre …Σn Υ¨ko.koΥàro/ [p Äe …Σ≈΄ Υ¨ko.k äΥà Äo]
- unexpectedly, without warning, suddenly, abruptly, unannounced, occurring in a way that could not have been predicted;
- all at once, overnight, instantly, in one fell swoop, as a single action with no intervening time in which to interrupt

Etymology: idiom attested from the 16th century, literally "before a cockcrow". The noun cocoro "cockcrow", here used figuratively to denote "first light, daybreak, dawn", is assuredly onomatopoeic in origin (as, ultimately, one assumes coq "cock, rooster" was).

Il se courn mariΟΑaΟΑ pre un cocoro.
/…Σl se Υàku.rnΧ© Υ¨ma.riΥàΟΑaΈΗ pre …Σn Υ¨ko.koΥàro/
[…Σl s…ΣΥàk äΥên Υ¨ma. Ä…ΣΥàΟΑah p Äe …Σ≈΄Υ¨ko.k äΥà Äo]
3p rfl get.pst-3p marry-p.pst before indf cockcrow
They got married unexpectedly.

138.4: suicr "soot"

suicr /Υàsai.krΧ©/ [Υàsai.k…ê]
- soot, lampblack, particles of carbon and tar produced by incomplete combustion;
- blot, blemish, flaw, a mark of imperfection in or on a surface;
- rust, smut, any of various fungi which cause plant diseases

Etymology: via Old Borlish sui, suicle "soot" from synonymous s≈Ϊdia, s≈Ϊdicula in Insular Latin (compare French suie, Welsh huΟΑygl, both meaning "soot"). In the more general uses the word is attested from the fifteenth century; the botanical senses date in writing to the seventeenth.

Y mur ne DamvaΟΑ VeΟΑerrem son pegntaΟΑ con suicr.
/i m…Σr ne damΥàvaΈΗ veΥàΟΑ…¦. Ä…¦m s…în pijnΥàtaΈΗ k…în Υàsai.krΧ©/
[i m…ΣΥê ne d…ê…±Υàvah v…ΣΥàΟΑ…¦. Ä…êm s…în p…ΣjnΥàtah k…în Υàsai.k…ê]
df wall in Damvath elder be.3p paint-p.pst with soot
The walls of Old Damvath are coated with soot.

##

138.5: Steeplepost Tapestry

The Steeplepost Tapestry (Vascon: Lo TapΟ§s Campanaral) was a screen of several myriad black or white tiles built in Ambrosia's capital city of Aiga d'Angoixa [~Quebec City]. Completed in 1820 and erected to overlook one of the city's central squares, it was inspired by the (fairly new) 'latticework' art movement.

One ambitious goal for the Tapestry was to be able to portray journalist's sketches of events from across the country with great speed, using the flag-lamp steeplemesh infrastructure that had been built up over the last few decades. Although this was achieved several times, the practical limitations of transmitting so much information by steeplepost prevented it from becoming routine. Instead, the majority of the tapestry's time was spent displaying short pieces of news or government messages in text and simple symbols.

The use of the word 'diced' to mean 'blocky, pixellated' dates back to a calque of Provincial icon dau 'die image'. This phrasing was coined in 1821 in disparaging reference to the Tapestry.

Just a few years into its operation, a series of hoax messages which spread rapidly via the Ambrosian steeplemesh made their way onto the Tapestry. These so-called 'Lies of 1824' included a report of the governor's death and culminated after several days with an alleged declaration of war from New Provence.

In 2020, a replica of the Tapestry was unveiled in Markland to mark the bicentenary of the original's completion.

##

139.1: Just a Man

translation into Borlish of the song Just a Man from the musical Epic, written by Jorge Rivera-Herrans.

Borlish:

    La jo te vey l'ec ogl a tey
    Jo memor l'oΟß heir a mey
    Tu n'a for sell'asc de cant eu jo ne var

    Mell'ec gest ou vaun ΟΑocaΟß? Toll'enmig oustaΟΑ?
    M'offirraun noghaΟß ΟΠternaΟΑ?
    QuΟ·sc ec ogl neΟΑan y sigt osar
    Com poΟΑ jo t'oundrar?

    Jo no's for hom gimmolent a repaΟΑr
    Jug lontan posc toll'annaΟΑ deull'oΟß meyon amaΟΑ
    Jo no's for hom y mort hasmant a strem
    Jo say tout volir por lou reveir, mell'heir e femn
    Jo no's for hom

    Ma cant es un comet denuΟΑ un assuvar?
    Cant es un bojay denuΟΑ un blas?
    Cant es un hom denuΟΑ un monstr?

    Cant es un ondol denuΟΑ un rasamar?
    Cant es y raΟßon denuΟΑ y blasm?
    Cant es un hom denuΟΑ un monstr?

    Absou mey (3v)
    Jo no's for hom

Literal translation:

    There I see these eye of yours
    I remember that son of mine
    You're only his age from when I went to war

    Do my acts deserve to plague me? All the slain enemies?
    Will they hit back at me with unending pain?
    Close your eyes so as not to dare see
    How can I wound you?

    I'm just a person striving for homecoming
    Still far after all the years from what I love
    I'm just a person resisting death utterly
    I know I'd do anything to see them again, my son and wife
    I'm just a person

    But when has a comet become a meteor?
    When has a candle become a blaze?
    When has a person become a monster?

    When has a ripple become a tidal wave?
    When has the reason become the blame?
    When has a person become a monster?

    Absolve me (x3)
    I'm just a person

139.2: truc "shove"

truc /tr…Σk/ [t Ä…Σk]
- shove, thump, jab, jostle, tackle, thrust, a forceful push without gentleness;
- try, go, stab, an (especially unpractised or clumsy) attempt at doing something

Etymology: Old Borlish deverbal from truccar "shove, jostle" (now archaic, superseded by truc fair), which reflects a Vulgar Latin extension tr≈Ϊdic≈ç of Classical tr≈Ϊd≈ç "I shove, thrust". In the latter senses it is attested from the nineteenth century.

Un truc par ados me fey gringlar a cainΟß.
/…Σn tr…Σk par aΥàd…îz me fi gr…ΣnΥàglar a kents/
[…Σn t Ä…Σk Υ¨p…êΥêβÄΩ Ä…êΥàd…îz me fi g Ä…Σ≈΄Υàgl…êΥêβÄΩ Äa kens]
indf shove by behind 1s.obl do.pst topple-inf to event
A shove from behind knocked me over.

139.3: zovay cavir "take a taxi"

zovay cavir /zoΥàve kaΥàv…Σr/ [z äΥàve k…êΥàv…ΣΥê]
- take a taxi, go via cab, to travel somewhere as a passenger in a taxicab;
- take the easy way out, follow the path of least resistance, to take a course of action which requires minimal personal effort

Etymology: late ninetenth century, literally "to get (a) taxi". The noun zovay "taxi" is a clipping of earlier carrot zovay "taxi carriage", often mistakenly capitalised to carrot Zovay by analogy with other types of carriage named after places.

The word was borrowed via French from Furlain slang term zovaie "coach for hire with a taximeter", literally "convenience" (one did not have to pay up front, unlike earlier hired coaches). This is a derivative of verb zovΟ† "be useful, be convenient", from Latin iuv≈ç "I help; I please".

In the metaphorical sense the phrase is seen only from the 1970s.

Nos coum zovay eΟΑ alandaum tost.
/n…îz kum zoΥàve …¦ΈΗ Υ¨a.lanΥàdom t…îst/
[n…îz kum z äΥàve …¦βÄΩΥ¨ΟΑa.l…ênΥàdom t…îst]
1p get.pst-1p taxi and turn.up-pst-1p early
We took a taxi and arrived early.

##

139.4: n'eΟß cahut "separately"

n'eΟß cahut /n…¦ts kaΥàh…Σt/ [Υ¨n…¦s.k…êΥàh…Σt]
- apart, siloed, segregated, separated from each other into disjoint areas;
- separately, independently, without any communication or collaboration;
- estranged, alienated, no longer speaking to one another (especially after a falling out)

Etymology: idiom attested from the eighteenth century, literally meaning "in cabins". The noun cahut "cabin, dorm, sleeping quarters" is a Middle Borlish borrowing, originally in a naval context, from synonyous caihute in Middle Dutch. This is possibly a portmanteau of Old French cabane and hutte, both meaning "hut, shack".

The Borlish idiom appears roughly contemporaneously with cognate idioms in other languages, such as Merch's in cahutes "independently".

Jug sein y domn egrtumnesc n'eΟß cahut.
/ £ai sin i Υàd…îmnΧ© Υ¨ijrΧ©.t…ΣmΥàn…¦x n…¦ts kaΥàh…Σt/
[ ùai sin i Υàd…î.m…ên Υ¨…Σj…ê.t…ΣmΥàn…¦Οß Υ¨n…¦s.k…êΥàh…Σt]
ever be.sbj-3p df woman sick in=p.dt cabin
Keep the sick women quarantined.

##

139.5: Monarchs of Borland

collected notes on various monarchs of Borland.

Victor Rossetus 'the Red-haired' (fl. c6) was the first Prase of the Boral Diocese. He rose to prominence after the fall of the First Roman Empire and unified the urbanised east of the land while the west and south were settled by Anglians to form Angland and Southbar, respectively. It was under him that so-called 'Roman Borland' first began to succesfully repel the new settlers. By the late sixth century the Diocese of Boral had become a monarchy (described in retrospect as the First Kingdom of Borland), whose ruling dynasty claimed descent from Rossetus.

Jothey, King of Borland (fl. 898), known in Borlish as JoΟΑeg / £oΥàΟΑij/, was the last ruler of the First Kingdom of Borland before it fell to the Danes in 898. Married to Brenna of Barrow, cousin of King Roderick of Dunclothe, they fled to neighbouring Southbar (and once Southbar also fell to Dane rule, to Kent).

In the first decade of the fifteenth century, Queen Natalia I and her husband King Joseph I ruled jointly. Their family had been and would continue to be beset by misfortune; all three of their sons predeceased them, as did their few male grandchildren (either in infancy or by misadventure). The succession crisis prompted by their deaths in 1411 and 1416 spelt the end of their dynasty.

From the ashes rose the Saulestran lineage of kings, whose banner comprised a white bar cutting a twilight-blue field. This dynasty would go on to last for over four hundred years until the dissolution of the Borlish monarchy itself.

Joseph III (d. 1534) was king of Borland, remembered for ruling the kingdom through the ravages of the Second Great Dying. Joseph never succeeded in producing a natural-born heir, despite several recognised yet illegitimate children by his various mistresses. Thus, after his death in 1534, he was succeeded to the throne by Brandon II, his nephew.

Natalia II (Borlish Natagl, abd. 1894) was the last queen of Borland before the dissolution of the monarchy. She abdicated on Revillon of 1894 during the political upheaval leading to the country joining the Drengot Collusion (several Borlish cities, although not the capital, had already joined as part of the New Urban Mesh).

##

140.1: Redstone Chair

notes in translation on the subject of the Redstone Chair.

Y CaΟΑer ne PieΟΑr Roug sta costroit ny siecr deg jagerrem atorn d'un los ne greut; y nom dy carpenter es repars ne vogt dell'hour.
The Redstone Chair was fashioned in the late tenth century around a block of sandstone; the name of the carpenter is forgotten.

L'oc los intermane (cos noncupaΟΑ) y los yon dessur y NuΟßal a Elved entr un princes Markesc eΟΑ y sovran de NorΟΑhumbr Dannesc er solemneyaΟΑ, rix entr y dou polity coyent tras un siecr pres enter.
This block was (allegedly) the block upon which the Elved Marriage between a princess of Markland and the ruler of Dane Northumber was solemnised, ending hostilities between the two polities for almost a century.

Bempart monarch n'Albion sousevent, e cos particuler de Marclont e de NorΟΑhumbr, ern coronnaΟΑ sur y CaΟΑer ne PieΟΑr Roug.
Many subsequent monarchs of Albion, and especially of Markland and Northumber, were crowned on the Redstone Chair.

Durant y Scon Tetrarchy ny siecr onΟß e doΟß, gran part all'Emperour DrengoΟßan asseyen sur y CaΟΑer, e cossy sceint l'er emportaΟΑ a Londr.
During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, most of the Drengot Emperors sat upon the Chair, and as such it was relocated to London.

140.2: nothe "forged"

nothe /noΥàΈΗe/ [n äΥàΈΗe]
- bastard, illegitimate, born to unmarried parents (when this means one is not able to inherit something);
- counterfeit, forged, claiming falsely to be something genuine;
- spurious, specious, fallacious, superficially plausible but not in fact logically deducible;
- (botany, zoology) false, denoting various plants and animals which resemble but which are not other species

Etymology: Middle Borlish borrowing from Scholastic Latin notheus "illegitimate, bastard", a pleonastic extension of synonymous nothus in Classical Latin. Originally restricted to legal contexts (where it replaced terms like bort and cif which were considered vulgar), it is now in general use. The term coexists as a higher-register term with French-derived bastart, which is somewhat crass.

Nos yemoim toΟΑ pront sciΟΑ y Jorala sta nothe.
/n…îz jeΥàm…îim t…îΈΗ pr…înt x…ΣΈΗ i Υ¨ £o.raΥàla sta noΥàΈΗe/
[n…îz j…ΣΥàm…îim t äΥàp Ä…înt Οß…ΣβÄΩΟΑi Υ¨ ùo. Ä…êΥàla sta n äΥàΈΗe]
1p get-pst-1p all immediate known df Jorala be.ipf forged
We immediately realised the Jorala was forged.

140.3: ΟΑocaΟß "haunting"

ΟΑocaΟß /ΟΑoΥàkats/ [ΟΑ äΥàkas]
- bedevilment, torment, vexation, the affliction of (especially mental) distress;
- trauma, haunting, hell, the aftereffects of terrible experiences

Etymology: noun derivation from the verb ΟΑocar "harrow, till; afflict, bedevil, torment, haunt" with the pejorative suffix -aΟß (from Latin -Ρ¹ceus "shaped like"). The verb descends from Vulgar Latin adocc≈ç "I till, harrow", an extension of the synonymous occ≈ç in Classical Latin. The verb gains its emotional senses in the sixteenth century.

Y ΟΑocaΟß lour coy de dormir ec noit.

140.4: hamac "bunk"

hamac /haΥàmak/ [h…êΥàmak]
- bunk, berth, cot, one of a series of beds installed in rows or tiers (especially in a nautical context);
- hammock, a hanging bed made of cloth or netting;
- swing, a seat hanging from two cords or cables and free to move back and forth;
- (mechanics) oscillation, harmonic motion, the behaviour of an object undergoing a returning force proportional to its distance from equilibrium

Etymology: originally in the second sense, borrowed via Morrove Arabic Ό΅ΌÖΌÉ ¬Ζ hamaca and French hamac from Tainow [Taino] hamaca "hammock". Extended in two directions, to various cramped sleeping arrangements aboard ships as well as to other swinging objects. The word's use in physics is attested from the nineteenth century.

Jo fey rongelon sur my hamac fin a quelt.
/ £o fi Υ¨r…în.geΥàl…în s…Σr mi haΥàmak f…Σn a kw…¦lt/
[ ùo fi Υ¨ Ä…î≈΄.g…ΣΥàl…în s…ΣΥê mi h…êΥàmak Υ¨fi.n…ê Υàkw…¦lt]
1s do.pst nap on 1s.gn bunk end at dinner
I dozed in my bunk until dinner.

140.5: Chemical Etymology (cont. 2)

excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary of Alchemy, a reference quire compiled by Rotcham University Primers [1].

corporal /ΥàkoΥê.p…ô.…Ψ…ôl/ [elements] a pure substance comprising atoms with a single number of mora [protons]; a single atom of such a substance. 1766 < earlier adjective "material, concrete, physical" < Scholastic Latin corporΡ¹lis < corpus "body, substance".

corporal muster /ΥàkoΥê.p…ô.…Ψ…ôl Υàm ä.st…ô/ [periodic table] the arrangement of corporals in rows by moronic count [atomic number] and in columns by alchemic properties. 1879, formed by compounding.

murine /Υàmj…ôu.…Ψ…ôin/ [chlorine] the corporal whose atoms have 17 mora, found as a green air which is highly poisonous. 1805 < Scholastic Latin murΡΪnus "murine; (adj.) briny" < muria "brine, salt solution" < Greek αΦ¹ΈΜΈΦαΩΓœ¹αΩêΧ¹œ² βÄΔ halm≈ΪrΟ≠s "saltiness". Compare dialectal mure "brine" < Drengot muire "brine", from the same Latin. The ending conforms to the corporals which share its column, such as tagline [fluorine] and janthine [iodine].

surfeited /ΥàsoΥêf…Σ.t…Σd/ [negatively charged] of an atom, endowed with an excess of plenty [electric charge]. 1891 < verb surfeit "oversupply" < Drengot French surfeire "exceed" < Latin super "over" and faci≈ç "I make".

tagline /Υàta.gl…ôin/ [fluorine] the corporal whose atoms have 9 mora, found as a wan yellow-green air which is highly poisonous. 1808 < Scholastic Latin taglΡΪnus, originally applied to minerals containing surfeited tagline [Fβ¹Μ] < Napolitan taglino (1643) "fondant [flux], substance for lowering the melting point of metals" < Arabic ΊΣΌ³ΌäΌäΌÜ βÄΔ talyΡΪn "softening; fondant".

---

[1] a publishing house based in the city of Rotcham [Rochdale, Greater Manchester] and affiliated with the local university.
