141.1: Vampire Film Review

excerpt in translation from Jacint Brunat's review of Zachariah, a horror film about a vampire released in 1950.

Ne spien de poΟΑir y lasc fornir con certan dell'aspect costumneressem de cont a vampier, Kiawaman soupley abiltaΟΑ thaumesc a Zachariah e depegnt ig nossy heros le vencn par mejan d'un spig boscer ognt (noc, com ny livr e lonc saivenΟß pouvlancer, par ΟΑurnΟß).
In an attempt to incorporate some of the more traditional aspects of vampire folklore, Kiawaman grants Zachariah mystic powers and has the heroes defeat him by means of a sanctified wooden stake (not, as in the book and in popular wisdom, by burning).

L'ec novel abiltaΟΑ, soll'auteΟß d'un forgour ottem, sceusn un conjogntur mis a plaΟß entr Zachariah e Vania ne sona de praxitel an art pareyβÄîaglon, nos receveu y jout cojan d'un domn jou follant entr y cortisment de dou segr.
These new powers, in the hands of a better writer, could have set up a dynamic between Zachariah and Vania as artists of the same craftβÄîinstead, we are given the tired trope of a woman torn between the affections of two men.

Offignt naΟΑusc es ig l'auditoir crein for apen no scey bogr un decijon a prendr; Vania manc attragnt vars y protagonist necun.
It doesn't help that the audience barely believes there is any choice to make; Vania has no chemistry with either lead.

141.2: ondolar "ripple"

ondolar /Υ¨…în.doΥàlar/ [Υ¨…în.d äΥàl…êΥê]
- ripple, undulate, billow, roil, eddy, to move like the disturbed surface of a body of water;

141.3: runastr "sorcerer"

runastr /riΥànastrΧ©/ [ Ä…ΣΥàna.st…ê]
- sorcerer, magician, wizard, mage, warlock, a person who practices magic;
- performer, trickster, illusionist, escapologist, stage magician, a person who entertains an audiences with the impression of performing magic;
- whiz, ace, genius, someone who is very skilled;
- enigma, mystery, someone who astounds or who leaves one baffled

Etymology: Middle Borlish, derivative of run "magic spell, talisman" with the pejorative suffix -astr < Latin -aster "resembling, quasi-" (compare contemporaneous but now obsolete runart "sorcerer"). Originally restricted to those doing evil or harm with magic, the word had become a generic word for a magic user by the late eighteenth century. In the last senses it is seen in slang from the early twentieth century.

Ja ou vos ignoraΟΑ comoscon y runastr l'acavau?
/ £a u v…îz Υ¨ai.noΥàraΈΗ Υ¨ko.moΥàx…în i riΥànastrΧ© Υ¨la.kaΥàvo/

yet q 2p ignore-2p how df wizard 3s.obl=manage-pst
Do you still not know how the magician did it?

##

141.5: Provence

collected notes on the polity of Provence.

Provence (Doccian ProenΟßa /p ÄuΥà…¦.s…î/) is a polity in the south of Gaul [France] on the Middlesea [Mediterranean] coast. It first became independent in 1499, and its capital city is Marsella [Marseille].

Toponymy
The name Provence descends directly from the Latin name Provincia Romana, so called as it was the first Roman province to be established on the Gaulish side of the Alps. The name of the polity's predominant language, Doccian, derives from the division of the French languages into the lengas d'oil (Northern, including Roun [Rouen] and Paris French) and the lengas d'oc (Southern, including Vascon and Doccian) depending on their word for "yes".

History
Medieval Provence was a vassal of various greater powers for almost a millennium. It was incorporated into the expanding Francian Empire in the sixth century and remained part of its western fragment after the empire was divided in two in the ninth. By the early eleventh century it had become mostly autonomous, but it was conquered once more as the last piece of the First Drengot Empire.

After the 1170 Emergency at Dijon, the Kingdom of Burgundy left Drengot control, taking the country of Provence with it. Provence was elevated to a duchy in the thirteenth century under the house of Savoy, and as the direct link between the capital at Geneva and all of Middlesea sea-trade, it became known as the panier de Borgogne (the 'Burgund breadbasket'). Provence was conquered by Vascony during the Vascon Ascendancy of the early fifteenth century, but was returned to Burgundy during the reign of Ambrose III in 1494.

Along with this political turmoil, the late fifteenth century saw extensive piracy in the Middlesea; in Marsella the raiding verged on occupation, and many of the wealthy fled to safer pastures inland. Aid requests presented to Rudolf VIII went largely unanswered, and things came to a head on the 21st of June 1497 when Daniel Menton (Lord AmigΟ≤t) led the local aristocracy in declaring independence from Geneva (to this day the 21st of June is a national holiday in Provence). The War of Provincial Independence lasted two years until Burgundy's defeat at the Battle of MargΟ®s.

Daniel Menton led the new republic for the next eighteen years, manoeuvring against a hungry Barcelon and their piratical allies who de facto ruled Marsella (it is generally understood that Lord AmigΟ≤t established Marsella as the capital of Provence in large part to closely monitor Jondumal's fleets). His successor and cousin, Antoin, ruled for only three years despite the guidance of his mentor and Daniel's friend GuillΟ®n daus Sanz; the coming of the Second Great Dying ravaged the AmigΟ≤t dynasty and the Provincial people both.

The following decades saw independent Provence take its place as a preeminent mercantile republic. By the end of the sixteenth century their trade network reached across the Atlantic to port towns in Mendeva [North America] like the newly-established Santrafew. These towns would eventually coalesce into the territory of New Provence.

By the end of the nineteenth century, the power of New Provence had eclipsed that of its Vetomundine progenitor. As the Drengot Collusion formed to the north, Provence became in many respects a European outpost of New Provence, in a reversal of their respective statures centuries earlier. The region stood with Vascony as a bulwark against Drengot hegemony until the Furore of 1961 and the collapse of the Drengot Collusion.

References
The Latin Republics: Jewels under Tyrian Standards by Jacob Trelaune, published by the Yievle Brethin Primers, 1902

##

142.1: Man-Made Monsters

excerpt in translation from Monstres per Mans "Man-Made Monsters", a work of fiction published serially from 1888 to 1903 by Carmel fi Domenc, author of parachthon [sci-fi/fantasy] tales and co-owner (with her brother Jered) of the Santrafew [~Boston] periodical Arcans Exumaz "Revealed Mysteries".

Var alandau a Tolon je Maucr, a plaΟß entarn d'un meis Avril caint freit cos mesteyant.
War came to Tolone on Wednesday, some way into a disappointingly chilly April.

Tras l'aubon sceye un foyaΟß de cry e de soldart par y rout angost demay, tandic meΟΑes haubrjour evolgan y novel ig l'ec intermanen veram y tourm ProvenΟßal bount ag tarmn Jennesc.
The morning was a confusion of shouts and soldiers through the too-narrow streets, even as criers spread the news that these were Provence's own men bound for the Jean frontier.

FlagitaΟßon a tegmn eΟΑ a mansc hellinabr apen dy citaΟΑ mal gazovaΟΑ ern colluΟ·ΟΑ par y Οßamoc de promeΟß nobr de stimaΟß reyal alcant aposc.
Demands for shelter and food which the raid-wracked city could barely afford were softened with lofty promises of royal favour to come.

Receut sta lon voΟß y saivenΟß ig toΟΑ refus volen forΟß recevir com offir.
There was no need to voice the understanding that refusal would be answered with force.

Y centuir costoja l'honour eΟΑ y scurtaΟΑ dy domain reyal, noc de sy souject leyal ma obstroint.
The army was concerned with the honour and protection of the king's domain, not of its loyal yet inconvenient subjects.

142.2: beuscr "monster"

beuscr /bauxrΧ©/ [Υàbau.x…ê]
- monster, beast, an animal considered dangerous and violent;
- brute, lout, hooligan, thug, a person considered to behave like a mindless and violent beast;
- bro, dude, guy, an ironic term of approbation for a male friend (especially one who has just been particularly cool);
- (colloq, adv.) very, extremely, particularly, to a surprisingly great extent.

Etymology: somehwat muddled. The -scr ending likely descends from Latin diminutive suffix -usculus, here used in either a belittling or euphemistic sense. The rest is thought to reflect Latin bΡ™lua "wild animal, beast", although the development of the /l/ is irregular.

In ironic usage it is attested from the early twentieth century; as a generic intensifier somewhat later. The derived comparative beuscerrem "to an even greater extent" is seen only very occasionally as a one-off neologism.

PieΟß beuscr son tijon svingant sur y lanΟßbal.
/pj…¦ts bauxrΧ© s…în tiΥà £…în sv…ΣnΥàgant s…Σr i Υ¨lantsΥàbal/
[pj…¦s Υàbau.x…ê s…în t…ΣΥà ù…în zv…Σ≈΄Υàgant s…ΣΥêβÄΩ Äi Υ¨lansΥàbal]
some brute be.3p torch wield-p.prs on df lineball
Some louts are up in arms about the lineball.

142.3: ec ogl quΟ·r "close one's eyes"

ec ogl quΟ·r /…¦k …îil kwaiΥà…Σr/ […ΣΥàg…îil kw…êΥà ù…ΣΥê]
- close one's eyes, to cover one's eyes with one's eyelids so that one cannot see;
- ignore, turn a blind eye, to intentionally fail to recognise that something (usually bad) is happening

Etymology: fixed idiom literally meaning "to extinguish eyes". Both this and its opposite ec ogl accendr "open one's eyes, lit. light eyes" first appear in the early Middle Borlish period, presumably via the notion of making things light and dark. In metaphorical use the phrase is seen from the fifteenth century.

The noun ogl "eye" descends unproblematically from Latin oculus "eye"; the verb quΟ·r "extinguish, darken; turn off, switch off" comes from Latin quiΡ™sc≈ç "quieten, stop" after analogy to other inchoative verbs like finir < fΡΪni≈ç~fΡΪniΡ™sc≈ç "to finish".

Jo quΟ·sceu ec ogl eΟΑ enΟßau a conteyar.
/ £o Υ¨kwai.iΥàxau …¦k …îil …¦ΈΗ …¦nΥàtso a Υ¨k…în.tiΥàjar/
[ ùo Υ¨kwajΥàxau …ΣΥàg…îil eβÄΩΟΑ…ΣnΥàtso a Υ¨k…întΥàj…êΥê]
1s extinguish-pst p.px eye and start-pst at count-inf
I closed my eyes and started counting.

142.4: oundrar "wound"

oundrar /unΥàdrar/ [ änΥàd Ä…êΥê]
- wound, injure, hurt, harm, to cause an injury or similar harm to;
- damage, break, impair, to cause not to function correctly;
- offend, spite, insult, to hurt the feelings or honour of

Etymology: from Old Borlish (v)ounrar, oundar "wound, injure", a conflation of the descendant of Latin vulner≈ç "I wound" and the borrowing of Old Norrish unda "to wound" (which are unrelated). The loss of the initial consonant would be unexpected if the Latin etymon was the only one; however, this was a regular feature of Old Norrish.

M'orruΟΑ un hom ay my bos oundraΟΑ.
/m…îΥàr…ΣΈΗ …Σn …îm e mi b…îz unΥàdraΈΗ/
[m äΥà Ä…ΣβÄΩΟΑ…ΣΥàn…îm e mi b…îz  änΥàd Äah]
1s.obj=seem indf perosn have.sbj 1s.gn gun wound-p.pst
I think someone's broken my gun.

##

142.5: Tremonnow

Tremonnow (Merch /t…Ψ…ôΥàm…î.n…ô/, Welsh Trevonwy /Υ¨t…Ψe.v…ôΥànui/) [Monmouth] is a town in the southeast of Wales, the administrative centre of the district of Ux [1].

Toponymy

The name Tremonnow derives from the Welsh trew "town" and the name of the river it lies on, the Monnow (compare Welsh Mynwy "the Monnow", which is of unclear origin).

History

The lands around Tremonnow were conquered from the medieval Welsh kingdom of Guent [Gwent] by Vigo the Magnificent during the formation of the kingdom of Greater Devon in the first half of the eighth century.

The Christian order called the Brethin was founded in Tremonnow at some point before 800 N under the patronage of Vigo's daughter Iseld. The order would become renowned for their efforts at historical documentation, and their archives in what eventually became the modern Tremonnow Gravatory preserve invaluable sources on early medieval Albion [Great Britain] (including a copy of the Chronicle of Carlile [Carlisle]).

From the mid-twentieth century, the Tremonow Open School, formerly known as the Brethin Mesh Institute, provided distance learning in Welsh and Wessern [2] across Albion and, latterly, also across British Mendeva.

People
Dewock Barclythe (1452-1539 N), Friar of Tremonnow and for several decades the prime Factor of Records at the Brethin House there.

---

[1] roughly corresponding to Monmouthshire, named for area's main river [the Usk], from an old Welsh word for "water".

[2] the English language variety spoken as a recognised minority language in Britain [Wales, Devon and Cornwall, Brittany, Cumbria, Strathclyde, Man and the Isles, parts of Ireland], mostly in its eastern regions bordering Markland and Kent.

##

143.1: Prehistory Curriculum

excerpt from the 1981 Report towards a Normal Teaching for Children at Mantleschool, a public report from the Ministry for Schools within Borland's contemporary anthracite administratration, which was intended to standardise the education of children up to the age of twelve.

Posc il ain schol lajarn compliΟΑ, val ig stuΟΑant broucn un recordaΟßon mozaΟΑ ag mendrem cas y defas des prell'aubon de scrittur jusc l'epoc dy Scon Tetrachy, con tan casc schol son hortaminaΟΑ a them recendessem admettr lonc y bustr d'envou vecin.
Upon completing mantleschool, pupils should have at least a surface-level understanding of the development from prehistory until the time of the Second Tetrarchy, although individual schools are encouraged to include more recent material as it relates to vicine matters.

MasctighaΟΑ es ig l'ec them sein abrangent dy costumn ag toun ben muncef local, aglon cainΟß diΟßr (battagl, descovart eΟΑ aval) ayent avenuΟΑ cos veΟßin.
This may include the traditions of the local village or quarter, or else notable events (battles, discoveries, etc) which occurred nearby.

EnombraΟΑ secuΟΑ, val ig stuΟΑant recievn y cΟΠros por ec splorar:
Taken in order, pupils should have the opportunity to learn about:

scamn de migraΟßon vars Istr Boral jusc e par y Kelt Albic
waves of migration into Borland up to and including the Albic Kelts

ΟΑar primal eΟΑ y hopr ne pieΟΑr effojaΟΑ ny tombr a Revel (comparaΟΑ con y plaΟßment Orcadesc a ScarreΟΑ ottem mantenuΟΑ)
early farming and the stoneware found in the Revel mounds (compared to the better-preserved Orkney site at Scarreth)

sojornaΟßon, conveyaΟßon e machinaΟßon, abrangent ag commarΟß obligaΟΑ por eramn, l'avent aval de far, eΟΑ y costroinΟß dy labyrinth a Ο΅adrosc
travel, transport and technology, including the trade necessary for bronze, the later introduction of iron, and the construction of the Ο΅adrosc labyrinth

y doujug entr tribu ne castr colliner nell'epoc pre Rom e lorry cultur cas autortaΟΑ, soumoin jaint vars art, ensignment e jovagl ja sceint
the intertribal system of hill forts in the pre-Classical period and the culture around authority, with reference to extant art, sculpture and jewellery

143.2: ΟΠternar "immortalise"

ΟΠternar /Υ¨e.t…¦rΥànar/ [e.t…ΣΥêΥàn…êΥê]
- perpetuate, continue, maintain, prolong, eternise, to make last indefinitely;
- immortalise, to cause to be renowned;
- memorialise, commemorate, mark, to ensure that someone or something is remembered

Made popular in the last senses after the Millstone War in the early twentieth century by its use in a FaΟΑig war poem.

143.3: rasamar "tsunami"

rasamar /Υ¨ra.zaΥàmar/ [Υ¨ Äa.z…êΥàm…êΥê]
- tsunami, tidal wave, a very large and destructive wave as caused by an earthquake or eruption;
- flood, onslaught, onrush, the sudden appearance of a large number or mass of things or people

Etymology: Middle Borlish, a univerbation of the earlier phrase ras a mar "onrush of sea". The now-obsolete word ras "rush, stampede, running" was borrowed either from Old English or Norrish. In metaphorical use the word rasamar is attested from as early as the fifteenth century.

There was a rush of customers as soon as the shop opened.

143.4: assuvar "meteor"

assuvar /Υ¨a.siΥàvar/ [Υ¨a.s…ΣΥàv…êΥê]
- meteor, shooting star, a piece of extraterrestrial rock falling through the Earth's atmosphere;
- meteoroid, meteorite, such a piece of rock before it enters the Earth's atmosphere or after it has landed on the ground;
- harbinger, omen, herald, something that foretells the coming of some person or event (especially something bad)

This is a borrowing from Arabic Ί¥ΌèΌ΅ΌèΊ® βÄΔ shuhub "shooting stars", plural of Ί¥ΌêΌ΅ΌéΊßΊ® βÄΔ shihΡ¹b "blaze, brand; shooting star". In the last senses it reflects a long history of comets and similar celestial appearances being used to foretell the future.

143.5: In the Solar System

    excerpt taken from 1936 work Tell Me Everything About the Solar System [1], part of the Tell Me, Telmy! series of childrenβÄôs educational books. These were originally written by Adamara Godwilham [2] and featured a fictionalised version of her father Bartelmy, βÄ€TelmyβÄù, acting as guide and entertainer. After her death she bequeathed the series to her publishers, Zizania Nexus, who have continued the series to the present day.

βÄΠfound the four biggest jovial moons right in the middle of the Revitalist period. This time during the sixteenth century was when scholars and artists across the Middlesea [Mediterranean] and the Persias looked back to the works and beliefs of Classical Greece and Rome.

As word spread of the first really new celestial bodies to be discovered for thousands of years, people came up with lots of names for these pharΡΪ Iovis βÄ€lighthouses of JoveβÄù. Some people wanted to bring glory to their patrons, lords or lands. Others reached to retellings of Classical legends which were in fashion.

The convention that has lasted in European languages, and which has been maintained since we have found many more small jovial moons in the last century, has been to name JupiterβÄôs gyrers [satellites] for his mortal descendants. We have done the same with the two moons of Mars, Dimus [Phobos] and Phobus [Deimos], which are named for his twin sons and attendants.

In myth, ΟÜacos was the grandfatherβÄΠ

βÄî

[1] originally published in Kentish as Tell Me Wrich by th'Ambit ot Son, pronounced /t…¦l m…ô v Ä…Σt É bi ΈΗamΥàb…Σt …ît s…în/.

[2] Adamara is a feminine form of Adamar, from Classical German roots audas βÄ€wealthβÄù and mΡ™ras βÄ€fameβÄù. The surname, pronounced /Υàgw…Σ.l…ôm/, is from placenames deriving from early Kentish godwille βÄ€fortune, blessingβÄù and hamme βÄ€enclosureβÄù.

144.1: The Kernovan Conjuring

notes in translation on the subject of the Kernovan Conjuring.

Y ConjuraΟßon Cornovan sta un collaboraΟßon entr y Reyaum a Dorogn Massem e Roger Drengot, Duc a Normandy por y pretenjon dy Duc sur y thron de Cantuer appoyar. 
The Kernovan Conjuring was a collaboration between the Kingdom of Greater Devon and Roger Drengot, Duke of Normandy to press the Duke's claim to the throne of Kent.

CossiraΟΑ es l'oc vellisqment com y caus y proismessem por ig y Preu Empir DrengoΟßan inceveus tras Gal eΟΑ Albion souΟΑer.
This plot is considered the proximate cause for the creation of the First Drengot Empire across Gaul [France] and southern Albion [Great Britain].

Y ConjuraΟßon sta accommis d'an 1029, ne souc dy mort de Rey Rodolf de Cantuer.
The Conjuring was carried out in 1029, following the death of King Rodulf of Kent.

Cantuer sou Rodolf regnant jaΟßol aye se monfeyaΟΑ, ant dominanΟß reconnuΟΑ sur y Vessax diminoit (con tan noc inkerellaΟΑ par Dorogn) e sur Essax ny norΟΑ.
Kent under the long-reigning Rodulf had prospered, with recognised supremacy over the rump Wessex (albeit not uncontested by Devon) and over Essex to the north.

Posc ogt dezein de manovranΟß politic, Rodolf parmane ag sougl de mort eΟΑ y succession no sta bogr certan.
After eight decades of political manoeuvring, Rodulf lingered on his deathbed and the succession was deeply uncertain.

144.2: cavir yert "boot up"

cavir yert /kaΥàv…Σr j…¦rt/ [k…êΥàv…ΣΥê  ù…¦Υêt]
- wake up, come to, stir, to regain consciousness;
- turn on, switch on, activate, start, boot up, to begin operating;
- (of a business) open, to start serving customers

Etymology: early Modern Borlish idiom literally meaning "to get awake". The verb cavir "get" is a direct descendant of capi≈ç "take, pick, recieve", used in the sense "become" since the Middle Borlish period. The adjective yert "awake, alert; (of a business) open" descends via Old Borlish yerte "standing, upright, vertical" and synonymous Ρ™rctus in Vulgar Latin from Classical Ρ™rΡ™ctus "raised, built, erected". Extended from people to machinery and thence to businesses by the nineteenth century.

J'attenoy cos infruniscent tandic y grimmour cave yert.
/Υ¨ £a.teΥàn…îi k…îz …ΣnΥ¨fri.niΥàx…¦nt tanΥàd…Σk i gr…ΣΥàmur kaΥàve j…¦rt/
[Υ¨ ùa.t…ΣΥàn…îi k…îz …Σ…±Υ¨f Äi.n…ΣΥàΟß…¦nt t…ênΥàd…Σk i g Ä…ΣΥàm äΥê k…êΥàve  ù…¦Υêt]
1s=wait-pst adv bored while df computer get-ipf awake
I waited impatiently while the computer booted up.

144.3: parsemnar "dot"

parsemnar /Υ¨par.s…¦mΥànar/ [Υ¨p…êΥê.s…ΣmΥàn…êΥê]
- plant, sow, to throw (the seeds of a crop) onto fertile ground;
- spread, disperse, scatter, sprinkle, strew, to throw anywhere in such a manner;
- broadcast, disseminate, to make widely known;
- dot, stud, to place at regular or irregular intervals across the whole expanse of something

Etymology: Middle Borlish, either a native derivation of semnar "sow, plant" or directly from a Scholastic Latin persemin≈ç "I sow throughout" (compare cognate pessembrar in Vascon). Almost immediately extended from its agricultural roots, and used of news since the seventeenth century.

EnceΟΑ a y novel parsemnar ig jo sail oc jorn.
/…¦nΥàts…¦ΈΗ a i noΥàv…¦l Υ¨par.s…¦mΥànar aj  £o sel …îk  £…îrn/

start-ipf.p at df new sow-inf cmp 1s leave.1s s.px day
Start spreading the news that I'm leaving today.

144.4: congenir "conceive"

congenir /Υ¨k…în. £eΥàn…Σr/ [Υ¨k…î…≤. ù…ΣΥàn…ΣΥê]
- conceive, beget, to have as a child;
- to be the ancestor of, more generally;
- create, generate, to bring into being;
- originate, invent, to be responsible for the existence of

Etymology: from Scholastic Latin congenΡ™sc≈ç "I bring into being", a regularised derivative of gign≈ç "I beget" (past participle genitus "begotten"). Originally restricted to familial legal contexts, the word had entered the wider lexicon via patent law by the early modern period. In modern usage the verb is preferentially used as an infinitive or participle, although conjugated forms are still well attested.

Y parol 'sauΟΑ' e 'veΟΑ' son congeniΟΑ de VeΟΑr Norresc.
/i paΥàr…îl soΈΗ e v…¦ΈΗ s…în Υ¨k…în. £eΥàn…ΣΈΗ de v…¦ΟΑrΧ© n…îΥàr…¦x/
[i p…êΥà Ä…îl soβÄΩΟΑe v…¦h s…î≈΄ Υ¨k…î…≤. ù…ΣΥàn…Σh de Υàv…¦.ΟΑ…ê n äΥà Ä…¦Οß]
df word ewe and ram be.3p beget-p.pst of old Norrish
The words 'ewe' and 'ram' descend from Old Norrish.

##

144.5: Mashick

Mashick (Borlish Mascic /maΥàx…Σk/, Nawat MaishΡΪ'co /maiΥà ÉiΥê î.ko/, Mashick Arabic ΊßΌ³ΌÖΊ¥ΌäΌ² ¬Ζ al-MashΡΪq) is a polity in Lower Mendeva and in fact by land area the largest polity in all of Mendeva. At its greatest extent the then-Empire stretched as far northwest as Mivock.

Morrack vessels of the New World Company established trading relations in the late fifteenth century with the relatively-new Mashick Empire, which at that time was much more geographically restricted. The next decades saw many Novomundine crops make their way across the Atlantic from Mashick, including tashban and shockle.

Mashick (and, to the south, Tavancy) served as bases for exploration of Mendevan lands. Fleets charted as far afield as the Ax, and Mashick was used to exercise control over parts of the Arcabil and their growing riches.

Nineteenth-century Mashick was one of the hotbeds of the nascent political philosophy of Ubiquity. It lagged behind British Mendeva in the adoption of the new morals of the Household Renovation, although by the early twentieth century it had caught up (unlike the more regressive New Provence).

The Lovac Wars of the 1920s and 30s saw the first major bombing campaigns on civilian targets, including many cities in Mashick like Moshtar. The unrest and economic downturn following these wars, more than actual military losses, led to Mashick losing significant parts of its northern territory.

In the modern day Mashick is a major food exporter. Droughts in the late 1960s led to reduced amback and mause supplies and sharply rising prices for these fruits in the Vetomund.

People

Mollam Bushta (fl. c19), composer remember for his pieces featuring the torriot and an ally of the early Deviance movement.

Yollamaniza Elcabir (fl. 1943), spy and later author of La te Hozuho Sineton (No Slumber Will Seize Him). The work's depiction of Mashick's Ministry for Knowledge was revealing enough that he was forced to flee the country to avoid arrest.

Tazim Gennun (fl. 1823), author of short story 'The Witch of Tebjacka'.

SΟ≥lin IzcoΟΓl (fl. c18, Latinised as Solinus Iscovalis and known in Merch as Iscoval), the life scientist who first expounded a scientific account of evolution. He is responsible for the terms 'Iscovalian variation' and 'living web'.

Poshack Nerwaman (fl. 1848), famed lady explorer whose written accounts remained popular works of writing into the twentieth century.

##

145.1: Hasiny

notes in translation on the polity of Hasiny.

Hasiny collint y polity de Taisca nell'ovest, e sy citaΟΑ caver es Nadacou, oc se situant tras y rif dy Bahassassin.
Hasiny borders the polity of Taisha [~Texas] to the west, and its capital city is Nadacow, which lies on the Bahassassin [Mississippi].

Y nom Hasiny es congeniΟΑ des y federaΟß parey nommaΟΑ, oΟß autr annaΟΑ abrangent d'eiΟΑel sceint oy sou y gournanΟß de Hasiny e Taisca.
The name Hasiny derives from the federacy of the same name, which once extended across lands which are now part of Hasiny and Taisha.

Y lengaΟß administraΟßonal ne Hasiny son Jaugta (JahtΟΓ, y lengaΟß Mendevan parolabr par y majortaΟΑ dy pouvlanΟß) eΟΑ y gendr vecin de Cambresc (discrepant a faΟßon signfeyant deur y vernacr de Cambresc Brittan ben Chrysian).
The languages of administration in Hasiny are Jaughta (JahtΟΓ, the Mendevan language spoken by a majority of the populace) and the local variety of Welsh (which differs in important ways from the dialects of British or Chrysian Welsh).

Hasiny sta l'orin de kiveury com spaiΟΑoir de romanΟß, dont y scop eye a un bucol rustr depegntar con emphas a peyer.
Hasiny was the homeland of the herdtale genre of fiction, which sought to depict a rural idyll with a focus on animal husbandry.

145.5: Furlain

Furlain (Merch pronunciation /f äΥêΥàl…ôin/, endonym Furlagne /furΥàla…≤…ô/) is a polity in the northeast of the Italian peninsula, whose principal cities are Verona and Odin. It borders Lombardy to the west, Bavarn to the north, Crain to the east, and Venise to the south.

History

During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, the March of Furlain was the southernmost part of the German Empire, at first a direct imperial vassal and later specifically consolidated under the suzerainty of the Kingdom of Bavarn. After the collapse of the German Empire in the thirteenth century, Furlain's most frequent rival became the Republic of Venise.

Furlain suffered a severe earthquake in 1348 which destroyed both the castle and cathedral in Odin [Udine]. Reference to this event is the first attestation of the word quagment "earthquake" in the Borlish language.

The armourcloth trend in women's fashion (which emulated men's military dress) began in Furlain during the Long Peace in the late eighteenth century. The local term voinacciale (from voina, a slang term for a soldier) was borrowed into several other languages to describe the style.

Troops from Furlain fought in the Second German War (1815βÄ™23), principally against Bavarn. Furlain was allied with such countries as Borland, with whom they undertook the disastrous Battle of Hulm in 1821.

People

Chiarina Bruno (fl. 1824), librettist and composer who wrote the opera Wings Afire.
Jacomo Bruno (fl. 1821), twin of the better-known Chiarina and soldier of the Second German War.

##

145.7: 1st Lexember - des caucet "utterly"

des caucet /d…¦z koΥàts…¦t/ [d…¦z k äΥàts…¦t]
- utterly, absolutely, downright, to the fullest extent possible;
- completely, totally, all the way, all-out, every inch, done to completion;
- irreversibly, irretrievably, irrevocably, in a way that cannot be taken back

Etymology: idiom attested from the late nineteenth century, literally "out of (one's) socks". Thought to have arisen in Damvath slang as a sexual metaphor (taking one's socks off prior to sleeping with someone), although in modern usage it is not considered at all obscene. The noun caucet "sock" is a diminutive of obsolete cauΟß "hose, stockings, tights", via Medieval Latin calcea from Classical calceus "boot".

Nos stou la javigar des caucet.
/n…îz stu la Υ¨ £a.viΥàgar d…¦z koΥàts…¦t/
[n…îz stu la Υ¨ ùa.v…ΣΥàg…êΥê d…¦z k äΥàts…¦t]
1s needed there trawl-inf out.of sock
We must leave no stone unturned.

146.1: 2nd Lexember - deunx "close shave"

deunx /daunks/ [daunks]
- near thing, close call, close shave, a situation where an injury or other undesirable outcome is narrowly avoided;
- (interj.) an expression of shock or relief at such a situation;

deunx fair
- get lucky, dodge a bullet, to narrowly escape an undesirable outcome

Etymology: university slang among the Vithor New School's students, attested there from the eighteenth century and entering the wider lexicon some decades afterwards. The noun is a borrowing from Latin deunx "eleven twelfths", related to uncia "one twelfth". The story linking its modern usage to a twelve-man jury is almost certainly false; more likely is simply the sense of being almost but not quite the whole thing.

Nos farreu noc deunx cossy alcheΟΑr.
/n…îz faΥàrau n…îk daunks k…îΥàsi alΥàk…¦ΟΑrΧ©/
[n…îz f…êΥà Äau n…îg daunks k äΥàsi …êΥàg…¦.ΟΑ…ê]
1p do-fut.1p ng close.shave thus again
We won't get so lucky again.

The Deviance movement

notes in translation on the subject of the Deviance movement (also called Anti-Paradise Theology).

Un ouvr primal signfeyant por y commouΟΑ a DesveyanΟß sta l'ediΟßon lon nom 'E Nos Amareu y Foy', enΟßant a s'evolgar d'an 1799 eΟΑ offiruΟΑ par sdegn cas rigteΟß incommorant.
An important early work to the Deviance movementhm was the anonymously-published 'And We Will Love the Fire', which began circulating in 1799 to immediate moral outrage.

Con tan scein pres un doΟßan de possibiltaΟΑ creibr soumis cos serious por cuscon sta sy scrittour, jamay no scey alcun accort acavaΟΑ sull'oc them.
Though almost a dozen plausible candidates for its authorship have been seriously proposed, no consensus has ever been reached on the issue.

Y commouΟΑ a DesveyanΟß joun coincideu coll'ersteigon dy nuton a trevogt deshormejaΟΑ par y livr d'an 1795 par Elsebeth Sneider 'Par un Argent Neir'.
The early Deviance movement coincided with the height of the voidtale craze launched by Elsebeth Sneider's 1795 trevold 'Through a Black Mirror'.

Plusour dell'eΟß idea introdoit n'ouvr a trevogt se courn sousunt afont de theology Contr-Paradis, eΟΑ y commouΟΑ au taur a l'eΟß ouvr de Sneider adoptar cos particuler.
Many of the ideas conjured in voidtale works were incorporated into Anti-Paradise theology, and Sneider's works in particular were embraced by the movement.

Interim, Sneider meΟΑes abhorre y doctrin Contr-Paradis; lonc sy parol meΟΑes:
However, Sneider herself despised Anti-Paradism; in her own words:

"Cay doutr lorrell'idea stragn e lorrell'univers solumnesc e nienter, car ThifΟΛne, pu tan ig bempart alcot de lou aun me traitaΟΑ com ΟΑa jo sey lorry confraΟΑr l'evidendessem!"
βÄ€It is not only their strange ideas and lonely, nihilist universe, dear ThifΟΛne, but that so many of their number have approached me as if I were their most natural ally!βÄù

146.2: 3rd Lexember - collindar "abut"

collindar /Υ¨k…î.l…ΣnΥàdar/ [Υ¨k…î.l…ΣnΥàd…êΥê]
- abut, neighbour, border, to lie adjacent to (as of a piece of territory);
- run up against, bump into, to start encountering problems with;
- resemble, recall, to remind one of due to similarity

Etymology: regular descendant of Latin collimit≈ç "I border on", from limit≈ç "I delimit, bound". Alternatively, a derivation in the Old Borlish period from an unattested lindar or now-dialectal lint "fence". Extension to conceptual closeness is attested from the seventeenth century.

I reconnoy y segr luy collindant.
/i Υ¨re.k…îΥàn…îi i sijrΧ© lai Υ¨k…î.l…ΣnΥàdant/
[i Υ¨ Äe.k äΥàn…îi i Υàs…Σ.j…ê lai Υ¨k…î.l…ΣnΥàdant]
3s recognise.pst df man 3s.obl abut-prs
She recognised the man next to her.

146.3: 4th Lexember - verdeyant "lush"

verdeyant /Υ¨v…¦r.diΥàjant/ [Υ¨v…¦Υê.d…ΣΥà ùant]
- verdant, green, lush, abundant with (especially new-grown) vegetation;
- rife, plentiful, abounding, present in large numbers;
- thriving, flourishing, prosperous, successful, characterised by happiness and achievements

Etymology: present participle of verdeyar "become green; flourish; abound", a verbal derivative of vert "green" (or directly from Vulgar Latin virdidi≈ç "I turn green"). Metaphorical extensions parallel those in neighbouring Romance and Germanic languages.

Cou je meis Juglot an smout de forest verdeyant.
/ku  £e miz  £aiΥàl…ît an smut de foΥàr…¦st Υ¨v…¦r.diΥàjant/
[ku  ùe miz  ù…êiΥàl…ît an zmut de f äΥà Ä…¦st Υ¨v…¦Υêd…ΣΥà ùant]
fall.pst time.of month July at-indf riot of forest lush
July came with a profusion of lush forest.

146.4: 5th Lexember - roΟß "cabbie"

roΟß /r…îts/ [ Ä…îts]
- cabbie, taxi driver, driver hired by a passenger and paid according to the distance and time of the trip;
- cab, taxi, the vehicle driven by such a person

Etymology: early 20C from Napolitan rozzo "cabbie", further origin disputed. Formally identical with earlier rozzo "boor, lout", from Latin adjective rudis "rough, unskilled, rude", but this term seems to have been obsolete by the mid-19th century. Occasionally identified with dialect ruzza "wheel", from Latin rotula "small wheel, scroll".

I save cojan alcot volen y canΟßon accis par y roΟß.
/i saΥàve koΥà £an alΥàk…ît voΥàl…¦n i kanΥàts…în akΥàs…Σz par i r…îts/
[i s…êΥàve k äΥà ùan …êΥàg…ît v äΥàl…¦n i k…ênΥàts…în …êkΥàs…Σz p…êΥêβÄΩ Äi  Ä…îs]
3s know-ipf mundane how.much will-ipf.3p df song turn.on by df cabbie
He knew how boring the cabbie's music would be.

146.5: 6th Lexember - lanΟßbal "lineball"

lanΟßbal /Υ¨lantsΥàbal/ [Υ¨lansΥàbal]
- lineball, a particular team sport similar to handball or rugby;
- the ball used in this sport;
- (loosely, informal) other team sports involving throwing a ball;
- hot potato, something which gets passed on rapidly due to nobody wanting to deal with it

Etymology: eighteenth-century compound from lanΟßar "to throw" and bal "ball". The verb descends from Late Latin lance≈ç "I launch, shoot, pierce", a derivative of Classical lancea "javelin, spear". Compare caugtrbal "kickball, football", which dates from roughly the same time as a word but whose rules were standardised several decaded later than lineball. In the last sense the word is attested from the 1950s.

Candon y nuverrem es ig Istr Boral vencoy ny torne a lanΟßbal?
/kanΥàd…în i niΥàv…¦.r…¦m …¦z ai …ΣstrΧ© boΥàral v…¦nΥàk…îi ni torΥàne a Υ¨lantsΥàbal/
[k…ênΥàd…în i n…ΣΥàv…¦. Ä…êm …Σz ai Υà…Σ.st…ê b äΥà Äal v…Σ≈΄Υàk…îi ni t äΥêΥàne a Υ¨lansΥàbal]
when df recent-cmp be cmp island Borland win-pst in-df contest at lineball
When did Borland last win the lineball tournament?

Twentieth Century History

excerpt from a 2021 book called Excourse Puzzlers, published by the Royal Tommarth Castinghouse. This was a collection of casual party games using the branding and popularity of a long-running series begun by Merch couple Anna and Ralph Highwater in the 1930s.

Twentieth Century History II (answers on sheet 143)

1. In which Mendevan [North American] polity were the first sobot [radio] transmitters and receivers invented in 1903?

2. Which of the adolescences [coming-of-age novels] of Dorotha Quistack, published in 1917, won the Hatfell Honour and was adapted into a film starring Joke Pentawer just two years later?

3. Who led the government whose collapse in 1925 was the beginning of the so-called Portingale Anarchy?

4. What is the full name of the painkiller present in "Tommy pills", first synthesised in 1932 as the result of collaboration between the Branwen Sanatory and the University of Rimack?

5. How was the length of the (dime) foot redefined in 1946, giving it the value it has to this day?

6. Who was the monarch of Hasiny whose death due to a convoy accident in 1950 is alleged to have been purposefully orchestrated with the knowledge of his grandson Pether?

7. Which national team achieved its only victory in the Lineball Global Tourney to date in 1965?

8. As part of the 120-year celebrations of the Laic Declarions of Belgrade, what was legalised by laws passed simultaneously in 1970 across over a dozen polities?

9. What was the name given to the natural disaster which struck Mashick [Mexico] on the 19th of September 1985?

10. The restored steamship displayed from 1991 to 1995 in Deganny [Deganwy, Wales] was originally part of what polity's "CulombΟΓlu" fleet?

##

146.6: 7th Lexember - clacqment "clatter"

clacqment /klakΥàm…¦nt/ [kl…êkΥàm…¦nt]
- clatter, rattle, a succession of loud and abrupt noises;
- gunfire, the sound of firearms being repeatedly shot;
- racket, din, commotion, ruckus, an environment of loud noise and disturbance;
- controversy, discord, strife, bitter conflict arousing much debate or even violence

Etymology: noun formation from clacquar "rattle, clatter, cause a disturbance". This was borrowed in the Middle Borlish period from contemporary French clacquer "clap, slap, slam", or directly from that word's Germanic origin (compare Willem or Kentish clacken "whack, shake"). Used for gunfire since the eighteenth century, and for general disruption from the nineteenth.

Jo sveilau des y clacqment a jarry jusser.
/ £o sviΥàlo d…¦z i klakΥàm…¦nt a  £aΥàri  £iΥàs…¦r/
[ ùo zv…ΣΥàlo d…¦z i kl…êkΥàm…¦nt a  ù…êΥà Äi  ù…ΣΥàs…¦Υê]
1s wake-pst from df clatter at crockery downstairs
I woke to the clatter of plates downstairs.

146.7: 8th Lexember - inquillin "roommate"

inquillin /Υ¨…Σn.kw…ΣΥàl…Σn/ [Υ¨…Σ≈΄.kw…ΣΥàl…Σn]
- tenant, lodger, renter, one dwelling in a residence they do not own;
- roommate, dormmate, a person with whom one shares living quarters;
- (crude, euphemistic) bedfellow, bedmate, mistress, a sexual partner one lives with

Etymology: from Latin inquilΡΪnus "sojourner, lodger; inmate", related to incol≈ç "I inhabit". Influenced in sense by the unrelated noun quil "bed" and verb inquillir "embed"; these descend from Latin cubΡΪle "bed, lair, kennel", which is related to cub≈ç "I lie down". The euphemistic sexual sense has a possible citation in the seventeenth century, but is only securely attested in the nineteenth.

Deu mien, il s'ayen por inquillin!
/dau mj…¦n | …Σl s…¦Υàj…¦n p…îr Υ¨…Σn.kw…ΣΥàl…Σn/
[dau mj…¦n | …Σl s…ΣΥà ù…¦n p…îΥêβÄΩΥ¨ Ä…Σ≈΄.kw…ΣΥàl…Σn]
God mine | 3p rfl=have-ipf-3p for roommate
Oh my God, they were roommates!

147.1: 9th Lexember - bount "en route"

bount /bunt/ [bunt]
- en route, heading, on the way, along the way, in the course of travelling from one place to another;
- directed, pointing, facing, oriented in a particular direction;
- determined, willing, intent, bent, wanting to do something;
- set, set up, ready, prepared, arranged as necessary ahead of time

Etymology: from Old Borlish boun "ready, willing", altered to bount by analogy with the present participle ending -nt by the early modern period. This is a borrowing from Old Norrish bΟΚinn "adapted, fit", the past participle of bΟΚa "reside, prepare, decorate". In archaic or poetic use the word may be used as a preposition meaning "about to; approaching".

I me quistau por m'addocir ig bount son il ag quarf.
/i me kw…ΣΥàsto p…îr Υ¨ma.doΥàts…Σr ai bunt s…în …Σl …¦i kwarf/
[i me kw…ΣΥàsto p…îΥê Υ¨ma.d äΥàts…ΣΥêβÄΩ Äai bunt s…în …Σl …¦i kw…êΥêf]
3s 1s.obl phone-pst for 1s.obl=inform cmp en.route be.3p 3p to-df station
She phoned me to say they're en route to the station.

Alburgh Anecdotes

excerpt in translation from a letter from the Danish playwright Absolon Mortenszen to his Borlish friend Anscon d'Aupont.

Scey un novel freir ag hostel, lovastr a men e (ΟΑa tu vil my femn confeyar) formous a men sou Daniel.
There is a new friar at the hostel, less talkative and (if you will believe my wife) less handsome than Daniel.

Lonc mey soumonendessem es i cas min all'astuΟßour demant encontraΟΑ n'eΟß dram a rigteΟß inerrevibr ancour scenaΟΑ ny citaΟΑ aval; l'a heu d'un segr sognant de parrinment fair a Rom e posc alandar de le stimar noc Chrestan aΟΑief!
More than anything he reminds me of the moralising mentor one sees in the dreadful morality plays they still show down in the city; he seems the sort to dream of visiting Rome and upon arriving declare it insufficiently Christian! 

Se poΟΑ appondr ig y novel ensignment y MachinaΟßon de Helios de Lorenzo yon tu m'expateyau tras un asc volois y bossir appoussar colombant vars un raivottaΟß, oc m'ajoutaΟßant toΟΑ cas.
One supposes Lorenzo's new Machines of Helios sculpture about which you droned on at me for hours might send the good man directly into an apoplexy, which would at least be entertaining.

Y novel dram es un picq issaur, noc obstroint y temp moglastr y vertaΟΑ desconent naΟΑusc ig Albourg no's, a my sorc, Barcelon.
The new play is a mild success, despite the inclement weather rather betraying the fact that Alburgh is not, to my regret, Barcelon.

##

147.2: 10th Lexember - tourm "troop"

tourm /turm/ [t äΥêm]
- troop, squadron, company, platoon, a body of soldiers;
- barracks, base, fort, fortification, encampment, a structure built for the (temporary or permanent) housing and mustering of soldiers;
- crew, squad, gang, a group of people brought together to do a particular task;
- team, group of people playing on the same side of a sport or game;
- swarm, colony, hive, collective noun for various insects

Etymology: Middle Borlish, via the conflation of contemporary French tourme "troop" and Dutch torem "tower, fort". The former is a borrowing from an Italian descendant of Latin turma "troop, team", while the latter is probably a borrowing from a Romance decendant of Latin turris "tower". As a collective noun it is attested from the sixteenth century for ants and somewhat later for some other animals.

Stoura un tourm ottem all'oc kevlar.
/stuΥàra …Σn turm Υà…î.t…¦m aΥàl…îk k…¦Υàvlar/
[st äΥà Äa …Σn t äΥêm Υà…î.t…êm …êΥàl…îk k…ΣΥàvl…êΥê]
need-fut indf crew better to-df=s.px heist
This heist will need a better crew.

147.3: 11th Lexember - alcant aposc "eventually"

alcant aposc /alΥàkant aΥàp…îx/ […êΥàgan.t…êΥàp…îx]
- some day, in the future, eventually, at some point, after an unspecified amount of time;
- (interjection) about time, finally, jibe at someone or something taking a notionally excessive amount of time

Etymology: idiom found in this form since the eighteenth century, literally "once later". The adverb alcant "once" is an analogical form derived from cant "when" with the word element alc- < Latin aliquΡΪ "some". The adverb aposc "later" is from the preposition posc "after", from Latin post quid "after what". As an interjection it is only securely attested in the twentieth century.

Quippe l'oc havara un hom alcant aposc.
/kw…ΣΥàpe l…îk Υ¨ha.vaΥàra …Σn …îm alΥàkant aΥàp…îx/
[kw…ΣΥàpe l…îk Υ¨ha.v…êΥà Äa …ΣΥàn…îm …êΥàgant …êΥàp…îx]
surely df=s.px hail-fut indf person once later
Surely someone will notice eventually.

147.4: 12th Lexember - tegmn "shelter"

tegmn /ΥàtijmnΧ©/ [Υàtij.m…ên]
- shelter, cover, protection, refuge, a physical structure that keeps one safe;
- (specifically) shade, an area shielded from direct sunlight;
- buffer, cushion, anything used to isolate or minimise the effect of one thing on another;
- leeway, grace, wiggle room, freedom, flexibility, latitude, a range in which one can operate safely

Etymology: originally in the first senses, a direct descendant from Latin tegimen "cover, protection". This noun comes from verb teg≈ç "I cover, conceal", which became Old Borlish ter "span, cover (an amount of land)" and thence modern teir "amount to, number". Extended to safety in more abstract contexts by the early modern period.

Jo duvau y bosaΟß con y tegmn d'un hey.
/ £o diΥàvo i boΥàzats k…în i Υàtij.mnΧ© d…Σn hi/
[ ùo d…ΣΥàvo i b äΥàzas k…în i Υàtij.m…ên d…Σn hi]
1s duck-pst df gunfire with df cover of=indf fence
I used a fence as cover from the gunfire.

147.5: 13th Lexember - zizania "discord"

zizania /Υ¨zi.zaΥànja/ [Υ¨zi.z…êΥànja]
- discord, disharmony, dissonance, cacophony, a harsh combination of sounds;
- discord, tension, strife, dissension, arguing, lack of agreement of harmony between people;
- anguish, distress, angst, inner conflict;
- temptation, spite, vice, sin, feeling drawn to doing wicked things

Etymology: seventeenth-century borrowing from Lombard zizania "ryegrass, weed; discord, vice", which displays a metaphorical extension dating back to its ancestor in Late Latin zizanium "cockle, ryegrass, darnel; vice", a borrowing from Greek. Its modern association with cacophonous noise is probably due to sound symbolism.

Nos oyau zizania dereΟΑr dy mur dy rajognoir.
/n…îz oΥàjo Υ¨zi.zaΥànja deΥàr…¦ΟΑrΧ© di m…Σr di Υ¨ra. £…îiΥàn…îir/
[n…îz  äΥà ùo Υ¨zi.z…êΥànja d…ΣΥà Ä…¦.ΟΑ…ê di m…ΣΥê di Υ¨ Äa. ù äiΥàn…î.j…ê]
1p hear-ipf.1p discord behind of-df wall of-df boardroom
We heard arguing through the boardroom's wall.

All Change

collected notes on a variety of topics.

Vigo VII (fl. 1121) was king of Greater Devon [Devon, Conrwall, Brittany] in the twelfth century, which was a major vassal of the First Drengot Empire under Emperor Roger II (and later briefly under Roger's son). In 1121 N he invaded the south coast of Wales, attempting a reconquest of the southeastern Welsh principalities, without the support of the Emperor.

Zahid Russian is a Slavone language spoken in Crovatia, Servia and surrounding regions, although having little official recognition anywhere. Despite its current location it is mostly closely related to eastern Slavone languages like Kievan [~Ukrainian]; it descends from communities in the late medieval and early modern period who migrated westward due to the disruption of Islamic conquests from the south. The language is named –Ζ–ΑΧ¹―Ö―•–¥ βÄΔ zΟΓhid, meaning "west". It is the source of some words in modern Merch (and other Englishes), such as rasgatto "dΟ©nouement", which comes via Venisian from –†–Α–Ζ–≥–Α–¥―²–Ψ, a nineteenth-century mystery trevold by Olexi Harchenko.

New Skies, Old Stories (originally published in Provincial French as NovΟ® Cial, Anzan Conte) is a work of popular history detailing the origins of the various threads of parachthon [scifi/fantasy] fiction. It was written by Simon d'Estiala and published by Prindor MarsillΟ®s in 1980. Its thesis is that many of the tropes often considered to have been invented by particular parachthon authors are in fact simply examples of existing tropes used in new settings.

Margaret of Iscombe [Exeter] (fl. 1435) was the wife of Mark of Tolose and the mother of King Ambrose III of Vascony. She married at some point shortly before 1435 N. She bore four children who survived infancy, including the future King Ambrose: her first child Alexandra and two younger children Victor (later Duke of Astorga) and Natalia.


##

147.6: 14th Lexember - a plaΟß entarn "some way in"

a plaΟß entarn /a plats …¦nΥàtarn/ […ê Υàplats …ΣnΥàt…êΥên]
- some way in, lodged, embedded, inside, located (usually not far) underneath the surface of something;
- along, at some point in the length of some object or space;
- during, at some point in the course of some time period;
- after a while, not at first, eventually, only at some time later than the beginning of something

Etymology: phrase meaning "at (a) position inside", used idiomatically from as early as the fourteenth century. The noun plaΟß "position" is an Old Borlish borrowing via Old French place "location" from Latin platea "courtyard". The adverb entarn "inside", which corresponds to the preposition ne "in", descends directly from Latin adjective internus "internal, interior".

Y melody dy sanfoin me hamtau a plaΟß entarn.
/i Υ¨me.loΥàdi di sanΥàf…îin me hamΥàto a plats …¦nΥàtarn/
[i Υ¨me.l äΥàdi di s…ê…±Υàf…îin me h…êmΥàto …ê Υàplats …ΣnΥàt…êΥên]
df tune of-df bagpipe 1s.obl win.over-pst at place inside
The bagpipe tune grew on me eventually.

##

147.7: 15th Lexember - kiveury "herdtale"

kiveury /Υ¨ki.vauΥàri/ [Υ¨ki.v…êuΥà Äi]
- herdtale, a genre of fiction originating in nineteenth-century Hasiny and depicting rural (prototypically, cattle-herding) life as a virtuous idyll;
- pastoralism, bucolicism, Arcadianism, the idealisation of rural life perceived as morally superior to urban modernity

Etymology: 19th-century derivative of kiveu "cattle, herd" with the suffix -ry "a collection of things, a shop, a gestalt quality", coined as a calque of Hasinic Welsh jade≈²ath "herd-dom". The noun kiveu descends from Old Borlish kifew "cattle", a borrowing a Borland Old English cyfeoh "cattle-herd". To describe an ideology the term is seen from the early twentieth century, usually in critical contexts.

Jo cof un grivougl de kiveury ag quarvol.
/ £o k…îf …Σn griΥàvuil de Υ¨ki.vauΥàri …¦j kwarΥàv…îl/
[ £o k…îf …Σ≈΄ g Ä…ΣΥàvuil de Υ¨ki,v…êuΥà Äi …¦j kw…êΥê]
1s get.pst indf scribble of herdtale at.df airport
I got a herdtale potboiler at the airport.

148.1: 16th Lexember - prophthan "faster than light"

prophthan /pr…îfΥàΈΗan/ [p ¹ äfΥàΈΗan]
- faster than light, FTL, superluminal, travelling more quickly than the so-called "normal speed" of ten great leagues per second;
- (informal) at warp speed, double-time, lightning fast, with any extremely rapid pace

Etymology: coined in 1949 by Napolitan author Ludgia Pitceri as awareness of the normal speed entered popular culture and parachthon (fantasy/sci-fi) writers reached for various justifications for speedy interstellar travel. The word is a borrowing from Scholastic Greek œÄœ¹ΈΩœÜΈΗΈ§ΈΫœâΈΫ βÄΔ prophthΟΓn≈çn "preceding, overtaking", from œÜΈΗΈ§ΈΫœâ βÄΔ phthΟΓn≈ç "I precede, overtake". In its imprecise use the word is attested from the 1970s.

Capitan, cay uncos inovrant cas y motour prophthan.
/Υ¨ka.piΥàtan | ke …ΣnΥàk…îz Υ¨i.n…îΥàvrant kaz i moΥàtur pr…îfΥàΈΗan/
[Υ¨ka.piΥàtan | ke …Σ≈΄Υàk…îz Υ¨i.n äΥàv Äant kaz i m äΥàt äΥê p Ä äfΥàΈΗan]
captain | fall sth out.of.order wrt df engine FTL
Captain, something's gone wrong with the warp drive.

Maths and Gossip

excerpt in translation from the correspondence of Darren Brodus to NiklΟΓs der Fecht; this letter was sent in 1828.

Y majon livr a me forniΟΑ tandem con y homily de DΟΦmmeyer a them de tas e mission; jo nil attendr a te molfeyar ne fegndr a aΟ·r le leut, ma se poΟΑ t'aiΟßar ig jo vil l'hour stovent benoΟßar l'oc semman meΟΑes!
The library has finally provided me with DΟΦmmeyer's treatise on class and delivery; I will not appease you by pretending to have read it, but be assured I will find the hours for it this very week!

Car Johan flagit ig l'es tout theology inutr des l'atmospher, ma contrig Johan da fieltaΟΑ a alcun mathematic posc y livr de Craddock sur roum a lonctumn.
Dear Johan insists it is all atmospherical and futile nonsense, but then Johan trusts no mathematics since Craddock's book on distance rooms.

VertaΟΑ volent so jo soupris ig l'acquΟ·sc por y method machinal meΟΑes benoΟßar, me j'appon ig i n'a veram un test ossos aΟΑief por se rendr sy modellaΟßon n'atelier defaglessem.
Honestly I'm surprised he consents to use even the mechanical methods, but I suppose he isn't quite bone-headed enough to make his workshop designing more difficult for himself.

Es tu pretenent vars eir ne souΟΑ l'oc staΟΑ?
Have you any plans to go south this summer?

J'oy il voun l'area ne Veron rallumar alcheΟΑr por parformβÄîrumoraΟΑ es ig Al Igne par Bruno sera y primer la scenaΟΑ posc y var!
I hear they shall reopen the arena in Verona to performancesβÄîthey say Bruno's Wings Afire will be the first shown there since the war!

148.2: 17th Lexember - ja sceint "extant"

ja sceint / £a xint/ [ ùa Οßint]
- extant, in existence, surviving, not yet having disappeared or died;
- attested, recorded, present, in evidence, proven to exist or have once existed;
- (orig. law) preexisting, being present before some other event (and thus having certain legal privileges or protections)

Etymology: phrase meaning "still being there", used as legal jargon from the early Middle Borlish period. The adverb ja "already, still, yet" descends directly from Latin iam "already, now" (the homonym ja "yes" is a Germanic borrowing and thus likely unrelated). The participle sceint "being there, existing" comes from the verb sceir "for there to be" used in existential sentences (scey uncos "there is something"). This descends via Old Borlish seer, sier, scer "exist, be located" from Latin sede≈ç "I sit".

Cay gravatoir y massem de descovan ja sceint dy siecr catorΟß.
/ke Υ¨gra.vaΥàt…îir i Υàma.s…¦m de Υ¨de.xoΥàvan  £a xint di Υàsj…¦.krΧ© kaΥàt…îrts/
[ke Υ¨g Äa.v…êΥàt…îj…ê i Υàma.s…êm de Υ¨de.x äΥàvan  ùa Οßint di Υàsj…¦.k…ê k…êΥàt…îΥêts]
fall archive df big.cmp of folio yet there.be-p.prs of-df century 14
It's the largest collection of extant fourteenth-century folios.

148.3: 18th Lexember - ersteigon "climax"

ersteigon /Υ¨…¦r.stiΥàg…în/ [Υ¨…¦Υê.st…ΣΥàg…în]
- climax, the high point of a narrative or a piece of music;
- peak, apogee, zenith, acme, pinnacle, culmination, the highest point or best state of something;

l'ersteigon yembr "come to a head; reach a critical point"

Etymology: originally a term of art, borrowed from Saxon ersteigon "rise, climb, ascension", a noun from steige "climb". This properly referred to the events before the climax, but by the time of borrowing the shift in meaning had become entrenched. The term was used in more general contexts in Borlish by the mid-nineteenth century.

Y trevold de Docquerl es l'ersteigon dy commouΟΑ Futurist.
/i treΥàv…îld de d…îΥàkw…¦rl …¦z Υ¨l…¦r.stiΥàg…în di k…îΥàmuΈΗ Υ¨fi.tiΥàr…Σst/
[i t Ä…ΣΥàv…îld de d äΥàkw…¦Υêl …Σz Υ¨l…¦Υê.st…ΣΥàg…în di k äΥàmuh Υ¨fi.t…ΣΥà Ä…Σst]
df novel of Docquerl be df=peak of-df movement Futurist
Docquerl's novel is the pinnacle of the Futurist movement.

148.4: 19th Lexember - euxon "quartz"

euxon /aukΥàs…în/ […êukΥàs…în]
- quartz, an abundant mineral made of crystalline or amorphous silicon dioxide;
- jargon, technobabble, scientific words or concepts that do not (or appear to not) make any sense

Etymology: compare Scholastic Latin eux≈ç "quartz" and Malchassian Greek ΈΒαΫîΈΨœéΈΫΈ±œ² βÄΔ euxαΙ™nas "quartz", both first attested in the tweflth century. Traditionally connected to early Ancient Greek αΦêΈΑΈΨΈΩΈΩœ² βÄΔ eΟΚxoos "well-polished" and the verb ΈΨœçœâ βÄΔ xΟΚ≈ç "scrape, polish", though this theory has some diachronic and semantic issues. The adjective is attested in Epic literature but no later, and moreover the root pertains very specifically to wood (being related to ΈΨœ¨Έ±ΈΫΈΩΈΫ xΟ≥anon "wooden image" and ΈΨœçΈΜΈ± βÄΔ xΟΚla "timber").

More recently it has been suggested that the word was borrowed into Latin (and then Greek) from a vernacular. Multiple early attestations have led scholars to propose that the word might have come from Borlish itself. If so, the most accepted theory is that the word is originally from attested Old English eolh-sond "amber", literally "elk-sand".

The latter sense is seen mostly in the idiom l'oc m'es euxon (literally "this is quartz to me"), used to express unfamiliarity with a technical field.

Y clocq parey l'hour con un scard d'euxon.
/i kl…îk paΥàri lur k…în …Σn xard daukΥàs…în/
[i kl…îk p…êΥà Äi l äΥê k…în …Σ≈΄ x…êΥêd d…êukΥàs…în]
df clock match df=time with indf shard of=quartz
The clock keeps time with a piece of quartz.

148.5: 20th Lexember - incommorant "immediate"

incommorant /…ΣnΥ¨k…î.moΥàrant/ […Σ≈΄Υ¨k…î.m äΥà Äant]
- immediate, instant, happening straight away;
- expeditious, speedy, acting with efficiency and rapidity;
- proactive, driven, showing initiative and acting without delay;
- punctual, prompt, timely, acting or happening on time

Etymology: Middle Borlish borrowing from Scholastic Latin incommorΡ¹ns "not lingering; not delaying", a derivative of the root moror "I linger, loiter" (compare also commorar "tarry", borrowed or backformed somewhat later).

Sell'ec gest volen rebondir cos incommorant.
/Υàs…¦.l…¦k  £…¦st voΥàl…¦n Υ¨re.b…înΥàd…Σr k…îz …ΣnΥ¨k…î.moΥàrant/
[Υ¨s…¦.l…ΣgΥà ù…¦st v äΥàl…¦n Υ¨ Äe.b änΥàd…ΣΥê k…îz …Σ≈΄Υ¨k…î.m äΥà Äant]
3s.gn=p.px act will.ipf rebound-inf adv immediate

---

The Fourteenth Century

collected notes on various features of the fourteenth century.

Nicolas of Avignon (Provincial NicolΟ† s Avignon /ni.koΥàl…î sa.viΥà…≤uΧÉ/, fl. 1347) was a renowned Burgund architect. He is principally remembered for leading the renovation and expansion of Saint Sophia's Cathedral in Stamboul, the capital city of the Second Roman Empire. His use of experimental techniques in supporting the central dome are alleged to have rendered the entire main body of the cathedral slightly unstable. For this reason he is sometimes jocularly referred to as the "architect of the Empire's demise", given the tragic collapse of the building during a coronation ceremony three hundred years later that killed most of the imperial family (and hundreds more) and sparked the unrest that would end the Empire for good.

The Noccair Lyceum (Borlish Lyce a Noccair /liΥàtse a n…îΥàker/) was established in the early 1300s in the minor town of Noccair; it was the first institute of higher learning on Borland. Like many similar European universities, it initially taught students the so-called Five Romantic Arts of literature, argument, reckoning, geometry and songcraft. By the end of the century alchemy had been introduced to the curriculum.

The Tales of Enfield Wood was a compendium of folk tales compiled in Kent in the late fourteenth century. It is usually credited to the author John of Yare, although this has been questioned. It contains many interpretations of episodes in the life of the Farmer Duke, a folk hero common to English-speaking peoples, as well as demonstrating the growing divergence between the Kentish and Merch tongues.

148.6: 21th Lexember - grivougl "scrawl"

grivougl /griΥàvuil/ [g Ä…ΣΥàvuil]
- scrawl, scribble, chickenscratch, illegible handwriting;
- potboiler, shlock, hackwork, a written work of no artistic merit

Etymology: deverbal noun from grivoglar "scrawl, scribble, write illegibly", a borrowing via synonymous French grivouiller from Willem criblen "scratch". The second sense is attested from the early twentieth century.

Quippe porra tu noc my grivougl leir.
/kw…ΣΥàpe p…îΥàra ti n…îk mi griΥàvuil lir/
[kw…ΣΥàpe p äΥà Äa ti n…îg mi g Ä…ΣΥàvuil l…ΣΥê]
surely can-fut 2s ng 1s.gn scrawl read-inf
Of course you can't read my scrawl.

148.7: 22th Lexember - piver "livestock"

piver /piΥàv…¦r/ [p…ΣΥàv…¦Υê]
- livestock, cattle, farm animals, creatures domesticated for any type of cultivation;
- breeding, husbandry, ranching, the cultivation of domesticated animals;
- (work that is) practical, hands-on, experiential, first-hand experience as opposed to theoretical knowledge

Etymology: via Old Borlish pe»ùvair "livestock" from Latin pecuΡ¹rius "pertaining to sheep or cattle", from pec≈Ϊ "domestic animals". In the last sense attested from the early modern period, especially in constructions like y piver de X "the practical skills required to do X".

L'Agathin a Οêar derra nis prestar ag stoccad cas piver.
/Υ¨la.gaΥàΈΗ…Σn a ΥàΟΑar d…¦Υàra n…Σz pr…¦Υàstar …¦i st…îΥàkad kaz piΥàv…¦r/
[Υ¨la.g…êΥàΈΗ…Σn a ΥàΟΑ…êΥê d…ΣΥà Äa n…Σz p Ä…ΣΥàst…êΥêβÄΩ…¦i st äΥàkad kaz p…ΣΥàv…¦Υê]

149.1: 23rd Lexember - nuton "fad"

nuton /niΥàt…în/ [n…ΣΥàt…în]
- will-o'-the-wisp, marshlight, ignis fatuus, a pale flickering light appearing at night over swampland;
- caprice, whim, fancy, vagary, an idle impulse or idea;
- fad, fashion, craze, rage, a short-lived general public interest in something

Etymology: Middle Borlish borrowing from various French forms such as luiton, nuitin, lutin, neton, all meaning "sea monster; mischievous spirit; will-o'-the-wisp". These appear to have been influenced by luire "shine" and nuit "night"; the oldest form is netun "sea monster", which descends from Latin Neptunus "the sea god Neptune".

The second sense is seen from as early as the sixteenth century; the last only in the twentieth.

Lor sceye un nuton a tapeΟßment e pieΟΑr nuaΟß.
/l…îr xiΥàje …Σn niΥàt…în a Υ¨ta.p…¦tsΥàm…¦nt e Υàpj…¦ΟΑrΧ© njats/
[l…îΥê ΥàΟßje …Σn n…ΣΥàt…în a Υ¨ta.p…ΣsΥàm…¦nt e Υàpj…¦.ΟΑ…ê njats]
then there.be-ipf indf fad to tapestry and stone naked
Then there was a fad for hangings and stonework.

Marking Rubric

excerpt in translation from the censor's guide for the Dijon New School 2019 Concurrence History scitation paper Family, Tradition and Language: the Changing Culture of the Good Game Decades.

Entrou 1: Comoscon poΟΑe Brittan sy stat de prestanΟß cultural sur Mendeva Brittanesc vardir durant l'eΟß annaΟΑ final ny siecr novoΟß cant meΟΑes l'oΟß un sovra Brittan cas pouvlanΟß e laboriostur, e diΟßr alcot sta l'oc famostaΟΑ por ig y moraltaΟΑ dy RenovellaΟßon Famigler fosn enajaΟΑ vars y Novomont?
Question 1: How did Britain retain its position of cultural prestige over British Mendeva during the final years of the nineteenth century even as the latter overtook Britain in population and industry, and to what extent were the ethics of the Household Renovation exported to the Novomund via this cachet?

- cas y primer, caraΟΑ svoccanΟß de tramigraΟßon monfeyaΟΑ dy vige novel tras l'Atlantic e de frequenΟß de mariΟΑaΟß combinant entrig.
- for the first, look for talk of increased transmigration of the new rich across the Atlantic and rates of intermarriage thereamongst.

- ancour, prΟΠmieΟΑ soumoin vars y scanjon dy spaiΟΑoir Vetomondin a masquira e vars y faΟßon yon i destarrisceu y costumn autochthon de kiveury (cos particuler caraΟΑ referiscenΟß ag chronic de Hasiny par Coswteg).
- further, reward mention of the boom of the Vetomundine masquira genre and how it displaced the autochthonous herdtale tradition (specifically look for reference to Coswteg's Hasiny diaries).

- cas y dovem, jayaΟΑ soumoin for vars respos caglou irrayant sur y disputaΟßon irresoluΟΑ cas y defas d'adopΟßon Laic ne Mendeva Bugter; stou discorrir cas dou scrittour ag mendrem.
- for the second, only give credit to responses highlighting the ongoing debate as to the timeline of Laic adoption in Gulf Mendeva; at least two disagreeing authors must be mentioned.

149.2: 24th Lexember - revottaΟß "tantrum"

revottaΟß /Υ¨re.v…îΥàtats/ [Υ¨ Äe.v äΥàtats]
- tantrum, fit, outburst, pique, apoplexy, a contemptible display of bad temper;
- scene, disruption, embarrassment, the result of somebody having a tantrum

Etymology: pejorative derivation from revot "annoyance, irritation". This is an alteration of earlier raivot, diminutive of now-obsolete raif "rage", which descends from Late Latin rabia "rage" (the usual modern word is voudy, a borrowing). The word is considered impolite as a description of an adult's behaviour.

J'oye un revottaΟß allors nell'oc varosset.
/ £…îΥàje …Σn Υ¨re.v…îΥàtats aΥàl…îrz n…¦Υàl…îk Υ¨va.r…îΥàs…¦t/

1s=hear-ipf indf tantrum elsewhere in-df=s.px shop
I heard a tantrum elsewhere in the shop.

149.3: 25th Lexember - errevour "saviour"

errevour /Υ¨…¦.reΥàvur/ [Υ¨…¦. Ä…ΣΥàv äΥê]
- saviour, rescuer, deliverer, person who prevents danger or harm befalling another;
- the Redeemer, the figure of Jesus Christ in his role as deliverer from sin;
- (attributive, of meat) stuffed with a particular combination of breadcrumbs, wine, herbs and chestnuts

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish erevor "saviour", agentive derivation of erevre, erever "save" (which gives modern errevir "rescue"). This comes from Latin Ρ™ripi≈ç "I set free; I remove", related to rapi≈ç "I grab, seize". The term has been applied to Christ along with similar sauvour, reΟΑentor "saviour" since the tenth century. In the last sense referring to a preparation of meat now often served around Christmas on Borland (although historically it, minus the chestnuts, was usually eaten on Easter Sunday).

Gehennant ig nos ayau porc errevour tost y dou por y fest.
/Υ¨ £e.h…¦Υànant ai n…îz eΥàjo p…îrk Υ¨…¦.reΥàvur t…îst i du p…îr i f…¦st/

149.4: 26th Lexember - scop "idea"

scop /x…îp/ [x…îp]
- aim, end, purpose, point, goal, what something is intended to achieve;
- crux, heart, gist, essence, thrust, pith, the central idea of a longer or more complicated matter;
- idea, conceit, a summary of the novel part of a piece of fiction

The second sense is attested from the seventeenth century; the last only in literary criticism of the twentieth.

Jo poΟΑe noc me quottar ag scop dy trevold parachthon.

149.5: 27th Lexember - demastr "judgemental"

demastr /deΥàmastrΧ©/ [d…ΣΥàma.st…ê]
- judgemental, moralising, opinionated, critical, quick to form (especially negative) opinions of things or people;
- overstepping, butting in, inserting one's opinions when they aren't welcome;
- hypocritical, holier-than-thou, snobby, sanctimonious, having an inflated sense of one's own moral high ground

Etymology: derivation from demar "to opine, to express an opinion" with -astr, a pejorative suffix. The verb was borrowed in the tenth century from Old English dΡ™man "judge, decide". First attested in the eighteenth century as the name of a character in one of Tamottan's satirical dramas.

Revisionist History

excerpted from The Realignment of Borlish Inflection and Derivation, an article in the Winter 1909 issue of Theoretical Uncoverings in the History of Languages. This is a venerable scientific journal published at that time through the University of Lester [Leicester].

βÄΠundeniable that the modern form -aurn of the first conjugation's third-person plural preterite, as well as its ancestor in Old Borlish -avren /-Υàau.r…ôn/, would descend regularly from the Latin *pluperfect* -Ρ¹vΡïrant and is less straightforward a reflex of the Latin perfect -Ρ¹vΡ™runt (from which we would naΟ·vely expect modern Borlish -eirn). Nevertheless, the attestation, though rare, of syncopated perfect -Ρ¹runt (from which Vascon -aron, among many others) offers a plausible direct ancestor. This is especially tempting if we are permitted to assume influence from the other persons of the Borlish preterite, all of which employ an -au- theme.

We turn to perhaps the most contentious claim of this article. The fate of the Latin occupational suffix -Ρ¹tor in Borlish has been a perennial puzzle since Bughter noted that modern Borlish -our cannot be its simple reflex. It was possible to suspect that an early -aΟΑour or -dour was reduced, but absolutely no evidence of such forms has emerged from any medieval manuscript (the word saΟΑour "farmer" is not a counterexample, as the connection of Latin sator to the verb ser≈ç "I sow" was opaque). This further precludes the possibility of mere analogy from third-conjugation verbs (Old Borlish corsor "runner" < Latin cursor), which would have taken time to spread to the much more numerous first-conjugation verbs.

One might also attempt to claim the Borlish suffix is a borrowing from one of the French languages, most of which lost -t- entirely between vowels (e.g. Old French chant(e)or "singer" < cantatorem). We do not deny that such forms reinforced -or in Old Borlish, but the suffix is manifestly native, appearing productively as early as the eighth-century Halvuy chronicle. Our new proposal begins from a few aberrant words in modern dialects and extends back to the first decades of Roman Borland.

Now restricted to northern dialects, the term orrel "penis" isβÄΠ

##

149.6: 28th Lexember - sontiΟßtaΟΑ "linchpin"

sontiΟßtaΟΑ /Υ¨s…în.t…ΣtsΥàtaΈΗ/ [Υ¨s…în.t…ΣΥàstah]
- criticality, necessity, exigency, requirement, the state of needing something (especially when there are significant negative consequences for lacking it);
- linchpin, mainstay, backbone, keystone, sine qua non, a person or thing that is critical to a system working or even existing`

Etymology: borrowed from Scholastic Latin sonticitΡ¹s "urgency, exigency" or a derivation within Middle Borlish from sontic "urgent, critical" < Latin sonticus "dangerous, serious". The word has been used to refer to a necessary thing or person since the eighteenth century.

DesmettaΟΑ noc y sontiΟßtaΟΑ de nossell'entrpris!
/Υ¨d…¦.zm…¦ΥàtaΈΗ n…îk i Υ¨s…în.t…ΣtsΥàtaΈΗ de Υ¨n…î.s…¦Υ¨l…¦ntrΧ©Υàpr…Σz/
[Υ¨d…¦.zm…ΣΥàtah n…îg i Υ¨s…în.t…ΣΥàstah de Υ¨n…î.s…ΣΥàl…¦n.t…êΥàp Ä…Σz]

149.7: 29th Lexember - taur aΟ·r "look forward"

taur aΟ·r /tor …¦Υàjir/ [toΥêβÄΩ Ä…ΣΥà ù…ΣΥê]
- look forward, anticipate, to await with eagerness;
- be eager, be excited, to feel enthusiasm;
- be raring, be antsy, to feel impatience;
- (colloquial, dated) be horny, to feel arousal

Etymology: literally "to have bull", idiom attested (in the slightly different sense of impulsiveness) from the sixteenth century. The noun taur was altered from early Middle Borlish tor to be closer to Latin taurus "bull, steer". The last sense is attested sporadically from the late nineteenth century and reached its peak popularity in the mid-twentieth.

Nos orruΟΑe casc y gent ausn taur a enΟßar destr.
/n…îz Υ¨…î.riΥàΟΑe kax i  £…¦nt oznΧ© tor a …¦nΥàtsar d…¦strΧ©/
[n äΥ¨z…î. Ä…ΣΥàΟΑe kax i  ù…¦nt Υào.z…ên toΥêβÄΩ Äa …ΣnΥàts…êΥê Υàd…¦.st…ê]
1p seem-ipf each df person have.sbj.ipf-3p bull to start-inf out
It looked like everyone was eager to get going.

##

150.1: 30th Lexember - sdegn "scorn"

sdegn /zdijn/ [zd…Σjn]
- scorn, contempt, disdain, displaying one's feelings that something is inferior or vulgar;
- flak, criticism, bad press, censure, disparaging remarks made towards someone or something

Etymology: deverbal noun from sdegnar "scorn, malign", a derivative of archaic degnar "deign, condescend, begrudge", which comes from Latin dign≈ç "I deem worthy". The word nowadays often connotes that the criticism is undeserved (compare the phrase sdegn accarr "to be wrongly criticised").

A sy lasc novel scey un tombr a sdegn.
/a si lax noΥàv…¦l xi …Σn t…îmbrΧ© a zdijn/
[a si lax n äΥàv…¦l Οßi …Σn Υàt…îm.bβÄΩ Äa zd…Σjn]
at 3s.gn film new there.be indf heap at scorn
Her new film's garnered lots of criticism.

Death in the Spires

excerpt in translation from the beginning of (IRL) historical mystery Death in the Spires, by KJ Charles.

Jem gasau all'eΟß parol.
Jem stared at the words.

Lou remane tars eΟΑ accusant, hetrous demay sur y lettr tachygraphaΟΑ lou intermanent, ben y papiraΟß frail lou portant.
They sat stark and accusatory, too brutal for the typewritten letters that made them, or the thin cheap paper that bore them.

L'encost sta lessy naΟΑusc, com ΟΑa y rimband dy tachygraph aye pres s'usaΟΑ.
The ink was a little faded, as though the typewriterβÄôs ribbon was almost worn out.

No sceye alcun dat, alcun asmuth, alcun souscreut, nien for tre lign de scrit.
There was no date, no address, no signature, nothing but three lines of text.

Jeremy Kite es un mordrer. L'occeu Toby Feynsham. RoveΟΑ lour y raΟßon.
Jeremy Kite is a murderer. He killed Toby Feynsham. Ask him why.

Jem fey y papir reuvr, com ou se poΟΑ ec may trovar dereΟΑr.
Jem turned the paper over, as if there might be more on the back.

L'oΟß broyeu pog, y tremanΟß de sy man desconent.
It rattled slightly, betraying the tremor of his hands.

I le refey reuvr alcheΟΑr por y parol enverimmaΟΑ mostrar, e leyau gas vars Sr Leighton, oΟß asseyent oppos all'acraΟß gaubanneresc de tablaur, le spiant a min malvolent.
He turned it back again to the poisonous words, and looked up at Mr Leighton, who sat across the mahogany acreage of desk, regarding him with disfavour.

##

150.2: 31st Lexember - scaur ag mur ne PompeΟ· "write one's name in the history books"

scaur ag mur ne PompeΟ· /xor …¦i m…Σr ne Υ¨p…îm.piΥàji/ [xoΥêβÄΩ Ä…¦i m…ΣΥê ne Υ¨p…îm.p…ΣΥà ùi]
- write one's name in the history books, make a name for oneself, to do things which will cause one to be renowned for a long time;
- act with posterity in mind, to be conscious of one's legacy when deciding one's actions;
- (jocular) make last-minute revisions, to make rushed changes due to having little time left before a deadline

Etymology: literally "to write on the walls of Pompeii", first attested as an idiom in Napolitan some years after the unearthing of the ruins of Pompeii and the uncovering of its trove of graffiti. The verb scaur "write", which displaced now-obsolete or poetic scrir "write", descends from Latin scalp≈ç "I scratch; I engrave". The last sense, attested in Borlish since the 1920s, refers directly to the volcanic eruption of 79N which buried the city.

L'oΟß segr es dignt tojorn i vil scaur ag mur ne PompeΟ·.
/l…îts ΥàsijrΧ© …¦z daint toΥà £…îrn i v…Σl xor …¦i m…Σr ne Υ¨p…îm.piΥàji/
[l…îs Υàs…Σ.j…êβÄΩ Ä…Σz daint t äΥà ù…îΥên i v…Σl xoΥêβÄΩ Äe…Σ m…ΣΥê ne Υ¨p…îm.p…ΣΥà ùi]
df=s.dt man be say-p.prs always 3s will write-inf at.df wall in Pompeii
That guy's always saying he'll make a name for himself.

150.3: bucol "idyll"

bucol /biΥàk…îl/ [b…ΣΥàk…îl]
- idyll, haven, paradise, solace, oasis, a peaceful location or circumstance especially in the countryside;
- respite, break, relief, lull, a (usually temporary) decrease in stress or difficulty;
- reverie, daydream, idle and pleasant thoughts

Etymology: Middle Borlish, a backformation from earlier adjective bucolic "idyllic, peaceful", a borrowing from Latin bucolicus "pastoral, rustic". The word had broadened from the first senses by the late eighteenth century, and has been used for purely mental peace since the early twentieth.

Jo kiglau l'oΟß seyon rovaΟΑ dec j'er bucol hamtant.
/ £o kaiΥàlo l…îts siΥàj…în roΥàvaΈΗ d…¦k  £…¦r biΥàk…îl hamΥàtant/
[ ùo k…êiΥàlo l…îts Υàsj…în  Ä äΥàvah d…¦k  ù…¦Υê b…ΣΥàk…îl h…êmΥàtant]
1s miss-pst df=s.dt 3s.agt ask-p.pst because 1s=cop.ipf idyll dwell-p.prs
I missed what he asked because I was daydreaming.

150.4: coyol "cone"

coyol /k…îΥàj…îl/ [k äΥà ù…îl]
- (geometry) cone, the surface formed by rotating a deflected line segment around its end (or the solid thus circumscribed);
- anything conical;
- spire, point, a tapering structure built to cap a roof or tower;
- acme, zenith, peak, cap, summit, the best or highest-quality type of something;
- cap, coif, bonnet, a piece of headgear covering the top and sides of the head

Etymology: via Old Borlish co»ùol "hood; cone" from Latin cuc≈Ϊlla "hood, cowl; conical wrapper or case". Used consistently to translate Scholastic Latin c≈çnus "cone" in geometrical texts since the fourteenth century (possibly the unrelated con "with" discouraged directly borrowing that Latin). In the modern language restricted to detached headgear that is at least vaguely conical.

Y coyol dy cligs es coup de fourraΟß mout cavent.
/i koΥàj…îl di klaiz …¦z kup de fuΥàrats mut kaΥàv…¦nt/
[i k äΥà ù…îl di klaiz …Σz kup de f äΥà Äas mut k…êΥàv…¦nt]
df cone of-df church be hit of lightning often get-p.prs
The church's spire often gets struck by lightning.

##

150.5: H is for History

collected notes on three features of the Boralverse alternate history that begin with the letter H.

Hasinic Welsh (Cambroec Hasinia /k…ômΥàbreΥêk h…ôΥàziΥê.nj…ô/) is one of the primary languages of Hasiny [~S Mississippi River], along with the Mendevan language of Jaughta [~Choctaw]. It is mutually intelligible with other varieties of Welsh, although its orthography uses a different standard to British or Chrysian [Australian] Welsh. A few English words come via this language, like herdtale (a calque of jade≈²ath "herd-dom") and sobot "radio" (originating in Jaughta Οßobota "message").

Hatfell Honours (Merch /Υàhat.f…ôl Υà…î.n…ôz/) refer to several prizes awarded since the early twentieth century to artists in various fields across the Drengot Collusion (and, after that alliance-federacy collapsed in 1961, across all of Gaul and the Northwestern Isles [Ireland, GB, Borland]). The Honours in literature and drama are among the original set of prizes, while new arts have been admitted as recently as the 2010s. The Honours are named for Hatfell [Hatfield, Hertfordshire], a northern town in London near the border with Markland which was home to a collective of renowned authors in the nineteenth century. In 1918, the writer Dorotha Quistack won a Hatfell Honour for one of her adolescences [coming-of-age novels].

Hostilian (in Borlish often Stiglan /staiΥàlan/, latinised as HostΡΪliΡ¹nus) was a Borlish monk in the ninth century, often considered the father of Borlish history. His most famous work HistoriΟΠ Terrarum Boralensium "Histories of the Borlish Lands" is a semi-mythic account of Borland for the past five centuries since the arrival of Germanic peoples on the island. It includes a continuous list of rulers from Prase Victor Rossetus, several of which are not attested in any other source. This has led some historiographers to conclude that Hostilian was commissioned by the contemporary monarchy to legitimise their rule.

##

151.1: The Odyssey

excerpt in translation from the beginning of the Odyssey, as translated into prose Borlish from the original Epic Greek by Rosctey Villautin in 1910. The translation has been criticised for hewing too closely to the Greek syntax at the expense of sounding natural in Borlish.

D'un segr racont mey, Mus, mout tornant, oc magn laΟΑ
Of a man tell me, Muse, often turning, who very widely

sgarant, posc y citaΟΑ sagntaΟΑ a Troy i devastau;
wandered, after the holy city of Troy he sacked;

dy larc hom seyon vis, dy ment den seyon connuΟΑ
of the many people he saw, whose minds he knew

e dy larc cos sur mar seyon souvaΟΑ e caut ny cour,
and of the many things at sea he endured and took to heart,

gimmolent kireΟΑam a sy viΟΑ eΟΑ ag repaΟΑr de sy commilton.
striving wholeheartedly for his life and his comrades' homecoming.

Ma noc cossy sceus i sy commilton errevir, meΟΑes auvorn oblimant;
But not like this could he save his comrades, even longing to so much;

lou desig lorry fol temertaΟΑ il perisceurn;
they, for their foolish recklessness, perished;

borel, eΟß des y kiveu dy sol Hyperion
fools, who from the herd of the sun Hyperion

ayent repasc fait e lor i lou effurau deur y jorn de repaΟΑr.
dined, and so he deprived them of the day of homecoming.

##

151.2: nienter "void"

nienter /nj…¦nΥàt…¦r/ [nj…ΣnΥàt…¦Υê]
- (rare) void, empty, vacant, not filled or occupied;
- useless, ineffective, futile, vain, not producing any effect;
- invalid, worthless, without legal force or other analogous use;
- nihilist, pessimistic, holding that things do not have any point or purpose

Etymology: adjective derivation from nien "nothing" (earlier nient), which reflects either nΡ™ gentem "no person" or nΡ™ entem "no being" in Late Latin. Even in early texts the word has seldom been used literally (competing with vogt "empty"); senses to the effect of "amounting to nothing" predominate.

L'arra oΟß saut ig caye sy mariΟΑaΟß dovem nienter.
/laΥàra …îts sot ai k…¦Υàje si Υ¨ma.riΥàΟΑats Υàdo.v…¦m nj…¦nΥàt…¦r/
[l…êΥà Äa …îs sot ai k…ΣΥà ùe si Υ¨ma. Ä…ΣΥàΟΑas Υàdo.v…êm nj…ΣnΥàt…¦Υê]
3s=have.fut s.dt know-p.pst cmp fall-ipf 3s.gn marriage second void
He must have known his second marriage was void.
