151.3: scanjon "climb"

scanjon /xanΥà £…în/ [x…ênΥà ù…în]
- rise, ascent, climb, an increase in elevation;
- upgrade, uplift, overhaul, improvement, the replacement or betterment of something to have new or renewed features;
- boom, bonanza, a period of wealth or prestige

Etymology: noun from scandr "rise, climb" or directly from Latin scansi≈ç "a climbing", from Latin scand≈ç "I climb". The word is attested in metaphorical uses from the sixteenth century, and in its specifically economic sense it is seen from the nineteenth century.

Ty dois tach novel es un scanjon ou?
/ti d…îiz tak noΥàv…¦l …¦z …Σn xanΥà £…în u/
[ti d…îiz tak n äΥàv…¦l …Σz …Σn x…ênΥà ù…în u]
2s.gn mobile tactile new be indf upgrade q
Is your new smartphone an upgrade?

151.4: borel "idiot"

borel /boΥàr…¦l/ [b äΥà Ä…¦l]
- idiot, fool, numpty, nincompoop, numbskull, dunderhead, a person lacking intelligence or common sense;
- sap, patsy, rube, chump, schmuck, a person who is notably naΟ·ve and easy to dupe;
- yokel, bumpkin, rustic, an unsophisticated person from a rural area

Etymology: disputed. The word is seen from the early Middle Borlish period with indeterminate meaning. Possibly a diminutive from an unattested *bour "peasant", which would have been borrowed from Old English (ΡΓe)b≈Ϊr "farmer". Otherwise, perhaps a direct descendant from Latin puerΡΪlis "youthful; childish". However, both explanations require somewhat irregular sound changes.

L'agathin le nommau de borel a phasm prose.
/Υ¨la.gaΥàΈΗ…Σn le n…îΥàmo de boΥàr…¦l a ΥàfazmΧ© proΥàze/
[Υ¨la.g…êΥàΈΗ…Σn le n äΥàmo de b äΥà Ä…¦l a Υàfa.z…êm p Ä äΥàze]
df=minister 3s.acc name-pst of idiot at TV live
The minister called her an idiot on live TV.

##

151.5: War and Peace

Mystra (in full the Principality of Mystra; Greek: ΈîΈΒœÉœÄΈΩœ³αΨΕœ³ΈΩΈΫ œ³ΈΩαΩΠ Έ€œÖœÉœ³œ¹αΨΕ) was a mordether [crusader] state founded in the eleventh century in the Peloponnese. The holy order of soldiers known as the Convoy Australier was instrumental in conquering the peninsula as a staging post for further attempts at conquest in the Masreck [Levant]. It was named for its then-capital, the city of Mystras. Originally independent, the polity was after some decades reincorporated into the resurgent Roman Empire, and the capital eventually moved to Mysarnos [Pylos]. Mystra was formally dissolved in the mid-seventeenth century as a result of the Romantic Wars.

New Leudong (Welsh: Leudong NewΟΑ /Υ¨lyΥêΥàd…î≈΄ ΥànawΟΑ/) is a polity covering the southeast portion of Chrysia [Australia]. Named for the Cathayan state of Leudong, it has experienced significant Cathayan nation-building alongside British settlement since the late seventeenth century. In the early eighteenth century the trade network of Gurjat [Gujarat] extended as far as New Leudong. Born in New Leudong, mixed-heritage writer Pether Hetsong penned semi-autobiographical work The Land of Golden Lies and Sugared Fire in 1864, often considered the first Chrysian work of literature to enjoy Vetomundine [Eurasian] success. New Leudong was an early adopter of the new social mores of the Household Renovation in the late nineteenth century. A collaboration involving the Branwen Sanatory in New Leudong's capital invented the chemical painkiller cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine [ibuprofen]. In the late twentieth century the New Leudong city of Mehannin [Brisbane] became a technological hub, spurring developments in grimmer [computer] design and methodics [programming].

##

152.1: Apprenticeship

excerpt in translation from Guillotine Hadjatoux's 2001 trevold Tyrocinie "Apprenticeship", her first in a series of deconstructionist tales alluding to tropes in parachthon [specfic] fiction from unexpected points of view.

Y figl de casc gent reuloirn des y campagn autraΟΑ. Il stan veilessem, cos primer.
Everyone's children came back from the country changed. They were older, for a start.

Certan entr lou stan Οßoucherrem, e certan mancan ustolmentβÄîcertan stan metamorphosaΟΑ vars joun dy campagn lon alcun residu de Paris entarn, parfait con y vernacr.
Some of them were wilder, and some had no mannersβÄîsome had transmogrified into country children with not a trace of Paris in them, complete with accent.

ToΟΑ y mont souprenent y jammel Tovat stan reuluΟΑ fort ustolent ant un zeltaΟΑ a gimmoldr, ig degnant il stan paravant eΟß picq beuscr.
To everyone's surprise the Tovat twins had come back well-behaved with a strong work ethic, despite the little louts they had been.

Y joun Pinze, interim, reuluΟΑ stragn. Caye noc un autranΟß vecinabr cos fagl.
The PinzΟ© children, though, came back odd. It wasn't something readily identifiable.

Il ayen cortiseΟß eΟΑ ovranΟß aΟΑief por surcrescr de coΟΑelostaΟΑ, ma tojorn il pariscen gasar vars nuton mental par ogl ayent ancour may vis meΟΑes all'eΟß dy soldart excavaΟΑ. 
They were polite and tactful to the point of overcaution, but they always seemed to look into the middle distance with eyes that had seen even more than the hollowed-out soldiers.

152.2: prΟΠmiar "reward"

prΟΠmiar /preΥàmjar/ [p Ä…ΣΥàmj…êΥê]
- reward, recompense, honour, to recognise (some achievement) and give some boon for it;
- incentivise, encourage, motivate, to incite (an action) by giving something as a reward for doing it

Etymology: borrowed from Scholastic Latin prΟΠmi≈ç "I reward, honour", a verbal derivative from prΟΠmium "profit, prize, bribe". Though the verb was once poetically used with the person rewarded as the direct object, now only the honourable act itself may take that position.

Nos visau y costroinΟß de randy prΟΠmiar.
/n…îz viΥàzo i k…îΥàstr…îints de ranΥàdi preΥàmjar/
[n…îz v…ΣΥàzo i k äΥàst Ä…îins de  Ä…ênΥàdi p Ä…ΣΥàmj…êΥê]
1p aim.at-1p df construction of apartment reward-inf
We aim to incentivise building apartments.

152.3: rustr "rural"

rustr /Υàr…ΣstrΧ©/ [Υà Ä…Σ.st…ê]
- rural, agrarian, rustic, of the countryside away from urban areas;
- provincial, backwoods, boorish, unsophisticated, unrefined, narrowminded, having a worldview considered typical of rural people;
- uncouth, awkward, clumsy, bumbling, unaware of niceties or unable to competently observe them

Etymology: from Old Borlish rustre "rural", either directly from Latin r≈Ϊsticus "rural" or via synonymous rustre in Old French. The insulting senses date as far back as Classical times, and may have been reintroduced during the Middle Borlish period.

Me stoura Janvier passar con gent rustr.
/me stuΥàra  £anΥàvj…¦r paΥàsar k…în  £…¦nt Υàr…ΣstrΧ©/
[me st äΥà Äa  ù…ê…±Υàvj…¦Υê p…êΥàs…êΥê k…în  ù…¦nt Υà Ä…Σ.st…ê]
1s.obl necessary-fut January pass-inf with people rural
I'll have to spend January among rustics.

152.4: bosaΟß "gunfire"

bosaΟß /boΥàzats/ [b äΥàzas]
- gunfire, gunshot, report, the act or sound of shooting a gun;
- shooting, shootout, a confrontation in which guns are fired;
- criticism, disapproval, flak, censure, bad press, opprobrium, the bad opinion of others

Etymology: 20C, pejorative or augmentative derivation from bos "gun"; attested almost immediately in the metaphorical senses. The noun bos "gun" (cf. Merch boss "gun") is borrowed from Willem Dutch (arm)bΟ≥sz "gun", which descends via armbaust "crossbow" and synonymous Old French arbaleste from Latin arcuballista "crossbow".

Dou madarc interromoirn un bosaΟß ny routel.
/du maΥàdark …ΣnΥ¨t…¦.roΥàm…îirnΧ© …Σn boΥàzats ni ruΥàt…¦l/
[du m…êΥàd…êΥêk …ΣnΥ¨t…¦. Ä äΥàm…î.j…ên …Σm b äΥàzas ni  Ä äΥàt…¦l]
two cop interrupt-pst-3p indf gunfire in-df alley
Two cops interrupted a shoutout in the alley.

##

152.5: False Friends

a complete arbitrary selection of dictionary entries from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (fifth edition), a reference work published in 1961 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

carjack [/ΥàkaΥê.d £ak/ "breakup"] n. the ending of a romance, especially if unexpected or one-sided; v. intr. of a romance, to end; v. tr. to end a romance with (someone). The term once referred specifically to a letter informing one's partner of the end of your romance. Early 20C < Borlish soldiers' slang carjacq, car-Jacq meaning "dear John". During the Millstone Wars, this referred to a dreaded letter from one's beloved back home cancelling promises of marriage after the war.

danny [/Υàda.ni/ "nuisance"] n. an annoying person or thing, especially when obstructing what one wants to achieve; adj. annoying or obstructive. 10C Old English danneΡΓ "stinging nettle" < Old Borlish danney, nanney (ΟΠq.), thought to reflect a Borland Kelt ?naindia "nettles" (cf Irish ≈³andΟ≥c).

eager [/ΥàeΥê.g…ô/ "acid"] n. a sour or corrosive substance, (in alchemy) a substance which gives up mora [protons] in aqua or directly to another substance; adj. sour or corrosive, (in alchemy) giving up mora in aqua or directly to another substance, (by extension) unlikable or spiteful. Early Merch eagre "sour" < Middle French aigre (ΟΠq.) This descends from colloquial Latin Ρ¹crus "sour, bitter", variant of Ρ¹cer in Classical Latin.

marksman [/ΥàmaΥêks.m…ôn/ "marquess"] n. the noble ruler of (originally) a borderland region known as a mark [march], traditionally ranking above earls due to the prestige of defending the realm's borders. 13C Early Merch < compound of Old English mearc "boundary" and mann "person" used to translate Old French marchis "marquess" in reference to the Marksman of Jerusalem. The term is primarily used for titleholders overseas; within Albion (possibly due to collision with "Markland") predominates the synonymous term edgelord.

153.1: Americas Quiz

questions in translation from the 1930 book Prove your Knowledge!, by Anna and Ralph Highwater.

-5. De cal cainΟß cas astronomy vene y motif por y nommanΟß dy gran citaΟΑ Mascic a Mosctar d'an 1780?
-5. What astronomical event inspired the naming of the major Mashick city of Moshtar [~San Francisco] in 1780?

-6. Y descovrenΟß de cal natur soustanΟßal fis loy ny citaΟΑ CappaΟßan a VaronΟΗu e l'Asteller de NΟΩstad la d'an 1902?
-6. What property of matter was discovered by the Cappatian city of VaronΟΗw [~VitΟ≥ria, ES]'s NΟΩstad Atellier in 1902?

-7. Calun des entr y staddomain lonc y rimot CappaΟßan auster se cof primer stabliΟΑ, ant un chronic corporalleyaΟΑ enΟßant d'an 1561?
-7. Which was the first of the staddomains [colonial tradeposts] along the eastern Cappatian coast to be established, with an official chronicle dating to 1561?

-8. Stant un vangard por y Torney Mondial a LanΟßbal, com digna y concors a lanΟßbal tras tarm accommis primer n'Avasouconda d'an 1909 entr veint tourm?
-8. A predecessor to the Lineball Global Tourney, what was the name of the international lineball competition first held in AwasΟΚconda [~ParanΟΓ] in 1909 between twenty teams?

-9. Calun entr y polity Novomondin se pone soul par un delegaΟßon amenar ag federaΟßon dy Declar Laic ne Belgrad?
-9. Which was the only Novomundine polity to have a delegation present at the signing of the Laic Declarations in Belgrade?

-10. Com digna y preu vascel Scinesc stant alandaΟΑ ny Novomont?
-10. What was the name of the first Chinese ship to alight in the Novomund?

153.2: prose "live"

prose /proΥàze/ [p ¹ äΥàze]
- direct, straight, immediate, proceeding by the shortest possible route;
- unmediated, firsthand, primary, without any intermediary;
- live, in real time, having no delay between recording and broadcast

Etymology: either from Scholastic Latin pr≈çseus "direct" or pr≈çsΡ™ "directly", both derivatives of pr≈ç(r)sus "direct". This in turn appears to be from the prefix pr≈ç- "forward" and Old Latin vorsus "turned". In the last senses the term is attested from the 1920s.

Y deixist sceu tandem un inquit prose vecinar.
/i dikΥàs…Σst xau Υàtan.d…¦m …Σn …ΣnΥàkw…Σt proΥàze Υ¨ve.tsiΥànar/
[i d…ΣgΥàz…Σst xau Υàtan.d…êm …Σn …Σ≈΄Υàkw…Σt p Ä äΥàze Υ¨ve.ts…ΣΥàn…êΥê]
df archivist can.pst at.last indf quote live locate-inf
Y deixist sceu tandem un inquit prose vecinar.

##

153.3: Οßougr "wild"

Οßougr /tsugrΧ©/ [Υàtsu.g…ê]
- wild, untamed, not having been domesticated or trained for husbandry;
- uncontrolled, uninhibited, unrestrained, without limits on behaviour;
- misbehaving, unruly, acting out, disobedient, not doing as one is told;
- rude, tactless, without regard for etiquette

Etymology: 19C, imported from slang used on the Borlish-speaking islands of the Arcabil [Caribbean] but cognate with terms used across the whole region, including northern Cappatia [South America]. These originate in Run'allo [Quechua] chucru "difficult", as used to describe animals. The unrelated bougr "boor, scoundrel" may have influenced the word's sense development.

Vos saveΟΑ ou l'instroy eΟß stuΟΑant Οßougr?
/v…îz saΥàv…¦ΈΗ u l…ΣnΥàstr…îi …¦ts stiΥàΟΑant tsugrΧ©/
[v…îz s…êΥàv…¦βÄΩΟΑu l…ΣnΥàst Ä…îi …¦s st…ΣΥàΟΑant Υàtsu.g…ê]
2p know-2p whether 3s=teach p.dt student wild
Do you know if he has unruly students?

153.4: saget "arrowhead"

saget /saΥà £…¦t/ [s…êΥà ù…¦t]
- arrowhead, the pointed part of an arrow or similar projectile;
- arrow, the pictorial representation of an arrow used to indicate direction;
- chevron, vee, a V shape;
- cursor, pointer, an icon indicating the current active position on a computer screen;
- one of the suits in the so-called "Maritime" card deck [1]

Etymology: Old Borlish saget "arrow", a semi-learned form reflecting Latin sagitta "arrow, shaft, bolt". The term was displaced in referring to the whole arrow by Germanic loan flesc "arrow". Metaphorical extensions are seen first in heraldic descriptions; the computing sense dates to the late twentieth century.

Nos vaun saget forgar n'adamantin ou?
/n…îz von saΥà £…¦t f…îrΥàgar Υ¨na.da.manΥàt…Σn u/
[n…îz von s…êΥà ù…¦t f äΥêΥàg…êΥê Υ¨na.d…ê.m…ênΥàt…Σn u]
1p worth-3p arrowhead make-inf in=titanium q
Are titanium arrowheads worth it?

[1] Maritime decks have five suits, known in Borlish as scet, scuΟΑ, bac, gem, saget "coin, shield, rod, gem, arrowhead" after their approximate shapes.

##

153.5: Maritime Cards

excerpt from Crossing the Rubicon: a Hidden History of Games, a 1982 work of popular history written (originally in Borlish as Y Rubicon Trasir: un Stoir Scos de Joy) by writer and stage performer Jonnovar Meyaunot. She became famous later in life as a humourist and sobot [radio] host of documentaries and quiz games.

βÄΠmore common across the Northwestern Isles [Ireland, Britain, Borland], as well as Denmark and Willemy [~Lotharingia]; however, this particular style of deck actually originates in fifteenth-century Poland. The Maritime deck comprises fifty cards organised into five ensigns [suits] and three colours: these are gold, bucklers, staves, jewels and arrows (depicted below).

Many of the cards furthermore have conventional titles separate to their rank and ensignβÄîfor example, the deuce of staves is called the Duel, while the sept of jewels is known as the Stars. Over time the artwork on these cards has come to incorporate these thematic elements. In games where some cards trump the rest, the gold ensign typically takes this role, although some more modern games will use the aforementioned "named" cards in this way.

The use of a Maritime deck for divination mirrors many other sources of randomness, although the modern occult practices only date back to the early nineteenth century. They appear to have been popularised by Promethean clubs associated with the Deviance movement, and specifically the storied Winchester group.

Early records of the specific rules of card games using the Maritime deck are unfortunately thin on the ground. The first real description of a particular game appears in 1555 court records regarding the gambling debts of one Marc LevosclinβÄΠ

154.1: The Failed Crusade

notes in translation on the topic of the Failed Crusade.

Y Mordeth Disceint sta un accommission oΟß visant y Marc a Jerusalem reformar.
The Failed Crusade was an undertaking which aimed to recreate the March of Jerusalem.

Y citaΟΑ cosogr sy cercondment au se caut gaΟΑannaΟΑ durant y Preu Mordeth eΟΑ y preu Marchis a Jerusalem, Savino y Just, au acceut d'an 1119, ma l'au cas men a settant annaΟΑ apres.
The city and its surrounds had been captured during the First Crusade and the first Marksman of Jerusalem, Savino the Just, had acceded in 1119, but it had fallen less than seventy years later.

L'oc mordeth sta soluΟΑ d'an 1210 par Pap Just (un prosequent dreit de Segr Savino), eΟΑ i surcou un fracas absoluΟΑ.
This crusade was called in 1210 by Pope Just (a direct descendant of Lord Savino), and it was utterly unsuccessful.

Y campagn farau mal aΟΑief por ig, cas y Convey Australier, y deleΟßon de lorrell'auteΟß sur Port Simon eΟΑ Antioch d'an 1208 es cos usual stimaΟΑ un fallout por y Convey.
The campaign went so badly that the Convoy Australier having their control of Port Simon and Antioch revoked in 1208 is generally regarded as a boon for the Convoy.

Y catastroph militer divers daurn impet a averjon ne Rom eΟΑ allors, a cost l'irrouΟßon dy Preu Gran MorrenΟß (un pest creut uncos ramenaΟΑ vars Europ par mordether retragnt.
The various military catastrophes stoked discontent in Rome and elsewhere, along with the outbreak of the First Great Dying (a plague thought to have been brought to Europe by returning crusaders).

154.2: paon ne PaΟßongr "incongruity"

paon ne PaΟßongr /paΥà…în ne paΥàts…în.grΧ©/ [Υàpaun ne p…êΥàts…î≈΄.g…ê]
- something appearing in a unexpected or jarring context;
- incongruity, anomaly, aberration, a mismatch between expectation and reality

Etymology: idiom from the nineteenth century, literally meaning "a peacock on PaΟßongr (Place)". The noun paon "peacock" descends directly from synonymous pΡ¹v≈ç in Latin. The placename RoΟΑon PaΟßongr, a major intersection in Borland's capital city Damvath, is a borrowing from the Italian name Pazzongolo; this is presumed to reflect in part the uncommon diminutive -unculus in Latin, but the root is of unknown origin.

154.3: gajannar "win"

- win, be awarded, to receive as a prize;
- earn, make, to receive as a wage;
- gain, take, get hold of, to succeed in obtaining;
- conquer, to acquire (territory) by force of arms;
- (dated, slang or poetic) tup, bed, swive, to have sex with

Etymology: presumably (along with noun gajan "victory, triumph") via Old French from a Frankish descendant of Classical German vaethan≈ç "I hunt, pursue". However, the Borlish apparently reflects a Late Latin *vadian≈ç "I win, earn" as opposed to the form *vadani≈ç which must be the etymon for e.g. Tuscan guadagnare "earn" and Norman wain "win". Attested in the last sense from as early as the fourteenth century.

I didn't think the prize was enough.

154.4: mesmerist "straight shooter"

mesmerist /Υ¨m…¦.zmeΥàr…Σst/ [Υ¨m…¦.zm…ΣΥà Ä…Σst]
- truth-teller, straight shooter, plain speaker, someone who is honest and forthright perhaps to the point of bluntness;
- clarifier, demystifier, someone who helps you understand things more clearly;
- realist, pragmatist, someone noted for seeing things as they really are without blinkers of expectation;
- whistleblower, leak, someone who reveals information that powerful parties wanted to keep secret

Etymology: 18th century borrowing from Scholastic Latin mesmerista "truth-teller" and mesmeriz≈ç "I reveal the truth"; this verb comes from synonymous ΈΦΈΒœÉΈΦΈΒœ¹Έ·ΈΕœâ βÄΔ mesmeriz≈ç in Malchassian Greek, a derivative of ΈΦΈΒœÉΈΦΈ≠œ¹ΈΙ βÄΔ mesmΟ©ri "midday; (fig.) clarity, understanding".

Sy sour luΟ·sc tojorn sona de mesmerist.

3s.gn sister play always role of truth.teller
Her sister always tells it like it is.

##

154.5: Northumber

Northumber (Borlish NorΟΑumbr /n…îΥêΥàΟΑ…Σm.brΧ©/) is a polity on the east coast of Albion which borders Markland, Dunclothe and Scotland. Its largest cities are Yorwick (the capital) and Edinbrough.

History

Originally a pair of English kingdoms settled through the fifth and sixth centuries, Northumber was invaded and conquered from the late ninth century onwards by forces from Denmark and Horther. The city of Yorwick is even alleged to have practically given itself over to Dane rule, deposing the alderman and hoping to offer enough loot freely to the invaders so as to avoid the total destruction of the city.

Relations with neighbouring Markland were fraught until the tenth-century Elved Marriage between a Merch princess and the king of Northumber, which ended direct hostilities for nearly a century. Wars continued however with its other neighbours; for example, Northumber captured the city of Edinbrough (then called Eidin) from Dunclothe in 928, taking the last vestiges of Dunclothe's eastern coast.

By the end of the tenth century the territory of Northumber was united under Dane rule from Yorwick. With the waning of the Dane Supremacy the ability of Horther to project its rule overseas collapse; in Albion, Northumber became first among equals in a confederation of the local Dane kingdoms, including Dunclothe, Sodrick and Dublin. At this point Yorwick was by some way the richest city in Albion.

In the eleventh and twelfth centuries, Northumber became the northernmost vassal of the First Drengot Empire, which ruled over much of Albion and most of Gaul.

People

Wolfstan II (fl. c9), last English monarch of Northumber before it fell to Dane forces.

##

155.1: Wings Afire

notes in translation on the opera Wings Afire (Ale in Fiamme).

Al Igne es un melodram compos par y Venijan Chiarina Bruno e preu parformaΟΑ nell'Arena de Verona d'an 1824.
Wings Afire is an opera written by Venisian Chiarina Bruno and first performed in the Verona Arena in 1824.

Caye l'ouvr primer scenaΟΑ nell'Arena de Verona posc i dene closiΟΑ con y sounΟß dy Var Theudesc Dovem.
It was the first work staged in the Verona Arena since it was shuttered at the outbreak of the Second German War.

Y melodram es un renarranΟß dy legend d'Icar oc loy fignt cos enter apres dy cainΟß ny mythos, ant soumoin jait vars Icar mort ny collocq.
The opera is a retelling of the legend of Icarus which takes place entirely after the events of the myth, with references to the dead Icarus in the dialogue.

Entarn scey y matron d'un vessonman acconnosc ayent a DΟΠdal gemiscent; i l'aΟ·ΟΑ a y gavisc d'orΟΑenjar redescovrir.
It follows the matron of a clothiery who meets a grieving Daedalus; she helps him to rediscover the joy of invention.

Y music dy melodram es abrangent dy melody mogn e dolorous dy viol cemtir des cont a pouvr Borallesc; rumoraΟΑ es ig Bruno au parol oΟ·ΟΑ den de sy fraΟΑr Jacomo.
The opera's music features the low-pitched mournful melodies of the graveyard fiddlers from Borlish folklore; Bruno is said to have heard of them from her brother Jacomo.

155.5: Everywhere and Everything

excerpt from popular work of nonfiction Everywhere and Everything: the Third Millenium, published in April 2000 (originally in Leon as Jallondes y Jallisca: la TerΟßa Milada) by Justino Silvera at the request of his publishers. The book purports to contain two thousand pieces of unusual or obscure trivia about the world; while most are accurate, the rushed research process led to several inaccuracies and a few flat-out lies finding their way into the book.

βÄΠthreshold force mill [nuclear power plant] in the Groin [A CoruΟ±a].

Due to the region's janual religious history, the archbishop of Waduz [Vaduz, Liechtenstein] (which in laic terms is a minor city in Bavarn) is the only Christian official who can vote as a cardinal in the conclaves for electing both the Augsburgher and the Roman Popes. Fortunately, conclaves have never been called simultaneously for both PopesβÄînobody knows how the archbishop of Waduz would attend both conclaves in such a situation.

The tabre unit of heft, equal to one hundred (dime) ounces [~2.7kg, ~6lb] and first introduced in its modern form with the rest of the dime system in the 1820s, is named after Tabres' batman (Persian man tabrΟ©z), the principal commercial weight used in the Persian city of Tabres [Tabriz] and its surrounds. It is related to traditional units used across the Vetomund from Kiew [Kyiv] to Madray [Madurai], which vary widely from one-half a tabre to over thirty tabres.

The Draconic Age is now generally agreed to have ended abruptly sixty-six hundred myriad years ago, when a cisjovial sax [asteroid within the orbit of Jupiter] over three leagues acrossβÄΠ

156.1: A Parody of Literature

    excerpt in translation from the 1990 short story The Littlest Things We Know to be Small (originally in Vascon as Los Mens que Sems come Pochins) by Anna Rocamora. It parodied the overelaborate, overspecific style that had become popular in the regional highbrow literary scene in recent years and almost singlehandedly changed publishing trends overnight.

Mell'inquillin e mey entrquΟ·sceurn a plaΟß meg dy torn ag droccon por aug libar e gostaΟßon pascr des y plat a lox.
My roommate and I paused mid-lap on the driveway to sip water and nibble from the tray of pizza slices.

Caye l'yeul un marrenquon harif eΟΑ affrous de ciscr, hurlanΟß e slenget occajonal entr groun abrevaΟΑ.
The street was a weird, scary nightmare of squeals, shrieks and occasional snogs between tipsy teens.

Un camil vabian closnautr passau clacquant. Un connart magrastr quidam craccau un mot all'orlaΟß groinant, senglottaΟß e glouΟß indugnt de sbayour.
A battered jeep rattled by. Some scrawny bastard spat a wad onto the kerb with a grunt, prompting whines and giggles from bystanders.

PieΟß pas ados, un frot flasq a cedar sonau cant lamp a xanthal cardaurn y teneur.
A few footsteps behind, a pathetic guitar strum sounded as neon lights teased the darkness.

Jo senteu un stig leger ag ventr. "Or bon, tenau a traiscnar," murmurau mell'amig cos ouveg, cur tornant deur y stril gapiscent de fren.
I felt a slight pang in my gut. "Alright, letβÄôs shuffle on," my friend mumbled with a pout, ignoring the intermittent squeals of brakes.

N'oc hour surrun, jo vole eir por y salonnour nell'avoccoir destarrir e por l'entrou pondr dell'oΟß diabr de rumour cas cahut.
Sometime soon, IβÄôd go to relieve the receptionist at the rec centre and inquire about that damned dorm rumour.

- a particular gambling card game, not dissimilar to bezique or pinochle, which originated in Bengal and became popular internationally during the nineteenth century

In the second sense, the word is a calque from Bengal bonoro "pangolin; bezique", apparently named for a deck which featured art of a pangolin on some of the cards.

156.3: vajot "perk"

vajot /vaΥà £…ît/ [v…êΥà ù…ît]
- benefit, advantage, something in one's circumstances that is helpful;
- perk, bonus, any incidental benefit that one receives;
- (archaic) wage, profit, salary, money or other compensation received for work

Etymology: originally in the last senses, from a diminutive of Old Borlish uai "legal contract; salary, wage", from synonymous wadium in Medieval Latin (compare gage in Old French). This comes from Frankish wadi "stake, pledge", which is related to English "wedding". The word was displaced in its original sense by parsou "wage, rate; worth, value".

Y masctig trovar sta un vajot imprefait.
/i maxΥàtai troΥàvar sta …Σn vaΥà £…ît Υ¨…Σm.preΥàfet/

156.4: droccon "driveway"

droccon /dr…îΥàk…în/ [d Ä äΥàk…în]
- lay-by, an indentation in the side of a road for vehicles to stop in;
- parking space, berth, an area where a vehicle is intended to be left;
- driveway, a private area at the front of a dwelling where one might keep one's vehicles

Etymology: twentieth century, a noun formation from verb droccar "notch, indent" calqued from dentelon "lay-by" in Parisian French. The verb comes from droc "notch, indentation", which is of obscure origin. Putative connections with English trough or Welsh rhych "furrow" have too many issues to be workable, and nobody credits a link with draucus "strongman" in Latin; perhaps the word (which is first attested in the Old Borlish period) is simply onomatopoeia.

Cayen deg minut pre un vec alandau ny droccon.
/keΥàj…¦n dij miΥàn…Σt pre …Σn v…¦k Υ¨a.lanΥàdo ni dr…îΥàk…în/
[k…ΣΥà ù…¦n di m…ΣΥàn…Σt pre …Σ…± v…¦k Υ¨a.l…ênΥàdo ni d Ä äΥàk…în]
fall-ipf-3p ten minute before indf bus arrive-pst in-df lay.by
It was ten minutes before a bus pulled into the lay-by.

##

156.5: Through the Ages

collected notes on a variety of background figures in Boralverse history, listed in chronological order.

Franco Cicala was the console of the maritime republic Jeans [Genoa] from 1119 until either 1122 or 1124 (records differ). The console being an elected position voted on by the local nobility, Franco would be followed intermittently by over a dozen consoli from among his descendants.

Andrew II was a martial-minded king of Markland in the early fourteenth century. He succeeded his uncle, who is remembered as being far more diplomatic. Andrew II oversaw the Sack of Rexam in 1301, an act which reignited the on-again off-again conflict between Markland and Wales known as the Mallor Wars. The original medieval building at St Brigid's Abbey was razed during this offensive.

Dewock Barclythe (1452-1539 N) was the Friar of Tremonnow and for several decades the prime Factor of Records at the Brethin House there. Records of his correspondence with clergy and academics across western Europe have survived to the present day. These include some of the earliest attestations of dart [vector] geometry, and thus the beginnings of the unification of geometry and computation.

Lord Simon, the sixth Baron MonΟßating (b. 1770) was an intellectual, a notorious libertine of the Damvath melee, and an early proponent of Deviance Theology in Borland. He was of Warnine [GuaranΟ≠] descent through his mother. His family had been notorious for decades due to a protracted legal battle between his father and second cousin over the Barony of Haubrath. By the age of eighteen Simon could speak Latin, Dutch (and according to his defamers, Warnine), and he could play the clavier and the torriot [a bassoon-like wind instrument].

Marcio Dourao was an author and rabblerouser best remembered for his work Galatheus, or the Knowledge of Good and Evil. This collection of short stories pastiching Greek mythology was written in 1930 during the decade-long Portingale Anarchy. The title references the story of Galatea and Pygmalion. Galatheus was banned in neighbouring Vascony and Morrack for its background politics and, unofficially, for its throughline of gender exploration; neither polity had quite forgotten their roles in the traditionally-minded Modest Arrangement of the previous century.

##

157.1: Typewriters

notes in translation on the early development of tachygraph [typewriter] technology.

Tachygraph primal de dezein 1830 e 40 stan fatuous cos imman, dec y majortaΟΑ a appragl no poΟΑen for lettr inscaur con un mendranΟß ag hast de lettranΟß a man doit.
Early tachygraphs in the 1830s and 40s were extremely cumbersome, with most models only capable of setting type at a fraction of the speed of practised handwriting.

Y Tachygraph d'Armanbourg, oc stant accatabr ne Pologn des an 1842, surcayen impernix ne benoΟß aΟΑief por se cavir narreyaΟΑ ny fougl pavarrot sou nom dy Bradygraph (a verb y "scrittour lent")
The Armanburg Tachygraph, which was commercially available in Poland from 1842, was so awkward to use that it was lampooned in the bit-sheets as the Bradygraph (literally the "slow writer").

Ceu incommorant destr, sceyen gimmolt vars poΟΑir tachygraph ligar ag sar campaner, a fin oc se sceus un pistol coutoccar sur papir e lor cos simoltane un facsimile dy pistol boΟΑar vars un autr hom.
Since very early on, attempts have been made to connect tachygraphs to the steeplemesh, so that one could type a message onto paper and simultaneously transmit a copy of the message to someone else.

Y preu tachygraph poΟΑent cos particuler pistol boΟΑar par y sar campaner sta y Teleclavia de Vilmanas, oc orΟΑenjaΟΑ ne Lithou par Toman Vilmanas d'an 1859. 
The first tachygraph specifically able to send messages on the steeplemesh was the Vilmanas Teleclavia, designed in Lithow by Tomas Vilmanas in 1859.

Sy boΟΑanΟß primer er conveyaΟΑ sull'oΟß Sant MichΟΠl vars sell'amig Margreta Legaite ne Tvaniste.
Its first message was sent on Michaelmas of that year to his friend Margreta Legaite in Tvaniste.

##

157.2: libar "quaff"

libar /liΥàbar/ [l…ΣΥàb…êΥê]
- sip, nurse, to drink slowly;
- quaff, imbibe, swig, to drink a mouthful of;
- absorb, take up, suck up, (of a substance or object) to take in (a fluid);
- take in, contemplate, appreciate, to consider or to enjoy experiencing;
- (uncommon, religion) to offer a libation, to pour out a liquid as a sacrifice to a deity

Etymology: borrowed from Latin lΡΪb≈ç "I taste, sip; I offer a libation". Extension to metaphorical drinking is attested from the eighteenth century. In the last sense, a backformation or reborrowing from Latin which is much less common than the synonymous phrase libaΟßon fair "to offer a libation".

Y haprman libau sy bier e reuloy a racont fair.
/i Υ¨haprΧ©Υàman liΥàbo si bj…¦r e rauΥàl…îi a raΥàk…înt fer/
[i Υ¨ha.p…êΥàman l…ΣΥàbo si bj…¦ΥêβÄΩ Äe  Ä…êuΥàl…îi a  Ä…êΥàk…înt feΥê]
df grocer sip-pst 3s.gn beer and come.back-pst to tale do.inf
The grocer quaffed his lager and kept talking.

157.4: harif "weird"

harif /haΥàr…Σf/ [h…êΥà Ä…Σf]
- weird, eldritch, uncanny, off, unsettling in a mysterious way;
- unexpected, surprising, unusual, not having been anticipated;
- grotesque, distorted, misshapen, having a wrong or abnormal form;
- (in parachthon works) fey, supernatural, ethereal, mystical, belonging to beings or powers that are not of this world

Etymology: borrowed in the late Middle Borlish period from Vascon harif (cf. Leon or Portingale harivo) "misshapen, grotesque; weird, uncanny". This in turn comes from Arabic Ί°ΌéΊ±ΌêΊ® βÄΔ kharib "ruined, destroyed". The term gained its conventionalised usage in speculative fiction in the late nineteenth century, popularised by the works of Nan.

Jo parceveu my cigl er se portant cos harif.

I noticed my beau was acting weird.

##

157.5: Painkiller Philology

notes on the chemistry and linguistics of the painkiller cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine [ibuprofen].

Cozebdo-diamaschal benjamine (as a medicine sold under several names, including "CD" or "Tommy pills") is a hebetant [painkilling] substance developed in the early twentieth century. It was the result of collaboration between the Branwen Sanatory in New Leudong [south-eastern Australia] and the University of Rimack [Lima] in Tavancy [descendant state of the Incan Empire], which sought to develop hebetants with faster action and milder adversions [side effects]. It was first synthesised in 1932, and a decade later it had surpassed salix derivatives [e.g. aspirin] in sales worldwide, due to providing better pain relief (as opposed to merely reducing the symptoms of fever).

Name

The name of this substance comprises three parts.

The core of the substance is a benjamine [benzene] ring; the name "benjamine" is borrowed via Scholastic Latin and Vascon from Arabic Ό³ΌèΊ®ΌéΊßΌÜ Ί§ΌéΊßΌàΌêΌäΌëβÄé βÄΔ lubΡ¹n jΡ¹wiyy, literally "Jawan [Javanese] frankincense", corrupted at some point by the name Benjamin.

The first of the extensions in this substance is a cozebdone branch [isobutyl]. Cozebdone spirit [isobutane] is a rearrangement of the linear zebdone spirit [butane], a colourless and flammable air with a spine of four coke [carbon] atoms. The root zebdo- "four colk atoms" derives from Cairene Arabic Ί≤Ί®Ί·Ί© βÄΔ zΟ©bda "butter", first used when zebdone acid [butyric acid] was isolated from milk fat.

The second of the extensions is located directly opposite the first, as indicated by the prefix dia- "across, over", from Greek. It consists of a maschale [propionic acid] branch, which is the acid oil with a spine of length three, a malodorous clear liquid. The substance was named in 1850 after the Ancient Greek ΈΦαΨΑœÉœ΅αΨΑΧ¹ΈΜΈΖ βÄΔ maschΟΓlΡ™ "armpit", since it was isolated from human sweat excreted by skin zoia [microorganisms].

##

158.1: Medieval Provence

notes in translation on the territory of Provence in the medieval period.

ProvenΟß MitaΟΑ-Asc sta un vassal sou massem possenΟß divers tras un millenner pres.
Medieval Provence was a vassal of various greater powers for almost a millennium.

I denoy incorporaΟΑ nell'Empir FranΟßan durant sy spanjon ny siecr seg eΟΑ i parmaneu com un sodal de sy ronΟß ovester posc ig l'empir dehisceu a dou part ny siecr nouf.
It was incorporated into the expanding Francian Empire in the sixth century and remained part of its western fragment after the empire was divided in two in the ninth.

Pre y siecr onΟß primal y region er denuΟΑ autonom majortaΟΑer, ma l'er concas alcheΟΑr oc surcaint y kynous final por y Preu Empir DrengoΟßan intermanir.
By the early eleventh century it had become mostly autonomous, but it was conquered once more as the last piece of the First Drengot Empire.

Posc l'UrgenΟß a Dijon d'an 1170, y Reyaum a Borgogn fey exeunt des auteΟß DrengoΟßan, y comtaΟΑ a ProvenΟß apportant galaΟΑ.
After the 1170 Emergency at Dijon, the Kingdom of Burgundy left Drengot control, taking the country of Provence with it.

ProvenΟß er extolliΟΑ an ducaΟΑ ny siecr treΟß oc gournaΟΑ par y linaΟß de Savoy, e ne sona dy conjogntur direct entr y citaΟΑ caver a Genef e l'entertaΟΑ dy commarΟß maritim ny Miterran, i receveu y dob 'y paner Borgond'. 
Provence was elevated to a duchy in the thirteenth century under the house of Savoy, and as the direct link between the capital at Geneva and all of Middlesea sea-trade, it became known as the 'Burgund breadbasket'.

158.3: ciscr "squeal"

ciscr /ts…ΣxrΧ©/ [Υàts…Σ.x…ê]
- squeal, screech, shriek, a strident high-pitched sound as may be caused by friction;
- a loud, high-pitched animal cry;
- scream, yelp, a high-pitched vocalisaton due to fright or surprise

Etymology: attested in Old Borlish ciscle "shriek", a deverbal from cisclar "to shriek, scream" (now obsoleted by ciscr fair "to do a shriek"). This descends from a synonymous cistul≈ç in Vulgar Latin reflected also in Vascon chillar "creak" and Parisian gicler "spurt". In turn this is presumed to be an alteration of Classical Latin fistul≈ç "I whistle, play the flute" via assimilation or sound symbolism.

Y belem ny forest se mis afaΟß con ec ciscr d'aucel.
/i beΥàl…¦m ny foΥàr…¦st se m…Σz aΥàfats k…în …¦k ts…ΣxrΧ© doΥàts…¦l/
[o b…ΣΥàl…¦m ni f äΥà Ä…¦st se m…Σz …êΥàfas k…în …¦k Υàts…Σ.x…ê d äΥàts…¦l]
df scene in-df forest rfl put.pst forward with p.px squeal of=bird
The forest scene opened with shrieks from birds.

158.4: slengettar "snog"

slengettar /Υ¨sl…¦n.g…¦Υàtar/ [Υ¨zl…¦≈΄.g…ΣΥàt…êΥê]
- snog, pash, make out, French kiss, to kiss passionately (especially with tongue);
- mangle, botch, fumble, to do something sloppily;
- overegg, splurge, slather, to do something overenthusiastically;
- meddle, pry, butt in, overstep, badger, to be overly familiar or intrusive into personal or private matters

Etymology: originally in the first senses, attested along with the related noun slenget "snog, make-out session" in the late eighteenth century. It applies the somewhat pejorative verbal derivation s- -ar and the diminutive suffix -et to the root lenc "tongue" < Latin lingua "tongue". The first metaphorical extensions are each attested from the early twentieth century; in the sense of "meddle" it is an innovation of the last couple of decades.

Nos slengettaum pegnt gauvn sull'ec stig de hey.

We slathered yellow paint onto the fenceposts.

##

158.5: Roun

Roun (Norman, Kentish / ÄaΧÉwΧÉ/) is a port on the Seine river and the capital city of Kent.
History

Founded by the Gauls, the city was known to the Romans as Rotomagus and it became the seat of the local bishopric in the early medieval period. The ninth century saw the incursion of the Normans into the region as part of the wider Dane Supremacy; by 850 Roun was under their control. By the eleventh century Roun had become the seat of the Drengot family, ruling the Duchy of Normandy as a semi-autonomous vassal of the Kingdom of the Franks (the western fragment of the erstwhile Francian Empire).

After the Kernovan Conjuring and the formation of the First Drengot Empire covering much of Albion [Great Britain] and most of Gaul [France], Roun was sidelined. The Drengot Emperors moved their court to Paris, although they continued to personally own the duchies of Normandy and Kent. This period marks the beginning of the persistent political union of these two regions on either side of the English Channel.

One of the successor states to the First Drengot Empire was this united Norman Kent, ruled from Roun. King Vincent II of Kent, who ruled in the mid-fifteenth century, is remembered for the expansion of the city keep and for his twin sons (the "Two Henries") who ruled jointly after his death. During this period Roun French developed fully as one standard written form of the language (as opposed to Parisian or Burgund French).

Roun was the administrative centre of the Drengot Collusion from the nineteenth century until its collapse in 1961. The city housed the "Red House", the headquarters of Collusion's Ministry for Information.

Authors
    Mark Deuthex (fl. 1973), whose spycraft tale We Wake to Broken Windows draws on his own experiences in the Red House.
    Josephene Foilliers (fl. 1913), writer of the first work of fiction to sell over a million copies in a single year.
    Ashford Pasquier (fl. 1968), inventor of the term "pistol mesh" in his parachthon work Starsail.

##

159.1: Furlain

notes in translation on the polity of Furlain [Friuli].

Durant y Scon Tetrarchy ny siecr onΟß e doΟß, y Marc a Furlagn intermane y porΟßon y souΟΑeressem nell'Empir Theudesc, cos primer un vassal imperial prose eΟΑ aval sousunt cos particuler ny paramontaΟΑ dy Reyaum a Bavarn.
During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries, the March of Furlain was the southernmost part of the German Empire, at first a direct imperial vassal and later specifically consolidated under the suzerainty of the Kingdom of Bavarn.

Posc y dissoluΟßon dell'Empire Theudesc ny siecr treΟß, y compeΟΑour y frequendessem de Furlagn denoy y Republic a Venis.
After the collapse of the German Empire in the thirteenth century, Furlain's most frequent rival became the Republic of Venise.

Furlagn soffreu un quagment gra d'an 1348 oc ayent y castel e cathedral y dou devastaΟΑ n'Odin. 
Furlain suffered a severe earthquake in 1348 which destroyed both the castle and cathedral in Odin.

L'eutaΟΑ por voinaΟßal cas accostur femel (oc y vesson mascr dy miliΟß emprontant) inceveu ne Furlagn durant y PaΟß Lonc ny siecr ogtoΟß nuverrem.
The armourcloth trend in women's fashion (which emulated men's military dress) began in Furlain during the Long Peace in the late eighteenth century.

Y dob vecin (des voina, un vanagl por un soldart) se cof scavlaΟΑ n'autr lengaΟß plusour por l'oc model stikettar.
The local term (from voina, a colloquialism for a soldier) was borrowed into several other languages to describe the style.

- well-timed, timely, seasonable, opportune, taking place at the correct moment;

159.3: groun "teen"

groun /grun/ [g Äun]
- a member of a countercultural and rowdy youth movement in the latter days of the Drengot Collusion;
- (colloquial) teen, teenager, adolescent, youth, a young person in the years around the age of majority;
- rascal, hooligan, brat, scamp, rebel, an rowdy and usually immature person;
- roughcut, sketch, work-in-progress, stub, an incomplete item or piece of work;

Etymology: borrowed with the first sense in the mid-twentieth century from Willem Dutch grΟ≥ng "adolescence; unruly young person", cognate with growing in English. Applied to all youths of a similar age since the 1970s, the word quickly also became a mild insult describing stereotypical teen behaviours. In the final senses it is only attested in the last twenty years.

159.4: abrevar "water"

abrevar /Υ¨a.breΥàvar/ [Υ¨a.b Ä…ΣΥàv…êΥê]
- water, irrigate, to pour water over in order to help grow;
- wet, soak, drench, moisten, to cause to be wet;
- slake, quench, to satisfy the thirst of;
- to supply with (usually alcoholic) drinks;
- schmooze, butter up, chat up, to curry favour with

This is a metathetic variant of abevrer, which descends from Vulgar Latin abbiber≈ç "I water" (which the Old Borlish may alternatively reflect directly), a derivative of Classical bib≈ç "I drink". The word's association with alcohol and social gatherings shows influence from slang term abrevaΟΑ "tipsy, squiffy, somewhat drunk", literally "watered".

##

159.5: Cambrick Codices

summaries of two twentieth-century works of writing which were originally written in one or another standard of the Welsh language.

Children of the Battle of Rexam [Wrexham, 1] is a sensation trevold [hit novel] written by Jon Paldreth and published in 1931. It is a fictionalised account of the orphaned children of the Duke of Chester in the decades following his death at the 1483 Battle of Rexam. By Paldreth's account, the children met practically every famous personage in contemporary western Europe, from General daus Sanz to King Andrew of Markland (in fairness, the eldest child was indeed briefly a hostage of the then-prince Andrew). Moreover, Paldreth contrives to have the children present at almost every significant historical event of the time; unlikely as this all is, the book has enjoyed much use in school history lessons as an approachable introduction to the politics of the time.

---

The Grimmer Hall [2] is a scholastic reference quire about developments in early methodics [programming]. It was published in 1999 and written by New Leudong methodist Cai Sonquo during his tenure at the University of Mehannin [Brisbane]. The work covers the use of algorithms from antiquity (for example Euclid's method for finding coarsest division), through the early modern period (e.g. the waveform display [Fourier transform] and its application to astronomy), and into the titular "grimmer halls" (rooms of many peopleβÄîusually womenβÄîperforming calculations longhand) of the early twentieth century. The work ends its account just before the invention of the entirely mechanical general-purpose grimmer [computer].

[1] released in Merch as Chulder oth Feight at Rexam /Υàt É äl.d…ôβÄΩ…Ψ äΟΑ f…¦ Ét …ôt Υà…Ψ…¦k.s…ôm/, and originally published in Welsh as Pa Οêywdan y Plant Car Vantel /pa ΟΑ…ôuΥàd…în …ô plant kaΥê v…ônΥàt…¦…§/ "What the Children of Rexam Said"

[2] originally in Welsh as ≈¹is y GrimrageΟΑ /…§iΥês …ô Υ¨g…Ψ…Σm.…Ψ…ôΥàg…¦ΟΑ/ "The Computing-Women's Hall".

Theo Carin er caut sou siesc par livr.

160.2: camul "jeep"

- jeep, off-roader, SUV, a small military-style vehicle suited to rough terrain;
- (attributive) rugged, weatherproofed, hardened, fortified, treated or designed so as to resist the elements

Etymology: mid-twentieth century, genericised from Drengot military jargon but of uncertain further origin. Possibly an alteration of camel "camel" in reference to transporting soldiers and materiel; perhaps based on Latin cham≈Ϊlcus "a kind of chariot". 160.3: magrastr "scrawny"

magrastr /m…¦iΥàrastrΧ©/ [m…ΣjΥà Äa.st…ê]
- scrawny, lean, gangly, lanky, weedy, being notably unmuscular;
- malnourished, gaunt, underweight, unheathily thin as from illness or hunger;
- flimsy, spindly, rickety, not held together securely and liable to break or fall apart;
- unsound, tenuous, sketchy, (of an idea) not convincing due to a lack of solid argumentation

Etymology: early Modern formation using the pejorative suffix -astr < Latin -aster "incomplete resemblance to" from adjective magr "limited, scarce, meagre". This descends directly via Old Borlish ma»ùre /Υàma…Θ.r…ô/ from Latin macer "lean, skinny". The word is attested in metaphorical use from the nineteenth century.

##

160.5: Diversions and Dilettantes

notes on various features and historical figures beginning with a common pair of letters.

Diana Theodora Queiroz (fl. 1810) was a writer and polemicist from Vascony who was primarily active during the Long Peace period. She was a major figure in opposition to Deviance theology and an ally of the Modest Arrangement, hewing especially closely to the latter's emphasis on morality and familial obligation. Her views have seen her cast as a precursor to the Blue Marian movement, which sought to advance the status of women within a highly religious and stratified framework.

A dice is a value or score of ten in a game or sport. For example, the Maritime deck contains cards like "the dice of staves" or "the dice of gold", and the chances of getting a dice when rolling a pair of dice are one in twelve. This term, along with its cohort [1], were borrowed into the English languages from the Norman names for numerals during the First Drengot Empire in the early second millennium. In certain sports, including lineball, the most points one can score at once is ten; because of this, the word "dice" appears in some idioms meaning "sudden good fortune" or "an impressive feat".

Dimus [Phobos] is the larger of the two moons of Mars. It was discovered, along with the smaller moon Phobus [Deimos], in 1869 by Mashick astronomer Coscatl bint InΟΓm; she also discovered the eighth planet of Janus [Neptune]. Following the custom set by the four great jovial moons, she named the pair after the twin sons of Mars, who personify dread and panic respectively.

Dion Becolla (fl. 1968) was a Borlish author who grew up in the industrial city of Neuvort. She wrote several trevolds [novels] during the 1960s which capitalised on the so-called General Italian Fervour of the period. Her most famous work is the period piece Waging World Peace [2], set during the 1880s in the midst of the Good Game period and owing much to the masquira genre and its hidden identities, high society and complicated schemes.

[1] in full: ace, duce, trey, cater, cink, sice, sept, hute, nuve and dice [Merch /…ôis, dj…ôus, t…Ψ…ôi, k…¦Υêt…ô, s…Σ≈΄k, s…ôis, s…¦t, hj…ôut, nj…ôuv, d…ôis/].

[2] Borlish PaΟß Mondial Accommettent /pats m…înΥàdjal Υ¨a.k…îΥàm…¦.t…¦nt/.

161.1: Many Happy Returns

excerpt in translation from a conversation on the streets of Manster township of Utter Safford, Markland [1], on Friday the 13th of March 2020.

"Ou ave, Ben! Jo n'ay te vis posc larc semmanβÄîTorn all'An Allagr! Increibr no's y temp ou nos caint cos nuvr?"

161.2: sbayour "bystander"

sbayour /sb…¦Υàjur/ [zb…ΣΥà ù äΥê]
- bystander, onlooker, witness, spectator, rubbernecker, a person present at but not involved in some event;
- voyeur, peeping Tom, someone viewing a scene from hiding for their own  (often erotic) pleasure;
- gossip, busybody, quidnunc, nosy parker, somebody considered to have an inappropriate interest in others' affairs

Etymology: first seen with the later senses in the nineteenth century, agentive derivation from sbayar "gawp, goggle". This reflects Medieval Latin exbad≈ç "I gape", from early slang verb bad≈ç "I yawn". Originally the term was exclusive negative or insulting; by the early twentieth century more neutral usage begins to be attested.

ToΟΑ gent saun ig cay ty morrig un sbayour jaΟßol.

161.3: senglottaΟß "whine"

senglottaΟß /Υ¨s…¦n.gl…îΥàtats/ [Υ¨s…¦≈΄.gl äΥàtats]
- whine, whinge, moan, a noise of complaint or pain;
- gripe, grievance, cavil, a petty or trivial objection or criticism;
- any long, drawn-out, high-pitched sound (especially one which is annoying)

Etymology: nineteenth century derivative from senglot or the related verb senglottar "sob, sigh, lament". These descend from Vulgar Latin singluttus "sob, hiccup", a alteration of synoynmous singultus in Classical Latin under the influence of glutti≈ç "I swallow, gulp". Metaphorical use is attested from the early twentieth century.

L'er resoluΟΑ a casc senglottaΟß ignorar.

3s=cop.ipf resolve-p.pst at each whine ignore-inf
He was bent on ignoring any criticism.

- leisure, diversion, recreation, time off from work doing activities for their own sake;
- fun, entertainment, amusement, activity designed to give pleasure or relaxation to their audience or participants;

Etymology: nominal derivation from avoccar "divert, amuse, entertain" or directly from Latin Ρ¹vocΡ¹ti≈ç "distraction", both originally from Latin Ρ¹voc≈ç "I call away, distract, dissuade". The meaning was influenced by contrast to its traditional counterpart voccaΟßon "career, occupation".

Sceye all'oΟß asc pog avoccaΟßon a phasm.

##

161.5: Dunclothe

Dunclothe (Merch /Υàd ä≈΄.kluΥêΟΑ/, Welsh Dincluth /d…Σ≈΄ΥàklyΥêΈΗ/) is a political and cultural region of western Albion, located south of Scotland and north of Markland. From the Clothe river valley it extends down along the coast to the Bay of Morcambe.

Toponomy

Dunclothe takes its name from the early medieval petty kingdom known alternately as Alt Clud, Strad Clud, or Din Clud; these are ancient British or Guile phrases meaning "cliff, valley or fort of the river Clothe". It is uncertain whether the Dun- element seen in English names for the region reflects Old Irish dΟΚn "fort" or Old English dΟΚn "hill".

History

The British territory of Dunclothe was partially conquered settled by Dane invaders during the ninth century. However, the region was not subsumed into the Dane Supremacy as fully as neighbouring Sodrick and Northumber to the west and east respectively.

Dunclothe appears to have controlled the fort at Eidin (modern Edinbrough) until it was sacked by Northumber in 928. Meanwhile Sodrick's power was waning; under King Roderick, Dunclothe conquered the isles of Arran and Mann by 942. The bride of King Radulf of Northumber (r. 951βÄ™), one Atre of Cheswic, may perhaps be one of King Roderick's daughters.

In the early eleventh century, Dunclothe is part of a confederation of Dane Kingdoms. However, this would shortly collapse with the rapid expansion of the First Drengot Empire to the south, which incorporated Northumber along with most of southern Albion. This period sees Dunclothe grow closer to newly-united Wales, allies in opposition to Drengot power.

In the fourteenth and fifteenth centuries, Dunclothe-led fisheries in the North Atlantic were a major exporter of dried and/or cured cod, selling it via the meshes of Borland and Vascony as far afield as the Eastern Middlesea. This trade was a notable source of specie for the vicinity.

The Kingdom of Britain was founded in the fifteenth century, formalising the suzerainty of Wales over the tallaths of Devon and Dunclothe.

162.1: Raymond al Mustafa

collected notes in translation on Raymond al Mustafa.

Raymond al Moustafa sta un mathematorg e philosoph veunt a plaΟß meg ny siecr ogtoΟß.
Raymond al Mustafa was a mathematician and philosopher in the mid-eighteenth century. 

I reseyeu lonc un mesur ne Barcelon sou y patronnement dy Duc a Aragon.
He was resident for some time in Barcelon under the Duke of Aragon's patronage.

Durant l'oc epoc i corresponoy retrasiscent dy theorist soicer Rachel Desselut, e lorry pistol sovemblaurn certan entrell'idea volent tandem y sce de methodic signfeyar.
During this time he corresponded with fellow theorist Rachel Desselut, and their letters outlined some of the concepts that would go on to define the field of methodics [programming].

I passau sell'annaΟΑ aval ne sojornaΟßon, souseyent cos final ne Brasil, no stant ancour un part dy polity d'Ambrosia.
He spent his latter years travelling, eventually settling in Brasil [Newfoundland], which was not yet part of the polity of Ambrosia.

Sy granfigl Fabian MostΟ†f denoy un astronom parnommaΟΑ all'Observatoir a Paratzon, se sarvent de lodrignaΟΑ mathematic ec defassaΟΑ cos parΟßal par Raymond (com y despley ondatim por gyr celest augrimar).
His grandson Fabian MostΟ†f became an eminent astronomer at the Paratzon Observatory, using mathematical techniques developed in part by Raymond (like the waveform display to compute celestial orbits).

162.2: glouΟß "giggle"

glouΟß /gluts/ [gluts]
- cluck, gobble, the sound made by poultry;
- giggle, snicker, titter, a high-pitched and silly sort of laugh;
- (interj.) pah, pshaw, faugh, a noise of derision or incredulity

Etymology: Middle Borlish deverbal from now-obsolete gloΟßar "cluck; chuckle" (now usually glouΟß fair "to do giggles"). This reflects Vulgar Latin gl≈çciΡ¹re "to cluck", a regularised conjugation class variant of synonymous gl≈çcΡΪre in Classical Latin. As an interjection the word is attested from the eighteenth century and is now somewhat old-fashioned or facetious.

Jo parmanoy a hoissar, y glouΟß seponent.

162.3: ΟΑesmbal "bombshell"

ΟΑesmbal /ΟΑ…¦zmΧ©Υàbal/ [Υ¨ΟΑ…¦.z…êmΥàbal]
- a sport with similarities to dodgeball in which players aim to throw a ball so it hits their opponents;
- dodgeball, the ball used in this sport;
- surprise, bombshell, bolt from the blue, an unexpected event or arrival that is felt to come out of nowhere;
- inspiration, flash of insight, a sudden realisation or deduction

Etymology: verb-noun compound formed in the twentieth century by analogy to earlier analogous words like caugtrbal "football", lit. "kick-ball". The noun bal "ball" comes from a variety of Germanic sources and potentially via Medieval LatinβÄîcf. Old English beall, Old Norrish bΟ≤llr, Frankish bal "ball". The verb ΟΑesmar "aim, target, direct" reflects Old Borlish ΟΑestmar "work out, consider, turn one's attention to", which descends from an extension of Classical Latin ΟΠstim≈ç "I determine the worth of, I value"; thus, ΟΑesmar is a doublet of semi-learned stimar "consider, deem". The word has been used metaphorically for unexpected things since the 1990s.

All'oc ostendour fey jougl y ΟΑesmbal d'Apollon.
/a.l…îk Υ¨…î.st…¦nΥàdur fi  £uil i ΟΑ…¦zmΧ©Υàbal Υ¨da.p…îΥàl…în/
[Υ¨a.l äβÄΩΥ¨g…î.st…ΣnΥàd äΥê fi  ùw…Σl i Υ¨ΟΑ…¦.z…êmΥàbal Υ¨da.p äΥàl…în]
to-df=s.px blogger do.pst KO df dodgeball of=Apollo
This blogger unexpectedly predicted the future.

162.4: frottar "strum"

frottar /fr…îΥàtar/ [f Ä äΥàt…êΥê]
- rub, chafe, scour, scrub, scrap, to move one object over another with pressure and friction;
- tickle, to touch (a person) in a manner so as to cause laughter;
- strum, to draw the fingers or a plectrum over the strings of a musical instrument;
- pluck, pick, press, to sound the individual strings or keys of an instrument;
- play, to make sounds with a guitar/harp/keyboard instrument

Etymology: from Old Borlish, borrowed from (or possibly the source of) Old French frotter "rub, polish". This is in either case of uncertain origin, but is generally thought to reflect Latin participle frictus of fric≈ç "I rub, chafe". Musical senses are attested from the Middle Borlish period.

Jo's y jarry frottant tras un demy d'hour.

1s=be df crockery rub-p.prs across indf half of=hour
I've been scrubbing dishes for half an hour.

162.5: Mathematical Etymology (cont.)

more excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary o'Mathematics, a reference quire in the Merch language compiled by Rotcham [Rochdale] University Primers.

ΟΠther /ΥàiΥê.ΈΗ…ô/ [field] the assignment of a quantity (e.g. a dart [vector]) to each point in space. In the modern sense from the late 19C, formerly used to refer to the supposed medium within which phenomena like light, heat or astraphor [electricity] propagate. Early Merch ether (later re-classicised) < Old French < Latin ΟΠthΡ™r "the heavens" < Greek Έ±αΦΑΈΗΈ°œ¹ βÄΔ aithαΗ½r "heavens, open sky".

hedre /Υàh…¦.d…ô/ [face; bivector] one of the mozate [2D] components of the surface of a solid figure; a physical quantity of mozate kind, such as rotation. 16C, extracted from compounds like octahedre < Scholastic Latin -hedrum "-faced" < Greek αΦïΈ¥œ¹αΨ± βÄΔ hΟ©drΡ¹ "seat, chair; face".

matching /Υàma.t É…Σ≈΄g/ [bijection] a reversible correspondence between the elements of two collections W and X, both severing [injective] and covering [surjective], so that each element of X is associated to a unique element of W and vice versa. 1831 < translation of Saxon jochung "yoking" in DΟΦmmeyer, 1827. The verb match comes from the identical noun < Old English mΟΠΡ΄Ρ΄a "consort" < Classical German macci≈ç "partner".

oversamn /ΥàoΥê.sa.mnΧ©/ [superset] a collection X which contains all of the elements of another collection W, and usually more besides. 1831 < calque of Saxon ΟΕvrsammel "superset" in DΟΦmmeyer, 1827. The noun samn "set" is from the identical verb < Old English samnian "collect, gather" < Classical German samn≈ç "I gather".

stepth /Υàst…¦pΈΗ/ [gradient] the ratio of a change in vertical displacement to the corresponding change in horizontal displacement, a measure of how sloped an incline is. 1690 in mathematics < early Merch steapth < adjective steap "steep, sheer" < Old English stΡ™ap "high, lofty" < Classical German staupas "high, lofty".

vater /Υàv…¦Υê.t…ô/ [sine] the ratio of the lengths of the side of a triangle opposite a given angle to the length of the hypotenuse. 17C < Scholastic Latin vatrus (cf. earlier alvatr≈ç etc) < Arabic ΌàΌéΊΣΌéΊ± βÄΔ watar βÄ€bowstring, chordβÄù < calque of Sanscrit ύΛ€ύΞçύΛ·ύΛΨ jiΡ¹ "bowstring; vater". The polysemy arises by picturing an arc of a circle with the chord drawn as a bow with bowstring.

163.1: This Side of Aldebaran

Jo m'appen contr y pereΟΑ ny roum d'occajon sarraΟΑ oΟß se sarvent an entrquΟ·scoir insolemn por machinour vassaler, sgardant tandic Darios faye loit coll'entragl d'un indagour defonct.
I lean against the bulkhead in the cramped utility space that serves as an unofficial break room for junior engineers, watching Darius wrestle with the innards of an antiquated scanner.

Meyon atorn, y locomotour prophthan ny Polletia son un zoum kieΟΑ e fix generant oΟß y paiΟΑ vibrant sou mell'azigβÄîun son me denuΟΑ consolandessem sur ouΟß tras y semman derran abord.
Around me, the Polletia's superluminal engines create a constant low hum that vibrates through my bootsβÄîa sound that's become more comforting than silence over the last weeks on board.

"Yonoscon a tu l'oΟß ollom trovaΟΑ?" rou jo, ne my dois a mantien fignt. 
"Where'd you find that relic?" I ask, setting down my maintenance slate.

Darios se ley noc y gas cant i respon la, sy deit Οßosc stant pernix cos imprefait ne lorry maneyaΟßon de fil angost com seΟΑ.
Darius doesn't look up while he replies, his thick fingers surprisingly nimble as they manipulate silk-thin wires.

163.2: flasq "pathetic"

flasq /flask/ [flask]
- limp, sagging, flaccid, flabby, flimsy, loose, not taut or otherwise lacking stiffness;
- unenthusiastic, half-hearted, dispassionate, tepid, lacking interest or passion;
- pathetic, pitiful, hapless, inspiring scorn or compassion due to inadequacy or ineptitude

Etymology: borrowed in the Middle Borlish period from early Norman flasque "soft, malleable". This appears to be a hypercorrected variant of earlier flaque, descending from Latin flaccus "flabby, sagging". The unetymological /s/ would reflect analogy from other words whose coda /s/ had been lost in Parisian French. The term has been applied to emotion since the seventeenth century, and to people since the nineteenth.

Jo scavau un grinment flasq dy melodram.
/ £o xaΥàvo …Σn gr…ΣnΥàm…¦nt flask di Υ¨me.loΥàdram/
[ ùo x…êΥàvo …Σ≈΄ g Ä…ΣmΥàm…¦nt flask di Υ¨me.l äΥàd Äam]
1s write-pst indf review flabby of-df musical
I wrote an unenthusiastic review of the show.

163.3: cedar "guitar"

cedar /tseΥàdar/ [ts…ΣΥàd…êΥê]
- guitar, a fretted string instrument strummed or plucked with the fingers or a plectrum;
- zither, gittern, any of several similar such instruments in the lute family;
- (informal) skate, ray, any flat-bodied fish with wing-like fins and a whip-like tail

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed from early Jean cedara "guitar", a descendant of Latin cithara "lyre, lute" which was repurposed to refer to a related instrument of Morrove descent imported via Spain. The term has been used in the last sense since the nineteenth century (cf earlier ray a cedar "guitar ray") in reference to the shape of these fish.

Sy femn es y frottour de cedar y faverrem ny muncef.
/si Υàf…¦mnΧ© …¦z i fr…îΥàtur de tseΥàdar i faΥàv…¦.r…¦m ni m…ΣnΥàts…¦f/
[si Υàf…¦.m…ên …¦z i f Ä äΥàt äΥê de ts…ΣΥàd…êΥêβÄΩ Äi f…êΥàv…¦. Ä…êm ni m…ΣnΥàts…¦f]
3s.gn wife be df player of guitar df clever-cmp in-df borough
Her wife is the best guitar player in the borough.

163.4: gapir "pop up"

gapir /gaΥàp…Σr/ [g…êΥàp…ΣΥê]
- happen intermittently, to occur at unpredictable intervals;
- pop up, crop up, to appear (multiple times) without warning;
- be flaky, shilly-shally, to behave unpredictably or unreliably

Etymology: verbal derivative from gap "patch", apparently borrowed in the Middle Borlish period from Welsh gap "hole; patch, a distinct section of some larger whole". This in turn was taken from Old Norrish gap "empty space, gap, chasm", deverbal from gapa "to gape, be open wide".

L'oΟß astuΟßour es gapiscent par toΟΑ y lasc.

df=s.dt mentor be pop.up-p.prs by all df film
That mentor keeps turning up in the movie.

163.5: Like Oil and Water

collected notes on two unrelated topics.

Psolic aphlox (Merch /Υàs…î.l…Σk Υàa.fl…îks/) [carbon monoxide] is a colourless, odourless and toxic air formed by burning organic material without enough ignifex [oxygen].

Though the noxious effects of breathing the air in closed spaces where a coal fire is burning have been known since antiquity (and likely since prehistory), truly theoretical investigation did not begin until the seventeenth century, and the air was not isolated from the acid oil palacy [oxalic acid] until the Long Peace period at the turn of the nineteenth century. Its modern name, first used in 1854, comprises the adjective psolic [deoxygenated, with a lower oxidation number] from Greek œàœ¨ΈΜΈΩœ² βÄΔ psΟ≥los "soot, smoke" and the noun aphlox [carbon dioxide] from Greek αΨΑΧ™œÜΈΜœ¨ΈΨ  βÄΔ ΡÉphlΟ≥x "without flame".

---

The Graven Sea (Merch /Υàg…Ψ…¦Υê.v…ôn ΥàseΥê/) [Baltic Sea], formerly or poetically also the Eastmere, is an arm of the Atlantic Ocean in northern Europe east of Denmark and enclosed by several polities.

The name derives from Scholastic Latin Gravinia, a term for the region around this sea. This is a backformation from gravinicus, graunicus "from or pertaining to the Graven Sea". In turn, this appears to have been a minim error by a late medieval scribe from former granvicus, a borrowing from Old Norrish Gandvik. This placename, identified with the Graven Sea since the fourteenth century, may have originally referred to any of several bodies of water, including the Arctic Ocean.

The archaic synonym Eastmere is a calque from the name in various German languages; for instance, in Frise [Frisian] the sea is called the Astmar "east sea".

The work A Baptism of Fire: Christianity and the Graven was written by Agata Naziunas and published in 1988 by LeidΟ©iai GivatΟ©s. This work chronicles the history of the Christianisation of the states and peoples on the Graven Sea's coasts, with a particular focus on Naziunas's homeland of Lithow [Lithuania].

##

164.1: The Zenda Manuscript

excerpt in translation from the beginning of [real-life] book The Henchmen of Zenda by KJ Charles, a 2018 pastiche of the 1894 romp The Prisoner of Zenda.

"Candon jo ley un stoir, jo sepon l'eΟß exposson; or pront la jo me met a l'un autorar, j'yem sciΟΑ ig jo dey un exposson aΟ·r."
"When I read a story, I skip the explanations; yet the moment I begin to write one, I find that I must have an explanation."

Cossy da Rudolf Rassendyll capel commet a sy cont aventoresq de masquira, amour, traiΟßon, occijon de sanc freit, e segr de sanc ΟΑus.
This is Rudolf RassendyllβÄôs introduction to his swashbuckling tale of intrigue, love, treachery, cold-blooded murder, and hot-blooded men.

Sy tesmogn, parsemnaΟΑ coram, es denuΟΑ y vertaΟΑ creut cos general entrell'eΟß rar canΟßous por le leir. 
His account, privately circulated, has become the accepted truth amongst the few privileged to read it.

Cay un stoir d'audaΟß sou teneur covart, honour malgraΟΑ amour, nobiltaΟΑ sur tout.
It is a story of courage in the dark, honour in the teeth of love, nobility above all.

I nos fornisc de princes bel eΟΑ apassionnaΟΑ, d'un segr oΟß amour e coroun deneyant pro gra d'un caus diΟßerrem e puressem, e d'un malvaΟßβÄîuntal malvaΟß.
It gives us a beautiful, passionate princess, a man who renounces love and crown for the sake of a greater and purer cause, and a villainβÄîsuch a villain.

Rupert de Hentzau: temerer e coΟΑelous, graΟßous e desgraΟßous, fouceur, bland, vil, eΟΑ invencuΟΑ.
Rupert of Hentzau: reckless and wary, graceful and graceless, handsome, debonair, vile, and unconquered.

Rupert fug y fougl ag stoir de Rassendyll com monstr contrdontaΟΑ, nolent jamay plu reapparir; Rassendyll se destourm deur y campagn a honour aure; eΟΑ y vray Rey a Ruritania regn a Strelsau.
Rupert flees the pages of RassendyllβÄôs story a thwarted monster, never to be seen again; Rassendyll retires from the field with honour unstained; and the true King of Ruritania reigns in Strelsau.

Alcot connardaΟß.
What a pile of shit.

164.2: cardonar "unknot"

cardonar /Υ¨kar.doΥànar/ [Υ¨k…êΥê.d äΥàn…êΥê]
- card, tease, to separate and align the fibres of wool, etc;
- comb, curry, to run a carding tool through fur or hair;
- disentangle, unknot, to unfasten a knotted string or to separate several entwined strings;
- sort through, organise, to go over a collection of things and separate them into groups;
- resolve, sort out, simplify, clarify, streamline, to make a situation less complex

Etymology: verbal derivative from Old Borlish cart "carding tool" (since obsoleted by cardon, a synonymous variant). This is a descendant of Latin carduus or card≈ç "wild thistle". The verb has been used for metaphorical disentangling since at least the fourteenth century.

Tu vel nos aΟ·ΟΑar a l'ec fil cardonar ou?

2s will.sbj 1p help-inf to df=p.px wire card-inf q

164.3: stig "pang"

stig /stai/ [stai]
- stitch, a stabbing pain in the side caused by exercise or laughter;
- ache, pain, spasm, paroxysm, any similar sensation of pain;
- pang, throe, a sudden outburst of a negative emotion

Etymology: from Old Borlish stii, sti»ù "stitch, stabbing pain", a borrowing from synonymous stiΡΓe in Borland Old English. This is a variant of stiΡ΄e "stitch", which descends from Classical German sticis "prick, poke, a sticking". For more general pains the word is attested since the sixteenth century, and for emotions it is seen soon afterward.

Con un stig de remors jo ΟΑureu y pistol.

with indf stitch of regret 1s burn-pst df letter
With a pang of regret I burned the letter.

164.4: ahoy "imminent"

ahoy /aΥàh…îi/ […êΥàh…îi]
- ahoy, (nautical) interjection to warn of something coming into sight or approaching;
- in view, visible, within sight as from a lookout;
- nearby, close, within reach, accessible, not particularly far away;
- approaching, oncoming, imminent, heading towards one or about to occur (especially of something negative)

Etymology: originally in the first sense, used as an exclamation to attract attention or hail another ship. The extended uses are seen from the nineteenth century, initially restricted to colloquial or humorous contexts.

OrruΟΑ ig alcun hom parceveus y catastroph ahoy.
/…îΥàr…ΣΟΑ ai al.k…Σn …îm Υ¨par.tseΥàvauz i Υ¨ka.taΥàstr…îf aΥàh…îi/
[ äΥà Ä…ΣβÄΩΟΑai Υ¨a.g…ΣΥàn…îm Υ¨p…êΥêΥàts…ΣΥàvauz i Υ¨ka.taΥàst Ä…îf …êΥàh…îi]
seem cmp no person perceive-ipf.sbj df calamity ahoy
No-one seemed to notice the imminent disaster.

##

164.5: Kelt Borland

notes on the people and culture of Borland before the Roman invasion.

The Iron Age began on Borland at approximately the same time as on the other Northwestern Isles [Ireland, GB] in the ninth century aN. The identity of the first Borland ironworkers is debated and is made complicated by overlapping waves of migrations, but eventually they became identifiably Kelt. The last significant such wave was of early Brythonic speakers on Albion settling Borland from the west in the fourth century aN, the ancestors of the small hill-fort kingdoms which covered the island at the time of the Roman invasion in the first century N.

There are several archaelogical structures on Borland which predate the Kelt migrations but which the Kelts repurposed for ritual use. The most famous of these sites is a so-called "labyrinth" located on Ο΅adrosc Heath in the northwest of the island. There are also many cairns dating to this period, now called sarzin stones (from a late medieval use of sarzin "pagan" deriving from Latin SaracΡ™nus "Muslim").

The name Borland (Istr Boral) is thought to descend from the Kelt enis borag "island of the dawn [viz. East]" and bora ale "the other Easterners", which would imply the name was originally as exonym used by Brythonic speakers on Albion. The adoption of this name for the island by the Romans was undoubtedly influenced by its similarity to unrelated Latin boreΡ¹lis "northern"; this connection is referenced by contemporary writers.

The Borland tradition of Revillon can trace its origins to a winter feast celebrated by the Kelts, recorded as the Flid≈çlus (further etymology unknown) by third-century Roman historian Ammian.

##

165.1: Length and Mass

notes in translation on lengths and masses in the dime system of measures.

Scein tre font cas y lonctumn degm: y polΟß, y pie (10 polΟß) eΟΑ y lau (10β¹¥ pie).
There are three bases for dime lengths: the inch, the foot (10 inches) and the league (10β¹¥ feet).

Nell'enΟß, y lonctumn dy pie denoy fis cos quidam naΟΑusc com y mojol entr y "pie romannesc" eΟΑ y "paum accenser" Cathayan, stant un picq pog inequal.
At first, the length of the foot was fixed somewhat arbitrarily as the midpoint of the very slightly different Roman foot and the Cathayan "surveyor's span".

D'an 1946, y pie sta redefiniΟΑ por ig y veloΟßtaΟΑ normal din equal cos exact a deg gran lau par second.
In 1946, the foot was redefined to make the speed of light equal to precisely ten great leagues per second.

Bempart autr mesur a lonctumn pouvler receveurn valour degm.
Several other traditional measures of length were given dime values.

Com bustr, cas y mesur degm y mil val cinq mil pie ben demy d'un lau.
For instance, in dime measures the mile is equal to five thousand feet or half a league.

Scein catr font cas y moul degm: y scrupol, l'onΟß (100 scrupol), y taber (100 onΟß) eΟΑ y ton (100 taber).
There are four bases for dime hefts: the scruple, the ounce (100 scruples), the tabre (100 ounces) and the ton (100 tabres).

165.2: groinar "grunt"

groinar /gr…îiΥànar/ [g Ä äiΥàn…êΥê]
- oink, grunt, snort, to make the sound of e.g. a pig;
- growl, snarl, (of an animal) to make a deep rumbling sound;
- grumble, scoff, (of a person) to make a similar such sound to indicate disapproval or scorn

Etymology: from Old Borlish gro»ùnar, gro»ùndir "grunt", reflecting Vulgar variants of Classical Latin grunni≈ç "I grunt". To describe sounds made by people the word is attested as early as the Old Borlish period. The expected modern spelling grognar was once sporadically used but may have been avoided on aesthetic grounds (compare genoil "knee" < geno»ùl in Old Borlish).

Y bourgmer ag Groin groin posc y decret.
/i burgΥàm…¦r …¦i gr…îin gr…îin p…îx i deΥàkr…¦t/
[i b äΥêgΥàm…¦ΥêβÄΩ Ä…¦i g Ä…îin g Ä…îin p…îx i d…ΣΥàk Ä…¦t]
df mayor at.df CoruΟ±a grumble after df order
A CoruΟ±a's mayor grumbles about the act.

165.3: sax "asteroid"

sax /saks/ [saks]
- asteroid, planetoid, comet, a naturally occurring solid object orbiting the Sun which is too small to become spherical under gravity;
- (specifically) such a small body located with the cisjovial ring [asteroid belt]

Etymology: 1813, a borrowing from Latin saxum "stone, rock", used as a calque from synonymous tsa'ra in Mashick Arabic. The term was originally used to describe the various apparently-pointlike bodies in the cisjovial ring, which at that time were being discovered by telescope. The first celestial body this word was applied to, Ixion [Ceres], is now known to be spherical under gravity and so is no longer considered to be a sax. In modern usage saxes include comets, although knowledge of comets dates back to antiquity.

Theorist prevein un collijon par sax d'an 2026.
/ΈΗauΥàr…Σst preΥàvin …Σn Υ¨k…î.liΥà £…în par saks dan du m…Σl vint sij/
[ΟΑ…êuΥà Ä…Σst p Ä…ΣΥàvin …Σn Υ¨k…î.l…ΣΥà ù…în p…êΥê saks dan du m…Σl vint s…Σi]
scientist foresee-3p indf collision by asteroid of=year 2026
Scientists predict an asteroid impact in 2026.
