165.4: or bon "alright"

or bon /…îr b…în/ [ äΥêΥàb…în]
- alright, OK, cool, sure, an interjection indicating acknowledgement or acceptance;
- so, anyway, look, see, hey, an interjection used after a pause to introduce a new topic

Etymology: phrase comprising or "well, however" and bon "good, correct". The particle or (now archaic in isolation) descends from Old Borlish or "now", either straight from Latin (hΡ¹c) h≈çrΡ¹ "in (this) hour" or via synonymous (a)ore in Old French. The adjective bon comes directly from Latin bonus "good, right".

Or bon, tenau a le javigar.
/…îr b…în | teΥàno a le Υ¨ £a.viΥàgar/
[ äΥêΥàb…în | t…ΣΥàno a le Υ¨ ùa.v…ΣΥàg…êΥê]
well good | hold-1p.sbj at 3s.acc trawl-inf
Alright, let's keep searching.

##

165.5: The 1910s

collected notes on various happenings in the decade 1910 to 1919.

LoΟΑaΟß Berxon (b. 1865) led the Borland chapters of the Outland Arcabil Association from 1913 until 1918. Berxon had emigrated to the capital city of Damvath from her birthplace in the Arcabil [Caribbean] a decade earlier, due to the disruption of the Millstone War. She was an established author, best known for her first work L'AskouΟΑ Pyrrhaz (The Firekeeper's Stronghold), published in 1910. The Outland Arcabil Association was a collection of societies working for the benefit of the Arcabil diaspora across the globe.

The first then-called "house of odyssey" [cinema] to sell tickets to the public on a regular basis was established in Boxa [Novi Sad, Serbia] in 1912. One of the venue's first great successes was a recording of a ballet concert accompanied by the St Hurroco Youth Orchestra of Furlain [Friuli].

Un Seir Deura Soffire (One Evening Will Have to be Enough) was a 1913 work in the masquira genre by Roun author Josephene Foilliers. Its riproaring execution of the classic masquira plot structure of schemes and opulence galore made it an instant success across the Drengot Collusion and beyond; this work was the first book to sell more thna a million copies in its first year.

The literary genre of social comedy has its origins in the late 1910s, with authors like Dorotha Quistack and Judeta Barracin adapting elements of prior genres like lovetales and adolescences. Social comedy is characterised by plots with low stakes, an emphasis on personal friendships and enmities, and generally either rural or low-class urban settings.

##

166.1: The Ceres Gambit

excerpt in translation from 1952 spycraft trevold The Ceres Gambit (originally published in Borlish as Vellisqment: Ixion), written by Joron Aldaxinte. She was born in the industrial Borlish town of Neuvort, the daughter of a clerk and a minor functionary in the local government.

Caye crevoscr cos tost l'oc jorn. Y ray gaster d'un lun decaint trencan tras ranscel spars e yemal.
Dusk had fallen early that day. The ghost-light of a waning moon cut through sparse, wintry branches.

Sceye veil ney com manton sur y tar dy forest, piestaΟΑ dessur pall'ombr de pas a toΟΑ asmuth.
Old snow blanketed the forest floor, trampled upon with footprints in every direction.

Un hom er le sevent. L'idea parvenoy lon prevart ben motivaΟßon.
She was being followed. The thought arrived without warning or justification.

I s'interromoy entr y taisc visnous de dou casner antic, cant sy respir fayen dragon nell'aer, eΟΑ i da cur all'ouΟß paΟßent ny bosc.
She paused between the gnarled trunks of two ancient oaks, her breath dragoning in the air, and took in the woods' patient silence.

Con un maldicΟßon soΟßavaver i se commoveu cos lejar y pis, e de sell'azig i sgranfeyau y crost de glaΟß sou y neyanΟß.
With a whispered oath she shifted her weight slightly, her boots crunching faintly on the crust of ice beneath the snow.

I s'ortelle y man cos lent vars y nuivr dy glay a sy cegntur eΟΑ i se torcheu y doit atorn dell'ans frottaΟΑ ne cogr.
Her hand moved slowly to the hilt of the blade at her belt and her fingers curled around the worn leather grip.

166.2: fren "brake"

fren /fr…¦n/ [f Ä…¦n]
- bit, piece of metal placed in a horse's mouth and connected to the reins;
- brake, device used to slow or stop a vehicle usually via friction;
- deceleration, slowdown, reduction in speed;
- ritenuto, ritardando, (in music) reduction of tempo;
- restraint, reserve, tendency to act with caution or prudence

Etymology: originally in the first sense, via Old Borlish directly from Latin frΡ™num "bridle, harness, curb, bit". The word was extended metaphorically some centuries before it was reapplied to physical devices on other vehicles. In music the word is calqued from Willem Dutch afrem "slow down (imp.)", potentially also due to phonetic resemblance.

J'ay hougr y fren de my raΟΑ sein inovrant.
/ £e h…îir i fr…¦n de mi raΈΗ sin Υ¨i.n…î.vrant/
[ ùe h…î.jΟΠβÄΩ Äi f Ä…¦n de mi  Äah sin Υ¨i.n äΥàv Äant]

I'm worried my bike's brakes are broken.

166.3: fougl pavarrot "tabloids"

fougl pavarrot /fuil Υ¨pa.vaΥàr…ît/ [fw…Σl Υ¨pa.v…êΥà Ä…ît]
- tabloids, newspapers considered to be more commercial and less prestigious than "high-brow" journalism;
- gutter press, yellow press, sensationalist reporters or newspapers in general;
- sensationalism, puffery, hype, the practise of exaggerating things for attention

Etymology: nineteenth-century phrase coined as an insult, literally meaning "little cash pages"; compare equivalent Merch term "bit-sheets". The noun fougl "leaf; page, sheet" descends via Old Borlish fou»ùl from Latin folium "leaf". The noun pavarrot is a diminutive of pavar "poppy", here referring to a particular small coin used on Borland in the early modern period and reintroduced with a different denomination in the late twentieth period. The word descends from synonymous papΡ¹ver in Latin, of unknown further origin.

Y fougl pavarrot l'evolgaurn prell'aubon.
/i fuil Υ¨pa.vaΥàr…ît Υ¨le.v…îlΥàgornΧ© Υ¨pr…¦.loΥàb…în/
[i fw…Σl Υ¨pa.v…êΥà Ä…ît Υ¨le.v älΥàgoΥên Υ¨p Ä…¦.l äΥàb…în]
df leaf poppy-dim df=leak-pst.3p before-df=morning
The tabloids got the story out overnight.

166.4: ouger "host"

ouger /uΥà £…¦r/ [ äΥà ù…¦Υê]
- landlord, landlady, proprietor, innkeeper, the person who owns a public building (especially a bar or lodging house);
- host, one who receives or entertains guests;
- emcee, moderator, the person running an event;
- presenter, anchor, the face of a programme on radio, TV, etc.

Etymology: from Middle Borlish ouger "landlord/lady", altered from louger via rebracketing of the definite article. This is an agent derivative of now-obsolete lousc "shelter; inn", borrowed from Old French loge "shelter; covered walkway, hut, small room", via synoymous laubia in Medieval Latin from the Classical German root laubam "leaf, foliage". The word has been applied also to other host roles since the seventeenth century.

L'es un ouger sobottal cay set annaΟΑ.
/l…¦z …Σn uΥà £…¦r Υ¨so.b…îΥàtal ke s…¦t aΥànaΈΗ/
[l…¦z …Σn  äΥà ù…¦Υê Υ¨so.b äΥàtal ke s…¦t …êΥànah]
3s=be indf host radio fall seven year
He's been a radio host for seven years.

##

166.5: The North Wind and the Sun

The Borrhatic League is a collection of charitable organisations and political action groups with a focus on laic governance, education and (in recent decades) also pacifism and objection. The League grew from collaboration between working-class groups of minority faiths across Northern Europe in the early nineteenth century; the name derives from the Attic Greek Έ£ΈΩœ¹œ¹αΨΕœ² βÄΔ BorrhΟΔs "the North Wind". The League first rose to prominences on the scale of polities during the Second German War of the 1820s and in its aftermath. For instance, Hilgart and Michael Bervisson were influential in Borland's Borrhatic League at this time, especially with regards to calls for religious tolerance.

Although the Borrhatic League and the contemporaneous Deviance movement has (occasionally explosive) disagreements, both are known to have contributed verbiage to the 1850 Laic Declarations in Belgrade. Later in the nineteenth century the Borrhatic League funded orphanages and schools across the region, such as the Borrhatic School of History in the south-eastern Borlish city of Combert, which exists to this day.

I Macchine di Helio (The Machines of Helios) is a marble sculpture created by Lorenzo dell'Acchedia in the 1550s, during the regions Revitalist period. It was carved in Acchedia's workshop in Rome and displayed in that city for the first decades of its existence. The work is a stylised depiction of the Greek god Helios "the Sun" with the six known planets gyring around him. The notion of a Sun-centred cosmos that inspired the work may have been transmitted to Acchedia via his close friend MartΟ®l fi Bacchero, mathematician at the library of Saint Jerome, who is recorded to have debated the issue in public fora as early as 1552.

##

167.1: Astraphor

notes in translation on the subject of astraphor [electricity].

Astraphor (tan dignant forΟß barric) es y cougl d'occorrenΟß alligaΟΑ con y presenΟß eΟΑ y locomoΟßon de soustanΟß plentivous, ben y nis harneisabr des tal locomoΟßon.
Astraphor (also called barric force) is the set of phenomena associated with the presence and movement of plentiful [charged] substances, or the thrift [energy] that one can harness from such movement.

Y conjogntur profont cas theory entr astraphor e lumner dene sploraΟΑ cos minuΟßous ny siecr novoΟß a plaΟß meg; y magn ouvr de titr lon ingeniostaΟΑ Astraphor e Lumner scaut par y theorist Markesc Wexter Hathwall acavau a y mechanism d'astraphor descreir cos enter coll'un equaΟßon soul.
The deep theoretical connection between astraphor and light was explored thoroughly in the mid-nineteenth century; the unimaginatively titled magnum opus Astraphor and Light by Merch theorist Wexter Hathwall managed to fully characterise the mechanics of astraphor in a single equation.

Temporane, y sar campaner preluyent a gonfan e lucern sta a mesur de denir destarriΟΑ par eΟß sar barric.
At this time, the earlier flag-lamp steeplemesh [telegraph] was in the process of being displaced by barric meshes.

Region lon doujug a gonfan e lucern stant effus e sceint surcourn larc cas rapidessem por y sar barric adoptar; com bustr, d'an 1870 y lign barric y lonchessem ny mont tras heilant afferjarau y lonctumn de Mendeva Bassem.
Places without a pre-existing extensive flag-lamp system were in many cases faster to adopt the barric mesh; for example, by 1870 the world's longest wired post line on land spanned the length of Lower Mendeva [Central America].

167.2: spanjon "spread"

spanjon /spanΥà £…în/ [sp…ê…≤Υà ù…în]
- expansion, growth, spread, increase of size, territory or influence;
- elaboration, explanation, further description;
- extension, addition, putting more pieces or sections onto something;
- generalisation, broadening, applying something to new contexts

Etymology: noun derivation from spandr "expand, spread, broaden, explain" or directly from Latin expansio "an instance of spreading out". In either case these derive from Latin root verb pand≈ç "spread, unfold, narrate". The metaphorical senses of expansion are partially modelled after use of the term in Scholastic Latin.

Nos survism y spanjon dy quaΟΑeglad.

167.3: dob "nickname"

dob /d…îb/ [d…îb]
- honour, accolade, epithet, title, cognomen, a descriptor conferred upon someone as a status symbol;
- term, name, signifier, word, lexeme, a descriptor of any concept (especially one from a specialised area of knowledge);
- byname, nickname, moniker, sobriquet, a familiar form of address for someone or something;
- handle, username, a string of characters used to identify an account on a digital system

Etymology: originally in the first sense, a deverbal noun from dobar "confer knighthood upon, dub". This is a borrowing of Old French adober "equip, arm; dub", which appers to come from a German root related to Classical German dubilas "peg, dowel". In extended use the word is seen from as early as the fourteenth century; the first attestation of the last sense is from 1964.

Cu vel un nom sceint issarr com dob?

167.4: salubr "virtuous"

salubr /saΥàl…ΣbrΧ©/ [s…êΥàl…Σ.b…ê]
- healthy, healthful, good for someone's health;
- beneficial, advantageous, expedient, generally good for someone or something;
- virtuous, righteous, honourable, principled, having good moral character;
- generous, magnanimous, charitable, willing to give and share things unsparingly

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed directly from Latin sal≈Ϊber "healthy, beneficial". The terms has been applied to moral goodness almost from the moment of its first attestation in Borlish. In the monetary sense the word has been used since the eighteenth century.

J'abhoir cont de heros saluberrem.

I despise tales of virtuous heroes.

167.5: Chemical Etymology (cont. 3)

further excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary of Alchemy, a reference quire compiled by Rotcham University Primers [1].

calamine /Υàka.l…ê.m…ôin/ [calamine] conventionalised name for the chalk [carbonate] of jast [zinc], a mineral known since antiquity, used in the smelting of brass and the treatment of rashes. 1645 < Scholastic Latin calamΡΪna < sporadic alteration of Classical cadmΡΪa "calamine; slag, dross" < Ancient Greek ΈöαΨΑΧ¹Έ¥ΈΦΈΩœ² βÄΔ KαΚ·dmos "Cadmus, legendary founder of Thebes".

od configure /Υà…îd Υàk…în.f…ΣΥ¨gj…ôu…ô/ [diastereoisomer] one of two or more configures [stereoisomers] of a substance which cannot be superimposed upon each other or upon each other's mirror images. Contrast with a mirrored pair of even configures [enantiomers]. 1922, compound of od "unpaired, indivisible by two" and configure "shape, arrangement". The first < Early Merch odde < Old Norrish [Norrish] oddi "third; odd" < oddr "sharp point; triangle" < Classical German usdas "sharp point; position". The second < Scholastic Latin c≈çnfig≈Ϊra "moulding, shape" < Classical c≈çnfig≈Ϊr≈ç "I mould" < fig≈Ϊr≈ç "I form, shape" < fig≈Ϊra "shape, form".

palaquate /Υàpa.l…êΥ¨kw…¦Υêt/ [oxalic acid] the simplest twice-acid oil [dicarboxylic acid], containing only two atoms of coke [carbon] and being a colourless and crystalline substance found in plants. 1834 < Scholastic Latin palaquΡ¹tum < Sanscrit ύΛΣύΛΨύΛ≤ύΛïύΞçύΛ·ύΛΨύΛΒύΛΛύΞç βÄΔ pΡ¹laciΡ¹vat or its Gurjat [Gujarati] reflex ύΣΣύΣΨύΣ≤ύΣïύΣΒύΣΨύΣΓύΪ΄ βÄΔ pΡ¹lacvΡ¹dΧΠo "characterised by spinach" < Gurjat ύΣΣύΣΨύΣ≤ύΣï βÄΔ pΡ¹lac "spinach" < Sanscrit ύΛΣύΛΨύΛ≤ύΛïύΞçύΛ·ύΛΨ βÄΔ pΡ¹laciΡ¹ "beet, leafy vegetable". The substance was first extracted from the leaves of spinach during the Long Peace period.

excerpt in translation from the winter 2021 scitation questions for the Hulm Siebenhaus's avenue "Zwanzistewes Entwicklungen" (Advancements of the 20th Century).

(iii) Cal methodist laurant ag Schol AlΟßor de Nadacou acavau a y composson d'agr heredian preveir ne lorrell'ediΟßon d'an 1929, a dou dezein enter pre lorry prefacΟßon vole denir corroulaΟΑ par experienΟß?

168.2: pegr "lazy"

pegr /pijrΧ©/ [Υàpi. ù…ê]
- idle, inactive, motionless, not engaged in any activity;
- asleep, on standby, (of a device) in a mode with the power on but not in active use;
- lazy, indolent, slothful, averse to work or exercise;
- slovenly, unkempt, having an untidy appearance;
- careless, negligent, sloppy, messy, with a lack of attention or precision;
- pointless, inane, silly, occurring to no particular purpose;

Etymology: from Old Borlish pe»ùr "unintelligent; idle; lazy", a semi-learned descendant of Latin piger "dull, lazy, sluggish; loth, averse". Use of physical appearance is attested from the sixteenth century; in the latter more general senses the word is seen from the early nineteenth century.

    Il passaurn y ralley par jacas pegr.

    They spent the afternoon chatting idly.

168.3: paramontaΟΑ "supremacy"

paramontaΟΑ /Υ¨pa.ra.m…înΥàtaΟΑ/ [Υ¨pa. Ä…ê.m änΥàtah]
- supremacy, dominance, primacy, precedence, the quality of having power or priority;
- liegedom, suzerainty, the political arrangement inverse to vassalage in which one polity has legal control over the governance of another

Etymology: nominal derivation from paramont "supreme, primary". This is a borrowing from Old French, a univerbation of par amont "by upward", from Latin per "through" and ad montem "to a mountain; upwards". The word was originally used in the thirteenth century to describe the relation of the Drengot Emperor over the kings in his demesne.

Il affirmn y paramontaΟΑ de rigteΟß sur ley.

3p affirm-3p df supremacy of justive on law
They assert the primacy of ethics over law.

168.4: pereΟΑ "bulkhead"

pereΟΑ /peΥàr…¦ΈΗ/ [p…ΣΥà Ä…¦h]
- bulkhead, a vertical separator dividing the hull of a ship into separate compartment, often made watertight;
- an inner wall in a building;
- partition, any structure that divides an area into sections;
- (anatomy) septum, a biological structure analogous to a partition and often specifically the nasal septum

The word's anatomical sense is first attested from the seventeenth century in translation from Scholastic Latin.

168.5: And Good And Gay

excerpt from the 1986 work Birds Yet Sing, a popular account by Ivainchuque fi Cap (originally published in Vascon as Ja Cantan AucΟ®is) of the history of the Household Renovation in the late nineteenth century. This was a precipitous societal shift in attitudes to marriage, family and the role of children, as to the ultimate causes of which there has been much speculation.

βÄΠorigin of the word "tov" [gay] itself may seem to be evident. The term is attested from the 1810s in the underground tovaric communities of the Riverine Cities [1], and is notable for being (unlike so many similar words) a self-descriptor used to signal ingroup membership, instead of an insult hurled by offended outsiders or the avenging law (whatever it may be avenging). This initial covertness can be seen in the somewhat confused definition given in Manjurich's 1823 work The Vernacular of Boxa [Novi Sad, Serbia] as "a loving salutation between brothersβÄΠavoided in polite company".

The existence of the synonymous "tovaric" certainly suggests that "tov" is simply a clipping of e.g. Zahid Russian ―²–Ψ–≤–ΑΧ¹―Ä–Η―à βÄΔ tovΟΓrish "friend, companion" or one of its cognates, and indeed this connection appears to have been consciously reinforced through the nineteenth century. By the time of the Household Renovation, academic works had further adapted the root, borrowing words like "tovarism" [homosexuality] into dozens of languages.

A closer look into the religious and social dynamics of the Riverine Cities, however, reveals tantalising alternative etymologies. The Riverine Cities were also a hotbed of the Deviance movement due to the presence of significant religious minorities and the entwining of the Church with (resented) local and baronial authority. In particular, there was a significant connection between tovaric circles and the Jewish quarters of cities like Pojon [Bratislava]. Use of the phrase ΉßΉΔΉ®Ή€ Ή‰ΉïΉë βÄΔ karl tov "good chap" (the noun coming from German) between Jewish men is attested in correspondence from the mid-eighteenth century.

Another more prosaic idea is that the word "tov" is a clipping in the other direction, a rebracketed form of the patronymic suffix -ov from surnames like Kotov or Soldatov, butβÄΠ

---

[1] an alliance-federacy of small independent states along the Danube River and its tributaries linked by treaty and trade in the nineteenth and early twentieth centuries.

169.1: Trapped

excerpt in translation from the children's tale of adventure Joke Tawyer and the Emeralds in the Castle, written (originally in Merch) in 1964 by an unidentified member of the Kilshaw writers' collective.

Jo pogn y pujoncr des y moΟΑ moglastr par un resmargenΟß baurous d'energy.
I snatch the knife from the damp earth with a desperate surge of energy.

Sy pis suet m'interman un hormesc consolant ny scompigl.
Its familiar weight is a comforting anchor in the chaos.

Y lamn fay trenc mordent par y cabr gros dy sar cos fagl, cant casc coup hastous spein un cascat a cort se cardonant com y torjon d'un sarpent.
The blade bites through the net's coarse fibers with ease, as each swift cut releases a cascade of ropes that unwind like the coils of a serpent.

Jo me sent n'impisantour tras demy de pousson pre jo plombet par gringr lon graΟß; my corp liesc vars y makisaΟß con martelment oc mell'os peyaΟßant eΟΑ y respir volant des my poumon.

Leaves and twigs snag my clothes, and a sharp pain lances through my shoulder, but there's no time to linger in discomfort.

169.2: sacr "wicked"

sacr /Υàsa.krΧ©/ [Υàsa.k…ê]
- (formal or poetic) sacred, sainted, holy, hallowed, characterised by solemn religious ceremony or religious use;
- essential, vital, key, crucial, pivotal, of extreme importance;
- (colloquial, dated) cool, wicked, awesome, very interesting or impressive in an exciting way

Etymology: originally in the first senses, a Middle Borlish reborrowing from Latin sacer "sacred, holy". With these meanings the words is mostly confined to idioms, collocations (y Bibr Sacr "the Holy Bible") and poetry, supplanted by words like sacraΟΑ and sagntaΟΑ "holy". In the last senses the word was briefly popular in the mid-twentieth century and it is occasionally still used (alongside the comparative sacherrem which saw similar use somewhat later) to evoke the period.

SaΟΑour sialler seraun sacr sy seucq saufgardant.

Rye farmers will be vital to securing his legacy.

- competitor, contender, a person or team taking part in a competition;

##

169.5: Time for T

collected notes on four features of the Boralverse alternate history that begin with the letter T.

Tewis Camigner (fl. c16) was a botanist from Willemy during the Revitalist period, sometimes called the father of farm theory. His writings in experientia include the first reference to the high sugar content of beet syrup, which would go on to inspire the 1730s Pentrose survey. This was a search for a domestic alternative to sugarcane which resulted in the braxoch, a hybrid of Silesian and Cassuvian beets with a characteristic gold colour and the ancestor of almost all modern sugar beets.

Tightrope Walking: a Linguistic History of Borland (originally in Napolitan as Funamblisme: n'Istorio Lingual d'e Isule Bural) is a scholastic quire written by Ghendaline Cecchino and published in 1991 by the Edizore UniversitΟ† Napule. The work chronicles how the Borlish language has changed from the Roman period until the modern day.

Tolose is a city and region in the south of Gaul [France], formerly an independent polity. It was a march (and then a duchy) vassal of the First Drengot Empire in the eleventh and twelfth centuries, bordering the kingdom of Barcelon to the south. However, the region maintined significant independence, with its own standing army. Following the collapse of the First Drengot Empire, Tolose became a hotly contested border region. It was conquered by Vascony from the west in the fifteenth century (see Prince Mark of Tolose, heir to the Vascon throne who predeceased his father in 1453). In the first decade of the sixteenth century, Barcelon conquered Tolose from Vascony, and the region has been territory belonging to the kingdom of Barcelon since (barring some brief exceptions).

Tuscany is an Italian polity on the Tyrrhene Sea, bordering Jeans [Genoa], Lombardy and Rome. Its capital city is Florence. Between the tenth and thirteenth centuries it was ruled by the Pisan dynasty, including the renowned Duchess Matilda (r. 1102-1146). The Italian language of Tuscany has contributed many loanwords to European languages due to the prestige and trade wealth of the region.

##

170.1: Wales in the Wars

excerpt in translation on the subject of Wales during the Wars of Fealty, and on the Battle of Rexam (1483) in particular.

Y Reyaum a Brittan joun naΟΑusc (compos par Cambry e sy taleΟΑ fragossau, cosogr Cornou e Sodric) broucau nell'enΟß un eΟΑegl de forΟß, e l'acavau a bempart concession stragr des Marclont durant l'annaΟΑ primal dy rix.
The relatively new Kingdom of Britain (formed of Wales and its outlying tallaths [provinces], along with Kernow [Brittany] and Sodrick [Man and the Hebrides]) started from a position of strength, and managed to extract many concessions from Markland in the early years of the wars.

L'ec fey ersteigon par un victoir consecter ag Battagl a Car Vantel, yon fo y Duc a Castr final occeut.
These culminated in a decisive victory at the Battle of Rexam, where the last Duke of Chester was killed.

Posc l'oc battagl, Brittan sceu auteΟß durabr garantir dy region atorn de Car Vantel (ma contrig y gazou sur y citaΟΑ a Castr pog apres se cou offiruΟΑ).
After this battle, Britain was able to take lasting control of the area around Rexam (though the attack on the city of Chester shortly thereafter was rebuffed).

Y polity acavau soulor a se refusar deur y conflict continuant dy Rix cas FieltaΟΑ; y ligt entr y polity n'Albion volen remanir jannestaΟΑ entarn tras trent annaΟΑ aposc.
The polity then managed to recuse itself from the continued conflicts of the Wars of Fealty; the rest of the polities of Albion would remain embroiled for another thirty years.

- (historical) dithyramb, a raucous and ardent choral hymn sung in ancient Greece in honor of the god Dionysus;
- ode, ballad, any of various poetic or musical forms characterised by the communication of intense emotions;
- diatribe, rant, tirade, polemic, an impassioned oration or piece of writing (often with positive affect)

Etymology: originally in the first sense, borrowed in the early modern period via Latin from Έ¥αΩëΈΗαΩ†Χ¹œ¹αΨΑΈΦΈ≤ΈΩœ² βÄΔ dΡΪth≈≠Χ¹rΡÉmbos in Ancient Greek. 170.3: souquondar "lurk"

souquondar /Υ¨su.kw…înΥàdar/ [Υ¨su.kw änΥàd…êΥê]
- lurk, hide, skulk, to lie in wait out of sight;
- prowl, sneak, to move stealthily to avoid detection;
- be dormant, bide one's time, to appear to be inactive while waiting for the best opportunity;
- (botany) of a bulb, to remain underground over winter;
- (medicine) of a disease, to be asymptomatic but still contagious;
- stake out, case, to conduct surveillance upon a location

Etymology: Middle Borlish, derivation with the prefix sou "under, below" from the root aquondar, covondar et al. "lie in wait, ambush". This appears to reflect an unattested Vulgar Latin adjective *accupΡ¹bundus "lurking, hiding, hunting animals", which would be a confused form influenced by Classical aucupor "I catch birds or bees" and occup≈ç "I occupy, seize, anticipate".

The botanical sense is attested from the seventeenth century, and the medical sense from the nineteenth. The last senses appear to have arisen in journalistic circles within the past few decades.

ToΟΑ y volcan sur Istr Boral souquondn tras millenner.

170.4: theu "habit"

theu /ΈΗau/ [ΈΗau]
- wont, habit, custom, practice, way, MO, one's usual manner of doing things;
- will, willpower, free will, the capacity to act and make decisions to carry out one's wishes;

170.5: Decim(alis)ation

excerpt from the 1st July 1975 edition of the Scavlour (Reflecter), a workdaily paper from Borland which focuses on political news.

βÄΠimpractical to import astraphor [electricity] from Portingale.

GOVERNMENT ANNOUNCES FIRST DECIMATION IN FIFTY YEARS

In a long-anticipated move, the Ministry for Wealth has confirmed that all four conditions of the '15 Food Prices Act have been met. The gemma (βçö), which has been the base unit of Borlish currency since 1925, is to be first supplemented and eventually replaced with a coin worth 10βçö.

Minister Gottart has expressed his intention that new coin and notes denominated in the new currency will enter circulation on David's Day of 1977. For the next four months the ministry is accepting public input on the name and designs of the new coinage, although calls for a popular vote on the final name have been rejected (perhaps due to the unfortunate example of Burgundy, which went through its own decimation three years ago). No timeline has yet been confirmed for the deprecation of the gemma, but exchange schemes are likely to be available for at least a year after gemma-denominated coins are no longer legal tender.

This will be only the third currency in Borland since the polity moved to a decimal system of coinage upon its 1894 incorporation into the Drengot Collusion. The process of so-called "decimation" is common to all Drengot successor states and has been taken up by several others through the twentieth century. It obtained its violent name as a cod-Latin pun and insult by Sarzan Dawkinholm, one-time Outland Minister in Markland and one of the most vociferous opponents to decimation.

The necessity of decimation has been questioned by trade theorists, especially given that many parts of the world seem to have no issue spending hundreds or thousands of the local currency on (say) a loaf of bread, butβÄΠ

171.1: Maritime Cards

notes in translation on the subject of the Maritime deck of cards, which have five suits: gold, bucklers, staves, jewels and arrows.

Doutr allors, magn entr y blad aun cas ustol un dob desjognt deur lorry step eΟΑ ensignβÄîcom bustr, y dou de verc dign y Duel, cant y set de jovel es connuΟΑ com y ConstellaΟßon.
Many of the cards furthermore have conventional titles separate to their rank and ensignβÄîfor example, the deuce of staves is called the Duel, while the sept of jewels is known as the Stars.

Tras asc y composson sull'ec blad son enΟßaΟΑ a l'ec aspect noΟßonal sousumir.
Over time the artwork on these cards has come to incorporate these thematic elements.

Ne joy yon certan blad contrdontn toΟΑ autr, l'ensign d'aur luΟ·sc cos usual l'oc sona, interim pieΟß joy modernessem se sarviraun dy blad "dobaΟΑ" soumont amont cossy.
In games where some cards trump the rest, the gold ensign typically takes this role, but some more modern games will use the aforementioned "named" cards in this way.

Y benoΟß d'un pachet Maritim por y ventur preveir scavr bempart autr poiΟΑ d'arveiΟΑrostaΟΑ, ma meΟΑes l'issar occult e modern n'aun lorrell'origin for ny siecr novoΟß primal.

171.3: visnous "gnarly"

visnous /v…ΣΥàznuz/ [v…ΣΥàznuz]
- wizened, rugged, made rough by age or exposure;
- gaunt, haggard, emaciated, looking severely ill and malnourished;
- gnarly, knotty, knobbly, bumpy, shaped like wood with many knots

Etymology: Middle Borlish, adjectival derivation from now-obsolete verb visnar "to wither". This descends from an Old Borlish borrowing from Old English wisnian "dry up, shrivel". The arboreal senses (literally or figuratively) are now the most current, with a noun visn "knot in wood" having been backformed from the adjective.

Il ern zaquesc visnous a tatover pascent.
/…Σl …¦rn zaΥàkw…¦x v…ΣΥàznuz a Υ¨ta.toΥàv…¦r Υàpa.x…¦nt/
[…ΣΥàl…¦Υên z…êΥàkw…¦x v…ΣΥàznuz a Υ¨ta.t äΥàv…¦Υê Υàpa.x…ênt]
3p cop.imp-3p snack gnarly at potato eat-p.prs
They were eating knobbly potato snacks.

##

171.4: ne minemini "eeny meeny miney moe"

ne minemini /ne miΥ¨ne.miΥàni/ [Υ¨ne.m…ΣΥ¨ne.m…ΣΥàni]
- (law) the presumption of innocence, the legal principle that an accused party is innocent until they are found guilty;
- benefit of the doubt, an appeal to this principle in more general settings;
- eeny meeny miney moe, an (essentially) meaningless phrase at the beginning of any of various children's rhymes which involve pointing at people or things in turn with the goal of picking one

Etymology: fourteenth century in legal Latin as nΡ™ minΡ™minΡΪ "do not threaten, do not accuse", from the prohibitive particle nΡ™ and the second-person plural subjunctive of deponent verb minor "I threaten, accuse". Its original denotation was closer to a admonition against obtaining confessions by duress or threats, only attaining its modern meaning in the seventeenth century. Shortly thereafter the phrase appears in nursery rhymes, presumably more for phonetic reasons than any semantic connection. Nonetheless, some have dubiously linked these rhymes to the act of picking a scapegoat for a crime.

Nos orruΟΑ stou le fornir con ne minemini.
/n…îz …îΥàr…ΣΈΗ stu le f…îrΥàn…Σr k…în ne miΥ¨ne.miΥàni/
[n…îz  äΥà Ä…Σh stu le f äΥêΥàn…ΣΥê k…în Υ¨ne.m…ΣΥ¨ne.m…ΣΥàni]
1p seem must 3s.acc furnish-inf with presumption.of.innocence
She seems worth the benefit of the doubt.

##

171.5: Novomundine Landfall

The Novomundine Landfall (Borlish Abanc Novomondin) occurred in 1471 N, when a ship that was part of a New Navarre Enterprise fishing expedition in the North Atlantic sighted and made landfall on the island of Brasil [Newfoundland]. It marks the start of prolonged contact between the Vetomund [Afroeurasia] and the Novomund [Americas] (notwithstanding evidence of sporadic Norrish [Norrish] contact with the region), and the first century following the Landfall is known to historians as the Intertwining Century.

The terms Novomund and Mendeva [North America] date to this voyage; a surviving map from the Landfall voyage itself is marked terrae mendevalensis, after the Vask [Basque] mendeval "west".

The Landfall occurred at the height of the so-called Vascon Ascendancy, when Vascony [N Spain, SW France] reached its territorial maximum under Ambrose III, and the trade opportunities that arose over the following decades were just as beneficial for the polity. Ambrose's rival, Munir al-Hamdawi of Morrack [Morocco], quickly redirected the efforts of his presciently-named New World Company westward (having previously maintained trade routes to the East), leading to the discovery of the faster southern route to the Novomund and the islands of the Arcabil [Caribbean].

The first Vetomundine royal to set foot in the Novomund was King Ambrose's elder sister Princess Alexandra of Vascony, who visited the new settlement of Paratzon [St John's, NL] in the 1490s. It is unknown why she (and several female attendants) undertook the voyage, although her second husband's various deficiencies and scandals have been implicated. Her visit is remembered for advancing understanding of scurvy (the chemical sandrine [vitamin C] is named in her honour), although it would take over a century and experiments with guy-conies [guinea pigs] before ships could come close to effectively preventing the malady.

##

172.1: Hatfell Honours

notes in translation on the subject of a coveted artistic accolade.

Honour de Hatfell son veΟΑrdon plusour soupleyaΟΑ de posc y siecr veintem primal a artejan ne sce divers y Collujon DrengoΟßan hamtant (e, posc ig l'oΟß alleyanΟß-federaΟß sfondrau d'an 1961, l'entertaΟΑ de Gal e l'eΟß Istr NorΟΑovester).
Hatfell Honours refer to several prizes awarded since the early twentieth century to artists in various fields across the Drengot Collusion (and, after that alliance-federacy collapsed in 1961, across all of Gaul and the Northwestern Isles [Ireland, GB, Borland]).

Y dou Honour ne literatur e ne dram son abrangiΟΑ par y cougl original de veΟΑrdon, cant novel art ern admis tan recent com y dezein 2010.
The Honours in literature and drama are among the original set of prizes, while new arts have been admitted as recently as the 2010s.

L'ec Honour prenn y nom de Hatfell, un toun norΟΑer ny stat citaΟΑer de Londr jos y tarmn contr Marclont stant y mojol d'un communtaΟΑ d'autour parnommaΟΑ ny siecr novoΟß.
The Honours are named for Hatfell [Hatfield, Hertfordshire], a northern town in the city-state of London near the border with Markland which was home to a collective of renowned authors in the nineteenth century.

D'an 1918, y scrittour Dorotha Quistack gajannau un Honour de Hatfell por un entr sell'adolescenΟß.
In 1918, the writer Dorotha Quistack won a Hatfell Honour for one of her coming-of-age novels.

##

172.2: allocquar "address"

allocquar /Υ¨a.l…îΥàkwar/ [Υ¨a.l äΥàkw…êΥê]
- address, speak to, to communicate with by means of speech;
- affect, touch, impact, to have some sort of relevance to or effect upon;
- (dated) court, woo, romance, to endeavour to gain someone's affections

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed (with regularised conjugation modelled after collocquar "converse") from Latin alloquor "I address, greet", derived from the root loquor "I speak". The term is seen in metaphorical use from the eighteenth century, and in romantic contexts from around the same time.

Lor rou y diac ouΟΑ y ley vel y taus terren allocquar.
/l…îr ru i djak uΈΗ i li v…¦l i toz t…¦Υàr…¦n Υ¨a.l…îΥàkwar/
[l…îΥê  Äu i djak uβÄΩΟΑi li v…¦l i toz t…ΣΥà Ä…¦n Υ¨a.l äΥàkw…êΥê]
then ask df journo q df law will.sbj df rate of.land address.inf
The journo then asks if the law will affect land tax.

172.3: incombir "loom"

incombir /Υ¨…Σn.k…îmΥàb…Σr/ [Υ¨…Σ≈΄.k ämΥàb…ΣΥê]
- behove, befit, benefit, suit, to be proper or advantageous for someone to do;
- incite, impel, urge, provoke, to provide an impetus to do;
- press, overlay, to put pressure upon from above;
- loom, tower, to stand tall nearby (usually in a threatening manner)

Etymology: originally in the first senses, a Middle Borlish borrowing from Latin incumb≈ç "I lie upon, I recline". The physical senses are attested later and may be based on the Latin (or may show influence from related descombr "go to bed, lie down to sleep).

T'incombisce te leyar tostessem.
/t…ΣnΥ¨k…îm.biΥàxe te liΥàjar t…îΥàst…¦.s…¦m/
[t…Σ≈΄Υàk…îm.b…ΣΥàΟße te lj…êΥê t äΥàst…¦.s…êm]
2s.obl=behove-ipf 2s.obl raise-inf early-cmp
You should have got up earlier.

172.4: ostendar "blog"

ostendar /Υ¨…î.st…¦nΥàdar/ [Υ¨…î.st…ΣnΥàd…êΥê]
- exhibit, display, to show for public consumption;
- publish, to have (usually a work of writing) publicised via some outlet;
- blog, post, to write (or otherwise create content) and publish on the pistol mesh [Internet]

Etymology: early Modern Borlish, a borrowing (probably via a French or Italian language) from Latin ostend≈ç "I expose to view, reveal". In journalism the term originally connoted commercial writing as opposed to actual news. In the context of the mesh there are attestations from the earliest meshfora in the late 1960s.

Οêe n'aun nien ostendaΟΑ tras dou meis.
/ΟΑe non ni…¦n Υ¨…î.st…¦nΥàdaΈΗ traz du miz/
[ΟΑe non nj…¦n Υ¨…î.st…ΣnΥàdah traz du miz]
3p n=have.3p nothing post-p.pst across 2 month
They've posted nothing in two months.

##

172.5: How to spell Borlish

I've finally started a proper reference work for Borlish; I've finished the orthography section!

Here are the twenty-seven letters of the standard Borlish alphabet, along with their letter names.

The character βÄΙ w βÄΚ, which is used in foreign words and some names (like Willaum βÄ‰WilliamβÄô), is pronounced like βÄΙ v βÄΚ and called vescon βÄ‰second-veeβÄô. There are also some compound characters. The ligatures βÄΙ ΟΠ ≈™ βÄΚ, primarily used in Classical loanwords, are called ΟΠsc and ≈™thel respectively and pronounced as if written βÄΙ e βÄΚ. The character βÄΙ Ο· βÄΚ is referred to as i jammel βÄ‰twin iβÄô and pronounced as if written βÄΙ yi βÄΚ.

In the following we will consider βÄΙ y βÄΚ to be a vowel when written between consonants and/or word boundaries, and a consonant otherwise.

Consonants

The following letters regularly denote a single phoneme.

Of these, the letters βÄΙ b d f l m n p r βÄΚ may appear doubled, which usually does not affect their pronunciation.

    abbaΟΑ joddy affis ollom commet bannir Οßoppin arrum
    /aΥàbaΈΗ  £…îΥàdi aΥàf…Σz …îΥàl…îm k…îΥàm…¦t baΥàn…Σr ts…îΥàp…Σn aΥàr…Σm/
    abbot cuppa tip relic ban start toffee cosine

The only exception to this is that βÄΙ mm nn rr βÄΚ may denote a single consonant followed by a syllabic consonant. This is the reading when βÄΙ mm nn rr βÄΚ occurs word-finally or before a consonant.

    yem-m grin-n car-r
    /Υàj…¦m.mΧ© Υàgr…Σn.nΧ© Υàkar.rΧ©/
    get-1p judge-3p seek-inf

The letter βÄΙ ΟΑ βÄΚ is pronounced /ΈΗ/ word-finally and /ΟΑ/ otherwise; the letter βÄΙ x βÄΚ regularly denotes the phoneme sequence /ks/.

Consider the final six letters βÄΙ c g h s t y βÄΚ when they occur alone (not part of a digraph). In this case, the letters βÄΙ t y βÄΚ regularly denote /t j/ respectively. The letter /t/ may be doubled, without any effect on its pronunciation.

    tien yoc cattin
    /tj…¦n j…îk | kaΥàt…Σn/
    yours husband kitten

The letter βÄΙ c βÄΚ is pronounced /ts/ before βÄΙ e i y βÄΚ and /k/ otherwise. The doubled βÄΙ cc βÄΚ is pronounced /kts/ before βÄΙ e i y βÄΚ and /k/ otherwise.

By contrast, the letter βÄΙ g βÄΚ may be pronounced as either of /g  £/ before βÄΙ e i y βÄΚ and is pronounced /g/ otherwise (and similarly for doubled βÄΙ gg βÄΚ).

    cigl cuivr bac | gevou genoil tenguΟΑ
    /tsajl Υàkajv.rΧ© bak | geΥàvu  £eΥàn…îjl t…¦nΥàg…ΣΈΗ/
    darling copper twig | (watch out) knee bauble

The letter βÄΙ h βÄΚ is either pronounced /h/ or is silent (the latter only in some vocabulary inherited from Latin and after vocalic βÄΙ g βÄΚ); The letter βÄΙ s βÄΚ is pronounced /z/ in coda position or between vowels, and /s/ otherwise or when doubled.

    heu hom augher | bevis bosaΟß bustr bossir
    /hau …îm oΥàj…¦r | beΥàv…Σz boΥàzats Υàb…Σst.rΧ© b…îΥàs…Σr/
    mien person tumbler | note gunfire example gent

Consonant digraphs

The six letters βÄΙ c g h s t y βÄΚ combine with other graphemes to form digraphs. The sequences βÄΙ sc ch th βÄΚ regularly denote the phonemes /x k ΈΗ/. The potentially ambiguous sequence βÄΙ sch βÄΚ is read /sk/. The sequence βÄΙ cq βÄΚ is pronounced /k/.

    sclar chym thral schol jalicq
    /xlar k…Σm ΈΗral sk…îl  £aΥàl…Σk/
    lightning lymph slave school tuxedo

The graphemes βÄΙ g y βÄΚ only form digraphs when combined with vowels, for which see below.
Vowels

Divide written vowels into two groups. Free vowels are either wordfinal or separated from the next vowel in the word by a single consonant grapheme (or one of βÄΙ sc ch th βÄΚ).

    choma epu idiocy nothe
    /koΥàma eΥàpi Υ¨i.djoΥàtsi noΥàΈΗe/
    gnome express stupidity illegitimate

Checked vowels are either in a final closed syllable or separated from the next vowel in the word by at least two consonant graphemes.

    commun ambasctour sincer
    /k…îΥàm…Σn Υ¨am.baxΥàtur s…ΣnΥàts…¦r/
    ordinary emissary genuine

Now consider the letters βÄΙ a e i o u y βÄΚ when they occur alone (not part of a digraph). When they are free, βÄΙ a e i o u y βÄΚ when they occur alone (not part of a digraph). When they are free, βÄΙ a e i o u y βÄΚ are pronounced /a e i o i i/.

    cla vige bro cru try
    /kla viΥà £e bro kri tri/
    key wealthy dude raw turn

When they are checked, βÄΙ a e i o u y βÄΚ are pronounced /a …¦ …Σ …î …Σ …Σ/.

    vindron vampyr effus
    /v…ΣnΥàdr…în vamΥàp…Σr …¦Υàf…Σz/
    vintner vampire extensive

Between a consonant and one of the vowels βÄΙ a e o u βÄΚ, the graphemes βÄΙ i u βÄΚ denote the semivowel βÄΙ j βÄΚ. The grapheme βÄΙ u βÄΚ denotes the semivowel /w/ specifically after βÄΙ q βÄΚ.

    diac suet quelt
    /djak sj…¦t kw…¦lt/
    mic familiar dinner

Vowel digraphs

The digraphs βÄΙ au eu ou βÄΚ are pronounced /o au u/ respectively. The rarer βÄΙ ao eo βÄΚ share their pronunciation with βÄΙ eu βÄΚ.

    taur teun tout | paon theory
    /tor taun tut | paun thauΥàri/
    bull scant everything | peacock science

The digraphs βÄΙ ai ei oi ui βÄΚ and βÄΙ ay ey oy uy βÄΚ are pronounced /e i …îj aj/ respectively. Intervocalically, βÄΙ y βÄΚ does double duty, simultaneously affecting the previous vowel as stated and acting as an onset βÄΙ j βÄΚ.

    deit joivr | rayon huyaΟß
    /dit Υà £…îjv.rΧ© | reΥàj…în haΥàjats/
    finger frost | sunbeam corruption

The vast majority of coda βÄΙ g βÄΚ and some other βÄΙ g βÄΚ before consonants form vowel digraphs. Here, the digraphs βÄΙ ag eg ig og ug aug oug βÄΚ may be pronounced /…¦j …Σj aj …îj aj oj uj/ respectively.

    bag deg rig pog tug faug voug
    /b…¦j d…Σj raj p…îj taj foj vuj/
    berry ten queue few passion sickle wrong

However, there are cases where a βÄΙ g βÄΚ in one of the above environments is read consonantally.

    magr magr | migrar scigrar
    /Υàmag.rΧ© Υàm…¦j.rΧ© | m…ΣΥàgrar xajΥàrar/
    rash scarce | migrate redistrict

173.1: Nicolas of Avignon

notes in translation on the subject of Nicolas of Avignon (Provincial NicolΟ† s Avignon /ni.koΥàl…î sa.viΥà…≤uΧÉ/, fl. 1347).

Nicolas d'Avignon sta un architect Borgond parnommaΟΑ.
Nicolas of Avignon was a renowned Burgund architect.

L'es cos primer ΟΠternaΟΑ desig i condugheu y reconstroinΟß eΟΑ y spanjon dy Cathedral de Sant Sophia ne Stamboul, y citaΟΑ caver nell'Empir Roman Dovem.
He is principally remembered for leading the renovation and expansion of Saint Sophia's Cathedral in Stamboul, the capital city of the Second Roman Empire.

RumoraΟΑ es ig sy benoΟß de lodrignaΟΑ experiencer por y dom central basfeyar ayen y corp principal enter dy cathedral rent precartaΟΑer cos minuscr.
His use of experimental techniques in supporting the central dome are alleged to have rendered the entire main body of the cathedral slightly unstable.

Deut all'oc i dign parveg e cos joculant y dobanΟß de "l'architect dy ruin dell'Empir", la sceint y sfondranΟß tragic dell'edifiΟß a mesur d'un ceremony de coronnaΟß aval de tre cent annaΟΑ ayent gran part ag dynasty imperial occeut (jos larc cent ec may) e l'insorgenΟß akirtaΟΑ volent l'Empir rasgattovar jug.
For this reason he is sometimes jocularly referred to as the "architect of the Empire's demise", given the tragic collapse of the building during a coronation ceremony three hundred years later that killed most of the imperial family (and hundreds more) and sparked the unrest that would end the Empire for good.

173.2: entrquΟ·scoir "break room"

entrquΟ·scoir /Υ¨…¦ntrΧ©Υ¨kwajiΥàx…îir/ [Υ¨…¦n.t…êΥ¨kwajΥàx…î.j…ê]
- break room, a space in a workspace set aside for staff to spend short periods away from work to e.g. eat or talk;
- (metaphorically) respite, a brief interval of rest or relief between times of some unpleasant experience

Etymology: mid-twentieth century, place derivation (using the suffix -oir < -≈çrium in Medieval Latin) from the verb entrquΟ·r "pause, stop work". This is a late medieval borrowing from Latin interquiΡ™sc≈ç "I rest briefly", adapted to native prefix entr- "between" and verb quΟ·r "to put out, turn off".

Y soucorsour all'entrquΟ·scoir dy piscin teyen catr.
/i Υ¨su.k…îrΥàsur aΥ¨l…¦ntrΧ©Υ¨kwajiΥàx…îir di piΥàx…Σn tiΥàj…¦n ΥàkatrΧ©/
[i Υ¨su.k äΥêΥàs äΥêβÄΩ Ä…êΥ¨l…¦n.t…êΥ¨kwajΥàx…î.j…ê di p…ΣΥàΟß…Σn tj…¦n Υàka.t…ê]
df lifeguard at-df=break.room of-df pool number-ipf-3p four
Four lifeguards were in the pool's break room.

173.3: contrdontaΟßon "downfall"

contrdontaΟßon /Υ¨k…întrΧ©Υ¨d…în.taΥàts…în/ [Υ¨k…în.t…êΥàd…în.t…êΥàts…în]
- downfall, fall, doom, demise, defeat, a loss that is particularly noteworthy or spectacular;
- disgrace, humiliation, a sudden nosedive in public opinion of someone or something

Etymology: noun derivation from the verb contrdontar "foil, stymie, thwart". This is a pleonastic extension of now-obsolete dontar "thwart, defeat", which descends from Latin domit≈ç "I tame, I vanquish".

Cay descreuΟßon dy contrdontaΟßon dy Reyaum ag Danau.
/ke Υ¨d…¦.xrauΥàts…în di Υ¨k…întrΧ©Υ¨d…în.taΥàts…în di riΥàjom …¦i daΥàno/

fall description of-df downfall of-df kingdom at.df Danube
It describes the downfall of the Danube Kingdom.

173.4: mesaventoresq "shambolic"

mesaventoresq /Υ¨me.zaΥ¨v…¦n.toΥàr…¦sk/ [Υ¨me.z…êΥ¨v…¦n.t äΥà Ä…¦sk]
- farcical, clownish, slapstick, comprising a series of ridiculous goings-on;
- shambolic, pell-mell, haphazard, with a complete lack of sensible organisation;

Etymology: nineteenth century, a blend of two preexisting words. The first is mesaventur "mishap, accident", which comprises the prefix mes- "badly" (from Germanic) and the obsolete noun aventur "event, chance"; the second is aventoresq "swashbuckling, gleefully adventurous", a borrowing via Parisian from synonymous avventoresco in Tuscan.

Smars sta un cont mesaventoresq e stendiscent.
/smarz sta …Σn k…înt Υ¨me.zaΥ¨v…¦n.toΥàr…¦sk e Υ¨st…¦n.diΥàx…¦nt/
[zm…êΥêz sta …Σ≈΄ k…înt Υ¨me.z…êΥ¨v…¦n.t äΥà Ä…¦sk e Υ¨st…¦n.d…ΣΥàΟß…¦nt]
arise.p.pst be.ipf indf tale farcical and stretch-p.prs
What emerged was an overlong, farcical tale.

173.5: American Toponymy

    excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary o'Mendevan Placenames, a reference quire in the Merch language compiled by Rotcham [Rochdale] University Primers.

Ax /aks/ [~SW Alaska] a peninsular polity in northwestern Mendeva [North America], along with the surrounding mainland region and an extended chain of islands leading west into the northern Lovaqua [Pacific]. 1780 < French l'Axe < poss. Vascon or Portingale l'Axa, a Axa < early 18C Arabic ΊßΌ³ΊΘΌéΌ²Ό£ΊΒΌéΌâ βÄΔ al-Aq≈¦ΟΓ "the Furthest" < folk etymology from Oranger [Aleut] alaxsxaq "mainland", influenced by its northerly location and sheer distance from Mashick [Mexico]. Originally referring specifically to the peninsula, the term was extended to the island chain itself by the Morrack [Moroccan] surveyors who first recorded the term in a Vetmundine [Afroeurasian] language.

Bahassassin /Υ¨ba.h…êΥàsa.s…Σn/ [Mississippi River] the longest and most voluminous river in Mendeva at over twenty hundred leagues, flowing south into the Gulf of the Arcabil [Gulf of Mexico] through Hasiny. 1704 < Mid 17C Welsh Bahassassin < Carovacian [Caddo] bΟΓhassΟΓssin "mother of rivers".

Cocain /k äΥàk…ôin/ [Cuba] the largest of the islands of the Arcabil [Caribbean], or the polity that occupies this region and part of mainland Mendeva. 1530, repurposing of the word's previous meaning of "an imaginary land of folklore with an abundance of food, drink and other pleasures". This dates back to the late 14C < French Cocagne, Lombard Cuccagna, of uncertain further origin. The term has been connected to Willem Dutch cΟΚkye "small cake" < Classical German c≈çco≈ç "cake" and to Old Irish cucann "food ration" < Latin coquΡΪna "kitchen", but both have issues of provenance.

##

174.1: Modern Provence

notes in translation on the later history of Provence.

L'eΟß dezein apres stan l'asc cant y ProvenΟß sovran hurstau y sona d'un republic marcander excellent.
The following decades saw independent Provence take its place as a preeminent mercantile republic.

Ag fin dy siecr seΟß lorry sar marcer aye se spanuΟΑ tras l'ocean Atlantic vars treborth ne Mendeva com Santrafeu nou stabliΟΑ.
By the end of the sixteenth century their trade network reached across the Atlantic to port towns in Mendeva [North America] like the newly-established Santrafew.

L'ec toun volen alcant aposc se combinar all'eiΟΑel de Nou ProvenΟß.
These towns would eventually coalesce into the territory of New Provence.

Ag fin dy siecr novoΟß, y possenΟß broucaΟΑ de Nou ProvenΟß au ombr jait sull'oΟß de sy progentour Vetomondin.
By the end of the nineteenth century, the power of New Provence had eclipsed that of its Vetomundine progenitor.

A mesur dy pestriΟßon norΟΑeressem dy Collujon DrengoΟßan, ProvenΟß dene cas monfeyaΟΑ un fragossau European de Nou ProvenΟß, com envarjon de lorry magntumn a cascun ec siecr paravant.
As the Drengot Collusion formed to the north, Provence became in many respects a European outpost of New Provence, in a reversal of their respective statures centuries earlier.

Y region luΟ·sce cosogr Vascony y sona de bastegl contr paramontaΟΑ DrengoΟßan fin ag Tourf d'an 1961 eΟΑ y sfondranΟß dy Collujon DrengoΟßan.
The region stood with Vascony as a bulwark against Drengot hegemony until the Furore of 1961 and the collapse of the Drengot Collusion.

##

174.2: de sanc ΟΑus "horny"

de sanc ΟΑus /de sank ΟΑ…Σz/ [de sa≈΄k ΟΑ…Σz]
- hot-blooded, hot-tempered, heated, easily provoked to anger or excitement;
- passionate, ardent, zealous, fervent, given to strong expression of emotions;
- horny, lustful, torrid, beset by sexual feelings

Etymology: idiom attested from the seventeenth century, coined approximately at the same time as de sanc freit "cold-blooded, emotionless", literally meaning "of burning blood". The noun sanc "blood" descends directly from Latin sanguis "blood" (albeit from a Vulgar oblique form like sanguem rather than Classical sanguinem). The adjective ΟΑus "on fire" is the past participle of now-archaic verb ΟΑurr "burn (tr.), set on fire", which reflects Latin ad≈Ϊr≈ç "I kindle, set fire to". In the last senses the phrase is attested from the nineteenth century.

I stima vos fosseΟΑ un hom troΟß e de sanc ΟΑus.
/i stiΥàma v…îz f…îΥàs…¦ΈΗ …Σn …îm tr…îts e de sank ΟΑ…Σz/
[i st…ΣΥàma v…îz f äΥàs…¦βÄΩΟΑ…Σn …îm t Ä…îts e de sa≈΄k ΟΑ…Σz]
3s think-ipf 2p be.sbj.ipf-2p indf person grim and of blood afire
He thought you were fierce and hot-blooded.

174.3: bland "interesting"

bland /bland/ [bland]
- suave, debonair, urbane, affable, charming, of smooth and sociable demeanour;
- well-liked, popular, widely admired by people;
- interesting, entertaining, compelling, engaging people's attention;
- complicated, detailed, having a multitude of facets or features;
- sophisticated, worldly, cosmopolitan, having had plenty of varied experiences and lacking naΟ·vetΟ©

Etymology: Middle Borlish, a borrowing from Latin blandus "pleasant, agreeable; fawning, flattering". In the first senses the word originally had a somewhat negative or insincere connotation; by the eighteenth century the word is mostly seen in uncomplicatedly positive use.

Racont mey dy domn bland voscon encontraΟΑ.

174.4: plentivous "brimming"

plentivous /Υ¨pl…¦n.tiΥàvuz/ [Υ¨pl…¦n.t…ΣΥàvuz]
- brimming, awash, flush, overflowing, in such an abundance as to be almost too much;
- (in physics) charged, having an electrical charge;
- suspenseful, tense, thrilling, expectant, filled with the tension of awaiting an uncertain outcome or event

Etymology: late Middle Borlish, borrowed from French plentiveus "flush, rich". This is a derivation from plentif "abundant" and plentΟ© "abundance", via Latin plΡ™nitΡ¹s "fullness" from plΡ™nus "full". The emotional senses, attested from the seventeenth century, appear to evoke the danger of an overfull vessel. In scientific use the word is modelled on prior plentaΟΑ "fullness; (physic) electric charge" (compare the analogous plentiful "charged" in Merch).

Sceye un ouΟß plentivous cant alcun hom parolau.
/xiΥàje …Σn uts Υ¨pl…¦n.tiΥàvuz kant alΥ¨k…Σn …îm Υ¨pa.roΥàlo/
[Οß…ΣΥàje …Σn us Υ¨pl…¦n.t…ΣΥàvuz kant Υ¨a.g…ΣΥàn…îm Υ¨pa. Ä äΥàlo]
there.be-ipf indf silence awash when no person speak-pst
There was a tense silence before anyone spoke.

174.5: False Friends (cont.)

some more completely arbitrary dictionary entries from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (fifth edition), a reference work published in 1961 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

cockapple [/Υàk…îkΥ¨a.p…ôl/ "apricot"] n. a stone fruit resembling the passock [peach] and in the plum group, with pink-yellow flesh and downy skin; the tree which bears this fruit; a colour or dye in the colour of this fruit. Late 17C, variant rationalised to usual Merch compounding patterns < late 15C applecock, variant by analogy to "apple" of apricock, abrecock < French abricoc < Barcelon abrecoc, albercoc < Morrove ΊßΌ³Ί®Ί±Ό²ΌàΌ² βÄΔ al-barq≈Ϊq < Malchassian Έ≤ΈΒœ¹ΈΙΈΚΈΩΈΚΈΚΈ·Έ± βÄΔ bericoccΟ≠a < Latin (mΡ¹lum) praecoquum "early-ripening apple".

handiwork [/Υàhan.d…ΣΥ¨w…îΥêk/ "surgery"] n. the medical practice of using incisions to remove, replace or repair an internal part of the body; such an individual procedure; (archaic or jocular) any manual labour, work that is done by hand. 16C in the modern sense, possibly influenced by synonymous Welsh ≈²odrinneth "hand-treatment" < Old English hand»ùeweorc "manual labour; handcraft, something made by hand", a compound of hand "hand" and »ùeweorc "piece of work".

ough [/uΥêf/ "bad"] adj. of low quality or standard; incorrect or mistaken; sickly or unwell; faulty or dysfunctional, clumsy or unskilled. Early Merch wough, wo»ù [woΥêx] "wrong, incorrect" < Old English w≈çh, w≈çg "crooked, bent; wrong".

penguin [/p…ΣnΥàgj…ôw…Σn/ "pangolin"] n. any of several species of long-tailed and scaly ferents [mammals] found in tropical Africa and Asia; a game of cards played for money which originated in Bengal. Early 18C via earlier attestations in other European languages < BΡ¹san [Malay] penguwing "one who rolls". In the second sense, the word is a calque from Bengal bonoro "penguin, apparently named for a deck which featured art of a pangolin on some of the cards.

excerpt in translation from 1967 trevold Un A≈™d des Uisq ("A Bard out of Owisk", fourth book in the Xiphary "Sellswords" series), written by a Borlish writing duo who collectively went by the name Pincerno (possibly based on Latin pincerna "cupbearer").

175.4: yemal "cool"

yemal /jeΥàmal/ [ ù…ΣΥàmal]
- wintry, chilly, cold, pertaining to or reminiscent of winter;
- standoffish, cool, making no overtures of politeness or friendliness;
- stern, severe, strict, acting without flexibility or mercy

Etymology: Middle Borlish (h)iemal "wintry", borrowed from Latin hiemΡ¹lis "pertaining to winter", from hiems "winter". The term lost its initial h- (unlike most contemporaneous Latin borrowings) due to contamination from native yarn "winter" < Latin hΡΪbernum "winter". Emotional extension of the term is seen shortly thereafter, in parallel to stival "summery, friendly".

##

175.5: Stillian

collected notes on the subject of King Stillian, a medieval Borlish ruler.

Stillian of Borland (r. ca. 790βÄ™823 N, Borlish Stiglan /stajΥàlan/) was king of Roman Borland in the late eighth and early ninth centuries. This so-called First Kingdom of Borland had grown out of the sub-Roman Diocese of Boral in the early medieval period, having become a monarchy in tandem with (and possibly influenced by) the new petty kingdoms settled by Germanic peoples in the west and south of the island of Borland.

Like all kings of Roman Borland, Stillian claimed to be descended from Victor Rossetus, the semi-mythic figure who united much of the land once external Roman authority receded in the fifth century. The name Stillian descends from Latin HostΡΪliΡ¹nus, attested also as the name of a Roman emperor who ruled briefly in 251 N.

Stillian is primarily remembered for actions which put an end to the perennial Tripartite Wars and unifed the island under one polity for the first time since the Roman Empire. This term refers to a centuries-long series of conflicts over territory and fealty, principally between Roman Borland and the kingdoms of Southbar and Angland; by the eighth century these three powers controlled the vast majority of the island. Kings of Borland had made temporary alliances before with either of Southbar and Angland.

In 803, Stillian's son Marc was married to Alfegh of Angland's only child (variously recorded as Raunilt or Raunvig, likely from Old English hrΟΠfn "raven" and a name element like hild or wig "war, battle"). The two royal dynasties had been connected by marriage before, and this wedding allowed both kingdoms to claim continuity with the new unified polity. Approximately seven years later, Southbar was forced to pledge fealty to the new kingdom after the Battle of Fendal.

##

176.1: New Urban Mesh (cont.)

additional notes in translation on the subject of the New Urban Mesh, an alliance of autonomous cities in Northwest Europe in the nineteenth century.

Y citaΟΑ ny Sar son alligaΟΑ lonc y gentagl con y politic dell'entrlaΟßorant, tan com y benoΟß aggressionner e mondial de spionnaΟß. Interim, outrvaluΟΑ cos plausibr es diΟßr alcot y Sar aye (com bustr) y capabiltaΟΑ de gournment nell'Aust dirigir por gra de lorry commarΟß.
The cities of the Mesh are popularly associated with wheeling-and-dealing policy, as well as aggressive and global use of espionage. However, the extent to which the Mesh was (for example) able to steer politics in the East to their trade benefit is likely overestimated.

Ny siecr novoΟß nuverrem, Londr amenau y Sar vars union cos politic con y Collujon DrengoΟßan, oc stant temporane un alleyanΟß smargent dy polity Fransc de Cantuer Norman, Paris e Borgogn. Ou vrayessem es digr ig y Sar denoy sousunt ny Collujon, ben ig y Sar broucau auteΟß dy Collujon, interman un souject closnautr de disputaΟßon ΟΑus. 
In the late nineteenth century, London led the Mesh to unite politically with the Drengot Collusion, which at the time was a nascent alliance of the French polities of Norman Kent, Paris and Burgundy. Whether it is more correct to say that the Mesh was subsumed into the Collusion, or that the Mesh took control of the Collusion, has long been a topic of heated debate.

Sr Desarden, PrinΟß NuΟßal (1968), un lasc font ayent des y scandal d'an 1887 atorn de James Desarden, l'ambasctour Londresc a Nacon Day. Creut cos general es ig l'oc affer aye raΟßon politic e se produghe des stancoranΟß prose par y Sar.
Mr Desarden, Prince Consort (1968), a film based on the 1887 scandal around James Desarden, London's ambassador to Nackon Day [~Thailand]. The affair is widely thought to have been politically motivated and a product of direct meddling from the Mesh.

176.2: n'impisantour "carefree"

n'impisantour /n…ΣmΥ¨pi.zanΥàtur/ [n…ΣmΥ¨pi.z…ênΥàt äΥê]
- weightless, in freefall, accelerating due to gravitional forces alone;
- unencumbered, unburdened, not carrying any load;
- carefree, untroubled, unconcerned, not feeling any worry;
- insouciant, blithe, nonchalant, ignoring any potential concerns (perhaps recklessly or impolitely);

Etymology: 1812, coined along with the noun impisantour "weightlessness" during the voidtale craze of popular fiction set out among the stars. The noun is from the much older pisantour "weightiness", itself from adjective pisant "weighty, burdensome". These all derive originally from pis "weight", the descendant of synonymous pΡ™nsum in Latin. In extended use the word is attested from the late nineteenth century, with the slightly disparaging connotation established shortly thereafter.

You will feel weightless for four minutes.

176.3: jom "cobalt"

jom / £…îm/ [ ù…îm]
- cobalt, the corporal [element] with twenty-seven mora [protons] and which is a hard and lustrous silver-grey metal;
- deep blue, a colour produced from the ash [oxide] or chalk [carbonate] of this corporal (or a similar hue of any origin)

Etymology: sixteenth century from Scholastic Latin iomus or Tuscan jomo, originally referring to a sort of blue pigment containing cobalt often used to dye glass. These are trade-words borrowed from the east, via (Bagliemman) Arabic from Turk or Persian Ί§ΊßΌÖ βÄΔ jΡ¹m "glass". The term was repurposed for the metal once it was isolated in the late seventeenth century.

176.4: gevrment "circuitry"

gevrment /Υ¨g…¦vrΧ©Υàm…¦nt/ [Υ¨g…¦.v…êΥàm…¦nt]
- wiring, circuitry, a system of electrical components and their connections within a particular device;
- nitty-gritty, nuts and bolts, the (possibly figurative) inner workings of any object or concept;
- minutiae, intricacies, the (often trivial) tiny or precise details of a situation

Etymology: derivation with the gestalt suffix -ment < Latin -mentum from the noun gevr "electrical wire". This is a borrowing via synomous guΟ®vre in Parisian from Welsh guivr "wire". In turn this appears to have been borrowed from Old English wΡΪr "metal thread", likely influenced in form by guevr "amber" due to the latter's electrical properties. In metaphorical use the term is seen from the mid-twentieth century.

Din noc divertiΟΑ par gevrment.
/d…Σn n…îk Υ¨di.v…¦rΥàt…ΣΈΗ par Υ¨g…¦vrΧ©Υàm…¦nt/

become.sbj ng distract-p.pst by wiring
Don't be distracted by minutiae.

##

176.5: The Light that Casts a Thousand Shadows

The Light that Casts a Thousand Shadows (originally published in Borlish as Un Flam, Mil Ombr "1 Flame, 1000 Shadows") was a 2017 N work of pop history, economics and philosophy written by Ollamec Vamncoury and published by a subsidiary of the Peujon Rous (Red Dove) media guild.

Her book chronicles the duelling ideologies of Ubiquity and Vicinity, whose rivalry defined many of the greatest conflicts of the twentieth century and which together form the basis for most modern schools of political though. In broad strokes, Ubiquity stands for equality, universalism and homogeneity, while Vicinity valourises liberty, localism and diversity.

Despite various administrations' propaganda, neither ideology holds the undisputed moral high ground. Vicinity has been linked to the flourishing of regional languages and to the freedom to pursue nontraditional familial structures; it has also been used to justify the formation of ghettos and even ethnostates. Ubiquity has led to the toppling of truly oppressive hierarchies in the name of egalitarian redistribution of wealth and power; it has also marginalised and attempted to extirpate elements of society that do not fit into one of the many visions for society that leaders have propounded.

However, the point of Ollamec's work is in fact to complicate the idea that these two frameworks are naturally opposed and completely contradictory. She achieves this in part by noting the many times that similar policies have been justified by appeals to both Ubiquity and Vicinity in different times or places. For example, the modern Runeplate Body headquartered in Lengrod [Lviv] determines the global standard for glyphs in almost all known writing systems. This centralisation is undoubtedly Ubiquitous in nature, yet the inclusion of such a diversity of scripts is a far cry from post-Millstone Mashick [Mexico] and their ambitions of "one world language".

##

177.1: Laic Declarations

notes in translation on the Laic Declarations.

Y Declar Laic intermanen un series de document traitancer prodoit par un consegl tras tarmn de delegaΟßon des doΟßan plusour de polity ne Belgrad d'an 1850.
The Laic Declarations were a series of diplomatic documents produced by an international committee of delegations from several dozen polities in Belgrade in 1850.

L'ec document forgaurn un ossatur de lodrignaΟΑ por religion statuer desjovar deur y foncΟßon administraΟßonnal de polity, e por y stat cas ley de gent a fieltaΟΑ mendrancer.
These documents outlined procedures for uncoupling established religions from administrative state functions, and the legal status of people with minority faiths.

All'oc epoc, Belgrad sta sodal ny CitaΟΑ Floyan, un alleyanΟß-federaΟß de picq polity autonom lonc y Floy Danau e sy confluyenΟß.
At this time, Belgrade was one of the Riverine Cities, an alliance-federacy of small independent states along the Danaw River and its tributaries.

L'ec citaΟΑ ern leyaΟΑ des y cendr dy Reyaum Danavan, oc stant sfondraΟΑ de faΟßon spectacler posc y Var Theudesc Dovem ny dezein 1820.
These cities had risen from the ashes of the Kingdom of the Danaw, which had collapsed in spectacular fashion after the Second German War of the 1820s.

Cossy scient, Belgrad sta un amaΟßgat ΟΑescant primal por doctrin novel cas religion e ley, ant un Οßaucr Promethean parnommaΟΑ alligaΟΑ ag commouΟΑ Desveyant eΟΑ un communtaΟΑ tovaresc meg ovart.
As such, Belgrade was an early hotbed of new religious and legal thinking, with a renowned Promethean club associated with the Deviance movement and a semi-public tovarick [queer] community.

177.3: soucorsour "lifeguard"

soucorsour /Υ¨su.k…îrΥàsur/ [Υ¨su.k äΥêΥàs äΥê]
- (historical) bodyguard, attendant, someone who protects a dignitary (often a monarch) from direct harm;
- lifeguard, lifesaver, an expert swimmer employed to rescue people at risk of drowning;
- (by extension) saviour, hero, someone who has just saved the speaker from a dangerous or embarrassing situation

Etymology: Middle Borlish, an agentive derivation from verb soucorrir "rescue, go and help, come to the aid of". This descends from Latin succurr≈ç "I help, aid, succour", adapted synchronically to the etymological sou- "under" prefix and corrir "run" root. The historical sense had been out of use for approximately two centuries before the modern senses appear, and so the latter are presumably an independent rederivation.

177.4: destourmar "demob"

destourmar /Υ¨d…¦.sturΥàmar/ [Υ¨d…¦.st äΥêΥàm…êΥê]
- demobilise, demob, to release from military service;
- decommission, sunset, shelve, to cease supporting the use of or to replace with an updated version;

##

177.5: C is for Chronology

collected notes on features of the Boralverse alternate history that begin with the letter C.

Coscatl bint InΟΓm (fl. 1869) was a Mashick [Mexican] astronomer. She was born in the then-young city of Moshtar [~San Francisco]; she received her further education at the University of Anawack [~Mexico City] and the nearby observatory at Chapel Hill [Chapoltepec]. As an astronomer, bint InΟΓm is remembered for discovering and helping to name several astronomical objects. These include the moons of Mars, Dimus [Phobos] and Phobus [Deimos], and most famously the eighth-most distant planet of Janus [Neptune]. Furthermore, her calculations of the perceived timing of the jovial phares [Galiliean moons] in their transits of Jupiter helped to pin down the speed of light.

Crain (Bavarn /kr…ëΥên/, Borlish Carniol /karΥànj…îl/) is a region of Central Europe, east of Furlain [Friuli] and north of Crovatia [Croatia]. During the Second Tetrarchy of the eleventh and twelfth centuries it was a march in the southeasternmost part of the German Empire, initially automonous but later subordinate to the Kingdom of Bavarn. It was prosperous at this time mostly due to trade into its coastal city of Trieste. During the German Secession of the thirteenth century, when many polities broke ties with the Roman Pope in favour of the (hastily established) Augsburg See, the then-duchy of Crain supported the Roman Papacy. This was likely more from pragmatism than religious fervour, as its western and southern neighbours were both partisan of Rome. Nonetheless, the region become an important Roman stronghold, and the base from which it undertook the Baptism of the North.

Cuonrat Aihuosc (fl. c8?, name often Latinised to Corradus Aevoscus) was the dubiously historical founder of the noble house of Avosche and namesake of the region of Avosche in Willemy [~Lotharingia]. His surname is of obscure origin; the first element presumably reflects Classical German ehu- "horse", while the second may be from suffix -iscas "-ish" (though the vowel is problematic).

##

178.1: The Romantic Wars

notes in translation on the Romantic Wars.

Y Rix Romannesc intermanen un cougl circonscreut cos flois de var, de smout e d'autr conflict tras Europ ec ayent loy fis a mesur atorn dy mojol dy siecr settoΟß.
The Romantic Wars were a loosely-circumscribed collection of wars, uprisings and other conflicts across Europe which took place through the middle of the seventeenth century.

Com un entertaΟΑ il son cossiraΟΑ parveg com y derran conflict vecinabr veram, dec ny siecr sevent tal rix de magntumn comportant volen y Novomont envouvr, e tan volen alleyanΟß abrangir entr y possenΟß n'Europ, Africa eΟΑ Asia cos parey.
As a whole they are sometimes considered the last truly localised conflicts, since by the next century wars of similar scales would begin to involve the Novomund, and would feature alliances between the powers of Europe, Africa and Asia alike.

L'ec an des 1640 vars 1670 afferjarn l'eΟß conflict 'central' dy Rix Romannesc, con tan certan abatment peripherallessem fonn paravent ben apres.
The years 1640 to 1670 cover the 'central' conflicts of the Romantic Wars, although some more peripheral fights may spill earlier or later.

L'envou d'Istr Boral cas y Rix Romannesc no sta limitaΟΑ for an envajon imparyent den (ersteigon faint ag CapitulaΟßon a HaukirΟß d'an 1632), jos concort commotual con polity collindant sur auxigl militer por lorry concarnΟß.
Borland's involvement in the Romantic Wars was restricted to a failed invasion (culminating in the Hawkirch Surrender of 1632), along with unstable arrangements with neighbouring polities regarding military aid in their conquests.

178.2: locomoΟßon "travel"

locomoΟßon /Υ¨lo.ko.moΥàtson/ [Υ¨lo.k ä.m äΥàts…în]
- locomotion, motion, movement, the act or capability of changing position by going from one place to another;
- travel, the activity of going from place to place (usually when in a vehicle like a car or train);
- (physics) displacement, the vector quantity measuring the difference between two points in space

Etymology: sixteenth century, borrowed from Scholastic Latin locom≈çti≈ç "movement from place to place", from Classical Latin noun locus "place" and verb move≈ç "I move". The word is sometimes in technical contexts contrasted with moΟßon "motion of parts of a body relative to other parts". Its mathematical sense is attested from the eighteenth century as the vector counterpart of scalar lonctumn "length, distance".

AugrimeΟΑ y locomoΟßon entr y dou termin dy camin.
/Υ¨o.griΥàm…¦ΈΗ i Υ¨lo.ko.moΥàts…în …¦ntrΧ© i du terΥàm…Σn di kaΥàm…Σn/
[Υ¨o.g Ä…ΣΥàm…¦βÄΩΟΑi Υ¨lo.k ä.m äΥàts…în Υà…¦n.tβÄΩ Äi du t…ΣΥêΥàm…Σn di k…êΥàm…Σn]

178.3: malestr "discomfort"

malestr /maΥàl…¦strΧ©/ [m…êΥàl…¦.st…ê]
- unease, disquiet, concern, worry, a dissatisfaction with how things are unfolding;
- discomfort, soreness, irritation, issues with how one is physically feeling;
- bad mood, gloom, pessimism, a tendency to see things in the worst possible light;
- snit, moodiness, sulk, a propensity to displays of bad temper and impatience

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish malestre "discomfort". This was a compound of mal "bad", which descends from synonymous malus in latin, and estre "being, state" (a former infinitive of "be" since replaced by star), which descends from Latin essere "to be". The word has extended to cover various bad or unpleasant emotions, competing for space with hougr "fear, annoyance, discontent" < Latin augurium "omen, warning, foreboding".

No sceye l'hour por commorar ne malestr.

ng there.be-ipf df=time for tarry in unease

178.4: y voΟß se hoccovar "clear one's throat"

y voΟß se hoccovar /i v…îts se Υ¨h…î.koΥàvar/ [i v…îts se Υ¨h…î.k äΥàv…êΥê]
- clear one's threat, harrumph, to cough in order to make it easier to speak or to attract people's attention to you

Etymology: idiom attested from the eighteenth century, literally "to cough the voice". The noun voΟß "voice" descends directly from synonymous vox, vocis in Latin. The verb hoccovar "cough" is a derivation from noun hocco "cough, ahem, hem-hem", thought to be of onomatopoeic origin.

Tu te hoccovau y voΟß e cou ouΟß.
/ti te Υ¨h…î.koΥàvo i v…îts e ku uts/
[ti te Υ¨h…î.k äΥàvo i v…îts e ku uts]
2s 2s.obl ahem-pst df voice and fall-pst silence
You went "ahem" and silence fell.

##

178.5: CampottonΟ§ and Gorram

some notes on a pair of cities in New Provence, situated across a bay from each other.

CampottonΟ§ (Doccian /kaΧÉΥ¨p…î.tuΥàni/; Merch /k…êmΥàp…î.t ä.ni/) is a coastal city on the southern bank of the Guittoc [Delaware] river, in the Nappejoquin[1] region of New Provence. Founded in the late seventeenth century as a Provincial staddomain, it was intended to be a major trading port and to control the flow of goods along the Guittoc.

The second half of the placename reflects the Nappe term [Unami] utaney "city"; the first half is of obscure origin (clearly adapted to Doccian camp "field") but may be related to Nappe necama "first". In this case the toponym likely means "mother-city, home city"; compare the similar formation of ΈΦΈΖœ³œ¹œ¨œÄΈΩΈΜαΩêœ² "metropolis" in Ancient Greek. 

The settlement languished through the eighteenth century due to unfavourable marshy terrain and the better harbours across the river and further inland. Fortunes changed in the nineteenth with an influx of funds: a major shipping canal across the peninsula (connecting the Guittoc to the wealthy Esquanna [Susquehanna] river system) was dug, and the marshlands surrounding CampottonΟ§ were drastically canalised and drained. Further widening of the canal took place into the twentieth century.

Briefly known as the "Venice of Mendeva [North America]", CampottonΟ§ gained a fashionable reputation during the Good Game Period [late 19C] and by the modern day had risen to become a cultural and economic powerhouse.

Gorram (Doccian /guΥàram/; Merch /Υàg…î.…Ψ…ôm/) is a coastal city on the northern bank of the Guittoc [Delaware] river, in the Nappejoquin region of New Provence. It had extremely humble beginnings as a single Provincial farmhouse or possibly a collection of pastures.

There are Provincial dialect terms gorra "goat" and gorram "herd of goats" cognate with cavra and cavram in standard DoccianβÄîthe suffix -am forming collective nouns descends from -(Ρ¹)men in LatinβÄîand this is generally agreed to be the origin of the placename; compare the similar formation of Gotham (Merch /Υàg…î.t…ôm/), a village in Markland.

For a time in the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries, Gorram became by far the most important city on the river, having a better natural harbour. It also participated in the convoy [train, rail] craze of the mid-nineteenth century, being the terminus of an arterial line to the northeast.

Even into the twentieth century Gorram was a major hub of industry on a par with CampottonΟ§. However, by the modern day a lack of infrastructural investment in its own drained land and an inability to deindustrialise gracefully had led the city to become much poorer than its counterpart across the bay.

[1] approximately the watersheds of the Delaware and the lower Hudson.

179.1: The Duke of Dark Corners

excerpt in translation from The Duke of Dark Corners (originally published in Welsh as Dug y Gilfah Dow≈², meaning the same thing), an 1884 work of historical parachthony [speculative fiction] by author Taguen ah Owain. Her alternate version of the Medieval Imperia (a period of European and circum-Mediterranean centralisation in the eleventh and twefth centuries) features an entirely fictitious set of empires ruled not from Paris, Bardwick, Stamboul and Cairo [1], as in reality, but instead from Damvath [capital of Borland], Florence, Tanger and Kiew.

Y nievr mattin se penen sur y pil n'ollem dy havan cant FraΟΑr Aldric folla un camin tras y rout bassem ny citaΟΑ caver.
The morning mist clung to the harbor's elmwood quays as Brother Aldric made his way through the lower streets of the capital.

Le dessur, y gran tour cas y cancelry imperial faye gavlot ny ciel gris com un aguil ne pieΟΑr, sy gonfan drappeyan sou y vent saleyaΟΑ souvolant ne haut des ec aug Rustighan.
Above him, the great tower of the imperial chancery pierced the grey sky like a stone needle, its banners snapping in the salt wind that swept up from the Rustigh waters.

A cost dell'oΟß edifiΟß imposant, yon autr annaΟΑ y pentis de pescour ayen y rif parsemnaΟΑ, sceyen aglon sdroy plen de seΟΑ Toscan e d'arom Morrou, ant ossatur d'egnam fosc des anΟßantaΟΑ e roΟßon a mar.
Beside that imposing edifice, where once fishermen's huts had dotted the shoreline, now stood warehouses filled with Tuscan silk and Morrove spices, their timber frames dark with age and sea-spray.

Un marcant ne vestiΟΑ Flamesc er travodant a haut voΟß contr un maistr de quarf sur y pisantour de sy carc.
A merchant in Flemish cloth was loudly haggling with a dock-keeper over the weight of his cargo.

Aldric tirau sy rob broun a sarraΟΑessem atorn dy spal e tenoy a descendr sur peyan sdruΟßollabr.
Aldric pulled his brown robe closer about his shoulders and continued downhill over slick cobblestones.

===

[1] The Medieval Imperia, or Second Tetrarchy, comprised: the First Drengot Empire ruled from Paris; the German Empire often ruled from Bardwick [Bardowick, Lower Saxony]; the Second Roman Empire ruled from Stamboul [Istanbul]; the Single Caliphate ruled from Cairo.
