179.2: martelment "thud"

martelment /Υ¨mar.t…¦lΥàm…¦nt/ [Υ¨m…êΥê.t…ΣlΥàm…¦nt]
- hammering, pounding, the act of hitting something with a blunt implement;
- thud, thump, whack, knock, the sound made by a blunt collision;
- emphasis, accent, stress, focus, drawing attention to something particular

Etymology: noun derivation from martellar "hammer, pound; emphasise, accentuate", which itself comes from noun martel "hammer". This descends from Late Latin martellus "hammer", a variant diminutive of synoymous marculus in Classical Latin. This term developed its metaphorical senses in tandem with the verb, during the late Middle Borlish period.

J'ama y martelment sur huyaΟß nell'oraΟßon.

179.3: Οßosc "stubby"

Οßosc /ts…îx/ [ts…îx]
- stubby, notably short and thick;
- shortened, docked, cut short, stunted, made shorter or prevented from growing longer;
- squat, stocky, (of a person) short and solidly built;
- thick, broad, relatively great in extent across its shortest solid dimension

Etymology: adjectival use of Old Borlish noun Οßosc "stump of a tree", which was a variant (likely borrowed from Οßoche in Old French) of native cognate Οßoc "stump". These descend from Vulgar Latin tsucca "stump, trunk", though to come via Gaulish from Classical German stuccas "stick, beam; stump". The word was replaced in its noun use by taisc "stump", possibly from Latin taxeus "made of yew wood".

179.4: indagour "sleuth"

indagour /Υ¨…Σn.daΥàgur/ [Υ¨…Σn.d…êΥàg äΥê]
- investigator, detective, sleuth, a person employed to discover things that are unknown or hidden;
- tracker, tracer, a person or tool that keeps an eye on where someone or something is;
- analyser, scanner, any tool that extracts information from a source

Etymology: agentive derivation from verb indagar "track, investigate, scan, analyse", which is a sixteenth-century borrowing from Latin indΡ¹g≈ç "I track, trace; I investigate". The word was used only for people until the late nineteenth century, when the word was extended to various devices able to autonomously track or analyse things.

Nos ayau l'indagour scos ne sell'azig.

We'd hidden the tracker in his shoe.

179.5: Eastern History

excerpt from a 2021 book called Excourse Puzzlers, published by the Royal Tommarth Castinghouse. This was a collection of casual party games using the branding and popularity of a long-running series begun by Merch couple Anna and Ralph Highwater in the 1930s.

History of Eastern Asia I (answers on sheet 144)

1. Which Southern Emperor was it who, upon hearing of the planned circumnavigation of the globe by Morrack [Moroccan] and British [~Welsh] ships, issued the fateful Hangthen Edict in 1520?

4. Which Cathayan captain christened the Chrysian [Australian] territory of New Leudong [SE coast], named for the peninsula Leudong [Liaodong] in Cathay?

5. While extracting saltpetre from sea wrack, what purplish corporal [element] was discovered in the Contare [CαΚßn ThΤΓ] mills of Zampanagar [~Vietnam]?

6. The late fourteenth century saw the final and permanent sundering of Cathay from China, precipitated by the end of which imperial dynasty?

7. Who was the London ambassador to Nackon Day [~Thailand] who became the subject of a major scandal in 1887 involving the King's eldest son?

8. What was the name of the first Chinese ship to alight in the Novomund?

9. Which modern polity claims descent from the Cammojan Kingdom [~Khmer Empire]?

10. Who wrote The Sea Peoples, a sixteenth-century epic of exploration considered one of the greatest Chinese works ever written?

180.1: The Kernovan Conjuring

notes in translation on the subject of the 1029 Kernovan Conjuring, by which Normandy  conquered Kent.

Con tan alcun bustr tesmogn d'un concort scaut sein survescuΟΑ, presunt es ig y Majon de Cornou soloy droug a Normandy par lorry gimmolt ag thron Cantuer com troc contr denir y novel dynasty regnant sur Magn Dorogn.
Although no references to a written treaty have survived, it is presumed that the House of Kernow aided Normandy in their bid for the Kentish throne in exchange for support in becoming the new ruling dynasty of Greater Devon.

L'oc squid er mantenuΟΑ, ma cayen for un per de dezein pre Magn Dorogn denoy un vassal sou y Preu Empir DrengoΟßan.
This bargain was upheld, but it only took a couple of decades for Greater Devon to become a vassal of the First Drengot Empire.

180.2: ensign "suit"

ensign /…¦nΥàsain/ […ΣnΥàsain]
- emblem, logo, symbol, insignia, a distinguishing mark or design;
- token, badge, a physical object decorated with such a mark;
- designation, role, rank, honour, position, office, entitlement to privileges and responsibility for duties in some sort of hierarchy;
- (cards) suit, each of the sets of playing cards distinguished by a particular small symbol

Etymology: Middle Borlish, a semilearned borrowing from Latin insignia "emblem, badge", a noun formation from adjective insignis "marked, distinguished". The word has been used metonymically to refer to the position conferred or represented by an insignia since the fifteenth century, and it has been used in card games from approximately the same time.

180.3: vanaglous "jargony"

vanaglous /Υ¨va.n…¦iΥàluz/ [Υ¨va.n…ΣiΥàluz]
- vernacular, vulgar, colloquial, slangy, basilectal, demotic, coming from informal speech as used in lower-class contexts;
- jargony, technical, filled with specialised or technical vocabulary;
- obscurantist, obfuscating, sesquipedalian, grandiloquent, difficult to comprehend due to overuse of complicated or rare verbiage

This reflects Old Borlish vanagle "the Borlish language (as opposed to Latin)", which is thought to reflect Latin vernΡ¹culus "native, domestic". This comes from the root verna, which refers to a homeborn slave.

##

180.5: 19th Century Novels

summaries of a few works of fiction set in the nineteenth century.

The Red Man (originally in Bulgar as –€a–Εa―² –≤–ß–Β―Ä–≤–Β–Ϋ–Ψ "The Man in Red") is a fright tale written by Petcov in 1830s Sreddies [Sofia] and set a decade earlier. This is the aftermath of the Second German War and the concomitant disintegration of the Kingdom of the Danaw [Danube]. The book features newly-independent Boxa [Novi Sad], the recovering shell of Stamboul [Istanbul], and many of the cities of the region which would eventually band together to form the so-called "Riverine Cities". The Red Man has heroes trying to decipher the mysterious figure known as Zechariah, a vampire; this work codified many of the tropes of the vampire, such as fire, blood and butterflies.

Rasgatto (originally in Zahid Russian as –†–Α–Ζ–≥–Α–¥―²–Ψ "Unknotting") is a mystery romance of the 1870s written by Olexi Harchenko in Boxa. The work was sufficiently popular that its title was borrowed into Venise Italian to mean "resolution of a mystery" and later simply "dΟ©nouement"; subsequently the term made it into many European languages.

Flaxen Hearts (originally in Mostara as Querdos Llineros "Flaxen Hearts") is a 1919 sensation trevold [novel] in the nascent genre of social comedy popularised by the lovetales of Clarissa Bellamy and the adolescences of Dorotha Quistack, among others. The author Judeta Barracin wrote the work after returning from living for many years in the Arcabil [Caribbean]. It is set in the early nineteenth century and follows the petty dramas of a group of siblings in Cordova [Cordoba].

Earthly Scene (originally in Vascon as Vista Terrena "Landscape") is a trevold first published in 1809 by AntΟ≤in Herrel in the Vascon city of Santander. It was almost immediately banned on charges of obscenity, and only survived due to Herrel's flight to Portsmouth, where he published the word in Kentish under his new appellation Tony Smith (presumably aided in the translation by his companion Hal Brennan). The original manuscript was eventually published in 1870, some years after his death.

excerpt in translation from 2023 diplomatic thriller Follow Suit (originally in Borlish as Pengovin Pardr "Losing at Bezique"), written by author Jan Bervisson (better known for host roles on several TV and radio shows).

181.2: alligar "relate"

alligar /Υ¨a.liΥàgar/ [Υ¨a.l…ΣΥàg…êΥê]
- associate, relate, link, connect, to note resemblances or relevance of one thing to other things;
- compare, set against, juxtapose, to examine the similarities and differences between;
- match, correlate, to make the parts of two things or collections correspond

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed from Latin allig≈ç "I bind to, tie, fetter". Thus, this word is a doublet of inherited alleyar "ally, collaborate, partner". Formerly used for physical ties and links (as related ligar "bind, bandage" still is), but now restricted to abstract usage.

181.3: harneisar "exploit"

harneisar /Υ¨har.niΥàsar/ [Υ¨h…êΥê.n…ΣΥàz…êΥê]
- equip, wield, take up, wear, to pick up and affix to one's grip or person somehow;
- exploit, use, make use of, utilise, to put to some employment to one's or another's advantage

Etymology: Middle Borlish verbal derivation from harneis "equipment, gear, tackle". This descends via Drengot French from Old Norrish hernest, a compound of herr "army" and nest "provisions". As such, the word was restricted to military contexts until the sixteenth century.

Nos harneisaum graivet e gant.
/n…îz Υ¨har.niΥàzom greΥàv…¦t e gant/
[n…îz Υ¨h…êΥê.n…ΣΥàzom g Ä…ΣΥàv…¦t e gant]
1p use-pst-1p greave and glove
We put shinpads and gloves on.

181.4: sgranfeyar "crunch"

sgranfeyar /Υ¨sgran.fiΥàjar/ [Υ¨zg Äa…±Υàfj…êΥê]
- crunch, crush, to put pressure on and thus cause to make a diffuse noise of breaking;
- grind, pulverise, to reduce to small pieces with noise and often violence;
- munch, chew, chomp, to eat noisily

Etymology: seventeenth century, derived from the root gran "grain, seed" with the somewhat-pejorative prefix s- < Latin ex- "out" and the factitive suffix -feyar < Latin -fic≈ç "I make". The word was first used to refer to eating a century after its first attestation.

J'er un pom sgranfeyant cos pegr.
/ £…¦r …Σn p…îm Υ¨sgran.fiΥàjant k…îz pijrΧ©/
[ ù…¦ΥêβÄΩ Ä…Σm p…îm Υ¨zg Ä…ë…±Υàfjant k…îz Υàp…Σ.j…ê]
1s=cop.ipf indf apple crunch-p.prs adv idle
I was idly munching on an apple.

##

181.5: Chronicles of Kilshaw

The Kilshaw writers' collective were a group of Merch authors who all lived in or around Kilshaw [Culcheth], a village in the northwestern part of Markland. The group was active through the middle decades of the twentieth century, and is remembered mainly for several long-running series of children's books. These, including the Joke Tawyer adventures, the Emma and Matt mysteries and the Samborn Sisters fancies, helped to redefine the landscape of children's publishing in Markland and beyond. The Kilshaw series have been translated into dozens of languages, and many have been adapted to formats such as sobot [radio] or phasm [television].

A brother and sister pair, Kenna and Holcroft Strong, began collaborating on stories in the early 1930s. They, along with author friends picked up in university and later from literary circles in Safford, would go on to form the Kilshaw collective.

Stories from Kilshaw made an industry of aping, parodying or homaging trends and fads in adult literature, telling tales in genres like masquira, social comedy or voidtale that happen to feature child protagonists. Originally played mostly for comic effect or as entertainment for parents, later works tended to take their premises more seriously and (especially in books aimed at older children) explored more realistic consequences of their events.

Kilshaw is one of the smaller settlements in the intercity borough of Makerfell [Makerfield], west of Safford [Salford]. Its variable pre-reform spellings (including Kilchath and Culshet) point to its etymology in British Celt terms cul "narrow" or cil "corner" and coed "woodland". This makes the placename a likely cognate of other toponyms like Colquiet in Devon and Culgod in Hasiny. The village has become a minor tourist destination for families or for the literary-minded, with a museum and regular fetes themed around Kilshaw characters (costumes, reenactments, etc).

##

182.1: History Quiz, 1900βÄ™50

excerpt in translation from 2021 book Excourse Puzzlers, published by the Royal Tommarth Castinghouse.

Ne cal polity Mendevan ern y preu boΟΑour e diacustour sobottal orΟΑenjaΟΑ d'an 1903?

182.2: broΟΑoquin "gibberish"

broΟΑoquin /Υ¨bro.ΟΑoΥàkw…Σn/ [Υ¨b Äo.ΟΑ äΥàkw…Σn]
- gibberish, gobbledegook, nonsense, babble, double Dutch, speech without meaning;
- bullshit, tosh, balderdash, poppycock, blatant falsehoods

Etymology: fourteenth century with various spellings, originally meaning "the language of non-Borlish speakers on Borland, especially of the northern Norrish speakers". Both Norrish and English languages had been spoken for several centuries on the north and west of the island, respectively. The word is presumed to be an adaptation of the Borland Norrish phrase broΟΑ oc vin "bread and wine" (the alternative broΟΑr oc ven "brother and friend" is considered less plausible).

Jo vay noc a l'ec broΟΑoquin scoutar.
/ £o ve n…îk a l…¦k Υ¨bro.ΟΑoΥàkw…Σn xuΥàtar/
[ ùo ve n…îg a l…¦g Υ¨b Äo.ΟΑ äΥàkw…Σn x äΥàt…êΥê]
1s go ng to df=p.px BS listen-inf
I don't have to listen to this bullshit.

182.3: pat fair "fumble"

pat fair /pat fer/ [pat feΥê]
- fumble, paw, to handle with clumsy hands;
- bungle, botch, mishandle, to deal with (a situation) poorly;

Etymology: idiomatic phrase attested from the fifteenth century, literally "to do paws" in reference to the unsuitability of many animal's paws for holding things. The noun pat "paw; (colloq.) hand, foot" descends from Vulgar Latin patta "paw", a borrowing from Frankish related to English "pad". In metaphorical use the phrase is seen from the seventeenth century.

L'agathin a pat fait sad ag tourf.
/Υ¨la.gaΥàΈΗ…Σn a pat fet sad …¦i turf/
[Υ¨la.g…êΥàΈΗ…Σn a pat fet sad …¦i t äΥêf]
df=minister have paw do-p.pst badly to.df riot
The minister mishandled the riot.

182.4: mensc "politeness"

mensc /m…¦nx/ [m…¦≈΄x]
- (obsolete) honour, dignity, the state of being worthy of esteem;
- (archaic) mercy, kindness, relenting from causing or forbearing to cause harm;
- (dated, poetic or jocular) politeness, etiquette, manners, courtesy, decorum, appropriate social behaviour

Usage note: nowadays principally in the idiom sy mensc eΟΑ y mansc tenir "have it both ways".

Etymology: from Old Borlish mensc "praiseworthiness, repute, fame". This was borrowed from Old Norrish mennska "humanity", a descendant of Classical German manniscas "human". The semantic shift via honourable actions to general good behaviour was complete by the seventeenth century. The modern phrase literally means "to keep one's manners and one's food", in reference to being a good host while not actually offering anything to eat. It is first attested in a book of proverbs by an anonymous author of the late eighteenth century.

OrruΟΑ i crey poΟΑir sy mensc eΟΑ y mansc tenir.
/…îΥàr…ΣΈΗ i kri poΥàΟΑ…Σr si m…¦nx …¦ΈΗ i manx teΥàn…Σr/
[ äΥà Ä…ΣβÄΩΟΑi k Äi p äΥàΟΑ…ΣΥê si m…¦≈΄x …¦βÄΩΟΑi ma≈΄x t…ΣΥàn…ΣΥê]
seem 3s believe can-inf 3s.gn manners and df food hold-inf
He seems to think he can have it both ways.

##

182.5: Writing in the 1880s

notes on several works of writing published during the 1880s.

Pasti Mezziterreni is an 1880 mealbook which compiles recipes from around the Middlesea [Mediterranean]. It was authored by Josforo Sant'Angelo (or at least on his behalf), a traveller from Tuscany who had already garnered significant renown due to his travels, including a near-fatal circumnavigation. The book doubles as an itinerary of a continuous sunward journey along the Middlesea coast from Tarifa in Andalus to αΚär l Joas in Morrack [Ksar es-Seghir, Morocco]. Dozens of translations were published over the subsequent years, including a bilingual KernishβÄ™Wessern [minority Welsh and English languages] version by Iscombe Editors in 1892.

Cludor ncar Vantel "A Postman in Wrexham" is an 1882 trevold parachthon trevold [SFF novel]. It chronicles the misadventures of the titular postman who becomes trapped experiencing the same week over and over. It is notable for being the origin of the Welsh phrase speid dolenu "time loop, lit. coiling timespan", which has been calqued into many other language (compare e.g. liesc bouclant in Borlish).

Dug y Gilfah Dow≈² "The Duke of Dark Corners" is an 1884 word of historical parachthony by Taguen ah Owain. Set in the twelfth century, she depicts a fictious tetrad of empires controlling Europe and the circum-Middlesea, ruled respectively from Damvath [Borland's capital], Florence, Tanger [Tangier] and Kiew [Kyiv].

L'Aragn "The Spider" is an 1887 play written by Borlish playwright Osctra Jeichey, which has had several touring productions and adaptations to film. The play is about a group of people manipulated by a genius mastermind whose plots eventually collapse in dramatic fashion. A notable version of the play is the 1930 New Provincial version La Rogna, a loose retelling which swapped the sex of many of the characters. The protagonist remained a woman, giving the film tovaric [gay] subtext rather unusual for New Provence at this time.

The first installments of Monstres per Mans "Man-Made Monsters" were published in 1888 by Carmel fi Domenc. This was a serial work of parachthon and fright fiction which would continue until 1903. The episodes appeared in the Santrafew [~Boston] periodical Arcans Exumaz "Revealed Mysteries", which Carmel fi Domenc also co-owned with her brother Jered.

Murro Mendeva "The Making of Mendeva" is a historical textbook which narrativises the early exploration and settlements by Vetomundine [Old World] groups in Mendeva [North America]. Written in 1888 by In mac Harlach, an Irish Welshman, it has been roundly criticised as an inaccurate and blinkered polemic against the concepts of alliance and integration with the original Mendevan peoples and nations. It reveals the influence of a resentful bloc in Britain which saw the rise of Hasiny as a threat.

183.2: teun "scant"

teun /taun/ [taun]
- scant, scanty, meagre, not in any great quantity;
- insufficient, paltry, not at all enough;
- rare, scarce, very uncommon;
- rarefied, thin, (of a gas) of low density

Etymology: via Old Borlish tiun, tieun "thin" from Latin tenuis "slight, weak". Thus, this word is one of the only pieces of evidence as to the development of post-stress /-w-/ (following a non-velar consonant) from Latin into Borlish. The semantic shift to senses relating to insufficiency is evidenced from the early Middle Borlish period.

L'oc aer es teun neΟΑan nos respireu.
/l…îk ar …¦z taun neΥàΟΑan n…îz Υ¨r…¦.spiΥàrau/
[l äΥàg…êΥêβÄΩ Ä…Σz taun n…ΣΥàΟΑan n…îz Υ¨ Ä…¦.sp…ΣΥà Äau]
df=s.px air be scant lest 1p breath-sbj.1p
This air's too thin for both of us to breathe.

183.3: gaviscenΟß "festivities"

gaviscenΟß /Υ¨ga.viΥàx…¦nts/ [Υ¨ga.v…ΣΥàΟß…¦nts]
- festivities, celebration, gala, party, a social gathering for entertainment and fun;
- revelry, merrymaking, mafficking, frolic, the act of having fun at such an event

Etymology: sixteenth century, a noun formation from the verb gavir "rejoice, have fun". This descends from Latin gaude≈ç "I make merry". Originally the word often referred specifically to the celebration of religious holidays or feast days.

Vos vouΟΑ uncos special accommettr por y gaviscenΟß ou?

183.4: nis tenir "be at fault"

nis tenir /n…Σz teΥàn…Σr/ [n…Σz t…ΣΥàn…ΣΥê]
- be responsible, take responsibility, to be the ultimate cause of something;
- be at fault, be liable, take the blame, to have something bad attributed to one;
- more generally, to be the origin or driving force behind something

Etymology: idiom from the seventeenth century, originally seen in legal contexts, literally "to hold effort". The noun nis "effort, vigour; force; obligation; attention" comes from Old Boral nis, ne»ùs, ni»ùs et al. "effort, vigour", which descends uncomplicatedly from Latin nΡΪxus, nΡΪsus "strain, forward momentum". This is the participle of deponent verb nΡΪtor "I lean on, advance, strive, contend".

183.5: Defining the Dime

excerpt from the work Pa's Hyd Raf? "How Long is a Piece of String?", written in 2026 as a series of freely available posts on the mesh by ≈¹ew ab Ennis, which delves into the history of the development of systems of weights and measures.

βÄΠoriginally defined the "second" simply as the eight-and-sixty-four hundredth part of the mean solar day, but by the end of the nineteenth century it had been observed that this period would vary by as much as a few hundred scant (10β¹Μβ¹Η). In search of a stabler value, the Secular Second Undertaking set several astronomical groups worldwide to record the precise length of the tropical year from the summer solstice of 1900 until that of 1901, and defined the "(secular) second" to be the 3155,6926th part of this timespan.

While time has always had a single fundamental base in the dime measure system, other quantities are less simple. The dime principle that all quantities should be related by factors of ten did not prevent a proliferation of standards. Length has four basic units in common usage: a league is a hundred chains, a chain a hundred feet, a foot ten inches. Units of area and volume are defined by squares and cubes of the base lengths. For example, the farthing (formerly defined as one quarter of an acre) is equal to a square chain.

In the first decades of the twentieth century, the foot was still officially defined as the length of a particular rod of iron-baxon [nickel] alloy kept in Tammombay [Mumbai]. In 1946 the foot was redefined in terms of the second, so that the normal speed ≈™ [lightspeed] would be precisely 10,0000,0000 feet per second. This locked the precision of the foot to that of the second. Subsequent refinements have primarily come in the form of particular sources of light, whose spans [wavelengths] or equivalently cadences [frequencies] can be pinpointed extremely accurately.

The basic units of heft [mass] in the dime measuresβÄîthe scruple, ounce, tabre and tonβÄîwere originally defined in terms of the density of water. To wit, the ounce was set as the mass of a cube of distilled water of side length one (dime) inch; this was later refined to specify that the water should be at its threshold point [triple point]. In practice, due to the difficulty of operationalising the water-based definition to more than a hundred-scant precision, by the 1920s the tabre (100 ounces) was fixed to a particular joracker [platinum] alloy puck weighing a tabre (one hundred ounces)βÄΠ

===

Real-life comparisons:
1 dime second ~= 1 second - 0.84 nanoseconds

1 dime foot ~= 29.98 centimetres, 0.9836 feet

1 farthing ~= 0.08988 hectares, 0.2221 acres

1 dime ounce ~= 26.94 grams, 0.9502 ounces

1 tabre ~= 2.694 kilograms, 5.939 pounds

184.1: North America in the 19th Century

notes on British Mendeva [a region of NA which formed much fo the northern edge of the Gulf of Mexico].

Mendeva Brittanesc sovra Brittan cas pouvlanΟß e laboriostur durant l'eΟß annaΟΑ final ny siecr novoΟß.
British Mendeva overtook Britain in population and industry in the latter years of the nineteenth century.

Nentamen, jusc hour entarn Brittan poΟΑe un eΟΑegl de vry cultural vardir.
Nonetheless, for some time Britain retained a position of cultural cachet.

Soumis es paravant ig l'oc dau impet all'adopΟßon hastous ne Mendeva Brittanesc dy moraltaΟΑ dy RenovellaΟßon Famigler (ne comparaΟßon contr Nou ProvenΟß e Mascic magn mejoncherrem, com bustr) nell'oc epoc.
It has been suggested this contributed to British Mendeva's rapid adoption of the ethics of the Household Renovation (as compared to much more ambivalent New Provence and Mashick, for instance) during this time.

Impar nell'eΟß siecr anΟßan, ny siecr novoΟß sceye tramigraΟßon remarcabr dy select entr Mendeva Brittanesc e Brittan, tan com mariΟΑaΟß entrig magn monfeyaΟΑ.
Unlike earlier centuries, the nineteenth century saw significant transmigration of the Ο©lites between British Mendeva and Britain, as well as much more intermarriage.

Cos parΟßal se poΟΑ l'oc ascreir a nuvr alcot sta un select autochthon sceint ovest dell'Atlantic, ma majortaΟΑer l'oc se mantien for deut a vascel raudessem e donc aΟΑendr magn abrejaΟΑ.
In part this can be attributed to the newness of an autochthonous Ο©lite west of the Atlantic, but mostly this is simply due to faster ships and greatly-reduced travel times.

184.2: veΟΑrdon "prize"

veΟΑrdon /Υ¨v…¦ΟΑ.rΧ©Υàd…în/ [Υ¨v…¦.ΟΑ…êΥàd…în]
- prize, award, honour, a reward striven for in a competition or which may be won by chance;
- trophy, cup, an object (often a statuette or vessel) commemorating one's victory in a competition;

Etymology: the descendant of Old Borlish veΟΑerdon "payment, reward", which comes from synonymous widerd≈çnum in Medieval Latin. This is a borrowing from Germanic (cf. Old English wiΟΨerlΡ™an "recompense", literally "counter-reward"), which the second part calqued as Latin d≈çnum "gift". Compare the Old French cognate guerredon "payment; spoils, booty, loot", whose first element was also adjusted to guerre "war". Conflation with unrelated Borlish veΟΑr < Latin vitrum "glass" may have influenced the traditional concept of a trophy as made of glass or crystal.

I tenoy sy veΟΑrdon n'oc ort e soureyeu.
/i teΥàn…îj si Υ¨v…¦ΟΑ.rΧ©Υàd…în n…îk …îrt e Υ¨su.riΥàjau/
[i t…ΣΥàn…îj si Υ¨v…¦.ΟΑ…êΥàd…în n äΥàg…îΥêt e Υ¨su. Ä…ΣΥà ùau]

She held up her trophy and grinned.

184.3: adolescenΟß "bildungsroman"

adolescenΟß /Υ¨a.do.leΥàx…¦nts/ [Υ¨a.d ä.l…ΣΥàΟß…¦ns]
- adolescence, teenagerhood, the period of life between childhood and adulthood;
- coming of age, maturation, the process or event of becoming an adult;
- sweet sixteen, quinceaΟ±era, bat/bar mitzvah (etc.), any of several customary ceremonies performed in various cultures to mark a person coming of age;
- bildungsroman, coming-of-age story, a genre of tales about the growth (in several senses) of characters from children to adults

Etymology: Middle Borlish, a borrowing from Scholastic Latin adolΡ™scentia "maturation, growing up", nominal derivative of Classical verb adolΡ™sc≈ç "I grow up, become an adult". In the meaning of a type of tradition, the word is a clipping of former phrase rit d'adolescenΟß "coming-of-age rite". The term was first used for the literary genre in the early 20C to describe the works of London author Dorotha Quistack.

Cay un adolescenΟß a them d'alumn ne Tecamau.
/ke …Σn Υ¨a.do.leΥàx…¦nts a ΈΗ…¦m daΥàl…ΣmnΧ© ne Υ¨te.kaΥàmo/
[ke …Σn Υ¨a.d ä.l…ΣΥàx…¦ns a ΈΗ…¦m d…êΥàl…Σ.m…ên ne Υ¨te.k…êΥàmo]
fall indf teens at topic of=schoolkid in Tecamaw
It's a coming-of-age tale about Tecamaw schoolkids.

184.4: dom "dome"

dom /d…îm/ [d…îm]
- dome, cupola, the architectural feature of a hollow upper half-sphere on the roof of a building;
- (mathematics) hemisphere, the surface of half a sphere;
- pate, crown, the top of the head (especially when hairless)

Etymology: originally in the first sense, from Middle Borlish dom "dome; stately building, edifice" (now obsolete in the latter senses). This comes from the conflation of Italian duomo "cathedral", from Latin domus ecclΡ™siaeβÄ΄ "meeting-house, church", with French dosme "dome", which comes via Late Latin d≈çma "roof" from Greek Έ¥αΩΕΈΦαΨΑ "house, housetop". The merger, which had likely already occurred in Middle French before the term entered Borlish, was aided by the prominence of domes on Italian cathedrals.

Com s'augrim y stent a starn d'un dom lonc sy ray?
/k…îm soΥàgr…Σm i st…¦nt a starn d'…Σn d…îm l…înk si re/
[k…îm s äΥàg Ä…Σm i st…¦nt a st…êΥên d…Σn d…îm l…î≈΄k si  Äe]
how rf=calculate df area at outside of=df dome along 3s.gn radius 
What is a hemisphere's surface area in terms of its radius?

##

184.5: A Worldwide Web

various features of the Boralverse beginning with W.

War without Walls is a textbook of military history by Ajamarn [~Rajasthani] scholar Indvar Vijarrania, written in the early twentieth century during his tenure at the University of UdjΟ©n [Ujjain]. He chronicles the development of military strategy in the wake of the advent of cannon and other explosives, and in particular how these technologies obsoleted the formerly dominant strategy of defence via walled fortifications.

Wessern (Merch /Υàw…¦.s…ên/, Wessern Wesseron /w…ôsΥàs…¦…Ψ…ôn/, Welsh Sasneg /s…êΥàzn…¦g/, Borlish Wessaxon /Υ¨v…¦.sakΥàs…în/) is the language of the English minority living in Britain [~Wales, etc], primarily in eastern Devon and the downstream Severn. It is the third-most spoken English language on Albion [Great Britain], after Merch and Kentish, although it also has significant populations of speakers abroad in British Mendeva [NA] and elsewhere. Wessern features geminate consonants and the simplification of coda clusters (compare Merch dark /daΥêk/ to Wessern darick /Υàda.…Ψ…ôk/ "dark"), as well as fricative voicing between vowels and at the start of words.

Winchester Scholastic Primers are a publishing company which have operated in Kent since the early twentieth century. They have produced works principally of academic or educational interest, with several works used regularly in schools. Books from this publishing house include:
- The Alphabetical Albion (1920), by a consortium including Lucas Osborne
- A Comprehensive CyclopΟΠdia of Pre-Fracture Europe (1937), written by Martin and Elisabeth Forman and illustrated by Goffrey Jordan
- In Sickness and in Health: Medicine in pre-Arnoutszen Europe (2nd ed. 1961), by Andrew Knowles with foreword by Dr Laura Norris

Wolfston the Younger (fl. ca. 930 N) was the last English king of Northumber before the rump state was folded into the Dane territory ruled from Yorwick [York]. Northumber had controlled lands as far south as Yorwick until the ninth century, when that city became the first major settlement in England conquered during the Dane Supremacy. According to the Carlile chronicle, Wolfston's short reign ended ignominiously and without issue at the fortifications in Edinbrough, when those too were captured by Dane forces.

185.1: The Zenda Manuscript (cont.)

continued translation from the beginning of [real-life] book The Henchmen of Zenda by KJ Charles, a 2018 pastiche of the 1894 romp The Prisoner of Zenda.

Jo dign Jasper Detchard, e lonc y narraΟΑ de Rassendyll jo so mort.
My name is Jasper Detchard, and according to RassendyllβÄôs narrative I am dead.

L'oc derra vos orruΟΑar sy tranqment mesurar, daΟΑ ig jo no so l'ec parol dictant all'oregl d'un benoyour a fler de caul des dereΟΑr dy sougl.
This should give you some idea of his accuracy, since I do not dictate these words to some cabbage-scented medium from beyond the veil.

No scey alcun dout ig jo sera mort prell'hour yon alcun hom lein l'ec fouglβÄî
There is no doubt that I will be dead by the time anyone reads these pagesβÄî

βÄîveram, sontic cos absoluΟΑ es ig l'oc sey cossy, dec my tesmogn vel y gehen ou d'un condamnment magn stendiscent ben d'un cug magn abrejaΟΑ me sbolognar, e par surcresc se poΟΑ y doum d'un reyaum desvey deut a my parol.
βÄîin fact, it is absolutely necessary that should be the case, as my words would earn me the inconvenience of a very long prison sentence or a very short drop, and moreover the fate of a kingdom could be swayed by my words.

Ma jo remain, cant jo scau, veunt, vige, e vexaΟΑ.
But I am, as I write, alive, well, and irritated.

185.2: pestriΟßon "formation"

pestriΟßon /Υ¨p…¦.striΥàts…în/ [Υ¨p…¦.st Ä…ΣΥàts…în]
- formation, development, emergence, creation, genesis, the process during which something comes into being;
- assembly, arrangement, shape, form, configuration, structure, layout, the manner in which something is physically composed in space

Etymology: early Modern Borlish, a noun derivation from verb pestrir "knead; mould, form, shape". This is a direct descendant from Vulgar Latin pistri≈ç "I knead", from Classical Latin pistor "baker, miller". Initially referring to the act of kneading dough or moulding clay, the word took on more general senses almost at once.

Jo no's deja satisfait dy pestriΟßon nell'exedr.
/ £o n…îz deΥà £a Υ¨sa.t…ΣΥàsfet di Υ¨p…¦.striΥàts…în n…¦.l…¦Υàks…¦d.rΧ©/
[ ùo n…îz d…ΣΥà ùa Υ¨sa.t…ΣΥàsfet di Υ¨p…¦.st Ä…ΣΥàts…în n…¦.l…ΣΥàks…¦.d…ê]
1s ng=be yet content of-df form in-df=vestibule
I'm not yet happy with the anteroom's layout.

185.3: a cascun "respectively"

a cascun /a kaΥàx…Σn/ [a k…êΥàΟß…Σn]
- each, apiece, (e.g. of a price) for an individual item from a set;
- per head, per capita, (of a statistic) divided by the population of the region in question;
- respectively, corresponding in the order given when comparing two lists of things;
- pointwise, (in mathematics) occuring or true independently for each point in a set

Etymology: literally "to each one", in idiomatic use attested from the twelfth century. The pronoun cascun "each one" descends directly from Vulgar Latin casc≈Ϊnum "each one", a conflation of Classical quisque ≈Ϊnum and Late Latin cata ≈Ϊnum "each one". The phrase's use extended beyond financial and census-taking contexts through the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries.

Se poΟΑe pom accatar teint ogtoΟß solt a cascun.
/se poΥàΟΑe p…îm Υ¨a.kaΥàtar tint …îjΥàt…îts s…îlt a kaΥàx…Σn/
[se p äΥàΟΑe p…îm Υ¨a.k…êΥàt…êΥê tint  äjΥàt…îts s…îlt a k…êΥàΟß…Σn]
rfl can-ipf apple buy-inf amount-p.prs 18 penny at each 
You could buy apples for eighteen pence each.

185.4: bastegl "bulwark"

bastegl /baΥàst…Σjl/ [baΥàst…Σjl]
- fortress, fortification, a strongly built and defended military structure or enclosure used in warfare;
- bastion, stronghold, a secure position or place of power and influence;
- bulwark, defence, something that serves as a shield or protection against danger or attack;
- safeguard, protection, precaution, any means of preventing harm or ensuring safety

Etymology: a borrowing from Middle French bastille "fortress", itself from early Doccian [Occitan] bastida "fortress, edifice" adapted to the -ille suffix < Latin ΡΪle "enclosure". This comes from Late Latin basti≈ç "I build; I sew", a borrowing from Frankish. In metaphorical usage the word is in evidence from the eighteenth century.

L'oc alleyanΟß intermandra bastegl contr y Pologn.
/l…îk Υ¨a.liΥàjants …ΣnΥ¨t…¦r.manΥàdra baΥàst…Σjl Υàk…în.trΧ© i poΥàl…îjn/
[l äΥ¨galΥàjans …ΣnΥ¨ΈΗ…¦Υê.m…ênΥàd Äa b…êΥàst…Σjl Υàk…în.t…êβÄΩ Äi p äΥàl…îjn]
df=s.px alliance comprise-ft fortress against df Poland
This alliance will be a bulwark against Poland.

##

185.5: England

notes on the geographical region of England.

England (Merch /Υà…¦n.gl…ônd/, Norman Engleterre /Υà…¦ΧÉng.t…¦ Ä/, Welsh ≈¹oegr /Υà…§oΥê.g…ô/, Borlish Larry /laΥà Äi/) is the name of a loosely demarcated region of Albion [Great Britain]. In the modern day England may be defined politically to be the territory of Northumber, Markland, London and (Albic) Kent; it may also be defined linguistically to be the part of Albion inhabited by communities of speakers of English languages. The latter definition is more expansive, due to the many Wessern speakers in eastern Devon and Merch speakers in eastern Wales.

The majority of languages not listed above refer to this region by some borrowing or adaptation of Medieval Latin Angl≈çnia "England". This was formed by analogy to e.g. Sax≈çnia "Saxony" from the root AnglΡΪ "Angles", one of the German peoples who populated the region in the sub-Roman and early medieval period. The exceptional Welsh and Borlish toponyms are presumably cognate (see Old Borlish variant form La»ùr "Englishman"), reflecting a hypothesised Classical Kelt word laecor "warriors".

There are (according to mainstream analysis) four English languages spoken on Albion; in order of number of speakers these are Northumber, Wessern, Kentish and Merch. The polity of Northumber extends from the Humber to Edinbrough along the eastern side of Albion, but its local tongue, which has more Norrish influence than other Englishes, competes for use there with the more prestigious Merch. Wessern is predominantly spoken in Britain [Wales, Devon, etc], where it enjoys co-official status in some tallaths [~provinces] but a decidedly subordinate role. Wessern features geminate consonants and the simplification of coda clusters (compare Merch dark /daΥêk/ to Wessern darick /Υàda.…Ψ…ôk/ "dark"), as well as fricative voicing between vowels and at the start of words.

Kentish is one of the two languages of Kent along with Norman. Kent is a polity divided by the Kentish Channel [English Channel], both geographically and linguistically. Nonetheless, Kentish has had much more French influence over the past millennium than other Englishes, which continues to this day. For example, Kentish is the only English language within the so-called "roral ambit", having a uvular rhotic.

Merch is by some way the most-spoken English language, official both in Markland and London. The London standard of the language, while fully mutually intelligible with the Markland form (to a much greater degree than between different English languages), has several differences, notably in spellingβÄîLondon did not particpate in the 1870 reforms which somewhat rationalised Merch orthography.

##

186.1: Global Workshop

notes in translation on the subject of the Global Workshop period.

L'epoc dell'Atelier, ben l'Atelier Mondial, interman y dezein entrpos dy siecr novoΟß.
The Workshop period, or the Global Workshop, refers to the middle decades of the nineteenth century.

L'oc se descrey par y spanjon e defasment veloΟß de laboriostur atras dy mont.
It is characterised by the rapid expansion and development of industry across the world.

ScaunΟß historic normal situn l'epoc dell'Atelier entr y PaΟß Lonc eΟΑ y Bel Joy.
Usual historiography places the Workshop period between the Long Peace and the Good Game.

Se poΟΑ y rebondisc dell'epoc dell'Atelier veir par nuton cas art, con un locomoΟßon deur trevogt e vars (ny preu paum) depegnt mesmeristic dy ranc trevaglour ne laboriostur e (contrig) postulaΟßon mallovicher sur fanglanΟß cas claΟΑ alcant aposc.
The impact of the Workshop period can be seen in artistic trends, with the shift away from voidtales and towards (on the one hand) realistic portrayals of the industrial working class and (on the other) optimistic speculation on future material advancements.

Y colluy VS (Vindrons Sairzaus), oc stant y forgour dy preu vin aphlox mout venΟßaΟΑ, vole lor nell'oc epoc sell'orisont spandr vars autr kynous moliner por sforΟß lort rigir e por hermeticar, e cossy un eΟΑegl magn profitant gajannar.
The guild VS (Vindrons Sairzaus), which manufactured the first widely-sold fizzing wine, would in this period expand its scope to other high-pressure and sealing components in manufacture, securing a very profitable niche.

186.2: excellent "conspicuous"

excellent /Υ¨…¦k.ts…¦Υàl…¦nt/ [Υ¨…¦k.s…ΣΥàl…¦nt]
- preeminent, superior, leading, distinguished, held in particularly high regard;
- noteworthy, famous, renowned, widely known or celebrated for something or somebody's qualities or deeds;
- noticeable, conspicuous, standing out, blatant, remarkable or evident to the senses or the mind

Etymology: Middle Borlish borrowing from Latin excellΡ™ns "surpassing, eminent", the present participle of verb excell≈ç "I rise, project, excel". The extension of the word both to general noteworthiness and to obviousness had occurred by the eighteenth century.

I porta un min de derijon excellent.

She had a look of undisguised scorn.

186.3: progentar "beget"

progentar /Υ¨pro. £…¦nΥàtar/ [Υ¨p Äo. ù…ΣnΥàt…êΥê]
- (archaic) beget, sire, father, mother, to be the parent of;
- originate, give rise to, create, invent, devise, to cause to come into existence;
- inspire, provoke, engender, to cause to occur or to be felt;

Etymology: the verb is an early modern backformation of older progentour "creator", a borrowing from Latin pr≈çgenitor "ancestor". This is an agent noun derivation from verb pr≈çgign≈ç "I beget, produce". In metaphorical use for causing actions or feelings the word is seen from the early nineteenth century.

Hasiny progentau magn orΟΑenjanΟß sobottal.
/Υ¨ha.ziΥàni Υ¨pro. £…¦nΥàto m…¦jn Υ¨…îr.ΟΑ…¦nΥà £ants Υ¨so.b…îΥàtal/
[Υ¨ha.z…ΣΥàni Υ¨p Äo. ù…ΣnΥàto m…¦jn Υ¨…îΥê.ΟΑ…Σ…≤Υà ùans Υ¨so.b äΥàtal]
Hasiny beget-pst very invention radio-adj
Hasiny originated many radio innovations.

186.4: y sona hurstar "take one's place"

y sona hurstar /i soΥàna h…ΣrΥàstar/ [i s äΥàna h…ΣΥêΥàst…êΥê]
- take one's place, make it, to attain a position that one is supposed or destined to reach;
- step up, rise to the occasion, to assume some responsibility;
- come into one's own, flourish, thrive, to begin to do well in a newly cquired role

Etymology: idiom from the nineteenth century, literally "to equip the role". The noun sona "role" derives from thespian slang in the early modern period, a clipping of Latin pers≈çna "mask; character, part, role". The verb descends from Old Borlish hurstar "decorate, adorn", a borrowing from synonymous hyrstan in Old English (compare Merch hirstend "superfluous").

Y joun a y sona fort hurstaΟΑ cos nuverrem.
/i  £un a i soΥàna f…îrt h…ΣrΥàstaΈΗ k…îz niΥàv…¦.r…¦m/
[i  ùun a i s äΥàna f…îΥêt h…ΣΥêΥàstaΈΗ k…îz n…ΣΥàv…¦. Ä…êm]
df young have df role well equip-p.pst adv fresh-cmp
The kid's really come into her own lately.

186.5: False Friends (cont. 2)

some more completely arbitrary dictionary entries from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (fifth edition), a reference work published in 1961 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

Awry [/Υà…ôu.…Ψi/ "Hungary"] n.pr. a polity of Europe inhabited principally by the Majoric people, located east of Crain and Crovatia and west of Halich and Vallack. 10C Old English, allied with contemporary French Avrie, Saxon Aweri, Bavarn Awaren < Medieval Latin Avaria "the Avar Empire". This early medieval empire was erroneously conflated in Latin sources with (or perhaps merely repurposed as a geographical term for) the subsequent Majoric principality after their conquest of the region in the ninth century.

boss [/b…îs/ "gun"] n. a handheld weapon which fires a bullet using some explosive substance. 16C < Willem Dutch bΟ≥sz, armbΟ≥sz "boss" < early Dutch armbaust "crossbow" < Old French arbaleste < later Latin arcuballista "crossbow".

guy-coney [/Υàg…ôiΥ¨k ä.ni/ "guinea pig"] n. (also guy) a small, tail-less glire of Cappatia [South America] raised for meat or as a hearth animal. 17C, a borrowing in sense from Vascon conill guy (the second element altered in pronunciation to match the name) < 16C Morrove Arabic Ό²ΌÜΌäΊ© Ό²ΌèΌàΌêΌä βÄΔ (qnia qui) < Run'allo [Quechua] qowi "(female) guy-coney". For the coney element: 12C early Merch < reformed singular of Norman plural conis "coneys" of conil < Latin cunΡΪculus "coney; burrow, warren; mine".

sicker [/Υàs…Σ.k…ô/ "certain"] adj. free of any doubt or possibility of being incorrect; confident of one's correctness. Direct descendant of Old English sicor "safe, certain" < western Classical German sicur < Latin sΡ™c≈Ϊrus "unconcerned; fearless; safe".

##

187.1: Stopping Scurvy

notes in translation on efforts to prevent scurvy.

Jusc y siecr cinqueΟß nuverrem e seΟß primal, cosogr y veyaΟß geoscopic magn stendiscendessem ne souc dell'Abanc Novomondin d'an 1471, gimmolt por scurvog coΟ·r denoirn quichessem.
By the late fifteenth and early sixteenth centuries, with the much longer geoscopic voyages following the Novomundine Landfall of 1471, efforts to prevent scurvy became more active.

Com bustr, Rey Ambrosc III a Vascony ("l'Ascenent") corporalleyau y Decret Sandrin d'an 1485 yon ag vascel voloisn stovir fruit issugt e scarob bacagettar.
For example, King Ambrose III of Vascony (the "Ascendant") instituted the Sandrine Design in 1485 by which ships would be required to stock dried fruits and syrups.

Nentamen, tal teisment n'aurn rebondisc durabr.
However, such schemes did not have lasting effects.

Cos parΟßal l'oc se deye a careΟß ma magn moulosessem, ag defait de comprenjon cas y method comoscon fruit poΟΑen scurvog coΟ·r.
This was partly due to expense but much more significantly, due to the lack of understanding of the mechanism by which fruit prevented scurvy.

Cossy, fruit stan conservaΟΑ par mejan lorry matier sandrinal eliminant (ig inclus, par lou boglir ben par tas ne cuivr benoΟßar) e moΟΑnaut nanciscen scurvog toΟΑ cas, oc colluy menant vars y sdroyaΟßon de fruit lingr total.
Thus, fruit would be preserved in ways which removed their sandrine content (for example, boiling or use of copper vessels) and sailors would suffer scurvy anyway, leading guilds to abandon stocking fruits altogether.

187.2: feurΟΑon "decare"

feurΟΑon /faurΥàΟΑ…în/ [Υ¨fa.w…êΥàΟΑ…în]
- rood, vergΟ©e, a traditional unit of land area equal to one quarter of an acre;
- decare, a modern metric unit of land area defined as ten thousand square feet

Etymology: originally in the first sense, part of the traditional system of area measures used on Borland along with the acr "acre" (also called the jur in the east). The word reflects Old Borlish feorΟΑon, forΟΑin "rood", from Old English fΟ©orΟΑing or Old Norrish [Norrish] fiΟ≥rΟΑungr "quarter, fourth part". The word was redefined in the nineteenth century with the adoption of dime [metric] units, being the numerically closest measure available (compare the Turk Ί·ΌàΌÜΌàΌÖ βÄΔ dΟΕnΟΦm or the Nivoner [Japanese] ψ¹üψ²™ψ¹Ε βÄΔ tambu, units which were similarly adjusted to this new dime measure).

Y majon terraun set nell'oc feurΟΑon.
/i maΥà £…în t…¦Υàron s…¦t n…¦Υàl…îk faurΥàΟΑ…în/
[i m…êΥà ù…în t…ΣΥà Äon s…¦t n…¦.l äk Υ¨fa.w…êΥàΟΑ…în]
df house amount-fut-3p 7 in-df=s.px rood
There'll be seven houses in this decare.

187.3: yauvnar "pat"

yauvnar /jovΥànar/ [ ù ävΥàn…êΥê/
- pat, to gently tap the flat of one's hand against;
- tamp down, flatten, to hit or push against from above to make smooth or flat;
- unruffle, smooth out, to remove the bumpiness from by e.g. stretching out;

Etymology: originally in the latter senses, descendant of Old Borlish iawnar "to smooth, even, flatten". This is a borrowing from Old Norrish [Norrish] jafna "to make even", a verbal derivative of jafn "even; equal, same". Specialisation to the act of tapping the flat of one's hand against something is seen from the fifteenth century.

I s'yauvnau y cavel sborreyaΟΑ cos van.
/i sjovΥàno i kaΥàv…¦l Υ¨sb…î.riΥàjaΈΗ k…îz van/
[i sj ävΥàno i k…êΥàv…¦l Υ¨zb…î. Ä…ΣΥà ùaΈΗ k…îz van]
3s rf=pat-pst df hair unkempt adv vain
He failed to pat down his messy hair.

187.4: skillet "chip"

skillet /sk…ΣΥàl…¦t/ [sk…ΣΥàl…¦t]
- chip, fragment, shard, piece, a small part broken off a larger whole;
- splinter, sliver, a long and sharp fragment of a material such as wood;
- chip, fry, a fried strip of potato or similar food

Etymology: early modern Borlish, apparently from Kernish askleut or Devoner skylot "chips, shavings, splinters". These both descend from Classical British asclod "splinter", a borrowing from Late Latin ascla which itself is a variant of Classical assula "splinter". The word was first applied to foods in the seventeenth century and had specialised to starches like potatoes by the nineteenth.

Y pescot e skillet n'AusΟßagm son ragostant.
/i peΥàx…ît e sk…ΣΥàl…¦t noΥàs…¦jm s…în Υ¨ra.g…îΥàstant/
[i p…ΣΥàx…ît e sk…ΣΥàl…¦t n äΥàs…¦jm s…în Υ¨ Äa.g äΥàstant]
df fish and chip in=AusΟßagm be.3p tasty
The fish and chips in AusΟßagm is delicious.

##

187.5: The Void

notes on three things which are somehow related to outer space.

The Superdraconic Apocalypse (Borlish Apocalyps Surdraconic /aΥ¨po.kaΥàl…Σps s…ΣrΥ¨dra.koΥàn…Σk/) refers to the fall of a cisjovial sax [asteroid from the asteroid belt] approximately sixty-six hundred myriad years ago, probably during the northern spring. It was followed by the eradication of roughly three-quarters of all animal species, including all dragons [dinosaurs] bar the ancestors of modern birds. The name of this event, coined in the nineteenth century as a hypothetical (several decades before evidence of the impact was actually discovered) to explain the extinction in the fossil record, refers to its locationβÄîtemporally at the end of, and therefore geologically on top of, the Draconic Age (2.5βÄ™0.66 great myriad years ago).

The waveform display [Fourier transform] (Borlish despley ondatim /d…¦Υàspli Υ¨…în.daΥàt…Σm/) is the presentation of a mathematical function as the sum of vatre and arrhumb [sine and cosine] functions. This technique was developed in the eighteenth century as a way to help compute celestial orbits, in part by Barcelon mathematician Raymond al Mustafa.

The Auroric Anomaly of 1859 (Borlish Anomaly Auroric /aΥ¨no.maΥàli Υ¨o.roΥàr…Σk/) was a collection of phenomena associated with an intense sunstorm, including auroras andβÄîmost famouslyβÄîmalfunctioning barrick steeplepost [electric telegraph] equipment. Various operators reported sparks, fires, and even being able to sporadically use wires to transmit when they weren't powered. The first tachygraph [typewriter] capable of sending messages on the steeplemesh was tested by Tomas Vilmanas on Michael's Day, less than a month after the Anomaly. However, despite later dramatisation, there is little evidence that the two events are connected.

##

188.1: Millstone War

notes in translation on the subject of the Millstone War (1897βÄ™1908).

MalgraΟΑ noc tout entr y var envoluΟΑ la afferjarausn l'oncein d'annaΟΑ enter, ΟΑe son tout descreut par un inereΟΑm fondaminal: manouvr costojal ayen machinaΟßon gazovar survaluΟΑ, e donc tarmn de front tras y mont quaglaurn ne bastegl a fos ceu avanΟßabr.
Though not all of the wars involved spanned the entire eleven years, they are all characterised by a fundamental asymmetry: defensive tactics had outstripped offensive technology, and so front lines worldwide ground to barely-moving trench fortifications.

Par y Var a CouΟΑ se munc y fin dell'epoc Bel-Joy; ne sy faughanΟß sceye un rallignment abrangent dy doujug alleyancer paravant.
The Millstone War marks the end of the Good Game period; its aftermath saw a comprehensive realignment of the previous system of alliances.

Nell'eΟß annaΟΑ final dy Var a CouΟΑ sceye y preu benoΟß militer de nau aeral, tan com bombaΟßon des aer de citaΟΑ ig inclus Marsegl ne ProvenΟß.
The last years of the Millstone War saw the first military use of aircraft, as well as air-bombing of cities including Marsella in Provence.

Zizania politic montaurn cos mondial nell'ec dezein ne souc dy Var a CouΟΑ, tandic l'insuffignΟß dell'apprestanΟß posc y var denoirn evident.
Political tensions rose globally in the decades after the Millstone War, as the inadequacy of the post-war settlements became apparent.

188.2: bechettar "dig in"

bechettar /Υ¨be.k…¦Υàtar/ [Υ¨be.k…ΣΥàt…êΥê]
- dig in, chow down, tuck in, to begin eating;
- munch, nosh, to eat vigorously or noisily;
- get stuck in, engage, to become enthusiastically involved in an activity

Etymology: nineteenth century verbal derivative of the noun bec "beak, snout, muzzle; (colloquial, of people) mouth". This descends directly from Latin beccus "beak, bill", a borrowing from Gaulish. In metaphorical usage the word is seen from the early twentieth century.

Nos bechettaum jug posc l'elocquy.
/n…îz Υ¨be.k…¦Υàtom  £aj p…îx Υ¨le.l…îΥàkwaj/

##

188.3: mormonnar "mumble"

mormonnar /Υ¨m…îr.m…îΥànar/ [Υ¨m…îΥê.m äΥàn…êΥê]
- mumble, mutter, murmur, to say quietly and indistinctly;
- burble, babble, gurgle, to make the speech-like sounds of an infant;
- glug, flow, trickle, bubble, to make the sound of noisily flowing water

Etymology: late Middle Borlish, an onomatopoetic formation (along with contemporaneous mormonment "(a) mumble") allied with Latin murmur≈ç "I murmur" and Parisian marmotter "to mutter". The word had extended to similar non-speech sounds by the end of the eighteenth century.

Y Οßarf bondisceu tras un reu mormonnant.
/i tsarf Υ¨b…în.diΥàxau traz …Σn rau Υ¨m…îr.m…îΥànant/
[i ts…êΥêf Υ¨b…în.d…ΣΥàxau t Äaz …Σn  Äau Υ¨m…îΥê.m äΥànant]
df hart bound-pst across indf stream mumble-p.prs
The stag leapt over a babbling brook.

188.4: sborreyar "dishevel"

sborreyar /Υ¨sb…î.riΥàjar/ [Υ¨zb…î. Ä…ΣΥà ù…êΥê]
- dishevel, tousle, ruffle, to render (hair, clothes, etc.) untidy or disordered;
- disarray, disorganise, to throw into chaos more generally;
- tangle, entangle, knot, snarl, mat, to mix up (e.g. strands) so that they cannot be easily separated (also metaphorically);
- shuffle, to change the arrangement of (in the manner of cards or other game pieces)

Etymology: early modern Borlish, borrowed from northern dialect. The word is a verbal derivative of rural term borr "tuft, burr, clump", which descends from Latin burra "ragged garment, coarse fragment". The prefix s- < Latin ex- is somewhat pejorative; the verbal ending comes from -idi≈ç in Vulgar Latin.

Stou a casc lusour y pachet fort sborreyar.
/stu a kax liΥàzur i paΥàk…¦t f…îrt Υ¨sb…î.riΥàjar/
[stu a kax l…ΣΥàz äΥêβÄΩ Äi p…êΥàk…¦t f…îΥêt Υ¨zb…î. Ä…ΣΥà ù…êΥê]
be.needed at each player df deck well dishevel-inf
Each player should shuffle the deck well.

##

188.5: The Second Millennium

notes on various names ordered chronologically.

Damian VI "the Wise" (r. 1199βÄ™1208; Damianus SagΡ¹x) was a Pope in Rome during the tail end of the period known as the Medieval Imperia or the Second Tetrarchy. He was the first Roman Pope elected from the membership of the Brethin religious order, a Kelt monastic organisation dedicated among other things to record-keeping. Like others of his papal name, he honoured the twin saints Cosmas and Damian, third-century martyrs. Damain VI is remembered well, mostly in comparison to his successor Pope Just's numerous catastrophes.

The San TomΟ© (Portingale /saΧÉw tuΥàm…¦/ "Saint Thomas") was a myton [a type of merchant ship] of the Vascon trade fleet; as with many such ships, it was nominally the personal property of one of the powerful Vask noble families enirched by Atlantic fishing. Logs of its voyages have by happenstance been well-preserved. During one of its regular routes, from Cale [Oporto] to Damvath in September of 1492, the log records being unable to make port at Bournemouth due to battle in the city and harbour (part of the Wars of Fealty).

Gudmund and Rebecca Scradder (fl. 1677) were a married couple of Dane astronomers. In the 1670s they undertook a voyage to Seldiva [Sri Lanka] as part of a global scientific effort to observe a predicted transit of Mercury. Their observations helped to compute the mean distance from the Earth to the Sun.

Cadogan Torrior (fl. 1890) was an author from Nadacow [a city on the banks of the lower Mississippi] in Hasiny. Of relatively minor account during his life, his works of masquira like the 1890 trevold Men of Jet and Diamond have attained greater popularity through the mid-twentieth century. This was thanks to successful film adaptations, such as the New Provincial 1950 production Blanc Sabaje "Jet White".

##

189.1: Disappearance of the Redstone Chair

notes in translation on the subject of the Redstone Chair, containing the (alleged) block upon which the Elved Marriage between a princess of Markland and the ruler of Dane Northumber was solemnised.

Certan historicour aun l'apponment scas ig Preu ΟêurnΟß a Londr d'an 1290 fos cos parΟßal motivaΟΑ par un vellisqment vars y CaΟΑer emposseΟ·r; accont son discrepant cas y facΟßon envoluΟΑ.
Some historians have conjectured that the First Burning of London in 1290 was in part motivated by a plan to acquire the Chair; the responsible party varies in the telling.

Lonc y tesmogn costumner, l'intermane un kerel sur y plaΟßment dy CaΟΑer ne PieΟΑr Roug ayent y Rix cas FieltaΟΑ akirtaΟΑ, ec durant pres un cinquantein d'annaΟΑ tras y torn ag siecr seΟß eΟΑ ec ayent pres toΟΑ y polity sur Albion jannestaΟΑ.
According to the traditional account, it was a dispute over the location of the Redstone Chair which sparked the Wars of Fealty, which lasted almost fifty years over the turn of the sixteenth century and which embroiled almost all the polities of Albion.

Cos infeliΟß, y CaΟΑer denoy repars a milloy dy tourf cosogr y rix a plaΟß entarn entr d'an 1505 e 1509.
Unfortunately, the Chair was lost in the tumult of the wars at some point between 1505 and 1509.

Noc obstroint javig monfeyaΟΑ e dou sovembr ag mendrem, y CaΟΑer n'a jamay erref se caut, eΟΑ y recordaΟßon modern de sy pariscenΟß se plaΟß cos enter sur depegnt ne pieΟß portrait e belem de coronnaΟß.
Despite several searches and at least two hoaxes, the Chair has never been recovered, and the modern understanding of its appearance rests entirely on depictions in a few portraits and coronation scenes.

189.2: greim "lap"

greim /grim/ [g Äim]
- lap, the upper legs of a seated person;
- skirt, apron, the part of a garment that covers this part of the body;
- bosom, embrace, affection, care, the figurative position of being nurtured or protected by someone

Etymology: direct descendant via Old Borlish from Latin gremium "lap; bosom; embrace". The term has been used in tailoring from the thirteenth century. The metaphorical sense may continue the similar usage in Latin, but is more likely to be an independent innovation of the early modern period.

Y cociner le tirau des un cromn de greim.

189.3: lasdr "pathetic"

lasdr /Υàlaz.drΧ©/ [Υàla.zd…ê]
- (archaic) leprous, infected with one of the diseases known as leprosy;
- pitiable, pitiful, pathetic, wretched, arousing feelings of sympathy or pathos;
- contemptible, despicable, deplorable, arousing feelings of scorn or disdain;
- ineffectual, impotent, occurring to little useful effect

Etymology: Middle Borlish borrowing from contemporary French lasdre "n. leper, wretch; adj. leprous, wretched". This descends from Late Latin Lazarus, in reference to a beggar in the New Testamement who was traditionally understood to have been a leper.

Caye gimmolt lasdr a y smoutour ΟΑescar.

fall-ipf trial pitiful at df rioter bait-inf
It was a pathetic attempt to egg on the riots.

189.4: movaΟß "pout"

movaΟß /moΥàvats/ [m äΥàvats]
- pout, moue, an expression of displeasure via protruding lips;
- pet, brood, sulk, huff, mood, a fit of sullenness as characterised by pouting

Etymology: eighteenth century, pejorative noun formation from movar "pout, sulk". This reflects earlier moar, a borrowing from Old French verb moer and noun moe "grimace", each from a Frankish form reflecting Classical German mauv≈ç "sleeve; protruding lip".

Sy figl reloy sur y hayennaΟß con un movaΟß.

[si fajl  Ä…ΣΥàl…îj s…ΣΥêβÄΩ Äi Υ¨he.j…ΣΥànas k…în …Σm m äΥàvats]
3s.gn child comeback.pst on df burden with indf pout
His kid continued the homework with a pout.

##

189.5: The Three Rs

notes on three features of this alternate history setting which begin with the letter R.

Radulf "the Kite" (Borlish Radolf y Buttr /raΥàd…îlf i Υàb…Σt.rΧ©/, r. 951βÄ™) was the last English king of the rump Northumber, ruling from the castle at Edinbrough. He was wed to a woman referred to in the chronicles as one Atre of Cheswic, who may have been a daughter of King Roderick of Dunclothe [Strathclyde, Cumbria]. His reign ended with the expansion of Dane lands up and across from Yorwick [York]; by the turn of the millennium the entirety of the Neck was under Dane authority.

Ricardo IvarrachezurΟ≠ (fl. 1824) was a farm theorist from Vascony [SW France, N Spain], who fought as a mozardisto during the Second German War. IvarrachezurΟ≠ was the first theorist to record the ability of copper to kill sperm, noting in 1824 that βÄ€the seed of the bull, the ram and the stallion alike are made impotent in storing them for some hours in a syringe of copperβÄù. He recognised that this was the same mechanism by which copper prevented infections. Although this sterilising property of copper would later be productively used in contraceptives, IvarrachezurΟ≠ treated it as a problem for farmers attempting to artificially breed livestock.

The Runeplate Body (Borlish Corp a Runment /k…îrp a r…ΣnΥàm…¦nt/) is a charitable organisation with multinational funding, headquartered in the Kiewan city of Lengrod [Lviv, Ukraine]. Its stated purpose is to compile information on all the writing systems of the world and to recommend standard encodings of glyphs for use on e.g. grimmers [computers]. The organisation, formally established in 1942, grew out of several small groups interested in writing systems, either on an amateurish basis or affiliated with various higher education establishments. The word "runeplate" originally referred to a sheet of metal into which the shapes of writing have been etched.

##

190.1: Tell Me, Telmy!

    excerpt in translation from children's educational book Tell Me Everything About the Solar System, part of the Tell Me, Telmy! series originally written by Adamara Godwilham.

Tandic evolgaΟΑ stan y novel dy descovart dy preu corp celest cos vray insuet posc millenner, gent orΟΑenjaurn plusour nom poll'ec pharΡΪ Iovis "balis de Jou".
As word spread of the first really new celestial bodies to be discovered for thousands of years, people came up with lots of names for these pharΡΪ Iovis βÄ€lighthouses of JoveβÄù.

Certan gent desiran ou lorry patron, segnour ben eiΟΑel glorfeyar.
Some people wanted to bring glory to their patrons, lords or lands.

Autr gent tornan cur vars renarranΟß de legend classic lor stant all'eutaΟΑ.
Others reached to retellings of Classical legends which were in fashion.

L'ustol ayent l'ec an duraΟΑ cas y lengaΟß European, e stant mantenuΟΑ jug candon nos eu fort may de picq lun Jovial trovaΟΑ a mesur dy siecr derran, interman ig y gyrour de Jou nommar lonc dy prosequent mortal.
The convention that has lasted in European languages, and which has been maintained since we have found many more small Jovial moons in the last century, has been to name JupiterβÄôs gyrers [satellites] for his mortal descendants.

Nos eu l'issar parey accommis cas y dou lun de Mars, Dimus e Phobus, stant nommaΟΑ lonc sell'heir jammel e pedisecq.
We have done the same with the two moons of Mars, Dimus [Phobos] and Phobus [Deimos], which are named for his twin sons and attendants.

190.2: barrougl "wheelbarrow"

barrougl /baΥàrujl/ [b…êΥà Ä äjl]
- wheelbarrow, handcart, a small one-wheeled cart with handles used for transporting small loads;
- trolley, cart, a conveyance for carrying groceries or other items around a shop;
- buggy, pram, stroller, a small carriage in which an infant or toddler sits or lies and is pushed around

Etymology: via Old Borlish bero»ùl "handcart, trolley" from synonymous birocla in Vulgar Latin. This reflects birotula, a diminutive from late Latin birota "two-wheeled cart" which comes from bi- "double" and rota "wheel". Compare Tuscan baroccio and Old French beroue, brouette, both meaning "cart". The term was likely influenced by the unrelated Old English bearwe "basket, handcart". In more general use the term is comparatively recent, only seen from the late ninteenth century.

I sta quemant com babe ne barrougl.
/i sta kweΥàmant k…îm baΥàbe ne baΥàrujl/
[i sta kw…ΣΥàmant k…îm b…êΥà Ä äjl ne b…êΥà Ä äjl]
3s be.ipf cosy as baby in cart
He was as snug as a bug in a rug.

190.3: ΟΑejar "tuck"

ΟΑejar /ΟΑeΥà £ar/ [ΟΑ…ΣΥà ù…êΥê]
- tuck, insert, fit, wedge, stick, lodge, to push into a confined or secure position;
- to place underneath something else;
- ram, shove, to drive with some force;
- compact, compress, to cause to be in a more convenient shape or configuration;
- (reflexive) fold up, curl up, to bend onself and hold onto one's legs; 
- (reflexive) fit, be neat, to be suited to taking up some particular space

Etymology: disputed. The word is often presumed to be a metathetic reflex of Old Borlish (a)ieΟΑar [(a)d £iΥàΟΑar] "ram, drive, push forward". This descends from Latin ariet≈ç "strike, batter", a verbal derivative from ariΡ™s "ram; battering ram". However, this verb is last attested over two centuries before the first evidence of ΟΑejar in the Middle Borlish period.

The alternative with the most backing is that the word reflects Latin adici≈ç "I throw at; I attach, augment", which has semantic and phonetic plausibility, although no intermediate forms are in evidence. Other suggestions, like a first-conjugation variant of Latin adig≈ç "I drive, hurl, compel" or a Norrish [Norrish] verb like ΟΨjΟΓ "force" or ΟΨeisa "thrust, rush", have rarely been credited.

Jo ΟΑejau y pie neΟΑan y port closisceus.
/ £o ΟΑeΥà £o i pje neΥàΟΑan i p…îrt Υ¨klo.ziΥàxauz/
[ ùo ΟΑ…ΣΥà ùo i pje n…ΣΥàΟΑan i p…îΥêt Υ¨klo.z…ΣΥàxauz]
1s tuck-pst df foot lest df door close-ipf.sbj
I wedged the door open with my foot.

190.4: lan "wool"

lan /lan/ [lan]
- wool, the hair of sheep or other animals which is shorn to make clothing;
- curls, human hair which is notably curly;
- stuff, paraphernalia, junk, gear, undifferentiated possessions or objects in general;
- waffle, blather, meaningless or indecipherable speech or writing

Etymology: uncomplicated descendant of Latin lΡ¹na "wool". For human hair the word is attested sporadically from early Middle Borlish, with a resurgence in the twentieth century. The generic senses appear to descend from an obsolete slang use of the word to mean "cash, coins", which was briefly current in the early modern period.

Y barvier portan lan des lorrell'ovegl meΟΑes.
/i barΥàvj…¦r p…îrΥàtan lan d…¦z l…îΥ¨r…¦.loΥàvijl meΥàΟΑ…¦z/
[i b…êΥêΥàvj…¦Υê p äΥêΥàtan lan d…¦z l äΥ¨ Ä…¦.l äΥàv…Σjl m…ΣΥàΟΑ…¦z]
df shepherd wear-ipf-3p wool out.of 3p.gn=sheep even
The shepherds wore their own sheep's wool.

190.5: Multiple Choice Quiz

excerpt from the 2025 book Everything Under the Sun, published by the Royal Tommarth Castinghouse, a publisher based in the capital of Markland. It made use of the branding and popularity of a puzzle-book series begun by Merch couple Anna and Ralph Highwater in the 1930s. This collection of questions concerns the Drengot Collusion (1800sβÄ™1961), an alliance-federacy of northwest Europe.

History 22: The Drengot Collusion (answers on sheet 532)

-1. Which of the twenty-four ministeries which made up the Drengotian administration was established last, set up in 1932 under the aegis of Runcibal Sawyer Boon?
- the Minstry for Consequences
- the Ministry for Family
- the Ministry for Information
- the Ministry for Wealth

-2. Which of the following four trevolds [novels] was the first publication confirmed to have sold over one hundred myriad copies across the Collusion in its first year?
- A Door, Once Opened (1920) by Clarissa Bellamy
- Command That I be Healed (1914) by Honorine Maignard
- One Evening Will Have to be Enough (1913) by JosephΟ®ne Foilliers
- The Day We Let the Chaos In (1921) by Martha and Dean Armstrong

-3. The Drengot Collusion only gained that name in the late nineteenth century, after it expanded to absorb which political entity?
- Borland
- Markland
- the New Urban Mesh (also colloquially 'Greater London')
- Willemy

-4. Which of these theoretical inventions was not developed at the Gomster Atellier in the first half of the 20th century, the partially state-owned development wing of the Hathwall Magna astraphor guild [electric company]?
- CMM veronic [vinyl] 
- Glass-light [fibre optic] communication
- Immortal [stainless] steel
- 'Tommy' pills [ibuprofen]

191.1: The Vanilla Manoeuvre

excerpt in translation from the 1992 trevold The Vanilla Manoeuvre (originally published in Merch under the title Tashban Tacks), written by Vendetta Boon, the granddaughter of Lord Runcibal Sawyer Boon, one-time Minster for Consequences in the Drengot Collusion. Of middling success, this spycraft work owes more to the film adaptations of the classics of the genre than to those classics themselves.

Y contris alandau n'un descovannet mejoncr, fait impacent sdruΟßollar tras y tabr.
The dossier arrived in a nondescript folder, shoved impatiently across the table.

Cattridge l'ovreu varsant eΟΑ examinau l'icon y quagl depegntant: un domn eld y carantein ant un sourey picq sousonnant a secret massem.
Cattridge flipped it open and studied the photograph of the target: a woman in her forties with a small smile that hinted at larger secrets.

Tre agent mort a mesur de tan semman, verim detaxionnaΟΑ des fΟΠx souglβÄîno se poΟΑe l'oΟß panel mesveir.
Three dead agents in as many weeks, poisons derived from threshold drossβÄîthe pattern was unmistakable.

"I sera vos attenent ag fest cas l'ambasctour," dis y harvougl de Cattridge, ne sy jalicq vert cos martellaΟΑ yauvnant.
"She'll be expecting you at the embassy reception," Cattridge's handler said, adjusting her aggressively green waistcoat.

"L'ambasctour Napolitan fay recougl de jarry d'Istr Boral pre-classic. Val l'eΟß effojaΟßon ne Ο΅adrosc svoccar. I vil l'esc mordr."
"The Napolitan ambassador collects pre-Classical Borland ceramics. Mention the Ο΅adrosc excavations. She'll take the bait."

Cattridge encromnau y fougl. A starn, doutr fenestr angost e meg argentfeyaΟΑ, y ployot automnal ne Tommarth fey nuot a nimb teun dy lampader.
Cattridge pocketed the pages. Outside, through narrow and half-mirrored windows, Tommarth's autumn drizzle blurred the streetlamps into wan halos.

Allors, a plaΟß autr ny citaΟΑ, un domn er se preparant por un fest yon i nole surveur.
Elsewhere, across the city, a woman was preparing for a party she wouldn't survive.

191.2: gentagl "riffraff"

gentagl / £…¦nΥàt…¦jl/ [ ù…ΣnΥàt…¦jl]
- populace, population, inhabitants, demos, the people of a region (especially of a nation or polity);
- masses, rabble, riffraff, hoi polloi, the common people as opposed to the elite (often with pejorative affect)

Etymology: Middle Borlish derivative of gent "people, nation" and -agl, an unproductive instrumental or collective suffix. The noun descends straightforwardly from Latin gΡ™ns "clan, tribe, people"; the affix from Latin thematic vowel -Ρ¹- and suffix -cula "tool, instrument". Attestatations of gentΡ¹cula (with a first-declension variant of the suffix) "common people" in Scholastic Latin are likely modelled after the vernacular Romance forms.

Nuvr soΟΑ vos melle faint con y gentagl.

new be.2p 2p ton do-p.prs with df demos
You've been mixing with riffraff recently.

191.3: entrlaΟßour "hustler"

entrlaΟßour /Υ¨…¦nt.rΧ©.laΥàtsur/ [Υ¨…¦n.t…ê.l…êΥàts äΥê]
- wheeler-dealer, hustler, schemer, a shrewd or unscrupulous operator in politics or commerce;
- swindler, sharpster, grifter, con artist, racketeer, someone running scams;
- pedlar, huckster, a small-time or itinerant merchant of (usually poor-quality) goods

Etymology: nineteenth century, borrowed (probably via intermedaries) from Sicilia ntrlazzaturi "swindler, racketeer". This is an agentive derivation from the noun ntrlazzu "scam, racket", which reflects Latin inter- "between" and laqueus "noose". Specialisation to politics probably occurred before the word entered Borlish, although there was no shortage of corruption in contempory governance on Borland to inspire the shift.

Jo broiΟΑau l'entrlaΟßour coll'outrley.
/ £o br…îjΥàΟΑo Υ¨l…¦nt.rΧ©.laΥàtsur i Υ¨lut.rΧ©Υàli/
 ùo b Ä äjΥàΟΑo Υ¨l…¦n.t…ê.l…êΥàts äΥêβÄΩ Äi Υ¨lu.t…êΥàli/
1s blur-pst df=hustler and df=outlaw
I mixed up the scammer and the outlaw.

191.4: hoΟΑic "reeve"

hoΟΑic /hoΥàΟΑ…Σk/ [h äΥàΟΑ…Σk]
- reeve, headman, official, village leader, a person with authority in a small community;
- chief, chieftain, the leader of a clan or tribe;
- councillor, alderman, a member of a local governmental body

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish houΟΑic, hoΟΑinc "village leader; esp. leader of a Norrish settlement in the north of Borland". This is a borrowing from Old Norrish h«ΪfΟΑingi "chief, lord", which is a derivation of h«ΪfuΟΑ "head". The term was first used for elected positions in town or city councils in the fifteenth century.

Y bourg deleu l'oc hoΟΑic ne souc d'un courstray.
/i burg deΥàlaw l…îk hoΥàΟΑ…Σk ne suk d…Σn kurΥàstre/

df town delete-pst df=s.px reeve in wake of=indf affair
The town fired this councillor due to an affair.

##

191.5: Blue Trans-Willem War

The Blue Trans-Willem War (Willem: Blaw Trans-Willemsk Ο™rlog, Borlish: Var Tras-Willem Blau) was one of a series of wars in the early modern period which principally involved French and Dutch [German] polities (that is, those powers located on either side of the territory of Willemy [~Benelux, Lorraine]) and which were de facto waged over control of said territory.

Though the sporadic flare-ups of hostilies are difficult to divide into discrete wars without some arbitrariness, modern historiography usually works with the chromatic periodisation of nineteenth-century writer Andrysk de Santer. Her system, based either (depending on the account) on the colours of the victors' standards or on alchemical symbolism, has the Blue war as the penultimate conflict, ending in the late seventeenth century. Some historians, though not de Santer, have classified the Blue war as part of the broader collection of Romantic Wars of the mid-seventeenth century.

As a coup de grΟΔce following a series of smaller humiliations in earlier decades, the kingdom of Burgundy was formally dissolved after the Blue Trans-Willem War, with its territory partitioned between Provence and the kingdoms of France and Bavarn. For example, the duchy of Far Suebia was created as a vassal of Bavarn as a gift from King Frederick to the margrave Thorsten Villingen; the former Burgund capital of Geneva became a subordinate city in this new realm.

The duchy of Avosche and its seat at Axton [Aachen], which after a stable sixteenth century as a French vassal had changed hands five times in recent decades, was returned to French control as part of French Guillenne in the aftermath of the Blue Trans-Willem War.

##

192.1: Vector Geometry

excerpt in translation from Drawing in the Sand: the History of Calculation, a collaborative reference book originally published 1954 in Napolitan as Scheme ne Rene: a Storia d'Amiradanza by the Agenza Scholaria Fodgesa.

Nell'oc strait, nos indagau y defas dy "method a bout" com annexment de geometry Euclidean.
In this section, we chart the development of the "dart method" as an extension of Euclidean geometry.

Par toΟΑ y theorem dy codex Element, reconnuΟΑ no's alcun autranΟß entr un lign AB e sell'envarjon BA.
In all the theorems of The Elements, no distinction is made between a line AB and its reverse BA.

Caye for durant y siecr cinqueΟß yon scholer ig inclus Barclythe e da Treviso enΟßan a asmuth ascreir a lign.
It is only in the late fifteenth century that scholars like Barclythe and da Treviso begin to assign directions to lines.

Pall'oc affixion pariscent petty es ig geometour denoirn poΟΑir dou cos signfeyant complir.
It was this seemingly trivial addition that enabled geometers to do two important things.

Primer, ΟΑe errevn quantaΟΑ ablat destr a dessou dy nimb a convenibiltaΟΑ cas mathematic European.
First, they brought negative quantities into the realm of respectability in European mathematics.

Con tan l'idea de "mendrem a zefr pom" fos incossirabr, alcun hom no'r vexaΟΑ par y noΟßon d'un sojorn bouclant ag pognt iniΟßal par aΟΑendrar des A vars B, e soulor des B vars A.
Although the idea of "fewer than zero apples" was absurd, no-one was perturbed by the concept of returning to one's starting point by travelling from A to B, and then from B to A.

##

192.2: oΟΑig "especially"

oΟΑig /oΥàΟΑaj/ [ äΥàΟΑaj]
- (literary) especially, particularly, to a greater extent than is usual;
- to wit, i.e., viz., namely, that is, expressed in other words;
- or even, if not, moreover, but also, introducing an additional property or fact;
- (interjection) sure, just so, right, I see, expressing that something is (now) clear or a more general agreement

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish oΟΑi»ù "truly, certainly", which reflects Latin adverb utique "in any case, at least, in particular". The term in its adverbial use is now restricted to very formal writing, and the clarifying sense is also more of the written than spoken register.

The final two sets of senses are sometimes held to be an unrelated compound, comprising Old Borlish o, oΟΑ, ou, ouΟΑ "whether" and i»ù "there". These come from Latin aut "or" and ibi "there" respectively.

L'oc defey sera dur oΟΑig inacavabr.
/l…îk deΥàfi seΥàra d…Σr oΥàΟΑaj iΥ¨na.kaΥàvab.rΧ©/
[l…îg d…ΣΥàfi s…ΣΥà Äa d…ΣΥêβÄΩ Ä äΥàΟΑaj …ΣΥ¨na.k…êΥàva.b…ê]
df=s.px test be.fut-s hard or.even infeasible
This challenge will be hard or even impossible.

##

192.3: egnam "lumber"

egnam /ijΥànam/ […ΣjΥànam]
- timber, lumber, wood from trees used for construction;
- plank, a long and thick piece of timber;
- beam, post, rafter, a heavy piece of wood used to provide support for some part of a building;
- (colloquial, dated) idiot, dumbass, pillock, simpleton, somebody stupid;
- (vulgar, usually jocular) dick, cock, a penis

Etymology: Middle Borlish, rebracketing of earlier legnam "timber" reinterpreting the initial <l-> as the definite article. This is a borrowing from some Romance language's cognate of Medieval Latin lignΡ¹men "timber"; it cannot be a direct descendant because native words reflect -Ρ¹men as -amn, cf. eramn "bronze", scamn "swarm". The word is no longer current as a personal insult except in one-off comparisons of somebody to a literal plank; in vulgar use the term is attested from the 1970s but may be much older.

Dou carpenter son egnam de casn coupant.
/du Υ¨kar.p…¦nΥàt…¦r s…în ijΥànam de Υàkaz.nΧ© kuΥàpant/
[du Υ¨k…êΥê.p…ΣnΥàt…¦Υê s…în …ΣjΥànam de Υàka.z…ên k äΥàpant]
two carpenter be.3p timber of oak cut-p.prs
Two carpenters are cutting oak planks.
