192.4: pentis "bivouac"

pentis /p…¦nΥàt…Σz/ [p…ΣnΥàt…Σz]
- hut, shed, outbuilding, a small structure built as storage or shelter;
- annex, extension, a further interior section added to a building;
- lean-to, an attachment onto the outside of a building;
- bivouac, an improvised overnight shelter

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed from contemporary French apentis "appendage, attached building". This reflects appendicium, a Medieval Latin variant of Classical appendix "supplement, addition". In the last sense it was originally restricted to military encampments.

Y gardinner apportau un pal des y pentis.
/i Υ¨gar.d…ΣΥàn…¦r Υ¨a.p…îrΥàto …Σn pal d…¦z i p…¦nΥàt…Σz/
[i Υ¨g…êΥê.d…ΣΥàn…¦ΥêβÄΩΥ¨ Äa.p äΥêΥàto …Σm pal d…¦z i p…ΣnΥàt…Σz]
df gardener bring-pst indf spade out.of df hut
The gardener got a shovel out of the shed.

##

192.5: Borlish Adjectives

Borlish adjectives have two forms: a base form and a comparative form. The comparative is formed by adding a suffix, which shifts stress to the penultimate syllable.
Regular Comparative in -essem

The regular comparative suffix is -essem, which replaces or follows the final segment of the adjective and attracts penultimate stress.

    βÄΙleger legeressemβÄΚ
    /leΥà £…¦r Υ¨le. £eΥàr…¦.s…¦m/
    'light lighter'

Several orthographic adjustments may occur when the suffix is added. Before the vowel-initial suffix, βÄΙΟßβÄΚ becomes βÄΙcβÄΚ, while βÄΙcβÄΚ becomes βÄΙchβÄΚ to preserve the /k/ pronunciation.

    βÄΙlavaΟß lavacessem | starc starchessemβÄΚ
    /laΥàvats Υ¨la.vaΥàts…¦.s…¦m | stark starΥàk…¦.s…¦m/
    'wasteful {more wasteful} | strong stronger'

After a final orthographic βÄΙ-qβÄΚ, a linking βÄΙ-u-βÄΚ is inserted.

    βÄΙpicq picquessemβÄΚ
    /p…Σk p…ΣΥàkw…¦.s…¦m/
    'small smaller'

The final consonant may be doubled in the comparative form to maintain the checked quality of the preceding vowel. This occurs most commonly with final βÄΙ-lβÄΚ.

    βÄΙboncop boncoppessemβÄΚ
    /b…înΥàk…îp Υ¨b…în.k…îΥàp…¦.s…¦m/
    'cheap cheaper'

The final consonant may be voiced in the comparative form. This is seen most commonly in present participles ending in βÄΙ-ntβÄΚ (which form comparatives in βÄΙ-ndessemβÄΚ) and in adjectives ending in fricatives, though only final βÄΙ-fβÄΚ reflects this orthographically as βÄΙ-vessemβÄΚ.

    βÄΙtart tardessem | braf bravessemβÄΚ
    /tart tarΥàd…¦.s…¦m | braf braΥàv…¦.s…¦m/
    'late later | brave braver'

Adjectives ending in a written βÄΙ-VuβÄΚ diphthong form the comparative in βÄΙ-VvessemβÄΚ.

    βÄΙblau blavessemβÄΚ
    /blo blaΥàv…¦.s…¦m/
    'blue bluer'

Certain historically long vowels in the final syllable of an adjective may shorten in the comparative form. This occurs most commonly with adjectives ending in βÄΙ-ousβÄΚ.

    βÄΙfatuous fatuosessemβÄΚ
    /faΥàtjuz Υ¨fa.tjoΥàz…¦.s…¦m/
    'clumsy clumsier'

Adjectives ending in βÄΙ-eβÄΚ form the comparative in βÄΙ-eyessemβÄΚ.

    βÄΙidone idoneyessemβÄΚ
    /Υ¨i.doΥàne Υ¨i.doΥànj…¦.s…¦m/
    'convenient {more convenient}'

Adjectives in Syllabic -r

Adjectives ending in syllabic βÄΙ-rβÄΚ show several patterns. Occasionally they form regular comparatives in βÄΙ-ressemβÄΚ.

    βÄΙjundr jundressemβÄΚ
    /Υà £…Σn.drΧ©  £…ΣnΥàdr…¦.s…¦m/
    'junior {more junior}'

Some adjectives, most notably those ending in the suffix βÄΙ-abrβÄΚ "-able", form comparatives in βÄΙ-lessemβÄΚ rather than βÄΙ-ressemβÄΚ.

    βÄΙstabr stablessem | sengr senglessemβÄΚ
    /Υàstab.rΧ© staΥàbl…¦.s…¦m | Υàs…¦n.grΧ© s…¦nΥàgl…¦.s…¦m/
    'steady steadier | simple simpler'

The majority of adjectives ending in syllabic βÄΙ-rβÄΚ form the comparative with the suffix βÄΙ-erremβÄΚ, which replaces the final syllabic consonant and is stressed penultimately. The same adjustments that affect βÄΙ-essemβÄΚ may also affect βÄΙ-erremβÄΚ.

    βÄΙnuvr nuverrem | diΟßr dicerremβÄΚ
    /Υàn…Σv.rΧ© niΥàv…¦.r…¦m | Υàd…Σts.rΧ© diΥàts…¦.r…¦m/
    'fresh fresher | great greater'

    βÄΙallagr allagherrem | leur leverremβÄΚ
    /aΥàl…¦j.rΧ© Υ¨a.l…¦Υàj…¦.r…¦m | Υàlau.rΧ© leΥàv…¦.r…¦m/
    'happy happier | free freer'

Irregular Comparatives

A few adjectives show irregular stem changes in the comparative.

    βÄΙbrey bressemβÄΚ
    /bri Υàbr…¦.s…¦m/
    'short shorter'

Several common adjectives have fully suppletive comparative forms.

    βÄΙbon ottem | mal pessem | gran maissemβÄΚ
    /b…în Υà…î.t…¦m | mal Υàp…¦.s…¦m | gran Υàme.s…¦m/
    'good better | bad worse | big bigger'

##

193.1: Simon MonΟßating

notes in translation on the subject of Borlish nobleman Simon MonΟßating.

Segr Simon, y sest Baron MonΟßating (n. 1770) sta un panditour, un quomodolibet notoir cas y melle ne DamvaΟΑ, eΟΑ un partejan primal por Theology a DesveyanΟß n'Istr Boral.
Lord Simon, the sixth Baron MonΟßating (b. 1770) was an intellectual, a notorious libertine of Damvath high society, and an early proponent of Deviance Theology in Borland.

L'aye un orin Waranesc par y linaΟß de sy maΟΑr.
He was of Warnine [GuaranΟ≠] descent through his mother.

Sy famigl sta notoir tras dezein jusc oy deut an rix legal stendiscent entr sy paΟΑr e sy cosvour scon stemn poll'auteΟß dy Barony a Haubrath.
His family had been notorious for decades due to a protracted legal battle between his father and second cousin over the Barony of Haubrath.

Pre l'aye eld ogtoΟß Simon poΟΑe parolar ne Latin, Teudesc (e lonc y teurfeyour den, Waranesc), eΟΑ i save clavir frottar e torriot hoissar.
By the age of eighteen Simon could speak Latin, Dutch (and according to his defamers, Warnine), and he could play the clavier and the bassoon.

Posc un quoncein de sanc ΟΑus ne DamvaΟΑ, sy sojornaΟßon afferjaraurn y mont, ig inclus sy feu gradual sur y rimmot auster ne Cappatia.
After a fiery young adulthood in Damvath, his travels took him across the world, including to his titular fief on the eastern coast of Cappatia [South America].

193.2: mejoncr "mid"

mejoncr /meΥà £…înk.rΧ©/ [m…ΣΥàj…î≈΄.k…ê]
- mid, mediocre, middling, average, OK, decent, of only moderate quality (usually disappointingly so);
- indifferent, lukewarm, unenthusiastic, not showing much excitement or optimism;
- unremarkable, forgettable, failing to make a strong impression;
- safe, cautious, risk-averse, avoiding extremes or deviation (of choices or behaviour);
- ambivalent, mixed, neither clearly good nor bad

Etymology: alteration of Middle Borlish mejocr "middling" under the influence of unrelated words like coroncr "impostor" and pujoncr "dagger" (whose endings descend from the diminutive suffix -(un)culus in Latin). This was borrowed directly from Latin mediocris "mediocre", a possible compound of medius "middle" and ocris "rugged mountain" (as to suggest "halfway up").

I da empley a xiphar mejoncr naΟΑusc.
/i da …¦mΥàpli a ksiΥàfar meΥà £…înk.rΧ© naΥàΟΑ…Σx/
[i da …ΣmΥàpli a ks…ΣΥàf…êΥê m…ΣΥà ù…î≈΄.k…ê n…êΥàΟΑ…ΣΟß]
3s give job to sabre mid a.bit
She employs rather mid mercenaries.

##

193.3: desjovar "uncouple"

desjovar /Υ¨d…¦z. £oΥàvar/ [Υ¨d…¦z. ù äΥàv…êΥê]
- unyoke, uncouple, unlink, to remove from a yoke or harness;
- detach, disengage, unfasten, separate, to cause to be apart;
- liberate, free, to deliver from oppression or imprisonment;
- (colloquial, reflexive) divorce, to end a marriage;
- unplug, disconnect, to sever the link between an electrical appliance and the mains

Etymology: Middle Borlish, derivative of jovar "yoke, tie together, match; (later) plug in". This is either a direct descendant of Latin iug≈ç "I join, yoke" or more likely a re-verbing via jou < Latin iugum "yoke". The term was used metaphorically almost at once, especially in religious contexts. In both the colloquial and electrical senses the word is first attested in the nineteenth century.

Skig sdroyet desjovar cant moustr se migrn.
/skai sdr…îΥàj…¦t Υ¨d…¦z. £oΥàvar kant mus.trΧ© se Υàm…Σg.rnΧ©/
[skaj zd Ä äΥà ù…¦t Υ¨d…¦z. ù äΥàv…êΥê kant Υàmu.st…ê se Υàm…Σ.g…ên]
refrain stock-dim unlink-inf when data rfl migrate-3p
Do not unplug drives during file transfer.

193.4: outrvalir "overestimate"

outrvalir /Υ¨ut.rΧ©.vaΥàl…Σr/ [Υ¨u.t…ê.v…êΥàl…ΣΥê]
- overrate, inflate, to imagine to be better or greater in some respect than in truth;
- highball, overestimate, to make a guess at a value that is too high;
- (reflexive) overween, boast, swagger, brag, vaunt, to act in an arrogant or conceited manner

Etymology: early Modern Borlish, derivation of valir "be worth" < Latin vale≈ç "I am strong" with the prefix outr- < Latin ultrΡ¹ "beyond" (compare independent Borlish words outr "complete, utter" and doutr "beyond"). In reflexive use the word is attested from the Long Peace period at the turn of the nineteenth century.

L'oΟß bougr s'outrval cos outr.
/l…îts Υàbug.rΧ© Υ¨sut.rΧ©Υàval k…îz Υàut.rΧ©/
[l…îs Υàbu.g…ê Υ¨su.t…êΥàval k…îz Υàu.t…ê]
df=s.dt guy rfl=inflate adv total
That chap's an utter braggart.

##

193.5: Aqueous Axton

Axton (Parisian French Grand-Aix /g ÄaΧÉΥàd…¦iks/, Willem Dutch Aughen /Υà…î.xnΧ©/) [Aachen] is a major city in the polity of Willemy and the municipal centre of the region of Avosche. It is located on the Worm river, which flows via the Rudder [Rur] into the Maze [Meuse].

The name of the city derives from post-Classical German *ΡÄkΡ¹ "Axton", attested in late Old English as Aces t≈Ϊn "*ΡÄkΡ¹'s town". This in turn was borrowed from Latin aqua "water", in reference to the local thermal springs. The placename was pluralised in most Dutch languages, while in French languages the placename acquired the descriptor grand- "great" to distinguish it from other towns named Aix for their bathing waters. Compare the Borlish city of Axbane, from the Latin Aquis Balnei "to the bathing waters".

The city was of mostly ceremonial importance through the second millennium, as the seat of the county (then the march, then the duchy) of Avosche within Willemy. Roger Nassow, first duke of Avosche in the early sixteenth century, oversaw the construction and expansion of the Nassow palace complex in the foothills of the Eiffel range which overlooked the city. During this time, the city was in the territory of French Guillenne.

Axton grew dramatically into an industry town around the turn of the nineteenth century and through the Global Workshop period. This was made possible by the advent of transport of freight by convoy [train] instead of by water, since the Rudder and Worm rivers are not navigable by large ships. The city also became a hub for learning, with the Axton Benet School rising in prominence under the stewardship of professors such as mathematician Nicolas Defechet/NiclΟΓs de Feght.

##

194.1: Biography of a Mercenary

continued translation from the beginning of [real-life] book The Henchmen of Zenda by KJ Charles, a 2018 pastiche of the 1894 romp The Prisoner of Zenda.

Me reman un lonc veyaΟß par convey a souvar, un veΟΑr a brandvin sou man, eΟΑ un facsimile inscaut cos effus e hortumnesc dy descovan de Rassendyll a cost mey.
There is a long railway journey ahead of me, a glass of brandy to my hand, and a heavily and obscenely annotated copy of the Rassendyll manuscript by my side.

Jo preten a lou cascun benoΟßar por y vertaΟΑ digr.
I intend to use them all to tell the truth. 

Jo dign Jasper Detchard. J'er naΟΑ com ceyon d'un famigl vige ny bandlau deβÄîo bon Deu, me stou vray?
My name is Jasper Detchard. I was born to a well-off family in the outskirts ofβÄîoh dear God, must I?

Nell'ec cont costumner es y histoir linacer dy racontour recitar ant detagl minuΟßous lonc tre generaΟßon, ma jo's enter scur ig my leyour es, ΟΑa possibr, infruniscendessem meΟΑes par my granmaΟΑr agnat a sey jo.
It is customary in these accounts to rehearse oneβÄôs family history in vast detail over three generations, but I am quite sure my reader is, if possible, even less interested in my paternal grandmother than I am.

Ag diabr con. My nom no's Japser Detchard. Quippe l'oc no's i.
To hell with it. My name is not Jasper Detchard. Obviously it is not.

Jo digna un autr nom autr annaΟΑ, ma jo le teurfeya ny leiΟßoir par delit divers, ec ersteigon faint par un temertaΟΑ gra aΟΑief por ig my parent refusaurn l'occajon me parmettr de tan me cavir ablegaΟΑ d'universtaΟΑ, e me deleurn par meyan dy Centuir e d'un offiΟßment ne Hind.
I had another name once, but I stained it at school with various crimes, culminating in a sufficiently serious indiscretion that my parents declined to give me the opportunity of being thrown out of a university too, and disposed of me via the Army and a posting to India.

##

194.2: ou "whether"

ou /u/ [u]
- a particle forming polar questions placed directly after the constituent being interrogated;
- whether, if, whether or not, introducing clauses that are in doubt;
- either, introducing the first of two or more options

Etymology: Old Borlish o ou, "or; question particle", a direct descendant of Latin aut "or". In the last sense, the term was originally used for all the options in a list, but in the modern language the latter options are introduced by ben "or" < Latin bene "well". In the second sense it alternates with ig "that" < Latin ibi "there", which introduced realis clauses; the prototype examples are jo saif ig X "I know that X", jo saif noc ou X "I don't know whether X".

This word is unrelated to homographic ou "egg" < ≈çvum in Latin.

Se poΟΑ ou scadom ben ugnlac accatar.
/se p…îΈΗ u xaΥàd…îm b…¦n ainΥàlak Υ¨a.kaΥàtar/
[se p…îΈΗ u x…êΥàd…îm b…¦n …êjnΥàlak Υ¨a.k…êΥàt…êΥê]
rfl can q tomato or onion buy-inf
You can buy either tomatoes or onions.

##

194.3: farau "never mind"

farau /faΥàro/ [f…êΥà Äo]
- oh well, never mind, no big deal, no sweat, it's OK, an injunction not to worry about something;
- hang in there, c'est la vie, so it goes, a reassurance that things will turn out well despite a setback

Etymology: early Modern Borlish, a clipping of the phrase (still occasionally seen in full) tal farau nos "such is life", literally "so we fare". The verb farar "fare; exist (in a given state)" was borrowed in the Old Borlish period from Old English faran "go, travel; fare". The expression originally represented commiseration (a sense which it partially retains as a self-directed expletive); in its modern senses it is seen from the nineteenth century.

J'ignoy hayennaΟß assignar, ma farau.
/ £ajΥàn…îj Υ¨he.j…¦Υànats Υ¨a.sajΥànar | ma faΥàro/
[ ù…êiΥàn…îi Υ¨hejΥànats Υ¨a.s…êiΥàn…êΥê | ma f…êΥà Äo]
1s=forgo.pst homework task-inf | but nvm
I forgot to set homework, no big deal.

##

194.4: skighar "refrain from"

skighar /skaΥàjar/ [sk…êΥà ù…êΥê]
- avoid, abstain from, to not interact with in some way (as of food, vice, etc);
- eschew, shun, ostracise, exclude, to refuse to associate with (a person or idea) due to fear or moral rectitude;
- refrain from, not do, to stop oneself from performing some action (especially in the infinitive)

Etymology: Old Borlish iski»ùar "to flee, escape"; compare synonymous eschiver in Old French and sΡ΄ΡΪen "fear, recoil, startle" in Old English, all descending from Classical German sciuhas "frightened, timid, shy". The last senses are now by far the most common, originally a colloquial alternative for prohibitive constructions but now ubiquitous.

Val y signal ou "AbseΟ·r Jug" ben "Skig Acceir"?
/val i sajΥànal u Υ¨ab.ziΥàj…Σr  £aj b…¦n skaj akΥàtsir/
[val i s…êjΥànal u Υ¨ab.z…ΣΥà ù…ΣΥê  ùaj b…¦n skaj …êkΥàs…ΣΥê]
should df sign q be.absent-inf ever or not.do access-inf
Should the sign say "Keep Out" or "No Entry"?

194.5: False Friends (cont. 3)

some more completely arbitrary dictionary entries from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (fifth edition), a reference work published in 1961 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

coke [/kuΥêk/ "carbon"] n. the corporal [elements] whose atoms have six mora [protons], found in innumerable substances vital for life, as well as multiple inanimate forms like pencil-coke and diamond; any of several black substances which remain after the burning of wood, principally comprising coke. 17C, singular of cokes, colks < northern dialect term colk "apple core; heartwood; lump of coal". Further origin uncertain. Possibly allied with Frise colk "hole, socket", though the semantic connection is tenuous.

iliad [/Υà…ôi. £ad/ "glance"] n. the act of directing the gaze towards someone in an attempt to communicate something without words; such an act intended as a flirtation or amorous advance; any act of non-verbal communication esp. with flirtatious intent. 16C in various forms < Roun French uilliad "ogle" < uil "eye" < Latin oculus "ΟΠq."; formerly often eyliad or similar by analogy to ey [eye].

oakcorn [/ΥàoΥêkΥ¨koΥên/ "acorn"] n. the fruit of the oak tree; a suit in the Danaw [Danube] deck of cards; vulgarly, the head of the penis or burris [clitoris]. Folk formation from early Merch akern < Old ΟΠcern "oakcorn, tree-mast"; in sexual use from the 15C.

urchin [/ΥàoΥê.t É…Σn/ "hedgehog"] n. a small mammal characterised by its spiny back and habit of rolling up into a ball; in military use, an arrangment of spikes scattered across the ground as an obstacle. Alteration of early Merch irchon [/irΥàt Éun/] < Drengot French irechon < Vulgar Latin Ρ™rΡΪci≈ç < Classical Latin Ρ™rΡΪcius "urchin". Displaced contemporaneous iel < Old English iΡΓil "urchin".

##

195.1: Iron Age Borland

notes in translation on Borland in the Iron Age.

L'Asc de Far inceveu sur Istr Boral a veg temporane cos approximaΟΑ coll'autr Istr NorΟΑovester ny siecr nouf aN.
The Iron Age began on Borland at approximately the same time as on the other Northwestern Isles [Ireland, GB] in the ninth century aN.

ArgutaΟΑ es l'identiaΟΑ dy preu forgour ne far n'Istr Boral e tan rent convoluΟΑ par ont coincident de tramigraΟßon, ma tandem l'ec hom denoy Kelt cos identifeyabr.
The identity of the first Borland ironworkers is debated and is made complicated by overlapping waves of migrations, but eventually they became identifiably Kelt.

Y derran signfeyant entrell'ec ont intermane parolour de Brythonic primal sur Albion instanΟßant sur Istr Boral des l'ovest ny siecr catr aN, stant ancestr dy picq reyaum de fortalleΟß colliner l'istr afferjarant all'hour dell'envajon Roman ny siecr un N.
The last significant such wave was of early Brythonic speakers on Albion settling Borland from the west in the fourth century aN, the ancestors of the small hill-fort kingdoms which covered the island at the time of the Roman invasion in the first century N.

Scein costroinΟß palΟΠochthon plusour sur Istr Boral ec y migraΟßon de Kelt preluΟ·scent ma rebenoΟßaΟΑ par y Kelt ne lorry rit.
There are several archaelogical structures on Borland which predate the Kelt migrations but which the Kelts repurposed for ritual use.

Y parnommaΟΑessem entrell'ec plaΟßment es un "labyrinth" noncupaΟΑ se situant sur Vrug a Ο΅adrosc ny norΟΑovest dell'istr.
The most famous of these sites is a so-called "labyrinth" located on Ο΅adrosc Heath in the northwest of the island.

Scein tan bempart muΟΑel temp daΟΑ nell'oc asc, oy "pieΟΑr sarzin" nommaΟΑ.
There are also many cairns dating to this period, now called "sarzin stones".

195.2: sdroyet "drive"

sdroyet /sdr…îΥàj…¦t/ [zd Ä äΥà ù…¦t]
- (archaic, historical) Ο©tui, toolbox, a miniature (often ornamental) case for holding small items like needles or other utilitarian things;
- drive, card, a device for storing digital data;

Etymology: diminutive of noun sdroy "depot, warehouse, stockpile", first attested in Middle Borlish isdro»ù "freight, goods transported by water". This was borrowed via synoymous zdruog in contemporary Saxon, likely from Polish zdroi "spring, small stream; origin, source". The historical and modern senses are presumed to be independent derivations; the latter first appears over a century after the former became practically obsolete.

L'a echo vardiΟΑ ny sdroyet por reΟΑrtien.
/la eΥàko varΥàd…ΣΈΗ ni sdr…îΥàj…¦t p…îr Υ¨r…¦ΟΑ.rΧ©Υàtj…¦n/

3s=have copy keep-p.pst in-df drive for backup
She's got backup copies on the drive.

195.3: oistr "wedlock"

oistr /Υà…îjs.trΧ©/ [Υà…îi.st…ê]
- (poetic) marriage, wedlock, matrimony, the state of being married;
- unity, agreement, consonance, harmony, compatibility, the condition of fitting well together

Etymology: borrowed in the early modern period from old northern dialect term oistre "wife", which descends directly from synonymous uxor in Latin (compare Old French uissor "wife"). The shift in meaning appears to be due to the poet Bautrmeu, who introduced the term to standard Borlish in her work MornΟß a Morn "A Piece of the Action". It is presumed that she misinterpreted the (even then archaic) northern idiom prendr n'oistr "to wed, lit. to take as wife" as "to take in wedlock".

Noc saif no's oistr eld ogtoΟß.
/n…îk sef n…îz Υà…îjs.trΧ© …¦ld …îjΥàt…îts/
[n…îk sef n…îz Υà…îj.st…êβÄΩ Ä…¦ld  äjΥàt…îts]
ng wise ng-be wedlock aged eighteen
Marriage at eighteen is unwise.

195.4: foncΟßondry "agency"

foncΟßondry /Υ¨f…în.ks…înΥàdri/ [f…î≈΄.ks änΥàd Äi]
- agency, business, establishment, an entity engaged in commerce (usually in the service industry) or the building in which it is located;
- department, office, an administrative unit of a government or its headquarters;
- (in particular, euphemistically) intelligence agency, an organisation for gathering information relevant to national security and defence

Etymology: eighteenth century, derivation via the gestalt-forming suffix -ry from noun foncΟßon "purpose, use". In turn this was borrowed from Latin f≈Ϊncti≈ç "performance, execution (of a task)". The governmental sense is original; in commercial use compare similarly-formed words like vessondry "clothier" and govondry "canteen". In espionage the word is seen from the twentieth century, originally as in-house slang.

Οêe disrn empley broucar n'un foncΟßondry.
/ΟΑe Υàd…Σz.rnΧ© …¦mΥàpli bruΥàkar n…Σn Υ¨f…în.ks…înΥàdri/

3p say.pst-3p job have-inf in=indf agency
They said they had jobs at an agency.

##

195.5: Borland in the FIrst Millennium

questions and commentary from the August 2025 edition of an Insular History scitation [exam] taken by many Borlish school students at the age of fifteen.

βÄΠrulers of the First Roman Empire personally stepped foot on Borland, and what were the contexts of their visits?

Most students correctly named Claudius and Hadrian; almost all cited the former's role in orchestrating the conquests of Britain and Borland and incorporating them into the Roman Empire, and the latter's tour leading to the creation of a Borlish province (ΡΣnsula BorΡ¹lum or occasionally BorΡ¹lia) separate from Britain. Common mistakes included later emperors like Septimius Severus and Constantius Chlorus, who fought in Britain; by the third-century the territory of Borland was much more secure than Britain, having been conquered in its entirely and thus requiring fewer fortifications against incursions. Another mistaken inclusion seen repeatedly was Diocletian, likely due to the curriculum mentioning the creation of the Diocese of Borland (from the former Borlish province) during the administrative reforms of his reign.

4. Describe the process by which the post-Imperial Diocese of Borland became a hereditary monarchy (the First Kingdom of Borland) by the turn of the seventh century.

Answers tended either to parrot the pat narrative of a century ago or, more promisingly, to mirror the state of current scholarship. There are relatively certain historical facts regarding Victor Rossetus (r. ca. 420βÄ™?) that most could recall, being the first ruler of a re-cobbled-together Diocese of Borland after the island was abandoned by the state apparatus of the First Roman Empire around the year 400 N and while the west and south of the island were being settled by German peoples. At the other end, the genealogy of the so-called RosseΟΑ dynasty, first royal house of the First Kingdom of Borland (who claimed descend from Victor Rossetus), only becomes more plausible than semi-mythical around 620 N. The two intermediate centuries are frustratingly ill-documented, but the best student responses managed toβÄΠ

196.1: The Superdraconic Apocalypse

notes in translation on the subject of an event in the geologic record.

L'Apocalyps Surdraconic fornisc nom ag cug d'un sax cisjovial ayent se prodoit cos approximaΟΑ deur segsant seg cent murΟΑon d'annaΟΑ, cos plausibr durant y prinvir norΟΑer.
The Superdraconic Apocalypse refers to the fall of a asteroid from the asteroid belt approximately sixty-six hundred myriad years ago, probably during the northern spring.

Ne sy souc sceye l'extirpaΟßon de tre cart tanc entr toll'orn d'animal, toΟΑ dragon abrangent sauf ig l'ancestr d'aucel sceint.
It was followed by the eradication of roughly three-quarters of all animal species, including all dinosaurs bar the ancestors of modern birds.

Y nom dell'oc cainΟß, oc orΟΑenjaΟΑ ny siecr novoΟß com un dob apponmenter (dezein plusour pre scerra y descovart d'evidence concret dy quinment) por l'extirpaΟßon ny gravatoir reliquyer motivar, jay soumoin a sy zonβÄîtemporal ag fin, e donc chthonic a dessur, dell'Asc Draconic (2.5βÄ™0.66 gran murdon d'annaΟΑ paravant). 
The name of this event, coined in the nineteenth century as a hypothetical (several decades before evidence of the impact was actually discovered) to explain the extinction in the fossil record, refers to its locationβÄîtemporally at the end of, and therefore geologically on top of, the Draconic Age (2.5βÄ™0.66 great myriad years ago).

irruniscabiltaΟΑ /…ΣΥ¨ri.niΥ¨xa.b…ΣlΥàtaΈΗ/ […ΣΥ¨ Äi.n…ΣΥ¨xa.b…ΣlΥàtaΈΗ]
- contradiction, paradox, incongruity, inconsistency, unresolvable conflict between two or more parts of something;
- abnormality, oddity, something unusual;
- irregularity, a violation of bureaucratic rules or procedure especially as evidence of a crime

Alster is a commuter town in Markland around fifteen leagues [30 miles] south of the capital city of Tommarth [Tamworth].

March

...hosted Tuesdaily from the 17th for a course of twelve weeks at the Kit and Toad; single contestants will be admitted to teams for the cost of a round of drinks.

20 FridayβÄ™23 Monday: ceremonies and celebrations for the Ides of Breakfast [Eid al-Fitr]. All townspeople are warmly invited in particular to the regular Masket Ball on 21 Saturday, held in the town's Riverbank Masket [mosque]βÄîthe second-oldest continuously-operating masket in Markland.

25 WednesdayβÄ™26 Thursday, 31 Tuesday: three performances by the Lady Maud Orchestra as part of the Century Tour across all of Albion to mark one hundred years since the orchestra's creation. The programme will include a suite of excerpts from CrΟΓyenschot's opera Ad Pentaculum, as well as a medley of Merch film music through the decades.

April

1 Wednesday: monthly live reading and dramatic enactment at the library. April's story is The Hinterlands, first book in the Kilshaw authors' Samborn Sisters series of fancies by the Kilshaw collective. We recommend this month's event for children aged 3 to 13.

3 Friday: lineball match of our Ash Lads against the Gins, first home game of this year's league and first year in the Second Level. Commemorative red-caps will be free on the door until they run out, and can also be reserved by slate [~smartphone] and quister [phone].

197.2: aΟΑm "treasure"

aΟΑm /ΥàaΟΑ.mΧ©/ [Υàa.ΟΑ…êm]
- treasure, jewel, gem, something extremely valuable;
- darling, dear, sweetheart, a term of endearment for someone one loves;
- lover, mistress, cicisbeo, paramour, beau, an (illicit, unofficial or at least sub-marital) sexual or romantic partner;
- (media) MacGuffin, an object the pursuit of which drives the plot but whose precise identity and qualities are unspecified or irrelevant

Etymology: from Old Borlish aΟΑm "concubine, paramour; (poss.) treasure" (the object sense is contemporaneously unattested, but later use suggests a retention), alteration of presumed *maΟΑm via rebracketing with Old Borlish m(e) "my". It was borrowed from Old English mΡ¹ΟΨm "precious object, jewel". Until the advent of the last sense in the twentieth century, the word had usually been restricted to possessive constructions.

Y duc nos dau l'acconnosc de sell'aΟΑm.

197.3: plouΟßastr "theatre"

plouΟßastr /pluΥàtsas.trΧ©/ [pl äΥàtsa.st…ê]
- pretence, charade, deception, a faked appearance or series of events;
- pantomime, harlequinade, a clowning and fantastical theatrical performance;
- farce, sham, a ridiculous or inane show;
- theatre, lip service, tokenism, window dressing, pointless actions taken entirely for the look of the thing

Etymology: nineteenth century, pejorative derivation from plouΟß "screen, disguise; faΟßade, persona". This is a descendant of Latin pluteus "railing; screen", possibly via Old French. The term was originally used in the playhouse senses to describe a style of theatre imported from Saxony. In the last senses it dates to the 1940s in political criticism.

197.5: Landmarks and Legends

notes on various aspects of the Boralverse alternate history setting which begin with the letter L.

Laurent Burnows (Merch /laΥên ΥàboΥê.n…ôz/, fl. 2002βÄ™) is an author from Markland. He rose to fame in the first decade of the 21st century with Back to Front, a series of children's books about a partsicular school class going on time-travel adventures with their teachers. His first foray into a more mature style was the 2015 trevold [novel] The First Thing That Went Wrong, debuting the series An Incovenient Armageddon; the series proved a great success with the audienceβÄînow teens and adultsβÄîwho had grown up with his children's stories.

The Library of Sobjan (Majoric [Hungarian] Sobiani CΟΗnvtΟΓr) has a claim to the title of Europe's oldest continuously-operating library, having been commissioned by King Jambor of Awry [Hungary] and built during his reign in the twelfth century. The city of Sobjan [PΟ©cs], close to the Crovatian border, was an early Majoric capital and remains to this day a centre for arts and culture. Notably old items found in the Library include a copy of a 1261N liber numerorum (translated to Latin from Arabic works written in the Single Caliphate) incorporating a computed table of arrhumb [cosine] values, as well as a copy of the first folio of the mysterious Mysarnos Codex (whose rediscovery in 1939 proved that the Codex was written in a form of Greek).

Lux ΟÜterna: The First Century of Threshold Force (Willem Dutch Onslackbar Vlay: DrampelstΟΗweth tΟΩdes har RΟ©st Ew) is a 2002 work of documentary history by Cawen ba Dirxen, a scholar at the VaronΟΗw [~VitΟ≥ria, ES] Higher Edifice. It chronicles the theoretical developments necessary for threshold force [nuclear power], as well as the technology's political, economic and social effects over time. The book was commissioned to mark the centenary of the discovery of the atomic nux [nucleus] in 1902 at the city's NΟΩstad Atellier.

198.1: Lexember 2025 - 1: offiΟßar "set"

offiΟßar /Υ¨…î.fiΥàtsar/ [Υ¨…î.f…ΣΥàts…êΥê]
- appoint, nominate, select, elect, to formally name to a particular rank or position of responsibility;
- designate, assign, allocate, to bestow a role or duty upon;
- post, set, emplace, station, deploy, to place at a particular location to perform a particular task

Etymology: late Middle Borlish, a derivation either vernacularly or within Scholastic Latin from noun offiΟß "office, position; duty, responsibility, task", from synonymous officium in Classical Latin. The term was originally most common in military and ceremonial contexts, and has broadened significantly to many technical settings.

Vos val joddy offiΟßar ag costosc dy bauglment.
/v…îz val  £…îΥàdi Υ¨…î.fiΥàtsar …¦j k…îΥàst…îx di bojlΥàm…¦nt/

Kilshaw Collective

notes in translation on the subject of a group of Merch children's authors.

Y communtaΟΑ scrittour a Kilshaw sta un Οßaucr d'autour Markesc toll'ec veunt ne ben jos Kilshaw, un hamblot ny region norΟΑovester de Marclont.
The Kilshaw writers' collective were a group of Merch authors who all lived in or around Kilshaw [Culcheth], a village in the northwestern part of Markland.

Y Οßaucr sta floriscent durant y dezein meg ny siecr veint, e l'es reconnuΟΑ cos principal par lorry ranc plusour editaΟΑ tras lonc de stoir por ivan.
The group was active through the middle decades of the twentieth century, and is remembered mainly for several long-running series of children's books.

Y ranc de Kilshaw aun se caut tradoit vars eΟß doΟßan de lengaΟß, e bempart son denuΟΑ phthongaΟΑ ne form ig inclus sobot ben phasm.
The Kilshaw series have been translated into dozens of languages, and many have been adapted to formats such as sobot [radio] or phasm [television].

Stoir de Kilshaw affustaurn l'art de scavr, narreyanΟß ben mensc faint all'eutaΟΑ eΟΑ a nuton ne literatur adult, racont scaunt ne spaiΟΑoir com masquira, comedy soiΟßal ben trevogt ec heros joun protagoneyant par surcresc.
Stories from Kilshaw made an industry of aping, parodying or homaging trends and fads in adult literature, telling tales in genres like masquira, social comedy or voidtale that happen to feature child protagonists.

Y hamblot es denuΟΑ un mojolon cogmaΟΑer petty por famigl joun ben poll'eΟß envoluΟΑ cas livr, ant un relicquat e feir frequent panelleyaΟΑ de character de Kilshaw.
The village has become a minor tourist destination for families or for the literary-minded, with a museum and regular fetes themed around Kilshaw characters.

##

198.2: Lexember 2025 - 2: entisar "set"

entisar /Υ¨…¦n.tiΥàzar/ [Υ¨…¦n.t…ΣΥàz…êΥê]
- tighten, tauten, to cause to be under tension;
- strain, stress, to apply force to;
- stiffen, starch, to make rigid via some artificial means;
- lay, set, set up, prepare, arm, (of a trap) to make ready to function via some mechanism;
- cock, ready, prime, to bring a spring (etc.) under tension in advance of firing;
- (esp. reflexive) brace, poise, marshal, steel, to steady or prepare (a person, equipment) for sudden or violent action;
- engage, to bring (a mechanism or device) into a state of readiness or interlock

Etymology: Old Borlish, verbal derivation from tis, tes "taut, stretched". This descends from synonymous tΡ™nsus in Latin, past participle of tend≈ç "I stretch; I strive". The senses pertaining to traps and weaponry, first attested in the fourteenth century, derive from the action of arming a trap or crossbow by placing a string under tension. More general mechanical senses are attested from the seventeenth century, along with the reflexive usage.

L'entisau un traccart ny bosc entr dou casn.
/Υ¨l…¦n.tiΥàzo …Σn traΥàkart ni b…îx Υà…¦n.trΧ© du Υàkaz.nΧ©/
[Υ¨l…¦n.tiΥàzo …Σn t Ä…êΥàk…êΥêt ni b…îx Υà…¦n.t…ê du Υàka.z…ên]
3s=tauten-pst indf trap in-df wood between two oak
He set a trap in the woods between two oaks.

198.3: Lexember 2025 - 3: beyollir "set"

beyollir /bj…îΥàl…Σr/ [bj äΥàl…ΣΥê]
- set, insert, fit, embed, to affix into a recess or opening (as of a jewel);
- install, build in, to put into a dedicated position during construction;
- slot into, plug in, to place (an outward facing part) into (an opening which it is designed to fit into)

Etymology: Middle Borlish, derivation via the change-of-state suffix -ir, -isc < Latin -Ρ™sc≈ç "I become _" from noun beyol "socket, cavity; dimple, divot". This appears to be an Old Borlish backformation from short-lived abiolar "to form a cavity, ?to awl", via Medieval Latin albiol≈ç from Classical alveolus "small hollow; trough, basin". The word has been used for forming electrical connections since the late nineteenth century.

VeleΟΑ l'oc smaraut beyollir ne my Οßondr.
/veΥàl…¦ΈΗ l…îk smaΥàrot bj…îΥàlir ne mi Υàts…în.drΧ©/
[v…ΣΥàl…¦ΈΗ l…îg zm…êΥà Äot bj äΥàl…ΣΥê ne mi Υàts…în.d…ê]
will.sbj-2p df=s.px emerald set-inf in 1s.gn pendant
Please set this emerald into my pendant.

198.4: Lexember 2025 - 4: sciΟΑ "set"

sciΟΑ /x…ΣΈΗ/ [Οß…ΣΈΗ]
- known, understood, familiar, recognised, being within one's understanding;
- ordained, determined, having been decided upon by some authority;
- set, fixed, no longer subject to alteration;
- possible, feasible, permissible, being among the options one can or may choose from

Etymology: from Old Borlish sciΟΑ "known, determined", a direct descendant from Latin scΡΪtus "learnt, known, appointed". This was the perfect participle of both sci≈ç "I know" and scΡΪsc≈ç "I learn". The last senses reflect the word's Borlish use as the suppletive past participle of poΟΑir "to be able to", whose past tense, imperfect subjunctive and past participle were replaced with forms from sci≈ç in the Old Borlish period. In early use the "fixed" sense was used principally to refer to certain Christian feast days and canonical hours.

Cain entrquΟ·sc n'hour sciΟΑ par y liesc quic.
/ken Υ¨…¦n.trΧ©.kwaΥàj…Σx nur x…ΣΈΗ par i lj…¦x kw…Σk/
[ken Υ¨…¦n.t…ê.kw…êΥà ù…ΣΟß n äΥê Οß…ΣΈΗ p…êΥêβÄΩ Äi lj…¦Οß kw…Σk]
fall-3p break in=time set by df term active
Breaks are at set times during the workday.

198.5: Lexember 2025 - 5: orisontment "set"

orisontment /Υ¨o.ri.z…întΥàm…¦nt/ [Υ¨o. Ä…Σ.z äntΥàm…¦nt]
- (astronomy) set, the passage of a celestial body below the apparent horizon;
- conclusion, wrap-up, dΟ©nouement, the process of something coming to a close;
- (poetic) old age, dotage, the latter years of life;
- (mathematics) convergence, the property of a sequence or analogous object approaching a particular value

Etymology: Middle Borlish, originally a term of art for the first sense, noun derivation from orisontar "set, pass below the horizon". This comes from orisont "horizon; range, limit, ambit" (since displaced in the first sense by lovacq "horizon", from Arabic), via Latin from Greek αΫ¹œ¹αΩêΧ¹ΈΕœâΈΫ βÄΔ horΟ≠z≈çn "horizon". In mathematics the term is seen from the eighteenth century.

Cain minut alcot prell'orisontment Jovial?
/ken miΥàn…Σt alΥàk…ît pr…¦Υ¨lo.ri.z…întΥàm…¦nt  £oΥàvjal/
[ken m…ΣΥàn…Σt …êΥàg…ît p Ä…ΣΥ¨lo. Ä…Σ.z äntΥàm…¦nt  ù äΥàvjal]
fall-3p minute how.much before-df=set Jovian
How many minutes is it until the set of Jupiter?

===

The Thirteenth Century

collected notes on various features of the thirteenth century.

Wilhelm BrΟΦz (fl. 1225) was mayor of the city of Augsburg for at least a decade in the first half of thirteenth century. He was a personal friend of Paul von Burchadt, the future first Pope of the Augsburg See, and BrΟΦz's personal ambitions for his city are thought to have influenced some of the religious policy outlines in the Pauline Denuncations of 1225. BrΟΦz was portrayed by Hunter Noteworthy in the 1961 Merch-language film With the North Star to Guide Us, a dramatisation of the events of the German Secession. A magnified statue of BrΟΦz (not based on any extant description or depiction of the man, since none exists) was erected in 1975 outside Augsburg's town hall.

Ivan of Cartaw (fl. ca. 1280) was a lord of Cartaw [Cardiff] who, during his reign, undertook the conquest of the Devon peninsula. Devon had become formally independent from the kingdom of Kernow [Brittany] some decades prior, ruled by a cadet branch of the latter's royal house. Ivan of Cartau's conquest appears to have initially been without the assent or support of his liege King David; nonetheless, the king treated the campaign much like Wales's other territorial gains of the thirteenth century, creating the Tallath of Devon and awarding its rule to Ivan's son Marduth (Ivan having died of an illness shortly after the conquest's completion).

On Unknown Areas Scribed by Arcs (written ca. 1211)  is a work by Tal of Murcia. Containing little original work by the author, it is primarily a translation and compilation of earlier advancements in geometry from Cairene mathemticians. It contains the first attestation of the term mozate "two-dimensional, planar" in its Latinate form muzΡ¹tus, borrowed from Arabic ΌÖΌèΊ≥ΌéΊΖΌéΌëΊ≠ βÄΔ (musaαΙ≠αΙ≠aαΗΞ) "flat, even, level".

##

198.6: Lexember 2025 - 6: despondr "set"

despondr /d…¦Υàsp…în.drΧ©/ [d…ΣΥàsp…în.d…ê]
- arrange, organise, set up, to put in useful positions;
- prepare, lay, set, to make ready for use by placing things on or around it in a particular arrangement;
- incline, mind, dispose, prime, to make (someone) more likely to act in a particular fashion;
- have access to, have available, avail oneself, have on hand, to be able to conveniently use;
- employ, have employed, make use of, to have access to the labour or benefits of

Etymology: descending from Old Borlish desponre "distribute, set out" or reborrowed during the Middle Borlish period from its source in Latin disp≈çn≈ç "I arrange". The latter senses, likely influenced by cognates in other Romance languages, are intransitive and used with preposition de "of, from".

Me storra i vin y tabr despondr pre nos pascau.
/me st…îΥàra i v…Σn i Υàtab.rΧ© d…¦Υàsp…în.drΧ© pre n…îz paΥàxo/
[me st äΥà Äa i v…Σn i Υàta.b…ê d…ΣΥàsp…în.d…ê p Äe n…îz p…êΥàxo]
1s.obl need-fut 3s come.sbj df table set-inf before 1p eat-1p.sbj
I need him to come and set the table before we eat.

198.7: Lexember 2025 - 7: ereΟΑmfeyar "set"

ereΟΑmfeyar /eΥ¨r…¦ΟΑ.mΧ©Υàfjar/ […ΣΥ¨ Ä…¦.ΟΑ…êmΥàfj…êΥê]
- synchronise, sync, to cause to occur at the same time;
- set, wind, regulate, to adjust (a timepiece) so that it displays the correct time;
- align, line up, right, coordinate, harmonise, to position in a symmetric arrangement or (more generally) to cause to take a convenient or aesthetically pleasing form;
- regularise, normalise, standardise, to make more consistent or unexceptional; 
- superimpose, overlap, to place one atop another so that particular parts are in alignment

Etymology: Middle Borlish derivative of ereΟΑm "symmetry; coincidence; happenstance" with the factitive suffix -feyar < Latin -fic≈ç "I make _". The noun descends from Old Borlish eredme "alignment, order, regularity", of uncertain origin. The word has traditionally been connected to Latin rhythmus "rhythm, cadence", ignoring the phonetic problems and the lack of cognates in other Romance languages, mostly due to a dearth of other options. Neither a hypothetical Old English *ΡΓer«Θded≈çm "readiness" nor Old Norrish *«Ϊrr«ΪΟΑsemi "straight-line-ness" have been much credited.

J'ereΟΑmfeyau l'orlosc ne traiscn a tre minut lon theu.
/ £eΥ¨r…¦ΟΑ.mΧ©Υàfjo l…îrΥàl…îx ne Υàtrex.nΧ© a tre miΥàn…Σt l…în ΈΗau/
[ ù…ΣΥ¨ Ä…¦.ΟΑ…ê…±Υàfjo l äΥêΥàl…îx ne Υàt Äe.Οß…ên a t Äe m…ΣΥàn…Σt l…în ΈΗau]
1s=set-pst df=watch in lag at three minute without will
I accidentally set the pocketwatch three minutes late.

199.1: Lexember 2025 - 8: hoxar "set"

hoxar /h…îΥàksar/ [h äΥàks…êΥê]
- set, sic, to cause (e.g. an animal) to make an attack or pursuit;
- rile up, aggravate, rabble-rouse, agitate, to cause to become excited or angry especially at some perceived villain or scapegoat

Etymology: early Modern Borlish, allied (along with interjection hox "attack!") with Saxon hoxen, Bavarn huchsen and Carnish huschken, all meaning roughly the same thing. Possibly just a onomatopoetic formation, but the formal similarity of Crovatian hu≈Γkati "provoke" is also suggestive.

Y barvier hoxn lorry cloΟΑour sur y taisson.
/i barΥàvj…¦r h…îk.sn si kloΥàΟΑur s…Σr i teΥàs…în/
[i b…êΥêΥàvj…¦Υê h…îks si kl äΥàΟΑ äΥê s…ΣΥêβÄΩ Äi t…ΣΥàs…în]
df shepherd set 3s.gn terrier on df badger
The shepherds set their terrier on the badger.

Jasper Detchard's Military Career

continued translation from the beginning of [real-life] book The Henchmen of Zenda by KJ Charles, a 2018 pastiche of the 1894 romp The Prisoner of Zenda.

L'oc me convene fort, con ja Larry es petty demay por mell'arveiΟΑr e demastr demay por my gost.
This [my family disposing of me to India via the Army] suited me well, since England is too small for my fancy and too moralistic for my tastes.

Cos infeliΟß, y Centuir galau LarresctaΟΑ cosogr, eΟΑ all'envou d'un stoir stendiscent e scur scandalous abrejar, jo me cof depoussaΟΑ.
Unfortunately, the Army brought Englishness with it, and to cut a long and doubtless shocking story short, I was cashiered.

Jo saleu dy Centuir ant veΟΑrdon nombrous por scrim, un destourm lon mensc, un parnommanΟß de duvar a joy de blad (just dignaΟΑ), un autr por broucanΟß par y fout noc osant se nommar (cos parey), eΟΑ un rekest de my famigl por ig jo reconnoscray plu my parenΟß con lou, dec ΟΑe desiran alcun con mey. 
I left the Army with numerous prizes for swordplay, a dishonourable discharge, a reputation for cheating at cards (fairly earned), another for indulgence in the fuck that dare not speak its name (ditto), and a request from my family that I no longer claim kinship with them, since they wanted none with me.

Jo lour dau l'oΟß obeΟ·scenΟß e me nommau Detchard, lonc y toun yon primer jo favuau un segr, e Jasper, par raΟßon ig ΟΑa y fortun dy viΟΑ an hom es denir un beuscr dy Bel Joy, val aΟΑief y joy luΟ·r toΟΑ vars y nuivr.
I did them that service and named myself Detchard, after the first town where I sucked a man off, and Jasper, because if oneβÄôs lot in life is to be a Victorian villain, one might as well play the game properly.

199.2: Lexember 2025 - 9: phthongar "set"

phthongar /fΈΗ…înΥàgar/ [fΈΗ ä≈΄Υàg…êΥê]
- set, to compose a piece of sung music for (a given text);
- arrange, orchestrate, cover, to create a new version of (a piece of music) with different e.g. instrumentation;
- adapt, translate, to create a work based on (a piece of art, writing, etc) for a different medium;
- reformat, repackage, rebrand, to comprehensively change the structure and/or nature of

Etymology: originally in the first sense and specifically in religious contexts, as of writing choral music whose lyrics were taken from a particular hymn or homily. The term is a borrowing from Scholastic Latin phthong≈ç "I set (to music)", itself from synonymous œÜΈΗΈΩΈ≥Έ≥αΩΕ βÄΔ (phthongαΙ™) in Malchassian Greek. Broadening of the word's senses to other forms of art and writing is seen from the eighteenth century.

Alcun hom nole l'oΟß od phthongar a caum.
/alΥàk…Σn …îm noΥàle l…îts …îd fΈΗ…înΥàgar a kom/
[Υ¨a.g…ΣΥàn…îm n äΥàle l…îts …îd fΈΗ ä≈΄Υàf…êΥêβÄΩa kom]
no person ng.fut-ipf df=s.dt ode set-inf to piece
Nobody was going to set that ode to music.

199.3: Lexember 2025 - 10: ensembr "set"

ensembr /…¦nΥàs…¦m.brΧ©/ […ΣnΥàs…¦m.b…ê]
- band, ensemble, orchestra, group, musicians who perform together;
- set, kit, equipment, apparatus, a collection of instruments of tools collectively employed for some purpose;
- outfit, kit, a set of articles of clothing worn together

Etymology: early modern borrowing in all senses from French ensemble "together; outfit, kit, music group". This descends directly from the Latin adverb ΡΪnsimul "together, simultaneously". The term is particularly used for informal or temporary groupings, as well as games and other entertainment.

Scey un ensembr a scac yonoscon ny tarn.
/xi …Σn …¦nΥàs…¦m.brΧ© a xak Υ¨jo.noΥàx…în ni tarn/
[Οßi …Σn …ΣnΥàs…¦m.b…êβÄΩ Äa xak Υ¨ ùo.n äΥàx…în ni t…êΥên]
there.be indf set at chess wherever in-df pub
There's a chess set somewhere in the pub.

199.4: Lexember 2025 - 11: algebrar "set"

algebrar /Υ¨al. £…¦Υàbrar/ [Υ¨al. ù…ΣΥàb Ä…êΥê]
- set, reduce, to put (a broken or dislocated bone) back into an optimal position for healing;
- restitute, restore, repatriate, to return to a rightful owner;
- resolve, settle, to restore balance or proper standing in (a debt or conflict);
- take back, recover, reconquer, reunify, to reincorporate territory into a state

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed from Scholastic Latin algebr≈ç "I set (a bone)" along with noun algebr < algebra "bone-setting; restitution". This occurs in medical texts from as early as the twelfth century translated from Arabic, a borrowing from ΊßΌ³Ό£Ί§ΌéΊ®Ό£Ί± βÄΔ al-jabr "restoration, compulsion". Financial use is attested from the seventeenth century; political from the late nineteenth (in the run-up to the Millstone War).

Lou stove l'os a cois algebrar toΟΑ pront.
/lu stoΥàve l…îz a k…îiz Υ¨al. £eΥàbrar t…îΈΗ pr…înt/
[lu st äΥàve l…îz a k…îiz Υ¨al. ù…ΣΥàb Ä…êΥê t…î Υàp Ä…înt]
3p.acc need-ipf df=bone at thigh set-inf all immediate
They had to set the thighbone at once.

199.5: Lexember 2025 - 12: valcq "set"

valcq /valk/ [valk]
- bout, attack, fit, a short period of unpleasant suffering or violence;
- stint, spell, stretch, any delimited amount of time dedicated to a particular activity;
- (games) set, a group of rounds of a game (esp. racket sports) which counts as a unit for the team that wins more than half of them

Etymology: eighteenth century, allied with Norman valque, Kentish or Willem walk, Saxon walke, and many others all meaning "bout; (racket sports) set". Use for less unpleasant periods of time is a Borlish innovation dating to the early twentieth century, originally with sarcastic force. The ultimate origin is almost certainly Boham [Czech] wΟΓlca "war", but influence from Kentish verb walken "to move, travel; to occur, take place" is not unlikely.

Tu atten cal compleis a vencr ny valcq sevent?
/ti aΥàt…¦n kal k…îmΥàpliz a Υàv…¦n.krΧ© ni valk seΥàv…¦nt/
[ti …êΥàt…¦n kal k ämΥàpliz a Υàv…¦≈΄.k…ê ni valk s…ΣΥàv…¦nt]
2s await what player to win-inf in-df bout next
Which player do you think will win the next set?

===

Unresolved Linguistic History

excerpt in translation from 2004 article A New History of Rust (originally in Borlish as Un Histoir Novel de Rougl), an academic overview of the linguistic history of sub-Roman Borland (approximately, the time after Latin ceased to be regularly written on Borland outside monasteries and before the first surviving longform vernacular texts).

βÄΠsummary, the chronology of the following changes from Vulgar Latin to Old Borlish: (1) /au/ to /o/, as in Latin causa and Old Borlish cos "matter, thing"; (2) /alC/ to /auC/, as in Latin alter and Old Borlish autre "other"; (3) /al[…ô]C/ > /alC/, as in Latin calidus and Old Borlish calt "hot", is irreducibly blurry on the margins. Even if in general these are supposed to have taken place in this order, re-classicisations like taur "bull" (earlier tor) from Latin taurus and mere exceptions like unproductive adjectival suffix -autr from Latin -aliter must be accounted for.

We turn to the question on the early Old Borlish lexicon about which perhaps the most scholarship has been written. Since the Merch linguist Buthick Morstan first suggested the existence of an Insular Latin word *urum "man" in 1909, data supporting her theory has continued to trickle in, while never becoming overwhelmingly convincing. Moreover, even if the majority of experts accept the existence of this word, the field is split on whether it is simply a phonetic variant of Classical Latin virum "man", or else a borrowing from Borland Kelt vurum, urum "man" (compare Brythonic guur, whence Welsh gwr "man").

Morstan was aware of potential surviving traces of this term, such as dialect terms like orrel "penis" and orjan "scarecrow", as well as personal names like Orrin, but she was writing before the discovery of Roman-eraβÄΠ

##

199.6: Lexember 2025 - 13: tannir "set"

tannir /taΥàn…Σr/ [t…êΥàn…ΣΥê]
- tan, to change animal hide into leather through soaking in tannic acid;
- fire, bake, kiln, to treat (e.g. pottery) with heat;
- cure, pickle, preserve, to alter (e.g. meat) by chemical treat for preservation or use;
- set, fix, mordant, to make (e.g. dye) fast or permanent;
- vulcanise, to treat (rubber) with sulfur to make it harder and more durable

Etymology: Middle Borlish, verbal derviation from the noun tan "oak bark" (from which tannic acid is obtained). This is a borrowing from synonymous tan in Old French, ultimately from Gaulish tannos "green oak". Extension to various other procedure for making materials harder or more durable is seen through the early modern period and into the twentieth century.

Gent tanniscen gles par mejan d'yedic.
/ £…¦nt Υ¨ta.niΥàx…¦n gl…¦z par meΥà £an djeΥàd…Σk/
[ ù…¦nt Υ¨ta.n…ΣΥàΟß…¦n gl…¦z p…êΥê m…ΣΥà ùan dj…ΣΥàd…Σk]
people set-ipf.3p dye by means of=vinegar
People used to set dyes using vinegar.

199.7: Lexember 2025 - 14: hieratic "set"

hieratic /Υ¨hje.raΥàt…Σk/ [Υ¨hje. Ä…êΥàt…Σk]
- priestly, sacerdotal, pertaining to priests or analogous religious figures;
- (specifically) hieratic, pertaining to pharaonic priests in Ancient Egypt or to their cursive system of writing;
- impassive, phlegmatic, unemotional, not having or revealing any sentiment;
- stern, set, severe, unyielding, (of expression or countenance) fixed or rigid without any leniency

Etymology: borrowed directly via Scholastic Latin from Greek αΦ±ΈΒœ¹Έ±œ³ΈΙΈΚœ¨œ² βÄΔ hieratikΟ≥s "priestly", from the root αΩêΧîΈΒœ¹œ¨œ² βÄΔ hΡ≠erΟ≥s "sacred". In extended use from the seventeenth century, in parallel with other languages like Vascon, presumably in reference to the stereotypical manner and bearing of priests.

Nos entisaum gas, l'aye min hieratic eΟΑ intradugbr.
/n…îz Υ¨…¦n.tiΥàzom gaz | l…¦Υàje m…Σn Υ¨hje.raΥàt…Σk …¦ΈΗ Υ¨…Σn.traΥàdajbrΧ©/
[n…îz Υ¨…¦n.t…Σzom gaz | l…ΣΥà ùe m…Σn Υ¨hje. Ä…êΥàt…Σk …¦βÄΩΥ¨ΟΑ…Σn.t Ä…êΥàdaj.b…ê]
1p tighten-pst.1p gaze | 3s=have-ipf mien set and untranslatable

200.1: Lexember 2025 - 15: scenoir "set"

scenoir /xeΥàn…îjr/ [Οß…ΣΥàn…î. ù…ê]
- set, film set, the location where e.g. a movie's scenes are arranged to be filmed;
- studio, a place more generally where things such as TV segments are filmed;
- backdrop, framing, the way in which a situation is given context;
- setup, circumstances, the configuration or arrangement of elements or actors in an (e.g. social, political, military) context

Etymology: twentieth century, derivative of scenar "stage (a play), film (a movie)" with the place-forming suffix -oir < -≈çrium in Latin. The verb scenar itself comes from scen "stage, theatre", a borrowing from Latin scaena "stage, scene". In metaphorical use the term is seen (first in media criticism) from the 1960s.

L'actour fey exeunt voud dy scenoir.
/lakΥàtur fi …¦kΥàsaunt vud di xeΥàn…îjr/
[l…êkΥàt äΥê fi …ΣkΥàsaunt vud di Οß…ΣΥàn…î.j…ê]
df=actor do.pst exit mad from-df set
The actor stormed out of the studio.

Run!

excerpt in translation from 1988 work Run! (originally in Willem Dutch as Holt!), a future-set parachthon trevold [SFF novel] about a grey-market courier.

ToΟΑ sfondr nell'issenΟß pres incommorant. Toxen enΟßn a hurlar pre j'yem a plaΟß meg lonc y galleir de mantien.
The exit goes wrong almost immediately. Alarms start blaring before I'm halfway down the maintenance corridor.

Lumner ne roug eΟΑ endig faun pousson tras y pareΟΑ. Pas de skot rongn lontan, joddy rajognn des asmuth monfeyaΟΑ.
Red and purple lights strobe across the walls. Boots thunder in the distance, guards converging from multiple directions.

"Dinnocq," jo teΟΑey ne my torq. "Jo's parceut. Y galleir n'aust convendra plu."
"Dinnock," I hiss into my torc. "I'm blown. The east corridor won't work."

Sy voΟß se tramet hurislant ne mell'oregl, kieΟΑ e stabr. "Jo's te dant un rout autr. Torn ag scert primer, soulor scan par y scagl d'urgenΟß. Jo me mou vars y posson de poiΟΑanΟß dovem."
His voice crackles into my ear, calm and steady. "I have an alternate route for you. Take the next left, then up through the emergency stairwell. I'm moving to the secondary extraction point."

Jo sief casc sy commant, y graivet usn blasant lorry Οßut sdroyaΟΑ tandic jo ruΟΑ pall'immoubr.
I follow his instructions, the greaves burning through power reserves as I sprint through the building.

Jo smarc sur y fastig just exact candon y preu joddy parvienn ag los a dou taug meyon dessou.
I emerge onto the roof just as the first guards reach the flight two floors below me.

##

200.2: Lexember 2025 - 16: steΟΑig "set"

steΟΑig /steΥàΟΑaj/ [st…ΣΥàΟΑaj]
- obstinate, stubborn, resolute, convinced of one's rightness and resistant to correction;
- determined, bent, stuck, steadfast, firmly intending to stick to a particular course of action or point of view;
- set, (of a feeling or belief) fixed and not liable to change

Etymology: descendant of late Old Borlish steΟΑi»ù "stable, constant", borrowed from an early Middle English or Middle Dutch derivative of a cognate of stead "place, position". Initially describing strong convictions, the word has been extended to describe people with such convictions since the seventeenth century.

Sy morrig es steΟΑig sad cas theu.
/si m…îΥàraj …¦z steΥàΟΑaj sad kaz ΈΗau/
[si m äΥà Äaj …Σz st…ΣΥàΟΑaj sad kaz ΈΗau]
3s.gn aunt be set very home.of MO
Her aunt's very set in her ways.

200.3: Lexember 2025 - 17: govey "set"

govey /goΥàvi/ [g äΥàvi]
- brood, covey, a group of game birds such as pheasants or grouse;
- clutch, a group of eggs in a nest;
- litter, a group of newborn animals or (loosely or jocularly) children;
- set, society, circle, acquaintance, a group of persons associated by status or interest (often with mildly deprecatory force)

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed from contemporary Kentish or Norman covey, govee "brood". This is a substantivisation of the past participle of cover "to brood (an egg)", the regular descendant of Latin cub≈ç "I lie down; I incubate" (from whose present tense Borlish cair "to fall" takes its suppletive past tense forms). Extension from venery to other groups of animals is seen from the seventeenth century, and to groups of people from the nineteenth.

Nossy dottr es melle faint entr y govey galant.
/n…îΥàsi Υàd…ît.rΧ© …¦z m…¦Υàle fent Υà…¦n.trΧ© i goΥàvi gaΥàlant/
[Υ¨n…î.si Υàd…î.t…êβÄΩ Ä…Σz m…ΣΥàle fent Υà…¦n.t Äi g äΥàvi g…êΥàlant]
1p.gn daughter be ton do-p.prs among df set chivalrous
Our daughter's hobnobbing with the smart set.

200.4: Lexember 2025 - 18: hundy "set"

hundy /h…ΣnΥàdi/ [h…ΣnΥàdi]
- hotel room, suite, a room or collection of connected rooms in a lodginghouse;
- chambers, apartment, a distinguished group of private rooms in a larger building used as an office or a residence;
- (university) set, lodgings, a student or faculty residence with a separate bedroom (as opposed to cubicr "bedsit, studio")

Etymology: nineteenth century, borrowed from Welsh hundy "bedchamber; (by extension) hotel room". This is a compound of hun "sleep" and ty "house". Initially confined to upmarket use, the word was extended to the university setting around the turn of the twentieth century. Compare roughly contemporaneous randy "apartment" < Welsh rhandy "wing (of a building); apartment".

J'enveyau sy cigl risc aye un hundy a catr roum.
/ £…¦nΥàvjo si tsajl r…Σx …¦Υàje …Σn h…ΣnΥàdi a Υàkat.rΧ© Υàrum/
[ ù…Σ…±Υàvjo si tsail  Ä…ΣΟß …ΣΥà ùe …Σn h…ΣnΥàdi a Υàka.t…êβÄΩΥà Äum]
1s=envy-pst 3s.gn SO rich have-ipf indf set at four room
I envied his rich boyfriend's four-room set.

200.5: Lexember 2025 - 19: quaglar "set"

quaglar /kw…¦jΥàlar/ [kw…ΣjΥàl…êΥê]
- curdle, turn, (of e.g. milk) to form curds and no longer flow smoothly;
- clot, coagulate, to change from a liquid to a solid mass;
- set, solidify, stiffen, to become firm or solid in consistency;
- (slang, dated) freeze, be very cold, to be experiencing notably and inconveniently low temperatures

Etymology: direct descendant of Latin coΡ¹gul≈ç "I curdle, coagulate", reinforced in the Middle Borlish period by contemporary Portingale or Leon quallo "curds, cottage cheese". The last sense is attested only from the mid-twentieth century and is already rather old-fashioned.

AttenaΟΑ y zachet vel quaglar oΟß noit.
/Υ¨a.teΥànaΈΗ i zaΥàk…¦t v…¦l kw…¦jΥàlar …îts n…îit/
[Υ¨a.t…ΣΥànaΈΗ i z…êΥàk…¦t v…¦l kw…ΣjΥàl…êΥêβÄΩ Ä…îs n…îit]
wait-2p.sbj df glue will.sbj set-inf s.dt night
Wait for the glue to set overnight.

===

Ruritania

collected notes on the small Central European polity of Ruritania.

Ruritania (Borlish Rurtagn, Bavarn RΟΦrzen, Boham Ruravsko) is a minor polity and one of the easternmost European states which primarily speak some variety of German. It is located to the east of Bavarn [Bavaria], to the south of Bohamy [Bohemia] and to the north of Crain [~Slovenia, southern Austria]. Its capital city is Strelsau, located on the banks of the Danaw [Danube].

Toponymy

The traditional account of the origin of the name Ruritania, as a placename derivative of Latin r≈Ϊs, r≈Ϊris "countryside", runs into chronological difficulties. Roman control of the region as the province of Noricum shows no evidence of this toponym, and the earliest attestations of the name in the eleventh century do not appear to be Classically inspired (although the modern form <Ruritania> is almost certainly a folk-etymological classicisation based on this account).

In general, linguists expect that there is a Germanic or Slavic origin for the Rur- root. The Rugii, a Germanic tribe who settled the area in the sub-Roman period, are a tempting proposition, although their short-lived Rugiland cannot explain the second -r- in the name. It is also possible that one of the Danaw's tributaries in the region once had a name like Rur (compare the Rure and the Rudder to the northwest), which lent its name to a valley and eventually a state. Multiple Slavic etyma such as rogozi "reeds, rushes" have also been proposed.

The capital is more tractable, being almost certainly a compound of a Slavic root strΡ™la "arrow" and the Germanic placename suffix -au "meadow, floodplain" (the -s- likely representing a Germanic genitive), thought to be in reference to the straightness of the Danaw in the vicinity of Strelsau.

##

200.6: Lexember 2025 - 20: tas "set"

tas /taz/ [taz]
- bag, satchel, waterskin, a smallish cloth or leather container carried on one's person;
- pouch, pocket, a small and soft compartment for carrying things (also the natural pouch of a marsupial);
- vessel, container, any means of carrying anything (in full generality);
- saucer, bowl, a small and shallow piece of crockery;
- divot, hollow, a recess in a surface for holding small items;
- (mathematics) set, the abstract notion of a structure of which things may or may not be elements

Etymology: the conflation in early Modern Borlish of two words with unrelated etyma. Partially a borrowing from Dutch tas, tasch "bag, pouch", which descends straightforwardly from Classical German tascon "purse, pocket". Partially via French from Mostara tasa "bowl, vessel", a borrowing from Arabic ΊΖΌéΊßΊ≥ βÄΔ αΙ≠Ρ¹s "cup". In the mathematical sense from 1831, in translation from DΟΦmmeyer's 1827 work Sammel und Sendungen "Sets and Functions".

Apar cogleu nos y tas de v ne V yon v.m=x.
/aΥàpar k…îjΥàlaw n…îz i taz de ve ne ve j…în ve d…ît …¦m val …¦ks/
[…êΥàp…êΥê k äjΥàlaw n…îz i taz de ve ne ve  ù…în ve d…ît …¦m val …¦ks]
through gather-1p 1p df set of v in V where v.m worth x
Thus we get the set of w in W with m(w)=x.

200.7: Lexember 2025 - 21: assignar "set"

assignar /Υ¨a.sajΥànar/ [Υ¨a.s…êiΥàn…êΥê]
- task, assign, set, to give as a piece of work to do;
- deal, deal out, hand out, give out, distribute, to share items among a number of people;
- (law) summon, subpoena, to require to appear in court;
- (slang) hook up with, tryst, to meet and have (often illicit) sex with

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed from Latin assign≈ç "I assign, distribute". The legal sense appears to be a clipping of legal Latin brevem assign≈ç "I give out a writ". In the last sense the word is a backformation from earlier assignaΟßon "tryst", borrowed in the early modern period from French.

Stou dou trevold assignar all'ec alumn.
/stu du treΥàv…îld Υ¨a.sajΥànar aΥàl…¦k aΥàl…Σm.nΧ©/
[stu du t Ä…ΣΥàv…îld Υ¨a.s…êiΥàn…êΥêβÄΩ Ä…êΥàl…¦g …êΥàl…Σ.m…ên]
must two novel set-inf to-df=p.px pupil
You must set this class two novels.

201.1: Lexember 2025 - 22: niveu "set"

niveu /niΥàvaw/ [n…ΣΥàvaw]
- set, track, stream, a subdivision of students formed into a subject class on the basis of aptitude;
- (by extension) tier, echelon, rank, position in an administrative or military hierarchy

Etymology: late 19th century, borrowed from Roun French nivew "level, tier" during educational reforms in the Drengot Collusion (of which Borland was a part). This was itself borrowed from Parisian, descending after dissimilation and a gender change from Latin lΡΪbella "level (builder's tool)". Extension out of the school setting is seen from the mid-20th century.

Certan dy niveu catr son y scaunΟß de melodram parsiscent.
/ts…¦rΥàtan di niΥàvaw Υàkat.rΧ© s…în i xonts de Υ¨me.l…îΥàd Äam Υ¨par.ziΥàx…¦nt/
[ts…ΣΥêΥàtan di n…ΣΥàvaw Υàka.t…ê s…în i xonts de Υ¨me.l äΥàd Äam Υ¨p…êΥê.z…ΣΥàΟß…¦nt]
some of-df set four be.3p df writing of musical study-p.prs
Some in the fourth set are learning musical scriptwriting.

Ricardo IvarrachezurΟ≠

notes in translation on a nineteenth-century agricultural scientist.

Ricardo IvarrachezurΟ≠ sta un theorist ΟΑaral de Vascony, oc combat faint com mozardist durant y Var Theudesc Dovem.
Ricardo IvarrachezurΟ≠ was a farm theorist from Vascony, who fought as a mozardist during the Second German War.

IvarrachezurΟ≠ surcay y preu theorist a parcevir ig cuivr sceus sperm occeir, remarc scaunt d'an 1824 ig "y semn dy taur, dy veΟΑ ben y stoΟΑr toΟΑ parey dienn impossent par mejan de lou vardir tras pieΟß hour n'un spuit ne cuivr".
IvarrachezurΟ≠ was the first theorist to record the ability of copper to kill sperm, noting in 1824 that βÄ€the seed of the bull, the ram and the stallion alike are made impotent in storing them for some hours in a syringe of copperβÄù.

I connoy ig l'oc concorda ag mechanism comoscon cuivr coΟ·sce contamen.
He recognised that this was the same mechanism by which copper prevented infections.

Degnant ig l'oc natur poΟßonnant de cuivr vole alcant aposc benoΟß sarvent yembr ne contrgentour, IvarrachezurΟ≠ le traita com problem por saΟΑour attenent a piver pollenir cos artifiΟßal.
Although this sterilising property of copper would later be productively used in contraceptives, IvarrachezurΟ≠ treated it as a problem for farmers attempting to artificially breed livestock.

201.2: Lexember 2025 - 23: jahesment "set"

jahesment /Υ¨ £a.h…¦Υàzm…¦nt/ [Υ¨ ùa.h…ΣΥàzm…¦nt]
- device, contraption, gadget, gizmo, a clever or complicated otherwise-unspecified machine;
- set, receiver, transmitter, a piece of electronic equipment which participates in signalling (as of telegraphy, telephony or television);

Etymology: borrowed from Vascon jahesment "contraption", a derivative of jahΟ®s "harness, rig". This in turn was borrowed from Arabic Ί§ΌéΌ΅ΌéΊßΊ≤ βÄΔ jahΡ¹z "gear, utensil", from a root meaning "to prepare". Specialisation to devices such as telephone or radio sets was first applied to steeplepost equipment around the turn of the 19th century.

Ty jahesment es angostessem sur y mien.
/ti Υ¨ £a.h…¦Υàzm…¦nt …¦z Υ¨an.g…îΥàst…¦.s…¦m s…Σr i mj…¦n/
[ti Υ¨ ùa.h…ΣΥàzm…¦nt …Σz Υ¨a≈΄.g äΥàst…¦.s…êm s…ΣΥêβÄΩ Äi mj…¦n]

201.3: Lexember 2025 - 24: jagatteyar "set"

jagatteyar /Υ¨ £a.gaΥàtjar/ [Υ¨ ùa.g…êΥàtj…êΥê]
- set, to place the action of (a fictional or imaginative work) in a particular setting;
- situate, contextualise, to provide surrounding details for;
- (intransitive) worldbuild, to develop a fictional setting

Etymology: early 20th century, verbal derivation from jagat "setting, world, continuity". This comes via Vascon from Gurjat ύΣ€ύΣ½ύΣΛ βÄΔ jagat "world, universe", used as a term of art for "setting" in literary criticism. In the last sense the word enters general use only around the turn of the millennium.

Y lasc es jagatteyaΟΑ nell'Anarchy Portingal.

201.4: Lexember 2025 - 25: Suthocq "Set"

Suthocq /siΥàΈΗ…îk/ [s…ΣΥàΈΗ…îk]
- Set, Seth, Sutekh, an Egyptian deity with dominion over the desert and foreigners;
- Puck, Robin Goodfellow, a recurring trickster figure in various folk/fairy tales;
- an avatar of Christmas jollity in various parts of northern Europe and Mendeva, representing defiance, glittering frost and brightness in the dark

In the last sense, compare Har Suthock /haΥê Υàs ä.ΟΑ äk/ "(lit.) frost Set" in Merch, and Nacho Suthoc "(lit.) little-brother Set" in Ambrosian Vask [Basque].

Etymology: 18th century, originally in translation from Arabic Ί≥ΌèΊΣΌèΊ° βÄΔ Sutuch "Set". This in turn comes from the very new (and still somewhat inaccurate) decipherment of hieroglyphs, leading to a surge in interest in Egyptian mythology, reflecting the name now usually transcribed as /suΥê.tiΟß/ (whence Coptic β≤Ξβ≤èβ≤ß /seΥêt/).

The transfer of the name from Egyptian mythology to general folktales is the fault of Dutch writer Ton CrΟΓyenschot, who in the early 19th century repurposed the names of deities of various traditions for the supernatural characters in his short stories. The puckish figure of Har Suttock spread into the culture at large far more successfully than most of his inventions, and had become associated with winter festivals in general and Christmas in particular by the turn of the 20th century.

Y Grec identfeyan Suthocq e Typhon.
/i gr…¦k Υ¨i.d…¦ntΥàfjan s…ΣΥàΈΗ…îk e tiΥàf…în/
[i g Ä…¦k Υ¨i.d…ΣnfΥàfjan s…ΣΥàΈΗ…îk e t…ΣΥàf…în]
df Greek equate-ipf-3p Set and Typhon
The Greeks conflated Set and Typhon.

##

201.5: Lexember 2025 - 26: bot "set"

bot /b…ît/ [b…ît]
- sheaf, bundle, bunch, a group of thin objects held together by wrapping or tying;
- bale, a quantity of hay (straw, etc) compressed and tied up for transport;
- (attributive) set, package, (of e.g. a menu or something else bought) comprising several items bundled into a single purchase

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed from contemporary Dutch bote "bundle of flax; tuft". This is likely a derivation from the verb bΟ≥ten "beat", a descendant of Classical German baut≈ç "I beat, knock, push". In attributive use the word is seen from the late nineteenth century, originally in hotels as a calque from Welsh scub seggo "set menu, (lit.) bundle of dishes".

Y desc bot teyen boncoppessem sur ig l'aus hougr.
/i d…¦x b…ît tj…¦n Υ¨b…în.k…îΥàp…¦.s…¦m s…Σr aj loz huj.rΧ©/
[i d…¦Οß b…ît tj…¦n Υ¨b…î≈΄.k äΥàp…¦.s…êm s…ΣΥêβÄΩ Äaj loz hu.j…ê]
df dish set cost-ipf-3p cheap-cmp on cmp 3s=have.sbj.ipf fear
The set menu was cheaper than she'd feared.

False Friends (cont. 4)

some more completely arbitrary dictionary entries from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (fifth edition), a reference work published in 1961 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

glade [/gl…¦Υêd/ "sunbeam"] n. a ray, a columnar shaft of sunlight as cast through clouds or canopy; further, a moment of optimism during bleakness or of insight during confusion; a phenomenon of light in the sky near the poles due to the interaction of solar dust with air (also aurorick glade). 15C, early Merch glade, glode "ray of sunlight; sunny place", poss. < unknown Old English gl«Θd~glΡ¹d "brightness" related to known glΟΠd "shining, bright". Emotional uses are seen 17C; celestial use from 18C.

jackareigh [/Υàd £a.k…ô.…Ψ…¦ É/ "alligator"] n. either of two large swimming reptiles related to the cockadrills [crocodiles] and native to the Novomund and the Chinas. 17C, from Kentish [-/ri/], via Welsh or Willem Dutch from Yengatow [Tupi] yacarΟ© "jack-a-reigh". Final element adapted to reigh "skate [ray]", partly from Old English reohhe "skate" and partly from Old French raie "skate" (the two are probably related).

toward [/ΥàtuΥê.…ôd/ "future"] adj. still to come, not yet existing or taking place; of a period of time, next or impending; n. times yet to come, as opposed to now or to times which have already passed. Direct descendant of Old English t≈çweard adj. "still to come", stressed initially in opposition to homographic t≈çweard prp. "in the direction of" (modern Merch toward [/twaΥêd/] "facing").

yuckle [/Υàj ä.k…ôl/ "mend"] v. to restore to a state of correct or normal operation; to make whole as by the process of healing; to reattach separated pieces of something broken. 14C early Merch yokelen "reattach", frequentative or diminutive of yoken "yoke, harness", from Old English gegeocian "yoke". Probably unrelated to Old English gΡ™ocian "preserve, rescue, save".

201.6: Lexember 2025 - 27: ormal "set"

ormal /…îrΥàmal/ [ äΥêΥàmal]
- quiver, quiverful, a container for arrows (bolts, darts, etc) or the contents of such a container;
- (obsolete, vulgar) vagina, the sex organ connecting the vulva and the cervix;
- salvo, volley, an instance of concentrated fire from artillery;
- programme, set, the scheduled list of pieces of music that are to be played at a concert (or a similar timeline of discrete events)

Etymology: an Old Borlish borrowing from Old Norrish «Ϊrmalr "quiver". This is a compound of «Ϊr "arrow" and malr "case"; the former is from Classical German arhu≈ç "arrow", while the latter is likely allied with malh≈ç "leather bag" (although the change of declension is unexplained). The vulgar sense was current in the Middle Borlish period and enjoyed a brief resurgence in the 19th century due to conscious use in historical novels. The musical senses are attested from the 1920s, originally in reference to recorded music played over the radio.

L'ormal dy baΟΑrment fey commet par vrolliscour.
/l…îrΥàmal di Υ¨baΟΑ.rΧ©Υàm…¦nt fi k…îΥàm…¦t par Υ¨vr…î.liΥàxur/
[l äΥêΥàmal di Υ¨ba.ΟΑ…êΥàm…¦nt fi k äΥàm…¦t p…êΥê Υ¨v Ä…î.l…ΣΥàx äΥê]
df=set of-df orchestra do.pst start by crowdpleaser
The orchestra started the set with a crowdpleaser.

201.7: Lexember 2025 - 28: uscar "set"

uscar /iΥàxar/ […ΣΥàx…êΥê]
- usher, lead, to accompany someone (as from the entrance of a building to the approprioate place inside);
- direct, guide, to explain how to get to a particular location;
- point, face, orient, to cause to be turned in given direction;
- (computing) set, assign, to give a particular value

Etymology: early modern Borlish, a backformation from earlier uscer "doorman, porter". This is a borrowinng of Kentish usher "doorman", via synonymous ussier in Old French and ultimately ≈çstiΡ¹rius in Latin. This etymon is an agentive formation from ≈çstium "door". The word was first used for changing the orientation of inanimate objects in the 19th century, and in the computing sense in theoretical work during the 1910s.

Vos ignoist y mojol uscar ag contaΟΑ de settoΟß.
/v…îz ajΥàn…îjst i moΥà £…îl iΥàxar …¦j k…înΥàtaΈΗ de s…¦Υàt…îts/
[v…îz …êiΥàn…îist i m äΥà ù…îl …ΣΥàx…êΥêβÄΩ Ä…¦i k änΥàtaΈΗ de s…ΣΥàt…îts]

You neglected to set the variable to seventeen.

202.1: Lexember 2025 - 29: dos "set"

dos /d…îz/ [d…îz]
- dose, a prescribed amount of a medicine; 
- portion, amount, quantity, measure, any quota of a substance administered for some purpose;
- exercise, drill, an activity done as practice or training;
- set, a fixed number of repetitions of a particular exercise performed as a unit

Etymology: originally in the first sense, a semi-learned borrowing from Late Latin dosis "dose", itself from Greek Έ¥œ¨œÉΈΙœ² βÄΔ dΟ≥sis "gift, portion; dose". The word's extension to other measured quantities is seen in the Middle Borlish period. The latter senses originate in the military slang of the late nineteeth century, spreading into civilian use amid body-building fads during the twentieth.

Jo save noc ou poΟΑir un dos may de gouth complir.

Andalus

Notes in translation on the early history of the polity of Andalus.

Andalus es un polity ag souΟΑ dy peninsol Spagnart. Y lengaΟß d'Arabic Morrou e de Spagnart Mostaral se paroln y dou cos laΟΑ ny region.
Andalus is a polity in the south of the Spanish peninsula. The languages of Morrove Arabic and Mostara Spanish are both widely spoken in the region.

N'Andalus dy siecr nouf sceye y floriscment de fanglanΟß theoretic. Cos particuler, machinaΟßon por veΟΑry produgr acceleran cos veloΟß dec l'yeul den des l'aust s'istra, oc menant all'enascment lavaΟß de "veΟΑr zajadau" noncupaΟΑ.
Ninth-century Andalus saw the flowering of theoretic advancements. In particular, glassmaking techniques accelerated rapidly due to a cut-off of supply from the east, leading to the prodigious export of so-called "jackdaw glass".

L'orn religious dy Brethin respane ny zon de pouvlanΟß chrestan majortaΟΑer n'Andalus des y norΟΑ durant y siecr onΟß e doΟß.
The Brethin religious order spread into the Christian-majority areas of Andalus from the north during the eleventh and twelfth centuries.

Nell'oc epoc atorn Andalus parde un sunt signfeyant d'eiΟΑel ag norΟΑ e l'aust ne man de polity chrestan, tal com y Reyaum de Barcelon nou stabliΟΑ.
Around this time Andalus lost significant territory in the north and east to Christian polities, such as the newly-established Kingdom of Barcelon.

202.2: Lexember 2025 - 30: rancheyar "set"

rancheyar /ranΥàkjar/ [ Ä…ê≈΄Υàkj…êΥê]
- array, lay out, to put into an orderly arrangement (especially to form into rows);
- deploy, marshal, to cause (e.g. troops) to be in some particular formation;
- serialise, order, sequence, to cause to be in the form of an ordered list spread out over time or space;
- set, arrange, to organise (type) for printing a page

Etymology: Middle Borlish, verbal derivative of ranc "row, ordered line; rank, caste". This was borrowed from Old French rang, ranc "position", itself from a descendant either of Classical German rhingas "ring, circle" or of rancas "straight, upright". Extension from the verb to senses involving arrangements other than into literal rows is seen in the early modern period; in the printing sense it appears in the sixteenth century.

I trovau launt cant i rancheya y fougl.
/i troΥàvo lont kant i ranΥàkja i fujl/
[i t Ä äΥàvo lont kant i  Ä…ê≈΄Υàkja i fw…Σl]
3s find-pst error when 3s set-ipf df page
He found typos while he set the pages.

##

202.3: Lexmber 2025 - 31: se corruΟΑ "set"

se corruΟΑ /se k…îΥàr…ΣΈΗ/ [se k äΥà Ä…ΣΈΗ]
- sitting pretty, made in the shade, set for life, living on easy street, having a comfortable or certain supply of money or resources;
- made, set, happy, having removed any obstacles and anticipating smooth sailing from here on in;
- in the clear, home free, out of the woods, past the hard slog and properly equipped for future challenges;
- bulletproof, watertight, on solid ground, having made oneself completely secure;
- ready, prepared, good to go, locked and loaded, shipshape, having sorted out any issues and fully able to proceed

Etymology: past participle of se corrir "make it, survive, get through, succeed", the reflexive of corrir "run". This descends directly from Latin curr≈ç "I run". Idiomatic use of this phrase is seen from the fifteenth century poetry of Manuel Kigman. The phrase appears to have originally referred to simply being well-off or comfortable; semantic association with the verb has more recently given the idiom connotations of past struggle and present battle-readiness.

Posc resoum nos y fastig, nos arreu se corruΟΑ contrell'yarn.
/p…îx reΥàzum n…îz i faΥàstaj | n…îz aΥàraw se k…îΥàr…ΣΈΗ Υ¨k…în.tr…¦Υàljarn/
[p…îx  Ä…ΣΥàzum n…îz i f…êΥàstai | n…îz …êΥà Äaw se k äΥà Ä…ΣΈΗ Υ¨k…în.t Ä…ΣΥàlj…êΥên]
after fix-1p 1p df roof | 1p have-fut {set} against-df=winter
Once we fix the roof, we'll be set for winter.
