202.4: mornΟß a morn "piece of the action"

mornΟß a morn /Υàm…îr.nΧ©ts a m…îrn/ [Υàm…îΥêns a m…îΥên]
- piece of the action, foot in the door, an opportunity to take part in some activity;
- percentage, cut, commission, share, a portion of the monetary (or otherwise) gains produced by some venture

Etymology: idiom from the early modern period, literally meaning "bite at a carrot". The phrase was coined by the poet Bautrmeu as the title of her 1654 compendium of works. The word mornΟß "bite" is a noun from verb mordr "bite", which descends from Latin morde≈ç "I bite"; the word morn "carrot" was borrowed in the Old Borlish period from Old English moran, plural of more "carrot, parsnip".

Casc toun ajos son un mornΟß a morn flagitant.
/kax tun aΥà £…îz s…în …Σn Υàm…îr.nΧ©ts a m…îrn Υ¨fla. £iΥàtant/
[kax tun …êΥà ù…îz s…în …Σm m…îΥêns a m…îΥên Υ¨fla. ù…ΣΥàtant]
each town nearby be.3p indf bite at carrot demand-p.prs
Each suburb is demanding a piece of the action.

##

202.5: Braxoch beet

excerpt from a page from the distributed library on the Markland vicine mesh, about the braxoch beet.

βÄΠvariously also named golden beet, gilt beet, manegold, sweetbeet, is a variety of beet (plant β€Ψ2.1.17.1) which is characterised by the golden flesh and high sugar content of its root. Cultivation of braxoch beet is responsible for a significant fraction of sugar production in many temperate polities: as of 2025 it originated over four parts in five of sugar consumed everywhere on Albion. 

The name braxoch first enters the Merch language in the 1740s, via Borlish and Polish from Cassuvian braxoch "fine beet", an augmentative of brac "beet", which reflects Medieval Latin borΡ¹g≈ç "borage" (further origin disputed).

History

The Willem [~NE France, NW Germany, Benelux] botanist Tewis Camigner, who was active during the Revitalist period in the sixteenth century, offers the first surviving written sources noting the high sugar content of beet, describing the billrod [cochineal red] hue and sweetness of a syrup he extracted from natural beets. However, the low concentration of sugar (perhaps one part in eighty) and the presence of other untasteful substances in the syrup made it no substitute for the sugarcane increasingly cultivated in the Novomund [Americas] over the following two centuries.

This changed with the Pentrose survey of the 1730s. Borland having recently been severely limited in its access to Novomundine trade, the state coordinated with the University of Pentrose to locate productive or otherwise useful cultivars of many plants. The survey's crowning glory was the development of the braxoch, which seems to have been a hybrid of Silesian and Cassuvian stock. 

Alchemy

The golden colour and high sugar content of the braxoch beet are intimately connected. The clause [gene] which instructs beet to produce its red pigments is located in the same heredian volume [chromosome] (volume II), very near a clause regarding the production of a sugar tinderbox [enzyme]. It is believed that beet breeding led to miscopying of this volume which simultaneously broke the red pathway (causing yellow pigments to predominate) and triggered a much higher rate of sugar production in the root.

Altering volume II also led to a thinner, more brittle skin, which would usually leave such beet more vulnerable to fungal infection during growth (partially explaining, in an Iscovalian sense, why this presentation is absent in the wild). However, braxoch beet exhibits another, independent alteration; it produces a non-functional iron marker [sensor]. This causes it to remain stuck in a low-iron regime, producing a much higher concentration of oromarynx [riboflavin; vitamin Bβ²²], a yellow-orange substance and aqueous essence [water-soluble vitamin]. Oromarynx, which gleams under surblavic [UV] light, also has counterzoic [anti-microbial] properties which are activated by exposure to such light.

Hence, alterations which are individually costly becomeβÄΠ

203.1: Saxony

notes in translation on a German polity.

A mesur dy Scon Tetrarchy, y Reyaum a Saxony intermane un stat vassaler majour nell'Empir Teudesc administraΟΑ des Bardovic, e plusour ne lour dynasty de Hohenbrocken regnaurn sull'empir enter.
During the Second Tetrarchy, the Kingdom of Saxony was a major vassal state of the German Empire administered from Bardwick, and many of their Hohenbrocken dynasty ruled the empire outright.

Y Reyaum a Saxony deneu toccaΟΑ par y sfondranΟß dy Scon Tetrarchy ny siecr treΟß.
The Kingdom of Saxony was affected by the collapse of the Second Tetrarchy in the thirteenth century.

Y Stadbond smargent de citaΟΑ marcander ag norΟΑ secedeu eΟΑ abascau Saxony vars sell'eΟΑegl amont a Magdbourg e Lepcy.
The nascent Stadbund of mercantile cities in the north seceded and reduced Saxony to its lands upstream in Maidburg and Lepzi.

CossiraΟΑ es ig Saxony a y plurem dy vajot vancaΟΑ des y Var Theudesc Primer; ny souc den y polity gourna may d'eiΟΑel a alcot seyon posseut n'aucun hour de sy histoir.
Saxony is considered to have profited most from the First German War; in its aftermath the polity controlled more territory than it had at any point in its history.

Durant y siecr novoΟß, y Ο΅aucr Azur Marian (oc instauraΟΑ a Lepcy) tarromoy commouΟΑ vars reform cas ley de heritanΟß e de dreit slecΟßonal.
In the nineteenth century, the Blue Marian Society (which was founded in Lepzi) spearheaded movements for reform to inheritance and suffrage laws.

203.2: cug "fall"

cug /kaj/ [kaj]
- fall, drop, descent, motion downward;
- decrease, decline, reduction, the lessening of a value;
- downfall, defeat, loss, doom, an (especially precipitous) erosion of status;
- slide, flume, chute, slope, a structure along which things are made to travel from a higher to a lower position;
- (poetic) passing, death, the end of a person's life;
- (legal) nullification, repeal, the act of invalidating something (especially an act of law);
- (specifically) annulment, the dissolution of a marriage on the grounds that it was never formally valid

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish cau»ù, cu»ù /kaΥày…Θ ~ ky…Θ/ "fall, descent", which reflects Latin adjective cad≈Ϊcus "falling; doomed; futile; (law) lapsed". The word is seen in metaphorical use almost at once; for physical downward slopes it may have been influenced by Norman cute "fall; chute". Poetic and legal usages are both attested from the late Middle Borlish period.

L'intermane un cug de sy caldour corpore.
/l…ΣnΥ¨t…¦r.maΥàne …Σn kaj de si kalΥàdur Υ¨k…îr.poΥàre/
[l…ΣnΥ¨t…¦Υê.m…êΥàne …Σ≈΄ kaj de si k…êlΥàd äΥê Υ¨k…îΥê.p äΥà Äe]
3s=compose-imp indf fall of 3s.gn heat bodily
It was a decrease in her body temperature.

##

203.3: lat "root"

lat /lat/ [lat]
- side, edge, a one-dimesional segment of the surface of a shape;
- root, a number which when raised to a given power yields a particular quantity (especially the square root);
- cusp, precipice, an abrupt boundary between two relatively smooth planes;
- verge, brink, threshold, an edge or margin before a sudden or dramatic change

Etymology: Middle Borlish, a borrowing from Latin latus "side, flank". Originally used in geometry, and extended to the arithmetic usage by analogy to the example of a square's area and side-length. Senses referring to metaphorical sudden boundaries are attested from the eighteenth century.

Y lat vorpal de cinq cent e doΟß val ogt.
/i lat v…îrΥàpal de ts…Σnk ts…¦nt e d…îts val …îjt/
/i lat v äΥêΥàpal de ts…Σ≈΄k ts…¦nt e d…îts val …îjt/
df side diced of five hundred and twelve worth eight
The cube root of five hundred and twelve is eight.

203.4: vry "prestige"

vry /vri/ [v Äi]
- prestige, status, cachet, fame, esteem, the state of being well-thought-of by people;
- power, influence, the ability to effect one's will or to have people consider one an authority;
- kudos, acclaim, accolades, praise, credit for one's achievements

Etymology: nineteenth-century borrowing from a lenited form of Welsh bri "prestige, honour". This descends from pre-Classical Kelt brΡΪgos "importance, weight", though to be cognate with Latin gravis "heavy". Originally a somewhat dΟ©classΟ© slang term, the word had passed into the ordinary register by the mid-twentieth century. A brief period of use as a approbatory interjection was over outside ironic mimicry by the 1980s.

Un Honour de Hatfell gajannar gal vry moulous.
/…Σn oΥànur de hatΥàf…¦l Υ¨ga. £aΥànar gal vri muΥàluz/
[…Σn  äΥàn äΥê de h…êtΥàf…¦l Υ¨ga. ù…êΥàn…êΥê gal v Äi m äΥàluz]
indf honour of Hatfell win-inf escort kudos hefty
Winning a Hatfell Honour carries great cachet.

##

203.5: Through the Ages 2

more collected notes on a variety of background figures in Boralverse history, listed in chronological order.

NadΟ†lla fi Carol was an influential figure in the early years of the city of Santrafew [~Boston, MA], one of the first trading posts in Mendeva [NA] established by Provence. She was raised as the eldest daughter of town reverend Carol Zancoud, and as an adult she helped to author the 1621 Becouin Treaty which defined the amicable relationship of the traders with the local MastΟ≤iset [Massachusett] people for several decades. She is rumoured to have carried on an affair with the runaway heir to the Avosche duchy in her youth, although she makes no mention of such a relationship in her surviving diaries.

Dorothe Shock was the original proprietress of the De Marnoshring emporium in the early nineteenth century, which over the following two hundred years would become a quasipolitic guild [multinational corporation] controlling much of the market for children's toys and various material hobbies. She ran a store in the Saxon city of Miszen [Meissen], originally alone but from 1813 alongside her younger brother.

Agathe Bisset (d. 2025) was a French politician and author. Born in Blois during the latter years of the Drengot Collusion, she held various background positions in parliaments of France in the decades following its dissolution, only attaining wider recognition via her works of popular fiction from the 1990s onward. Her most successful work, 2003's Hommes en Noir (Men in Black), chronicles stories of spycraft and espionage from ancient times to the modern day; her mother's alleged position as a mid-century spy at the Ministry for Information may have been the source for some of Bisset's more recent accounts.

##

204.1: CampottonΟ§

notes on the New Provincial city of CampottonΟ§.

L'instanΟßment vivota a mesur dy siecr ogtoΟß deut ag terrentaΟΑ merscous e contrsalubr e tan all'eΟß ottem havan dereΟΑr dy floy e sceindessem derim.
The settlement languished through the eighteenth century due to unfavourable marshy terrain and the better harbours across the river and further inland.

Sy fortun cambisceu ny siecr novoΟß cosogr l'alant de moneΟΑ: caye fosseyaΟΑ un canal de sdroy majour trenc faint tras y peninsol (eΟΑ y Guittoc vars y doujug lucraΟΑeu a Squanna conjognent), eΟΑ y merscaΟß ag maugr de CampottonΟ§ deneurn canalfeyaΟΑ e darfaΟΑ cos drastic.
Fortunes changed in the nineteenth with an influx of funds: a major shipping canal across the peninsula (connecting the Guittoc to the wealthy Esquanna [Susquehanna] river system) was dug, and the marshlands surrounding CampottonΟ§ were drastically canalised and drained.

Slargiscment ancour dy canal figheu loy a plaΟß entarn dy siecr veintem.
Further widening of the canal took place into the twentieth century.

ReconnuΟΑ cos brey com y "Venis a Mendeva", CampottonΟ§ accareu un parnommanΟß d'eutaΟΑ durant l'epoc Bel Joy e fo nell'asc modern sorguΟΑ vars denir un aucoum forcer cas cultur eΟΑ empory.
Briefly known as the "Venise of Mendeva [North America]", CampottonΟ§ gained a fashionable reputation during the Good Game Period [late 19C] and by the modern day had risen to become a cultural and economic powerhouse.

##

204.2: quil Οßaveyour "feather bed"

quil Οßaveyour /kw…Σl tsaΥàvjur/ [kw…Σl ts…êΥàvj äΥê]
- feather bed, a soft mattress filled with down;
- (metonymically) easy street, comfort, well-being, security, the concept of a comfortable life;
- mod con, amenity, some device or feature that makes one's life more convenient

Etymology: phrase attested from the early modern period, literally "cushioner bed". This is an early example of a compound-forming construction, much more prevalent in the modern language, which uses agent nouns as modifiers. The noun quil "bed" descends directly via Old Borlish quil, coil "bed, cot" from Latin cubΡΪle "bed, couch; lair, kennel"; the verb Οßaveyar "cushion, soften; sugarcoat, sanitise" is a derivative of Οßaveyal "pillow", descending from synonymous cervΡΪcal in Latin. Use as a mascot for easy living is seen from the nineteenth century, and has persisted likely due to its use in some evergreen folk songs.

Jo dout ouΟΑ y quil Οßaveyour vel lou incombir.
/ £o dut uΈΗ i kw…Σl tsaΥàvjur v…¦l lu Υ¨…Σn.k…îmΥàb…Σr/
[ ùo dut uβÄΩΟΑi kw…Σl ts…êΥàvj äΥê v…¦l lu Υ¨…Σ≈΄.k ämΥàb…ΣΥê]
1s doubt whether df bed cushioner will.sbj 3p.acc befit-inf
I doubt life on easy street would suit them.

204.3: alant a haun "homecoming"

alant a haun /aΥàlant a hon/ […êΥàlant a hon]
- landfall, the end of a sea voyage;
- homecoming, the act of returning to one's home;
- (by extension) safe habour, deliverance, sanctuary, respite, the attaining of a position of safety after an extended period of danger or uncertainty

Etymology: collocation originating in Kigman's fifteenth-century poetry, literally meaning "docking at a haven". The noun alant "landfall" is a deverbal from alandar "arrive, turn up, dock"; this was borrowed in the Old Borlish period from Old English ΡΓelendan "approach, arrive". The noun haun "haven, port" was borrowed from Old Norrish h«Ϊfn "harbour".

The phrase was used by Kigman already to mean a return to a metaphorical homeland; it had become synonymous with safe places more generally by the end of the following century.

Nos coum alant a haun posc tan semman.
/n…îz kum aΥàlant a hon p…îx tan s…¦Υàman/
[n…îz kum …êΥàlant a hon p…îx tan s…ΣΥàman]
1p get.pst-1p landfall at haven after so.much week
We made landfall after so many weeks.

204.4: ne traiscn "late"

ne traiscn /ne trex.nΧ©/ [ne Υàt Äe.x…ên]
- slow, behind, (of a timepiece) showing a time earlier than the true time;
- late, delayed, occurring after the scheduled time due to unforeseen events or inefficiencies;
- laggy, buffering, responding slowly due to high latency

Etymology: originally horological jargon used by clockmakers, attested from the seventeenth century, literally "in lag". The noun traiscn "lag, delay" is a deverbal from traiscnar "trail, drag behind, lag", which reflects a Vulgar Latin verb traxin≈ç "I drag, pull, tug" which extends the verb trah≈ç in Classical Latin. The word has been extended from clocks to public transportation and then to computing in the modern era.

Casc phasmet tienn ne traiscn coll'oc emblemat.
/kax faΥàzm…¦t Υàtj…¦n.nΧ© ne Υàtrex.nΧ© k…îΥàl…îk Υ¨…¦m.bleΥàmat/
[kax f…êΥàzm…¦t Υàtj…¦.n…ên ne Υàt Äe.x…ên Υ¨k…î.l äβÄΩΥ¨g…¦m.bl…ΣΥàmat]
each clip hold-3p in lag with-df=s.px mosaic
Every video keeps buffering at this resolution.

204.5: Mathematical Etymology (cont. 2)

more excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary o'Mathematics, a reference quire in the Merch language compiled by Rotcham [Rochdale] University Primers.

covrend adj. [/Υàk ä.v…Ψ…ônd/ "surjective"] that given W βÜ£ X : m, for every x in X there is some w in W sent there; considering m as a portioning of W + X, that no ~!x is empty. 1831 < translation of Saxon deckend in DΟΦmmeyer (1827), participle of decken "cover" < Classical German thaci≈ç "I cover" (cognate with Merch thatch).

horizontal adj. [/Υ¨h…î.…Ψ…ΣΥàz…în.t…ôl/ "convergent"] that there is a value where, no matter which neighbourhood of the value is chosen, a given sequence or analogous object is eventually contained within that neighbourhood. Around 1750 < Scholastic Latin horizontΡ¹lis, adjectival derivation of horiz≈ç "horizon; [limit]" < Greek αΫ¹œ¹Έ·ΈΕœâ βÄΔ (horΟ≠z≈ç) "divide, separate, mark out".

mirthon num. [/ΥàmeΥê.ΟΑ än/ "ten thousand"] a quantity of ten thousands or one hundred hundreds. Around 1400 < slightly earlier mirth (possibly influenced by e.g. Kentish or Norman) < Middle Welsh myrΟΑ "mirthon" < Latin m»≥riadΡ™s "many thousands" < Greek ΈΦαΩΓœ¹ΈΙΧ¹ΈΩœ² βÄΔ (m≈ΪrΟ≠os) "countless". Originally and still poetically for any large quantity.

sevrend adj. [/Υàs…¦.v…Ψ…ônd/ "injective"] that given W βÜ£ X : m, for every x in X there is at most one w in W sent there; considering m as a portioning of W + X, that no ~!x has two distinct members. 1831 < translation of Saxon sondrend in DΟΦmmeyer (1827), participle of sondren "separate" < Classical German sundr≈ç "I separate".

zeffergoer n. [/Υàz…¦.f…ôΥ¨gu.…ô/ "root"] a value which a given W βÜ£ X : m sends to zeffer [zero]. 1897, compound of numeral zeffer and verb go; the former from Arabic ΊΒΌêΌ¹Ό£Ί± βÄΔ αΙΘifr "empty, void" (see entry for further detail) and the latter from Classical German g≈ç "I go".

205.1: Detchard in the Demimonde

continued translation from the beginning of [real-life] book The Henchmen of Zenda by KJ Charles, a 2018 pastiche of the 1894 romp The Prisoner of Zenda.

Alor sceye jo la, desracinaΟΑ, sgarant, rebateyaΟΑ n'un viΟΑ novel lon famigl ben histoir.
So there I was, rootless, wandering, rebaptised into a new life without family or history.

Jo fey regres des Hind vars l'ovest par my blad, faur, e spaΟΑ, ma jo skighau alcun feu Larresc ΟΑocar.
I worked my way back from India using my cards, wits, and blade, but did not trouble EnglandβÄôs shores.

Y tarmagn d'Europ es un jagat accoglendessem por y semblant de mey: aventurer, nomad, gentilhom volour e gentilhom stoccour, domn dy jorn e dy noit, hasardour e vlangr e dragoman de rumour, aristocrat rent abascaΟΑ, routuron ne cognit s'enhautiscent cos precartaΟΑer.
The Continent is a more welcoming place for my sort of people: adventurers, wanderers, gentleman thieves and gentleman thugs, ladies of the day and night, gamblers and fences and information brokers, aristocrats brought low, masquerading commoners reaching dangerously high.

Nos sodal dell'oΟß tribu cougln e soun viΟΑ e lengaΟß e paΟ·s, com un confraΟΑreΟß teun d'outrley ant un glonscel de fieltaΟΑ e d'enmigtaΟΑ ancour teunessem, e se poΟΑ un parson parey encontrar a mesur d'annaΟΑ n'un madrugour a Berlin, un palaΟß a Praug, eΟΑ un gajon a Rom.
We of that tribe drift in and out of lives and languages and countries, a loose fraternity of rogues with a looser tangle of loyalties and rivalries, and one may see the same face over the course of years in a Berlin nightspot, a Prague palace, and a Roman gaol.

Par mejan de bustr solter: j'er ne Vien cant primer j'encontrau un domn Fransc magn bonet eld settoΟß svingaΟΑ com aΟΑm par un trocour mentaΟß eld tre vey maissem.
To give a single example: I was in Vienna when I first met a very pretty Frenchwoman of seventeen flaunted as mistress by a dishonest stockbroker three times her age.

Jo l'encountrau aval prose cant jo cof laur com batman an bosty reloΟΑ e condonant a strem a Dresden yon la luΟ·sce sona d'ouger sull'eudΟΠmony.
I next encountered her when I was hired as bravo at an extremely elegant and accommodating brothel in Dresden where she presided over the festivities.

La me reconnoy incommorantβÄîoc bogr ΟΑesmal, dec j'aye sy preu patron occeut avant luyβÄîma la retenoy min douΟß cos absoluΟΑ a them de toll'affer, my time destarriscent n'affirmant ig l'aye lo stimaΟΑ ennoyant com aye l'eΟß ayent hur me daΟΑ oc por.
She recognised me at onceβÄîunsurprisingly, as I had killed her first protector in front of herβÄîbut she was utterly charming about the whole business, assuring me that she had found him as tiresome as the people who had hired me to do it.

205.2: harvougl "earwig"

harvougl /harΥàvujl/ [h…êΥêΥàvw…Σl]
- earwig, any of various winged insects with elongated bodies and rear-pointing pincers;
- earbud, a small speaker placed into the ear canal;
- (intelligence) handler, someone at a base who remotely directs an intelligence asset in the field

Etymology: originally in the first sense, from Old Borlish horvo»ùl, erve»ùl "earwig". Thought to be partially from Vulgar Latin forficula "small scissors", diminutive of forfex "scissors", and partially from Old English Ρ™arwicgel "earwig". The development of the initial consonant is unexplained, but compare Vascon horveix "earwig". Folklore connecting the insect to ear-burrowing influenced the modern senses, first attested in the twentieth century.

Me soupren ig ja n'ay jo l'ec harvougl pars.
/me suΥàpr…¦n aj  £a ne  £o l…¦k harΥàvujl parz/
[me s äΥàp Ä…¦n aj  ùa ne  ùo l…¦k h…êΥêΥàvwil p…êΥêz]
1s.obl surprise cmp yet ng=have.1s 1s df=p.px earwig lose.p.pst
I'm surprised I haven't lost my earbuds yet.

205.3: vrolliscour "crowdpleaser"

vrolliscour /Υ¨vr…î.liΥàxur/ [Υ¨v Ä…î.l…ΣΥàx äΥê]
- entertainer, performer, one who puts on a show for an audience;
- crowdpleaser, someone or something that is (reliably) entertaining for an audience

Etymology: nineteenth century, agentive derivation from verb vrollir "please, entertain, cheer, gladden". This itself is a backformation from noun vrollisc "entertainment, fun", borrowed in the early modern period from a Willem or Saxon Dutch adjective like vrolic, frolich "merry, cheerful". In the modern period, when referring to a person, the word often denotes someone who has an online audience.

Y vrolliscour er pildr entisour cirteyant.
/i Υ¨vr…î.liΥàxur …¦r Υàp…Σl.drΧ© Υ¨…¦n.tiΥàzur ts…ΣrΥàtjant/
[i Υ¨vr…î.l…ΣΥàx äΥêβÄΩ Ä…¦Υê Υàp…Σl.d…êβÄΩΥ¨ Ä…¦n.t…ΣΥàz äΥê ts…ΣΥêΥàtjant]
df entertainer cop.imp pill stiffener peddle-p.prs
The influencer was hawking boner pills.

205.4: barricheyar "electrify"

barricheyar /Υ¨ba.riΥàkjar/ [Υ¨ba. Ä…ΣΥàkj…êΥê]
- power, hook up, to connect to a source of electricity;
- electrify, charge, to power by means of electricity;
- galvanise, energise, thrill, excite, to render delighted or eagerly anticipatory;
- shock, zap, to cause electricity to pass through (as to cause pain or death)

Etymology: nineteenth century, verbal derivative with the causative suffix -eyar < Latin -idi≈ç, -iz≈ç "I make _" from adjective barric "electric" (formerly also a noun, now displaced by astraphor "electricity", from Greek). This in turn entered Borlish during the previous century, originating in Arabic Ί±Ό£Ό² βÄΔ barq "lightning". In metaphorical or emotional use the word is attested almost at once.

Y visn brasser ag port me barricheyau y man.
/i Υàv…Σz.nΧ© braΥàs…¦r …¦j p…îrt me Υ¨ba.riΥàkjo i man/
[i Υàv…Σ.z…ên b Ä…êΥàs…¦ΥêβÄΩ Ä…¦j p…îΥêt me Υ¨ba. ÄiΥàkjo i man]
df knob brass-adj at.df door 1s.obl zap-pst df hand
The brass doorknob gave me an electric shock.

205.5: The Alphabet

excerpt from near the end of the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (sixth edition), a reference work published in 2001 and compiled by the Utcheter Lexical Association.

βÄΠa clouded wine fermented from rice, brewed across Asia from northeastern Hind [India] to Nivon [Japan]. Around 1700 as zito < Napolitan zito, zuto "rice wine" < Tenivian [Angami]  êΟΚthΟ≥. Respelt in the nineteenth century in line with the possible cognate in Greek ΈΕαΩΠΈΗΈΩœ² βÄΔ (zΟΜthos) "an Egyptian barley beer".

Afterwords

1. Th'Alphabet

a ba ce dy e ef ge,
hake i jot, kea el em;
en o pa qua ro es te
u va win, ex wy zed.

The letter names in Merch are a mixture, some descending from Old English futhork runes and others from Old Northern French names for the Latin letters.

The vowels a e i o u [/…¦Υê iΥê …ôi uΥê j…ôw/] reflect Latin; four from early Merch long vowels [/aΥê eΥê iΥê oΥê/] and the last from the adaptation of ONFr [/y/] to [/juΥê/]. The name of the half-vowel y [/w…ôi/] is of unclear origin, but usually supposed to be a combination of the names of u and i (compare ONFr ui and modern Borlish oy "Y").

The inverted names ef el em en es [/…¦f …¦l …¦m …¦n …¦s/] come via ONFr from Latin, unusual for ending with their associated sound instead of starting with it. The names of kea ex zed [/k…¦Υê …¦ks z…¦d/] also come from Latin for lack of any corresponding rune (although some scholars suggests that the ill-attested elhs "elks" rune may have contributed to "X").

Other borrowings via ONFr from Latin are ce ge hake te [/siΥê d £iΥê h…¦Υêk tiΥê/]; alternatively, the name for "T" may stem from a rebracketing of tue < OE tiw as te + u due to the following u in the alphabet.

Descendants of OE futhorc include dy ro win [/d…ôi roΥê w…Σn/] < OE dΟΠg rΡ¹d wynn. The "R" name showcases final-consonant deletion, which also happened to ba pa qua [/baΥê paΥê kwaΥê/] < OE berc perΟΨ cΤΩerΟΨ in the early modern period as a form of regularisation.

The final two letters are jot and va [/d £…ît vaΥê/]; the first is an early Merch borrowing via Latin i≈çta from Greek ΈΙαΩΕœ³Έ± βÄΔ (iΟ¥ta) "iota" used to distinguish the consonant j from the vowel i, and the second is an alteration of earlier vaw (likely by rebracketing with the following win) borrowed in the early modern period via Latin vau from (presumed) Greek œùΈ±αΩΠ βÄΔ (waΟΜ) "waw".

2. The Numbers

one two threβÄΠ

206.1: Borrhatic League

notes in translation on the Borrhatic League.

L'AlleyanΟß Borrhatic es un congeries de colluy indrec e de band por gest politic oc caglou irrayant sur gournment laΟ·c, condoΟßment e (ne dezein recent) tan promoΟßon de paΟß e de reclam.
The Borrhatic League is a collection of charitable organisations and political action groups with a focus on laic governance, education and (in recent decades) also pacifism and objection.

L'AlleyanΟß se macquau des collaboraΟßon entr communtaΟΑ de creinΟß minortaΟΑer ny clas ovrer tras Europ NorΟΑer ny siecr novoΟß primal.
The League grew from collaboration between working-class groups of minority faiths across Northern Europe in the early nineteenth century.

L'AlleyanΟß denoy primer prominent ag taug de polity a mesur dy Var Teudesc Dovem ny dezein 1820 e par y souc den.
The League first rose to prominence on the scale of polities during the Second German War of the 1820s and in its aftermath.

Com bustr, Hilgart e Michel Bervisson broucan influyenΟß nell'AlleyanΟß Borrhatic n'Istr Boral lor, cos particuler cas hortaminanΟß vars soffrisc religious.
For instance, Hilgart and Michael Bervisson were influential in Borland's Borrhatic League at this time, especially with regards to calls for religious tolerance.

MalgraΟΑ ig l'AlleyanΟß Borrhatic eΟΑ y commouΟΑ a DesveyanΟß contemporane son discrepaΟΑ (cos occajonal conflagrant), reconnuΟΑ es y dou aun parol contriboit ny DeclaraΟßon LaΟ·c d'an 1850 ne Belgrad.
Although the Borrhatic League and the contemporaneous Deviance movement have had (occasionally explosive) disagreements, both are known to have contributed verbiage to the 1850 Laic Declarations in Belgrade.

206.2: aΟΑaun "as one"

aΟΑaun /aΥàΟΑon/ […êΥàΟΑon]
- (archaic, poetic or dialect) as one, together, at the same time or place or doing the same thing;
- (legal) in agreement, united, unanimous, without any dissent;
- in unison, in chorus, tutti, acting together in e.g. playing music or singing;
- (interjection) let's go, now, an exhortation to simultaneous action

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish aΟΑaun, extended variant of synonymous aun "together, at the same time". This comes either from a Late Latin phrase ad ≈Ϊnum "at one" or from a deverbal of ad≈Ϊn≈ç "I unite". Current in the language until the early modern period, it has fossilised in legal and musical uses; the interjection appears to have been reborrowed in the twentieth century from rural dialects that retained the term.

Y torriot hoissaurn ag preu gran entrjainΟß aΟΑaun.
/i t…îΥàrj…ît h…îjΥàsor.nΧ© …¦j prau gran Υ¨…¦n.trΧ©Υà £ents aΥàΟΑon/
[i t äΥà Äj…ît h äiΥàsoΥên …¦j p Äau g Äan Υ¨…¦n.t…êΥà ùens …êΥàΟΑon]
df bassoon blow-pst-3p at.df first big section tutti
The bassoons played in the first big tutti section.

206.3: Οßondr "pendant"

Οßondr /Υàts…în.drΧ©/ [Υàts…în.d…ê]
- pendant, a piece of jewellery which hangs down as an ornament (as attached to a necklace or earring);
- locket, such an ornament which can be opened to store some item of sentimental value;
- charm, amulet, bead, medallion, trinket, a small item attached to e.g. a bracelet especially as having notional protective properties

Etymology: Middle Borlish, via contemporary French from Tuscan ciondolo "pendant, charm". This is a deverbal from ciondolare "dangle, swing", which is thought to reflect a Vulgar Latin exundul≈ç "I sway", from unda "wave". The Borlish term has broadened to cheaper and less precious ornaments than its cognates in other languages.

Catr Οßondr sur sy boucr dreΟßn "ΟΑurr".
/Υàkat.rΧ© Υàts…în.drΧ© s…Σr si Υàbuk.rΧ© Υàdr…¦ts.nΧ© ΥàΟΑ…Σr.rΧ©/

four pendant on 3s.gn loop spell-3p burn-inf
Four charms on his bracelet spelt "burn".

206.4: erref "rescue"

erref /…¦Υàr…¦f/ […ΣΥà Ä…¦f]
- retrieval, recovery, restoration, return, salvage, the act of getting something back that was lost;
- rescue, salvation, liberation, relief, delivery, the saving of something or someone from danger;
- redemption, reformation, changing of one's character for the better

Etymology: Middle Borlish, deverbal from errevir "rescue". This reflects Old Borlish erever, erevre "save, redeem", from Latin Ρ™ripi≈ç "I set free; I remove", related to rapi≈ç "I grab, seize". In early use the term often had religious connotations, referring to the redemption of the world by Jesus Christ.

206.5: The Scathe of Saint Sophia

excerpted from 2014 popular history textbook The Fourth Burning of London, and other Screwups: the Eventful History of Things Going Wrong, written in collaboration with the Faculty of Domain History at the University of Huzatre [~Bayonne] by writer (formerly and more famously of historical lovetales) GalarΟßa fi MolhomΟ©, who undertook her studies there.

βÄΠby NicolΟ† sAvignon, who in the fourteenth century led the renovation and expansion of Saint Sophia's Cathedral [the Hagia Sophia] in Stamboul [Istanbul], which had stood mostly unchanged since its construction in the sixth century during Justinian I's reign. It has been suggested that the expansion was commissioned in order to cement the cathedral as the largest one in all Christendom; challengers in Valencia and Augsburg were in the process of being built at that time that would have surpassed it if not for sAvignon's redesign.

There is a popular theory that sAvignon is responsible for the Scathe of Saint SophiaβÄîdespite the fact that this calamitous building collapse (which killed Emperor Theosthenes, much of his family, and dozens of dignitaries) occurred in 1651 N, over three hundred years after his reconstruction project was completed. The theory appears to have originated in 1890 via Jordan hixPasqual's popular history work The Last Years of the Last Roman Empire, in which he contends with little evidence that sAvignon employed experimental and faulty techniques in reinforcing the great central dome of the cathedral, weakening the structure over the centuries until the eventual disaster.

Of course, it is difficult to know which precise modifications were made to the cathedral in the 1340s, but most historians agree that the bulk of the changes were in extending the complex, not rebuilding supports. Nonetheless, the dramatic quality of the Scathe of Saint Sophia, along with the narrative that it led directly to the Empire's final collapse less than a decade later, have led this theory to becomeβÄΠ

##

207.1: Blue Trans-Willem War

notes in translation on the Blue Trans-Willem War

Y Var Tras-Willem Blau sta l'un entr un ranc de var nell'epoc modern primal ec polity Fransc e Teudesc envouvent cos principal (oc a digr, l'eΟß polity l'eiΟΑel de Willemy parlocant) eΟΑ ec stan a gest accommis a fin auteΟß broucar sull'oΟß eiΟΑel.
The Blue Trans-Willem War was one of a series of wars in the early modern period which principally involved French and Dutch [German] polities (that is, those powers located on either side of the territory of Willemy [~Benelux, Lorraine]) and which were de facto waged over control of said territory.

Noc obstroint ig defagl sey l'akirtanΟß sporadic de combat porΟßonnar com var n'eΟß cahut lon un poy d'arveiΟΑrostaΟΑ, scaunΟß historic ne nostrell'epoc benoΟßn y periodeyaΟßon par colour lonc y scrittour dy siecr novoΟß Andrysk de Santer.
Though the sporadic flare-ups of hostilies are difficult to divide into discrete wars without some arbitrariness, modern historiography usually works with the chromatic periodisation of nineteenth-century writer Andrysk de Santer.

Sy model, concreut ou (lonc y tesmogn) des y colour sur y gonfan dell'eΟß victour ben des l'ongit de symbol alchemic, attriboy y var Blau com y rix pΟΠnultem, oc cessant ny siecr settoΟß nuverrem.
Her system, based either (depending on the account) on the colours of the victors' standards or on alchemical symbolism, has the Blue war as the penultimate conflict, ending in the late seventeenth century.

Certan historicour, ma noc de Santer, aun ag var Blau y spaiΟΑoir ascreut d'un kynous entr y coglenΟß abrangendessem de Rix Romannesc ny siecr settoΟß mejan.
Some historians, though not de Santer, have classified the Blue war as part of the broader collection of Romantic Wars of the mid-seventeenth century.

207.2: gouth "pushup"

gouth /guΈΗ/ [guΈΗ]
- pushup, press-up, a bodyweight exercise resting on hands and toes and pushing away from the floor;
- bounce back, comeback, rally, a return to form after some sort of setback;
- rebound, a romantic partner one dates in order to get over the end of a relationship

Etymology: late nineteenth century, during an early instance of the many physical exercise fads of the modern period. The term was borrowed from Welsh gwth "push, thrust, shove". The metaphorical uses (principally in gouth dar "to make a comeback, to attempt to get over someone by dating; lit. to give pushups") are seen from the 1970s.

207.3: nougl "distaff"

nougl /nujl/ [nw…Σl]
- distaff, a device storing natural fibres to be spun;
- spinneret, the organ with which a spider spins its web;
- spinning, fibrecraft, the art of making things like textiles or rope;
- (regional, poetic) cattail, teasel, mullein, foxtail, or various other herbs and reeds with distaff-shaped flower spikes or seedheads

Etymology: from late Old Borlish no»ùl "distaff". This is though to conflate two earlier forms: (1) cono»ùl "distaff" < synonymous conucula in Late Latin, dissimilatory derivative of Classical colus "distaff"; (2) rocle "distaff" < synonymous rokkill in Old Norrish. Dialects differ greatly on which particular plants may be referred to by this term; its meaning is not clarified by its indiscriminate use in poetry.

L'oΟß mersc verdeya de nougl spinous.

207.4: yedic "vinegar"

yedic /jeΥàd…Σk/ [ ù…ΣΥàd…Σk]
- vinegar, a sour liquid formed from the fermentation of alcohol;
- acetic acid, the principal acidic component of vinegar;
- (attributive) irritable, narky, testy, responding nastily to any interaction

Etymology: descendant of Old Borlish iedic, edic "vinegar", borrowed from synonymous edic in Old Dutch. This is apparently a metathetic variant coming from late Classical German aceda, acita "vinegar", a borrowing from Latin acΡ™tum "vinegar" possibly influenced by related acidus "sour". The Borlish word is attested as an adjective in the modern sense from as early as the twelfth century.

TanniscaΟΑ casc ugnlac sou yedic.

207.5: Lineball Global Tourney

collected notes on a sporting event.

The Lineball Global Tourney (Borlish: Torne Mondial a LanΟßbal /t…îrΥàne m…înΥàdjal a lantsΥàbal/), often abbreviated in the English languages to the LGT, is a knock-out competition in which teams from around the world compete in the sport of lineball. The LGT has been held every five years since 1920, and it has grown from a dozen entrants to the modern complement of over sixty. The precise line-up of each tourney is affected by the ways geopolitics interacts with the institution's founding charter, as well as economic considerations (which is to say bribery of the bilateral committee).

In principle, teams in the LGT represent nations or at least polities. In practice, the complexity of defining what counts as a polity (an entire alliance-federacy? A province? Half of a double-crown?)βÄîas well as the inequity of polities having wildly different sizes of talent poolβÄîhave led to guidelines defining loose bounds on the population size of applicant polities.

The sport of lineball was formalised many decades before the establishment of the LGT, and there were several small-scale international lineball competitions that preceded it. For instance, the Cup of Cappatia [South America] was first held in AwasΟΚconda [~ParanΟΓ] in 1908, pitting twenty city teams from various Cappatian staddomains against each other.

The 1935 LGT was the first to be held in Mendeva [North America], hosted by Hasiny [~lower Mississippi watershed]. The territory of Norman Kent, which was then a constituent of the Drengot Collusion, was a competitor whose team included one VrΟ©queu Margouille. His son, Vincent, also became a professional lineball player, and indeed he captained Kent to its only victory in the 1965 LGT.

208.1: Detchard in the Demimonde - 2

continued translation from the beginning of [real-life] book The Henchmen of Zenda by KJ Charles, a 2018 pastiche of the 1894 romp The Prisoner of Zenda.

Nos se collaborau accougl tras un ogtoΟßad de meis magn avoccant, fin a l'acceptau y rebateyaΟßon tars d'un ambasctour, e jo decideu de xiphar luΟ·r sou un patron munfeyandessem.
We worked together for a highly entertaining eighteen months, until she accepted an ambassadorβÄôs carte blanche, and I decided to sell my blade to a more generous bidder.

Tre annaΟΑ apres, cant jo jace n'un masmorrat Parijan ant gur dy descovart incoΟ·br d'un catoum solter e fatal de star sont e dy sojorn incΟΕibr equal vars y thron mixallour, a my bel domn Fransc fo yon scaveu jo cos baurous, e luy la parsoloy casc zaloc eΟΑ ognt por m'errevir, stant un careΟß amaug.
Three years later, when I lay in a Paris gaol cell awaiting the inevitable discovery of a single, deadly proof of my guilt and the equally inevitable trip to Madame Guillotine, it was my pretty Frenchwoman to whom I wrote in desperation, and she who bailed and bribed me out, at eye-watering cost.

Untal interman y viΟΑ varenastr, yon y hasart eΟΑ y lim son imman, vray amigtaΟΑ son preΟßous e teun com diamant, e moraltaΟΑ n'es for incommod.
Such is the demimonde life, where risks and passions run high, true friendships are as precious and as rare as diamonds, and morals are an inconvenience.

Acconnosc aun Sr Jasper Detchard e vos, ou sunt ou soluΟΑ, e luy le sta y segr cas par Michael Elphberg nell'oΟß harvest frige.
That is Mr. Jasper Detchard for you, take him or leave him, and that was the man sought out by Michael Elphberg that chilly autumn.

Michael, Duc a Strelsau, heir scon naΟΑ a Rudolf IV a Ruritania. Michael, volent y rey.
Michael, Duke of Strelsau, second son to Rudolf IV of Ruritania. Michael, who would be king.

Veram me storra l'exposson vos soupleyar n'oc hour.
I really am going to have to do the explanations now.

208.2: a to-rutto "single file"

a to-rutto /a Υ¨to.r…ΣΥàto/ […êΥ¨to. Ä…ΣΥàto]
- single file, in a line or queue one person wide;
- in a row, sequentially, one after another, successively, located in a linear span across space or time;
- one at a time, individually, occurring in discrete instances

Etymology: attested in various forms since the late Middle Borlish period, an ideophonic idiom used to refer to single-file sorts of arrangement or motion. The phrase is hypothesised to originate in farmers' livestock-counting numerals, which may be reflected by the modern appearance of to-rutto in counting-style nursery rhymes. These rural tally-like systems (e.g. an, to, ry, bo, bum, ...), which are present across Borland, derive from Germanic or Celtic numbers.

VeleΟΑ aular a to-rutto lonc y forcul.
/veΥàl…¦ΈΗ oΥàlar a Υ¨to.riΥàto l…înk i f…îrΥàk…Σl/
[v…ΣΥàl…¦βÄΩΟΑ äΥàl…êΥêβÄΩ Ä…êΥ¨to. Ä…ΣΥàto l…î≈΄k i f äΥêΥàk…Σl]
will.sbj-2p walk-inf at single.file along df pass
Cross the mountain pass in single file.

208.3: lon theu "accidentally"

lon theu /l…în ΈΗau/ [l…în ΈΗau]
- idly, carelessly, thoughtlessly, mindlessly, unintentionally, without much or any conscious drive;
- accidentally, by accident, by mistake, happening due to some misapprehension or lack of knowledge;
- by chance, unexpectedly, coincidentally, spontaneously, for no apparent reason

Etymology: phrase meaning "without will", attested from the Old Borlish period. The preposition lon "without, lacking" is thought to descend from Latin adverb longΡ™ "far away, for a long time". The noun theu "will, habit" was borrowed in the Old Borlish period from Old English ΟΨΡ™aw "wont, behaviour". Extension to happenstances and mistakes is seen from the early modern period.

Y stuΟΑant er y scaumel maneyant lon theu.
/i stiΥàΟΑant …¦r i xoΥàm…¦l maΥànjant l…în ΈΗau/
[i st…ΣΥàΟΑant …¦ΥêβÄΩ Äi x äΥàm…¦l m…êΥànjant l…în ΥàΈΗau]
df student cop.imp df pen handle-p.prs w/o will
The student was idly fidgeting with the pen.

208.4: foyer figr "settle down"

foyer figr /f…îΥàj…¦r Υàfaj.rΧ©/ [f äΥà ù…¦Υê Υàfa.j…ê]
- put down roots, settle down, to establish a long-lasting home in a particular community (especially via marriage and children);
- take root, take hold, to become established in some form

Etymology: idiom from the early modern period, literally "to set (a) hearth down". The noun foyer "hearth, fireplace; (fig.) homestead" is a direct descendant of Vulgar Latin focΡ¹rium "hearth", an extension of synonymous focus in Classical Latin. The verb figr "put down, lay, place" reflects Latin fΡΪg≈ç "I fasten, fix".

Comoscon fig mesnoΟßon foyer?
/Υ¨ko.moΥàx…în faj Υ¨m…¦.snoΥàts…în f…îΥàj…¦r/
[Υ¨ko.m äΥàx…în faj Υ¨m…¦.zn äΥàts…în f äΥà ù…¦Υê]

How do misconceptions take hold?

208.5: Chemical Etymology (cont. 4)

further excerpts from the Veriloquace Glossary of Alchemy, a reference quire compiled by Rotcham University Primers [1].

- adipous adj. [/Υàa.d…Σ.p…ôuz/ "fat-soluble"] of a substance, able to dissolve in media such as fats, oils or spirits, as opposed to being able to dissolve in water (see also aqueous); more generally, lacking any significant imbalances of plenty between its atoms [non-polar]. Early 18C, from Scholastic Latin adip≈çsus "fatty, made of fat", repurposed from earlier non-theoretical usage (see Middle French adipeux "fatty", from the same source) < Classical Latin adeps "fat, lard, grease".

- essence n. [/Υà…¦.s…Σns/ "vitamin"] any of a specific group of substances, either aqueous or adipous, which are must be ingested in small quantities for human health because they are not prodcued internally. In alchemical use circa 1860, formed backwards from essential "necessary, indispensible" < Medieval Latin essentiΡ¹lis "intrinsic, necessary" < Classical Latin essentia "nature, being" < esse "to be".

- oromarynx n. [/Υ¨…î.…Ψ äΥàma.…Ψ…Σnks/ "riboflavin"] an orange-yellow aqueous essence with various functions in the human body, including case [cell] fuelling and thyreous action [immune response]. 1923 via Welsh < Scholastic Latin oromarynx < Greek αΫÄœ¹œ¨œ² βÄΔ (orΟ≥s) "whey", since the substance was first isolated from cow's-milk whey, and ΈΦΈ±œ¹œÖΈ≥ΈΨ βÄΔ (marynx) "marynx" [fluorescent substance], due to its characteristic halvation under surblavic [UV] light. This latter word, first attested in the Malchassian period in the sense "flash, glimmer", is thought to be related to verb ΈΦΈ±œ¹ΈΦΈ±Έ·œ¹œâ βÄΔ (marmaΟ≠r≈ç) "(of metal) to flash, sparkle" or ΈΦΈ±œ¹ΈΙΈΒœçœ² βÄΔ (marieΟΚs) "quicklime".

[1] a publishing house based in the city of Rotcham [Rochdale, Greater Manchester] and affiliated with the local university.

209.1: Medieval Mayor

notes in translation on Wilhelm BrΟΦz.

Willaum BrΟΦz (fl. 1225) sta bourgmer ny citaΟΑ d'Augsbourg tras un dezein ag mendrem ny preu mitaΟΑ dy siecr treΟß.
Wilhelm BrΟΦz (fl. 1225) was mayor of the city of Augsburg for at least a decade in the first half of the thirteenth century.

Le sta un amig coram de Paul von Burchadt, y preu Pap alcant aposc dy Sce a Augsbourg, e cossiraΟΑ es ig y pretenjon peculier de BrΟΦz por sy citaΟΑ influΟ·sceu certan entrell'idea politic cas religion ny DenonΟßaΟßon Paulin d'an 1225.
He was a personal friend of Paul von Burchadt, the future first Pope of the Augsburg See, and BrΟΦz's personal ambitions for his city are thought to have influenced some of the religious policy outlines in the Pauline Denuncations of 1225.

Ny sona de BrΟΦz protagoneyau Hunter Noteworthy y lasc d'an 1961 e dy lengaΟß Markesc Ant y Govon com Mecchiat Nostr, un dramateyaΟßon dy cainΟß dy Secession Teudesc.
BrΟΦz was portrayed by Hunter Noteworthy in the 1961 Merch-language film With the North Star to Guide Us, a dramatisation of the events of the German Secession.

Un Οßocr magnfeyaΟΑ de BrΟΦz (oc font noc ayent a alcun descreuΟßon ben depegnt dy segr, dec no scein nien) deneu fis d'an 1975 a starn dy hal muncever n'Augsbourg.
A magnified statue of BrΟΦz (not based on any extant description or depiction of the man, since none exists) was erected in 1975 outside Augsburg's town hall.

##

209.5: Maria Joquinnera

collected notes on a sixteenth-century figure.

Maria Joquinnera (b. c. 1512, d. 1565?), also known as Princess Maria, the Princess of Mendeva and (among other spellings) Dona Quixote, was a woman of Nappe [Lenape] origin, born in the region of the lower Guittoc [Delaware] River, who travelled to the Vetomund [Old World] in the late 1530s and became one of the most remarkable figures of the Intertwining Century. Presented at several Vetomundine courts as a usurped and displaced princess of a Mendevan people, she was by all contemporary accounts a formidable polyglot and political intellect. She is best known for her patronage of the first systematic lexicon of a Nappe [Unami] tongue. The circumstances of her birth, her crossing, and her death remain disputed.

Nomenclature

The name recorded least often, which Maria may have used in private among family and close friends, appears inconsistently in various phonetic renderings and earliest as as Quixote in the account-book (ca. 1538βÄ™41) of merchant Bartholomeu fi Acher. The root element has later been tentatively linked to the Nappe word kixuh "sun, moon", which may be connected to the epithet la Dama del OcΟ†s "the Sunset Lady", attested only once in the Queen of Barcelon's correspondence.

Meanwhile, "Maria" is almost certainly a baptismal name, a common choice in this era and region for converts from non-Christian backgrounds. The name or epithet Joquinnera is a demonyn derivative related to Nappejoquin, the Pronvincial traders' name for the peoples of the lower Guittoc and Esquanna [Susquehanna] rivers. This was borrowed from the Nappe word hoking "land, territory".

Origins and Voyage

Maria's early life is shrouded in mystery. She may indeed have been of saquima [~noble] lineage, but no independent Nappe account survives of a "usurper" (described vaguely in European accounts as one of her father's kinsmen) seizing control of a nation and expelling the family of the former ruler. It cannot be determined how much of the traditional tale of her origins was invented by the Provinical merchants who brought her to Europe, how much was shaped by Maria herself, and what if any actual events underlie it.

Two broad hypotheses to explain Maria's crossing to European between 1539 and 1540 have been advanced. First, she may have emigrated voluntarily as an emissary, out of simple curiosity or in order to flee real danger. This is supported by accounts of her subsequent behaviour, which reflect a clear understanding of her position and a canniness regarding how to best advance it. Alternatively, she may have been abducted or otherwise coerced aboard by the Provincial merchants, as a curiosity for display or a diplomatic prop. This fits the broader unfortunate trend of similar mistreatment of Mendevan peoples more closely, but is harder to reconcile with the deference apparently shown to her as early as her arrival in Marsella [Marseilles], unless she managed to win over the captain (whose identity has unfortunately been lost) and crew in the course of the voyage.

The question of her son, Barthem Joquinnel, further complicates matters. Born within months of Maria's arrival, he could conceivably have been fathered before her voyage or during it. The identity of Barthem's father is not mentioned in surviving contemporary accounts of the birth; Barthem himself, who became a minor diplomat in later life, appears never to have discussed it.

Life in the Vetomund

Maria Joquinnera arrived in a Provence which had been devastated by the Second Great Dying only a couple of decades earlier. She is recorded as having already learnt to speak Doccian [~Occitan] when she arrived in Marsella, and to have picked up several other European tongues (Vask, Burgund, possibly even Borlish) in her first years in Europe.

The fiction (or partial fiction) of a displaced Mendevan princess proved durable, effective and convenient for all parties. It gave the royal courts of Europe a legible category to place her it; it provides the merchants with a compelling story of the western lands to spur further investment; it gave her more room to manoeuvre than any other transplant from Mendeva would receive in this century. She is known to have sojourned at the courts of Burgund, Barcelon and Lombardy; one noblewoman of Furlain described her as "accustomed both to being observed and to observing others without the slightest awe".

Her most enduring legacy is a dictionary of the Nappe tongue (Onomatoir de la Lenga Nappena), in whose front matter she is named as both patron and primary informant. The most complete surviving manuscript contains some 1400 words, along with set phrases and diplomatic formulae, grammatical notes of mediocre accuracy, and a short text which may be a song of mourning or of formal welcome.

The record thins markedly after approximately 1558. It is possible that she attempted to return to Mendeva (she appears alongside attendants on a Provincial vessel's manifest, but may have disembarked before the ocean crossing, as no record of her arrival exists; alternatively, she may have died at sea). Her son visited the Novomund [New World] only once, married a Burgund woman of the lower gentry, and spoke Nappe into adulthood (albeit with a Doccian accent that is recorded as having greatly amused his mother); it is thought that he did not pass the language on to his children.

References

- Bartholomeu fi Ascer,  Accounts of the Merchantmen of Provence (1535βÄ™45)
- Dictionary of the Nappe Tongue (compiled ca. 1547βÄ™51)
- Physican, Heal Thyself: the First Century of Santrafew (anthology; the source of several documents regarding early Provincial contact with Mendevan peoples)
- A. Thendar, Princess or Prisoner? The Curiosity of Kixuhque

210.3: time "qualm"

time /tiΥàme/ [t…ΣΥàme]
- qualm, misgiving, reservation, scruple, doubt, a feeling of unease or uncertainty regarding a course of action;
- timidity, shyness, diffidence, self-effacement, a lack of courage or confidence; 
- con, disadvantage, drawback, a weakness or undesirable feature of something

Etymology: derived from earlier adjective time "doubtful; shy, timid", a borrowing from Scholastic Latin timeus "frightened, cowrdly". This comes from the root verb time≈ç "I fear, I am afraid". The word is seen as a noun from the seventeenth century, and as a disadvantageous quality of something (as opposed to something felt by a person) since the mid-nineteenth century.

210.4: mogn "low"

mogn /m…îjn/ [m…îjn]
- low, low down, situated close to the ground;
- short, squat, having little vertical extent (esp compared to other dimensions);
- trifling, trivial, petty, of no particular importance;
- silly, unserious, provoking amusement through foolishness

Etymology: direct descendant of Old Borlish mogne /mo…≤…ô/ "low down", of disputed further origin. Probably not related to Old French moins "less", despite use of the word in parallel comparative constructions in dialectal modern Borlish. The most secure lead is Kernish [Breton] monn "oaf", connected with Old French moing "someone mutilated" to a proposed Gaulish munni≈ç "blunt", although the semantics are somewhat tenuous.

##

210.5: Journeys and Junctures

notes on various aspects of the Boralverse alternate history setting which begin with the letter J.

Jaibos Breweries (Borlish: Yeustman Jaibos) is a beer-making guild based in the southwestern Borlish city of Axbane operating under the ownership of the Jacobin Fraternity. The Fraternity was founded in tenth-century Flanders under the patronage of Amelia of Bruges, but was forced to flee to Borland in 1067 N after being implicated in a popular uprising against Drengot Emperor Geoffrey II; it thereafter established its principal seat at the Peninsular Monastery on a promontory overlooking Axbane.

Japetos III is an barric steeplepost line [telegraph cable] running undersea between the port of Gadire [Agadir] in Morrack and the city of Paratzon [Saint John] in Brasil [Newfoundland], a distance of some 1,500 leagues. It is the third cable to bear the name Iapetos, a Titan who as the father of Atlas has supplied a ready name for successive endeavours to span the Atlantic by wire. The project was jointly conceived in 1943 N by the Morrack and Ambrosian governments, but was set back first by protracted disagreements over funding between the two administrations and then by the outbreak of war, before the cable at last conveyed its first messages in October 1950 N.

Josforo Sant'Angelo was a traveller and writer from Tuscany, and the youngest son of the last Marksman of CremunaβÄîa title that lapsed when the Lombard marquisates were abolished in the 1860s. Sant'Angelo recorded one of his travels in Pasti Mezziterreni (Middlesea Meals, 1880 N), a mealbook documenting a single sunward journey along the full arc of the Middlesea [Mediterranean] coast, from Tarifa in Andalus to the Atlantic-facing port of XΧÄr l Joas [Ksar es-Seghir] in Morrack.

Judeta Barracin  was a novelist born in Andalus who spent her formative years among the islands of the Arcabil [the Caribbean], the daughter of a trading family with commercial interests in those ports. She returned to Andalus as an adult with a sharp eye for the social textures of its cities. Her 1919 N trevold [novel] Querdos Llineros (Flaxen Hearts) made was an immediate sensation: set in early-nineteenth-century Cordova [CΟ≥rdoba] and following a cast of market traders, minor gentry, and household staff through a web of interconnected small dramas, it is now considered a founding text of social comedy alongside the contemporaneous output of Dorotha Quistack.

Also known in his role as a Kentish media personality as Godwilham Desk, he wrote modernised adaptations of myths and fairy tales in order to appeal to children (not entirely through bowdlerisation).

The kingdom suffered a deep winter, and the turn of the seasons didn't offer much relief.

Snow fell in spring, and the storm-winds of summer howled with a biting, icy breath.

Rumour parvasiscen y rout ny citaΟΑ creint ig y CauΟß Ragnt aye y coroun n'argent volaΟΑ dy Domn de Joivr, e la pretene a y tar venjar fin a lo fos luy algebraΟΑ.
Rumour spread through the city streets that the Wild Hunt had stolen the silver crown of the Lady of Frost, and she intended to punish the land until it was returned.

Je Coyol de StaΟΑ, y Rey ny Dal amandau haubrjour por heros voccar des par toll'eiΟΑel e por lou armar a y reyaum soucorrir.
On Midsummer's Day, the King in the Valley sent heralds to summon heroes from throughout the realm and to charge them with saving the kingdom.

Sir Berengal arrived with his holy lance, and the witches of Ο΅adrosc flew in from the western sky.

211.2: a tug "for fun"

a tug /a taj/ [a taj]
- for fun, for the love of the game, done for one's own enjoyment or other intrinsic desire to do so;
- friendly, penny-ante, (of a game) without serious stakes;
- voluntarily, intentionally, by one's own free will;
- (conjunction) so that, in order to, with a desire to bring about (some result)

Etymology: Middle Borlish idiom, literally "to passion". The noun tug "passion, fervour, enthusiasm" descends from Old Borlish tu»ù "inclination, impulse, drive". This was borrowed from Old English tyge "a pull, a draw", which is related to the verb tΡ™on "pull, drag, lead". As a conjunction (attested from the sixteenth century) the construction requires someone's explicit agency in a way which the near-synonyms por and a fin do not.

Masctig por cors a tug teyen boncop.

211.3: lon mensc "dishonourable"

lon mensc /l…în Υàm…¦nx/ [l…îm Υàm…¦≈΄x]
- (military) dishonourable, due to conduct that is considered reprehensible;
- without a reference, without a character, not being entitled to an employer's testimony when applying to a new job;
- (by extension) cold turkey, abruptly and without consideration for comfort

Etymology: originally in legal expressions such as destourm lon mensc "dishonourable discharge", the phrase literally means "without honour". The preposition lon "without" reflects Latin longΡ™ "far away"; the archaic noun mensc "honour; mercy; courtesy; decorum" was borrowed in the Old Borlish period from Old Norrish mennska "humanity". Extension to non-miltary and non-employment contexts occurred in the mid-twentieth century.

211.4: haval fair "play pretend"

haval fair /haΥàval fer/ [h…êΥàval feΥê]
- play pretend, make believe, role-play, to engage in imaginative activity;
- feign, dissemble, put on an act, to perform insincerely;
- go through the motions, to do something perfunctorily or without conviction

Etymology: idiom from the sixteenth century, literally "do dolls". The noun haval "doll, puppet" was originally a southern dialect term out of Pentrose, borrowed in the Middle Borlish period from Welsh haval "likeness, image; doll, effigy". Extension out of the realm of childhood play is seen from the nineteenth century.

211.5: Who's Who (1900s Edition)

collected notes on various figures active during the twentieth century.

Mark Deuthex (fl. 1973) was born in Roun [Rouen], the administrative capital of the Drengot Collusion. He spent his early career as an operative for the Red House (the informal name for the Collusion's Ministry for Information); his service there spanned the Collusion's final years and its abrupt collapse in 1961. In civilian life Deuthex took to writing. His 1973 spycraft novel We Wake to Broken Windows drew on his years in the Ministry with a frankness that might have constituted a criminal offence had the Collusion still existed. It remains one of the most widely read firsthand accounts of the Collusion's intelligence apparatus; a sequel was reportedly planned but never completed.

Johan Pamfey (fl. 1971) was a collocker [journalist] for the Borlish Sunday magazine Niacer (a word meaning "pertaining to nests or, metaphorically, to households"), where he contributed most notably a column of political and cultural observation over roughly two decades. He was famous for a reliable willingness to voice unpopular positions, and for various exposΟ©s of corporate malfeasance. One of his articles revealed that the Ministry of Health had taken money to downplay the effects of impure imports of detergent chemicals by large companies including Pax and Chandler.

Maldwen Tamaha (b. 1890, fl. 1950) was a long-reigning king of Hasiny [lower watershed of the Mississippi]. He acceded to the throne in 1916 and presided over an unusually steady few decades in Hasiny's history. His reign saw the expansion of the country's sobot [radio] network, and contributed personally in the form of patronage in exchange for weekly speeches broadcast to the nation via the technology. He is also remembered for a personal investment in the expansion of universal primary schooling to the rural parts of the country.

The programme will include a suite of excerpts from CrΟΓyenschot's opera Ad Pentaculum, as well as a medley of Merch film music through the decades.

212.2: routuron "commoner"

routuron /Υ¨ru.tiΥàr…în/ [Υ¨ Äu.t…ΣΥà Ä…în]
- commoner, plebeian, proletarian, a citizen who holds no noble title or rank;
- (with plural agreement) hoi polloi, masses, rank and file, people considered as opposed to the elite

Etymology: Middle Borlish derivative of routur "vulgarity, working-class quality" (compare late Medieval Latin rupt≈Ϊr≈ç "freeman"), which originally referred to a small quantity of land as might be granted to or owned by a common farming household. This descends from Latin rupt≈Ϊra "fracture, rupture, breach; (later) a division of land". Outside of historical texts, the term is usually metaphorical or jocular.

212.3: quoncein "young adulthood"

quoncein /kw…înΥàtsin/ [kw änΥàtsin]
- (strictly) twenties, the decade when a person is aged between 20 and 29 inclusive;
- young adulthood, a nebulous period of one's life which follows childhood and lasts approximately a decade;
- prime, trial run, Arcadia, a period in the existence of something which has any of various similarities to a person's young adulthood (the precise sense depending heavily on the speaker)

Etymology: twentieth-century calque of Willem quonzerΟΓd "young adulthood", an abstract derivation from southern dialect quonze "twenty" (standard twinte); the second element was replaced with the Borlish suffix -ein denoting a number of years (as in dezein "decade", carantein "a period of forty years"), which descends from Latin -ennium "years". Metaphorical extension of the word usually refers either to inexperience and naΟ·vetΟ© or to a pinnacle of performance and flourishing.

La passau y quoncein par y Novomond aΟΑendrar.
/la paΥàso i kw…înΥàtsin par i Υ¨no.voΥàm…înd Υ¨a.ΟΑ…¦nΥàdrar/
[la p…êΥàso i kw änΥàtsin p…êΥêβÄΩ Äi Υ¨no.v äΥàm…înd Υ¨a.ΟΑ…ΣnΥàd Ä…êΥê]
3s.f spend-pst df twenties by df new.world tour-inf
She spent her twenties travelling the Americas.

212.4: benoyour "medium"

benoyour /Υ¨be.n…îΥàjur/ [Υ¨be.n äΥà ù äΥê]
- medium, spiritualist, a person who attempts communication with the dead;
- shaman, witch doctor, a member of a community representing or speaking for the spiritual realm and often also acting as a healer

Etymology: agentive derivation from now-obsolete benoΟ·r "to receive blessings; (specifically) to receive communications from the deceased". This verb was formed in the early Middle Borlish period by analogy with bendigr "to bless" (itself obsolete by parallel beneΟ·r, both from Latin benedΡΪc≈ç "I commend, praise; I bless") from the root oΟ·r "to hear" < Latin audi≈ç "I hear". The word enjoyed some use in lay Christian practice before dropping out of use in the Revitalist period of the early sixteenth century; it was revived in reference to non-Christian roles as well as to rites of the Novomundine "Prodigal Faiths" during the nineteenth century.

212.5: German languages

paragraphs excerpted in translation from a page on Borland's distributed library about the German languages, as it stood in February 2021 N.

βÄΠdescended from Classical German [Proto-Germanic], which was spoken in roughly the same time period as Classical Latin. It fractured during the first millennium N into three major lineages, which we label Dutch German, Gothic German and Norrish German [West, East, and North Germanic].

It is uncertain whether the language of the Goths is truly the only member of its lineage. Its possible contemporary relatives (the languages of the Vandals, the Burgundians, and some others) are too little attested in writing to be certain, and the proposed modern relatives (the German languages in Halich, Kiew or Vallack [regions of southeastern Europe], mostly) are too far displaced in time and too much influenced by the Dutch languages to connect definitively to Gothic.

Dutch German is the most widespread lineage, and can further be divided geographically into the Mainlander Dutch languages and the English languages. Some of the Mainlander Dutch languages are the official languages of a polity or polities, like Willem, Saxon or Bavarn (the last of which is official in both Bavarn and Ruritania), while others enjoy at most regional recognition (for example Frise).

Here is an example of a sentence in various modern Dutch languages, along with reconstructions for older forms.

Classial German: Gultham fl≈çidi thurhu quen≈çnom Ρ™thr≈çnuns.
First Dutch: GolΟΨ fl≈çidi ΟΨuruh quen≈çn≈ç Ρ¹dr≈çn.

Old English: Gold flΡ™wΟΨ ΟΨurh ΟΨΡ¹ra wΡΪfa «Θdran.
Old W Dutch: Gold fluoieth thuro thero wΡΪvo Ρ¹theran.
Old N Dutch: Gold fliuted thuruh thero wΡΪv≈ç Ρ¹theran.
Old S Dutch: Golt fliuzit duruh dero wΡΪbo Ρ¹daran.

Merch: Gold flows through the womens veins.
Willem: Goud vloyeth thΟ≥r the vrown aren.
Saxon: Gold fletet durgh de fruen odren.
Bavarn: Goit fliest drugh der frauen adern.

-4. A mesur de cal dezein dy siecr veintessem figheu loy l'Anarchy Portingal? Cal fanglanΟß mandaΟΑer dell'eΟß annaΟΑ preluyent se courn raussiΟΑ cos desconnex com issent entrig dy sfrondranΟß cas auteΟß politic?

- interpreter, translator, a person who translates speech to enable people without common languages to communicate;
- communicator, PR person, someone whose job is to convey technical information to the public;

213.3: pedisecq "attendant"

pedisecq /Υ¨pe.diΥàz…¦k/ [Υ¨pe.d…ΣΥàz…¦k]
- attendant, servant, page, valet, a person employed to see to a person's needs;
- goon, henchperson, thug, an enforcer for an organised crime syndicate;
- lackey, follower, toady, sycophant, a servile flatterer

Etymology: early modern period as a direct borrowing from Latin pedisequus "attendant", used as a general term encompassing the pre-existent specific terms for various types of personal servant. The Latin term literally means "following on foot", from pΡ™s "foot" and sequor "I follow". The word had gained generally pejorative connotations by the twentieth century, reinforced by its use in media depicting gangs and similar groups.

213.5: Stobor

excerpt from 2004 work Figures of Fable, a compendium of fantastical creatures and beings found in the world's mythology and folklore. Originally published as Sers Fabloses, it was written by folklorist FlorenΟßa Garaut, who lived most of her life in the New Provincial city of Gorram, and who claimed her interest in folklore arose from the inexhaustible bedtime questions of her triplet children.

βÄΠthe ever-enigmatic riddles of the sphinx.

Stobor

Stobor is a broad term referring to any beings which arise from the magical animation of artificial depictions of people (or animals, or nonexistent creatures), or which otherwise take the form of animated figurines or dolls. Traditionally and in common usage stobor are miniatures, but in more recent taxonomy the term has been broadened to life-size figures like animated statues (or even, occasionally, larger-than-life colossi).

The word stobor first referred to sprites in the folklore of Awry [~Hungary] and Kiew [~Ukraine] which animated children's dolls, going on miniature adventures of their own or performing helpful or mischievous deeds around the house. It is allied with the modern Majoric [Hungarian] stobroΡ΄ke "figurine", Bulgar ―¹―²–Ψ–±–ΨΧ¹―Ä βÄΔ (stobΟ≥r) "woodcarving, wooden doll" and Lithowan stabΟΓ "idol".

Peoples the world over have imagined that the images they create taking on a life of their own. Some of the oldest civilisations created figurines like the Egyptian voshapty [ushabti] or the warriors of Lleswan [Terracotta Army] to act as attendants or guardians in the afterlife. Other peoples have associated intangible spirits with physical representations, like the minx [nkisi] of middle Africa or the assistants of Hephaestus.

The carved wooden figures erected at Norrish [Norse] sacred sites were understood not merely as representations of the gods but as temporary residences: theβÄΠ

Por ig un famigl vel cavel rous eΟΑ ogl azur retenir lonc dou siecr, n'un eiΟΑel noc verdeyant necun de rous ben de nobr, stovent es ou focment scrutinious ben un mescomprenjon dell'axiom sengr de piver.
For a family to remain red-headed and blue-eyed down two centuries, in a land not filled with redheads or aristocrats, requires either careful breeding or a failure to grasp simple principles of stock management.

Sceint y bustr vecin dy linaΟß reyal Hapsburg, dont y caΟΑen ancestral visnous rasgattovau par un lunatic lasdr ant un granmaΟΑr stant oΟΑig sy morrig, se poΟΑ demar ig l'Elphberg volois lorry costumn de mariΟΑaΟß entr cosvour recuidar cos coΟΑelosessem, ma com l'ec fougl voun l'illustrar, inacavabr es un aristocrat Rurtan addocir de nien.

She gave him a sickly brat of a son, also Rudolf (every second person in this damned tale is called Rudolf, for which I can only apologise), and promptly expired, her duty done.

- nightclub, nightspot, club, a venue that is open late at night to provide entertainment (especially alcoholic drinks, music and dancing);

- rebaptised, born again, dedicated anew to a religious identity (often a more strident or demanding version of one's former faith);

214.4: varennastr "underworld"

214.5: Saints Isida

excerpts from The Alphabetical Almanac, an enumeration of Christian saints in alphabetical order along with short biographies and information such as their feast days. Written in 1854 by Christopher "Kipper" Bytheway, a Merch domain historian, the book was lauded for the quantity of original research that Bytheway conducted on his travels and pilgrimages, but was also controversial for eliding most details concerning the saints' miracles.

βÄΠtwenty-four ravens.

Isida of Toled [Toledo]

Isida of Toled (fl. 1220; feast day April 21) was a nun of the Brethin Order during the twilight of the Single Caliphate's Cairene authority over Andalus. The Brethin House in Toled was a relatively recent incursion into a Christian-majority region of Andalus, but had already accrued a small but influential scholarly community.

She is remembered principally for her work De Mutationibus LinguΟΠ "On the Transformations of a Language", in which she contended that the changes between Latin and vernaculars like Mostara or Barcelon were not the result of corruption or carelessness, but instead comprised divinely ordainedβÄîand, vitally, enumerable and consistentβÄîpatterns of sound change. Her work anticipated by some margin the comparative method of later scholars. She also appears to have had some command of Arabic and Hebrew, given some references to an incomplete and now-lost companion which may have drawn similar comparisons between those languages.

Isida of Aldegia [Lake Ladoga]

Isida of Aldegia (fl. 1280; feast day December 21; known locally as SiterΟΗ) was a princess of the Inger and an integral part of the Baptism of the North. Her marriage to the king of Holmgrod [Novgorod] was contingent on the conversion of the Inger, a pagan holdout. The conversion of the Inger was completed in stages, with the baptism of the elite preceding that of the broader population by some years. Isida is said to have served as interpreter for the Convoy Australier's missionaries during this intermediary period. Her feast day on the winter solstice is held by most historians to reflect a deliberate alignmentβÄΠ

This is the aftermath of the Second German War and the concomitant disintegration of the Kingdom of the Danaw [Danube].

Y livr abransc y Boxa nou sovran, y ruin se recostroyent de Stamboul, e plusour dy citaΟΑ ny region ec volent alcant aposc unir por y "CitaΟΑ Floyan" noncupaΟΑ stablir.

215.2: ou sunt ou soluΟΑ "take it or leave it"

ou sunt ou soluΟΑ /u s…Σnt u soΥàl…ΣΈΗ/ [u s…Σnt u s äΥàl…ΣΈΗ]
- take it or leave it, expression asserting that something is fixed regardless of whether the listener accepts it;
- either way, no matter what, regardless, no different under any circumstances

Etymology: idiom attested from the seventeenth century, literally "whether used or discarded". The archaic coordinating construction ou βÄΠ ou "either βÄΠ or" (now usually ou βÄΠ ben) descends directly from Latin aut "or", while the past participles come from poetic sumir "seize, take, use" < Latin s≈Ϊm≈ç "I get" and souvr "let go, release" < Latin solv≈ç "I loosen, untie".

215.3: sabler reuvr "turn back time"

sabler reuvr /saΥàbl…¦r rauvrΧ©/ [s…êΥàbl…¦Υê Υà Äau.v…ê]
- turn back time, (used literally) to travel into the past;
- try again, have a do-over, get another chance, to be permitted to disregard some result and attempt something anew;
- live in the past, to dwell (usually overmuch) on memories or nostalgia;
- (computing) reset, to restore to a previous state

Etymology: idiom attested from the nineteenth century, literally "to flip (an) hourglass". The noun sabler "hourglass" is a derivative of sabr "sand" influenced by synonymous sablier in French, from Latin sabul≈ç "sand, gravel". The verb reuvr "flip, upend" descends directly from Latin revolv≈ç "I unwind; I turn over". Originally restricted to metaphor, the phrase is attested in literal use from the late nineteenth century in parachthon [sci-fi, fantasy] works of writing.

La peΟΑeu a sabler reuvr dy scitaΟßon.
/la peΥàΟΑau a saΥàbl…¦r rauvrΧ© di Υ¨xi.taΥàts…în/
[la p…ΣΥàΟΑau a s…êΥàbl…¦Υê Υà Äau.v…ê di Υ¨Οßi.t…êΥàts…în]

She asked to resit the exam.

215.4: y gra valir "measure up"

y gra valir /i gra vaΥàl…Σr/ [i gra v…êΥàl…ΣΥê]
- be up to the challenge, meausre up, to have some quality allowing one to succeed at some difficult endeavour;
- hold one's own, rise to the occasion, to succeed despite adversity;
- have the balls, dare, to possess enough courage or audacity to attempt something;
- matter, count for something, be worth it, to have enough merit to bother considering

Etymology: idiom attested from as early as the fifteenth century in Kigman's poetry, literally "to be worth (the) importance". The verb valir "be worth; should" descends directly from Latin vale≈ç "I have power, I am well". The noun gra "importance, degree, extent" reflects Old Borlish gra "level, sake, weight", which conflates the reflex of Latin gravis "heavy" with that of Latin gradus "step, rank, position".

215.5: Ministry for Consequences

excerpt from The Structure of Twentieth-Century Polities, a 2024 reference work by Neuwillem historian Mbaiara Hormelingh, translated from the original Willem (Stellingen van TwintixtΟ©w StΟΓtsvorm) into Merch by Jet Torpenhow.

βÄΠfirst Ministry for Consequences to be established was that of the Drengot Collusion, the alliance-federacy of northwestern Europe whose dramatic collapse in 1961 is covered in more detail in the section of Ministries for Information in chapter 4. The last of the twenty-four ministries of the Collusion to be created, it was set up in 1932 under the leadership of Runcibal Sawyer-Boon (nowadays eclipsed in fame by his grand-daughter Vendetta Boon). 

The original remit of the Ministry for Consequences was purely reactive: it was to monitor the impact of policies implemented by laws and by the decrees of other ministries (e.g. Family, Farming or Wealth) and to publish reports on their effects. Institutions with similar functions had existed in many governments for centuries (and arguably millennia, in the cases of Cathay, China and the Persias), though they were rarely so centralised and so independent in their activities. Despite its later failings, the Collusion's anthracite government in the 1930s recognised that Consequences would have to maintain editorial autonomy or else be subject to censorship from the very government it was intended to monitor.

At the time of writing, Ministries for Consequences have been established in several dozen polities worldwide, from the Ministry for Effectiveness in Tavancy [~Incan Empire] to the Ministry for the Future in Nackon Dai [Thailand]. The concept spread most rapidly in the late 1960s and through the 1970s, as the fallout of the Furore of 1961 revealed how importantβÄΠ

216.1: Axbane and Commerce

notes in translation on the Borlish city of Axbane, located on the southwestern coast of the island.

Nell'oc epoc, Ausbagn se situant all'un cost dy Stuer Rustig vale immens cas eΟΑegl.
At this time, the location of Axbane on one side of the Rustigh Strait was of immense strategic value.

Ausbagn denoy tandem un entr y citaΟΑ protagoneyour ny Stadbond, un colluy de citaΟΑ marcander ny region.
Axbane eventually became one of the star cities of the Stadbund, an association of mercantile cities in the region. 

CossiraΟΑ es ig d'an 1400N, y colanΟß de sdroy tras y havan d'Ausbagn teye may d'un tarΟß de commarΟß European deur y Miterran.
The throughput of the Axbane port is thought to have accounted for over a third of European trade outside the Middlesea by 1400N. 

Ny siecr catorΟß nuverrem caye tan ig l'oc citaΟΑ Borallesc corrogau un alleyanΟß cordial cos particuler con Bremen.
The late fourteenth century also saw this Borlish city develop a particularly close relationship with Bremen.

Ny dezein d'an 1850 sceye y lign barric sou mar entr Ausbagn e Texel, un dy primer tal connexion campaner ny mont tras un mar. 
The 1850s saw the Axbane-Texel undersea barric line, one of the world's first such steeplepost connections across the sea.

Nell'oc epoc, Ausbagn sta sodal ny Nou Sar Urban cosogr citaΟΑ autonom com Londr.
At this time, Axbane was part of the New Urban Mesh along with independent cities like London.

216.2: vlangr "fence"

vlangr /Υàvlan.grΧ©/ [Υàvla≈΄.g…ê]
- fence, receiver, middleman, someone who buys and sells stolen goods;
- accessory, abettor, a person not present at a crime but bearing some responsibility for it as an assistant or instigator

Etymology: mid-19C in underworld slang, imported from the slightly earlier British form recorded in the Glamour of Deganny. This in turn reflects Suahile [Swahili] valanguri, plural of mlanguri "healer", used in Ongutian [Zanzibari] argot to mean "profiteer, black marketeer". The Borlish term was popularised outside criminal circles by its use in a series of high-profile mob trials in the first decade of the twentieth century.

Sy vlangr porra noc y diamant vendr pre annaΟΑ.
/si Υàvlan.grΧ© p…îΥàra n…îk i djaΥàmant Υàv…¦n.drΧ© pre aΥànaΈΗ/
[si Υàvla≈΄.g…ê p äΥà Äa n…îg i dj…êΥàmant Υàv…¦n.d…ê p Äe …êΥànaΈΗ]
3s.gn fence can-fut ng df diamond sell-inf before year
Her fence won't be able to sell the diamonds for years.
