216.5: False Friends (cont. 5)

some more dictionary entries, this time defining technologies, from the Concise Onomatory o'Merch (seventh edition), a reference work published in 2011 and compiled by the Utcheter [Uttoxeter] Lexical Association.

diacustor [/Υàdja.k ä.st…ô/ "microphone"] n. a device for transferring sound into a signal in another medium for storage or transmission. 1891 < Scholastic Latin diac≈Ϊstor < Greek Έ¥ΈΙΈ±- βÄΔ diΈ±- "through", αΨΑΧ™ΈΚΈΩœçœâ βÄΔ ΡÉkoΟΚ≈ç "I hear" and agent suffix -œ³œâœ¹ βÄΔ -t≈çr. Primarily in military use until the 1910s. See also colloquial jack "diacustor", a clipping.

phasm [/Υàfa.z…ôm/ "TV"] n. the technology of transmitting and storing moving images with audio to be displayed on devices in e.g. households; in historical use, an apparition or spectre. 1620 in the latter sense < Latin phasma < Ancient Greek œÜΈ§œÉΈΦΈ± βÄΔ phΟΓsma "phantom, portent, monster". Out of use for some time before its reintroduction in 1927.

quister [/Υàkw…Σ.st…ô/ "phone"] n. a device for real-time communication via speech over great distances. 1915, agent derivative of quist v. "use a quister", itself from 1899 quist n. < 1888 perloquist < Scholastic Latin per- "completely", loquor "I speak" and agent suffix -ista.

sobot [/s äΥàb…ît/ "radio"] n. the technology of converting information such as sound into longform light [radio waves] for transmission; a device performing this conversion in either direction. 1909 < backwards from earlier adjective sobottal (e.g. 1904 sobottal sender) < Hasinic Welsh sobota≈² "signal" < JaughtΟΓ [Choctaw] Οßobota "smoke; message".

veronic [/v…ΣΥà…Ψ…î.n…Σk/ "plastic"] n. any of manifold substances with an extended chain [polymer] structure widely used in manufacturing, which may be drawn out or moulded when liquid, and which set in a rigid or elastic form; adj. made of veronic; (often overly) willing to comply or change one's view without objection. 1854, tradename in Veronic shellack < Medieval Latin veronΡΪx "resin, varnish", perhaps < Greek Έ£ΈΒœ¹ΈΒΈΫαΩëΧ¹ΈΚΈΖ βÄΔ BerenΡΪΧ¹kΡ™, a city of Libya. As an adjective, the word is seen from 1922.

217.1: Lower Mendeva

notes in translation on the subcontinental region of Lower Mendeva.

Mendeva Bassem es un region geographic abrangent dy part souΟΑer ny tarmagn de Mendeva, se situant all'ovest dy Bugt Mendevan.
Lower Mendeva is a geographical region comprising the southern section of the continent of Mendeva [North America], located west of the Mendevan Gulf.

Parveg l'oc dob contien oΟΑig l'eΟß istr dell'Arcabil, eΟß se situant entarn dy Bugt amont.
The term sometimes also includes the islands of the Arcabil [Caribbean], which are located within said Gulf.

CommuneyaΟßon entr Mendeva Bassem eΟΑ y Vetomond fey commet ny siecr cinqueΟß jagerrem par vascel marcander soll'ΟΠgid dy Colluy dy Nou Mont des Morrac.
Contact between Lower Mendeva and the Vetomund [Old World] began in the late fifteenth century with merchant ships from the Morrack New World Company.

Ny region prepondr y polity de Mascic, oc se respandent des cost vars cost e vars y norΟΑ cos suffighent por tarmn broucar con Mivoc all'ovest e con Taisca all'aust.
The region is predominated by the polity of Mashick [~Mexico], which stretches from coast to coast and so far north as to border Mivock [~northern California] in the west and Taisha [~Texas] in the east.

Plusour fanglanΟß barric a mesur dell'epoc dell'Atelier smargoy des Mendeva Bassem, cos plausibr deut a piver cas issar lonc y spanjon hastous d'accaΟß a gevr.
Many barric [electric] innovations of the late Workshop period came out of Lower Mendeva, likely due to practical concerns around the rapid spread of wired post.

Jusc y siecr doΟß jagerrem, l'oc region fo denuΟΑ enajour diΟßr de mansc ig inclus maus eΟΑ ambac.
By the late twentieth century, this region had become major exporters of foods such as mause [bananas] and ambacks [mangoes].

Larc marΟß trocaΟΑ aun lorrel'orin ne Mendeva Bassem, com bustr gles a bilrod, scoclet, scadom e tascban.
Many trade goods originated in Lower Mendeva, including billrod dye [cochineal], shockle [chocolate], shadomes [tomato] and tashban [vanilla].

217.3: mojolon "nexus"

mojolon /Υ¨mo. £oΥàl…în/ [Υ¨mo. ù äΥàl…în]
- nexus, hub, interchange, a point on many transit routes and thus where many travellers pass through;
- destination, attraction, a location that draws visitors from afar for an entertaining or otherwise enriching experience

Etymology: 18th century, augmentative derivative of mojol "hub of a wheel, centrepoint". This is itself from Latin modiolus "small cup, hub of a wheel", diminutive of modius, a unit of dry volume. Originally a term of art in cartographic circles, it entered common use with the spread of convoy [rail] networks.

Scerra un trasson de deg minut ag mojolon.

there.be-fut indf transit of ten minute at.df nexus
There's a ten-minute layover at the interchange.

217.4: morvaΟß "snot"

morvaΟß /m…îrΥàvats/ [m äΥêΥàvats]
- snot, mucus, bogey, boogers, a secretion from the nose;
- glanders, strangles, any of several infectious diseases of e.g. horses presenting with nasal discharge;
- brat, rascal, imp, urchin, scamp, a child regarded as unruly or selfish

Etymology: pejorative derivation from earlier morf "snot". Possibly a direct descendant of Latin morbus "disease", but also connected to Old French morve "snot, glanders", which may instead be a metathetic borrowing from Old Doccian vorm (compare Barcelon vorm "glanders, jellyfish"), from Frankish wurm "worm". The word has been used for misbehaving children since the 16th century.

J'ascovau e jey morvaΟß sur my camis.

1s=sneeze-pst and throw.pst snot on 1s.gn shirt
I sneezed and got snot on my shirt.

217.5: Anna-Dorota's Discovery

excerpt from Light and Learning in the Long Peace (Chapter 7: The Glade of Iris), by Merch historian Wilwyn Droppett, published in 1928 through Rotcham [Rochdale] University Primers

βÄΠa few years earlier, even in 1816 the span-glass remained primarily a workshop novelty. Hunnady had made thorough observations of the sun during his tenure as Second Lecturer at the Library of Sobjan [PΟ©cs], and his demonstration hall that April was filled with a dozen scholars witnessing the dark bands in the sun's iris [spectrum].

Among them was a young StilΟΓge Anna-Dorota (1788βÄ™1863N), already known for papers on the alignment [polarisation] of reflected light. She remained in Sobjan through the summer, assisting with the grinding of more lenses and the construction of a more portable model. She appears to have been the one to notice the opportunity to observe the sun during an overshadowing [eclipse] later that year in the course of her calculating the timing of additional celestial events for the library's archives.

StilΟΓge reached Lengrod [Lviv] on November 2 with three assistants. At the time it was a frontier city in the Kingdom of the Danaw contested with Poland. The weather was finer here than to the west, where the Two-Years' Winter was ruining harvests and contributing to political instability, and via a cousin contact she was able to hire a shepherd's hut in the hills of Vennike [Vynnyky], two leagues outside the city and along the projected path of the overshadowing.

With dictation and a maid watching a pendulum clock, StilΟΓge recorded an obscuration [totality] of 101 seconds. She recounted her observations in a letter in Latin to Hunnady that day, excerpted in translation here.

βÄΠextinguished the sun, a wreath appeared at once, drawn into rays as if combed. Tracing at once the aperture, I saw at once the two marks of aquiferous air [hydrogen], yet further (between the 39th and 40th points) a golden spark nearly as bright. Though I am not an alchemist, Lector, I would venture no air [gas] of my acquaintance blazes with suchβÄΠ

This golden spark is, of course, the primary band of the corporal we now call aureol [helium], from the Latin aureola, the crown of saints and of the overshadowed sun). StilΟΓge and her collaborators in Sobjan published these observations the following year, but were not widely read for another decade due to the disruption of the Second German War and the collapse of the Kingdom of the Danaw.

StilΟΓge went on to several other discoveries and close correspondence with Wexter Hathwall, whose magnum opus Astraphor [Electricity] and Light included herβÄΠ

excerpt in translation from the 1975 July 1 edition of the Scavlour (Reflecter), a workdaily paper from Borland which focuses on political news.

Com un gest prefait jaΟßol, y Minister a AbondanΟß a la corroulaΟΑ ig toΟΑ y catr claus nell'Act cas PreΟß de Mansc d'an '15 son satisfait.

218.2: bosty "brothel"

bosty /b…îΥàsti/ [b äΥàsti]
- brothel, bordello, an establishment where people engage in sexual activity for pay;
- mess, shambles, jumble, disorder, a collections of things in a confused or dirty state;
- (interjection) what the hell, an expression of shock or incomprehension at how bad or undesirable some situation is

Etymology: from Middle Borlish bosty "brothel", further origin disputed. Possibly from an unattested northern dialect *bosteul, *bostib, which would reflect Latin prostibulum "prostitute, brothel". Alternatively from an unattested southern dialect *bosty borrowed from a contemporary Welsh compound of bos "lump, knob; (poss.) penis?" and ty "house", the former element itself coming via Old French boce "lump". Metaphorical extension to situations considered similar to a notional brothel is attested from the seventeenth century.

Y sleut hamta mout y bosty.
/i slaut hamΥàta mut i b…îΥàsti/
[i zlaut h…êmΥàta mut i b äΥàsti]

218.3: focment "husbandry"

focment /f…îkΥàm…¦nt/ [f äkΥàm…¦nt]
- breeding, (animal) husbandry, the management and care of farm animals by which quailties advantageous to humans are developed and perpetuated;
- curation, selection, the act of carefully choosing a particular subset of candidates for some purpose;

This descends, possibly via Norse, from Classical German fucc≈ç "I strike, hit; I have sex with". Extension to other winnowing processes is seen from the sixteenth century; in the last senses it is contrasted with engin "nature, innate character".

##

218.5: Taisha

Taisha (Borlish Taisca) is a polity of Gulf Mendeva [NA Gulf], bordered by Hasiny to the east and Mashick to the south. Its coastline stretches from east of the Tocolon [~Brazos River] to southwest of the Nar Mottare [Rio Grande], its capital is Yanaquana [~San Antonio].

Toponymy

The name Taisha comes via Arabic Tayisha' from Carovacian [Caddo] taΟ≠sha' "welcome", first written down by Morrack cartographers of the New World Company in the early sixteenth century. It is presumed that this word was simply the most common formula the expedition heard on their voyage.

History

The eastern half of modern Taisha was, until the seventeenth century, part of the Hasiny confederacy of Carovacian peoples, from which the neighbouring polity takes its name. The southern and western reaches were under the influence of a series of Mashick [Aztec] vassals, trading upriver along the Tocolon and across the interior.

First Vetomundine contact was with Morrack's New World Company in the 1510s, and through the later sixteenth century Mashick expanded northeast along the coast, establishing frontier towns. Mashick's authority in the far northeast remained thin, and from the middle seventeenth century onward British and Jaughta [Choctaw] ranchers and merchants (operating with the tacit imprimatur of the Kingdom of Britain) moved steadily west into the Tocolon basin. For most of the eighteenth century the region had three overlapping structures of governance: Mashick regents in the south and west, Hasinick towns along the coast, and Carovacian councils in the interior.

The Concordat of Aberdocolon (1850), signed in the namesake port city, formalised this arrangement. The agreement involved the Mashick crown, the newly autonomous Hasinic crown, and elected Carovacian leaders. The Concordat's practical effect was minimal (its commissioners spent most of the subsequent decades arbitrating cattle-rustling disputes) but later Taishan historiography elevated it into a founding document in which the region's three constituent communities were recognised as co-equal parties.

The late nineteenth century saw Mashick reassert central control; the country was reorganised as the northeasternmost province of the Mashick Empire. This period was also the great age of Taishan herdtale [Western] literature, the genre having by then cooled in Hasiny itself. The grounded and unsentimental stories out of Taisha are generally considered to be the pinnacle of the genre.

The Lovac Wars of the 1920s and 30s brought economic collapse, two cycles of conscription and the first bombardment of Yanaquana by airship. A loose self-rule coalition, drawing explicitly on the Concordat's tripartite model, issued the Declaration of Yanaquana in 1936. Mashick, undergoing its own Ubiquitous revolution, did not contest it, granting Taisha legal independence that same year.

219.1: Another Queen of Ruritania

continued translation from the beginning of [real-life] book The Henchmen of Zenda by KJ Charles, a 2018 pastiche of the 1894 romp The Prisoner of Zenda.

Y Rey, cos evident noc scroΟßaΟΑ par cordougl, er remariΟΑaΟΑ ant un hast cossiraΟΑ par y livraΟß com impoliΟΑ, all'oc vey an domn lon alcun grad naΟΑusc de pres dy tarmn souΟΑer.
The King, evidently not racked by grief, married again with what sticklers regarded as indecent haste, this time to a woman of no significant birth from near the southern border.

La sta fosc de cavel e ΟΑus de character, e jo presum ig l'aus un vray largeΟß seyon indignaΟΑ, cos parΟßal dec sy mariΟΑaΟß sta sine sanguine.
She had dark hair and a hell of a temper, and I expect she had a great deal to be angry about, in part because her marriage was morganatic.

Oc a digr, l'istranΟß gradual entr yoc e femn intermane tan por ig un progenies calscon nolois par engin star heriter dy grad de lorry paΟΑr.
That is to say, the difference in rank between husband and wife was such that any offspring would not be automatically heir to the fatherβÄôs titles.

Οêa stove se dar ne mariΟΑaΟß an Elphberg, desiraΟΑ cos starc fos y vajot broucar.

219.2: cambir "swap"

cambir /kamΥàb…Σr/ [k…êmΥàb…ΣΥê]
- exchange, switch, swap, to place in each other's positions;
- permute, rearrange, reorder, to change the order or layout of;
- change, replace, substitute, to stop employing and to pick up something different

Etymology: from Old Borlish cambir "exchange", itself descending from Vulgar Latin cambi≈ç "I change, exchange" (in a regionally retained fourth conjugation). Along with contemporaneous cambium "a change" this was borrowed from a Gaulish descendant of Classical Kelt cambos "twisted, crooked". Formerly used also to mean "barter, trade", it was supplanted in these senses by trocar, from French.

Οêe son cuidant vars tostrman cambir.
/ΟΑe s…în kaiΥàdant varz Υ¨t…î.strΧ©Υàman kamΥàb…Σr/
[ΟΑe s…în k…êiΥàdant v…êΥêz Υ¨t…î.st…êΥàman k…êmΥàb…ΣΥê]
3p be.3p think-p.prs towards barber change-inf
They're thinking of changing barber.

##

219.3: nancir "contract"

nancir /nanΥàts…Σr/ [n…ênΥàts…ΣΥê]
- contract, catch, to become infected or afflicted with;
- incur, bring on, meet with, to become affected by (some negative condition or consequence);
- be saddled with, to be required to be in the possession of (something one would rather not have)

Etymology: Middle Borlish, borrowed from medical Latin nancΡΪsc≈ç "I contract (an illness)", a non-deponent variant of Classical nancΡΪscor "I encounter, stumble on". The word is seen in non-medical contexts from the eighteenth century.

Sell'heir a sculd demay nanciΟΑ.
/s…¦Υàlir a x…Σld deΥàme nanΥàts…ΣΈΗ/
[s…ΣΥàli.j…êβÄΩ Äa Οß…Σld d…ΣΥàme n…ênΥàts…ΣΈΗ]
3s.gn=son have debt too contract-p.pst
Her son's incurred too much debt.

##

219.4: scouΟΑir "rescue"

scouΟΑir /xuΥàΟΑ…Σr/ [x äΥàΟΑ…ΣΥê]
- rescue, save, deliver, to remove from a dangerous situation;
- buy back, redeem, to convert some security back into (e.g. cash, a prize or a pawned item);
- bail out, pull out, to withdraw someone from a commitment before it worsens;
- snap out of, jolt out of, to abruptly end a confused or mistaken psychological state

Etymology: via Old Borlish directly from Latin excutio "I drive out, throw off" (via the notion of causing someone to leave their current circumstances). In modern use the word usually implies that the rescuee either got themselves into trouble or that they were unfairly tricked into a bad situation.

ScouΟΑ tey den neΟΑan un hom caif oundr.
/xuΈΗ ti d…¦n neΥàΟΑan …Σn …îm kef undrΧ©/
[xuΈΗ ti d…¦n n…ΣΥàΟΑan …Σn …îm kef Υàun.d…ê]
rescue 2s.dsj thereof lest indf person get.sbj wound
Snap out of it before someone gets hurt.

##

219.5: Lumbridge

Lumbridge (Merch /Υàl äm.b…Ψ…Σd £/; Saxon LΟΦmbrΟΦgh) is a city in the northern plains of Saxony, situated on the Elmenaw river. It is one of the three founding cities of the Stadbund, alongside Hamburgh and Bardwick, and hosts the headquarters of the Shethous, a quasipolitic banking guild.

Toponymy

The earliest attestation of the town's name is in the form Luniburc (tenth century). The first element is usually identified with the placename Hliuni attested two centuries earlier; the second is burg "castle; walled town". The forms LΟΦmbΟΕrgh and LΟΦmbrΟΦgh (using the component brΟΦgh "bridge") competed for some time, but already by the end of the fifteenth century the latter was predominant. This is likely due to the storming and razing of the keep in the thirteenth century and the subsequent completion of the Bridge of Salt landmark (see below), which rendered "castle" an etymological ghost.

History

Founded and fortified with a keep on the SΟΦltberg hill by the tenth century, Lumbridge first grew rich from the export of salt. Toll agreements with Bardwick and Bremen in the 1120s, aiming to restrict levies on salt-barges bound downstream, are among the earliest mercantile contracts between the cities that would later form the Stadbund (also known as the Corvine Concert).

Lumbridge formally renounced the authority of the First German Empire in 1214, seven years after Hamburgh and six after Dorstad. Despite this, the city remained nominally a subject of the Kingdom of Saxony. The religious disruption of the German Secession in subsequent decades had little direct effect on Lumbridge itself, already operating as a mercantile rather than a vassal city. However, the raising of levies to fight against Saxony's in two successive years is what led the city to revolt and destroy the keep in 1251.

By the close of the thirteenth century the Kingdom of Saxony had been reduced to upstream lands in Lepzi and Magdeburgh. Lumbridge became a fully autonomous member of the Stadbund, and by 1326 it had completed the ambitious public work of constructing the Bridge of Salt, a fortified stone span over the Elmenaw which still stands to this day, making it the oldest river-crossing bridge in Europe.

In 1341, Lumbridge's delegation voted in favour of the admission of Wrozlav into the Corvine Concert, unlike its neighbours, having formed an alliance with the Borlish city of Axbane.

Although by the sixteenth century Lumbridge was once again vassal to an empire (the Second German Empire), the city preserved much of its mercantile independence. Emperor Matthias II (r. 1542βÄ™1568), attempting to direct Stadbund trade policy, secured only symbolic statements of loyalty from the city council; in reality, the Shethous loans to the imperial treasury gave the merchant nobility of Lumbridge significant leverage. Compare the contemporaneous Drachman merchant dynasty, who dealt similarly with the French, Provincial and Malchassian crowns.

Saxony's ascendancy following the First German War of 1770 brought substantive limits on Stadbund autonomy for the first time since the fourteenth century. Lumbridge retained its seat on the Concert but was obliged to concede customs oversight and a fraction of its salt-monopoly revenues to the Saxon crown at Lepzi.

During the nineteenth-century Workshop period, the Shethous helped to fund Lumbridge's transition from the salt industry to the production more refined alchemical substances.

People

Aldwin SΟΦlt (fl. 1251), burgh mayor who took part in the razing of the SΟΦltberg keep

Janin Feldweiser (1847βÄ™1921), a scholar funded by the Shethous whose 1884 work Volk out SΟΦlver (People of Silver) established the modern periodisation of Stadbund history
